topics.title: Introduction and Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction and Basic Concepts ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Engineering Thermodynamics **Thermodynamics** is the branch of physical science that deals with the relationships among **heat, work, temperature, and energy**, and the properties of matter that govern the transformation of energy from one form to another. The word itself is derived from the Greek words *therme* (heat) and *dynamis* (power). In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMET403)**, *Engineering Thermodynamics* is the applied science concerned with the conversion of energy, the evaluation of energy interactions, and the study of the properties of working substances that govern the operation of heat engines, refrigerators, compressors, turbines, and other energy-conversion devices. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > The first module lays the foundational vocabulary and conceptual framework — System, Surroundings, Boundary, State, Property, Process, Cycle, Equilibrium, Temperature, Pressure, Specific Volume — on which every subsequent module (Laws of Thermodynamics, Entropy, Power Cycles, Refrigeration) depends. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Account" of Energy Think of a thermodynamic system as a **bank account**: - The **system boundary** is the wall of the bank locker. - The **surroundings** are everything outside the locker. - **Energy** is the money inside the locker. - **Heat (Q)** and **Work (W)** are deposits and withdrawals. - A **thermodynamic process** is a sequence of transactions over a financial year. - A **cycle** is when the account returns to its opening balance. This analogy makes one of the deepest ideas of thermodynamics immediate: **energy is conserved, but it can be transferred across the boundary, and its quality (or availability) can degrade — analogous to losing money to transaction fees even though the total balance is preserved.** ### 1.3 Macroscopic vs. Microscopic Approach | Approach | Alternative Name | Characteristic Length Scale | Key Variables | Governing Equations | |----------|------------------|------------------------------|---------------|---------------------| | **Macroscopic** | Classical / Continuum | $10^{-3}$ m and above (engineered systems) | P, V, T (measurable, bulk) | Laws of Classical Thermodynamics | | **Microscopic** | Statistical | $10^{-9}$ m and below (molecular) | Velocities, positions of molecules | Statistical Thermodynamics (Maxwell-Boltzmann, etc.) | > [!NOTE] > **Why Engineering Thermodynamics is Macroscopic:** > In real engineering practice (boilers, IC engines, turbines, refrigerators), we cannot track $10^{23}$ individual molecules. Instead, we measure **pressure**, **temperature**, and **volume** — quantities that represent the *time-averaged behavior* of billions of molecules. This is the **continuum assumption**, and it is the bedrock of Module 1. ### 1.4 Branches of Classical Thermodynamics - **Equilibrium Thermodynamics (Classical):** Systems at or near equilibrium. - **Non-Equilibrium (Transport) Thermodynamics:** Systems with gradients (heat, mass, momentum transfer). - **Statistical Thermodynamics:** Molecular-level explanation of macroscopic phenomena. > [!TIP] > **Common Student Mistake:** > Do not confuse *thermodynamics* with *heat transfer*. Thermodynamics tells us **whether** a process is possible (the *why*) and the **quantity** of energy involved; heat transfer tells us **how fast** the energy moves (the *rate*). Module 1 introduces only the *why* and the *quantity*. ### 1.5 Dimensions and Units in SI The fundamental dimension set used throughout PCMET403 is: $$ [M],\;[L],\;[t],\;[T] \quad \text{(Mass, Length, Time, Temperature)} $$ The derived SI units are tabulated below. | Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Unit Symbol | |----------|--------|---------|-------------| | Length | L | metre | **m** | | Mass | m | kilogram | **kg** | | Time | t | second | **s** | | Temperature | T | kelvin | **K** | | Force | F | newton (kg·m/s²) | **N** | | Pressure | P | pascal (N/m²) | **Pa** | | Energy | E | joule (N·m) | **J** | | Power | $\dot{W}$ | watt (J/s) | **W** | | Density | $\rho$ | kilogram per cubic metre | **kg/m³** | | Specific volume | v | cubic metre per kilogram | **m³/kg** | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 System, Surroundings, and Boundary - **System:** A defined quantity of matter or region in space chosen for study. - **Surroundings:** Everything external to the system. - **Boundary:** The real or imaginary surface that separates the system from its surroundings. - *Real boundary:* Cylinder walls of an engine. - *Imaginary boundary:* An arbitrary section drawn across a nozzle to define a control volume. ### 2.2 Classification of Systems #### (a) On the basis of mass transfer - **Closed System (Control Mass):** No mass crosses the boundary, but energy (heat, work) may cross. Example: piston-cylinder device with sealed gas. - **Open System (Control Volume):** Both mass and energy cross the boundary. Example: turbine, compressor, nozzle. - **Isolated System:** Neither mass nor energy crosses the boundary. Example: thermos flask (idealized, perfectly insulated and sealed). #### (b) On the basis of interaction with surroundings - **Adiabatic System:** No heat transfer (Q = 0). Boundary is a perfect insulator. - **Diathermic (Permeable) System:** Heat can be exchanged. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-Favourite Question:** "Differentiate between closed and open systems with examples." > Always mention that the *boundary* may move (in a closed system like a piston) but mass cannot cross it. In an open system, the *control volume* is fixed in space, but mass flows across. ### 2.3 Properties of a System A **property** is any observable characteristic of a system that has a definite, unique value at each equilibrium state. Two independent properties fix the state of a pure simple compressible system (Phase Rule). #### Intensive vs. Extensive | Property Type | Definition | Example | Additivity | |---------------|------------|---------|------------| | **Intensive** | Independent of system mass | Temperature T, Pressure P, Density $\rho$ | NOT additive | | **Extensive** | Proportional to system mass | Total Volume V, Total Mass m, Total Energy E | ADDITIVE | | **Specific (per unit mass)** | Extensive ÷ mass | Specific volume $v=V/m$, Specific energy $e=E/m$ | Intensive | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Dividing any extensive property by another extensive property (mass, volume) yields an intensive property. This is why specific volume $v$ is intensive even though total volume V is extensive. ### 2.4 State and State Postulate - **State:** The condition of a system described by its properties. - **State Postulate:** *The state of a simple compressible system is completely specified by two independent intensive properties.* Example: At state 1 of a gas, $P_1 = 200$ kPa and $T_1 = 500$ K. All other properties ($v_1, u_1, h_1, s_1$) are uniquely determined. ### 2.5 Thermodynamic Equilibrium A system is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** when it is simultaneously in: 1. **Mechanical equilibrium** — no unbalanced forces (uniform pressure). 2. **Thermal equilibrium** — no temperature gradients (uniform temperature). 3. **Chemical equilibrium** — no chemical potential gradients (no net reaction). 4. **Phase equilibrium** — no mass transfer between phases. > [!WARNING] > Without equilibrium, properties like P and T cannot be meaningfully defined at a point. Equilibrium is the *prerequisite* for classical thermodynamics to apply. ### 2.6 Process and Cycle - **Process:** A change in the state of a system from initial to final state. - **Cycle:** A process whose final state equals the initial state (returns to the starting point). **Common Process Classifications:** - *Isothermal* (T = constant) - *Isobaric* (P = constant) - *Isochoric* (v = constant) - *Adiabatic* (Q = 0) - *Isentropic* (s = constant — preview of Module 3) - *Polytropic* ($Pv^n = $ constant) ### 2.7 Temperature — The Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics **Statement:** If two bodies are each in thermal equilibrium with a third body, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other. This seemingly simple law is the foundation of *temperature measurement* — without it, thermometers would be meaningless. ### 2.8 Temperature Scales — Conversion Relations The fundamental temperature intervals: $$ T(K) = t(°C) + 273.15 $$ $$ T(R) = T(K) \times \frac{9}{5} $$ $$ t(°F) = t(°C) \times \frac{9}{5} + 32 $$ A temperature *difference* converts as: $$ \Delta T(K) = \Delta t(°C) = \frac{5}{9}\Delta T(R) = \frac{5}{9}\Delta t(°F) $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Apply +273.15 only to a *temperature value*, never to a *temperature difference*. Offsets are not the same as intervals. ### 2.9 Pressure **Pressure** is the normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area: $$ P = \frac{F}{A} \quad [\text{Pa}] $$ #### Atmospheric, Gage, Vacuum, and Absolute Pressures $$ P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} + P_{\text{gage}} $$ $$ P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac}} $$ where $P_{\text{atm}}$ is the local atmospheric pressure (standard value: **101.325 kPa**). #### Pressure Variation in a Static Fluid (Hydrostatics) At a depth $h$ below the free surface of a liquid of density $\rho$: $$ P = P_0 + \rho g h $$ where $P_0$ is the surface pressure and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. ### 2.10 Density and Specific Volume $$ \rho = \frac{m}{V} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}\right] $$ $$ v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{1}{\rho} \quad \left[\frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{kg}}\right] $$ **Specific Gravity (Relative Density):** $$ SG = \frac{\rho_{\text{substance}}}{\rho_{\text{water at } 4°C}} $$ Water at $4°C$ has $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$. ### 2.11 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Relation | Equation | SI Unit | |---|---------------------|----------|---------| | 1 | Pressure definition | $P = F/A$ | Pa | | 2 | Absolute pressure | $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} \pm P_{\text{gage}}$ | Pa | | 3 | Hydrostatic pressure | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Pa | | 4 | Density | $\rho = m/V$ | kg/m³ | | 5 | Specific volume | $v = 1/\rho$ | m³/kg | | 6 | Specific gravity | $SG = \rho_{\text{sub}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$ | — | | 7 | K to °C | $t(°C) = T(K) - 273.15$ | °C | | 8 | K to R | $T(R) = 1.8 \cdot T(K)$ | R | | 9 | Force | $F = ma$ | N | | 10 | Mass–weight | $W = mg$ | N | ### 2.12 Real-World Engineering Relevance | Concept | Engineering Application | |---------|------------------------| | **System classification** | Decides whether to apply 1st Law to closed or open form | | **Intensive vs Extensive properties** | Choosing basis for per-mass or total analysis | | **Pressure measurement (gage vs abs)** | Designing pressure gauges, safety valves, barometers | | **Zeroth Law** | Every thermocouple, RTD, and thermometer | | **State Postulate** | Two-property lookup in steam tables (Module 2) | | **Hydrostatic pressure** | Dam design, hydraulic press, manometer analysis | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Variation with Depth in an Incompressible Fluid **Premise:** Consider an elemental fluid column of cross-section $A$, density $\rho$, vertical extent $dz$, at depth $z$ below the free surface. **Step 1 — Force balance on the elemental slice.** The forces acting vertically are: - Pressure force from below acting upward: $P(z)\,A$. - Pressure force from above acting downward: $P(z+dz)\,A$. - Weight of the element acting downward: $W = (\rho A\,dz)\,g$. **Step 2 — Apply mechanical equilibrium** (no acceleration, $\sum F_z = 0$): $$ P(z)\,A - P(z+dz)\,A - \rho A g\,dz = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Divide through by $A$ and rearrange:** $$ P(z) - P(z+dz) = \rho g\,dz $$ **Step 4 — Rewrite using the differential operator:** $$ dP = P(z+dz) - P(z) = -\rho g\,dz $$ **Step 5 — Integrate from the free surface (where $z=0$, $P=P_0$) to depth $h$:** $$ \int_{P_0}^{P(h)} dP = -\int_{0}^{h} \rho g\,dz $$ **Step 6 — Assume incompressible fluid (constant $\rho$):** $$ P(h) - P_0 = -\rho g h $$ **Final boxed result:** $$ \boxed{P(h) = P_0 + \rho g h} $$ **Interpretation:** Pressure *increases* linearly with depth; the slope is the *specific weight* $\gamma = \rho g$ (units: N/m³). --- ### 3.2 Derivation: The Ideal-Gas "Combined" Law (Preview to Module 2) The three classical gas laws combine to give: $$ \frac{P_1 v_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 v_2}{T_2} = R \quad [\text{specific gas constant}] $$ We will use this in Module 2; the verification is simple: - Boyle's Law (T const): $Pv = $ const - Charles's Law (P const): $v/T = $ const - Gay-Lussac's Law (v const): $P/T = $ const Combining the three gives the **combined gas law** above, with $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) divided by molar mass. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example **Problem (Typical KTU 3-mark level):** A vertical cylindrical tank of diameter $D = 2$ m contains water to a depth of 5 m. The space above the water is pressurized air at $P_0 = 120$ kPa (gage). Find: (a) The gage pressure at the bottom of the tank. (b) The absolute pressure at the bottom, if local atmospheric pressure is 101.3 kPa. **Solution:** **(a)** Apply the hydrostatic relation: $$ P_{\text{bottom,gage}} = P_0 + \rho g h $$ $$ P_{\text{bottom,gage}} = 120\,000 + (1000)(9.81)(5) $$ $$ P_{\text{bottom,gage}} = 120\,000 + 49\,050 = 169\,050\ \text{Pa} $$ **(b)** Convert gage to absolute: $$ P_{\text{bottom,abs}} = P_{\text{bottom,gage}} + P_{\text{atm}} $$ $$ P_{\text{bottom,abs}} = 169.05 + 101.3 = 270.35\ \text{kPa} $$ **Final Answers:** $$ P_{\text{bottom,gage}} = \mathbf{169.05\ \text{kPa}}, \qquad P_{\text{bottom,abs}} = \mathbf{270.35\ \text{kPa}} $$ --- ### 3.4 Conversion Worked Example — Temperature **Problem:** The temperature of a furnace is reported as 1800°F. Express it in K and in °C. **Step 1:** Convert °F → °C: $$ t(°C) = \frac{5}{9}\bigl[t(°F) - 32\bigr] = \frac{5}{9}(1800-32) = \frac{5}{9}(1768) $$ $$ t(°C) = 982.22\ °C $$ **Step 2:** Convert °C → K: $$ T(K) = t(°C) + 273.15 = 982.22 + 273.15 $$ $$ T(K) = 1255.37\ \text{K} \approx \mathbf{1255\ K} $$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Thermodynamic System Classification — Concept Map ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["THERMODYNAMIC SYSTEM"] nodeB["CLOSED SYSTEM
Control Mass
No mass crosses boundary"] nodeC["OPEN SYSTEM
Control Volume
Mass and energy cross"] nodeD["ISOLATED SYSTEM
No mass, no energy, no work"] nodeE["ADIABATIC
Q = 0"] nodeF["DIATHERMIC
Q is allowed"] nodeG["Examples: Piston-Cylinder,
Pressure Cooker, Rigid Tank"] nodeH["Examples: Turbine, Compressor,
Nozzle, Heat Exchanger"] nodeI["Examples: Thermos Flask
(Idealized)"] nodeJ["Properties of System"] nodeK["INTENSIVE
T, P, density"] nodeL["EXTENSIVE
m, V, E"] nodeM["SPECIFIC
v, e, h (per kg)"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeH nodeD --> nodeI nodeB --> nodeG nodeA --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK nodeJ --> nodeL nodeJ --> nodeM ``` > [!NOTE] > This concept map is the single most-tested image in KTU Module 1. Be able to draw and explain each branch. --- ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Thermodynamic Analysis ```mermaid graph TD stepA["1. PROBLEM STATEMENT
Identify real device"] stepB["2. SCHEMATIC DRAWING
Sketch with boundary"] stepC["3. SYSTEM SELECTION
Closed / Open / Isolated"] stepD["4. IDENTIFY PROPERTIES
State 1, State 2"] stepE["5. STATE POSTULATE CHECK
Two independent intensive
properties fix state"] stepF["6. APPLY THERMODYNAMIC LAWS
Module 2-4"] stepG["7. INTERPRET RESULT
Physical sanity check"] stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG ``` --- ### 4.3 Thermodynamic Equilibrium — Subsystem Requirements ```mermaid graph LR node1["THERMODYNAMIC
EQUILIBRIUM"] node2["MECHANICAL
Uniform Pressure"] node3["THERMAL
Uniform Temperature"] node4["CHEMICAL
No Reaction"] node5["PHASE
No Mass Transfer"] node1 --> node2 node1 --> node3 node1 --> node4 node1 --> node5 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define a thermodynamic system. Distinguish clearly between an *open system*, a *closed system*, and an *isolated system*, giving one engineering example of each. **Model Answer:** A *thermodynamic system* is a precisely defined quantity of matter or a region in space chosen for the purpose of thermodynamic analysis, separated from the rest of the universe (the *surroundings*) by a real or imaginary **boundary**. | System Type | Mass Transfer | Energy Transfer | Example | |-------------|---------------|-----------------|---------| | **Closed (Control Mass)** | None | Allowed | Gas sealed in a piston-cylinder | | **Open (Control Volume)** | Allowed | Allowed | Steam flowing through a turbine | | **Isolated** | None | None | A perfectly insulated, sealed rigid container (idealized) | --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** State the **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics**. Why is it called the "Zeroth" law and not the "Fourth"? **Model Answer:** **Statement:** If two bodies are each in thermal equilibrium with a third body, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other. **Why "Zeroth":** Historically, the First and Second Laws had already been numbered. The law underlying the very concept of *temperature* (which is needed to even state the 1st and 2nd laws) was recognized later. To preserve the numbering of the existing laws, it was placed *before* the First and given the index **zero**. **Significance:** It is the foundation of **temperature measurement** — a thermometer (the "third body") allows us to conclude that two bodies at the same thermometer reading are in thermal equilibrium. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** A cylindrical tank of diameter 1.5 m and height 4 m is filled with oil of specific gravity 0.85 up to a height of 3 m. The remaining space is pressurized air at a gage pressure of 80 kPa. Determine: - (i) The gage pressure at the bottom of the tank. [7 Marks] - (ii) The total weight of oil in the tank and the absolute pressure at the tank bottom, given atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **(a)(i)** Use the hydrostatic pressure equation: $$ P_{\text{bottom,gage}} = P_0 + \rho g h $$ Given: - $P_0 = 80$ kPa = 80,000 Pa - $SG = 0.85 \Rightarrow \rho = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850\ \text{kg/m}^3$ - $h = 3$ m - $g = 9.81$ m/s² Substitute: $$ P_{\text{bottom,gage}} = 80{,}000 + (850)(9.81)(3) $$ $$ P_{\text{bottom,gage}} = 80{,}000 + 25{,}015.5 = 105{,}015.5\ \text{Pa} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{\text{bottom,gage}} \approx 105.02\ \text{kPa}} $$ **(a)(ii)** Volume of oil: $$ V_{\text{oil}} = A \cdot h = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.5)^2 \cdot 3 = \frac{\pi}{4}(2.25)(3) = 5.301\ \text{m}^3 $$ Mass of oil: $$ m_{\text{oil}} = \rho \cdot V = 850 \times 5.301 = 4505.9\ \text{kg} $$ Weight of oil: $$ W = m g = 4505.9 \times 9.81 = 44{,}202.9\ \text{N} \approx \boxed{44.2\ \text{kN}} $$ Absolute pressure at bottom: $$ P_{\text{bottom,abs}} = P_{\text{bottom,gage}} + P_{\text{atm}} = 105.02 + 101.325 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{\text{bottom,abs}} \approx 206.34\ \text{kPa}} $$ --- **(b)** With reference to **Engineering Thermodynamics**: - (i) Differentiate between **intensive** and **extensive** properties. State whether *specific volume*, *total volume*, *pressure*, and *kinetic energy of a system* are intensive or extensive. [7 Marks] - (ii) Explain the **State Postulate** and its significance. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **(b)(i)** | Property | Definition | Mass Dependency | |----------|------------|-----------------| | **Intensive** | Independent of system size / mass | Remains same if system is divided | | **Extensive** | Proportional to system size / mass | Adds up when systems are combined | **Classification:** - *Specific volume* $v = V/m$ → **Intensive** (per unit mass). - *Total volume* $V$ → **Extensive** (depends on amount). - *Pressure* $P$ → **Intensive** (force per unit area, independent of mass). - *Kinetic energy of a system* $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}m V^2$ → **Extensive** (depends on mass). **(b)(ii) State Postulate:** *The state of a simple compressible system is completely specified by **two independent intensive properties**.* **Significance:** - Reduces a complex real-world problem to a 2D lookup. - Forms the basis of **property tables** (steam tables, refrigerant tables) — once you know, say, $P$ and $T$, all other properties ($v$, $u$, $h$, $s$) are uniquely determined. - Eliminates the need to track every microscopic variable. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** A room measures $6\ \text{m} \times 5\ \text{m} \times 3.5\ \text{m}$ and is initially at atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa) and 20 °C. The room is heated until the air temperature reaches 35 °C, while pressure is maintained atmospheric by a small vent. Treating air as an ideal gas, determine: - (i) The mass of air in the room (use $R = 0.287\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$). [4 Marks] - (ii) The new specific volume after heating. [3 Marks] **Model Solution:** **(a)(i)** Volume of room: $$ V = 6 \times 5 \times 3.5 = 105\ \text{m}^3 $$ Initial temperature: $$ T_1 = 20 + 273.15 = 293.15\ \text{K} $$ Apply ideal-gas relation: $$ P V = m R T \Rightarrow m = \frac{PV}{RT_1} = \frac{101.325 \times 10^3 \times 105}{287 \times 293.15} $$ $$ m = \frac{10{,}639{,}125}{84{,}134.05} \approx \boxed{126.47\ \text{kg}} $$ **(a)(ii)** Since pressure is constant (vent open) and the room is rigid, $V$ is constant: $$ v_1 = v_2 = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{105}{126.47} \approx \boxed{0.830\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}} $$ (Alternative check using $v_1 = RT_1/P$: gives the same value.) --- **(b)** Discuss in detail: - (i) The **macroscopic (classical)** versus **microscopic (statistical)** approach to thermodynamics, with **two advantages** of each. [7 Marks] - (ii) The different types of thermodynamic equilibrium a system must satisfy to be in complete equilibrium. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **(b)(i)** | Aspect | Macroscopic Approach | Microscopic Approach | |--------|----------------------|----------------------| | **Length scale** | Continuum ($\geq 10^{-3}$ m) | Molecular ($\sim 10^{-9}$ m) | | **Variables** | P, V, T (averaged) | Velocities, positions | | **Mathematical tool** | Calculus, classical laws | Statistics, probability | | **Data required** | Few bulk measurements | Detailed molecular data | | **Output** | Useful engineering properties (u, h, s) | Molecular interpretation of bulk behavior | **Macroscopic Advantages:** 1. Far fewer variables — practical for engineering design. 2. Requires only bulk measurements (e.g., pressure gauge, thermometer) — no need for molecular data. **Microscopic Advantages:** 1. Explains *why* macroscopic laws exist (e.g., why entropy increases). 2. Provides microscopic insights into specific heats, transport coefficients, phase behavior. **(b)(ii) Four Types of Thermodynamic Equilibrium:** 1. **Mechanical Equilibrium:** No unbalanced forces inside the system → uniform pressure throughout. 2. **Thermal Equilibrium:** No temperature gradients → uniform temperature. 3. **Chemical Equilibrium:** No tendency for chemical reaction or diffusion → uniform chemical potential. 4. **Phase Equilibrium:** No net mass transfer between coexisting phases (e.g., liquid–vapor interface). A system is in **complete thermodynamic equilibrium** only when all four are simultaneously satisfied. *If any one is violated, the system is in a non-equilibrium state and classical thermodynamic properties are not uniquely defined.* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students lose marks in Module 1 questions:** > > 1. **Gage vs Absolute Pressure:** Mixing up the two. KTU examiners explicitly *trap* students who report a negative pressure or omit the unit "gage" / "abs." Always state *which one* you are computing. > 2. **Temperature Offsets:** Adding 273 to a temperature *difference* — wrong. Offsets apply to *values*, not *intervals*. > 3. **Intensive/Extensive Confusion:** Writing "kinetic energy is intensive" or "pressure is extensive" — instant 1-mark cut per error. > 4. **System Definition:** Stating "a system is everything we study" without mentioning the **boundary** or **surroundings** — partial-mark only. > 5. **Hydrostatic Equation:** Forgetting the surface pressure $P_0$. If a tank is vented to atmosphere, $P_0 = P_{\text{atm}}$; if sealed, $P_0$ is whatever the trapped gas exerts. > 6. **Unit Mismatch:** Using bar instead of Pa without conversion ($1\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{Pa}$). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Thermodynamics** = science of energy, heat, work, and their conversions. - [ ] **Engineering Thermodynamics** = macroscopic, applied to engines, refrigerators, etc. - [ ] **Macroscopic approach** uses continuum properties (P, V, T); **Microscopic** uses molecular statistics. - [ ] **System** = study region; **Surroundings** = everything else; **Boundary** = the divider. - [ ] **Closed system:** no mass crosses, energy may. **Open system:** both cross. **Isolated:** neither crosses. - [ ] **Intensive** properties are mass-independent; **Extensive** are mass-proportional; **Specific** = extensive ÷ mass. - [ ] **State Postulate:** Two independent intensive properties fully specify the state. - [ ] **Thermodynamic equilibrium** requires mechanical + thermal + chemical + phase balance. - [ ] **Process** = state change; **Cycle** = process returning to the starting state. - [ ] **Zeroth Law** underlies temperature measurement and thermometer working. - [ ] **Pressure relations:** $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} \pm P_{\text{gage}}$, $P(h) = P_0 + \rho g h$. - [ ] **Hydrostatic pressure** increases linearly with depth with slope = specific weight $\rho g$. - [ ] **Density $\rho = m/V$**; **Specific volume $v = 1/\rho$**; **SG = ratio to water at 4 °C**. - [ ] **Temperature conversions:** $T(K) = t(°C) + 273.15$; $T(R) = 1.8 \cdot T(K)$. - [ ] **SI base units** for KTU: kg, m, s, K, with derived units N, J, Pa, W. - [ ] Always write absolute values in Kelvin and pressures in Pa (or consistent kPa) — examiners penalize mixed unit sets. ---

topics.title: Application Areas of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application Areas of Thermodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Engineering Thermodynamics** is the science that studies energy, its transformations, and its ability to do work. The application areas of thermodynamics encompass every branch of engineering where energy conversion, heat transfer work output, and property changes of matter are central to system design and performance evaluation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCMET403 - Module 1):**
> *Application areas of thermodynamics* covers the diverse engineering domains where thermodynamic principles govern the design, analysis, and optimization of systems involving energy and its conversion.

## 1.2 The Six Major Engineering Domains

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the application areas of thermodynamics are broadly classified into the following six engineering domains:

| S.No | Domain | Core Thermodynamic Principle |
|------|--------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Power Generation Engineering** | Heat-to-Work conversion (Rankine, Brayton cycles) |
| 2 | **Propulsion Engineering** | Jet engines, Rockets, IC Engines |
| 3 | **Refrigeration & Cryogenics** | Work-to-Heat transfer (Vapor compression, Linde-Hampson) |
| 4 | **Compressor & Pump Technology** | Work input for fluid pressurization |
| 5 | **Chemical Process Engineering** | Phase change, chemical reaction equilibria |
| 6 | **Direct Energy Conversion** | Fuel cells, MHD, Solar-PV, Thermoelectric |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Thermodynamics as the "Banking System of Energy"**
> Just as a bank manages money (deposits, withdrawals, transfers, balances), thermodynamics manages **energy**. It tracks:
> - **Deposits** (heat input `Q_in` from combustion)
> - **Withdrawals** (work output `W_out` or heat rejected `Q_out`)
> - **Transfers** (energy crossing the system boundary)
> - **Balances** (First Law: Energy In = Energy Out + Storage)

> **Real-World Analogy:** Your household refrigerator is a thermodynamic device. It takes electrical energy (`W_in`) and "pumps" heat (`Q_L`) from the cold interior (preserving food) and rejects it (`Q_H`) into your kitchen. Without the laws of thermodynamics, this process would be impossible to design or predict.

## 1.4 Why Study Application Areas?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Every engineer**, whether mechanical, civil, electrical, chemical, or aerospace, **uses thermodynamic principles** routinely.
> - **Approximately 80% of world energy** is produced and consumed via thermodynamic systems.
> - Modern problems like **climate change, energy sustainability, and engine efficiency** all demand deep thermodynamic insight.
> - The **First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics** are the foundational pillars for nearly every energy device in existence.
</!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Application Analysis

### 2.1.1 Power Generation Engineering (Steam Power Plants)

**Operating Principle:** Converts the chemical energy of fuel (coal, natural gas, biomass) into electrical energy via the **Rankine Cycle**.

**Key Components:**
- **Boiler** (Steam Generator) — Adds heat at high pressure
- **Turbine** — Converts steam enthalpy into rotational mechanical work
- **Condenser** — Rejects waste heat to cooling medium
- **Pump** — Pressurizes liquid condensate

**Cycle Used:** Rankine Cycle (subsonic, two-phase fluid)
**Typical Efficiency:** **30% – 42%** for modern subcritical plants; up to **45%+** for supercritical and ultra-supercritical plants.

### 2.1.2 Gas Turbine Power Plants

**Operating Principle:** Atmospheric air is compressed, heated at constant pressure, and expanded through a turbine to produce work.

**Cycle Used:** Brayton Cycle (open or closed)
**Components:** Compressor → Combustion Chamber → Turbine
**Efficiency Range:** **35% – 45%** (simple cycle); up to **60%** with combined cycle (Brayton + Rankine).

### 2.1.3 Internal Combustion (IC) Engines

**Types:**
- **Spark Ignition (SI):** Otto Cycle — petrol/gasoline engines
- **Compression Ignition (CI):** Diesel Cycle — diesel engines

**Thermal Efficiency:**
- Otto Cycle: $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$
- Diesel Cycle: $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$

Where $r$ is the **compression ratio** and $\rho$ is the **cut-off ratio**.

### 2.1.4 Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning

**Principle:** Work input is used to transfer heat from a low-temperature reservoir to a high-temperature reservoir — this is the **reverse of a heat engine** (Refrigerator / Heat Pump).

**Common Cycles:**
- **Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)** — Most common
- **Vapor Absorption Cycle** — Uses heat instead of work
- **Gas Refrigeration Cycle** — Bell-Coleman, for aircraft
- **Cascade Refrigeration** — For very low temperatures

**Coefficient of Performance (COP):**
- Refrigerator: $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$
- Heat Pump: $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A refrigerator and a heat pump are *thermodynamically identical* devices; they only differ in the *useful output* (cooling vs. heating).

### 2.1.5 Jet Propulsion and Aerospace

**Types of Jet Engines:**
- **Turbojet** — Continuous combustion, subsonic/supersonic
- **Turbofan** — Bypass airflow for efficiency (commercial aviation)
- **Turboprop** — Turbine drives a propeller
- **Ramjet** — No compressor, supersonic only (Mach > 1)
- **Scramjet** — Supersonic Combustion Ramjet (Mach > 5)
- **Rocket** — Carries oxidizer; works in vacuum

**Thrust Equation:** $F = \dot{m} (V_e - V_a) + (p_e - p_a) A_e$

### 2.1.6 Direct Energy Conversion Systems

These bypass the conventional mechanical path:

| System | Conversion | Efficiency |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| **Fuel Cell** | Chemical → Electrical | 40% – 60% |
| **MHD Generator** | Thermal → Electrical (via plasma) | 15% – 25% |
| **Photovoltaic (PV)** | Solar → Electrical | 15% – 22% |
| **Thermoelectric (Peltier/Seebeck)** | Heat ↔ Electrical | 5% – 8% |
| **Solar Thermal** | Solar → Heat → Electrical | 25% – 30% |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU 2024 Examinations on this Topic**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{First Law (Closed System):} \quad & Q - W = \Delta U \\
\text{First Law (Open System):} \quad & Q - W = \Delta h + \dfrac{\Delta V^2}{2} + g\Delta z \\
\text{Carnot Efficiency:} \quad & \eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H} \\
\text{COP of Refrigerator:} \quad & \text{COP}_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \\
\text{COP of Heat Pump:} \quad & \text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} \\
\text{Otto Efficiency:} \quad & \eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \\
\text{Diesel Efficiency:} \quad & \eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)} \\
\text{Brayton Efficiency:} \quad & \eta_{brayton} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} \\
\text{Specific Work (Turbine):} \quad & w_t = h_1 - h_2 \\
\text{Specific Work (Compressor):} \quad & w_c = h_2 - h_1 \\
\text{Work Ratio (Brayton):} \quad & r_w = \dfrac{w_{turbine}}{w_{compressor}} \\
\text{Thrust:} \quad & F = \dot{m} (V_e - V_a) + (p_e - p_a) A_e
\end{aligned}
$$

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | KTU Exam Tip |
|----------|--------|---------|---------------|
| Pressure | $P$ | Pa (N/m²) | Use kPa in most problems |
| Temperature | $T$ | K (Kelvin) | Always convert °C → K |
| Specific Enthalpy | $h$ | kJ/kg | Tabulated in steam tables |
| Specific Entropy | $s$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Constant for isentropic |
| Compression Ratio | $r$ | — | $V_1/V_2$ (dimensionless) |
| Cut-off Ratio | $\rho$ | — | $V_3/V_2$ (dimensionless) |
| Pressure Ratio | $r_p$ | — | $P_2/P_1$ (dimensionless) |
| Heat | $Q$ | kJ | Energy crossing boundary |
| Work | $W$ | kJ | Energy leaving as work |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** | Use per kg or per kmol |
| Specific Heat Ratio | $\gamma = C_p/C_v$ | — | Air ≈ **1.4** |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Mechanical Engineers** design engines, turbines, and HVAC systems using thermodynamic cycles.
> - **Chemical Engineers** apply the **First and Second Laws** to reactor design, distillation columns, and phase equilibria.
> - **Civil Engineers** apply thermodynamics in **HVAC design** for buildings and **geothermal energy**.
> - **Electrical Engineers** use thermodynamic principles in the **thermal management of transformers, generators, and power electronics**.
> - **Aerospace Engineers** use thermodynamics for **rocket propulsion and atmospheric re-entry thermal protection**.
> - **Environmental Engineers** apply the **Second Law (entropy)** to **pollution control and waste-heat recovery**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$

This is one of the **most frequently tested derivations** in KTU University Examinations for Module 1.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a **refrigerator** that absorbs heat $Q_L$ from a cold reservoir at $T_L$ and rejects heat $Q_H$ to a hot reservoir at $T_H$, with work input $W$.

By the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (Energy Balance on the cyclic device):

$$
Q_H = Q_L + W_{in}
$$

### 3.1.2 COP of Refrigerator

By KTU convention, the **useful effect** of a refrigerator is the heat removed from the cold space $Q_L$. So:

$$
\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{in}}
$$

Substituting $W_{in} = Q_H - Q_L$:

$$
\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}
$$

### 3.1.3 COP of Heat Pump

For a heat pump, the **useful effect** is the heat delivered to the hot space $Q_H$. The work input is the same $W_{in}$:

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{in}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}
$$

### 3.1.4 Establishing the Relation

Divide the two COPs:

$$
\dfrac{\text{COP}_{HP}}{\text{COP}_R} = \dfrac{Q_H / W_{in}}{Q_L / W_{in}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R \cdot \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \text{COP}_R \cdot \dfrac{Q_L + W_{in}}{Q_L} = \text{COP}_R \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{W_{in}}{Q_L}\right)
$$

Since $\dfrac{W_{in}}{Q_L} = \dfrac{1}{\text{COP}_R}$:

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The COP of a heat pump is always **exactly 1 unit greater** than the COP of a refrigerator using the same reservoirs.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Otto vs Diesel vs Dual vs Brayton

| Feature | **Otto Cycle** | **Diesel Cycle** | **Dual Cycle** | **Brayton Cycle** |
|---------|----------------|------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Application** | SI Engines (Petrol) | CI Engines (Diesel) | Modern CI Engines | Gas Turbines |
| **Heat Addition** | Constant Volume | Constant Pressure | Part V + Part P | Constant Pressure |
| **Heat Rejection** | Constant Volume | Constant Volume | Constant Volume | Constant Pressure |
| **Compression Ratio $r$** | 8 – 12 | 14 – 22 | 14 – 20 | 4 – 12 (Pressure Ratio) |
| **Efficiency Formula** | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}\dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho-1)}$ | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\beta \gamma - 1}{(\beta-1) \gamma}$ | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ |
| **Working Fluid** | Air (ideal) | Air (ideal) | Air (ideal) | Air (ideal) |
| **Processes** | 1-2: Isentropic Comp., 2-3: Const V Heat Add, 3-4: Isentropic Exp., 4-1: Const V Heat Rej. | 1-2: Isentropic Comp., 2-3: Const P Heat Add, 3-4: Isentropic Exp., 4-1: Const V Heat Rej. | 1-2: Isentropic Comp., 2-3: Const V Heat Add, 3-4: Const P Heat Add, 4-5: Isentropic Exp., 5-1: Const V Heat Rej. | 1-2: Isentropic Comp., 2-3: Const P Heat Add, 3-4: Isentropic Exp., 4-1: Const P Heat Rej. |

Where:
- $r$ = compression ratio $V_1/V_2$
- $\rho$ = cut-off ratio $V_3/V_2$
- $\beta$ = pressure ratio $P_3/P_2$ (Dual)
- $r_p$ = pressure ratio $P_2/P_1$ (Brayton)

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Application Classification Logic

To determine the **correct thermodynamic application** for a given KTU problem, follow this decision tree:

**Step 1:** Identify the **energy conversion direction**.
- Heat → Work ⇒ **Power Plant / Heat Engine**
- Work → Heat (cold side) ⇒ **Refrigerator**
- Work → Heat (hot side) ⇒ **Heat Pump**

**Step 2:** Identify the **working fluid state**.
- Two-phase (liquid + vapor) ⇒ **Rankine / Vapor Compression**
- Gas phase only ⇒ **Brayton / Otto / Diesel**

**Step 3:** Identify the **process of heat addition**.
- Constant Volume ⇒ **Otto Cycle**
- Constant Pressure ⇒ **Brayton / Diesel**
- Combination ⇒ **Dual Cycle**

**Step 4:** Identify the **process of heat rejection**.
- Constant Volume ⇒ **Otto / Diesel / Dual**
- Constant Pressure ⇒ **Brayton / Rankine**

**Step 5:** Apply the **First Law** (energy balance) and **Second Law** (entropy generation) to derive the cycle efficiency.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*List any six major application areas of Engineering Thermodynamics with one example for each.*

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Power Generation** — Steam power plants (Rankine Cycle) convert heat to electricity.
2. **Internal Combustion Engines** — Petrol engines (Otto Cycle), Diesel engines (Diesel Cycle).
3. **Refrigeration & Air Conditioning** — Household refrigerators (Vapor Compression Cycle).
4. **Gas Turbines & Jet Propulsion** — Aircraft turbojet engines (Brayton Cycle).
5. **Compressors & Pumps** — Industrial air compressors (used in gas turbine, refrigeration).
6. **Direct Energy Conversion** — Fuel cells convert chemical energy directly to electricity.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Explain the difference between a heat engine, a refrigerator, and a heat pump with reference to the First Law of Thermodynamics.*

**Model Answer:**

- **Heat Engine:** Operates between a hot source ($T_H$) and cold sink ($T_L$); converts part of $Q_H$ into work $W$, rejecting $Q_L$. Energy balance: $W = Q_H - Q_L$.
- **Refrigerator:** Uses work input $W$ to extract heat $Q_L$ from a cold space and reject $Q_H$ to the surroundings. Energy balance: $Q_H = Q_L + W$.
- **Heat Pump:** Thermodynamically identical to a refrigerator; the useful output is $Q_H$ delivered to the hot space, not $Q_L$ extracted.
- **Relation:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] *Explain the working principle of a Rankine Cycle steam power plant. Sketch the cycle on a T-s diagram and state the energy balance equations for each component. List the four major methods used to improve the Rankine Cycle efficiency.*

**(b)** [7 Marks] *In an air-standard Otto cycle, the compression ratio is 9. The conditions at the beginning of compression are 100 kPa and 27 °C. The maximum temperature in the cycle is 2000 K. Determine: (i) the temperature and pressure at all four state points, (ii) the heat supplied, (iii) the heat rejected, and (iv) the thermal efficiency. Take $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, and $\gamma = 1.4$.*

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) [7 Marks]

**Working Principle:** The Rankine cycle is the ideal cycle for vapor power plants. Liquid water is pumped to high pressure, vaporized in a boiler, expanded in a turbine, and condensed back to liquid in a condenser.

**Four Components and Energy Balances:**

| Component | Process | Energy Equation (per kg) |
|-----------|---------|---------------------------|
| Pump 1→2 | Isentropic Compression | $w_P = h_2 - h_1 = v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ |
| Boiler 2→3 | Constant Pressure Heat Addition | $q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ |
| Turbine 3→4 | Isentropic Expansion | $w_T = h_3 - h_4$ |
| Condenser 4→1 | Constant Pressure Heat Rejection | $q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$ |

**Net Work:** $w_{net} = w_T - w_P$

**Thermal Efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$

**T-s Diagram:** (Plot with saturation dome; processes: 1-2 nearly vertical (pump), 2-3 horizontal at high T (boiler), 3-4 nearly vertical (turbine), 4-1 horizontal at low T (condenser)).

**Four Efficiency-Improvement Methods:**
1. **Increasing Boiler Pressure** (with superheating)
2. **Superheating the Steam** to higher temperatures
3. **Reheating the Steam** through multiple turbine stages
4. **Regenerative Feedwater Heating** using extracted steam

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $r = 9$, $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_3 = 2000$ K.

**State 1:** $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K.

**State 2 (after isentropic compression 1→2):**
$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 300 \cdot 9^{0.4}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
9^{0.4} &= e^{0.4 \cdot \ln 9} = e^{0.4 \cdot 2.1972} = e^{0.8789} \approx 2.408 \\
T_2 &= 300 \times 2.408 = 722.4 \text{ K} \\
P_2 &= P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = 100 \cdot 9^{1.4} = 100 \times 21.67 = 2167 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**State 3 (constant volume heat addition):** $T_3 = 2000$ K, $P_3 = P_2 \cdot \dfrac{T_3}{T_2} = 2167 \cdot \dfrac{2000}{722.4} \approx 6000$ kPa.

**State 4 (isentropic expansion 3→4):**
$$
T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1} = 2000 / 2.408 \approx 830.6 \text{ K}
$$
$$
P_4 = P_3 / r^{\gamma} = 6000 / 21.67 \approx 277 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(ii) Heat Supplied:**
$$
q_{in} = C_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.718 \times (2000 - 722.4) = 917.07 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected:**
$$
q_{out} = C_v (T_4 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (830.6 - 300) = 380.97 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**(iv) Thermal Efficiency:**
$$
\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{2.408} = 0.5847 = 58.47\%
$$

Cross-check: $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{q_{out}}{q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{380.97}{917.07} = 0.5847$ ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] *Explain the working principle of a Vapor Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for its Coefficient of Performance (COP) in terms of enthalpies at the four key state points.*

**(b)** [7 Marks] *A refrigeration system operates between temperature limits of $-10$ °C and 40 °C. The system has a COP of 3.0. Determine: (i) the COP of a Carnot refrigerator operating between the same temperature limits, (ii) the relative COP (ratio of actual to Carnot COP), and (iii) the work input per kW of refrigeration effect.*

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) [7 Marks]

**Working Principle:** VCRS uses a circulating refrigerant (R-134a, R-12, ammonia) that undergoes phase change. Work input to the compressor raises the refrigerant pressure, allowing it to reject heat at ambient temperature in the condenser. After throttling, the low-pressure liquid evaporates by absorbing heat from the cold space.

**Block Diagram & State Points:**
1 → 2: Compression in compressor (low pressure vapor → high pressure vapor)
2 → 3: Condensation (high pressure vapor → high pressure liquid, rejects $Q_H$)
3 → 4: Throttling / Expansion (high pressure liquid → low pressure two-phase mixture)
4 → 1: Evaporation (low pressure liquid → low pressure vapor, absorbs $Q_L$)

**Derivation of COP:**

Energy absorbed in evaporator (Refrigerating Effect per kg):
$$
\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4
$$

Compressor work input per kg:
$$
W_{in} = h_2 - h_1
$$

Heat rejected in condenser:
$$
Q_H = h_2 - h_3
$$

Coefficient of Performance:
$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}}
$$

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $T_L = -10 + 273 = 263$ K, $T_H = 40 + 273 = 313$ K, actual $\text{COP} = 3.0$.

**(i) Carnot COP:**
$$
\text{COP}_{carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{263}{313 - 263} = \dfrac{263}{50} = 5.26
$$

**(ii) Relative COP:**
$$
\text{Relative COP} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{actual}}{\text{COP}_{carnot}} = \dfrac{3.0}{5.26} = 0.570 = 57.0\%
$$

**(iii) Work input per kW of refrigeration:**

For a refrigerating effect of 1 kW (i.e., $Q_L = 1$ kW):
$$
W_{in} = \dfrac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_{actual}} = \dfrac{1}{3.0} = 0.3333 \text{ kW}
$$

For a refrigerating effect of 1 kW (i.e., 1 kJ/s):
$$
W_{in} = \dfrac{1}{3.0} = 0.333 \text{ kJ/s} = 0.333 \text{ kW (electrical)}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** before applying Carnot efficiency or COP — this is the **#1 mark killer** in KTU papers.
> 2. **Confusing COP of Refrigerator and Heat Pump** — examiners **WILL deduct 1 mark** if you interchange them.
> 3. **In the Otto cycle**, students often forget that **heat addition AND heat rejection** both occur at **constant volume** (not pressure).
> 4. **In the Brayton cycle**, the **heat addition and rejection** are at **constant pressure**, not constant volume.
> 5. **State points must be labeled 1, 2, 3, 4** in proper order on the P-v and T-s diagrams — missing arrows or wrong direction **costs 1 mark**.
> 6. **Not specifying "per kg"** for work and heat in cycles is a frequent **half-mark loss**.
> 7. **In COP problems**, always check if the answer is for refrigerator or heat pump — relation is $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU PCMET403 Module 1: Application Areas**

- **Thermodynamics is universal** — applied in **every branch of engineering** (ME, CE, EE, CH, AE).
- **Six major application domains:** Power Generation, Propulsion, Refrigeration, Compressors, Chemical, Direct Energy Conversion.
- **Power plants** use **Rankine (steam)** or **Brayton (gas)** cycles.
- **IC engines** use **Otto (SI/petrol)** and **Diesel (CI)** cycles.
- **Refrigerators and Heat Pumps** are **thermodynamically identical** devices; $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$.
- **Carnot Efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ — sets the **upper limit** for all heat engines.
- **Carnot COP:** $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ — sets the **upper limit** for refrigerators.
- **Jet engines** are essentially **open-cycle gas turbines** (Brayton cycle).
- **Rockets** are the **only propulsion devices** that work in **vacuum** (carry their own oxidizer).
- **Fuel cells** convert chemical energy directly to electrical energy, **bypassing the heat step** (higher efficiency potential).
- **Second Law of Thermodynamics** is the reason no engine can be **100% efficient** and no refrigerator can have **infinite COP**.
- **Always convert °C to K** for any efficiency or COP calculation — **mandatory** for KTU exams.
- **Key constants:** $\gamma_{air} = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R_{air} = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.
- **Standard atmospheric pressure** = **101.325 kPa** (often approximated as 100 kPa in textbook problems).
- **Renewable energy systems** (solar, wind, geothermal) are all subject to **First and Second Law** analysis.
</!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Macroscopic and Microscopic Viewpoints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Macroscopic and Microscopic Viewpoints ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Two Viewpoints of Thermodynamics Thermodynamics, as a branch of physics and engineering, studies **energy**, **heat**, **work**, and the **properties of matter**. The subject can be approached from two fundamentally different — yet complementary — perspectives. Every KTU board question on Module 1 is essentially asking you to reason from one (or both) of these viewpoints. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Macroscopic Viewpoint (Classical / Engineering Thermodynamics):** > Deals with the system as a **whole**. It is concerned with the *gross* or *bulk* behaviour of matter. Quantities studied (pressure $P$, temperature $T$, volume $V$, mass $m$) are *average effects* over an enormous number of molecules. No assumption is made about the discrete molecular structure of matter. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Microscopic Viewpoint (Statistical / Molecular Thermodynamics):** > Deals with the system as an **assembly of discrete molecules**. It assumes matter is made up of a vast number of particles that obey the laws of mechanics (classical or quantum). The bulk behaviour is derived by *averaging* the properties of individual molecules. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Stadium" Analogy) > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a packed cricket stadium (e.g., a T20 match in Kochi).** > > **Macroscopic approach:** You stand outside the stadium and observe the *overall* noise level, the *total* capacity (say, 55,000 people), and the *collective* behaviour of the crowd (a wave going around). You never see individual people. You only see averaged, measurable bulk properties. > > **Microscopic approach:** You sneak in with a microphone and track *every single fan* — where they are sitting, how they cheer, their heart rates, etc. From this microscopic data, you then statistically estimate the *overall* noise level that the macroscopic observer would have measured. > > In thermodynamics: the *stadium observer* is the macroscopic engineer; the *individual fan tracker* is the statistical physicist. Both arrive at the same final answer, but by very different paths. ### 1.3 The Concept of Continuum The macroscopic viewpoint is built on a fundamental assumption called the **Continuum Hypothesis**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Continuum Hypothesis:** Matter is assumed to be *continuous* and *homogeneous* at every point in the system. Properties like density $\rho$ and temperature $T$ are defined at every mathematical point in the system, even though we know the system is made of atoms. This assumption is valid as long as the **smallest volume element we consider still contains a statistically significant number of molecules** (typically $\geq 10^{20}$ molecules in a cubic micron for air at STP). ### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Magnitudes (Kerala Exam Ready) | Constant / Magnitude | Symbol | Value / Order | |---|---|---| | **Avogadro's Number** | $N_A$ | $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules/kmol | | **Boltzmann Constant** | $k_B$ | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K | | **Universal Gas Constant** | $R$ | $8.314$ kJ/kmol·K | | Molecules in $1$ cm³ of air (STP) | — | $\approx 2.7 \times 10^{19}$ | | Characteristic molecular diameter (air) | $\sigma$ | $\approx 3.7 \times 10^{-10}$ m | > [!NOTE] > **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** When a question asks you to justify the continuum assumption, the *order of magnitude* of $N_A$ is the standard tool. If the control volume contains molecules of this scale, macroscopic properties (mean free path $\ll$ system size) become physically meaningful. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Detailed Comparison of the Two Approaches > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **the most repeated content** in KTU 2024 Module 1 questions. Memorize the contrasts in a logical order — *cause → observable → outcome*. | Aspect | Macroscopic (Classical) | Microscopic (Statistical) | |---|---|---| | **Basic Postulate** | Matter is a continuum | Matter is a collection of discrete molecules | | **Number of particles considered** | Not considered | $10^{20}$ to $10^{25}$ molecules | | **Variables used** | $P, V, T, m, U, H, S$ | Molecular velocity $c_i$, position $r_i$, energy $\varepsilon_i$ | | **Methodology** | Empirical / Phenomenological | Statistical averaging | | **Equations used** | Algebraic & integral relations | Differential & probabilistic | | **Thermodynamic laws** | Stated as axioms (0, 1, 2, 3) | Derived from molecular mechanics | | **Engineering utility** | Direct design of devices (turbines, compressors) | Insight into why laws hold; property prediction | | **Typical exam questions** | "State Zeroth Law" | "Show that $P = \frac{1}{3}\rho \overline{c^2}$" | ### 2.2 Properties — The Bridge Between the Two Views In the macroscopic view, we talk about **properties** — observable characteristics of the system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Extensive Properties:** Depend on the *size* (mass) of the system. Examples: Volume $V$, Mass $m$, Total Energy $U$, Enthalpy $H$, Entropy $S$. They are *additive* — if you split the system, the sum of parts equals the whole. > > **Intensive Properties:** Independent of the *size* of the system. Examples: Pressure $P$, Temperature $T$, Density $\rho$, Specific volume $v$. They are *non-additive* — pressure on the left half + pressure on the right half does NOT equal the pressure of the whole. > [!NOTE] > **Specific properties** (per unit mass) are intensive by construction. Dividing any extensive property $X$ by mass $m$ gives an intensive property $x = X/m$ (e.g., $v = V/m$, $u = U/m$, $h = H/m$, $s = S/m$). ### 2.3 Thermodynamic System and Surroundings A clear vocabulary is essential before any thermodynamic analysis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **System:** The *specified region* in space we choose to study (a cylinder of gas, a beaker of water, a turbine rotor). > **Surroundings:** Everything *outside* the system that can interact with it. > **Boundary:** The *real or imaginary surface* that separates system and surroundings. It can be movable (piston) or fixed (rigid tank), and it can permit or restrict heat, work, and mass transfer. Three classical types of boundaries are tested in KTU boards: - **Open System (Control Volume):** Mass and energy cross the boundary. (e.g., a nozzle, compressor, turbine) - **Closed System (Control Mass):** Only energy crosses; no mass crosses. (e.g., piston-cylinder, balloon) - **Isolated System:** Neither mass nor energy crosses. (e.g., a perfectly insulated rigid flask — theoretical ideal) ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Equation | SI Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | **Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | kg/m³ | Intensive property | | **Specific Volume** | $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{V}{m}$ | m³/kg | Intensive property | | **Specific Internal Energy** | $u = \dfrac{U}{m}$ | kJ/kg | Extensive → Intensive | | **Specific Enthalpy** | $h = u + Pv$ | kJ/kg | Combines state into one variable | | **Total Energy** | $E = U + KE + PE = m(u + \tfrac{1}{2}V^2 + gz)$ | kJ | Sum of all macroscopic energy modes | | **Ideal Gas Law (Macroscopic)** | $Pv = RT$ | kJ/kg·K | Purely macroscopic relation | | **Microscopic Pressure** | $P = \tfrac{1}{3}\rho \overline{c^2}$ | Pa | Derived from kinetic theory | | **Average Molecular KE** | $\tfrac{1}{2}m_0 \overline{c^2} = \tfrac{3}{2}k_B T$ | J per molecule | Connects $T$ to molecular motion | | **Relation between $R$ and $k_B$** | $R = N_A k_B$ | kJ/kmol·K | Universal link | | **Number of molecules** | $N = \dfrac{m}{M} N_A$ | dimensionless | $M$ is molar mass in kg/kmol | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Exam Tip:** The last three rows are *the* bridge between the two viewpoints. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically asks you to "relate macroscopic properties to the behaviour of molecules." This is done by connecting $P$ and $T$ (macroscopic) to $\overline{c^2}$ (microscopic). ### 2.5 Real-World Utility The macroscopic viewpoint is the engineer's tool — it directly gives equations usable in **pump selection, refrigeration cycle design, IC engine performance, and power plant analysis**. The microscopic viewpoint gives **insight** — why gases deviate from ideal behaviour at high pressure (intermolecular forces), why specific heat varies with temperature (molecular degrees of freedom), and how to estimate properties of mixtures from molecular data. KTU emphasizes *engineering* applications, so quantitative problems in this module are overwhelmingly macroscopic, but **conceptual** questions (3-mark type) frequently test your understanding of the microscopic origin. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation A: Pressure as an Average Molecular Momentum Flux (Microscopic Origin of $P$) This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 1 because it explicitly demonstrates how a *macroscopic* property ($P$) arises from *microscopic* molecular motion. It has appeared in **almost every KTU university examination** for the last 8 years. > [!NOTE] > **Model Assumptions (Kinetic Theory of an Ideal Monatomic Gas):** > 1. The gas consists of a very large number of identical, perfectly spherical, perfectly elastic molecules. > 2. The molecules are in constant, random, straight-line motion. > 3. The size of each molecule is negligible compared to the average distance between them. > 4. Molecules do not exert forces on each other except during instantaneous elastic collisions. > 5. Collisions between molecules and with the walls are perfectly elastic. > 6. The average distribution of molecular velocities is the same in all directions (isotropy). **Step 1: Consider one molecule in a cubical container of side $L$.** Let the molecule have mass $m_0$ and velocity components $(c_x, c_y, c_z)$. Only $c_x$ produces a force on the wall perpendicular to the $x$-axis (the right wall of the cube). **Step 2: Calculate the momentum change per collision.** The molecule's $x$-component of momentum before hitting the right wall is $m_0 c_x$. After a perfectly elastic head-on collision with the wall, it reverses: $-m_0 c_x$. Change in momentum per collision: $$ \Delta p_x = m_0 c_x - (-m_0 c_x) = 2 m_0 c_x $$ **Step 3: Calculate the time between consecutive collisions with the right wall.** The molecule must travel across the cube ($L$) to the right wall and back (another $L$). $$ \Delta t = \frac{2L}{c_x} $$ **Step 4: Apply Newton's second law (force = rate of change of momentum).** Force exerted by *this one molecule* on the right wall: $$ F_{\text{one}} = \frac{\Delta p_x}{\Delta t} = \frac{2 m_0 c_x}{(2L / c_x)} = \frac{m_0 c_x^2}{L} $$ **Step 5: Sum over all $N$ molecules in the container.** Total force on the right wall: $$ F_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{m_0 c_{x,i}^2}{L} = \frac{m_0}{L} \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{x,i}^2 $$ **Step 6: Define the mean square velocity in the $x$-direction.** $$ \overline{c_x^2} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_{x,i}^2 $$ Substitute: $$ F_{\text{total}} = \frac{N m_0 \overline{c_x^2}}{L} $$ **Step 7: Convert force to pressure using the wall area $A = L^2$.** $$ P = \frac{F_{\text{total}}}{A} = \frac{N m_0 \overline{c_x^2}}{L \cdot L^2} = \frac{N m_0 \overline{c_x^2}}{L^3} = \frac{N m_0 \overline{c_x^2}}{V} $$ **Step 8: Introduce the density $\rho = N m_0 / V$.** $$ P = \rho \, \overline{c_x^2} $$ **Step 9: Use isotropy to relate $\overline{c_x^2}$ to total mean square speed $\overline{c^2}$.** Since $c^2 = c_x^2 + c_y^2 + c_z^2$ and the three directions are statistically equivalent: $$ \overline{c^2} = 3 \overline{c_x^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \overline{c_x^2} = \frac{1}{3} \overline{c^2} $$ Substitute: $$ \boxed{\,P = \frac{1}{3} \rho \, \overline{c^2}\,} $$ This is the **famous kinetic-theory expression for pressure** — it shows that what we measure as *pressure* is simply the *time-averaged rate of momentum transfer per unit area* caused by trillions of molecular impacts per second. --- ### 3.2 Derivation B: Temperature as a Measure of Mean Molecular Kinetic Energy Combine the result of Derivation A with the ideal gas equation. **Step 1: Start with the macroscopic ideal gas law.** $$ P v = R T \quad \text{(per unit mass)} $$ Multiply both sides by $\rho = 1/v$: $$ P = \rho R T $$ **Step 2: Equate with the kinetic-theory result.** $$ \rho R T = \frac{1}{3} \rho \overline{c^2} $$ Cancel $\rho$: $$ R T = \frac{1}{3} \overline{c^2} $$ **Step 3: Introduce the Boltzmann constant using $R = N_A k_B / M$ and $m_0 = M / N_A$ (molar mass relationships).** Divide both sides by Avogadro's number $N_A$ and multiply by the molecular mass $m_0$: $$ \frac{1}{2} m_0 \overline{c^2} = \frac{3}{2} \frac{R}{N_A} T $$ Recognize $k_B = R / N_A$: $$ \boxed{\,\overline{KE}_{\text{per molecule}} = \frac{1}{2} m_0 \overline{c^2} = \frac{3}{2} k_B T\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** Temperature is *not* the kinetic energy of a single molecule — it is a direct measure of the **average translational kinetic energy per molecule**. A gas at absolute zero would have $\overline{c^2} = 0$, i.e., *all molecular motion ceases* (consistent with the Third Law of Thermodynamics). --- ### 3.3 Derivation C: Relationship Between Macroscopic and Microscopic Mass A short but commonly asked proof. **Step 1:** Let $m$ be the macroscopic mass of a system and $M$ be its molar mass in kg/kmol. **Step 2:** Number of moles: $$ n = \frac{m}{M} $$ **Step 3:** Number of molecules: $$ N = n \cdot N_A = \frac{m}{M} N_A $$ **Step 4:** Mass of a single molecule: $$ m_0 = \frac{m}{N} = \frac{M}{N_A} $$ This is the foundation for problems where you are given, for example, a volume of gas and asked to compute the *total number of molecules* (microscopic) and the *total mass* (macroscopic). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Check of the Two Views | Quantity | Macroscopic Symbol | Microscopic Origin | Bridge Formula | |---|---|---|---| | **Pressure** $P$ | Force per unit area on a wall | Rate of molecular momentum transfer | $P = \tfrac{1}{3}\rho \overline{c^2}$ | | **Temperature** $T$ | Measured with a thermometer | Avg. translational KE per molecule | $\tfrac{1}{2}m_0 \overline{c^2} = \tfrac{3}{2}k_B T$ | | **Internal Energy** $U$ | Stored energy of system | Sum of molecular KE + PE | $U = N \cdot \bar{\varepsilon}$ | | **Entropy** $S$ | Measure of disorder / irreversibility | Statistical multiplicity $\Omega$ | $S = k_B \ln \Omega$ (Boltzmann) | | **Specific Heat** $c_v$ | Energy needed to raise $T$ by 1 K | Contribution per degree of freedom | $c_v = \tfrac{3}{2}R$ (monatomic) | > [!NOTE] > KTU may give a 3-mark question that asks: *"How is the macroscopic property of pressure related to the microscopic behaviour of gas molecules? Hence define temperature in terms of molecular motion."* This matrix gives you the entire logical skeleton. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Two Pathways to the Same Physics The following Mermaid flowchart shows how a thermodynamic system is analysed through both the macroscopic and microscopic routes, and where the two routes converge on identical physical predictions. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["SYSTEM
e.g. gas in a piston"]:::root A --> B["MACROSCOPIC ROUTE
Continuum View"]:::mac A --> C["MICROSCOPIC ROUTE
Molecular View"]:::mic B --> B1["Measure: P, V, T, m, U"]:::mac B1 --> B2["Apply 4 Laws of
Thermodynamics"]:::mac B2 --> B3["Empirical Equations
Pv = RT, dU = m c_v dT"]:::mac B3 --> B4["ENGINEERING DESIGN
Power Plants, HVAC, Engines"]:::mac C --> C1["Track N molecules
~10^23 particles"]:::mic C1 --> C2["Use Statistical Mechanics
Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution"]:::mic C2 --> C3["Compute averages
P, T, U from c_i, r_i"]:::mic C3 --> C4["INSIGHT
Why gases behave as they do"]:::mic B4 -.Converge to same P, T, U.-> X["UNIFIED THERMODYNAMICS"]:::unif C4 -.Converge to same P, T, U.-> X:::unif classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef mac fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef mic fill:#533483,stroke:#f9b208,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef unif fill:#2c7873,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px ``` --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram: The Continuum Concept A schematic explaining the continuum hypothesis used in the macroscopic viewpoint. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Real Gas
Molecules at discrete points"]:::discrete A2["Small Control Volume
dV with ~10^20 molecules"]:::smallvol A3["Property averaged
P, T, rho defined at a POINT"]:::point A4["Continuum Property Field
P(x,y,z,t), T(x,y,z,t), rho(x,y,z,t)"]:::field A1 -->|"Zoom out"| A2 A2 -->|"Statistical averaging
is meaningful only
if molecules >> 1"| A3 A3 -->|"Define smoothly varying
properties at every point"| A4 classDef discrete fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef smallvol fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef point fill:#ffe66d,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef field fill:#1a535c,stroke:#fff ``` --- ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Classification of Thermodynamic Systems This diagram maps the three classical system types — tested in nearly every KTU 2024 exam. ```mermaid flowchart TD S["THERMODYNAMIC SYSTEM"]:::sys S --> O["OPEN SYSTEM
Control Volume"]:::open S --> C["CLOSED SYSTEM
Control Mass"]:::closed S --> I["ISOLATED SYSTEM
No interaction"]:::isolated O --> O1["Mass crosses boundary"]:::open O1 --> O2["Energy crosses boundary"]:::open O2 --> O3["Example: Turbine, Nozzle,
Compressor, Boiler"]:::open C --> C1["No mass crosses boundary"]:::closed C1 --> C2["Energy crosses boundary
(Heat, Work)"]:::closed C2 --> C3["Example: Piston-Cylinder,
Pressure Cooker"]:::closed I --> I1["No mass crosses"]:::isolated I1 --> I2["No heat crosses
(adiabatic)"]:::isolated I2 --> I3["No work crosses
(rigid walls)"]:::isolated I3 --> I4["Example: Thermos Flask
(idealized)"]:::isolated classDef sys fill:#2d3047,stroke:#e63946,color:#fff,stroke-width:3px classDef open fill:#118ab2,stroke:#fff classDef closed fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000 classDef isolated fill:#9d4edd,stroke:#fff ``` --- ### 4.4 Mermaid Comparison: Macroscopic vs Microscopic Variables ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MAC["MACROSCOPIC VARIABLES"] P["Pressure P
Pa"]:::macvar T["Temperature T
K"]:::macvar V["Volume V
m^3"]:::macvar M["Mass m
kg"]:::macvar U["Internal Energy U
kJ"]:::macvar end subgraph MIC["MICROSCOPIC VARIABLES"] CX["Velocity c_x
m/s"]:::micvar R2["Position r_i
m"]:::micvar E2["Molecular energy e_i
J"]:::micvar N2["Number of molecules N"]:::micvar M0["Mass per molecule m_0
kg"]:::micvar end P <-->|"P = 1/3 rho c^2_bar"| CX T <-->|"1/2 m_0 c^2 = 3/2 k_B T"| CX V <-->|"V = total volume accessible"| R2 M <-->|"m = N * m_0"| M0 U <-->|"U = N * e_bar_i"| E2 classDef macvar fill:#e63946,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef micvar fill:#4895ef,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to use this diagram in a viva or written exam:** If asked "explain the two viewpoints", trace one arrow (e.g., $P \leftrightarrow c_x$) and explain that the *same* physical quantity is interpreted in two languages — a continuum force per area on one side, a momentum flux on the other. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern, Module 1):** > - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks > - Part B: Internal choice — answer 1 of 2 × 14 marks = 14 marks > - **Total Module 1 contribution to ESE:** 20 marks > - Combined with Module 2 (20 marks) and Modules 3, 4, 5 (40 marks) for the full 100-mark paper. --- ### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each) #### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **Explain the macroscopic and microscopic viewpoints in thermodynamics. Give two examples of properties analysed under each viewpoint.** **Model Answer (≈ 12 lines, KTU-recommended structure):** The **macroscopic viewpoint** treats the system as a *continuous* entity without considering the discrete molecular structure of matter. The properties studied (such as pressure $P$, temperature $T$, volume $V$, internal energy $U$) are *bulk* or *averaged* quantities that can be directly measured with laboratory instruments. This approach is also called **Classical Thermodynamics** and is the foundation of all engineering design calculations. The **microscopic viewpoint**, on the other hand, considers the system as an *assembly of a large number of molecules* ($10^{20}$ to $10^{25}$ particles). Each molecule is described by its position, velocity, and energy. The macroscopic properties are obtained by *statistical averaging* of the corresponding molecular quantities. This approach is also called **Statistical Thermodynamics**. | Viewpoint | Example Property | Example Quantity | |---|---|---| | Macroscopic | Pressure, Temperature | $P = 5$ bar, $T = 300$ K | | Microscopic | Mean square velocity | $\overline{c^2} = 1.5 \times 10^5$ m²/s² | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *thermodynamic laws* (which are macroscopic) with *kinetic theory results* (which are microscopic). If a question asks for the *origin* of a property, give the microscopic derivation; if it asks for *design calculations*, use the macroscopic form. --- #### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between intensive and extensive properties. Classify the following: pressure, volume, mass, density, temperature, internal energy, specific enthalpy.** **Model Answer:** **Extensive properties** depend on the size (mass) of the system and are additive — if the system is divided into parts, the property for the whole equals the sum of the parts. **Intensive properties** are independent of system size and are *not* additive. | Property | Classification | Reason | |---|---|---| | Pressure $P$ | **Intensive** | Same in two halves when system is split | | Volume $V$ | **Extensive** | Halves if mass is halved | | Mass $m$ | **Extensive** | Halves if mass is halved | | Density $\rho$ | **Intensive** | Independent of total mass | | Temperature $T$ | **Intensive** | Thermal equilibrium gives one $T$ | | Internal Energy $U$ | **Extensive** | Proportional to mass | | Specific Enthalpy $h$ | **Intensive** | Per unit mass | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is calling *specific* properties (e.g., $u$, $h$, $v$, $s$) as extensive. Specific properties are *per unit mass* and are therefore *always* intensive, by construction. --- ### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, internal choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule (ESE):** You must answer **one** of the two full questions, each worth 14 marks. Each question has sub-parts typically weighted 7 + 7 marks. Provide the *complete* solution with diagrams and final boxed answers. --- #### Question A (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** Define the macroscopic viewpoint. Starting from the kinetic theory of gases, derive an expression for the pressure exerted by an ideal gas in terms of its density and the mean square velocity of the molecules. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the result of part (a) and the ideal gas equation, derive an expression that relates the mean kinetic energy of a gas molecule to its absolute temperature. Hence, estimate the root mean square (RMS) speed of nitrogen molecules ($M = 28$ kg/kmol) at 300 K. Given $R = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation of $P = \frac{1}{3}\rho \overline{c^2}$ **Step 1 — Assumptions of Kinetic Theory** (2 marks): 1. Gas consists of a large number of identical, perfectly elastic, spherical molecules. 2. Molecular size is negligible compared to intermolecular distance. 3. Molecules exert no force on each other except during instantaneous elastic collisions. 4. Molecules are in continuous, random, straight-line motion. 5. Collisions with the wall are perfectly elastic. 6. The average distribution of molecular velocities is isotropic (same in all directions). **Step 2 — Set up the geometry** (1 mark): Consider a cubical container of side $L$ containing $N$ molecules. Focus on the wall perpendicular to the $x$-axis, area $A = L^2$. **Step 3 — Compute force from one molecule** (2 marks): Change in $x$-momentum per collision with the right wall: $\Delta p_x = 2 m_0 c_x$. Time between successive collisions: $\Delta t = 2L / c_x$. Force from one molecule: $F_{\text{one}} = \Delta p_x / \Delta t = m_0 c_x^2 / L$. **Step 4 — Sum over all $N$ molecules** (1 mark): $F_{\text{total}} = \frac{m_0}{L} \sum c_{x,i}^2 = \frac{N m_0 \overline{c_x^2}}{L}$. **Step 5 — Pressure and final form** (1 mark): $P = F_{\text{total}} / A = \frac{N m_0 \overline{c_x^2}}{L^3} = \rho \overline{c_x^2}$. Using isotropy $\overline{c_x^2} = \tfrac{1}{3}\overline{c^2}$: $$ \boxed{P = \frac{1}{3} \rho \, \overline{c^2}} $$ --- #### Part (b) — Mean KE and RMS speed **Step 1 — Relate to ideal gas** (2 marks): Ideal gas: $P v = R T \Rightarrow P = \rho R T$. Equate with kinetic-theory result: $\rho R T = \frac{1}{3} \rho \overline{c^2}$. **Step 2 — Mean KE per molecule** (1 mark): $$ \overline{KE} = \frac{1}{2} m_0 \overline{c^2} = \frac{3}{2} \frac{R}{N_A} T = \frac{3}{2} k_B T $$ **Step 3 — RMS speed formula** (1 mark): From $\frac{1}{2} m_0 \overline{c^2} = \frac{3}{2} k_B T$ and $m_0 = M / N_A$, $k_B = R / N_A$: $$ c_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\overline{c^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical substitution for N₂ at 300 K** (3 marks): Given $M = 28$ kg/kmol, $R = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K = $8314$ J/kmol·K, $T = 300$ K. $$ c_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 8314 \times 300}{28}} = \sqrt{\frac{7{,}482{,}600}{28}} = \sqrt{267{,}235.7} $$ $$ \boxed{c_{\text{rms}} \approx 517 \text{ m/s}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is using $R = 8.314$ (in kJ units) directly with $M$ in kg. You **must** keep units consistent: either $R = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K with $M$ in kg/kmol, or $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K with $M$ in kg/mol. Mixing units loses 1–2 marks immediately. --- #### Question B (14 marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** Define a thermodynamic system, surroundings, and boundary. With the help of neat sketches, classify systems into (i) open, (ii) closed, and (iii) isolated, giving two engineering examples of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is the continuum hypothesis? A spherical balloon of diameter 0.5 m contains air at atmospheric pressure and 300 K. Estimate (i) the total number of molecules inside, and (ii) the total internal energy of the gas, given $R = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K, $M_{\text{air}} = 29$ kg/kmol, and $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — System Classification **Definitions** (2 marks): - **System:** The specified region of space chosen for thermodynamic study. - **Surroundings:** Everything external to the system that may interact with it. - **Boundary:** The real or imaginary surface separating system and surroundings. **Classification Table** (4 marks, including 1 mark for diagrams): | System Type | Mass Transfer | Energy Transfer | Example 1 | Example 2 | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Open (Control Volume)** | Yes | Yes | Turbine | Nozzle | | **Closed (Control Mass)** | No | Yes | Piston-Cylinder | Pressure Cooker | | **Isolated** | No | No | Thermos flask (ideal) | Sealed Dewar | Diagrams should show the boundary (dashed line), the system inside, and arrows for mass ($\dot{m}$) and energy ($\dot{Q}, \dot{W}$) crossing the boundary where applicable. **(1 mark)** --- #### Part (b) — Continuum and Numerical Estimates **Step 1 — Continuum hypothesis** (1 mark): The continuum hypothesis states that matter is continuous and properties like density and temperature are defined at every mathematical point in the system, provided the smallest control volume contains a statistically large number of molecules (typically $\gg 10^{20}$). **Step 2 — Volume of balloon** (1 mark): $$ V = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (0.25)^3 = 0.0654 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 3 — Mass of air** (1 mark): Using $PV = mRT/M$: $$ m = \frac{P V M}{R T} = \frac{101325 \times 0.0654 \times 29}{8.314 \times 10^3 \times 300} = \frac{192{,}200}{2{,}494{,}200} \approx 0.0770 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 4 — Number of molecules** (1 mark): $$ N = \frac{m}{M} N_A = \frac{0.0770}{29} \times 6.022 \times 10^{26} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{24} \text{ molecules} $$ Wait — careful with unit consistency. Using $M$ in kg/kmol: $m$ is in kg, so $m/M$ gives kmol, multiplied by $N_A$ in molecules/kmol gives molecules. Let me recompute cleanly: $$ \frac{m}{M} = \frac{0.0770}{29} = 2.655 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol} $$ $$ N = 2.655 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.022 \times 10^{26} = 1.6 \times 10^{24} \text{ molecules} $$ (That order of magnitude — a balloon containing a billion-trillion molecules — beautifully justifies the continuum hypothesis!) **Step 5 — Internal energy** (1.5 marks): For air ($\gamma = 1.4$), $c_v = R/(\gamma - 1) = 8.314/0.4 = 20.785$ kJ/kmol·K. Specific internal energy: $u = c_v T = 20.785 \times 300 = 6235.5$ kJ/kmol. Total internal energy: $$ U = n \cdot c_v T = \frac{m}{M} c_v T = 2.655 \times 10^{-3} \times 6235.5 \approx 16.55 \text{ kJ} $$ $$ \boxed{N \approx 1.6 \times 10^{24} \text{ molecules} \quad ; \quad U \approx 16.55 \text{ kJ}} $$ **Step 6 — Continuum validation** (0.5 mark): Since $N \sim 10^{24} \gg 10^{20}$, the continuum assumption is **strongly valid** for this balloon. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert $N_A$ to consistent units ($6.022 \times 10^{26}$ molecules/**kmol** when $M$ is in kg/kmol) and incorrectly use $10^{23}$ (which is per mole).
