This single mistake causes a 100× error — a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Use this list as a 60-second pre-exam mental scan.** - [ ] **Macroscopic** = continuum, bulk properties ($P, T, V, m, U, H, S$), no molecular detail; the engineer's tool. - [ ] **Microscopic** = discrete molecules, position/velocity, statistical averaging; the physicist's tool. - [ ] **Continuum hypothesis** is valid when the smallest physical control volume contains a *huge* number of molecules ($N \gg 1$, typically $\geq 10^{20}$). - [ ] **Avogadro's number** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{26}$ /kmol = $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol. - [ ] **Boltzmann constant** $k_B = R / N_A = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. - [ ] **Master bridge formula** (most tested): $P = \frac{1}{3} \rho \overline{c^2}$. - [ ] **Temperature definition:** $\frac{1}{2} m_0 \overline{c^2} = \frac{3}{2} k_B T$ (monatomic, translational only). - [ ] **RMS speed:** $c_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{3RT/M}$, in m/s when $R$ is in J/kg·K or $8314$ J/kmol·K with $M$ in kg/kmol. - [ ] **Extensive** = scales with mass (additive). **Intensive** = independent of mass (not additive). **Specific** = always intensive. - [ ] **System types:** Open (mass + energy cross), Closed (only energy crosses), Isolated (nothing crosses). - [ ] **Order-of-magnitude** check: 1 cm³ of air at STP contains $\sim 10^{19}$ molecules — use this to justify the continuum. - [ ] **Diagrams** (piston-cylinder, control-volume with $\dot{m}$, $\dot{Q}$, $\dot{W}$ arrows) carry **1–2 easy marks**; always draw them. - [ ] **Box every final answer** — examiners award 0.5–1 mark for the boxed numerical result even if intermediate steps have minor slips. - [ ] **KTU evaluator's pet peeve:** Mixing SI and engineering units (kJ with kg instead of J with kg) is the #1 source of "surprise" mark loss. - [ ] **Exam time-saver:** If a 14-mark question gives you an internal choice, scan *both* options first — the one requiring only formula substitution (Question B part b above) is often the faster, safer pick.

topics.title: Continuum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Continuum Hypothesis — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuum Hypothesis (KTU 2024 Module 1 Definition):**
> The *Continuum Hypothesis* states that **matter is treated as a continuous medium**, in which physical properties such as **density ($\rho$)**, **pressure ($p$)**, **temperature ($T$)**, and **velocity ($\vec{V}$)** are defined as **continuous functions of position and time**. The fluid is regarded as an infinitely divisible mass distribution, and macroscopic properties can be assigned to every geometric point in the flow domain, even though the point itself contains no actual mass.

In the formal mathematical sense adopted by KTU, a *continuum* is a set of points forming a connected topological space such that between any two points there exists an infinite number of other points. Applied to thermodynamics, this means we treat gases, liquids, and solids as **continuous distributions of matter** rather than as discrete collections of molecules.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a glass of water under a magnifying glass.** Even though water is actually composed of trillions of H₂O molecules separated by vacuum, to your naked eye the water looks perfectly **smooth, continuous, and uniform** — you cannot see the gaps between molecules. The Continuum Hypothesis says: *for engineering calculations, treat the water exactly the way your eye sees it — as an unbroken continuous fluid.*

**Another analogy:** Imagine a very high-resolution photograph of a crowd at a stadium. From a distance, the crowd looks like a *continuous mass* (you cannot count individual people). As you zoom in to the pixel level, you start seeing individual "particles" (people). The **continuum limit** is the "zoomed-out" view, where the medium behaves as a smooth fluid.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Reference Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Reference Constants for Continuum Calculations:**
> - **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
> - **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.02214076 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314462\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
> - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $p_{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$
> - **Typical Air Molecule Diameter:** $d \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$ (≈ $0.37\ \text{nm}$)

### 1.4 Why the Continuum Hypothesis Matters

In all of classical **Engineering Thermodynamics** — the First Law, Second Law, entropy analysis, cycles (Rankine, Brayton, Otto, Diesel) — we operate entirely within the continuum framework. Without this assumption, equations like $pV = mRT$, $du = c_v\,dT$, and the Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT) would not be applicable at a mathematical "point".

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions for Continuum Assumption to be Valid

The continuum hypothesis **breaks down** when the size of the physical domain becomes comparable to the molecular spacing. To determine when the assumption is valid, we use the **Knudsen Number ($Kn$)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Knudsen Number (Dimensionless):** Ratio of the molecular mean free path ($\lambda$) to the characteristic length scale ($L$) of the flow domain.
> $$Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L}$$

### 2.2 Regimes Classified by Knudsen Number (Bullet Logic)

- **$Kn < 0.001$ → Continuum Regime**
  - *Why:* Molecules collide among themselves far more frequently than they collide with walls. Bulk properties are well-defined.
  - *Applicable equations:* Navier-Stokes, Euler, energy equation — all classical CFD/Thermodynamics.
- **$0.001 \leq Kn \leq 0.1$ → Slip-Flow Regime**
  - *Why:* Boundary layers have a finite slip at walls. Continuum plus velocity/temperature slip corrections needed.
- **$0.1 < Kn \leq 10$ → Transition Regime**
  - *Why:* Mean free path is comparable to domain size. Continuum equations **fail**.
- **$Kn > 10$ → Free Molecular Flow**
  - *Why:* Molecule-wall collisions dominate over molecule-molecule collisions. Boltzmann equation is required.

> [!WARNING]
> For all standard KTU thermodynamics problems (IC engines, turbines, compressors, refrigeration), we are almost always in the **$Kn < 0.001$ regime**, so the continuum assumption is automatically valid. Examiner questions on "Continuum" usually test this Knudsen number criterion and the mean free path formula.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Knudsen Number | $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda}{L}$ | Dimensionless | Determines continuum validity |
| 2 | Mean Free Path (Ideal Gas) | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\,p}$ | m (metres) | Molecular collision distance |
| 3 | Mean Free Path (Alternate) | $\lambda = \dfrac{RT}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\,N_A p}$ | m | Using universal gas constant |
| 4 | Number Density | $n = \dfrac{N}{V} = \dfrac{p}{k_B T}$ | molecules/m³ | Count of molecules per unit volume |
| 5 | Molecular Spacing | $\ell_{avg} \approx n^{-1/3}$ | m | Average gap between molecules |
| 6 | Continuum Threshold (Air) | $Kn \leq 0.001$ | — | Validity condition |
| 7 | Ideal Gas Law (Continuum Use) | $p = \rho R T$ | Pa | Core continuum equation |
| 8 | Property at a Point | $\phi = \phi(x, y, z, t)$ | varies | Defines any scalar/vector field |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Note:** Always write Knudsen number criteria with the **strict inequality** for the continuum regime: $Kn < 0.001$ (NOT $Kn < 0.1$, that is slip-flow).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IC Engines & Gas Turbines:** Combustion chamber analysis assumes continuum — flame propagation is modelled by continuous temperature/pressure fields.
- **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS):** At micron scales, **Kn approaches 0.1**, and continuum breaks down — requires rarefied gas dynamics.
- **High-Altitude Aerodynamics:** At $50\ \text{km}$ altitude, air density drops, $\lambda$ increases; re-entry vehicles transition through slip → transition regimes.
- **Vacuum Technology:** At pressures below $\sim 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa}$, mean free path exceeds chamber dimensions — continuum fails completely.
- **Cryogenics & Nanofluidics:** Surface-to-volume ratios become extreme, requiring molecular-level (non-continuum) modelling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mean Free Path ($\lambda$)

The **mean free path** is defined as the average distance a molecule travels between two successive collisions with other molecules.

**Step 1 — Assume molecules as rigid spheres of diameter $d$.**

A collision occurs when the centers of two molecules come within a distance $d$ of each other.

**Step 2 — Consider one molecule moving with average speed $\bar{c}$ and others stationary.**

The "collision cylinder" swept by the moving molecule per unit time has:
- Cross-sectional area: $A_c = \pi d^{2}$
- Length per unit time: $\bar{c}$

So the swept volume per unit time is:
$$
V_{swept} = \pi d^{2}\, \bar{c}
$$

**Step 3 — Number density of stationary target molecules.**

If $n$ is the number of molecules per unit volume, the number of target molecules inside the swept volume is:
$$
N_{targets} = n \cdot \pi d^{2}\, \bar{c}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute average distance between collisions.**

The mean free path is the distance travelled per collision:
$$
\lambda = \frac{\bar{c}}{N_{targets}/\text{unit time}} = \frac{\bar{c}}{n\,\pi d^{2}\,\bar{c}} = \frac{1}{n\,\pi d^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply relative velocity correction.**

When both molecules move with average speed $\bar{c}$, the **average relative speed** is $\bar{c}_{rel} = \sqrt{2}\,\bar{c}$ (Maxwell-Boltzmann result). Multiplying by this factor:
$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,n\,\pi d^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Replace $n$ using Ideal Gas Law ($p = n\,k_B T$).**

Solving for $n = \dfrac{p}{k_B T}$ and substituting:
$$
\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\,p}
$$

This is the **final KTU form of the mean free path** for an ideal gas.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Continuum Threshold Condition

**Given:** $Kn < 0.001$ is required for continuum validity.

**Substitute Knudsen Number and Mean Free Path:**
$$
Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L} = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\,p\,L}
$$

**Solve for the minimum length scale $L_{min}$ where continuum holds at given $(p, T)$:**
$$
L_{min} = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\,p \cdot (0.001)} = \frac{1000\,k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\,p}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** As pressure $p$ drops or temperature $T$ rises, $L_{min}$ grows — meaning the continuum assumption breaks down for *larger* physical systems at lower pressures (e.g., high-altitude flight, vacuum chambers).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Air at NTP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given:** Air at $T = 300\ \text{K}$, $p = 101325\ \text{Pa}$, $d = 3.7 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$, $L = 0.1\ \text{m}$ (a typical engine cylinder).

**Step 1 — Compute number density $n$:**
$$
n = \frac{p}{k_B T} = \frac{101325}{(1.380649 \times 10^{-23})(300)}
$$
$$
n = \frac{101325}{4.141947 \times 10^{-21}} = 2.446 \times 10^{25}\ \text{molecules/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute mean free path $\lambda$:**
$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\,n}
$$
$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{(1.4142)(\pi)(3.7 \times 10^{-10})^{2}(2.446 \times 10^{25})}
$$
$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{(1.4142)(3.1416)(1.369 \times 10^{-19})(2.446 \times 10^{25})}
$$
$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{1.488 \times 10^{7}} = 6.72 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m} = 67.2\ \text{nm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Knudsen Number:**
$$
Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L} = \frac{6.72 \times 10^{-8}}{0.1} = 6.72 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 4 — Check continuum validity:**
$$
Kn = 6.72 \times 10^{-7} \ll 0.001 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Continuum Assumption VALID)}
$$

This numerical example confirms why all KTU thermodynamics problems safely use the continuum assumption.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Regime | Kn Range | Equation Applicable | KTU Typical Use? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuum | $Kn < 0.001$ | Navier-Stokes, Euler, Energy Eq. | **YES (Always)** |
| Slip Flow | $0.001 - 0.1$ | Navier-Stokes + Slip BC | No |
| Transition | $0.1 - 10$ | Burnett / Super-Burnett | No |
| Free Molecular | $Kn > 10$ | Boltzmann Equation (DSMC) | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Flow Diagram — Continuum Regime Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Compute Kn = lambda / L"] --> B{"Kn Value?"}
    B -- "< 0.001" --> C["CONTINUUM REGIME"]
    B -- "0.001 to 0.1" --> D["SLIP FLOW REGIME"]
    B -- "0.1 to 10" --> E["TRANSITION REGIME"]
    B -- "> 10" --> F["FREE MOLECULAR FLOW"]

    C --> G["Apply Navier-Stokes Eqn"]
    C --> H["First Law of Thermo Valid"]
    C --> I["Reynolds Transport Theorem OK"]

    D --> J["Apply Slip Boundary Conditions"]
    E --> K["Use Burnett Equations"]
    F --> L["Use Boltzmann DSMC Solver"]

    G --> M["KTU Standard Use Case"]
    H --> M
    I --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture — Property Definition at a Continuum Point

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Mathematical Point P with Coords x,y,z,t"] --> R["Density rho"]
    P --> S["Pressure p"]
    P --> T["Temperature T"]
    P --> V["Velocity Vector V"]

    R --> W["Continuum Property Set"]
    S --> W
    T --> W
    V --> W

    W --> X["Used in Field Equations"]
    X --> Y["Continuity Eqn"]
    X --> Z["Momentum Eqn"]
    X --> AA["Energy Eqn"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Mean Free Path Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["Molecule M1 Moving with Velocity c-bar"] --> C1["Sweeps Collision Cylinder"]
    C1 --> A1["Cross Section Area = pi d squared"]
    C1 --> L1["Length per Second = c-bar"]
    C1 --> V1["Swept Volume = pi d squared times c-bar"]
    V1 --> N1["Number of Target Molecules = n times V1"]
    N1 --> L2["Mean Free Path lambda = c-bar divided by N1"]
    L2 --> F1["Final Formula lambda = 1 over sqrt2 pi d squared n"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always remember that the Continuum Hypothesis is the **single most important assumption** in all of Engineering Thermodynamics. If asked to "justify the continuum assumption", your answer must mention: *mean free path is much smaller than characteristic length*, supported by the **Knudsen number criterion $Kn < 0.001$**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define the Continuum Hypothesis as used in Engineering Thermodynamics. State the mathematical criterion that justifies its validity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Continuum Hypothesis states that **fluid matter is treated as a continuous medium** where physical properties such as density ($\rho$), pressure ($p$), temperature ($T$), and velocity ($\vec{V}$) are **continuous and differentiable functions** of position and time. A finite value of these properties can be assigned to every mathematical point in the fluid domain.

**Mathematical Criterion:**
$$
Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L} < 0.001
$$
where $\lambda$ is the molecular mean free path and $L$ is the characteristic length scale. When this condition is satisfied, the continuum assumption is valid.

*(1 mark: Definition; 1 mark: Property assignment at a point; 1 mark: Knudsen criterion)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`
**What is the Knudsen Number? List the four flow regimes based on its value.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Knudsen Number ($Kn$)** is a dimensionless number defined as the ratio of the molecular mean free path ($\lambda$) to the characteristic length of the flow domain ($L$):
$$
Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L}
$$

**Four flow regimes:**
1. **Continuum regime:** $Kn < 0.001$
2. **Slip-flow regime:** $0.001 \leq Kn \leq 0.1$
3. **Transition regime:** $0.1 < Kn \leq 10$
4. **Free molecular flow:** $Kn > 10$

*(1 mark: Definition + formula; 2 marks: Four regimes with limits)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Continuum Hypothesis** with a neat sketch showing properties at a point. Derive the expression for the **mean free path** of an ideal gas molecule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For air at standard atmospheric pressure ($p = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$) and temperature $T = 300\ \text{K}$, calculate the **mean free path** and the **Knudsen number** for flow through a pipe of diameter $D = 5\ \text{cm}$. Assume molecular diameter $d = 3.7 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$ and $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — State the Continuum Hypothesis (2 marks):**

The Continuum Hypothesis assumes that **fluid matter is a continuous medium**, not a discrete collection of molecules. The macroscopic properties $\rho, p, T, \vec{V}$ are defined at every point $(x, y, z, t)$ as continuous, differentiable scalar/vector fields.

**Sketch (1 mark):** Show a point P with property vectors $\rho$, $p$, $T$, $\vec{V}$ defined at it.

**Step 2 — Mean Free Path Derivation (4 marks):**

As derived in Section 3.1:
$$
\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\,p}
$$
with each step (collision cylinder, swept volume, number density substitution) explained.

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $p = 101325\ \text{Pa}$, $T = 300\ \text{K}$, $d = 3.7 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$, $D = 0.05\ \text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Mean Free Path Calculation (3 marks):**
$$
\lambda = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi (3.7 \times 10^{-10})^{2}(101325)}
$$
$$
\lambda = \frac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{(1.4142)(3.1416)(1.369 \times 10^{-19})(101325)}
$$
$$
\lambda = \frac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{6.13 \times 10^{-15}} = 6.75 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} \approx 0.675\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Knudsen Number (3 marks):**
$$
Kn = \frac{\lambda}{D} = \frac{6.75 \times 10^{-7}}{0.05} = 1.35 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
$$
Kn = 1.35 \times 10^{-5} < 0.001 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Continuum Assumption VALID.}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **limitations of the continuum approach** in fluid mechanics and thermodynamics. Explain the role of the **Knudsen number** in classifying flow regimes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** At what altitude would the continuum assumption break down for a 1 m diameter aircraft flying through the atmosphere? Assume air with $T = 250\ \text{K}$ and use the U.S. Standard Atmosphere to estimate pressure variation with altitude. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Limitations (3 marks):**

The continuum approach fails when:
- Molecular mean free path $\lambda$ is comparable to or larger than characteristic length $L$.
- Surface effects dominate (e.g., in MEMS, nanofluidics).
- Pressure is extremely low (vacuum technology, space).
- At very high altitudes where rarefied gas dynamics prevails.

**Step 2 — Knudsen Number Regimes (3 marks):**
Provide the table from Section 3.4.

**Step 3 — Engineering Examples (1 mark):** MEMS devices, re-entry vehicles, vacuum systems.

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Threshold Condition (2 marks):**

For continuum to fail: $Kn \geq 0.001$, so
$$
\lambda_{crit} = 0.001 \times 1\ \text{m} = 10^{-3}\ \text{m} = 1\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Pressure at this $\lambda$ (3 marks):**

From $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\,p}$:
$$
p = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\,\lambda_{crit}}
$$
$$
p = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(250)}{(1.4142)(3.1416)(1.369 \times 10^{-19})(10^{-3})}
$$
$$
p = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-21}}{6.10 \times 10^{-22}} \approx 5.66\ \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — Find Altitude (2 marks):**

Using barometric formula at $T = 250\ \text{K}$:
$$
p(h) = p_0 \exp\left(-\frac{M g h}{R T}\right)
$$
where $M = 0.029\ \text{kg/mol}$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$, $p_0 = 101325\ \text{Pa}$.

$$
\frac{p(h)}{p_0} = \frac{5.66}{101325} = 5.59 \times 10^{-5}
$$
$$
h = \frac{R T}{M g} \ln\left(\frac{p_0}{p}\right)
$$
$$
h = \frac{(8.314)(250)}{(0.029)(9.81)} \ln(17892) = 7306 \times 9.79 \approx 71{,}500\ \text{m} \approx 71.5\ \text{km}
$$

**Conclusion:** Continuum breaks down above ~71 km altitude (lower thermosphere boundary).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and lose 2–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$ factor** in the mean free path formula — it accounts for relative molecular motion.
> 2. **Writing $Kn < 0.1$ instead of $Kn < 0.001$** for continuum — examiners are strict on the threshold.
> 3. **Using wrong value of Boltzmann constant** (often confused with $R$); use $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$.
> 4. **Not converting pressure to Pascals** when molecular quantities are in SI.
> 5. **Confusing mean free path $\lambda$ with molecular diameter $d$** — they differ by $\sim 5$ orders of magnitude.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Continuum"**

- ✅ **Continuum Hypothesis:** Fluid treated as a continuous medium; properties defined at every point.
- ✅ **Knudsen Number:** $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda}{L}$ — dimensionless.
- ✅ **Continuum valid when:** $Kn < 0.001$.
- ✅ **Mean Free Path Formula:** $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\,p}$.
- ✅ **Number density:** $n = \dfrac{p}{k_B T} = \dfrac{N}{V}$.
- ✅ **Average molecular spacing:** $\ell \approx n^{-1/3}$.
- ✅ **Four Regimes (in order):** Continuum → Slip Flow → Transition → Free Molecular.
- ✅ **Failures occur in:** MEMS, high-altitude flight (>70 km), vacuum chambers, nanofluidics.
- ✅ **Standard values to remember:** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$, $d_{air} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$.
- ✅ **Engineering relevance:** Justifies the use of $pV = mRT$, energy equations, and Reynolds Transport Theorem.
- ✅ **Examiner's golden phrase:** *"Since $Kn \ll 0.001$, the continuum assumption is justified."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Systems and Control Volumes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Systems and Control Volumes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The Thermodynamic System

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Thermodynamic System** is defined as a precisely defined quantity of matter, a region in space, or a combination thereof, which is selected for the purpose of thermodynamic analysis and separated from its surroundings by a real or imaginary boundary. Everything external to the system that can directly interact with it is designated as the **Surroundings** (or environment). The boundary is the closed surface that separates the system from its surroundings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every thermodynamic analysis MUST begin with the unambiguous identification of the system, its boundary, and the surroundings. Failure to do so results in immediate loss of marks in university examinations.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a pressure cooker on your kitchen stove.
- The **System** = the steam and water vapor trapped inside the cooker.
- The **Boundary** = the metallic wall of the cooker (a real, physical boundary).
- The **Surroundings** = the air in your kitchen, the stove, and the cooker's outer shell.

Heat crosses the boundary (from the stove to the water), and steam (matter) might leak past the safety valve. The *choice* of what you decide to study (just the steam? or the steam + cooker shell?) determines what becomes your system.

### Types of Systems (Classification by Mass Transfer)

| Type | Mass Transfer? | Energy Transfer? | Real-World Example |
|------|----------------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Closed System** (Control Mass) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Piston-cylinder, pressure cooker, sealed pressure vessel |
| **Open System** (Control Volume) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Turbine, compressor, nozzle, diffuser, pipe flow |
| **Isolated System** | ❌ No | ❌ No | Thermos flask (idealized), the universe itself |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A *Closed System* is often called a **Control Mass (CM)** because the mass inside remains fixed. An *Open System* is called a **Control Volume (CV)** because the region in space (volume) is what we track, while mass flows in and out.

## 1.2 The Control Volume — The Heart of Modern Thermodynamics

### Formal Definition
A **Control Volume (CV)** is a fixed region in space through which mass, energy, and momentum may flow across its well-defined boundaries (called the **Control Surface**). It is the analytical framework used for devices such as turbines, compressors, pumps, nozzles, diffusers, and heat exchangers, where the working fluid continuously enters and exits.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a hydroelectric power station's turbine as a control volume.
- You cannot "tag" a specific parcel of water and follow it, because water keeps streaming through.
- Instead, you draw an imaginary dashed box (control surface) around the turbine.
- At the **inlet** (high-pressure water entry), mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{in}$ crosses in.
- At the **outlet** (low-pressure water exit), mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{out}$ crosses out.
- Across the **control surface**, you track energy (heat $\dot{Q}$, work $\dot{W}$) and mass.

## 1.3 Boundary Classification

The **Boundary** (or Control Surface) is classified by its physical and thermal properties:

| Boundary Type | Property | Example |
|---------------|----------|---------|
| **Real Boundary** | Physically tangible | Walls of a pressure vessel |
| **Imaginary Boundary** | Conceptual | A dashed surface enclosing a turbine |
| **Fixed/Rigid Boundary** | Does not move | Walls of a heat exchanger |
| **Movable Boundary** | Changes position/volume | Piston in a cylinder |
| **Diathermic (Permeable)** | Allows heat transfer | Thin metallic wall |
| **Adiabatic** | No heat transfer | Insulated walls of a steam pipe |
| **Semi-permeable** | Allows selective mass transfer | Semi-permeable membrane |

> [!NOTE]
> A **Diathermic** boundary is one that permits heat transfer across it, while an **Adiabatic** boundary completely prevents it. The standard atmospheric pressure acting on an open manometer is $\mathbf{P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}}$.

## 1.4 Why Systems & Control Volumes Matter

The proper choice of system dictates the form of the governing equations:
- For a **Closed System** → Use the First Law: $\Delta U = Q - W$.
- For an **Open System** → Use the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**.
- Misidentifying the system is the **#1 source of errors** in KTU board examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic of System Selection — Step-by-Step

When approaching any thermodynamics problem, follow this **decision algorithm**:

1. **Identify the physical device/phenomenon:** Is mass accumulating, depleting, or constant?
2. **Check for mass flow:** Does fluid enter/exit the region of interest?
   - If **NO** → Choose a **Closed System (Control Mass)**.
   - If **YES** → Choose an **Open System (Control Volume)**.
3. **Define the boundary:** Real or imaginary? Fixed or moving?
4. **List all interactions:** Identify heat ($Q$ or $\dot{Q}$), work ($W$ or $\dot{W}$), and mass flow ($\dot{m}$).
5. **Apply the appropriate conservation law.**

## 2.2 Properties: Extensive vs. Intensive

A critical KTU concept tied directly to systems:

| Property Type | Definition | Examples | Additive? |
|---------------|------------|----------|-----------|
| **Extensive** | Depends on the mass/size of the system | Mass $m$, Volume $V$, Total Energy $E$, Entropy $S$ | ✅ Yes |
| **Intensive** | Independent of system size | Pressure $P$, Temperature $T$, Density $\rho$, Specific volume $v$ | ❌ No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specific quantities** (lowercase symbols like $u$, $h$, $s$, $v$) are obtained by dividing an extensive property by mass — making them intensive. Example: $h = H/m$ (kJ/kg).

## 2.3 State, Process, Path, and Cycle

- **State:** The condition of a system described by its properties (e.g., $P = 200 \text{ kPa}$, $T = 400 \text{ K}$).
- **Process:** The change of state from State 1 to State 2.
- **Path:** The series of states through which a system passes during a process.
- **Cycle:** A process whose initial and final states are identical.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Applicable To |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\Delta E_{system} = E_{in} - E_{out}$ | General Energy Balance | All Systems |
| 2 | $\Delta m_{CV} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ | Mass Balance (rate form) | Control Volume |
| 3 | $\Delta m_{CV} = \sum m_{in} - \sum m_{out}$ | Mass Balance (transient) | Control Volume |
| 4 | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ | Conservation of Mass (Steady State) | Steady CV |
| 5 | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{out} - \left(h + \frac{ke + pe}{}\right)_{in}\right]$ | Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) | Steady CV |
| 6 | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[E_{CV}\right] + \sum \dot{m}_{out}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{in}$ | General Energy Balance (Unsteady CV) | Transient CV |
| 7 | $\Delta U = Q - W$ | First Law for Closed System | Closed System |
| 8 | $\dot{V} = A \cdot V_{avg}$ | Volumetric flow rate | CV inlets/outlets |
| 9 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V_{avg} = \frac{A \cdot V_{avg}}{v}$ | Mass flow rate | CV inlets/outlets |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** In the SFEE, $\dot{W}$ typically refers to **shaft work only** (turbine, compressor, pump work). Flow work $\dot{m}(Pv)$ is already *absorbed* into the enthalpy $h = u + Pv$, so it must NOT be counted separately.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The system/CV concept is foundational in:
- **Steam Power Plants:** Boiler, turbine, condenser, pump (all CVs).
- **Gas Turbines & Jet Engines:** Compressor, combustion chamber, turbine.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Compressor, condenser, expansion valve, evaporator.
- **Rocket Propulsion:** Nozzle (a CV with very high exit velocity).
- **Chemical Engineering:** Reactors, distillation columns, heat exchangers.
- **Pneumatic/Hydraulic Systems:** Pipe networks, valves, tanks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU thermodynamics problem in Modules 1–5 requires you to write the SFEE correctly. The **system selection step** is worth full marks even before calculation begins.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)

The SFEE is the cornerstone of open-system analysis. We derive it rigorously from the First Law applied to a moving fluid stream.

### Setup
Consider a generic open system (control volume) with **one inlet (1)** and **one outlet (2)**. A small parcel of fluid $\delta m$ enters at section 1 carrying energy $e_1$, and the same parcel leaves at section 2 carrying energy $e_2$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Total Energy of the Fluid Parcel**

For a flowing fluid, the total energy per unit mass is the sum of:
- Internal energy: $u$
- Kinetic energy (flow): $\dfrac{V^2}{2}$
- Potential energy (elevation): $gz$

$$e = u + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$$

**Step 2: Enthalpy — The Key Thermodynamic Combination**

When fluid enters the CV, the upstream fluid "pushes" it in — this is **flow work**. To push a volume $v$ (specific volume) at pressure $P$ across the inlet requires flow work $Pv$ per unit mass. This flow work is automatically delivered by the upstream fluid, so it is *part of the energy carried in*.

We therefore combine internal energy and flow work into **enthalpy**:

$$h = u + Pv$$

**Step 3: Energy Carried In by Mass $\delta m$ at Inlet**

The total energy entering with mass $\delta m$ is internal energy + flow work + KE + PE:

$$\delta E_{in} = \delta m \left(h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1\right)$$

**Step 4: Energy Carried Out by Mass $\delta m$ at Outlet**

$$\delta E_{out} = \delta m \left(h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2\right)$$

**Step 5: Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics**

For the control volume, the net energy change equals net energy in minus net energy out, plus any heat added and minus any work done:

$$\delta E_{CV} = \delta Q_{in} - \delta W_{out} + \delta E_{in} - \delta E_{out}$$

**Step 6: Substitute the Expressions**

$$\delta E_{CV} = \delta Q - \delta W + \delta m\left(h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1\right) - \delta m\left(h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2\right)$$

**Step 7: Convert to Rate Form (Divide by $dt$)**

Recall that mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \dfrac{dm}{dt}$, so $\dfrac{\delta m}{dt} = \dot{m}$. Also, $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{\delta Q}{dt}$ and $\dot{W} = \dfrac{\delta W}{dt}$.

$$\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \dot{m}\left(h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1\right) - \dot{m}\left(h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2\right)$$

**Step 8: Apply Steady-State Assumption**

At steady state, the energy stored inside the CV is **constant**, so $\dfrac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = 0$. The mass flow rate in equals the mass flow rate out ($\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}$).

$$0 = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \dot{m}\left[\left(h_1 - h_2\right) + \left(\frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2}\right) + g(z_1 - z_2)\right]$$

**Step 9: Final SFEE Form (Rearrange)**

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[\left(h_2 - h_1\right) + \left(\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}\right) + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Special Cases of SFEE

Different engineering devices use different simplifications of the SFEE. Memorize this matrix.

| Device | $\dot{Q}$ | $\dot{W}$ | $\Delta KE$ | $\Delta PE$ | Simplified SFEE |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Nozzle / Diffuser** | 0 (adiabatic) | 0 | ≠ 0 (dominant) | $\approx 0$ | $h_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2}$ |
| **Turbine** | 0 (adiabatic) | $\dot{W}_{out} > 0$ | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ | $\dot{W}_T = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_2)$ |
| **Compressor / Pump** | 0 (adiabatic) | $\dot{W}_{in} > 0$ | $\approx 0$ | 0 to small | $\dot{W}_C = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ |
| **Throttle Valve** | 0 (adiabatic) | 0 | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ | $h_1 = h_2$ (Isenthalpic) |
| **Heat Exchanger (no work)** | $\dot{Q}$ ≠ 0 | 0 | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ |
| **Boiler / Condenser** | $\dot{Q}$ ≠ 0 | 0 | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ |

## 3.3 Worked Example — Single-Stream Nozzle

A nozzle receives steam steadily at $h_1 = 3200 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $V_1 = 30 \text{ m/s}$ and discharges it at $V_2 = 180 \text{ m/s}$. The nozzle is adiabatic. Find the exit enthalpy $h_2$ (neglect PE changes).

**Solution:**

For an adiabatic nozzle: $\dot{Q} = 0$, $\dot{W} = 0$, $\Delta PE = 0$.

Apply SFEE per unit mass:

$$\frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2} = h_2 - h_1$$

Convert kinetic energies from $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ to $\text{kJ/kg}$ by dividing by $1000$:

$$\frac{V_1^2}{2} = \frac{(30)^2}{2 \times 1000} = \frac{900}{2000} = 0.45 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\frac{V_2^2}{2} = \frac{(180)^2}{2 \times 1000} = \frac{32400}{2000} = 16.20 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Substitute:

$$0.45 - 16.20 = h_2 - 3200$$

$$-15.75 = h_2 - 3200$$

$$h_2 = 3184.25 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

The kinetic energy increase of **15.75 kJ/kg** is supplied entirely by the drop in enthalpy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — System Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermodynamic System] --> B[Closed System or Control Mass]
    A --> C[Open System or Control Volume]
    A --> D[Isolated System]
    B --> B1["No Mass Transfer Across Boundary"]
    B --> B2["Energy Transfer as Q and W Allowed"]
    B --> B3["Example: Piston Cylinder, Pressure Cooker"]
    C --> C1["Mass and Energy Transfer Across Control Surface"]
    C --> C2["Governed by SFEE under Steady State"]
    C --> C3["Example: Turbine, Compressor, Nozzle, Pump"]
    D --> D1["No Mass and No Energy Transfer"]
    D --> D2["Idealized Universe, Perfect Thermos"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Control Volume Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Inlet Stream Section 1] -->|mass flow rate m dot, h1, V1, z1| CVS[Control Surface Boundary]
    CVS --> QC[Heat Transfer Q dot crosses surface]
    CVS --> WC[Shaft Work W dot crosses surface]
    CVS --> DEV[Control Volume contains Device or Process]
    DEV -->|mass flow rate m dot, h2, V2, z2| OUT[Outlet Stream Section 2]
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — SFEE Application Across Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SFEE[Steady Flow Energy Equation SFEE] --> N[Nozzle: KE change dominant]
    SFEE --> T[Turbine: Work output dominant]
    SFEE --> CMP[Compressor: Work input dominant]
    SFEE --> P[Pump: Work input liquid]
    SFEE --> V[Throttling Valve: Isenthalpic h1 equals h2]
    SFEE --> HEX[Heat Exchanger: Heat transfer dominant]
    SFEE --> MIX[Mixing Chamber: Streams combine]
    SFEE --> CHK[Chimney or Open Tank: PE may be significant]
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right System Type

| Situation | Choose | Reason |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Sealed gas in a rigid tank with heating | **Closed System** | No mass enters/exits |
| Steam flowing through a turbine | **Open System (CV)** | Steady mass flow in and out |
| Air being pumped into a leaking tank | **Open System (CV, unsteady)** | Mass accumulates in CV |
| Balloon losing air slowly | **Closed System** (per analysis convention) | The *air* is the system; balloon is boundary |
| Rocket expelling gas during flight | **Open System (CV around rocket)** | Propellant mass flows out |
| Piston-cylinder gas being compressed | **Closed System** | No mass crosses piston |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Define the following with suitable examples:**
(a) Thermodynamic system and surroundings
(b) Control volume
(c) Isolated system

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
(a) A **thermodynamic system** is a precisely defined region in space or a fixed quantity of matter selected for study, separated from the rest of the universe (called the **surroundings**) by a boundary. Example: The gas inside a piston-cylinder assembly.
(b) A **control volume (CV)** is a fixed region in space through which mass and energy can flow across its boundary (the control surface). Example: A steam turbine where fluid continuously enters and exits.
(c) An **isolated system** is one in which neither mass nor energy (heat or work) can cross the boundary with the surroundings. Example: A perfectly insulated, sealed rigid container (idealization of a thermos flask).

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Differentiate between a closed system and an open system. State the governing energy equation for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Closed System | Open System |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| Mass transfer across boundary | No | Yes |
| Energy transfer | Heat and work | Heat, work, and enthalpy of flowing streams |
| Mass inside | Constant ($m_1 = m_2$) | May vary (steady or unsteady) |
| Governing equation | First Law: $\Delta U = Q - W$ | SFEE: $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[\Delta h + \Delta \dfrac{V^2}{2} + g\Delta z\right]$ |
| Example | Piston-cylinder device | Steam nozzle |

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose Either A or B)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`**

(a) **[7 Marks]** **Explain in detail the classification of thermodynamic systems with neat sketches and suitable examples. Discuss the role of the boundary in each case.**

**Model Answer:**

The thermodynamic system is the chosen focus of analysis, and is classified based on mass and energy interactions with the surroundings.

**1. Closed System (Control Mass):**
A system where no mass crosses the boundary, but energy (heat $Q$ and work $W$) may cross. The mass of the system remains constant. The boundary may be rigid or movable. Example: A gas trapped in a piston-cylinder, where the piston compresses or expands without any gas escaping.

**2. Open System (Control Volume):**
A system where both mass and energy cross the boundary. The control volume is a fixed region in space bounded by a control surface. Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ enters and exits. Example: A compressor where air continuously enters at low pressure and exits at high pressure.

**3. Isolated System:**
A special case where neither mass nor any form of energy (heat or work) crosses the boundary. The system is completely insulated and sealed. Example: A perfectly insulated rigid dewar flask containing a fixed amount of fluid.

**Role of the Boundary:**
The boundary defines what is "inside" the system. It may be **real** (physical wall of a vessel) or **imaginary** (dashed surface around a turbine). It may be **adiabatic** (no heat transfer) or **diathermic** (heat transfer allowed). It may be **rigid** (no volume change) or **movable** (piston boundary). Every KTU problem begins by clearly drawing this boundary.

**Sketch:** (a) Closed system — piston cylinder with $Q$ entering and $W$ leaving; (b) Open system — turbine with inlet stream (1) and outlet stream (2) and a control surface enclosing it; (c) Isolated system — sealed insulated tank.

---

(b) **[7 Marks]** **A steady-flow adiabatic turbine receives steam at $h_1 = 3000 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $V_1 = 60 \text{ m/s}$, $z_1 = 10 \text{ m}$, and discharges at $h_2 = 2500 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $V_2 = 150 \text{ m/s}$, $z_2 = 6 \text{ m}$. The mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = 5 \text{ kg/s}$. Determine the power output of the turbine. Neglect heat transfer. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $h_1 = 3000 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_2 = 2500 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $V_1 = 60 \text{ m/s}$, $V_2 = 150 \text{ m/s}$
- $z_1 = 10 \text{ m}$, $z_2 = 6 \text{ m}$
- $\dot{m} = 5 \text{ kg/s}$, $\dot{Q} = 0$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Apply SFEE for adiabatic turbine:**

$$\dot{W}_T = \dot{m}\left[(h_1 - h_2) + \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2} + g(z_1 - z_2)\right]$$

**Convert KE and PE to kJ/kg (divide by 1000):**

$$\frac{V_1^2}{2} = \frac{60^2}{2000} = \frac{3600}{2000} = 1.80 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\frac{V_2^2}{2} = \frac{150^2}{2000} = \frac{22500}{2000} = 11.25 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$g(z_1 - z_2) = \frac{9.81 \times (10 - 6)}{1000} = \frac{39.24}{1000} = 0.03924 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Enthalpy drop:**

$$h_1 - h_2 = 3000 - 2500 = 500 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Substitute all values:**

$$\dot{W}_T = 5 \times \left[500 + (1.80 - 11.25) + 0.03924\right]$$

$$\dot{W}_T = 5 \times \left[500 - 9.45 + 0.03924\right]$$

$$\dot{W}_T = 5 \times 490.589$$

$$\boxed{\dot{W}_T \approx 2452.95 \text{ kW} \approx 2.45 \text{ MW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Top Reasons Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Kinetic energy in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ must be converted to $\text{kJ/kg}$ by dividing by $1000$. Many students write $1.80$ directly, giving a wrong answer.
> 2. **Sign convention errors:** $\dot{W}_T$ is power *output* — write SFEE so that positive $\dot{W}$ means work output.
> 3. **Forgetting PE term:** Even if small, KTU examiners reward including it explicitly.
> 4. **Not stating the system type:** Always write *"System: Open (Control Volume around turbine, steady state)"* before applying SFEE.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]`**

(a) **[7 Marks]** **Define the term "control volume." Derive the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) from the first law of thermodynamics for an open system with one inlet and one outlet.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Control Volume (CV)** is a fixed region in space, bounded by a control surface, through which mass and energy may flow. It is the framework for analyzing open systems like turbines, pumps, and nozzles.

**Derivation of SFEE:**

Applying the First Law to the CV with a small parcel of mass $\delta m$ moving from inlet (1) to outlet (2):

$$\delta E_{CV} = \delta Q - \delta W + \delta m\left(h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1\right) - \delta m\left(h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2\right)$$

Dividing by $dt$ and applying steady state ($\dfrac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = 0$, $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}$):

$$0 = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \dot{m}\left[\left(h_1 - h_2\right) + \left(\frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2}\right) + g(z_1 - z_2)\right]$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \left(\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}\right) + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]}$$

(Full step-by-step derivation is given in Section 3.1 of these notes.)

---

(b) **[7 Marks]** **Steam enters a nozzle at $P_1 = 1.4 \text{ MPa}$, $T_1 = 300 °\text{C}$, $V_1 = 30 \text{ m/s}$, and exits at $P_2 = 0.7 \text{ MPa}$, $V_2 = 200 \text{ m/s}$. For an adiabatic nozzle, determine the exit temperature. Use steam tables: $h_1 = 3040.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_{2@700 kPa, sat} = 2763.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_{f@700 kPa} = 697.22 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_{fg@700 kPa} = 2066.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**System:** Open (Control Volume around nozzle), adiabatic, steady state.

**SFEE for adiabatic nozzle:**

$$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$$

**Convert kinetic energies to kJ/kg:**

$$\frac{V_1^2}{2} = \frac{30^2}{2000} = 0.45 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\frac{V_2^2}{2} = \frac{200^2}{2000} = 20.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Solve for $h_2$:**

$$h_2 = h_1 + \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2} = 3040.4 + 0.45 - 20.0 = 3020.85 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Identify the state at exit:**

At $P_2 = 700 \text{ kPa}$: $h_{2@sat} = 2763.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

Since $h_2 = 3020.85 > h_{2@sat} = 2763.5$, the steam at exit is **superheated**.

**Find exit temperature by interpolation in superheated steam tables** (at $P = 0.7 \text{ MPa}$):

Standard superheated steam table values at $P = 700 \text{ kPa}$:
- At $T = 200 °\text{C}$: $h \approx 2844.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- At $T = 250 °\text{C}$: $h \approx 2950.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- At $T = 300 °\text{C}$: $h \approx 3059.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$

Interpolate between 250 °C and 300 °C to find $T$ where $h = 3020.85$:

$$T_2 = 250 + \frac{3020.85 - 2950.0}{3059.8 - 2950.0} \times (300 - 250)$$

$$T_2 = 250 + \frac{70.85}{109.8} \times 50 = 250 + 32.26$$

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 282.3 °\text{C}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. Many students forget that at exit pressure $700 \text{ kPa}$, the saturation enthalpy is **2763.5 kJ/kg**, and incorrectly assume the steam is wet without checking $h_2 > h_g$.
> 2. Interpolation errors are common — keep values to **4 decimal places** before rounding the final answer.
> 3. Always state *"System: Adiabatic nozzle, open CV, steady state"* at the top of your answer for full marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Board Revision — Systems & Control Volumes:**

- ✅ **System** = the chosen region for analysis; **Surroundings** = everything else; **Boundary** = the interface.
- ✅ **Closed System (Control Mass):** $m$ = constant, only $Q$ and $W$ cross the boundary. Governing law: $\Delta U = Q - W$.
- ✅ **Open System (Control Volume):** Mass flows across the control surface. Governing law: **SFEE**.
- ✅ **Isolated System:** No mass, no energy transfer — universe itself is the only true example.
- ✅ **Boundary Types:** Real vs. Imaginary, Fixed vs. Movable, Adiabatic vs. Diathermic, Rigid vs. Deformable.
- ✅ **Extensive properties** ($m, V, U, H, S$) scale with size; **Intensive properties** ($P, T, \rho, v$) do not.
- ✅ **Specific properties** are intensive (e.g., $u$, $h$, $s$).
- ✅ **SFEE (one inlet, one outlet, steady state):**

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$

- ✅ **Enthalpy** $h = u + Pv$ already includes flow work — do **not** add it separately.
- ✅ **Nozzle:** KE increases, $h$ decreases. **Turbine:** $W$ out, $h$ drops. **Compressor/Pump:** $W$ in, $h$ rises. **Throttle:** $h_1 = h_2$.
- ✅ **Always convert** $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ to $\text{kJ/kg}$ by dividing by $1000$ in SFEE problems.
- ✅ **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$. **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- ✅ **First step in every KTU answer:** Identify the system type explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "System" in Engineering Thermodynamics?

In **Engineering Thermodynamics**, a **system** is a precisely defined quantity of matter or a region in space chosen for the purpose of thermodynamic analysis, separated from its surroundings by a well-defined boundary (real or imaginary).

Everything outside the system boundary is called the **surroundings** (or environment), and the **boundary** itself is the closed surface that encloses the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 begins with the "Basic Concepts" — and the **classification of systems** is the foundational building block. Every subsequent law (1st Law, 2nd Law) is applied to a *specific type of system*, so identifying the system correctly is mandatory for marks in every Part B question.

### Formal Definition (KTU Board Terminology)

A **thermodynamic system** is a finite, geometrically definable region of the universe containing a definite quantity of matter (or a specific volume in space) upon which the attention is focused for thermodynamic study. The **boundary** may be **fixed**, **movable**, **real**, or **imaginary**, and it controls the exchange of **mass** and **energy** between the system and surroundings.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Fish Tank" Model

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a sealed rectangular **fish tank** filled with water (the system). The glass walls of the tank are the **boundary**. The room where the tank is kept is the **surroundings**.
> - If the tank is **sealed and rigid** → no water or air leaks in or out, but heat can pass through glass → **Closed System**.
> - If you cut a hole and water flows in/out continuously while you study the water level inside → **Open System**.
> - If the tank is also perfectly insulated with no heat transfer → **Isolated System**.

This single analogy maps all four major system types — making it the highest-recall visual for your KTU viva and 3-mark questions.

### Fundamental Categories at a Glance

| Category | Exchange of Mass? | Exchange of Energy? | Boundary Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open System** | Yes | Yes | Real or imaginary, often fixed |
| **Closed System** | No | Yes | Real, can be movable/rigid |
| **Isolated System** | No | No | Real, rigid, adiabatic |
| **Adiabatic System** | No (usually) | No (heat) | Perfectly insulated |

### Special Classifications (Frequently Asked in KTU 3-markers)

1. **Homogeneous System** — A system that is completely uniform in its chemical composition and physical state throughout (e.g., a mixture of gases in equilibrium, pure water).
2. **Heterogeneous System** — A system consisting of two or more **homogeneous parts (phases)** that are physically distinct and separated by definite boundaries (e.g., water + ice + vapour inside a closed vessel).
3. **Simple System** — A system that is *homogeneous*, *isotropic*, *chemically inert*, *non-magnetic*, *non-electrolytic*, and unaffected by surface, gravitational, or capillary effects. (Pure substances in classical thermodynamics are simple systems.)

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314 \text{ kJ/kmol}\cdot\text{K}$
- **Standard Temperature & Pressure (SATP):** $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$, $P = 100 \text{ kPa}$
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars — Detailed Mechanics

### (A) Closed System (Control Mass)

A **closed system** is a fixed quantity of matter across whose boundary **no mass transfer** occurs, but **energy** (as heat or work) can cross the boundary.

- Mass inside = **constant** ($m = \text{const}$)
- Volume may change (piston-cylinder).
- Energy $E$ can change due to heat $Q$ and work $W$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?** Every 1st Law application in closed systems revolves around $\Delta E = Q - W$. Most KTU Module 1 numerical problems use a closed system (piston-cylinder, rigid tank, etc.).

### (B) Open System (Control Volume)

An **open system** is a chosen region in space across whose boundary **both mass and energy** may flow.

- Fixed in space (the *control volume*).
- Mass within can change as fluid enters/exits.
- Energy crosses via **heat, work, AND mass flow**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sub-types KTU examiners love:**
> - **Steady Flow Open System:** All properties at any point inside the CV do not change with time.
> - **Unsteady (Transient) Flow:** Properties at a point change with time (e.g., tank filling).
> - **Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** is the flagship equation of open systems in Module 2 — but the *system type* must be identified in Module 1.

### (C) Isolated System

A system where **neither mass nor energy** (heat or work) crosses the boundary.

- Total mass $m$ = constant
- Total energy $E$ = constant
- Mathematically, $\Delta m = 0$, $\Delta E = 0$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** The **entire universe** is the ultimate isolated system (relative to itself). A perfectly insulated, sealed thermos flask in deep space approximates this.

### (D) Adiabatic System

A system where **no heat transfer** occurs across the boundary ($Q = 0$). Mass may or may not cross.

- $Q = 0$
- Work transfer may still occur (e.g., rapid compression of gas in an insulated piston).
- Closely related to **adiabatic process**, not a system type per se — KTU examiners frequently ask this distinction.

## 2.2 Extended Classifications (Beyond the Big Four)

### Homogeneous vs. Heterogeneous

A **phase** is a quantity of matter that is *homogeneous* in chemical composition, physical state, and structure.

- **Homogeneous System** → Single phase (e.g., air in a room).
- **Heterogeneous System** → Two or more phases (e.g., water boiling in an open kettle: liquid + vapour phases).

### Isolated, Closed & Adiabatic — Subtle Differences

| Feature | Closed | Isolated | Adiabatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass transfer | ❌ | ❌ | Allowed |
| Heat transfer | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Work transfer | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Best real example | Piston-cylinder | Sealed Dewar flask in space | Insulated compressor |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Governing Equation | Description | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass conservation (Closed) | $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = 0$ | Mass inside is invariant | kg |
| Mass conservation (Open, steady) | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ | Mass flow balanced | kg/s |
| Mass conservation (Open, unsteady) | \dfrac{dm_{cv}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} | CV mass changes with time | kg/s |
| Energy conservation (Closed) | $\Delta E = Q - W$ | 1st Law for closed system | kJ |
| Isolated system | $\Delta E = 0$, $\Delta m = 0$ | Total energy and mass fixed | — |
| Adiabatic condition | $Q = 0$ | No heat transfer across boundary | — |
| Ideal gas (within simple system) | $PV = mRT$ | Pressure-Volume-Temperature relation | Pa·m³, kg, J/kg·K |
| Density (homogeneous) | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Mass per unit volume | kg/m³ |
| Specific volume | $v = \dfrac{V}{m} = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Volume per unit mass | m³/kg |
| Molar quantity | $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ | Moles from mass & molar mass | kmol |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Guard:** In KTU exam answer sheets, students often write "$Q = 0$" for an *adiabatic system* and confuse it with an *isolated system*. Always remember: **Isolated ⇒ Adiabatic, but Adiabatic ⇏ Isolated** (because work can still cross an adiabatic boundary).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants (Steam/Rankine):** Open, steady-flow system across boiler, turbine, condenser, pump.
- **Reciprocating Compressors & IC Engines:** Closed system (gas inside cylinder) but adiabatic in idealised thermodynamic cycles.
- **Rocket Nozzles:** Open, steady, adiabatic systems — analysed using SFEE.
- **Cryogenic Dewar Flasks:** Approximate isolated systems for storing liquid nitrogen/helium.
- **Chemical Reactors (Reforming/Cracking):** Heterogeneous systems with multiple phases and reactive flow.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass Conservation for an Open System (Control Volume)

Consider a generic control volume (CV) with **$i$** inlets and **$j$** outlets. Let $\dot{m}_k$ be the mass flow rate crossing section $k$.

**Step 1 — Define the rate of change of mass inside the CV:**

$$
\frac{dm_{cv}}{dt}
$$

This is the *unsteady* contribution; positive if mass inside is increasing.

**Step 2 — Express net mass entering per unit time across all boundaries:**

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{i} \dot{m}_{in,k} - \sum_{k=1}^{j} \dot{m}_{out,k}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of mass (no mass is created or destroyed in classical thermodynamics):**

$$
\frac{dm_{cv}}{dt} = \sum_{k=1}^{i} \dot{m}_{in,k} - \sum_{k=1}^{j} \dot{m}_{out,k}
$$

**Step 4 — Specialisation to Steady State** (property $\phi$ at any point does not change with time, $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = 0$):

$$
\frac{dm_{cv}}{dt} = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{i} \dot{m}_{in,k} = \sum_{k=1}^{j} \dot{m}_{out,k}
$$

> **Logic:** Under steady state, what enters must leave — the simplest mass-balance form, used as the first line of almost every KTU Module 2 SFEE problem.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-System Energy Balance (First Law Sign Convention)

For a **closed system** of fixed mass $m$, let $E$ denote the total energy (internal $U$, kinetic $KE$, potential $PE$).

**Step 1 — State the First Law (energy is conserved):**

Net energy added to the system = Increase in stored energy of the system

**Step 2 — Define the quantities:**

- $Q$ = heat transferred *to* the system (positive when entering)
- $W$ = work done *by* the system (positive when leaving)

**Step 3 — Write the differential form:**

$$
dE = \delta Q - \delta W
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over a finite process (state 1 → state 2):**

$$
E_2 - E_1 = Q_{1\to 2} - W_{1\to 2}
$$

$$
\Delta E = Q - W
$$

**Step 5 — Specialisation to Isolated System** ($Q = 0$, $W = 0$):

$$
\Delta E = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_2 = E_1
$$

**Step 6 — Specialisation to Adiabatic Process** ($Q = 0$):

$$
\Delta E = -W
$$

> **Logic:** Notice the sign convention — this is the *thermodynamic* sign convention preferred in KTU valuation (heat *in* positive, work *by* positive). Mixing with the *mechanical engineering* sign convention (work *in* positive) is the #1 cause of 1-mark deduction in numerical answers.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Classify a System in a KTU Problem

| Observation in Problem Statement | Inferred System Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| "Gas in a sealed piston-cylinder" | **Closed** | Mass cannot escape the rigid piston boundary |
| "Rigid, perfectly insulated tank in space" | **Isolated** | No mass, no heat, no work crosses |
| "Compressor with continuous air flow" | **Open, steady** | Air streams in and out; properties are time-invariant |
| "Bucket filling with water from a tap" | **Open, unsteady** | Mass of water in bucket grows with time |
| "Boiling water + steam mixture in a kettle" | **Heterogeneous** | Two distinct phases co-exist |
| "Pure liquid water at 25 °C" | **Simple, homogeneous** | Single phase, single chemical species |
| "Insulated pipe carrying steam, $\dot{m}$ constant" | **Open, steady, adiabatic** | All three conditions simultaneously |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration — Density & Mass of a Homogeneous System

**Problem:** A copper sphere of radius $r = 0.10 \text{ m}$ is treated as a homogeneous system. Given copper density $\rho_{Cu} = 8960 \text{ kg/m}^3$, compute the mass and verify it as a closed-system invariant.

**Step 1 — Compute the volume of the sphere:**

$$
V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi (0.10)^3
$$

$$
V = \frac{4}{3} \pi (0.001) = 4.1888 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute mass using $\rho = m/V \Rightarrow m = \rho V$:**

$$
m = 8960 \times 4.1888 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
m = 37.53 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret as a closed system:**

Since the sphere is a solid, impermeable copper body, $dm/dt = 0$, confirming closed-system behaviour for any thermodynamic process acting on it.

## 3.5 Analytical Proof — Why an Isolated System has Constant Energy

Using the **First Law** for a closed system (from §3.2):

$$
\Delta E = Q - W
$$

For an **isolated system**, the boundary is rigid (no work: $W = 0$) and impermeable to heat ($Q = 0$). Substituting:

$$
\Delta E = 0 - 0 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad E = \text{constant}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "Prove that the total energy of an isolated system is constant," this three-line proof is the *exact* expected answer. Do not over-elaborate — brevity with correct logic scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Thermodynamic Systems

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Thermodynamic System] --> B1[Mass Transfer?]
    B1 -- YES --> C1[Open System]
    B1 -- NO --> C2[Closed System]
    C1 --> D1[Steady Flow]
    C1 --> D2[Unsteady Flow]
    C2 --> E1[Heat Transfer?]
    E1 -- YES --> F1[Adiabatic? NO]
    E1 -- NO --> F2[Isolated System]
    F1 --> G1[Work Transfer Possible]
    F1 --> G2[No Work Possible]
    C2 --> H1[Adiabatic Process]
    H1 --> I1[Q = 0]
    A0 --> J1[Homogeneous System]
    A0 --> J2[Heterogeneous System]
    J2 --> K1[Multiple Phases]
    J1 --> L1[Single Phase]
    L1 --> M1[Simple System]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Closed System (Piston-Cylinder)

```mermaid
graph LR
    BOUNDARY1[Boundary Rigid Cylinder Wall] --> ENERGY1[Energy Transfer]
    ENERGY1 --> HEAT[Heat Q In or Out]
    ENERGY1 --> WORK[Boundary Work W]
    MASSBLK[Fixed Mass m] --> STATE[State P T V U]
    STATE --> PROCESS[Process 1 to 2]
    PROCESS --> ANALYSE[Apply First Law dE = dQ minus dW]
    SURROUND[Surroundings] -- No Mass Transfer --> BOUNDARY1
    SURROUND -- Q and W Cross --> BOUNDARY1
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Open System (Control Volume)

```mermaid
graph TD
    INLET1[Inlet 1 Mass Flow rate m dot in] --> CV1[Control Volume Fixed in Space]
    INLET2[Inlet 2 Mass Flow rate m dot in 2] --> CV1
    CV1 --> OUTLET1[Outlet 1 Mass Flow rate m dot out]
    CV1 --> OUTLET2[Outlet 2 Mass Flow rate m dot out 2]
    QIN[Heat Transfer Q dot] --> CV1
    WIN[Work Transfer W dot] --> CV1
    CV1 --> ENERGY[Total Energy E Stored]
    CV1 --> STATE[Properties P T h ke pe at any point]
    STATE --> SFEE[Steady Flow Energy Equation Applies]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Visual Snapshot

```mermaid
graph TB
    NODE_A[Closed System] --> NOTE_A1[Mass fixed]
    NODE_A --> NOTE_A2[Energy crosses]
    NODE_B[Open System] --> NOTE_B1[Mass crosses]
    NODE_B --> NOTE_B2[Energy crosses]
    NODE_C[Isolated System] --> NOTE_C1[Mass fixed]
    NODE_C --> NOTE_C2[Energy fixed]
    NODE_C --> NOTE_C3[No Q and No W]
    NODE_D[Adiabatic System] --> NOTE_D1[Q = 0]
    NODE_D --> NOTE_D2[W may occur]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `NODE_A1`, `INLET1`) and labels contain raw text only — no markdown bold tags or HTML inside the double-quoted strings. This guarantees clean rendering in any Mermaid-compatible renderer (GitHub, Obsidian, VS Code preview).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`
**Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Closed system, (ii) Open system, (iii) Isolated system.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Closed System (Control Mass):** A system in which a fixed quantity of matter is enclosed within a boundary, and **no mass transfer** occurs across it, although energy in the form of heat and work may cross the boundary.
*Example:* Gas enclosed in a piston-cylinder assembly.

**(ii) Open System (Control Volume):** A system in which **both mass and energy** can cross the boundary. The chosen region in space is called the *control volume*.
*Example:* A turbine through which steam flows steadily.

**(iii) Isolated System:** A system in which **neither mass nor energy** (heat or work) crosses the boundary. The total mass and total energy remain constant.
*Example:* A sealed, rigid, perfectly insulated thermos flask in deep space.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently label a rigid, insulated tank as "closed" when it is actually **isolated**. The distinction matters: a closed tank can still receive heat (Q ≠ 0); only when both Q and W are zero does it become isolated. *Lose 1 mark per wrong classification.*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`
**Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous systems with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Homogeneous System | Heterogeneous System |
|---|---|---|
| Composition | Uniform throughout | Non-uniform; varies region to region |
| Number of phases | Single phase | Two or more distinct phases |
| Phase boundary | None | Definite phase boundaries present |
| Example | Pure liquid water at 25 °C | Boiling water + saturated steam in a kettle |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Choice Format:** Each question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Two complete alternative choices are provided below.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 1]` (14 Marks)

**(a) Classify thermodynamic systems. Describe open and closed systems with neat sketches and examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of System:** A thermodynamic system is a finite region of the universe containing a definite quantity of matter or a specific volume in space, separated from the surroundings by a real or imaginary boundary.

**2. Classification (complete tree):**

- Based on mass transfer: Open System, Closed System
- Based on energy transfer: Adiabatic System (Q = 0)
- Special: Isolated System (no mass, no energy)
- Based on composition: Homogeneous, Heterogeneous
- Based on phase: Simple System (single phase, inert, isotropic)

**3. Closed System:**

- Fixed mass, no mass transfer across boundary.
- Energy (heat and work) can cross.
- **Sketch:**

```
   ___________________________
  |    Gas (m = constant)     |  <-- Closed System
  |     T1, P1, V1, U1        |
  |___________________________|
   |Q -->|        |--> W|        <-- Heat & Work cross
   -----Boundary (movable piston)-----
```

- **Example:** Gas in a piston-cylinder; refrigerant sealed inside a refrigeration coil during the compression stroke.

**4. Open System:**

- Region in space (control volume), not a fixed mass.
- Both mass and energy cross the boundary.
- **Sketch:**

```
   m_in --->  _____________  ---> m_out
             |   Control   |
   Q_in ---> |   Volume    | ---> W_out
             |_____________|
```

- **Example:** Nozzle, diffuser, turbine, compressor, boiler, condenser.

**(b) Two kg of air is contained in a rigid, insulated tank of volume 0.1 m³ at 300 K. The tank is now (i) heated, then (ii) stirred with a paddle-wheel. Classify the system for each process and write the corresponding First Law equation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the system:**

- Tank contains a fixed mass of air ($m = 2$ kg) → **Closed System** throughout.
- Volume is rigid → no boundary work.
- Tank is insulated → $Q = 0$.

**Step 2 — Process (i): Heating:**

Even though the tank is given as insulated, the question says "heated" — so we revise: during heating, $Q \neq 0$, $W = 0$ (rigid tank).
First Law:

$$
\Delta U = Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad m c_v (T_2 - T_1) = Q
$$

**Step 3 — Process (ii): Paddle-wheel stirring:**

Now tank is insulated again ($Q = 0$), but work is done on the system by the paddle (shaft work $W_{sh} > 0$ into system). Using the convention that work *done on* the system is taken as input:

$$
\Delta U = W_{in} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m c_v (T_2 - T_1) = W_{sh}
$$

**Step 4 — Final state:**

For both processes, since volume is constant, temperature rises. Air behaves approximately as an ideal gas with $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, so $c_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$.

**Classification remains "Closed System" in both (i) and (ii).** Only the *type of energy transfer* changes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students will write "Open System" for the paddle-wheel process because a shaft crosses the boundary. **This is WRONG.** A rotating shaft does NOT transfer mass — it only transfers energy as work. Hence, it remains a **closed system**. *Lose 2 marks* for this conceptual error.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1]` (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the concepts of homogeneous and heterogeneous systems. Discuss how the phase rule applies. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Homogeneous System:**

A system that is *completely uniform* in chemical composition, physical state, and physical properties throughout its extent. It has **one phase**.

- *Examples:* Pure water at 25 °C; air (a mixture of N₂, O₂, Ar) in a sealed room — because gases mix uniformly, the mixture is treated as one phase.

**2. Heterogeneous System:**

A system consisting of two or more *homogeneous parts (phases)* separated from each other by definite phase boundaries.

- *Examples:* Water and ice together; oil floating on water; boiling water with steam bubbles.

**3. The Phase Rule (Gibbs Phase Rule):**

$$
F = C - P + 2
$$

where:
- $F$ = number of degrees of freedom (intensive variables that can be varied independently)
- $C$ = number of components
- $P$ = number of phases
- $2$ refers to the two intensive variables (T and P) commonly considered.

**4. Application Examples:**

- **Pure water (single phase):** $C = 1$, $P = 1$ → $F = 1 - 1 + 2 = 2$. We can independently vary $T$ and $P$ within the liquid region.
- **Water + steam (saturated):** $C = 1$, $P = 2$ → $F = 1 - 2 + 2 = 1$. Only one variable (e.g., $T$ or $P$) can be fixed independently; the other is determined.

**(b) A gas undergoes a process from state 1 (P₁ = 200 kPa, V₁ = 0.05 m³) to state 2 (P₂ = 100 kPa, V₂ = 0.10 m³). The system is closed. If the change in internal energy is ΔU = −20 kJ, find the heat transferred during the process. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify system type:** Closed system (fixed mass, no mass transfer).

**Step 2 — Apply First Law for a closed system:**

$$
\Delta U = Q - W
$$

**Step 3 — Compute boundary work** (assume process is quasi-static and follows the P-V path linearly — area under curve = average pressure × ΔV):

Since $P$ varies linearly with $V$:

$$
W = \frac{P_1 + P_2}{2} \times (V_2 - V_1)
$$

$$
W = \frac{200 + 100}{2} \times (0.10 - 0.05)
$$

$$
W = 150 \times 0.05 = 7.5 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Q:**

$$
Q = \Delta U + W = -20 + 7.5 = -12.5 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

Negative $Q$ means heat is **transferred out of the system** (i.e., from the gas to the surroundings). The gas expands, does 7.5 kJ of work on the surroundings, and loses an additional 12.5 kJ as heat.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Sign-convention errors dominate this question. If you use the alternative convention (work *in* positive), you will write $Q = \Delta U + W$ incorrectly. **Always** state your sign convention at the top of the answer — it earns you 1 free mark and protects against 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Types of Systems**

- **System** = chosen region; **Surroundings** = everything else; **Boundary** = the separating surface (real/imaginary, fixed/movable).
- **Closed System (Control Mass):** $m$ = const; energy crosses (heat/work). *Examples: piston-cylinder, rigid tank with gas.*
- **Open System (Control Volume):** Both $m$ and $E$ cross. Steady (SFEE applies) or Unsteady (transient). *Examples: turbine, nozzle, compressor, pipe flow.*
- **Isolated System:** $m$ = const, $E$ = const, $Q = 0$, $W = 0$. *Example: ideal sealed Dewar in space.*
- **Adiabatic System/Process:** $Q = 0$ only. $W$ may occur. *Example: rapid compression in insulated cylinder.*
- **Homogeneous vs. Heterogeneous:** Homogeneous = single phase, uniform. Heterogeneous = multiple phases with phase boundaries.
- **Simple System:** Homogeneous + isotropic + inert + non-magnetic + non-electrolytic + free from surface/gravitational effects.
- **First Law (Closed System):** $\Delta E = Q - W$.
- **Mass Conservation (Open):** $\dfrac{dm_{cv}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}$.
- **Gibbs Phase Rule:** $F = C - P + 2$.
- **Sign Convention:** $Q$ positive *into* system, $W$ positive *by* system (KTU standard).
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314 \text{ kJ/kmol}\cdot\text{K}$.
- **Common Mistake:** Paddle-wheel/shaft work does NOT change a closed system into an open system — only mass transfer does.
- **Board Favourite:** "Classify the following as open/closed/isolated" — practice with at least 10 scenarios before the exam.
- **Isolated ⇒ Adiabatic, but Adiabatic ⇏ Isolated** — remember this implication direction.
- **Watch the boundary:** If mass crosses it → Open; if only energy crosses → Closed; if nothing crosses → Isolated.

---

> **End of Notes — Module 1, Types of Systems**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, aligned to NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of a System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of a System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "System" in Thermodynamics?

In Engineering Thermodynamics, a **system** is precisely defined as a **macroscopically identifiable quantity of matter** (or a region in space) which is selected for thermodynamic study and set apart from its surroundings by a real or imaginary boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *system* is any specified region of the universe in which we are interested. Everything outside the system is termed the **surroundings**, and the dividing line (real or imaginary, fixed or movable) separating the two is called the **boundary**.

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy (The "Fish Tank" Model)

Imagine a sealed **fish tank** sitting on a table:
- **System** = The water + the fish inside the tank (what we want to study).
- **Surroundings** = The air in the room, the table, the room walls (everything else).
- **Boundary** = The glass walls of the tank (a *real* boundary, here). The top water-air interface is an *imaginary* boundary if we consider just the water.

This mental model immediately tells us: **we choose what to study**, and the boundary is just our tool for isolation.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Systems (KTU High-Priority)

Systems in thermodynamics are broadly classified based on **two independent criteria**: (a) matter transfer and (b) energy transfer.

### A. Based on Matter & Energy Transfer

| # | Type | Matter Transfer | Energy Transfer | Classic Example |
|---|------|----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Closed System (Control Mass)** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Piston-Cylinder device with sealed gas |
| 2 | **Open System (Control Volume)** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Turbine, Compressor, Nozzle, Pump |
| 3 | **Isolated System** | ❌ No | ❌ No | Sealed thermos flask + rigid + adiabatic |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** A *closed system* permits **energy transfer (heat/work)** but **not mass transfer**. An *isolated system* permits **neither** — but please note: truly isolated systems are *idealizations*; no perfect one exists in engineering practice.

### B. Based on Boundary Behavior

- **Rigid Boundary** → No volume change (no boundary work). The pressure-volume work, $W = \int P\,dV$, becomes zero.
- **Movable Boundary** → Volume can change (e.g., piston sliding in a cylinder).
- **Adiabatic Boundary** → No heat transfer, $Q = 0$. The boundary is thermally insulating.
- **Diathermic (Permeable) Boundary** → Heat can flow through it, $Q \neq 0$.

---

## 1.3 What are "Properties" of a System?

A **property** is any *observable*, *measurable*, and *calculable* characteristic of a system in equilibrium to which a **definite numerical value** can be assigned **without reference to the history** of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Historical Path Rule:** If you need to know *how* the system got to its present state to assign a value, then it is **NOT a property** — it is a **process function** (like heat $Q$ or work $W$).

### 💡 Intuitive Analogy (The "Medical Report")

When you get a full-body check-up, the report lists measurable numbers:
- Blood pressure: **120/80 mmHg**
- Body temperature: **98.6 °F**
- Heart rate: **72 bpm**

These tell your doctor *your current state of health* **without** knowing what you ate yesterday or how you got there. These are your body's "**properties**." In thermodynamics, pressure $P$, temperature $T$, volume $V$, and internal energy $U$ play the exact same role.

---

## 1.4 The Two Grand Families of Properties

### A. Extensive Properties
Properties that **depend on the size (mass or extent) of the system**. If you split a system into two halves, each half has *half* the value.

$$V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 \quad \text{(additive)}$$

**Examples:** Volume $V$, Mass $m$, Internal Energy $U$, Enthalpy $H$, Entropy $S$, Total Heat Capacity $C$.

### B. Intensive Properties
Properties that are **independent of the system size** — they remain the same no matter how you slice the system.

$$T_{total} = T_1 = T_2 \quad \text{(non-additive)}$$

**Examples:** Temperature $T$, Pressure $P$, Density $\rho$, Specific Volume $v$, Specific Heat $c$, Velocity $\vec{V}$.
