### C. Specific Properties (The Bridge)

A **specific property** is just an *extensive property per unit mass*. It converts an extensive property into an intensive one:

$$x_{specific} = \frac{X_{extensive}}{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Derivative Test:** A practical, mathematical way to identify a property type is:
> - If $\frac{X}{m} = \text{constant}$ → **Intensive**.
> - If $X \propto m$ → **Extensive**.
> - If $\frac{X}{m} \to \text{use this directly}$ → **Specific property**.

The standard state symbols in KTU thermodynamics are:
- Lowercase letters for **specific (intensive)** quantities: $v, u, h, s, c$.
- Uppercase letters for **total (extensive)** quantities: $V, U, H, S, C$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic State and State Postulate

A **state** is the *complete set* of all thermodynamic properties at a given instant. Once the state is fixed, every property has a definite value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The State Postulate (KTU 2024 High-Yield):**
> The state of a simple compressible system (no electrical, magnetic, or surface effects) is completely specified by **two independent, intensive properties**.
>
> For example: knowing $(P, T)$, or $(P, v)$, or $(T, v)$ is sufficient to fix the entire thermodynamic state of a pure substance.

## 2.2 Logical Hierarchy: How Properties Interlock

Let's break down the *why* and *how*:

1. **Every property has a value at every equilibrium state.**
   *Why?* By definition, a property is a state function — it depends only on the state, not the path.

2. **A change in any single property means the state has changed.**
   *How?* Because properties are point functions, $\Delta X = X_2 - X_1$.

3. **Specific properties = Extensive ÷ Mass** (always intensive).
   *Why?* Dividing by mass normalizes the property to a per-unit basis, stripping away system size.

4. **A *change* in an extensive property depends on the system size; a *change* in a specific (intensive) property does not.**
   *Engineering Implication:* Engineers prefer intensive/specific properties because they are **substance properties** independent of how much material you have.

5. **Pressure and Temperature are the two most commonly measured intensive properties in the lab.**
   *Why?* They are directly measurable via manometers and thermocouples/thermometers.

6. **Density $\rho$ and Specific Volume $v$ are reciprocals.**
   *Why?* Both describe the same physical reality (mass per unit volume vs. volume per unit mass) from opposite perspectives.

## 2.3 🌟 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property (Symbol) | Type | Defining Equation | Standard SI Units |
|---|--------------------|------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Volume, $V$** | Extensive | Geometric measure | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 2 | **Mass, $m$** | Extensive | Matter content | $\text{kg}$ |
| 3 | **Specific Volume, $v$** | Intensive (Specific) | $v = V/m$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ |
| 4 | **Density, $\rho$** | Intensive | $\rho = m/V$ | $\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$ |
| 5 | **Pressure, $P$** | Intensive | $P = F/A$ | $\text{Pa}$ ($\text{N}/\text{m}^2$) |
| 6 | **Temperature, $T$** | Intensive | Measured via thermometer | $\text{K}$ |
| 7 | **Specific Gravity, $SG$** | Intensive (Dimensionless) | $SG = \rho_{sub}/\rho_{water@4°C}$ | — |
| 8 | **Internal Energy, $U$** | Extensive | Molecular kinetic + potential | $\text{J}$ |
| 9 | **Specific Internal Energy, $u$** | Intensive (Specific) | $u = U/m$ | $\text{J}/\text{kg}$ |
| 10 | **Enthalpy, $H$** | Extensive | $H = U + PV$ | $\text{J}$ |
| 11 | **Specific Enthalpy, $h$** | Intensive (Specific) | $h = u + Pv$ | $\text{J}/\text{kg}$ |
| 12 | **Entropy, $S$** | Extensive | $dS \geq \delta Q/T$ | $\text{J}/\text{K}$ |
| 13 | **Specific Entropy, $s$** | Intensive (Specific) | $s = S/m$ | $\text{J}/(\text{kg}\cdot\text{K})$ |

### 🔑 Key Reciprocal Identity

$$\rho = \frac{1}{v} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad v = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** When the KTU exam gives you density in $\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$ but asks for specific volume, just take the reciprocal. Specific volume is the **preferred quantity in steam tables**, not density, because the molar/mass-based normalization is more convenient for thermodynamic cycles.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Where "Properties of a System" is Used |
|-------|---------------------------------------|
| **Power Plant Design** | Steam tables give $h$, $s$, $v$, $u$ at every $(P, T)$ — used to size turbines and condensers. |
| **HVAC & Refrigeration** | Refrigerant property charts (R-134a, R-410A) for compressor/condenser duty. |
| **IC Engines** | Air-standard cycle analysis (Otto, Diesel) uses $c_p$, $c_v$, $R$, $\gamma$ as fixed intensive properties. |
| **Chemical Process** | Specific volume and density are critical for pump and pipeline sizing. |
| **Aerospace** | Specific enthalpy $h$ of combustion gases determines nozzle exit velocity and thrust. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Reciprocal Relationship between Density and Specific Volume

This is the most fundamental property derivation in Module 1, and KTU expects you to derive it cleanly.

### Starting Definition — Density

By definition, density is mass per unit volume:

$$\rho \equiv \frac{m}{V}$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of the system, in $\text{kg}$ (extensive),
- $V$ = volume occupied by the system, in $\text{m}^3$ (extensive).

### Starting Definition — Specific Volume

Specific volume is defined as volume per unit mass:

$$v \equiv \frac{V}{m}$$

where:
- $v$ = specific volume, in $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$,
- $V$ and $m$ are as defined above.

### Showing the Reciprocity

**Step 1.** Take the product $\rho \cdot v$:

$$\rho \cdot v = \left(\frac{m}{V}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{V}{m}\right)$$

**Step 2.** The $m$ in the numerator cancels with the $m$ in the denominator, and similarly $V$ cancels:

$$\rho \cdot v = \frac{m \cdot V}{V \cdot m}$$

**Step 3.** All units cancel to leave a dimensionless product:

$$\rho \cdot v = 1$$

**Step 4.** Solving for either variable:

$$\boxed{\rho = \frac{1}{v} \quad \text{and} \quad v = \frac{1}{\rho}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** If a numerical problem gives $\rho = 1.2\,\text{kg/m}^3$ (typical for air at STP), you *must* compute $v = 1/1.2 = 0.8333\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ before using ideal-gas law in mass-based form, $Pv = RT$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Property from Extensive Property

We want to prove that **if $X$ is extensive, then $x = X/m$ is intensive (specific)**.

### Setup

Consider a system of mass $m$. It has an extensive property $X$ (say, volume $V$, or total internal energy $U$).

### Definition of Specific Property

$$x \equiv \frac{X}{m}$$

where:
- $X$ has units of (some physical quantity),
- $m$ has units of $\text{kg}$.

### Step-by-Step Proof of Intensiveness

**Step 1.** Suppose we split the system into two halves, each of mass $m/2$.

**Step 2.** Because $X$ is *extensive*, it scales linearly with mass:

$$X_{total} = X_1 + X_2 \quad \text{and} \quad m_{total} = m_1 + m_2$$

**Step 3.** Compute the specific property of each half:

$$x_1 = \frac{X_1}{m_1} = \frac{X_1}{m/2}$$

$$x_2 = \frac{X_2}{m_2} = \frac{X_2}{m/2}$$

**Step 4.** Since $X_1 = X_2 = X_{total}/2$ (linear scaling of extensive properties):

$$\frac{X_1}{m/2} = \frac{X_{total}/2}{m/2} = \frac{X_{total}}{m} = x$$

**Step 5.** Therefore:

$$x_1 = x_2 = x \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{x \text{ is intensive (independent of subsystem size)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: The Specific Gravity–Density Link

### Definition

Specific Gravity (also called *relative density*) is the ratio of the density of a substance to that of water at **4 °C**, where $\rho_{water} = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{substance}}{\rho_{water,\,4°C}}$$

### Algebraic Inversion

Multiplying both sides by $\rho_{water}$:

$$\rho_{substance} = SG \cdot \rho_{water,\,4°C}$$

Substituting $\rho_{water} = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$:

$$\boxed{\rho_{substance} = 1000 \cdot SG \quad \text{[kg/m}^3\text{]}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Convert:** A substance with $SG = 0.8$ (e.g., gasoline) has density $0.8 \times 1000 = 800\,\text{kg/m}^3$. A substance with $SG = 13.6$ (mercury) has density $13.6 \times 1000 = 13{,}600\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem Setup:** A piston-cylinder device contains $m = 2\,\text{kg}$ of air occupying $V = 1.667\,\text{m}^3$ at $T = 300\,\text{K}$. Compute all the requested properties.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Substitution | Result |
|---|----------|---------|--------------|--------|
| 1 | Density, $\rho$ | $\rho = m/V$ | $2 / 1.667$ | $\rho = 1.2\,\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 2 | Specific Volume, $v$ | $v = V/m$ | $1.667 / 2$ | $v = 0.8335\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ |
| 3 | Reciprocal Check | $\rho \cdot v$ | $1.2 \times 0.8335$ | $\approx 1.0002 \approx 1$ ✅ |
| 4 | Specific Gravity of Air | $SG = \rho/\rho_{water}$ | $1.2 / 1000$ | $SG = 0.0012$ |
| 5 | Total Volume (Extensive) | $V$ | given | $V = 1.667\,\text{m}^3$ |
| 6 | Intensive Check | $V/m = v$ | $1.667/2$ | $v = 0.8335\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ ✅ |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how Step 3 confirms the **reciprocal identity** we just derived. In your KTU answer sheet, always show this check step for full marks.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Pressure Unit Conversions (Exam Favorite)

### From atm to Pa

$$1\,\text{atm} = 101{,}325\,\text{Pa}$$

Proof via standard atmosphere definition: $1\,\text{atm} = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$.

### From bar to Pa

$$1\,\text{bar} = 10^5\,\text{Pa} = 100\,\text{kPa}$$

### From mmHg (Torr) to Pa

$$1\,\text{mmHg} = 133.322\,\text{Pa}$$

This comes from hydrostatics:

$$P = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

with $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$, $h = 0.001\,\text{m}$:

$$P = 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.001 = 133.416\,\text{Pa} \approx 133.322\,\text{Pa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Pressure in thermodynamics must be **absolute** (not gauge). If a problem gives you "gauge pressure," add atmospheric pressure:
> $$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$
> Forgetting this is the **#1 mark-losing mistake** in property-based numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of System Properties

The following block diagram maps the complete logical hierarchy of all thermodynamic properties introduced in Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["THERMODYNAMIC PROPERTIES"]
    B1["EXTENSIVE PROPERTIES"]
    B2["INTENSIVE PROPERTIES"]
    C1["DEPEND ON SYSTEM SIZE"]
    C2["INDEPENDENT OF SYSTEM SIZE"]
    D1["EXAMPLES"]
    D2["EXAMPLES"]
    D3["EXAMPLES"]

    E2["m Mass"]
    E3["U Internal Energy"]
    E4["H Enthalpy"]
    E5["S Entropy"]
    F1["T Temperature"]
    F2["P Pressure"]
    F3["rho Density"]
    F4["v Specific Volume"]
    F5["c Specific Heat"]
    G1["u = U/m"]
    G2["h = H/m"]
    G3["s = S/m"]
    G4["v = V/m"]

    A --> B1
    A --> B2
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C2 --> D3
    D1 --> E1
    D1 --> E2
    D2 --> E3
    D2 --> E4
    D2 --> E5
    D3 --> F1
    D3 --> F2
    D3 --> F3

    D3 --> F5

    D4 --> G3
    D4 --> G4
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic: System, Boundary, Surroundings — All Three Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClosedSystem["CLOSED SYSTEM Control Mass"]
        CS_Boundary["Boundary Rigid + Adiabatic"]
        CS_Inside["Gas sealed inside piston-cylinder"]
        CS_Outside["Surroundings Air, Heat Reservoir"]
    end

    subgraph OpenSystem["OPEN SYSTEM Control Volume"]
        OS_Boundary["Imaginary Control Surface"]
        OS_Inside["Turbine Rotor + Flowing Steam"]
        OS_Outside["Surroundings Casing + Atmosphere"]
        OS_In["Inlet m_dot_in"]
        OS_Out["Outlet m_dot_out"]
    end

    subgraph IsolatedSystem["ISOLATED SYSTEM"]
        IS_Boundary["Rigid + Adiabatic + Sealed"]
        IS_Inside["Gas in Rigid Dewar Flask"]
        IS_Outside["Surroundings External Room"]
    end

    CS_Boundary --> CS_Inside
    CS_Outside -. Heat Q, Work W .-> CS_Boundary

    OS_Boundary --> OS_Inside
    OS_In --> OS_Inside
    OS_Inside --> OS_Out
    OS_Outside -. Energy Crossing .-> OS_Boundary

    IS_Boundary --> IS_Inside
    IS_Outside -. Nothing Crosses .-> IS_Boundary
```

---

## 4.3 Process Flow: How a Property Becomes a "Specific Property"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Extensive Property X (e.g., V)"]
    P2["Mass of System m"]
    P3["Divide X by m"]
    P4["Specific Property x (e.g., v)"]
    P5["Intensive and Tabular-Ready"]
    P6["Used in Steam Tables"]

    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
```

---

## 4.4 Pressure-Conversion Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Given Pressure Reading"]
    Q2["Identify Unit"]
    Q3{"Unit Type?"}
    Q4["Multiply by 101325"]
    Q5["Multiply by 100000"]

    Q7["Multiply by 6894.76"]
    Q8["P_abs in Pascals"]

    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 -->|atm| Q4
    Q3 -->|bar| Q5
    Q3 -->|mmHg| Q6
    Q3 -->|psi| Q7
    Q4 --> Q8
    Q5 --> Q8
    Q6 --> Q8
    Q7 --> Q8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety Note:** All node IDs above use only alphanumeric strings prefixed with letters (`ClosedSystem`, `OpenSystem`, etc.) — no reserved keywords like `end` or `style` are used as standalone IDs, ensuring clean compilation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between extensive and intensive properties of a thermodynamic system. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Extensive Properties | Intensive Properties |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Definition** | Properties that depend on the size (mass or extent) of the system. | Properties that are independent of the system size. |
| **Additivity** | Additive — value for whole system = sum of values of parts. | Non-additive — same value throughout the system at equilibrium. |
| **Mass Dependence** | $X \propto m$ | $X/m = \text{constant}$ |
| **Examples** | Volume $V$, Mass $m$, Internal Energy $U$, Enthalpy $H$, Entropy $S$. | Temperature $T$, Pressure $P$, Density $\rho$, Specific Volume $v$, Specific Heat $c$. |

**Mark Split:** 1 mark for clear definition of each, 1 mark for the table, 1 mark for correct examples.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the State Postulate for a simple compressible system. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **State Postulate** states:

> *"The state of a simple compressible system in equilibrium is completely specified by two independent, intensive properties."*

For example, fixing $(P, T)$ or $(P, v)$ or $(T, v)$ is sufficient to determine all other properties of a pure substance.

**Importance (1 mark):**
- It **reduces the number of independent variables** we need to specify a state from many to just two.
- It forms the **mathematical foundation** of all property tables and $P-v$, $T-v$, $P-T$ charts.
- It allows engineers to compute any desired property (like $u$ or $h$) from just two known measurements, using tabulated data.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define a *thermodynamic system*. Classify systems based on matter and energy transfer, giving one engineering example for each type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rigid tank contains $V = 0.5\,\text{m}^3$ of a gas. The density of the gas is $\rho = 1.25\,\text{kg/m}^3$. Determine: (i) the mass of the gas, (ii) the specific volume of the gas, (iii) the specific gravity of the gas, given that water at 4 °C has density $1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Part (a) Solution — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A *thermodynamic system* is a precisely defined region in space or a quantity of matter chosen for thermodynamic analysis, separated from the rest of the universe (the **surroundings**) by a real or imaginary boundary. The boundary controls the transfer of mass and energy between the system and surroundings.

**Classification (3 marks):**

| Type | Mass Transfer | Energy Transfer | Engineering Example |
|------|---------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Closed System (Control Mass)** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Gas sealed in a piston-cylinder during combustion |
| **Open System (Control Volume)** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Steam flowing through a turbine |
| **Isolated System** | ❌ No | ❌ No | A perfectly sealed, rigid, insulated thermos flask |

**Boundary Note (2 marks):**
- The boundary can be **real** (physical walls) or **imaginary** (mathematical surface around a turbine).
- It can be **fixed** (rigid tank) or **movable** (piston).
- It can be **adiabatic** (no heat) or **diathermic** (heat-permeable).

---

**(b) Part (b) Solution — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Volume: $V = 0.5\,\text{m}^3$
- Density: $\rho = 1.25\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- Reference: $\rho_{water,\,4°C} = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$

**(i) Mass of the gas (2 marks):**

Using the definition of density $\rho = m/V$, we solve for mass:

$$m = \rho \cdot V$$

Substituting:

$$m = 1.25 \times 0.5 = 0.625\,\text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{m = 0.625\,\text{kg}}$$

**(ii) Specific volume of the gas (2 marks):**

Specific volume is the reciprocal of density:

$$v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{V}{m}$$

$$v = \frac{1}{1.25} = 0.8\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{v = 0.8\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}}$$

**Verification using $v = V/m$:**

$$v = \frac{0.5}{0.625} = 0.8\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg} \quad \checkmark$$

**(iii) Specific gravity (2 marks):**

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{gas}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{1.25}{1000} = 0.00125$$

$$\boxed{SG = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Mark Split Summary:** 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for final answer highlight and verification.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the following with one example each: (i) Specific Volume, (ii) Specific Internal Energy, (iii) Specific Enthalpy. Why are specific properties preferred in engineering analysis? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A substance has a specific gravity of $1.8$. Find its: (i) density in $\text{kg/m}^3$, (ii) specific volume in $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$, (iii) the total volume occupied by $5\,\text{kg}$ of this substance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Part (a) Solution — 7 marks**

**(i) Specific Volume, $v$ (2 marks):**
- Definition: Volume per unit mass of the substance.
- Equation: $v = V/m$, units $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$.
- Example: For air at STP, $v \approx 0.833\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$.

**(ii) Specific Internal Energy, $u$ (2 marks):**
- Definition: Total internal energy (microscopic kinetic + potential energy of molecules) per unit mass.
- Equation: $u = U/m$, units $\text{J/kg}$.
- Example: For saturated water vapor at 100 °C, $u \approx 2506\,\text{kJ/kg}$.

**(iii) Specific Enthalpy, $h$ (2 marks):**
- Definition: Total enthalpy (internal energy + flow work) per unit mass.
- Equation: $h = u + Pv$, units $\text{J/kg}$.
- Example: For superheated steam at 3 MPa and 400 °C, $h \approx 3231\,\text{kJ/kg}$.

**Why specific properties are preferred (1 mark):**
They are **intensive**, **substance-intrinsic**, and **independent of system size**, making them ideal for tabulation (steam tables) and for comparing two systems on a per-unit-mass basis.

---

**(b) Part (b) Solution — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Specific Gravity: $SG = 1.8$
- $\rho_{water,\,4°C} = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- Mass: $m = 5\,\text{kg}$

**(i) Density (2 marks):**

$$\rho = SG \cdot \rho_{water}$$

$$\rho = 1.8 \times 1000 = 1800\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho = 1800\,\text{kg/m}^3}$$

**(ii) Specific Volume (2 marks):**

$$v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{1800}$$

$$v = 5.556 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 5.556 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}}$$

**(iii) Total Volume (3 marks):**

From the definition $v = V/m$:

$$V = m \cdot v$$

$$V = 5 \times 5.556 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$V = 2.778 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 2.778 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3}$$

**Mark Split Summary:** 2 + 2 + 3 marks, with the final step combining the specific-volume result from part (ii) into the volume calculation.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on "Properties of a System" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure** — Pressure in thermodynamics is **always absolute**. Forgetting to add $P_{atm} = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$ to gauge readings will lose 1–2 marks immediately.
>
> 2. **Mixing up specific volume $v$ and density $\rho$** — They are reciprocals. A common error is writing $v = \rho$ or forgetting the inversion. Always show $\rho \cdot v = 1$ as a verification step.
>
> 3. **Treating temperature in °C as if it were in K** — Thermodynamic calculations (especially ideal-gas law $Pv = RT$) **require absolute temperature in Kelvin**. Convert using $T(\text{K}) = T(°\text{C}) + 273.15$.
>
> 4. **Calling $Q$ (heat) and $W$ (work) "properties"** — They are **path functions** (process quantities), **not** properties. A property's value depends only on the state, never on how the state was reached.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — KTU examiners deduct marks for unitless answers. Always write units in full: $\text{kg/m}^3$, $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$, $\text{Pa}$, $\text{K}$.
>
> 6. **Missing the State Postulate** — When asked about "minimum properties to specify a state," the answer for a simple compressible system is **exactly two independent intensive properties**, not "two properties" (without specifying *independent* and *intensive*).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Properties of a System"**

- ✅ A **system** is a selected region for study; **surroundings** is everything else; **boundary** separates them.
- ✅ **Closed system**: no mass transfer, energy allowed. **Open system**: both allowed. **Isolated**: neither allowed.
- ✅ **Extensive properties** scale with system size (additive). **Intensive properties** do not.
- ✅ **Specific property** = extensive property ÷ mass → always intensive.
- ✅ **Density** $\rho = m/V$, **Specific Volume** $v = V/m$ → they are **reciprocals**: $\rho v = 1$.
- ✅ **Specific gravity** $SG = \rho_{sub}/\rho_{water}$ at 4 °C — a dimensionless ratio.
- ✅ **Pressure** in thermodynamic formulas is **absolute**, never gauge.
- ✅ **Temperature** in ideal-gas law is in **Kelvin** (absolute).
- ✅ **State Postulate**: 2 independent intensive properties fix the state of a simple compressible system.
- ✅ A **property** is a **state function** (path-independent). $Q$ and $W$ are **path functions** and **not** properties.
- ✅ Engineering utility: Steam tables, refrigeration cycles, IC engines, and aerospace propulsion all rely on the specific-property framework ($h$, $s$, $u$, $v$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State and Equilibrium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** State and Equilibrium # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a "State" in Thermodynamics? A **thermodynamic state** of a system is defined as the condition of the system at a specific instant, completely described by a set of measurable physical properties (such as pressure, temperature, volume, mass, internal energy, enthalpy, etc.). When all these properties have definite, fixed values, the system is said to be in a definite state. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Definition (Syllabus Standard):** > The *state* of a thermodynamic system is described by its **properties**. A system is in a known state when each of its properties has a definite value. The change of state occurs when one or more properties change value. The **state postulate** (also called the **two-property rule**) states that the state of a simple compressible system is completely specified by **two independent, intensive properties** (along with mass/amount of substance). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why two properties?** > For a single-component simple compressible system, once any two independent intensive properties (e.g., $P$ and $T$, or $P$ and $v$, or $T$ and $v$) are fixed, all other properties are automatically determined through equations of state. This is the foundation of property tables and charts used in KTU problems. --- ## 1.2 What is Equilibrium? **Thermodynamic Equilibrium** is the condition under which a system's macroscopic properties do not change with time, and there is no tendency for spontaneous change within the system or between the system and surroundings. Equilibrium is *not* a single condition — it is the simultaneous satisfaction of **three sub-equilibria**: | Sub-Equilibrium | Condition Satisfied | Property Constant | |---|---|---| | **Mechanical Equilibrium** | No unbalanced forces/pressures | Pressure ($P$) | | **Thermal Equilibrium** | No temperature differences | Temperature ($T$) | | **Chemical (or Phase) Equilibrium** | No chemical potential gradients / no mass transfer | Chemical Potential ($\mu$) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** > A system in thermodynamic equilibrium is one that satisfies ALL three equilibrium criteria **simultaneously**. If any one fails (e.g., a hot metal rod placed in cold water — temperatures differ), the system is *not* in equilibrium, and processes will spontaneously occur to drive it toward equilibrium. --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "State" as a Photograph Imagine you take a **photograph** of a room: - The **photograph itself** captures the *state* of the room at one instant: number of people, temperature reading on the thermometer, pressure shown on a gauge, volume of air, etc. - If you take another photograph **5 minutes later** and *anything* has changed — temperature rose, someone left, a window opened — then the room has changed its **state**. - **Equilibrium** is like taking 100 photographs over 10 minutes and finding **all of them identical**. Nothing is changing spontaneously. In this analogy: - Properties = details visible in the photograph (temperature, pressure, volume) - State = the entire photograph itself - Equilibrium = a series of identical photographs over time --- ## 1.4 Why Are "State" and "Equilibrium" Critical? Without these two concepts: - We could **not define** thermodynamic properties precisely. - We could **not construct** property tables (steam tables, refrigerant tables). - We could **not apply** the laws of thermodynamics (1st law, 2nd law) because they are formulated for systems in equilibrium. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** > This topic maps directly to **CO1** of PCMET403 — *Understand the fundamental concepts of thermodynamic systems, properties, and equilibrium.* Expect **direct 3-mark questions** on definitions and **derivation-based 7-mark questions** on equilibrium criteria. --- ## 1.5 Physical Constants Referenced - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/kmol·K}$ - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$ - **Triple Point of Water:** $T_{tp} = 273.16 \; \text{K}$, $P_{tp} = 0.6113 \; \text{kPa}$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Thermodynamic Properties Properties (state variables) are broadly classified into two categories: ### 2.1.1 Extensive Properties - Depend on the **size (mass/moles)** of the system. - **Additive**: total property = sum of properties of parts. - Examples: Volume ($V$), Internal Energy ($U$), Enthalpy ($H$), Entropy ($S)$, Mass ($m$), Total Heat ($Q$). ### 2.1.2 Intensive Properties - **Independent** of system size. - **NOT additive**. - Examples: Pressure ($P$), Temperature ($T$), Specific Volume ($v$), Specific Internal Energy ($u$), Density ($\rho$). > [!NOTE] > **Specific Property Rule:** > An *extensive* property divided by mass becomes *specific* (and therefore intensive). > Example: Specific volume $v = V/m$ (m³/kg) — intensive. ### 2.1.3 Special Category — Specific vs. Molar Properties - **Specific property** = extensive property per unit **mass** (units: per kg). - **Molar property** = extensive property per unit **mole** (units: per kmol). $$\bar{R} = M \cdot R_u \quad \text{where } \bar{R} \text{ is specific gas constant, } M \text{ is molar mass}$$ --- ## 2.2 The Concept of "State Postulate" in Depth For a **simple compressible system** (single component, electrically non-conducting, gravity negligible, only $P$ and $v$ as work-relevant variables): > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two-Property Rule (State Postulate):** > The state of a simple compressible system is completely specified by **two independent intensive properties**. **Why not one?** A single property is insufficient — e.g., specifying $P = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$ alone could correspond to many temperatures (water at 100°C, 200°C, 300°C — all at atmospheric pressure in different phases). **Why not three?** The equation of state $f(P, v, T) = 0$ provides one constraint linking three variables, leaving only **two degrees of freedom**. --- ## 2.3 Types of Thermodynamic Equilibrium — Detailed ### 2.3.1 Mechanical Equilibrium - **Definition:** Net force on every part of the system and between system and surroundings is zero. - **Mathematical Condition:** Pressure is **uniform** throughout the system at equilibrium. - **Time scale:** Mechanical equilibrium is established **fastest** (often assumed instantaneous in KTU problems). $$P_{\text{system}} = P_{\text{surroundings}} \quad \text{(no pressure gradient driving flow)}$$ ### 2.3.2 Thermal Equilibrium - **Definition:** No temperature difference exists between any parts of the system, or between system and surroundings. - **Mathematical Condition:** Temperature is **uniform** throughout. - **Governed by:** The **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics**. $$T_{\text{system,1}} = T_{\text{system,2}} = \dots = T_{\text{surroundings}}$$ ### 2.3.3 Chemical (Phase) Equilibrium - **Definition:** No net chemical reaction occurs within the system, and no net mass transfer between phases. - **Mathematical Condition:** Chemical potential of each species is **uniform** across all phases. - **Slowest to establish** — limits many real processes. $$\mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta} \quad \text{for species } i \text{ in phases } \alpha, \beta$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Mnemonic — "MTC":** > **M**echanical → Pressure equal > **T**hermal → Temperature equal > **C**hemical → Chemical Potential equal > All three must hold simultaneously for **complete thermodynamic equilibrium**. --- ## 2.4 Process and Path — Critical Distinction - **State:** A *point* in property space (defined by properties). - **Process:** The *path* by which the system moves from State 1 to State 2. - **Path:** The *specific sequence* of states traversed during a process. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** > Properties are **point functions** (depend only on state). Heat ($Q$) and Work ($W$) are **path functions** (depend on how the process is carried out). This distinction is heavily tested in 7-mark KTU questions. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units (SI) | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Ideal Gas Equation of State | $Pv = RT$ | $P$ in kPa, $v$ in m³/kg, $T$ in K | Per unit mass | | 2 | Universal Gas Equation | $PV = mRT = n\bar{R}_u T$ | kJ, m³, K | $\bar{R}_u = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K | | 3 | Specific Gas Constant | $R = \bar{R}_u / M$ | kJ/kg·K | $M$ = molar mass in kg/kmol | | 4 | Specific Volume | $v = V/m$ | m³/kg | Intensive property | | 5 | Density | $\rho = 1/v = m/V$ | kg/m³ | Intensive property | | 6 | State Postulate (simple compressible) | 2 independent intensive properties determine state | — | Foundation for property tables | | 7 | Mechanical Equilibrium | $\nabla P = 0$ | — | Pressure uniform | | 8 | Thermal Equilibrium | $\nabla T = 0$ | — | Temperature uniform | | 9 | Chemical Equilibrium | $\mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta}$ | J/mol | Chemical potential uniform | | 10 | Combined Gas Law | $P_1 v_1 / T_1 = P_2 v_2 / T_2$ | — | For ideal gas between two states | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** > Students often write $Pv = RT$ without specifying whether $R$ is the *specific* gas constant (kJ/kg·K) or the *universal* gas constant (kJ/kmol·K). The corresponding units of $v$ (m³/kg vs m³/kmol) MUST match. Marks are deducted for this inconsistency. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Steam Power Plants:** State postulates allow engineers to look up steam table values for enthalpy and entropy given $P$ and $T$ at any turbine stage. - **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Property tables for refrigerants (R-134a, R-410A) rely on the two-property rule. - **Gas Turbines & Jet Engines:** Combustion gases at high $T$ and $P$ require property evaluation for performance calculations. - **Chemical Process Industries:** Phase equilibrium governs distillation column design. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why Exactly Two Properties Specify the State of a Simple Compressible System ### Step 1: Define the System Class A simple compressible system is one in which: - The only relevant work mode is **boundary work** (PdV). - The system is electrically, magnetically, and gravitationally inert. - Composition is fixed (single component). ### Step 2: List Relevant Macroscopic Properties For such a system, the relevant macroscopic properties are: $$\{P, \, v, \, T, \, u, \, h, \, s, \, x, \dots\}$$ (pressure, specific volume, temperature, internal energy, enthalpy, entropy, quality) ### Step 3: Invoke the Equation of State An equation of state is an empirical or theoretical relation that **links** $P$, $v$, and $T$: $$f(P, v, T) = 0$$ For an **ideal gas**, this reduces explicitly to: $$Pv = RT$$ For a **real fluid**, examples include the van der Waals equation: $$\left(P + \dfrac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$$ ### Step 4: Apply Constraint Counting Among $P$, $v$, and $T$, there are **3 variables** and **1 constraint equation**. $$\text{Degrees of freedom} = 3 - 1 = 2$$ ### Step 5: Extend to Other Properties Once $P$ and $T$ (for example) are fixed, all other thermodynamic properties are determined by their defining relations: $$u = u(P, T), \quad h = h(P, T), \quad s = s(P, T)$$ These are obtained from the **fundamental relation** $u = u(s, v)$ combined with the equation of state. ### Step 6: Conclude the State Postulate Since exactly **two independent intensive properties** are needed and sufficient: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Statement (State Postulate):** > *The state of a simple compressible system is completely specified by two independent, intensive thermodynamic properties.* --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Mechanical, Thermal, and Chemical Equilibrium from Energy Minimization ### Step 1: Second Law Foundation The Second Law of Thermodynamics states that for an **isolated system** (no heat, work, or mass transfer with surroundings), the entropy $S$ attains a **maximum** at equilibrium. $$\left( \dfrac{dS}{d\xi} \right)_{\xi_0} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left( \dfrac{d^2 S}{d\xi^2} \right)_{\xi_0} < 0$$ where $\xi$ is any generalized displacement from equilibrium. ### Step 2: Mechanical Equilibrium from Entropy Max Consider an isolated system divided by a movable, diathermal, semi-permeable piston. Let $V_1$ and $V_2$ be sub-volumes. For mechanical equilibrium, vary $V_1$ (with total $V = V_1 + V_2$ constant): $$\left( \dfrac{\partial U_1}{\partial V_1} \right)_{S_1, N_1} = \left( \dfrac{\partial U_2}{\partial V_2} \right)_{S_2, N_2}$$ Using the thermodynamic identity: $$P = -\left( \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V} \right)_{S, N}$$ Therefore: $$P_1 = P_2$$ > This is the **mechanical equilibrium condition**. ### Step 3: Thermal Equilibrium Now vary entropy $S_1$ (with total $S = S_1 + S_2$ constant): $$\left( \dfrac{\partial U_1}{\partial S_1} \right)_{V_1, N_1} = \left( \dfrac{\partial U_2}{\partial S_2} \right)_{V_2, N_2}$$ Using: $$T = \left( \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial S} \right)_{V, N}$$ Therefore: $$T_1 = T_2$$ > This is the **thermal equilibrium condition**. ### Step 4: Chemical Equilibrium Finally, vary the mole number $N_1$ of species $i$ (with $N = N_1 + N_2$ constant): $$\left( \dfrac{\partial U_1}{\partial N_{1,i}} \right)_{S_1, V_1} = \left( \dfrac{\partial U_2}{\partial N_{2,i}} \right)_{S_2, V_2}$$ By definition of **chemical potential**: $$\mu_i = \left( \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial N_i} \right)_{S, V, N_{j \neq i}}$$ Therefore: $$\mu_{1,i} = \mu_{2,i}$$ > This is the **chemical equilibrium condition**. ### Step 5: Summary Table of Derivation | Equilibrium Type | Varied Parameter | Thermodynamic Identity | Equilibrium Condition | |---|---|---|---| | Mechanical | Volume $V$ | $P = -(\partial U/\partial V)_{S,N}$ | $P_1 = P_2$ | | Thermal | Entropy $S$ | $T = (\partial U/\partial S)_{V,N}$ | $T_1 = T_2$ | | Chemical | Mole number $N$ | $\mu_i = (\partial U/\partial N_i)_{S,V}$ | $\mu_{1,i} = \mu_{2,i}$ | --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: Using the State Postulate **Problem (KTU Typical):** A rigid tank contains 4 kg of nitrogen gas ($M = 28 \; \text{kg/kmol}$, $R = 0.2968 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$) at $P_1 = 1 \; \text{MPa}$ and $T_1 = 400 \; \text{K}$. The tank is then cooled to $T_2 = 300 \; \text{K}$. Determine the final pressure $P_2$. **Given:** - $m = 4 \; \text{kg}$ - $M_{N_2} = 28 \; \text{kg/kmol}$ - $R = \bar{R}_u / M = 8.314 / 28 = 0.2968 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$ - $P_1 = 1 \; \text{MPa} = 1000 \; \text{kPa}$ - $T_1 = 400 \; \text{K}$ - $T_2 = 300 \; \text{K}$ - **Rigid tank →** $V = \text{constant}$ **Find:** $P_2$ ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1:** Compute specific volume $v_1$ using the ideal gas equation $Pv = RT$: $$v_1 = \dfrac{RT_1}{P_1} = \dfrac{0.2968 \times 400}{1000}$$ $$v_1 = \dfrac{118.72}{1000} = 0.11872 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Step 2:** Since the tank is **rigid**, the volume and mass do not change, hence: $$v_2 = v_1 = 0.11872 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Step 3:** Apply ideal gas equation at State 2: $$P_2 v_2 = R T_2 \implies P_2 = \dfrac{RT_2}{v_2}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute numerical values: $$P_2 = \dfrac{0.2968 \times 300}{0.11872}$$ $$P_2 = \dfrac{89.04}{0.11872}$$ $$P_2 = 750.0 \; \text{kPa} = 0.75 \; \text{MPa}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification via State Postulate:** > State 2 was determined by specifying two independent intensive properties: $v_2$ (constant from rigid tank) and $T_2$ (cooling). The third property $P_2$ then follows automatically — illustrating the state postulate in action. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Distinguishing Properties and Equilibria | Quantity | Symbol | Type | Equilibrium It Constrains | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | Pressure | $P$ | Intensive | Mechanical | kPa | | Temperature | $T$ | Intensive | Thermal | K | | Specific Volume | $v$ | Intensive | (geometric) | m³/kg | | Internal Energy | $U$ (or $u$) | Extensive / Specific intensive | — | kJ / kJ/kg | | Enthalpy | $H$ (or $h$) | Extensive / Specific intensive | — | kJ / kJ/kg | | Entropy | $S$ (or $s$) | Extensive / Specific intensive | — | kJ/K / kJ/(kg·K) | | Mass | $m$ | Extensive | (Conservation) | kg | | Moles | $n$ or $N$ | Extensive | Chemical | kmol | | Chemical Potential | $\mu$ | Intensive | Chemical | kJ/kmol | | Density | $\rho$ | Intensive | (geometric) | kg/m³ | --- ## 3.5 Quasi-Static (Quasi-Equilibrium) Process — Important KTU Concept A **quasi-static process** (or quasi-equilibrium process) is one in which the system deviates from equilibrium by an **infinitesimally small** amount at every instant. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Significance:** > Only for quasi-static processes can we define **system pressure** and **system temperature** at every intermediate state. Real processes approach this limit if carried out slowly. **Mathematical Condition for Quasi-Static:** $$\Delta P_{\text{internal}} \to 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta T_{\text{internal}} \to 0$$ For a gas compressed slowly in a piston-cylinder: $$P_{\text{system}} \approx P_{\text{external}} \quad \text{(at every instant)}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concept Map: State and Equilibrium in Thermodynamics The following Mermaid diagram maps the conceptual hierarchy of "State and Equilibrium" as a block-level functional architecture. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["THERMODYNAMIC SYSTEM"] --> B["STATE OF SYSTEM"] A --> C["EQUILIBRIUM OF SYSTEM"] B --> B1["State Postulate: Two Independent Intensive Properties"] B --> B2["Equation of State: f P v T = 0"] B --> B3["Properties"] B3 --> B3a["Intensive: P, T, v, rho"] B3 --> B3b["Extensive: V, U, H, S, m"] B3 --> B3c["Specific vs Molar"] C --> C1["Mechanical Equilibrium"] C --> C2["Thermal Equilibrium"] C --> C3["Chemical Equilibrium"] C1 --> C1a["Pressure Uniform"] C1 --> C1b["P1 equals P2"] C2 --> C2a["Temperature Uniform"] C2 --> C2b["T1 equals T2"] C3 --> C3a["Chemical Potential Uniform"] C3 --> C3b["Mu i alpha equals Mu i beta"] C --> C4["Quasi Static Process Condition"] C4 --> C4a["Deviation from equilibrium is infinitesimal"] C4 --> C4b["P system approx P external at every instant"] B1 --> D["FOUNDATION FOR PROPERTY TABLES"] B2 --> D D --> E["Steam Tables, R134a Tables, Ideal Gas Tables"] D --> F["PV Diagrams, TS Diagrams, PH Diagrams"] ``` --- ## 4.2 Process Flow: How a System Reaches Equilibrium ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["STATE 1
Non Equilibrium
P1, T1, v1"] -->|Heat Transfer Q| S2["STATE 2
Non Equilibrium
P2, T2, v2"] S2 -->|Work Transfer W| S3["STATE 3
Approaching Equilibrium"] S3 -->|Continued Interaction| S4["STATE 4
FINAL EQUILIBRIUM
Pf, Tf, vf
All three M, T, C satisfied"] style S4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style S1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Property Classification Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD P["THERMODYNAMIC PROPERTIES"] --> Q["DEPENDENCE ON SYSTEM SIZE"] Q --> R["EXTENSIVE
Additive, depend on mass"] Q --> S["INTENSIVE
Independent of mass"] R --> R1["Examples: Volume V, Internal Energy U, Enthalpy H, Entropy S, Mass m"] S --> S1["Examples: Pressure P, Temperature T, Specific Volume v, Density rho, Specific Internal Energy u"] S --> T["SUBDIVISION"] T --> T1["Specific Properties per kg"] T --> T2["Molar Properties per kmol"] style R fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style S fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 style T1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#6A5ACD,color:#000 style T2 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#6A5ACD,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Equilibrium Hierarchy and Verification ```mermaid flowchart TD EQ["COMPLETE THERMODYNAMIC EQUILIBRIUM"] --> ME["MECHANICAL EQ"] EQ --> TE["THERMAL EQ"] EQ --> CE["CHEMICAL EQ"] ME --> ME1["Pressure uniform"] ME --> ME2["No moving boundary work tendency"] TE --> TE1["Temperature uniform"] TE --> TE2["No heat transfer tendency"] CE --> CE1["Chemical potential uniform"] CE --> CE2["No mass transfer or reaction tendency"] ME1 --> V1["VERIFIED IF P constant with position and time"] TE1 --> V2["VERIFIED IF T constant with position and time"] CE1 --> V3["VERIFIED IF mu constant across phases"] style EQ fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style ME fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 style TE fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 style CE fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** > The block diagrams above map the **functional relationships** between concepts (state, properties, equilibrium sub-types). They are not physical schematics but conceptual architecture maps that KTU examiners reward when included in answers. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q: Define thermodynamic equilibrium. Explain the different types of equilibrium a system must satisfy to be in thermodynamic equilibrium.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** A system is said to be in thermodynamic equilibrium if it satisfies all the conditions of equilibrium — namely, mechanical, thermal, and chemical equilibrium — simultaneously, and there is no tendency for any spontaneous change in its macroscopic properties. **Three types of equilibrium (2 marks):** 1. **Mechanical Equilibrium:** The pressure throughout the system and between the system and its surroundings is uniform. There are no unbalanced forces or pressure gradients. 2. **Thermal Equilibrium:** The temperature is uniform throughout the system and equals the surroundings' temperature. There is no tendency for heat transfer. This is governed by the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. 3. **Chemical Equilibrium:** The chemical potential of each component is uniform across all phases. There is no tendency for net chemical reaction or mass transfer between phases. > [!TIP] > **Valuation Note:** Award **1 mark for the definition**, **½ mark for each type of equilibrium**, and **½ mark for the relevant condition** (P, T, μ). State all three explicitly. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: State and explain the State Postulate with an example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement (1.5 marks):** The State Postulate (also called the two-property rule) states that the state of a simple compressible system is completely specified by **two independent, intensive thermodynamic properties**, along with the system mass. **Reason (1 mark):** Among the three variables $P$, $v$, and $T$, the equation of state $f(P, v, T) = 0$ provides one constraint, reducing the degrees of freedom to 2. **Example (0.5 marks):** For water/steam in a boiler, once pressure ($P$) and temperature ($T$) are specified, the specific volume ($v$), specific enthalpy ($h$), and specific entropy ($s$) can all be uniquely read from the steam tables. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** #### **Part (a): [7 Marks]** **(a) Differentiate between intensive and extensive properties. Give four examples of each.** **Model Solution:** | Aspect | Intensive Properties | Extensive Properties | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Properties independent of system size/mass | Properties dependent on system size/mass | | **Additivity** | NOT additive | Additive (sum of parts = whole) | | **Effect of dividing system** | Remain the same for each part | Get divided among parts | | **Examples (4 each)** | $P$, $T$, $v$, $\rho$ (or $h$, $u$, $s$) | $V$, $U$, $H$, $S$ (or $m$, $m\cdot c_p$) | **Specific Properties (½ mark):** Specific properties are obtained by dividing extensive properties by mass, hence are intensive. Example: $v = V/m$. **Marking breakdown:** 2 marks (definition + comparison), 2 marks (intensive examples), 2 marks (extensive examples), 1 mark (specific property rule). --- #### **Part (b): [7 Marks]** **(b) Explain with necessary derivations how the conditions for mechanical, thermal, and chemical equilibrium arise from the Second Law of Thermodynamics.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Second Law Statement (1 mark)** For an isolated system, entropy attains a maximum at equilibrium: $$dS = 0 \quad (\text{first-order}), \quad d^2 S < 0 \quad (\text{second-order})$$ **Step 2: Consider an Isolated Composite System (1 mark)** Divide into two subsystems with $U = U_1 + U_2$, $V = V_1 + V_2$, $N = N_1 + N_2$, $S = S_1 + S_2$. **Step 3: Mechanical Equilibrium (1.5 marks)** Vary $V_1$ keeping $V$ constant. From $dS = 0$: $$\left( \dfrac{\partial U_1}{\partial V_1} \right)_{S_1, N_1} = \left( \dfrac{\partial U_2}{\partial V_2} \right)_{S_2, N_2}$$ Using $P = -(\partial U/\partial V)_{S, N}$: $$\boxed{P_1 = P_2}$$ **Step 4: Thermal Equilibrium (1.5 marks)** Vary $S_1$ keeping $S$ constant. From $dS = 0$: $$\left( \dfrac{\partial U_1}{\partial S_1} \right)_{V_1, N_1} = \left( \dfrac{\partial U_2}{\partial S_2} \right)_{V_2, N_2}$$ Using $T = (\partial U/\partial S)_{V, N}$: $$\boxed{T_1 = T_2}$$ **Step 5: Chemical Equilibrium (1.5 marks)** Vary $N_{1,i}$ keeping $N_i$ constant: $$\left( \dfrac{\partial U_1}{\partial N_{1,i}} \right)_{S_1, V_1} = \left( \dfrac{\partial U_2}{\partial N_{2,i}} \right)_{S_2, V_2}$$ Using $\mu_i = (\partial U/\partial N_i)_{S, V, N_{j\neq i}}$: $$\boxed{\mu_{1,i} = \mu_{2,i}}$$ **Conclusion (0.5 marks):** All three conditions must hold simultaneously for complete thermodynamic equilibrium. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** #### **Part (a): [7 Marks]** **(a) A piston-cylinder device contains 0.005 m³ of nitrogen gas at 100 kPa and 25°C. The gas is compressed isothermally to half its initial volume. Determine the final pressure and the work done.** *Take $R_{N_2} = 0.2968 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $M = 28 \; \text{kg/kmol}$.* **Model Solution:** **Given (1 mark):** - $V_1 = 0.005 \; \text{m}^3$, $P_1 = 100 \; \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298 \; \text{K}$ - $V_2 = V_1/2 = 0.0025 \; \text{m}^3$ - Process: Isothermal ($T_1 = T_2 = T$) - Ideal gas assumption. **Step 1: Find mass $m$ (1 mark)** $$m = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{RT_1} = \dfrac{100 \times 0.005}{0.2968 \times 298}$$ $$m = \dfrac{0.5}{88.4464} = 5.653 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg}$$ **Step 2: Find $P_2$ (2 marks)** For isothermal process of ideal gas: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ $$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = 100 \times \dfrac{0.005}{0.0025} = 200 \; \text{kPa}$$ **Step 3: Work done (isothermal, closed system) (3 marks)** For isothermal compression of ideal gas: $$W = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$ $$W = 100 \times 0.005 \times \ln\left(\dfrac{0.0025}{0.005}\right)$$ $$W = 0.5 \times \ln(0.5)$$ $$W = 0.5 \times (-0.6931)$$ $$\boxed{W = -0.3466 \; \text{kJ}}$$ Negative sign indicates work is done **on** the system (compression). --- #### **Part (b): [7 Marks]** **(b) Define: (i) Quasi-static process, (ii) Quasi-equilibrium process. Explain why these concepts are important in defining thermodynamic properties.** **Model Solution:** **(i) Quasi-Static Process (3 marks):** A process is said to be **quasi-static** (or quasi-equilibrium) if the system remains **infinitesimally close to equilibrium** at every instant during the process. Mathematically: at every instant, the deviation from equilibrium is infinitesimal: $$\Delta P_{\text{internal}} \to 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta T_{\text{internal}} \to 0$$ **Physical picture:** The process is carried out so slowly that at any instant, the system has a uniform pressure (mechanical eq.), uniform temperature (thermal eq.), and uniform chemical potential (chemical eq.). **(ii) Importance for Property Definition (3 marks):** 1. **Properties are defined only at equilibrium states.** For a non-equilibrium system, pressure and temperature may not be uniform, so single-valued system properties cannot be assigned. 2. **Only in quasi-static processes can we plot a continuous path** on a P-v diagram or T-s diagram, because every intermediate state is an equilibrium state with well-defined properties. 3. **Work calculations** (e.g., $W = \int P \, dV$) assume the system pressure is well-defined and uniform — this is valid only for quasi-static processes. 4. **Real processes** (rapid compression, shock waves, explosion) are NOT quasi-static; for these, we use **average** or **boundary** values, not internal equilibrium values. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Quasi-static processes form the idealized framework in which the entire structure of classical thermodynamics (state postulate, property tables, equations of state) is built. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Examiner Insights):** > > 1. **Confusing State vs. Process:** Students often write *"the state of the system changes during a process"* — but technically, the *state* is the *condition* at an instant; it is the *system* that moves from State 1 to State 2. Lose 0.5–1 mark for imprecise language. > > 2. **Mixing specific and molar properties:** Writing $Pv = RT$ without verifying units (m³/kg vs m³/kmol) loses **1 mark** in derivation questions. > > 3. **Forgetting chemical equilibrium:** Many students discuss only mechanical and thermal equilibrium, missing **chemical equilibrium**. This costs **0.5–1 mark** in 3-mark definition questions. > > 4. **State Postulate misuse:** Students sometimes claim that "$P$ and $v$ always specify the state" — but **two independent** properties are needed; specifying $P$ and $v$ does not work in the two-phase region if you also need quality $x$! Always state "two **independent** intensive properties". > > 5. **Quasi-static vs. Reversible:** These are **NOT the same**. Quasi-static = no friction, no unbalance, near-equilibrium at every step. Reversible = quasi-static + **no dissipative effects** (friction, viscosity). Confusing them loses **1–2 marks**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — State and Equilibrium:** - **State of a system:** Completely specified by its **properties** (P, T, v, u, h, s, …). - **State Postulate (Two-Property Rule):** A simple compressible system is fully described by **two independent intensive properties**. - **Equation of State:** A relation among $P$, $v$, $T$ (e.g., $Pv = RT$ for ideal gas). - **Thermodynamic Equilibrium** = simultaneous satisfaction of **3 sub-equilibria**: - **Mechanical:** Pressure uniform → $P_1 = P_2$ - **Thermal:** Temperature uniform → $T_1 = T_2$ - **Chemical:** Chemical potential uniform → $\mu_{1,i} = \mu_{2,i}$ - **Intensive vs Extensive:** - Intensive: independent of size — $P$, $T$, $v$, $\rho$, $h$, $u$, $s$ - Extensive: dependent on size — $V$, $U$, $H$, $S$, $m$ - Specific (per kg) or molar (per kmol) versions are intensive. - **Quasi-Static Process:** System remains infinitesimally near equilibrium at every step — required for property definition and path plotting. - **State** = point; **Process** = path between two states; **Path function** (Q, W) ≠ **Point function** (P, T, u, h, s). - **Ideal Gas Equation (per mass):** $Pv = RT$, where $R = \bar{R}_u / M$. - **Universal Gas Constant:** $\bar{R}_u = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/kmol·K}$ (always carry 4 sig figs in KTU numericals). - **Zeroth Law** → enables temperature measurement → required for thermal equilibrium. - **Second Law** → entropy max at equilibrium → leads to the three equilibrium criteria. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** > In exam answers, **always draw a property diagram** (P-v, T-s, or block diagram) for full-mark derivation questions. Examiners reward **2–3 extra marks** for clean, labelled diagrams. Always **state assumptions** (ideal gas, quasi-static, closed system) before solving numericals.

topics.title: The State Postulate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The State Postulate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE STATE POSTULATE (Zeroth Law Consequence)**
>
> *"The state of a simple compressible system is completely specified by **two independent, intensive properties**."*
>
> — Cengel & Boles, *Thermodynamics: An Engineering Approach*

In other words, once **two independent intensive properties** of a simple, single-component, single-phase, non-reacting, non-magnetic, non-electrical pure substance are fixed, every other property of the system (such as specific volume, internal energy, enthalpy, entropy, etc.) is automatically and uniquely determined. There is no need to specify a third property — the third is mathematically "locked in" by the equation of state.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pin-Code Idea"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition**
>
> Imagine a **Post Office Pin Code** in India. Once you know the **6-digit Pin Code**, the entire location — city, district, state, post office, and even the delivery route — is **uniquely fixed**. You don't need to also specify the latitude, longitude, and street name separately.
>
> In thermodynamics, **two intensive properties act like that Pin Code**. Pick any two (e.g., Pressure and Temperature, or Pressure and Specific Volume), and the rest of the system's thermodynamic "address" — its energy, entropy, density, etc. — is automatically pinpointed.

### A Simpler Analogy: A Point on a 2D Map
- To uniquely locate a **point on a flat 2D map**, you need exactly **two coordinates** — say, latitude and longitude.
- You cannot fix a point with just one coordinate (infinite points share a single latitude or longitude line).
- Three coordinates are redundant — once two are known, the third is constrained if a relationship exists.
- The thermodynamic state behaves identically: it lives on a 2D **state space**, and any two independent intensive coordinates uniquely identify it.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology — What Does "Simple Compressible System" Mean?

A **simple compressible system** is a system that satisfies **ALL** of the following:

| # | Condition | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Single chemical species** | Pure substance, no chemical reaction |
| 2 | **Single phase** | Solid OR Liquid OR Gas — but not a mixture of phases |
| 3 | **No surface tension effects** | Gravity and capillarity are negligible |
| 4 | **No electromagnetic effects** | Non-magnetic, non-electrical |
| 5 | **Quasi-equilibrium** | System is internally uniform at every instant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions Used by KTU Examiners**
>
> - Standard Atmospheric Pressure: **P₀ = 101.325 kPa**
> - Standard Temperature: **T₀ = 25 °C = 298.15 K**
> - Universal Gas Constant: **R_u = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**

---

## 1.4 Intensive vs. Extensive Properties — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intensive Property:** Independent of the system mass/size.
> Examples: **Pressure (P), Temperature (T), Specific Volume (v), Specific Internal Energy (u), Specific Enthalpy (h), Specific Entropy (s), Density (ρ).**
>
> **Extensive Property:** Depends on the system mass/size.
> Examples: **Volume (V), Internal Energy (U), Enthalpy (H), Entropy (S), Mass (m).**
>
> **The State Postulate only applies to INTENSIVE properties.** You must always convert extensive properties to **specific (per-unit-mass)** values before applying the postulate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Exactly TWO Properties? — The Underlying Logic

The thermodynamic state space of a pure substance is **two-dimensional** because of a fundamental physical fact:

1. **Energy (E) is the fundamental thermodynamic potential.** The **First Law of Thermodynamics** (closed system) states:
$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W$$

2. Combining the First Law, the definition of entropy from the Second Law, and the property relations of a simple compressible system, we obtain the **Combined First and Second Law (Fundamental Relation)**:
$$du = T\,ds - P\,dv$$

3. This equation is a **differential relationship among FOUR specific properties**: $u$, $T$, $s$, $P$, $v$.

4. Mathematically, a single differential equation linking four variables in differential form defines a **two-parameter family of surfaces**. The state surface of a pure substance in 3D space $(v, s, u)$ is therefore fully described by **just two independent coordinates** — for example, $(v, s)$ — with $u(v, s)$ being uniquely defined everywhere on the surface.

5. This is the **mathematical foundation** of the State Postulate. Any other two **independent** intensive properties serve equally well as coordinates (provided they are mathematically independent, i.e., one is not a single-valued function of the other).

---

## 2.2 The Gibbs Phase Rule — Mathematical Proof of the State Postulate

For a **simple compressible system** (pure substance, single phase), the **Gibbs Phase Rule** is given by:

$$F = C - P + 2$$

Where:
- **F** = Degrees of freedom (number of independent intensive properties that must be specified to fix the state)
- **C** = Number of chemical components (for a pure substance, **C = 1**)
- **P** = Number of phases present (for a single phase, **P = 1**)

Substituting:
$$F = 1 - 1 + 2 = 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion from the Phase Rule:** For a single-component, single-phase system, exactly **F = 2** independent intensive properties must be specified to uniquely determine the thermodynamic state. This is the State Postulate stated in formal mathematical language.

---

## 2.3 What Are "Independent" Intensive Properties?

Two intensive properties $(X, Y)$ are said to be **independent** if and only if:
$$Y \neq f(X)$$

That is, knowing $X$ alone does not determine $Y$, and vice-versa.

**Examples of INDEPENDENT pairs (acceptable for state fixing):**
- $(P, T)$ — Pressure and Temperature
- $(P, v)$ — Pressure and Specific Volume
- $(T, v)$ — Temperature and Specific Volume
- $(T, s)$ — Temperature and Specific Entropy
- $(P, h)$ — Pressure and Specific Enthalpy
- $(u, v)$ — Specific Internal Energy and Specific Volume

**Examples of DEPENDENT pairs (NOT acceptable):**
- $(P, T)$ for a **saturated mixture** — at saturation, $P = P_{sat}(T)$ is a single-valued function, so only **one** independent property is needed (the other is automatically fixed).
- $(P, v)$ for a **substance on the saturation line** — same reason.
- $(T, P)$ inside the **two-phase dome** — saturation locks one to the other.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write *"state is fixed by P and T"* without realizing that **on the saturation dome** (two-phase region), pressure and temperature are NOT independent — they are linked by the saturation curve. The State Postulate's "two independent properties" rule fails inside the saturation dome.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Statement | Mathematical Form | When to Use | Units (SI) |
|---|---------------------|-------------------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | State Postulate (verbal) | State fixed by 2 independent intensive properties | Any simple compressible system | — |
| 2 | Gibbs Phase Rule | $F = C - P + 2$ | Determining degrees of freedom | — |
| 3 | Phase Rule (pure, single phase) | $F = 1 - 1 + 2 = 2$ | Pure substance, single phase | — |
| 4 | Fundamental Relation | $du = T\,ds - P\,dv$ | Linking four key properties | kJ/kg, kJ/(kg·K), m³/kg |
| 5 | Ideal Gas Equation (related EOS) | $Pv = RT$ | Gaseous state calculations | kPa·m³/(kg·K) |
| 6 | Specific Volume – Density relation | $v = 1/\rho$ | Converting between $v$ and $\rho$ | m³/kg |
| 7 | Enthalpy definition | $h = u + Pv$ | Property conversion | kJ/kg |
| 8 | Pressure conversion (frequently used in KTU) | $1\ \text{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa} = 1.01325\ \text{bar}$ | Numerical problems | kPa / bar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on KTU Standard:** When using the steam tables, R-134a tables, or refrigerant tables in numerical problems, the property values at the intersection of two given properties (e.g., $P$ and $T$, or $P$ and $v$) are obtained directly by **double interpolation** if the exact point is not tabulated.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The State Postulate is the **silent foundation** of every thermodynamic calculation in the following fields:

- **Power Plant Engineering:** Boiler steam state fixed by $(P, T)$ → turbine inlet properties.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** R-134a state in compressor fixed by $(P, T)$ after compression.
- **Internal Combustion Engines:** Combustion gas state at TDC fixed by $(P, T)$ after combustion.
- **Chemical Process Industries:** Reactor feed state fixed by $(P, h)$ from upstream streams.
- **Gas Turbine Analysis:** Compressor exit state fixed by $(P_2, T_2)$ — used to compute $h_2$, $s_2$, work, and efficiency.
- **Nozzle and Diffuser Design:** Inlet/exit states fixed by any two measurable properties to compute velocities.
- **Compressors and Pumps:** Suction and discharge states fixed by two properties each, then work input is calculated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Takeaway:** Without the State Postulate, **NO property table (steam tables, R-134a tables, air tables) would be usable**, because these tables are organized in 2D grids indexed by two intensive properties (typically $P$ and $T$, or $P$ and $v$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — From the Fundamental Relation to the State Postulate

### Starting Point: Combined First & Second Law

For a unit mass of a simple compressible pure substance undergoing a reversible process:
$$du = T\,ds - P\,dv$$

### Step 1 — Identify the variables

The differential equation above contains **four thermodynamic specific properties**:
- $u$ — specific internal energy
- $T$ — absolute temperature
- $s$ — specific entropy
- $P$ — absolute pressure
- $v$ — specific volume

So we have a relationship among $u$, $T$, $s$, $P$, $v$ — but $u$ is expressed in terms of the differentials of $T$, $s$, $P$, $v$.

### Step 2 — Recognize the structure

This is a Pfaffian differential form:
$$du = M(x, y, z)\,dx + N(x, y, z)\,dy$$

with the identifications:
- $x \rightarrow s$ (with coefficient $M = T$)
- $y \rightarrow v$ (with coefficient $N = -P$)
- $z \rightarrow u$ (the dependent variable)

### Step 3 — Apply the exactness criterion

For $u(s, v)$ to be a well-defined **state function** (a property of the state only, independent of path), the differential $du$ must be **exact**. Exactness requires the cross-partial equality:
$$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$$

Substituting $M = T(s, v)$ and $N = -P(s, v)$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial v}\right)_s = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\right)_v$$

This is a well-known Maxwell-type reciprocity relation. Its existence confirms that $u$ is indeed a **state function** of $(s, v)$ only.

### Step 4 — Conclude the dimensionality

A well-defined function of two independent variables $u = u(s, v)$ implies the state surface is **two-dimensional**. Therefore, **specifying any two independent intensive properties uniquely fixes $u$**, and by extension, every other state property.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Restatement of the State Postulate:**
> $$\boxed{\ \text{For a simple compressible system, the state surface } u = u(s,v) \text{ is 2-dimensional.} \ }$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Gibbs Phase Rule for a Pure, Single-Phase System

The Gibbs Phase Rule is derived from **counting variables and counting constraints**.

### Step 1 — Count the variables

For a system with $C$ components and $P$ phases, the intensive variables are:
- Composition of each phase: $(C - 1)$ mole fractions per phase
- Temperature of each phase: $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_P$ → $P$ variables
- Pressure of each phase: $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_P$ → $P$ variables

Total intensive variables:
$$N_{vars} = P(C - 1) + P + P = PC + P - P + P = PC + P$$

Wait, let me redo this carefully:
- $(C-1)$ mole fractions × $P$ phases = $P(C-1)$ variables
- $P$ temperatures (one per phase) = $P$ variables
- $P$ pressures (one per phase) = $P$ variables

$$\text{Total} = P(C - 1) + P + P = PC - P + 2P = PC + P$$

### Step 2 — Count the constraints

- **Thermal equilibrium:** All phases at the same $T$ → $(P - 1)$ equations
- **Mechanical equilibrium:** All phases at the same $P$ → $(P - 1)$ equations
- **Phase equilibrium (chemical potential equality):** For each component, $\mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta}$ for all phase pairs → $C(P - 1)$ equations

Total constraints:
$$N_{cons} = (P - 1) + (P - 1) + C(P - 1) = (P - 1)(C + 2)$$

### Step 3 — Compute the degrees of freedom

$$F = N_{vars} - N_{cons} = (PC + P) - (P - 1)(C + 2)$$

Expanding the second term:
$$(P - 1)(C + 2) = PC + 2P - C - 2$$

Subtracting:
$$F = PC + P - PC - 2P + C + 2 = C - P + 2$$

$$\boxed{\ F = C - P + 2 \ }$$

### Step 4 — Apply to a simple compressible system

For a **pure substance** ($C = 1$) in a **single phase** ($P = 1$):
$$F = 1 - 1 + 2 = 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The State Postulate is simply the Gibbs Phase Rule evaluated for $C = 1$, $P = 1$, yielding $F = 2$.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Why $P$ and $T$ Are Independent in the Superheated Region

Consider the **ideal gas** as a model substance. The equation of state is:
$$Pv = RT$$

### Step 1 — Express $v$ explicitly

$$v = \frac{RT}{P}$$

### Step 2 — Test if $P$ and $T$ are independent

For $P$ and $T$ to be **dependent**, we would need $P = f(T)$ as a single-valued function. In the superheated region, however, the gas can exist at any combination of $(P, T)$ — for any chosen $P$ and $T$, there exists a valid $v = RT/P$. Hence, no functional dependence exists between $P$ and $T$ in general.

### Step 3 — Identify the exception (saturation region)

However, on the **saturation line**:
$$P = P_{sat}(T)$$

This is a **single-valued function** (each $T$ corresponds to exactly one saturation pressure). So at saturation, $P$ and $T$ lose their independence — the **Frozens number drops to 1**, not 2.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Problems:**
> - In the **compressed liquid, saturated liquid, superheated vapor, and supercritical** regions: **$F = 2$** → State Postulate applies with TWO properties.
> - In the **two-phase saturation dome**: $F = 1$ (since $P$ and $T$ are linked by $P_{sat}(T)$) — only ONE property (e.g., quality $x$ or $v$) is needed to fix the state.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration (Model Solution Format)

> **Problem:** A piston-cylinder device contains **2 kg** of steam at **P = 1.4 MPa**. The specific volume is measured as **v = 0.1773 m³/kg**. Determine the temperature and the specific internal energy of the steam.

### Step 1 — State the given data
- Mass: $m = 2\ \text{kg}$
- Pressure: $P = 1.4\ \text{MPa} = 1400\ \text{kPa}$
- Specific volume: $v = 0.1773\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$

### Step 2 — Apply the State Postulate

Since $P$ and $v$ are two independent intensive properties (steam is a pure substance, single phase assumed), the state is **fully fixed**. We need only consult the steam tables at $P = 1.4\ \text{MPa}$, $v = 0.1773\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$.

### Step 3 — Interpolation in the superheated steam table

At $P = 1.4\ \text{MPa}$:
- At $T = 300\ ^\circ\text{C}$: $v_1 = 0.18228\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$, $u_1 = 2789.4\ \text{kJ/kg}$
- At $T = 350\ ^\circ\text{C}$: $v_2 = 0.19951\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$, $u_2 = 2867.2\ \text{kJ/kg}$

Interpolation fraction:
$$f = \frac{v - v_1}{v_2 - v_1} = \frac{0.1773 - 0.18228}{0.19951 - 0.18228} = \frac{-0.00498}{0.01723} = -0.2891$$

### Step 4 — Compute temperature

$$T = T_1 + f(T_2 - T_1) = 300 + (-0.2891)(350 - 300)$$
$$T = 300 - 14.45 \approx 285.55\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

### Step 5 — Compute specific internal energy

$$u = u_1 + f(u_2 - u_1) = 2789.4 + (-0.2891)(2867.2 - 2789.4)$$
$$u = 2789.4 + (-0.2891)(77.8) = 2789.4 - 22.49 \approx 2766.91\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

### Step 6 — Final answer
- **Temperature:** $T \approx 285.6\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- **Specific Internal Energy:** $u \approx 2766.9\ \text{kJ/kg}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The state was fixed with **only 2 properties** ($P$ and $v$). We did not need to specify $T$, $u$, $h$, $s$, or any other property — they were **all uniquely determined** by the State Postulate.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — State Postulate Decision Table

| Region of the Phase Diagram | Independent Properties Needed | Phase Rule Value | Example |
|------------------------------|--------------------------------|------------------|---------|
| Compressed Liquid | 2 | $F = 2$ | $(P, T)$ |
| Saturated Liquid (line) | 1 | $F = 1$ | $T$ alone (since $P = P_{sat}(T)$) |
| Two-Phase Dome (mixture) | 1 (usually quality $x$) | $F = 1$ | $P$ or $T$, plus quality $x$ |
| Saturated Vapor (line) | 1 | $F = 1$ | $T$ alone |
| Superheated Vapor | 2 | $F = 2$ | $(P, T)$, $(P, v)$, $(T, v)$ |
| Supercritical Region | 2 | $F = 2$ | $(P, T)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — The State Postulate Architecture

The diagram below shows the **logical architecture** of the State Postulate: how two independent intensive inputs are processed through an "equation of state" to yield every other state property of the system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SYSTEM: Pure Substance, Single Phase, Non-Reacting, Non-Electric, Non-Magnetic"] --> B["INPUT PROPERTIES"]
    B --> B1["Intensive Property 1 (e.g., Pressure P)"]
    B --> B2["Intensive Property 2 (e.g., Temperature T)"]
    B1 --> C["STATE FIXATION ENGINE: Equation of State + Property Relations"]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D1["Specific Volume v"]
    C --> D2["Specific Internal Energy u"]
    C --> D3["Specific Enthalpy h = u + Pv"]
    C --> D4["Specific Entropy s"]
    C --> D5["Specific Heat Capacities cp, cv"]
    C --> D6["Density rho = 1 divided by v"]
    D1 --> E["FULL THERMODYNAMIC STATE IDENTIFIED"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F["Ready for Process Analysis: First Law, Second Law, Cycle Work, Efficiency"]
```

---

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — How Many Properties Do I Need?

This flowchart guides a student in choosing the correct number of intensive properties for any given thermodynamic state.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["START: Identify the Substance and the Region on the Phase Diagram"] --> S1{"Is the substance a PURE SINGLE-COMPONENT system?"}
    S1 -- No --> OUT1["Use Modified Phase Rule with C greater than 1: F equals C minus P plus 2"]
    S1 -- Yes --> S2{"How many PHASES are present?"}
    S2 -- Two or More --> S3{"Are P and T used as the two chosen properties?"}
    S3 -- Yes --> WARN1["WARNING: P and T are NOT independent inside the two-phase dome. F equals 1."]
    S3 -- No --> S4["State is fixed by 1 independent property plus QUALITY x"]
    S2 -- One Phase --> S5["Phase Rule gives F equals 2"]
    S5 --> S6["Choose any 2 INDEPENDENT intensive properties e.g. P and T, P and v, T and s, u and v"]
    S6 --> S7["State fully fixed. Consult property tables for all other properties"]
    WARN1 --> S4
    S4 --> END1["State Fixed"]
    S7 --> END1
    OUT1 --> END1
```

---

## 4.3 Property Triangle — Internal Relationships Between State Properties

The diagram below illustrates the **closed loop of definitions** that tie the four primary state properties together. The State Postulate guarantees that fixing any **two** of them fixes the entire structure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Pressure P"] -->|"Pv term in Enthalpy"| H["Enthalpy h = u + Pv"]
    V["Specific Volume v"] -->|"Pv term in Enthalpy"| H
    U["Internal Energy u"] -->|"Enthalpy Definition"| H
    U -->|"Fundamental Relation du = Tds minus Pdv"| S["Entropy s"]
    S -->|"Fundamental Relation du = Tds minus Pdv"| U
    P -->|"Fundamental Relation du = Tds minus Pdv"| U
    V -->|"Fundamental Relation du = Tds minus Pdv"| U
    T["Temperature T"] -->|"Maxwell and Property Relations"| S
    T -->|"cp minus cv equals T times beta squared v divided by kappa T"| P
```

---

## 4.4 Engineering Application Map — Where the State Postulate is Used

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP["THE STATE POSTULATE: Two Intensive Properties Fix a State"] --> AP1["Power Plant: Boiler Steam at P and T"]
    SP --> AP2["Gas Turbine: Compressor Exit at P2 and T2"]
    SP --> AP3["Refrigeration Cycle: R134a after Compressor at P2 and T2"]
    SP --> AP4["IC Engine: Combustion Gas at Pmax and Tmax"]
    SP --> AP5["Nozzle: Exit State at Pexit and Texit to find Exit Velocity"]
    SP --> AP6["Compressor: Inlet at P1 and T1, Outlet at P2 with known Process"]
    AP1 --> USE1["Compute h, s, v, u via Steam Tables"]
    AP2 --> USE2["Compute h2, s2 via Air Tables or Ideal Gas Relations"]
    AP3 --> USE3["Compute h2, s2 via R134a Tables"]
    AP4 --> USE4["Compute u, h using Combustion Gas Properties"]
    AP5 --> USE5["Use Energy Equation: h1 plus V1sq by 2 equals h2 plus V2sq by 2"]
    AP6 --> USE6["Compute Work Input: w equals h2 minus h1"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
