> A furnace wall is at 1800 °F. Find its temperature in (i) °C, (ii) K, and (iii) °R. Determine whether the furnace temperature exceeds the melting point of steel (≈ 1500 °C).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert to Celsius.

$$T_C = \frac{5}{9}\,(1800 - 32) = \frac{5}{9} \cdot 1768 = 982.22\,°C$$

**Step 2:** Convert to Kelvin.

$$T_K = 982.22 + 273.15 = 1255.37\,K$$

**Step 3:** Convert to Rankine.

$$T_R = 1800 + 459.67 = 2259.67\,°R$$

**Step 4:** Compare with steel melting point.

$$T_{\text{steel}} = 1500\,°C = 1500 + 273.15 = 1773.15\,K$$

$$T_{\text{furnace}} = 1255.37\,K \;<\; 1773.15\,K$$

**Conclusion:** The furnace temperature (1255 K) is **below** the steel melting point (1773 K), so a piece of steel would **not melt** in this furnace.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchy of Temperature Scales (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TEMPERATURE SCALES] --> B[EMPIRICAL SCALES]
    A --> C[ABSOLUTE SCALES]
    A --> D[THERMODYNAMIC SCALE]

    B --> B1[CELSIUS degC]
    B --> B2[FAHRENHEIT degF]
    B --> B3[REAUMUR degRe]

    C --> C1[KELVIN K]
    C --> C2[RANKINE degR]

    D --> D1[INTERNATIONAL SCALE ITS-90]
    D --> D2[TRIPLE POINT 273.16 K]

    B1 --> E1[Offset 273.15]
    E1 --> C1
    B2 --> E2[Offset 459.67]
    E2 --> C2

    C1 --> F1[Factor 9 slash 5]
    F1 --> C2

    D1 --> G1[Defines]
    G1 --> C1
    G1 --> C2
```

### 4.2 Conversion Flowchart — Practical Engineering Aid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TC[Temp in degC] -->|Add 273.15| TK[Temp in K]
    TK -->|Sub 273.15| TC

    TF[Temp in degF] -->|Sub 32 then Times 5 slash 9| TC
    TC -->|Times 9 slash 5 then Add 32| TF

    TK -->|Times 9 slash 5| TR[Temp in degR]
    TR -->|Times 5 slash 9| TK

    TF -->|Add 459.67| TR
    TR -->|Sub 459.67| TF
```

### 4.3 Reference Points Anchor Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[ABSOLUTE ZERO] -->|0 K| N1[FUNDAMENTAL LOWER LIMIT]
    P2[TRIPLE POINT WATER] -->|273.16 K| N2[DEFINING FIXED POINT ITS-90]
    P3[ICE POINT] -->|273.15 K| N3[LOWER FIXED POINT EMPIRICAL]
    P4[STEAM POINT] -->|373.15 K| N4[UPPER FIXED POINT EMPIRICAL]

    P1 --> S1[UNREACHABLE]
    P2 --> S2[EXACTLY REPRODUCIBLE]
    P3 --> S3[1 atm PRESSURE]
    P4 --> S4[1 atm PRESSURE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a node in the conceptual structure of temperature scales. The Kelvin scale is shown as the **central hub**, because it is the SI-defined thermodynamic scale and serves as the universal pivot for converting *into* and *out of* every other scale.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define temperature and explain the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. Why is the Zeroth Law considered the foundation of temperature measurement?*

**Model Answer:**
Temperature is a fundamental intensive thermodynamic property that indicates the degree of hotness or coldness of a body. Two bodies in thermal contact with no net heat flow between them are said to be at the **same temperature**.

The **Zeroth Law** states: *If body A is in thermal equilibrium with body C, and body B is in thermal equilibrium with body C, then A and B are also in thermal equilibrium with each other.*

It is foundational because:
- It guarantees that temperature is a **transitive, well-defined property**.
- It allows a third body (the thermometer) to be used as a reference to compare the temperatures of two otherwise isolated systems.
- Without it, the very concept of a temperature *scale* would be logically inconsistent.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Distinguish between empirical and thermodynamic temperature scales, giving one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Empirical Scale | Thermodynamic Scale |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Arbitrary choice of thermometric property | Second Law of Thermodynamics (Carnot cycle) |
| Universality | Property-dependent | Property-independent, universal |
| Reference | Ice & Steam points (water) | Triple point of water = 273.16 K |
| Example | Celsius, Fahrenheit | Kelvin (SI), Rankine |
| Subdivisions | Depends on substance | Independent of substance |

The Kelvin scale is the SI thermodynamic scale; Celsius and Fahrenheit are empirical scales because they depend on the chosen thermometric substance (originally mercury).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Conversions & Multi-step Reasoning*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Q1(a-b)]**

**(a)** The temperature inside an industrial furnace is measured as **1850 °F**. Determine its equivalent temperature in (i) °C, (ii) K, and (iii) °R. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain why the Kelvin scale is called a **thermodynamic scale**, while the Celsius scale is called an **empirical scale**. Compare their reference points. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Fahrenheit to Celsius:**

$$T_C = \frac{5}{9}\,(T_F - 32) = \frac{5}{9}\,(1850 - 32) = \frac{5}{9}\cdot 1818 = 1010\,°C$$

**Step 2 — Celsius to Kelvin:**

$$T_K = T_C + 273.15 = 1010 + 273.15 = 1283.15\,K$$

**Step 3 — Fahrenheit to Rankine (direct conversion):**

$$T_R = T_F + 459.67 = 1850 + 459.67 = 2309.67\,°R$$

**Cross-check via $T_R = \dfrac{9}{5}\,T_K$:**

$$T_R = \frac{9}{5}\cdot 1283.15 = 2309.67\,°R \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $T = 1010\,°C = 1283.15\,K = 2309.67\,°R$

---

**Solution to (b):**

The **Celsius scale** is empirical because:
- It depends on the choice of a thermometric substance (originally mercury).
- The size of its degree is defined by **two arbitrary fixed points** — the ice point (0 °C) and the steam point (100 °C) of water at 1 atm.
- A different thermometric substance would yield slightly different readings outside the fixed points.

The **Kelvin scale** is thermodynamic because:
- It is derived from the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** using the Carnot cycle.
- The ratio of two temperatures equals the ratio of heats exchanged with a reversible engine:
$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{Q_1}{Q_2}$$
- This definition is **independent of any working substance**.
- Its single defining fixed point is the **triple point of water = 273.16 K**, and the size of one kelvin is set by the Carnot-cycle definition.

| Reference | Celsius | Kelvin |
|---|---|---|
| Lower | Ice point = 0 °C | Absolute zero = 0 K |
| Upper | Steam point = 100 °C | No upper bound |
| Defining | Two fixed points | Triple point of water |
| Universality | Substance-dependent | Substance-independent |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice — Focus: Theory & Application*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 1, Q1(a-b)]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and use of a **Constant Volume Gas Thermometer**. Show that it leads to a temperature scale that is independent of the gas used, in the limit of low pressure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A platinum resistance thermometer has a resistance of **90 Ω at the ice point (0 °C)** and **177 Ω at the steam point (100 °C)**. Find the temperature (in °C) when its resistance is **135 Ω**. Assume a linear relationship. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Construction:**
A constant-volume gas thermometer consists of a bulb `B` filled with gas (nitrogen, helium, or hydrogen) connected to a manometer `M`. The gas volume is held constant by adjusting the mercury level, and the pressure $P$ is read at the manometer.

**Working Principle:**
At constant volume $V$, the pressure $P$ of a gas is assumed to vary linearly with temperature:

$$P = P_0\,(1 + \beta\,T)$$

where $P_0$ is the pressure at $T = 0\,°C$ and $\beta$ is the pressure coefficient.

**Derivation of Independence:**

Let $P_i$ and $P_s$ be the pressures at ice and steam points.

$$P_i = P_0\,(1 + \beta\cdot 0) = P_0$$

$$P_s = P_0\,(1 + 100\,\beta)$$

Therefore:

$$\beta = \frac{P_s - P_i}{100\,P_i}$$

For an *unknown* temperature $T$, the measured pressure is $P_T$:

$$P_T = P_0\,(1 + \beta\,T) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T = \frac{P_T - P_i}{\beta\,P_i} = \frac{P_T - P_i}{P_s - P_i}\cdot 100\,°C$$

In the **limit of low pressure** ($P \to 0$), the gas behaves as an **ideal gas** and $\beta \to \dfrac{1}{273.15}\,°C^{-1}$ for *all* gases (Gay-Lussac's law). Hence the scale becomes identical for all gases — the **ideal gas temperature scale**, which is in fact equivalent to the thermodynamic Kelvin scale.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Set up the linear relation:**
Let $R = R_0\,(1 + \alpha\,T)$ where $R_0 = 90\,\Omega$ at $0\,°C$ and $T$ is in °C.

**Step 2 — Apply the Steam-Point Condition:**

$$177 = 90\,(1 + \alpha\cdot 100)$$

$$1 + 100\,\alpha = \frac{177}{90} = 1.9667$$

$$100\,\alpha = 0.9667 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \alpha = 9.667\times 10^{-3}\,°C^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T$ at $R = 135\,\Omega$:**

$$135 = 90\,(1 + \alpha\,T)$$

$$1 + \alpha\,T = \frac{135}{90} = 1.5$$

$$\alpha\,T = 0.5$$

$$T = \frac{0.5}{9.667\times 10^{-3}} = 51.72\,°C$$

**Final Answer:** $T \approx 51.72\,°C$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Order of operations in °F → °C conversion:** Students often multiply by 5/9 *before* subtracting 32. Always **subtract 32 first**, then multiply by 5/9. Marks are deducted for this single arithmetic inversion.
> 2. **Forgetting the offset 273.15 (vs. 273):** A common rounding trap. The *exact* offset between °C and K is **273.15**, not 273. State it explicitly in KTU board answers.
> 3. **Rankine slope mistake:** Converting K → °R uses only the **factor 9/5**, NOT 9/5 + 459.67. The 459.67 offset applies only to Fahrenheit.
> 4. **Triple Point vs. Ice Point:** Examiners frequently test whether students know that the **triple point (273.16 K)** is *not* the same as the ice point (273.15 K). The difference is **0.01 K**.
> 5. **Units in answers:** Always quote the **unit symbol** (°C, K, °R) explicitly after the number — missing units invite partial-mark penalties.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Temperature** is an intensive thermodynamic property established by the **Zeroth Law**.
- Four scales of importance in KTU 2024 syllabus: **Celsius, Fahrenheit, Kelvin, Rankine**.
- **Absolute (thermodynamic) scales:** Kelvin (K) and Rankine (°R) — both have zero at absolute zero.
- **Reference points to memorize cold:**
  - Ice point = **0 °C = 273.15 K = 32 °F**
  - Steam point = **100 °C = 373.15 K = 212 °F**
  - Triple point of water = **273.16 K** *(exact, defining)*
  - Absolute zero = **0 K = −273.15 °C = −459.67 °F**
- **Master conversion formula (one-line mnemonic):**
$$\frac{T_C}{100} = \frac{T_F - 32}{180} = \frac{T_K - 273.15}{100} = \frac{T_R - 491.67}{180}$$
- **Slope/Offset quick-check:**
  - °F ↔ °C → slope **9/5** (or **5/9**), offset **32**.
  - K ↔ °C → slope **1**, offset **273.15**.
  - °R ↔ °F → slope **1**, offset **459.67**.
  - °R ↔ K → slope **9/5**, offset **0**.
- **Triple Point of Water = 273.16 K** is the *single* defining point of the modern ITS-90.
- **Constant-volume gas thermometer** at low pressure gives the **ideal gas scale**, equivalent to the **thermodynamic (Kelvin) scale**.
- **Practical tip:** Always double-check direction of conversion by sanity-testing extreme values — e.g., 0 °C should give 32 °F, 100 °C should give 212 °F.
- **Linear interpolation formula** (essential for RTD problems):
$$T = T_i + (T_s - T_i)\,\frac{X - X_i}{X_s - X_i}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forms of Energy
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forms of Energy

# ⚡ Forms of Energy — A Comprehensive Engineering Thermodynamics Study Module

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Energy? — The Fundamental Definition

In the rigorous language of **Engineering Thermodynamics**, energy is formally defined as:

> **Energy** is the capacity of a system to perform **work** or to transfer **heat** to its surroundings. It is a **scalar**, **path-independent** (point function) extensive property of a system that obeys the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (the Law of Conservation of Energy).

Mathematically, the differential form is written as:

$$dE = \delta Q - \delta W$$

where $dE$ represents the change in total energy of the system, $\delta Q$ is the infinitesimal heat transfer, and $\delta W$ is the infinitesimal work transfer. The crossed-delta notation ($\delta$) is a thermodynamic convention used to indicate that heat and work are **inexact differentials** (path functions), while $dE$ is an **exact differential** (point function).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1, PCMET403):** Students must be able to identify, classify, and express mathematically all forms of energy — macroscopic (potential, kinetic), microscopic (internal), and energy-in-transit (heat, work). This forms the foundation for the First Law application in Modules 2 and 3.

### 1.2 The Three-Group Classification of Energy

Energy manifests in countless physical forms, but the KTU 2024 Engineering Thermodynamics curriculum groups all energy into **three fundamental categories** based on how the energy is stored or transferred:

| # | Group | Forms of Energy | Status |
|---|-------|----------------|--------|
| 1 | **Macroscopic Energy** (Stored — system as a whole) | Potential Energy (PE), Kinetic Energy (KE) | Stored within the system as observable motion/position |
| 2 | **Microscopic Energy** (Stored — molecular level) | Internal Energy (U), Chemical Energy, Nuclear Energy | Stored due to molecular structure and activity |
| 3 | **Energy in Transit** (across boundary) | Heat (Q), Work (W) | Being transferred across system boundary at that instant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Energy of a System:** The total energy $E$ of a closed system is the sum of all macroscopic, microscopic, and (sometimes) flow energies:
> $$E_{total} = U + KE + PE + E_{flow} + \ldots$$
> where $E_{flow}$ is the energy associated with mass flow (relevant for open systems, covered in Module 3).

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Waterfall & the Lake"

Think of energy in the universe like a giant water system:

- **Imagine a calm lake at the top of a mountain** → This represents **stored energy** in a system. The water *has* the capacity to do work (it could be released to turn a turbine), but right now it is simply sitting still. The energy is **stored** in the form of gravitational potential energy, plus a tiny bit of thermal (internal) energy from its temperature.

- **Now imagine opening a dam** → Water rushes down. As it flows through pipes, it moves (kinetic energy), falls (potential energy conversion), and pushes against a turbine blade (does work). The energy is **in transit**.

- **The friction in the pipes generates heat** → This is heat energy being transferred to the surroundings.

This single analogy captures the essence: **Stored energy is "what the system has" and Transit energy is "what is moving across the system boundary right now."**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exam:** **"S-S-W"** → **Stored energy (S)** is what a system **H**as; **Work (W)** and **Heat** are energy in **T**ransit. The boundary in a system diagram is your visual cue — anything crossing it is transit energy.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (for KTU Reference)

The following universal constants are frequently used in energy calculations:

- **Standard gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$
- **1 kcal** = **4.1868 kJ**
- **1 kWh** = **3600 kJ** = **3.6 MJ**
- **1 Btu** = **1.05506 kJ**
- **1 ft·lbf** = **1.35582 J**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU problems are almost exclusively in **SI units**: energy in **kJ**, mass in **kg**, velocity in **m/s**, elevation in **m**. Always convert to SI before solving to avoid unit-mismatch deductions in the answer script.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Macroscopic Forms of Energy — The "Big Picture" Energies

Macroscopic energy is the energy a system possesses **as a whole** due to its position or motion relative to a reference frame. It is the energy visible at a glance — no microscope needed.

### 2.1.1 Potential Energy (PE) — Energy of Position

**Definition:** Potential energy is the energy possessed by a system by virtue of its **elevation** in a gravitational field relative to a reference datum (usually Earth's surface or any arbitrary horizontal plane).

**Physical Interpretation:** When you lift a heavy object to a height, you do work *against gravity*, and that work is "stored" in the object as gravitational potential energy. Drop the object, and this energy converts to kinetic energy.

**Key Assumptions in KTU Problems:**
- Gravitational field is **uniform** (constant $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$).
- The reference datum is **arbitrary but must be fixed** for the duration of a problem.
- Sign convention: **PE is positive above datum, negative below datum**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The datum choice **does not affect the change** in PE — only absolute values. For exam problems, always quote $\Delta PE$, never absolute $PE$ (since absolute PE has no physical meaning — the gravitational field theoretically extends to infinity).

---

### 2.1.2 Kinetic Energy (KE) — Energy of Motion

**Definition:** Kinetic energy is the energy possessed by a system by virtue of its **velocity** relative to a reference frame (usually the Earth's surface, considered inertial in most engineering problems).

**Physical Interpretation:** A moving car, flowing water in a pipe, rotating turbine blade — all have kinetic energy. The faster they move (or the more massive), the more kinetic energy they possess.

**Reference Frame Warning:** Kinetic energy is **frame-dependent**. A passenger sitting motionless on a train has zero KE relative to the train, but non-zero KE relative to a ground observer. KTU problems always assume the **stationary Earth frame** unless otherwise stated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Relativistic caveat (beyond KTU scope):** At velocities approaching the speed of light, $KE = (\gamma - 1) m c^2$, where $\gamma$ is the Lorentz factor. However, for all engineering thermodynamics at KTU level, we use the classical Newtonian form $KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^2$.

---

## 2.2 Microscopic Forms of Energy — The "Molecular Scale" Energies

Microscopic energy is energy stored at the **molecular and atomic scale** — invisible to the naked eye. It is the dominant form of energy in most stationary thermodynamic systems.

### 2.2.1 Internal Energy (U) — The Master Microscopic Energy

**Definition:** Internal energy is the **sum of all microscopic forms of energy** of a system — the kinetic energy of molecular motion (translational, rotational, vibrational) plus the potential energy of molecular bonds and intermolecular forces.

$$U = U_{translational} + U_{rotational} + U_{vibrational} + U_{bond} + U_{nuclear} + \ldots$$

**Key Properties of Internal Energy (KTU High-Yield):**
- $U$ is an **extensive** property (depends on mass of system).
- The **specific** internal energy $u = U/m$ is an **intensive** property.
- For an **ideal gas**, $U$ is a function of **temperature only**: $u = u(T)$.
- For real substances (vapors, liquids), $u = u(T, v)$ — depends on both temperature and specific volume.
- Internal energy of an ideal gas: $u_2 - u_1 = c_v (T_2 - T_1)$.

**Molecular Sub-Components (for conceptual depth):**

| Sub-form | Physical Cause | Dominant in |
|----------|----------------|-------------|
| **Sensible energy** | Translational KE of molecules | All phases (gases, liquids, solids) |
| **Latent energy** | Intermolecular potential energy (phase change) | Phase transitions (boiling, melting) |
| **Chemical energy** | Atomic bonds within molecules | Combustion, reactions |
| **Nuclear energy** | Binding energy in nucleus | Fission, fusion |
| **Radiant/surface energy** | Electron energy levels | Plasma, very high temperature systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For Module 1 of PCMET403, the focus is on **sensible internal energy** only. Chemical and nuclear energies are *excluded from the system boundary* and treated separately in combustion (Module 4) and nuclear engineering (elective courses).

---

### 2.2.2 Sensible Energy vs. Latent Energy — A Subtle Distinction

Within the sensible internal energy (the part that changes with temperature), and latent internal energy (the part that changes with phase), we have:

- **Sensible energy** ($u_{sens}$): Energy change detected by a *thermometer*. It changes with $T$.
  - $u_{2,sens} - u_{1,sens} = c_v (T_2 - T_1)$ for an ideal gas.
- **Latent energy** ($u_{lat}$): Energy change associated with *phase change* at constant $T$.
  - $u_{fg} = h_{fg} - P v_{fg}$ (related to latent heat of vaporization).

> [!TIP]
> **Why "sensible"?** Because it can be **sensed** (felt or measured) by a thermometer. **Latent** means "hidden" — temperature doesn't change during phase change, but energy is being absorbed/released nonetheless.

---

## 2.3 Energy in Transit — The Boundary-Crossing Energies

When energy crosses a system boundary, it is no longer "stored" — it is in **transit**, and we call it either **heat** or **work**. These are the only two ways energy can be transferred into or out of a closed system (per the First Law).

### 2.3.1 Heat (Q) — Energy Transfer Due to Temperature Difference

**Definition:** Heat is the form of energy that is transferred across a system boundary **by virtue of a temperature difference** between the system and its surroundings.

**Critical Properties of Heat:**
- Heat is **energy in transit** — we never say "a system contains heat." A system *contains internal energy*; it *receives/transfers heat*.
- Heat is a **path function**: the amount of heat transfer depends on the *process path*, not just initial and final states. Hence the use of $\delta Q$ (inexact differential) and the notation $Q$ (not $\Delta Q$).
- **Sign convention (KTU standard):** Heat transferred **to** the system is **positive (+Q)**; heat transferred **from** the system is **negative (-Q)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Saying "the system has 500 kJ of heat" — this is **thermodynamically wrong**. The correct phrasing is "500 kJ of heat was transferred *to* the system" or "the system's internal energy *increased by* 500 kJ (assuming no work)."

---

### 2.3.2 Work (W) — Energy Transfer Due to Force/Moment Displacement

**Definition:** Work is the form of energy that is transferred across a system boundary **by virtue of an intensive property difference** other than temperature (e.g., force, pressure, voltage, magnetic field) acting through a displacement.

**The Generalized Work Equation:**

$$\delta W = F \cdot ds$$

For a closed system undergoing a quasi-equilibrium process with pressure as the driving force:

$$\delta W = P \, dV$$

For a control volume (open system), additional work terms arise — flow work, shaft work, electrical work, etc.

**Sign Convention (KTU standard):** Work done **by** the system on the surroundings is **positive (+W)**; work done **on** the system is **negative (-W)**.

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks (especially physics-oriented ones) use the *opposite* sign convention. KTU strictly follows the **engineering thermodynamics convention**: W done by system = positive. Memorize this — it's a frequent exam trap.

---

### 2.3.3 The "Path Function" vs. "Point Function" Distinction

This is one of the most heavily tested concepts in KTU exams, and it warrants a dedicated table:

| Property | Type | Notation | Differential | Example |
|----------|------|----------|--------------|---------|
| Internal energy, $U$ | Point function (state property) | $dU$ (exact) | $dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$ | Independent of path |
| Heat, $Q$ | Path function (process quantity) | $\delta Q$ (inexact) | $\oint \delta Q \neq 0$ in a cycle | Depends on path |
| Work, $W$ | Path function (process quantity) | $\delta W$ (inexact) | $\oint \delta W \neq 0$ in a cycle | Depends on path |
| Enthalpy, $H$ | Point function | $dH$ (exact) | $dH = TdS + VdP$ | Independent of path |

---

## 2.4 Mechanical Energy — A Special Composite

**Mechanical energy** is often used as a collective term for the macroscopic forms only:

$$E_{mech} = KE + PE = \frac{1}{2}m V^2 + m g z$$

In open systems (Module 3), mechanical energy changes are linked to useful work output and frictional losses via the **modified Bernoulli equation**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the complete, high-yield formula set for "Forms of Energy" in PCMET403 Module 1. Memorize all of these — they appear in nearly every Part A and Part B question.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Type |
|---|----------|---------|------------|------|
| 1 | Kinetic Energy | $KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^2 = \frac{m \dot{V}^2}{2}$ | kJ or kJ/kg | Stored (Macroscopic) |
| 2 | Potential Energy | $PE = m g z$ | kJ or kJ/kg | Stored (Macroscopic) |
| 3 | Change in KE | $\Delta KE = \frac{m(V_2^2 - V_1^2)}{2}$ | kJ or kJ/kg | Stored (Macroscopic) |
| 4 | Change in PE | $\Delta PE = m g (z_2 - z_1)$ | kJ or kJ/kg | Stored (Macroscopic) |
| 5 | Internal Energy Change (Ideal Gas) | $\Delta U = m c_v (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ | Stored (Microscopic) |
| 6 | Internal Energy Change (Real Substance) | $\Delta U = m (u_2 - u_1)$ — from tables | kJ | Stored (Microscopic) |
| 7 | Total Energy of Closed System | $E = U + KE + PE$ | kJ | Composite |
| 8 | Total Energy of Open System | $E = U + KE + PE + PV$ (where $PV$ is flow work) | kJ | Composite |
| 9 | Specific Total Energy | $e = u + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$ | kJ/kg | Composite |
| 10 | Boundary Work (Quasi-Equilibrium) | $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$ | kJ | Transit |
| 11 | Isobaric Work | $W = P (V_2 - V_1)$ | kJ | Transit |
| 12 | Isothermal Ideal Gas Work | $W = m R T \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | kJ | Transit |
| 13 | Polytropic Work | $W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$ for $n \neq 1$ | kJ | Transit |
| 14 | Heat Transfer | $Q = m c \Delta T$ (sensible) or $Q = m h_{fg}$ (latent) | kJ | Transit |
| 15 | Power | $\dot{W} = \frac{W}{\Delta t}$ | kW | Transit rate |

> **CRITICAL FORMULA ISOLATION RULE (from execution constraints):** Notice that absolute-value expressions in formulas above (such as the logarithm argument $\frac{V_2}{V_1}$ or the exponential of a difference) have been typeset to avoid any markdown-breaking pipe character. This is to ensure clean table rendering on the KTU e-learning portal.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the **Forms of Energy** is not merely academic — it underpins every thermal and mechanical system in industry:

1. **Hydropower Plants:** Water stored at height (PE) → falls through penstock → spins turbine (KE conversion) → drives generator → electrical energy. The whole cycle is governed by the energy classification we just studied.

2. **Internal Combustion Engines:** Chemical energy of fuel → combustion (chemical → thermal/internal) → high-pressure gas pushes piston (work output) → exhaust heat loss (heat to surroundings).

3. **Refrigeration & HVAC Systems:** Electrical work input → compressor raises refrigerant pressure/temperature → refrigerant absorbs heat in evaporator → cycle repeats. Energy classification tells you *where* the energy is at each stage.

4. **Rocket Propulsion:** Chemical energy of propellant → combustion → high-velocity exhaust (KE) → reaction force lifts rocket (PE gain).

5. **Wind Turbines:** Kinetic energy of moving air (KE) → rotational KE of blades → mechanical shaft work → electrical energy.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU viva/practical exams:** When asked "Why is this concept important?" always cite a real industrial application. Examiners reward practical awareness — it differentiates a 90% answer from a 75% answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Kinetic Energy from Newton's Second Law

**Goal:** Derive the expression $KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^2$ for a particle of mass $m$ moving with velocity $V$.

**Starting Point — Newton's Second Law:**

$$F = m a = m \frac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Work-Energy Theorem Statement:** The work done by a net force on a particle equals the change in its kinetic energy. In differential form:

$$\delta W_{net} = F \cdot ds$$

**Step 2 — Express Displacement in terms of Velocity and Time:** Using the chain rule of calculus, $ds = V \, dt$ (since velocity times time equals distance traveled). Substituting:

$$\delta W_{net} = F \cdot V \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Force from Newton's Second Law:** Recall $F = m \frac{dV}{dt}$. Substituting:

$$\delta W_{net} = m \frac{dV}{dt} \cdot V \, dt = m V \, dV$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from Initial State (1) to Final State (2):** The work done by the net force during the change in motion from velocity $V_1$ to $V_2$ is:

$$\int_1^2 \delta W_{net} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} m V \, dV$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Right-Hand Integral:** Since $m$ is constant:

$$\int_{V_1}^{V_2} m V \, dV = m \left[ \frac{V^2}{2} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \frac{m V_2^2}{2} - \frac{m V_1^2}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Equate and Interpret:** By the work-energy theorem, $\int \delta W_{net} = \Delta KE$:

$$\boxed{\Delta KE = \frac{m V_2^2}{2} - \frac{m V_1^2}{2} = \frac{m(V_2^2 - V_1^2)}{2}}$$

**Step 7 — Special Case (Starting from Rest):** If the particle begins at rest ($V_1 = 0$) and accelerates to velocity $V$:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^2 \quad \text{(absolute kinetic energy)}$$

**Engineering Units Check:** In SI, $[m] = \text{kg}$, $[V^2] = \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$, so $[KE] = \text{kg·m}^2/\text{s}^2 = \text{J}$ ✓. In kJ: divide by 1000.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gravitational Potential Energy from Work Against Gravity

**Goal:** Derive $PE = m g z$ for a body of mass $m$ at elevation $z$ above a reference datum.

**Starting Point — Definition of Work:** Work is force times displacement in the direction of force. To lift a body vertically, the applied force must balance gravity:

$$F_{applied} = m g \quad \text{(for quasi-static, no acceleration)}$$

**Step 1 — Work Done in Lifting:** The displacement is vertical, $dz$. The work done by the external (lifting) force is:

$$\delta W_{lift} = F_{applied} \cdot dz = m g \, dz$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from Datum ($z = 0$) to Elevation $z$:**

$$\int_0^z \delta W_{lift} = \int_0^z m g \, dz$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Right-Hand Integral:** Assuming $g$ and $m$ are constant over the elevation range:

$$\int_0^z m g \, dz = m g [z]_0^z = m g z - 0 = m g z$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Stored Energy:** This work input is "stored" as potential energy in the body:

$$\boxed{PE = m g z}$$

**Step 5 — Change in PE Between Two States:**

$$\Delta PE = PE_2 - PE_1 = m g (z_2 - z_1)$$

**Note on Datum Independence:** The *change* $\Delta PE$ is independent of the choice of datum. The absolute $PE$ depends on datum, but for any thermodynamic problem we always work with $\Delta PE$, so datum choice is free.

**Engineering Units Check:** $[m] = \text{kg}$, $[g] = \text{m/s}^2$, $[z] = \text{m}$, so $[PE] = \text{kg·m}^2/\text{s}^2 = \text{J}$ ✓. In kJ: divide by 1000.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Boundary Work for a Quasi-Equilibrium Process

**Goal:** Derive $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$ for a closed system undergoing quasi-equilibrium expansion or compression.

**Starting Point — Definition of Mechanical Work:**

$$\delta W = F \cdot ds$$

**Step 1 — Convert Force to Pressure:** For a piston-cylinder system with piston face area $A$, the force exerted by the gas on the piston is:

$$F = P \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Express Displacement as Volume Change:** When the piston moves a distance $ds$, the volume swept is $dV = A \cdot ds$, so $ds = dV / A$. Substituting:

$$\delta W = (P \cdot A) \cdot \frac{dV}{A} = P \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Over the Process:**

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

This is the **area under the curve** on a $P$–$V$ diagram — a key visualization for KTU exam problems.

**Step 4 — Special Case: Constant Pressure (Isobaric Process):** If $P$ = constant:

$$W = P \int_{V_1}^{V_2} dV = P (V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 5 — Special Case: Isothermal Ideal Gas ($PV = mRT$ = constant):** Substituting $P = mRT / V$:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{mRT}{V} dV = mRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V} = mRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

**Step 6 — General Polytropic Process ($PV^n = $ constant):** For $n \neq 1$:

$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$$

For $n = 1$ (isothermal ideal gas), the above expression is indeterminate ($0/0$), and we use the logarithmic form from Step 5.

**Engineering Units Check:** $[P] = \text{kPa} = \text{kN/m}^2$, $[V] = \text{m}^3$, so $[W] = \text{kPa·m}^3 = \text{kJ}$ ✓ (this is the elegant SI property).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Internal Energy Change for an Ideal Gas

**Goal:** Show that for an ideal gas, $\Delta U = m c_v (T_2 - T_1)$.

**Starting Point — Joule's Law:** For an ideal gas, internal energy is a function of temperature only: $U = U(T)$.

**Step 1 — Total Differential of $U(T, V)$:** Since $U = U(T, V)$:

$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 2 — Apply Joule's Law:** The second term vanishes because $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = 0$ for an ideal gas:

$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Partial Derivative as $c_v$:** The specific heat at constant volume is defined as:

$$c_v = \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_v$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by Mass:** For a system of mass $m$:

$$dU = m \, c_v \, dT$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Over the Process:**

$$U_2 - U_1 = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} m \, c_v \, dT$$

**Step 6 — Assume $c_v$ is Constant** (valid for many ideal gases over moderate temperature ranges):

$$U_2 - U_1 = m \, c_v \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT = m \, c_v (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 7 — Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{\Delta U_{ideal\,gas} = m \, c_v \, (T_2 - T_1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $c_v$ varies with temperature, we use the **polynomial form** or **table lookups** (covered in Module 2). For Module 1, the constant $c_v$ assumption is standard.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Combining All Forms of Energy

**Problem:** A water pump delivers **5000 kg/h** of water from a lower reservoir to an upper reservoir. The water enters the pump at a velocity of **2 m/s** and exits at a velocity of **12 m/s**. The elevation difference is **25 m**. The pipe diameter is constant. Assuming no heat transfer and no change in internal energy (incompressible flow), calculate the mechanical energy input required by the pump per hour.

**Given Data:**
- $\dot{m} = 5000 \text{ kg/h} = 5000/3600 \text{ kg/s} = 1.389 \text{ kg/s}$
- $V_1 = 2 \text{ m/s}$, $V_2 = 12 \text{ m/s}$
- $z_1 = 0 \text{ m}$ (datum at lower reservoir), $z_2 = 25 \text{ m}$
- $\Delta U = 0$ (given — temperature unchanged, incompressible water)
- $Q = 0$ (given)
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Identify All Forms of Energy Changes:**

| Form | Initial | Final | Change |
|------|---------|-------|--------|
| Internal Energy, $\Delta U$ | 0 | 0 | 0 (given) |
| Kinetic Energy, $\Delta KE$ | $\frac{1}{2}m V_1^2$ | $\frac{1}{2}m V_2^2$ | $\frac{1}{2}m (V_2^2 - V_1^2)$ |
| Potential Energy, $\Delta PE$ | 0 | $m g z_2$ | $m g (z_2 - z_1)$ |

**Step 2 — Apply the First Law for a Control Volume (Module 3 preview, but conceptually introduced here):**

$$W_{pump,in} = \Delta KE + \Delta PE + \Delta U$$

(The pump work input equals the total mechanical energy gained by the fluid — ignoring losses.)

**Step 3 — Compute $\Delta KE$ in kJ/s:**

$$\Delta \dot{KE} = \frac{\dot{m}}{2} (V_2^2 - V_1^2) = \frac{1.389}{2} (12^2 - 2^2) = \frac{1.389}{2} (144 - 4) = \frac{1.389 \times 140}{2}$$

$$\Delta \dot{KE} = \frac{194.46}{2} = 97.23 \text{ J/s} = 0.09723 \text{ kJ/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta PE$ in kJ/s:**

$$\Delta \dot{PE} = \dot{m} \cdot g \cdot (z_2 - z_1) = 1.389 \times 9.81 \times 25$$

$$\Delta \dot{PE} = 1.389 \times 245.25 = 340.65 \text{ J/s} = 0.3407 \text{ kJ/s}$$

**Step 5 — Total Mechanical Power Required:**

$$\dot{W}_{pump} = \Delta \dot{KE} + \Delta \dot{PE} + \Delta \dot{U} = 0.09723 + 0.3407 + 0 = 0.4379 \text{ kJ/s} = 0.4379 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Energy per Hour:**

$$W_{pump,hourly} = \dot{W}_{pump} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 0.4379 \times 3600 = 1576.5 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 7 — Final Answer with Units:**

$$\boxed{W_{pump,hourly} \approx 1576.5 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 1.577 \text{ MJ/h}}$$

**Verification — Dimensional Check:**
- $\Delta KE$: kg × m²/s² = J ✓
- $\Delta PE$: kg × m/s² × m = J ✓
- Both are energies as expected ✓
- Sum is positive → pump *consumes* work to deliver fluid upward and faster, as physically intuitive ✓

> [!TIP]
> This problem integrates the three forms of energy (KE, PE, U) in a real-world engineering context. It is a *highly representative* KTU 14-mark problem. The exact structure (given flow rate, velocities, elevation, asked to find pump work) is one of the most common templates in KTU End Semester Examinations.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Forms of Energy Summary

| Energy Form | Symbolic | Depends on | Path/Point? | Typical KTU Problem Type |
|-------------|----------|------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Kinetic Energy | $\frac{1}{2}m V^2$ | Mass, velocity | Point (state) | Calculate KE of fluid jet, moving vehicle |
| Potential Energy | $m g z$ | Mass, elevation, gravity | Point (state) | Calculate PE change due to elevation change |
| Internal Energy | $U$ | Temperature, specific volume (real); $T$ only (ideal gas) | Point (state) | Use steam tables; ideal gas formula |
| Flow Energy | $PV$ | Pressure, volume | Point (state) | Open systems, enthalpy $H = U + PV$ |
| Heat (transfer) | $Q$ | Path-dependent | Path | Compute $Q = \Delta U + W$ in a cycle |
| Work (transfer) | $W$ | Path-dependent | Path | $\int P dV$, $\int \tau d\theta$ (shaft), etc. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Diagram — Forms of Energy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ENERGY E total]:::root
    A --> B[MACROSCOPIC ENERGY]:::macro
    A --> C[MICROSCOPIC ENERGY]:::micro
    A --> D[ENERGY IN TRANSIT]:::transit

    B --> B1[Kinetic Energy KE = 1 by 2 m V squared]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Potential Energy PE = m g z]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Internal Energy U]:::leaf
    C1 --> C1a[Sensible u sens]:::leaf
    C1 --> C1b[Latent u lat]:::leaf
    C1 --> C1c[Chemical]:::leaf
    C1 --> C1d[Nuclear]:::leaf

    D --> D1[Heat Q]:::leaf
    D --> D2[Work W]:::leaf
    D2 --> D2a[Boundary Wb]:::leaf
    D2 --> D2b[Shaft Wsh]:::leaf
    D2 --> D2c[Electrical We]:::leaf
    D2 --> D2d[Flow Wflow]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef macro fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef micro fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef transit fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- The root node **E (total energy)** branches into the three primary categories.
- **Macroscopic energy** (blue branch) contains KE and PE — visible, observable energies.
- **Microscopic energy** (pink branch) contains U, with sub-components including sensible, latent, chemical, and nuclear sub-forms.
- **Energy in transit** (green branch) is the only branch that crosses the system boundary; it splits into heat Q and work W, with work further sub-divided into boundary, shaft, electrical, and flow work.

---

## 4.2 System Boundary Schematic — Energy Crossing the System

```mermaid
graph LR
    Surr[SURROUNDINGS]:::surr
    Sys[SYSTEM]:::sys
    Bd[System Boundary]:::bd
    Surr ---|Heat Q| Bd
    Surr ---|Work W| Bd
    Bd --- Sys
    Sys -->|Stored Energy U KE PE| Sys

    classDef surr fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sys fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef bd fill:#FFD700,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:4px,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **System** (red box) holds the *stored* forms of energy — U, KE, PE.
- The **Boundary** (gold line) is the imaginary surface that separates the system from the surroundings.
- The **Surroundings** (purple) exchange energy with the system only as **heat Q** and **work W** — these are the *only* two modes of energy transfer for a closed system (First Law basis).

---

## 4.3 Energy Conversion Flow — Hydroelectric Power Plant (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Reservoir at Height z]:::reservoir
    B[PE Stored m g z]:::pe
    C[Penstock Pipe Falling Water]:::penstock
    D[KE Gained 1 by 2 m V squared]:::ke
    E[Turbine Spinning Shaft]:::turbine
    F[Mechanical Shaft Work Wsh]:::mech
    G[Generator Rotor]:::gen
    H[Electrical Energy Output]:::elec
    I[Waste Heat Qloss]:::loss

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    E -.->|Inefficiency| I

    classDef reservoir fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pe fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef penstock fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ke fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef turbine fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gen fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#FFD700,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#FF6347,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:**
This block-level functional architecture flow illustrates the cascading energy transformations in a hydroelectric power plant — one of the most illustrative real-world examples for the *Forms of Energy* concept. Each colored block represents a stage where one form of energy converts to another. The dashed arrow to "Waste Heat" represents irreversible losses (entropy generation, not covered in Module 1, but mentioned for industrial context).

---

## 4.4 Energy Mapping at Each Stage (Annotation Table)

| Stage | Energy Form Present | Symbolic Form | Magnitude (typical) |
|-------|---------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| Reservoir | Potential (dominant) + Internal (water temp) | $PE = mgz$ | Very high (e.g., 500 m head) |
| Penstock | Kinetic (growing) + Potential (decreasing) | $KE + PE$ | Sum = constant (if no friction) |
| Turbine | Mechanical (rotational KE) | $KE_{rot} = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ | Spins at ~300 rpm |
| Shaft | Mechanical (work transfer) | $W_{sh} = \int \tau d\theta$ | High (MW scale) |
| Generator | Electrical | $P = VI$ | Stepped up to grid voltage |
| Environment | Thermal (waste heat) | $Q_{loss}$ | Lost to river + air |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table summarizes a complete energy audit of a real engineering system. Notice that **at every stage, the total energy is conserved** — only its *form* changes. This is the deepest truth that the study of "Forms of Energy" is trying to convey.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions in KTU typically test direct conceptual understanding, definitions, and one-line derivations. Answers should be **concise (3-5 sentences) but precise** — examiners deduct marks for vagueness or missing keywords.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Distinguish between macroscopic and microscopic forms of energy with two examples each." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Macroscopic energy is the energy a system possesses as a whole due to its position or motion relative to an external reference frame, while microscopic energy is the energy stored at the molecular and atomic level due to molecular structure, motion, and interactions.

- **Macroscopic energy** examples:
  1. **Kinetic energy** ($KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^2$) — energy due to bulk motion.
  2. **Potential energy** ($PE = mgz$) — energy due to elevation in a gravitational field.

- **Microscopic energy** examples:
  1. **Internal energy** ($U$) — sum of molecular translational, rotational, vibrational kinetic energy plus intermolecular potential energy.
  2. **Chemical energy** — energy stored in atomic/molecular bonds (e.g., energy in fuels like methane, gasoline).

Macroscopic energy is observable at human scale; microscopic energy requires knowledge of molecular/atomic structure to characterize.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Why are heat and work classified as path functions, while internal energy is a point function? Give one example for each." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **point function** (or state function) depends only on the state of the system, not on how that state was reached. A **path function** depends on the *process path* taken between two states.

- **Internal energy** is a point function because it is a thermodynamic property — a unique function of state variables (T, P, v). For example, the internal energy of water at 100°C and 1 atm is the same regardless of whether the water was heated from 20°C or cooled from 200°C.

- **Heat** is a path function because the amount of heat transferred between two states depends on the process path. For example, the heat required to change the state of an ideal gas from (T₁, V₁) to (T₂, V₂) differs for an isochoric process versus an isobaric process.

- **Work** is similarly a path function. The work done by an expanding gas depends on whether the expansion is isothermal, adiabatic, or polytropic — even when initial and final states are identical. Mathematically, $\oint \delta W \neq 0$ around a closed cycle (work is not conserved), while $\oint dU = 0$ (internal energy is conserved around a cycle).

This distinction is fundamental to the First Law of Thermodynamics: $Q - W = \Delta U$, where $Q$ and $W$ are path-dependent but their difference is path-independent.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry 14 marks each and include an *internal choice* — students must answer either "OR" option. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. We provide **TWO full alternatives** (Q-A and Q-B) below, each fully solved.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q-A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**
**A pump draws water from a lower reservoir and delivers it to a higher reservoir through a pipe of constant cross-section. Given:**
- Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = 10 \text{ kg/s}$
- Water velocity at pump inlet: $V_1 = 3 \text{ m/s}$
- Water velocity at pump outlet: $V_2 = 15 \text{ m/s}$
- Elevation difference: $z_2 - z_1 = 20 \text{ m}$
- Assume no change in internal energy and no heat transfer to surroundings.

**Find:**
- **(a)** The change in kinetic energy of water per second (in kJ/s). (7 Marks)
- **(b)** The change in potential energy of water per second (in kJ/s), and the total mechanical power input to the pump in kW. (7 Marks)

---

**Solution to Q-A:**

**Given Data Summary:**
- $\dot{m} = 10 \text{ kg/s}$
- $V_1 = 3 \text{ m/s}$, $V_2 = 15 \text{ m/s}$
- $\Delta z = z_2 - z_1 = 20 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\Delta u = 0$ (no internal energy change), $Q = 0$ (no heat transfer)

---

**Part (a) — Change in Kinetic Energy per second:**

The kinetic energy per unit mass is $ke = \frac{V^2}{2}$. The change in kinetic energy per unit mass is:

$$\Delta ke = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} = \frac{15^2 - 3^2}{2} = \frac{225 - 9}{2} = \frac{216}{2} = 108 \text{ J/kg}$$

Converting to kJ/kg: $\Delta ke = 0.108 \text{ kJ/kg}$

The rate of change of kinetic energy (power associated with KE change) is:

$$\Delta \dot{KE} = \dot{m} \times \Delta ke = 10 \text{ kg/s} \times 0.108 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta \dot{KE} = 1.08 \text{ kJ/s} = 1.08 \text{ kW}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Change in Potential Energy and Total Pump Power:**

The change in potential energy per unit mass is:

$$\Delta pe = g (z_2 - z_1) = 9.81 \times 20 = 196.2 \text{ J/kg}$$

Converting: $\Delta pe = 0.1962 \text{ kJ/kg}$

The rate of change of potential energy is:

$$\Delta \dot{PE} = \dot{m} \times \Delta pe = 10 \times 0.1962 = 1.962 \text{ kJ/s} = 1.962 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta \dot{PE} = 1.962 \text{ kJ/s} = 1.962 \text{ kW}}$$

**Total Mechanical Power Input to the Pump:**

Applying the energy balance for the control volume (steady-state, no heat loss, no internal energy change, neglecting KE/PE of surroundings):

$$\dot{W}_{pump,in} = \Delta \dot{KE} + \Delta \dot{PE} + \Delta \dot{U}$$

Substituting the calculated values:

$$\dot{W}_{pump,in} = 1.08 + 1.962 + 0 = 3.042 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{W}_{pump,in} = 3.042 \text{ kW} \approx 3.04 \text{ kW}}$$

**Verification — Sanity Check:**
- This is a small, residential-scale pump (3 kW ≈ 4 HP). Realistic ✓
- PE change (1.96 kW) > KE change (1.08 kW) because the elevation change is the dominant energy requirement. ✓
- The pump work is positive, meaning energy is being supplied to the fluid. ✓

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

#### **Q-B. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Define the following terms, giving one example of each:
  1. Sensible energy
  2. Latent energy
  3. Chemical energy
  4. Nuclear energy
  (4 Marks)

**(b)** A piston-cylinder assembly contains 0.05 m³ of an ideal gas at 200 kPa. The gas is heated at constant pressure until its volume becomes 0.10 m³. If the gas has $c_v = 0.717 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, and the initial temperature is 300 K, calculate:
  1. The mass of the gas (2 Marks)
  2. The final temperature (2 Marks)
  3. The change in internal energy of the gas (3 Marks)
  4. The boundary work done by the gas (3 Marks)

---

**Solution to Q-B:**

**Part (a) — Definitions (1 Mark Each):**

1. **Sensible Energy:** It is the internal energy of a substance that is associated with a change in its temperature, detectable by a thermometer. *Example:* Heating water from 20°C to 80°C involves a change in sensible energy.

2. **Latent Energy:** It is the internal energy associated with a phase change of a substance at constant temperature. *Example:* The energy absorbed by water at 100°C during vaporization into steam (latent heat of vaporization).

3. **Chemical Energy:** It is the energy stored in the bonds between atoms within molecules. It is released or absorbed during chemical reactions. *Example:* The energy released during the combustion of methane (CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O + energy).

4. **Nuclear Energy:** It is the energy stored in the nucleus of an atom, released during nuclear reactions like fission or fusion. *Example:* Energy produced in nuclear power plants via uranium-235 fission, or in the sun via hydrogen fusion.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem:**

**Given Data:**
- Initial volume, $V_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$
- Pressure, $P = 200 \text{ kPa}$ (constant)
- Final volume, $V_2 = 0.10 \text{ m}^3$
- $c_v = 0.717 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Initial temperature, $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$
- Ideal gas assumption

**Step 1 — Find $c_p$:**

$$c_p = c_v + R = 0.717 + 0.287 = 1.004 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Mass of Gas Using Ideal Gas Law:**

$$PV_1 = m R T_1$$

Solving for $m$:

$$m = \frac{P V_1}{R T_1} = \frac{200 \times 0.05}{0.287 \times 300} = \frac{10}{86.1} = 0.1161 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 0.1161 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Final Temperature Using Constant Pressure Law (Charles's Law):**

At constant pressure, $\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2}$:

$$T_2 = T_1 \times \frac{V_2}{V_1} = 300 \times \frac{0.10}{0.05} = 300 \times 2 = 600 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 = 600 \text{ K}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Change in Internal Energy:**

For an ideal gas, $\Delta U = m c_v \Delta T$:

$$\Delta U = 0.1161 \times 0.717 \times (600 - 300)$$

$$\Delta U = 0.1161 \times 0.717 \times 300 = 0.1161 \times 215.1$$

$$\boxed{\Delta U = 24.97 \text{ kJ} \approx 25.0 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Boundary Work:**

For an isobaric (constant pressure) process:

$$W_b = P (V_2 - V_1) = 200 \text{ kPa} \times (0.10 - 0.05) \text{ m}^3 = 200 \times 0.05$$

$$\boxed{W_b = 10 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Verification — Cross-check with First Law (not asked, but good practice):**
- $Q$ (heat added) at constant pressure: $Q = m c_p \Delta T = 0.1161 \times 1.004 \times 300 = 34.97 \text{ kJ}$
- $W_b = 10 \text{ kJ}$
- $\Delta U = Q - W_b = 34.97 - 10 = 24.97 \text{ kJ}$ ✓ (matches Step 4)

This cross-verification confirms the calculation is consistent with the First Law of Thermodynamics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Students often compute $P \Delta V$ with $P$ in kPa and $V$ in liters, getting nonsensical units. Always convert: $V$ in **m³** and $P$ in **kPa** → result in **kJ**. If $V$ is in liters, divide by 1000 first.
> 2. **Forgetting $R$ in ideal gas law:** $PV = mRT$ requires the **specific** gas constant $R$ (not the universal $R_u = 8.314$). For air, $R_{air} = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$. Using $R_u$ instead of $R$ is a 2-mark deduction in many answer scripts.
> 3. **Sign convention flip:** Writing $W = -P \Delta V$ (when the system *expands* and *does* positive work) — a classic sign error. KTU follows the convention: $W > 0$ when work is done **by** the system.
> 4. **Confusing $c_p$ and $c_v$:** Students frequently use $c_p$ instead of $c_v$ in the $\Delta U$ formula. Remember: $\Delta U = m c_v \Delta T$ (always), $\Delta H = m c_p \Delta T$ (always).
> 5. **Datum confusion in PE problems:** If the problem says "datum at the lower reservoir" and the elevation difference is given, do not add an extra PE term for the absolute height. Always use *change* in elevation.
> 6. **Treating heat as a property:** Never write "system contains 50 kJ of heat." This is a fundamental conceptual error. The system *contains* internal energy; heat is *transferred across the boundary*.
> 7. **Missing the $R$ vs $R_u$ distinction in ideal gas problems:** Multiply/divide by molecular weight to convert between specific and universal gas constants.
> 8. **Rounding too early:** In a 14-mark problem, round only the final answer to 3-4 significant figures. Intermediate calculations should retain at least 5-6 digits to avoid cumulative error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> This high-density summary serves as your **last-minute revision sheet** before the KTU exam. Read it the night before the test. If you can tick every box below, you are well-prepared for any "Forms of Energy" question.

### ✅ Core Concept Checklist

- [x] **Energy definition** — Capacity to do work or transfer heat. Scalar, extensive, point property.
- [x] **Three categories** — Macroscopic (KE, PE), Microscopic (U), Transit (Q, W).
- [x] **KE formula** — $KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^2$ (point function, frame-dependent).
- [x] **PE formula** — $PE = m g z$ (point function, datum-dependent; only $\Delta PE$ is meaningful).
- [x] **Internal energy, $U$** — Sum of all molecular-scale energies. For ideal gas, $U = U(T)$ only.
- [x] **Heat, $Q$** — Energy in transit due to *temperature difference*. Path function. Notation: $\delta Q$ (inexact differential).
- [x] **Work, $W$** — Energy in transit due to *force/displacement*. Path function. Notation: $\delta W$ (inexact differential).
- [x] **First Law (Energy Conservation)** — $\Delta E = Q - W$ (closed system, sign convention: Q to system positive, W by system positive).
- [x] **Path vs. point function** — Path functions: Q, W. Point functions: U, H, T, P, V, S, etc.
- [x] **Boundary work (quasi-equilibrium)** — $W_b = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P dV$.
- [x] **Isobaric work** — $W_b = P (V_2 - V_1)$.
- [x] **Isothermal ideal gas work** — $W_b = m R T \ln(V_2 / V_1)$.
- [x] **Polytropic work** — $W_b = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$ for $n \neq 1$.
- [x] **Ideal gas internal energy change** — $\Delta U = m c_v (T_2 - T_1)$.
- [x] **Total energy of closed system** — $E = U + KE + PE$.
- [x] **Total energy per unit mass** — $e = u + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$.

### ✅ High-Yield Numerical Conversions

- [x] **1 kJ** = 1000 J = 1000 N·m
- [x] **1 kWh** = 3600 kJ
- [x] **1 kcal** = 4.1868 kJ
- [x] **1 Btu** = 1.05506 kJ
- [x] **g** = 9.81 m/s² (Earth surface, SI standard)
- [x] **R (air)** = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K)
- [x] **c_v (air)** = 0.717 kJ/(kg·K)
- [x] **c_p (air)** = 1.004 kJ/(kg·K)
- [x] **γ (air)** = c_p / c_v ≈ 1.4

### ✅ Conceptual Traps to Avoid

- [x] Heat is **not** a property — don't say "system has heat."
- [x] KE and PE are **relative** to a frame and datum — only their *changes* matter in problems.
- [x] Work done **by** system is **positive** (KTU convention).
- [x] Heat transferred **to** system is **positive** (KTU convention).
- [x] For ideal gas, $U$ depends only on $T$ — not on $P$ or $V$ (Joule's Law).
- [x] The "$\delta$" prefix on $Q$ and $W$ indicates an *inexact differential* — always use this notation in derivations.
- [x] The "boundary work = area under P-V curve" — visualize before integrating.
- [x] A closed system has no mass transfer, but energy (Q, W) crosses boundary freely.
- [x] Internal energy *includes* sensible, latent, chemical, nuclear, etc. — context tells you which to include.

### ✅ Quick-Reference Decision Flow for Exam

When faced with a "Forms of Energy" problem, ask:

1. **Is the system closed or open?** → Determines whether mass crosses the boundary.
2. **What are the given properties?** → Mass, velocity, elevation, temperature, pressure, volume, $c_v$, $c_p$, $R$.
3. **What is being asked?** → $\Delta KE$, $\Delta PE$, $\Delta U$, $Q$, $W$, or total energy change.
4. **Apply the right formula from the cheat sheet** → Plug in values, watch units.
5. **Verify with the First Law** → $Q - W = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$ (for closed system).
6. **Check sign convention** → Q to system = +, W by system = +.
7. **Final answer with correct units** → kJ for energy, kW for power, K for temperature, m/s for velocity.

---

> **End of Notes — Module 1: Forms of Energy | PCMET403 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Energy (U)** is defined as the **sum of all microscopic forms of energy** associated with the molecules of a system. It represents the energy stored at the molecular and atomic level, which is neither macroscopic kinetic energy nor macroscopic potential energy of the system as a whole.

In rigorous thermodynamic notation, the **total energy (E)** of a closed system is partitioned as:

$$E = U + \text{KE} + \text{PE}$$

where **KE** is the macroscopic kinetic energy of the system (due to its motion relative to a reference frame), **PE** is the macroscopic potential energy (due to its position in a gravitational or external field), and **U** is the **internal (microscopic) energy**.

Microscopically, internal energy can be expressed as:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{KE}_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{PE}_i$$

where the first summation is the **total kinetic energy of all molecules** and the second is the **total potential energy of all molecules** arising from inter- and intra-molecular forces.

The **specific internal energy** (per unit mass) is denoted:

$$u = \frac{U}{m} \quad [\text{units: kJ/kg}]$$

### 1.2 Decomposition of Microscopic Energies

| Component | Symbol | Physical Origin |
|-----------|--------|-----------------|
| Translational KE | $U_{trans}$ | Linear motion of molecules in space |
| Rotational KE | $U_{rot}$ | Rotation of molecules about their centre of mass |
| Vibrational KE | $U_{vib}$ | Oscillation of atoms within a molecule |
| Vibrational PE | $U_{vib,PE}$ | Elastic deformation of molecular bonds |
| Intermolecular PE | $U_{inter}$ | Van der Waals forces between molecules |
| Chemical bond PE | $U_{chem}$ | Energy stored in atomic bonds |
| Nuclear PE | $U_{nuc}$ | Energy within the atomic nucleus |

> [!NOTE]
> For an **Ideal Gas**, molecules are assumed to be point masses with **no intermolecular forces**. Therefore, $U_{inter} = 0$, and internal energy depends **only on temperature**, not on pressure or volume.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Bank Account"

Imagine a sealed container of gas as a **piggy bank** of microscopic energy:

- The **coins inside** represent the total internal energy.
- **Shaking the bottle (heating)** makes the coins (molecules) bounce around more violently — the kinetic energy increases, and so does internal energy.
- **Stretching the bonds between coins** corresponds to increasing potential energy.
- A **monatomic gas** is like a piggy bank full of only 1-rupee coins (only translational KE available).
- A **diatomic gas** is like having both 1- and 2-rupee coins (translation + rotation).
- A **polyatomic gas** is like having coins of every denomination (translation + rotation + vibration).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:** Internal energy is a **point function** and an **extensive property**. It is a *state function*, meaning its value depends only on the current state (P, V, T) and not on how the system arrived there. Mathematically: $\oint dU = 0$.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314$ **kJ/kmol·K**
> - **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
> - **Standard Temperature:** $T_0 = 298.15$ **K**
> - **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Microscopic Energy Modes

#### (A) Microscopic Kinetic Energy (Sensible Component)

This is the energy associated with the **motion of molecules** and is directly related to temperature.

**Step 1 — Translational Kinetic Energy:**
- Each molecule moves freely in 3D space.
- For a single molecule of mass $m_0$ moving with velocity components $u_x, u_y, u_z$:

$$\overline{\text{KE}}_{trans} = \frac{1}{2} m_0 (u_x^2 + u_y^2 + u_z^2)$$

- Due to isotropic (random) molecular motion:

$$\overline{u_x^2} = \overline{u_y^2} = \overline{u_z^2} = \frac{1}{3} \overline{c}^2$$

- Therefore:

$$\overline{\text{KE}}_{trans} = \frac{1}{2} m_0 \overline{c}^2$$

**Step 2 — Rotational Kinetic Energy:**
- Active for **non-linear** molecules and **diatomic/polyatomic** gases.
- Monatomic gases (single atom) have negligible moment of inertia → no rotation.
- Two rotational degrees of freedom contribute for linear molecules.

**Step 3 — Vibrational Kinetic & Potential Energy:**
- Becomes significant only at **high temperatures** (typically $T > 1000$ K).
- Each vibrational mode contributes both KE and PE terms.

#### (B) Microscopic Potential Energy (Latent Component)

This is the energy stored due to **intermolecular and intramolecular forces**.

- **Intermolecular PE**: For real gases (van der Waals), molecules attract or repel each other.
- **Chemical PE**: Energy stored in molecular bonds (released during combustion).
- **Nuclear PE**: Energy in the nucleus (relevant in nuclear reactions).

#### (C) The "Why" Behind the Physics

- **Why does gas temperature rise when heated?** → Heat increases molecular velocities → translational KE increases → internal energy increases.
- **Why is internal energy of an ideal gas a function of T only?** → No intermolecular forces → no PE → only KE → which depends only on temperature.
- **Why does a monatomic gas have lower $C_v$ than a diatomic gas?** → Fewer energy-absorbing modes (no rotation, no vibration).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---------|---------------------------|
| 1 | $E = U + KE + PE$ | Total energy of a system |
| 2 | $U = \sum \text{KE}_{micro} + \sum \text{PE}_{micro}$ | Microscopic definition |
| 3 | $du = C_v \, dT$ | Differential change (ideal gas) |
| 4 | $\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$ | Finite change (ideal gas) |
| 5 | $u = C_v T$ | Specific internal energy (ideal gas) |
| 6 | $U = \frac{3}{2} m R T$ | Monatomic ideal gas |
| 7 | $U = \frac{5}{2} m R T$ | Diatomic ideal gas |
| 8 | $C_v = \frac{f}{2} R$ | Equipartition of energy |
| 9 | $H = U + PV$ | Enthalpy definition |
| 10 | $h = u + Pv$ | Specific enthalpy |
| 11 | $dh = C_p \, dT$ | Specific enthalpy change (ideal gas) |
| 12 | $\Delta H = m C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | Finite enthalpy change |
| 13 | $C_p - C_v = R$ | Mayer's Relation |
| 14 | $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ | Specific heat ratio |
| 15 | $C_v = R / (\gamma - 1)$ | $C_v$ in terms of $\gamma$ |
| 16 | $C_p = \gamma R / (\gamma - 1)$ | $C_p$ in terms of $\gamma$ |
| 17 | $u_{mono} = \frac{3}{2} R T$ | Monatomic molar specific energy |
| 18 | $u_{di} = \frac{5}{2} R T$ | Diatomic molar specific energy |
| 19 | $u_{poly} \approx \frac{6}{2} R T$ | Polyatomic (approximate) |
| 20 | $h = C_p T = (C_v + R) T$ | Enthalpy ideal gas |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Hack:** The most common mistake is mixing up **specific gas constant R** (kJ/kgK) with **universal gas constant $\bar{R}$** (kJ/kmolK). Always check units in the question stem: per kg → use R, per kmol → use $\bar{R}$.

### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Internal Energy** is central to the **First Law of Thermodynamics**: $\Delta U = Q - W$. It quantifies the energy "stored" inside a working fluid in a heat engine.
- **Enthalpy (H)** is the natural property for analyzing **open systems** (nozzles, turbines, compressors, heat exchangers) because it accounts for flow work.
- In a **steam power plant**, enthalpy drop across the turbine directly gives the work output.
- In a **gas turbine**, internal energy change of air determines the heat added in the combustion chamber.
- In **refrigeration and air-conditioning**, enthalpy is used to compute cooling capacity per kg of refrigerant.
- In **rocket propulsion**, total energy $E = U + KE + PE$ governs propellant performance.
- In **combustion analysis**, internal energy change from reactants to products gives the calorific value of fuel.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Internal Energy of a Monatomic Ideal Gas Using Kinetic Theory

**Starting Assumptions:**
- Gas is **ideal** (no intermolecular forces, point-mass molecules).
- Molecules undergo **purely translational** motion (no rotation, no vibration for monatomic).
- Motion is **random, isotropic**, and follows Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution.

**Step 1 — Set up the kinetic energy per molecule**

For a single molecule of mass $m_0$ moving with random velocity components $(u_x, u_y, u_z)$:

$$\overline{\text{KE}}_{per \, molecule} = \frac{1}{2} m_0 \left( \overline{u_x^2} + \overline{u_y^2} + \overline{u_z^2} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply isotropy (random motion property)**

For random motion, all three directions are statistically equivalent:

$$\overline{u_x^2} = \overline{u_y^2} = \overline{u_z^2} = \frac{1}{3} \overline{c}^2$$

where $\overline{c}^2$ is the **mean square molecular speed**.

Substituting back:

$$\overline{\text{KE}}_{per \, molecule} = \frac{1}{2} m_0 \cdot 3 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \overline{c}^2 = \frac{1}{2} m_0 \overline{c}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total kinetic energy for N molecules**

$$U = N \cdot \frac{1}{2} m_0 \overline{c}^2 = \frac{1}{2} (N m_0) \overline{c}^2 = \frac{1}{2} M \overline{c}^2$$

where $M = N m_0$ is the total mass of gas.

**Step 4 — Relate to pressure using kinetic theory**

From kinetic theory of gases, pressure exerted by gas on container walls is:

$$P V = \frac{1}{3} M \overline{c}^2$$

Rearranging:

$$M \overline{c}^2 = 3 P V$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into internal energy expression**

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 3 P V = \frac{3}{2} P V$$

This is the **general kinetic theory result**.

**Step 6 — Apply ideal gas equation of state**

For $m$ kg of ideal gas:

$$P V = m R T$$

Substituting:

$$U = \frac{3}{2} m R T$$

Therefore, the **specific internal energy** for a monatomic ideal gas is:

$$u = \frac{U}{m} = \frac{3}{2} R T$$

$$\boxed{u_{monatomic} = \frac{3}{2} R T}$$

**Step 7 — Per kmole expression**

For $n$ kmoles of gas (where $R$ in the above formula is the **specific** gas constant in kJ/kgK):

For 1 kmole of monatomic ideal gas, $C_v = \frac{3}{2} \bar{R}$ where $\bar{R} = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K:

$$\bar{u} = \frac{3}{2} \bar{R} T$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Internal Energy of a Diatomic Ideal Gas (Equipartition Theorem)

**Step 1 — Identify degrees of freedom**

A diatomic molecule has:
- **3 translational** DOF (movement in x, y, z)
- **2 rotational** DOF (rotation about 2 perpendicular axes through centre of mass; the third axis gives negligible moment of inertia for a thin rod-like molecule)
- **0 vibrational** DOF (negligible at room temperature)

Total DOF: $f = 3 + 2 = 5$

**Step 2 — Apply the Equipartition Theorem**

The equipartition theorem states that each translational/rotational DOF contributes $\frac{1}{2} RT$ per kmole to internal energy.

Therefore:

$$\bar{u} = \frac{f}{2} \bar{R} T = \frac{5}{2} \bar{R} T$$

$$\boxed{u_{diatomic} = \frac{5}{2} R T \text{ (per kg, R = specific gas constant)}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Change in Internal Energy for an Ideal Gas (First Law Precursor)

**Step 1 — Start from specific internal energy**

For an ideal gas, $u$ depends only on $T$:

$$u = C_v T$$

**Step 2 — Take the differential**

$$du = C_v \, dT$$

Since $C_v$ is approximately constant over moderate temperature ranges:

$$\boxed{du = C_v \, dT}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate between states 1 and 2**

$$\int_{u_1}^{u_2} du = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_v \, dT$$

$$\Delta u = C_v (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by mass**

$$\Delta U = m \Delta u = m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$$

This is the **single most tested formula** on internal energy in KTU exams.

### 3.4 Derivation: Enthalpy (H) and its Physical Significance

**Step 1 — Define enthalpy from first principles**

Enthalpy is defined as:

$$H = U + PV$$

**Step 2 — Convert to specific form**

$$h = u + Pv$$

**Step 3 — Apply ideal gas relation $Pv = RT$**

For an ideal gas, $Pv = RT$ (where $R$ is specific gas constant):

$$h = u + R T$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $u = C_v T$**

$$h = C_v T + R T = (C_v + R) T$$

By definition, $C_p = C_v + R$, therefore:

$$\boxed{h = C_p T}$$

**Step 5 — Take the differential**

$$dh = C_p \, dT$$

**Step 6 — Integrate between states**

$$\Delta h = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

### 3.5 Derivation: Mayer's Relation ($C_p - C_v = R$)

**Step 1 — Start from $h = u + RT$**

Differentiating both sides:

$$dh = du + R \, dT$$

**Step 2 — Apply definitions of specific heats**

For an ideal gas:
$$dh = C_p \, dT$$
$$du = C_v \, dT$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$C_p \, dT = C_v \, dT + R \, dT$$

**Step 4 — Divide through by $dT$**

$$C_p = C_v + R$$

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- At constant pressure, energy added as heat is used for **two purposes**:
  1. Raising internal energy (raising temperature).
  2. Doing boundary work to expand the gas against the surroundings.
- Therefore, $C_p > C_v$ always.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Internal Energy Across Gas Types

| Gas Type | DOF $f$ | $C_v$ (per kmole) | $u$ (per kmole) | $\gamma = C_p/C_v$ |
|----------|---------|-------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Monatomic | 3 | $\frac{3}{2} \bar{R}$ | $\frac{3}{2} \bar{R} T$ | $5/3 \approx 1.667$ |
| Diatomic (at room T) | 5 | $\frac{5}{2} \bar{R}$ | $\frac{5}{2} \bar{R} T$ | $7/5 = 1.4$ |
| Polyatomic (non-linear) | 6 | $\frac{6}{2} \bar{R}$ | $\frac{6}{2} \bar{R} T$ | $4/3 \approx 1.333$ |

### 3.7 Illustrative Numerical Example

**Problem:** Calculate the change in internal energy when 3 kg of air is heated from 300 K to 500 K. Take $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kgK for air (assumed ideal).

**Solution:**

Given:
- $m = 3$ kg
- $T_1 = 300$ K
- $T_2 = 500$ K
- $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kgK

Applying the formula:

$$\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$$

$$\Delta U = 3 \times 0.718 \times (500 - 300)$$

$$\Delta U = 3 \times 0.718 \times 200$$

$$\Delta U = 430.8 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta U = +430.8 \text{ kJ (internal energy increased)}}$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Decomposition of Internal Energy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Total Internal Energy U] --> nodeB[Microscopic Kinetic Energy]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Microscopic Potential Energy]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Translational KE]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Rotational KE]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Vibrational KE]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Intermolecular PE]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Chemical Bond PE]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Nuclear PE]
    nodeB1 --> nodeD[Temperature Dependent]
    nodeB2 --> nodeD
    nodeB3 --> nodeD
    nodeC1 --> nodeE[Volume Dependent]
    nodeC2 --> nodeE
    nodeC3 --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF[For Ideal Gas U equals f by 2 mRT]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Negligible for Ideal Gas]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Relationship Between U, H, and Energy Forms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Total Energy E] --> node2[Internal Energy U]
    node1 --> node3[Macroscopic KE]
    node1 --> node4[Macroscopic PE]
    node2 --> node5[Enthalpy H equals U plus PV]
    node5 --> node6[Flow Work PV]
    node2 --> node7[Sensible U]
    node2 --> node8[Latent U]
    node7 --> node9[Function of T only]
    node8 --> node10[Function of Phase]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow: From Microscopic Motion to Macroscopic Property

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Molecular Motion] --> stepB[Kinetic Energy of Molecules]
    stepB --> stepC[Apply Equipartition Theorem]
    stepC --> stepD[Compute Average KE per DOF]
    stepD --> stepE[Sum Across all DOF]
    stepE --> stepF[Internal Energy U]
    stepF --> stepG[U equals m Cv T]
    stepG --> stepH[Enthalpy H equals U plus PV]
    stepH --> stepI[H equals m Cp T]
    stepI --> stepJ[Used in First Law for Closed and Open Systems]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Enthalpy Components (Engineering View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    x1[Enthalpy H] --> x2[Internal Energy U]
    x1 --> x3[Flow Work Pv]
    x2 --> x4[Molecular KE]
    x2 --> x5[Molecular PE]
    x3 --> x6[Pushes fluid through control volume]
    x4 --> x7[Temperature related]
    x5 --> x8[Phase related]
    x6 --> x9[Relevant for open systems]
    x7 --> x10[Relevant for closed systems]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These block diagrams map the **causal and functional relationships** among microscopic energy modes, internal energy, and enthalpy. For an ideal gas, all PE pathways are inactive, and U depends only on temperature.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

Define internal energy. Why is it considered a point function and an extensive property?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Internal energy (U) is the sum of all microscopic kinetic and potential energies of the molecules within a system. It excludes macroscopic kinetic and potential energies of the system as a whole.

> **Point Function (1 mark):** The value of internal energy depends only on the **state** of the system (specified by P, V, T). It is independent of the path or process by which the state is reached. Mathematically, $\oint dU = 0$ for any cyclic process.

> **Extensive Property (1 mark):** Internal energy is proportional to the mass of the system. Doubling the mass at the same state doubles U. Therefore, it is an **extensive** property (unlike temperature which is intensive).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

Distinguish clearly between **sensible internal energy** and **latent internal energy** with one engineering example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sensible Internal Energy | Latent Internal Energy |
|--------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Definition | Energy associated with molecular kinetic activity (translation, rotation, vibration) | Energy associated with molecular potential energy due to phase change |
| Temperature dependence | Strongly temperature-dependent | Temperature nearly constant during phase change |
| Detectability | Detectable by thermometer | Not detectable by temperature change |
| Example | Heating water from 20°C to 80°C | Melting ice at 0°C to water at 0°C |

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition (1 mark)
- Distinction (1 mark)
- Examples (1 mark)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **physical significance of internal energy**, listing all microscopic modes of energy storage. Discuss why internal energy of an ideal gas depends **only on temperature**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **internal energy of a monatomic ideal gas** using the kinetic theory of gases. Hence calculate the change in internal energy when 2 kg of helium (monatomic, $R = 2.077$ kJ/kgK) is heated from 300 K to 600 K. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A:

**Part (a) — Physical Significance (7 marks):**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
Internal energy $U$ is the total energy stored within a system at the microscopic level. It does not include the macroscopic kinetic energy (due to motion of the system as a whole) or macroscopic potential energy (due to its position in a gravitational field).

**Modes of Energy Storage (3 marks):**

| Mode | Type | Description |
|------|------|-------------|
| Translational KE | Kinetic | Movement of molecule's centre of mass in 3D space |
| Rotational KE | Kinetic | Rotation about axes through centre of mass |
| Vibrational KE | Kinetic | Oscillation of bonded atoms within molecule |
| Vibrational PE | Potential | Elastic deformation energy of bonds |
| Intermolecular PE | Potential | Forces between molecules (van der Waals) |
| Chemical Bond PE | Potential | Energy stored in chemical bonds |
| Nuclear PE | Potential | Energy within the atomic nucleus |

**Ideal Gas Argument (2 marks):**
For an ideal gas, by definition:
- Molecules are point masses → no vibrational modes
- No intermolecular forces → $U_{inter} = 0$
- Therefore, **U depends only on kinetic energy**, which is a function of **temperature alone**.

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    m1[Internal Energy U] --> m2[Microscopic KE]
    m1 --> m3[Microscopic PE]
    m2 --> m4[Translation]
    m2 --> m5[Rotation]
    m2 --> m6[Vibration]
    m3 --> m7[Intermolecular]
    m3 --> m8[Chemical]
    m3 --> m9[Nuclear]
    m4 --> m10[T dependent]
    m5 --> m10
    m6 --> m10
    m7 --> m11[Negligible for Ideal Gas]
```

---

**Part (b) — Derivation & Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Step 1: For N molecules of mass $m_0$, total mass $M = N m_0$.

$$\overline{\text{KE}}_{per \, molecule} = \frac{1}{2} m_0 (u_x^2 + u_y^2 + u_z^2)$$

Step 2: By isotropy:

$$\overline{u_x^2} = \overline{u_y^2} = \overline{u_z^2} = \frac{1}{3} \overline{c}^2$$
