Both are mathematically equal, but KTU board expects the **latter form**. Using the wrong form with the wrong sign of the answer loses full marks.
>
> 2. **Applying the isothermal formula with $n = 1$ in the polytropic formula (3 marks lost):** Plugging $n = 1$ into $W_b = \dfrac{P_1V_1 - P_2V_2}{n-1}$ gives $\frac{0}{0}$ — a complete nonsense answer. The student **must** recognize the $n = 1$ special case and switch to the logarithmic form.
>
> 3. **Unit mismatch (1–2 marks lost):** Mixing $P$ in Pa with $V$ in m³ and forgetting to convert to kJ. Always check: $1 \text{ kPa} \cdot \text{m}^3 = 1 \text{ kJ}$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to verify $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$ (1 mark lost):** KTU expects students to *state* the polytropic identity as a checkpoint before computing the work.
>
> 5. **Confusing per-unit-mass $w$ with total $W$:** If mass $m$ is given, the final answer must be multiplied by $m$. If not, examiners may deduct for "incomplete solution."

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**
>
> - **Core equation:** $PV^n = C$ defines a polytropic process; $n$ is the polytropic index.
> - **Master formula (non-isothermal):** $W_b = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1}$ — applies to all $n \neq 1$.
> - **Isothermal exception:** $W_b = P_1 V_1 \ln(V_2/V_1)$ for $n = 1$.
> - **Isobaric (n = 0):** $W_b = P(V_2 - V_1)$.
> - **Isentropic (n = k):** $W_b = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{k - 1}$.
> - **Isochoric (n = ∞):** $W_b = 0$.
> - **Per-unit-mass variant (ideal gas):** $w_b = \dfrac{R(T_1 - T_2)}{n - 1}$ — very useful in compressor/cycle problems.
> - **Sign convention:** KTU uses **work done BY system = POSITIVE**. Compression gives $W_b < 0$.
> - **State identity (always true):** $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$ — use it to find missing state variables before computing $W_b$.
> - **Quick diagnostic of process type:** isobaric if $P$ constant, isothermal if $T$ constant, isentropic if $s$ constant, isochoric if $V$ constant — otherwise polytropic with empirical $n$.
> - **Unit safety:** $1 \text{ kPa·m}^3 = 1 \text{ kJ}$; $1 \text{ bar·m}^3 = 100 \text{ kJ}$.
> - **Engineering relevance:** Reciprocating compressors, IC engines, pneumatic systems, refrigeration cycles — all modeled using polytropic relations.
> - **P-V diagram interpretation:** The P-V curve becomes *steeper* as $n$ increases from 0 to $\infty$. Area under the curve = boundary work.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Balance for Closed Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Balance for Closed Systems**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **closed system** (also known as a *control mass*) is a region in space with a fixed identity and a fixed amount of matter, across whose boundary only **energy** (heat and work) can be transferred, but no mass transfer occurs. The **Energy Balance for a Closed System** is the mathematical statement of the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to such a system, expressing the conservation of energy during a thermodynamic process.

For a closed system undergoing a thermodynamic cycle, the classical statement is:

> *"When a closed system undergoes a thermodynamic cycle, the net heat transfer into the system is equal to the net work transfer out of the system."*

For a change of state between two equilibrium states, the First Law is written as:

$$Q - W = \Delta U$$

where $Q$ is the net heat transfer **into** the system, $W$ is the net work transfer **out of** the system, and $\Delta U$ is the change in total internal energy of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The energy balance for closed systems is the foundational pillar for analyzing reciprocating compressors, IC engines, pressurized tanks, piston-cylinder assemblies, and all non-flow processes in the KTU thermodynamics curriculum.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **sealed pressure cooker on a stove**:

- The lid is tightly closed → **no mass enters or leaves** (it is a *closed system*).
- You supply heat from the stove (flame) → this is **heat transfer, $Q$** (energy entering).
- The steam pushes the safety valve or the piston up → this is **work done, $W$** (energy leaving).
- The temperature and pressure of the trapped gas rise → this means the **internal energy, $U$**, of the gas has increased.

So whatever heat you put in that doesn't leave as work must remain stored inside the gas as microscopic kinetic and potential energy of its molecules. The energy is simply *redistributed* — it is never created or destroyed. This is the essence of the First Law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Open Systems:** In *closed* systems, mass is fixed and energy crosses the boundary. In *open* systems (control volume), both mass and energy can cross the boundary. The present module focuses exclusively on the **closed (non-flow) system**.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Reference Data

For all KTU calculations on this topic, the following standard values are used:

- **Universal gas constant:** $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K)
- **Mechanical equivalent of heat:** $J = 1$ (in SI units, heat and work have identical units of kJ or J)
- **Standard temperature:** $T_0 = 298.15$ K (25 °C)
- **Standard pressure:** $p_0 = 101.325$ kPa
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81$ m/s²

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

The closed-system energy balance is built upon the following logical chain:

- **Step 1 — System Identification:** A fixed mass of substance is isolated as the system. The boundary may be real (piston wall) or imaginary, but it is impermeable to mass flow.
- **Step 2 — Energy Inventory:** The total energy $E$ of a closed system includes internal energy $U$, kinetic energy $KE$, and potential energy $PE$:
$$E = U + KE + PE = U + \frac{1}{2}mc^2 + mgz$$
- **Step 3 — Energy Crossing the Boundary:** Energy crosses the boundary in two forms only:
  - **Heat, $Q$:** energy transfer due to temperature difference.
  - **Work, $W$:** energy transfer due to all other driving forces (pressure, electrical, shaft, etc.).
- **Step 4 — Conservation Statement:** The net energy transferred into the system equals the change in its total energy.
- **Step 5 — Sign Convention:** Adopt the **thermodynamic sign convention** — *energy entering the system is positive, energy leaving is negative*. Some textbooks use the alternative *work-out-positive* convention; KTU 2024 follows the **classical convention** (work done *by* the system is positive, $W > 0$).

## 2.2 Expanded Forms of the First Law

**For a closed system undergoing a cycle (cyclic process):**

$$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$$

**For a closed system undergoing a change of state (process 1 → 2):**

$$Q_{1-2} - W_{1-2} = \Delta U = m(u_2 - u_1)$$

Including macroscopic energies (kinetic and potential):

$$Q - W = (U_2 - U_1) + \frac{m}{2}(c_2^2 - c_1^2) + mg(z_2 - z_1)$$

On a **rate (per unit time) basis** for a closed system:

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \frac{dU_{cv}}{dt} = m\frac{du}{dt}$$

**Differential (instantaneous) form:**

$$\delta Q - \delta W = dU$$

Note that $\delta Q$ and $\delta W$ are *inexact differentials* (path-dependent), while $dU$ is an *exact differential* (point function, path-independent).

## 2.3 Enthalpy — A Companion Property

For closed systems involving constant-pressure processes, the **enthalpy** $H$ is defined as:

$$H = U + pV$$

The change in enthalpy is:

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(pV)$$

For a constant-pressure process in a closed system, the First Law reduces to:

$$Q_p = \Delta H = m(h_2 - h_1)$$

## 2.4 Work Transfer Modes in a Closed System

The total work $W$ in a closed system is the algebraic sum of all work modes:

$$W_{net} = W_{boundary} + W_{shaft} + W_{electrical} + W_{unsteady} + W_{other}$$

For most KTU problems, only **boundary work** (expansion/compression work) and **shaft work** are relevant. The **unsteady flow work** term does not apply to a true closed system (it is an open-system term that cancels out in steady-flow).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shaft Work vs. Boundary Work:** Shaft work ($W_{sh}$) crosses the boundary via a rotating shaft and is path-independent in its net effect for a cycle. Boundary work ($W_b$) is the PdV work done by the expanding/compressing fluid and is path-dependent (its magnitude depends on the process path on a p-V diagram).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) |
|---|---------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | $Q - W = \Delta U$ | First Law — closed system, classical sign convention | kJ |
| 2 | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | First Law rearranged | kJ |
| 3 | $\Delta U = m(u_2 - u_1) = mc_v(T_2 - T_1)$ | Internal energy change (ideal gas) | kJ |
| 4 | $\Delta H = m(h_2 - h_1) = mc_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | Enthalpy change (ideal gas) | kJ |
| 5 | $H = U + pV$ | Definition of enthalpy | kJ |
| 6 | $W_b = \int_1^2 p\,dV$ | Boundary (displacement) work | kJ |
| 7 | $W_{b,iso} = p_0(V_2 - V_1)$ | Boundary work — constant pressure | kJ |
| 8 | $W_b = \frac{p_1V_1 - p_2V_2}{n-1}$ | Boundary work — polytropic | kJ |
| 9 | $W_{b,iso} = mRT\ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$ | Boundary work — isothermal (ideal gas) | kJ |
| 10 | $Q_v = \Delta U = mc_v(T_2 - T_1)$ | Constant-volume (isochoric) heat | kJ |
| 11 | $Q_p = \Delta H = mc_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | Constant-pressure heat | kJ |
| 12 | $c_p - c_v = R$ | Mayer's relation (specific, per unit mass) | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 13 | $\gamma = c_p / c_v$ | Specific heat ratio (adiabatic index) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $W_{b,adia} = \frac{p_1V_1 - p_2V_2}{\gamma - 1}$ | Boundary work — adiabatic (ideal gas) | kJ |
| 15 | $Q = \Delta U + W_{b} + W_{sh}$ | Energy balance with shaft work | kJ |
| 16 | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = dU/dt$ | Energy balance on rate basis | kW |
| 17 | $w = q - \Delta u$ | First law on per-unit-mass basis | kJ/kg |
| 18 | $pV = mRT$ | Ideal gas equation of state | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The closed-system energy balance is the analytical tool for the **indicator diagram analysis** of reciprocating engines and compressors, **non-flow calorimetry** (bomb calorimeter), and **batch reactor** thermal design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the First Law for a Closed System (from Classical Mechanics)

We derive the First Law by considering energy conservation for a closed system containing a pure substance.

**Step 1 — Define total energy of the system.**

The total energy $E$ of a closed system at any instant is the sum of internal, kinetic, and potential energies:

$$E = U + KE + PE$$

For a system of mass $m$ moving with velocity $c$ at elevation $z$:

$$E = U + \frac{1}{2}mc^2 + mgz$$

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of energy.**

The change in total energy between state 1 and state 2 must equal the net energy transferred across the boundary:

$$E_2 - E_1 = Q_{in} - W_{out}$$

Substituting the energy inventory:

$$\left(U_2 + \frac{1}{2}mc_2^2 + mgz_2\right) - \left(U_1 + \frac{1}{2}mc_1^2 + mgz_1\right) = Q - W$$

**Step 3 — Simplify for stationary system (no KE, no PE change).**

For the most common KTU problem (system at rest, negligible elevation change):

$$U_2 - U_1 = Q - W$$

$$\boxed{\,Q - W = \Delta U\,}$$

This is the foundational First Law relation for a closed system. Every derivation that follows is a special case of this identity.

**Step 4 — Derive on a per-unit-mass basis.**

Dividing both sides by the mass $m$ of the system:

$$\frac{Q}{m} - \frac{W}{m} = \frac{\Delta U}{m} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; q - w = \Delta u = u_2 - u_1$$

This is the **specific energy balance** — invaluable for table-based steam table and ideal-gas table lookups.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Boundary Work Integral

Consider a fluid contained in a piston-cylinder assembly. The piston has a cross-sectional area $A$ and the fluid exerts a uniform pressure $p$ on the piston face.

**Step 1 — Elementary force on piston.**

The force exerted by the fluid on the piston face is:

$$F = p \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Elementary displacement.**

If the piston moves outward by an infinitesimal distance $ds$, the volume of the fluid increases by:

$$dV = A \cdot ds$$

**Step 3 — Elementary boundary work.**

The elementary work done *by* the system on the piston (and thus on the surroundings) is:

$$\delta W_b = F \cdot ds = pA\,ds = p\,dV$$

**Step 4 — Total boundary work for finite process.**

Integrating from initial state 1 to final state 2:

$$W_b = \int_1^2 \delta W_b = \int_1^2 p\,dV$$

**Step 5 — Geometrical interpretation.**

The boundary work $W_b$ is the area under the process curve on a $p$–$V$ diagram, bounded by the process path and the volume axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Path Dependence:** Since $p\,dV$ is an inexact differential, $W_b$ depends on *how* the process is carried out, not just on the end states. This is why work is **not a property** of a system.

## 3.3 Derivation of Polytropic Boundary Work

A **polytropic process** is defined by the relation:

$$pV^n = \text{constant} = C$$

where $n$ is the polytropic index. The major ideal-gas processes are special cases:

- **Isobaric:** $n = 0$
- **Isothermal:** $n = 1$
- **Isentropic (reversible adiabatic):** $n = k = \gamma$
- **Isochoric:** $n = \infty$

**Step 1 — Express $p$ as a function of $V$.**

From $pV^n = C$:

$$p = \frac{C}{V^n} = \frac{p_1V_1^n}{V^n} = \frac{p_2V_2^n}{V^n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the boundary work integral.**

$$W_b = \int_1^2 p\,dV = \int_1^2 \frac{C}{V^n}\,dV = C\int_1^2 V^{-n}\,dV$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral (valid for $n \ne 1$).**

$$W_b = C \left[\frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n}\right]_1^2 = \frac{C(V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n})}{1-n}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back $C = p_1V_1^n = p_2V_2^n$.**

$$W_b = \frac{p_2V_2^n \cdot V_2^{1-n} - p_1V_1^n \cdot V_1^{1-n}}{1-n} = \frac{p_2V_2 - p_1V_1}{1-n}$$

**Step 5 — Final formula (with negative sign reversal for $n > 1$).**

$$\boxed{\,W_b = \frac{p_1V_1 - p_2V_2}{n - 1}\,} \quad \text{for } n \ne 1$$

**Step 6 — Special case: Isothermal process ($n = 1$).**

For an ideal gas, $pV = mRT = \text{const}$, so:

$$W_{b,iso} = \int_1^2 \frac{mRT}{V}\,dV = mRT\int_1^2 \frac{dV}{V} = mRT\ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

Equivalently, using ideal gas law to convert the volume ratio to a pressure ratio:

$$W_{b,iso} = mRT\ln\left(\frac{p_1}{p_2}\right)$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Internal Energy and Enthalpy for an Ideal Gas

For an ideal gas, internal energy is a function of temperature only (**Joule's Law**):

$$u = u(T) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; du = c_v\,dT$$

Therefore, for a process between two states:

$$\Delta u = u_2 - u_1 = \int_1^2 c_v\,dT$$

**Step 1 — For constant $c_v$ (most KTU problems):**

$$\Delta u = c_v(T_2 - T_1) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \Delta U = m c_v(T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 2 — Enthalpy derivation.**

Starting from the definition $H = U + pV$ and using $pV = mRT$ for an ideal gas:

$$h = u + \frac{pV}{m} = u + RT$$

Differentiating:

$$dh = du + R\,dT = c_v\,dT + R\,dT = (c_v + R)\,dT$$

Defining $c_p = c_v + R$ (Mayer's relation):

$$dh = c_p\,dT \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \Delta h = c_p(T_2 - T_1)$$

$$\Delta H = m c_p(T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 3 — Mayer's relation derivation.**

From $h = u + RT$ and $dh = c_p dT$, $du = c_v dT$:

$$c_p dT = c_v dT + R\,dT$$

$$\boxed{\,c_p - c_v = R\,}$$

On a molar basis: $\bar{c}_p - \bar{c}_v = R_u = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K).

## 3.5 Derivation of Constant-Volume and Constant-Pressure Specific Heats

**Constant-volume (isochoric) process** in a closed system: $V = \text{const}$, so $dV = 0$, thus $W_b = 0$. The First Law becomes:

$$Q_v = \Delta U = m c_v(T_2 - T_1) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; c_v = \frac{Q_v}{m(T_2 - T_1)}$$

**Constant-pressure (isobaric) process** in a closed system: $p = \text{const}$. The First Law gives:

$$Q_p = \Delta U + W_b = \Delta U + p\Delta V = (U_2 + p_2V_2) - (U_1 + p_1V_1) = \Delta H$$

$$Q_p = \Delta H = m c_p(T_2 - T_1) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; c_p = \frac{Q_p}{m(T_2 - T_1)}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Energy Balance for Each Ideal-Gas Process

| Process | Condition | Work $W_b$ | Heat $Q$ | $\Delta U$ | $\Delta H$ |
|---------|-----------|------------|----------|------------|------------|
| **Isochoric** | $V = \text{const}$ | $0$ | $m c_v \Delta T$ | $m c_v \Delta T$ | $m c_p \Delta T$ |
| **Isobaric** | $p = \text{const}$ | $p \Delta V = mR\Delta T$ | $m c_p \Delta T$ | $m c_v \Delta T$ | $m c_p \Delta T$ |
| **Isothermal (ideal gas)** | $T = \text{const}, \Delta U = 0$ | $mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | $W_b$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| **Adiabatic (reversible)** | $Q = 0$ | $\frac{p_1V_1 - p_2V_2}{\gamma - 1}$ | $0$ | $-W_b$ | $\gamma \cdot \Delta U$ |
| **Polytropic** | $pV^n = C$ | $\frac{p_1V_1 - p_2V_2}{n - 1}$ | $m c_n (T_2 - T_1)$ | $m c_v \Delta T$ | $m c_p \Delta T$ |
| **Cyclic** | Returns to state 1 | $W = Q$ | $W$ | $0$ | $0$ |

For the polytropic process, the polytropic specific heat is:

$$c_n = c_v \cdot \frac{n - \gamma}{n - 1}$$

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (Detailed)

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A piston-cylinder assembly contains 0.05 m³ of air at 200 kPa and 27 °C. The air is compressed polytropically with $n = 1.3$ to a final volume of 0.02 m³. Determine: (i) final pressure and temperature, (ii) boundary work, (iii) heat transfer, and (iv) change in internal energy. Take $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.4$.

**Step 1 — Mass of air.**

$$m = \frac{p_1V_1}{RT_1} = \frac{200 \times 0.05}{0.287 \times 300} = \frac{10}{86.1} = 0.1161 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Final pressure using $p_1V_1^n = p_2V_2^n$.**

$$p_2 = p_1 \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^n = 200 \times \left(\frac{0.05}{0.02}\right)^{1.3}$$

$$\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right) = 2.5 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 2.5^{1.3} = e^{1.3 \ln 2.5} = e^{1.3 \times 0.9163} = e^{1.1912} = 3.291$$

$$p_2 = 200 \times 3.291 = 658.2 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Final temperature using ideal gas law.**

$$T_2 = \frac{p_2V_2}{mR} = \frac{658.2 \times 0.02}{0.1161 \times 0.287} = \frac{13.164}{0.03332} = 395.1 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Boundary work (polytropic).**

$$W_b = \frac{p_1V_1 - p_2V_2}{n - 1} = \frac{200 \times 0.05 - 658.2 \times 0.02}{1.3 - 1} = \frac{10 - 13.164}{0.3} = \frac{-3.164}{0.3} = -10.55 \text{ kJ}$$

The negative sign indicates work is done **on** the system (compression).

**Step 5 — Change in internal energy.**

$$\Delta U = m c_v (T_2 - T_1) = 0.1161 \times 0.718 \times (395.1 - 300) = 0.1161 \times 0.718 \times 95.1 = 7.93 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 6 — Heat transfer from First Law.**

$$Q = \Delta U + W_b = 7.93 + (-10.55) = -2.62 \text{ kJ}$$

The negative sign indicates heat is rejected **from** the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of physical sense:** During compression, $W_b < 0$ (work input), $\Delta U > 0$ (temperature rises), and $Q < 0$ (heat rejected). The First Law is satisfied, confirming our energy balance.

## 4.1 Closed System Energy Balance — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the energy flow architecture of a closed system undergoing a thermodynamic process, showing how heat, work, and internal energy interact according to the First Law.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Heat Source Q_in] -->|Heat Energy| B(Closed System Boundary)
    C[External Work Sink W_out] -->|Work Energy Out| B
    B -->|Energy Conservation| D[Net Energy Accumulation]
    D --> E1[Change in Internal Energy dU]
    D --> E2[Change in Kinetic Energy dKE]
    D --> E3[Change in Potential Energy dPE]
    F[Surroundings at T_surr] -->|Thermal Interaction| A
    G[Piston or Shaft] -->|Mechanical Interaction| C
    H[State 1: p1, V1, T1] -->|Process Path| I[State 2: p2, V2, T2]
    I -->|Heat Rejection Q_out| J[External Heat Sink]
    C -->|Work Output W_b| K[Surroundings]
    B -->|No Mass Flow| L[Fixed Mass m]
    L --> M1[Specific Internal Energy u]
    L --> M2[Specific Enthalpy h]
    L --> M3[Specific Volume v]
```

## 4.2 First Law Application Decision Tree

The following diagram guides the student in selecting the correct formula branch based on the process type.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Closed System Energy Balance Q minus W equals dU] --> B{Process Type}
    B -->|Constant Volume| C[W_b equals 0]
    C --> C1[Q equals m cv times delta T]
    C --> C2[delta U equals m cv times delta T]
    B -->|Constant Pressure| D[W_b equals p times delta V]
    D --> D1[Q equals m cp times delta T]
    D --> D2[delta H equals m cp times delta T]
    B -->|Isothermal Ideal Gas| E[delta U equals 0]
    E --> E1[Q equals W equals mRT times ln of V2 over V1]
    B -->|Reversible Adiabatic| F[Q equals 0]
    F --> F1[W equals minus delta U]
    F --> F2[pV to the power gamma equals constant]
    B -->|Polytropic| G[pV to the power n equals constant]
    G --> G1[W equals p1V1 minus p2V2 divided by n minus 1]
    G --> G2[Q equals delta U plus W_b]
    B -->|Cyclic Process| H[delta U equals 0]
    H --> H1[Q_net equals W_net]
```

The following diagram represents the physical realization of a closed system used in KTU laboratory and exam problems.


The arrows depict the *direction* of energy flow in accordance with the thermodynamic sign convention. The closed boundary around the trapped gas mass $m$ emphasizes the defining feature of a closed system — **no mass crosses the boundary**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q:** State the First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system undergoing a cycle. How does it differ for a change of state?

**Model Answer:**

The First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system undergoing a thermodynamic cycle states that the net heat transfer into the system equals the net work transfer out of the system:

$$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$$

This statement, originally formulated by **Clausius** and **Kelvin-Planck**, reflects the equivalence of heat and work as forms of energy in transit.

For a **change of state** (process between two distinct equilibrium states 1 and 2), the First Law is written as:

$$Q_{1-2} - W_{1-2} = \Delta U = m(u_2 - u_1)$$

The cyclic form is recovered by summing the change-of-state First Law over a complete cycle, noting that $\Delta U = 0$ for a cycle (since $U$ is a point function and the system returns to its initial state). The key difference: the change-of-state form involves a *property change* ($\Delta U$), while the cyclic form involves only *energy transfers* (heat and work).

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q:** Define boundary work. Why is work a path function and internal energy a point function?

**Model Answer:**

**Boundary work** $W_b$ is the work done by a system due to the expansion or compression of its boundary, evaluated as:

$$W_b = \int_1^2 p\,dV$$

In a piston-cylinder assembly, $p$ is the pressure at the moving boundary and $dV$ is the elemental change in system volume.

**Work is a path function** because $\delta W = p\,dV$ is an *inexact differential*. Its value depends on the specific process path traversed on the $p$–$V$ diagram (the area under the curve), not merely on the end states. For example, the boundary work in going from $(p_1, V_1)$ to $(p_2, V_2)$ is different for an isothermal path versus a polytropic path.

**Internal energy is a point (state) function** because $dU = \delta Q - \delta W$ is the combination of two inexact differentials that yields an *exact differential*. The change $\Delta U = U_2 - U_1$ depends only on the initial and final states, independent of the path. The cyclic integral $\oint dU = 0$ rigorously confirms this.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

A rigid tank contains 5 kg of air initially at 300 K and 200 kPa. The air is heated at constant volume until the pressure becomes 500 kPa. It then expands polytropically with $n = 1.25$ to a final pressure of 300 kPa. Determine:

**(a)** (7 Marks) The total heat transfer for the entire process, the total boundary work done, and the total change in internal energy.

**(b)** (7 Marks) Sketch the process on a $p$–$V$ diagram and verify the First Law for the combined process.

**Given:** $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.4$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a)**

**Step 1 — Initial state properties.**

State 1: $T_1 = 300$ K, $p_1 = 200$ kPa, $m = 5$ kg.

$$V_1 = \frac{mRT_1}{p_1} = \frac{5 \times 0.287 \times 300}{200} = \frac{430.5}{200} = 2.1525 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — State 2 (end of constant-volume heating).**

$V_2 = V_1 = 2.1525$ m³, $p_2 = 500$ kPa.

$$T_2 = \frac{p_2V_2}{mR} = \frac{500 \times 2.1525}{5 \times 0.287} = \frac{1076.25}{1.435} = 750 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — State 3 (end of polytropic expansion).**

$p_3 = 300$ kPa, $n = 1.25$. Using $p_2V_2^n = p_3V_3^n$:

$$V_3 = V_2 \left(\frac{p_2}{p_3}\right)^{1/n} = 2.1525 \times \left(\frac{500}{300}\right)^{1/1.25} = 2.1525 \times (1.6667)^{0.8}$$

$$(1.6667)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \times \ln 1.6667} = e^{0.8 \times 0.5108} = e^{0.4087} = 1.505$$

$$V_3 = 2.1525 \times 1.505 = 3.239 \text{ m}^3$$

$$T_3 = \frac{p_3V_3}{mR} = \frac{300 \times 3.239}{5 \times 0.287} = \frac{971.7}{1.435} = 677.1 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Process 1→2 (constant volume): $W_{12} = 0$.**

$$Q_{12} = \Delta U_{12} = m c_v (T_2 - T_1) = 5 \times 0.718 \times (750 - 300) = 5 \times 0.718 \times 450 = 1615.5 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Process 2→3 (polytropic).**

Boundary work:

$$W_{23} = \frac{p_2V_2 - p_3V_3}{n - 1} = \frac{500 \times 2.1525 - 300 \times 3.239}{1.25 - 1} = \frac{1076.25 - 971.7}{0.25} = \frac{104.55}{0.25} = 418.2 \text{ kJ}$$

Change in internal energy:

$$\Delta U_{23} = m c_v (T_3 - T_2) = 5 \times 0.718 \times (677.1 - 750) = 5 \times 0.718 \times (-72.9) = -261.7 \text{ kJ}$$

Heat transfer:

$$Q_{23} = \Delta U_{23} + W_{23} = -261.7 + 418.2 = 156.5 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 6 — Totals for the combined process 1→2→3.**

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = Q_{12} + Q_{23} = 1615.5 + 156.5 = 1772.0 \text{ kJ}}$$

$$\boxed{W_{total} = W_{12} + W_{23} = 0 + 418.2 = 418.2 \text{ kJ}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta U_{total} = m c_v (T_3 - T_1) = 5 \times 0.718 \times (677.1 - 300) = 5 \times 0.718 \times 377.1 = 1353.8 \text{ kJ}}$$

#### **Model Solution for Part (b)**

**Step 1 — First Law verification.**

$$Q_{total} - W_{total} = 1772.0 - 418.2 = 1353.8 \text{ kJ} = \Delta U_{total} \;\;\checkmark$$

The First Law is satisfied, confirming energy conservation.

**Step 2 — p–V diagram description.**

On the $p$–$V$ diagram:
- State 1 at $(V_1 = 2.1525 \text{ m}^3,\; p_1 = 200 \text{ kPa})$.
- State 2 at $(V_2 = 2.1525 \text{ m}^3,\; p_2 = 500 \text{ kPa})$ — directly above State 1 (vertical line, isochoric).
- State 3 at $(V_3 = 3.239 \text{ m}^3,\; p_3 = 300 \text{ kPa})$ — to the right and below State 2 (polytropic curve with $n = 1.25$).
- The process traces a path going up vertically from 1 to 2, then curving down and right to 3.

The boundary work $W_{23} = 418.2$ kJ is the area under the polytropic curve from State 2 to State 3.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative]

A piston-cylinder assembly contains 0.3 m³ of nitrogen gas (ideal gas) initially at 100 kPa and 27 °C. The gas is compressed isothermally to one-fifth of its initial volume. It is then heated at constant volume to a final pressure of 600 kPa.

**(a)** (7 Marks) Determine the work done, heat transfer, and change in internal energy for the combined process.

**(b)** (7 Marks) If the entire process were carried out as a single reversible adiabatic (isentropic) compression from State 1 to the same final pressure of 600 kPa, calculate the final temperature, volume, and the work required. Comment on the comparison.

**Given:** $R = 0.2968$ kJ/(kg·K) for nitrogen, $c_v = 0.743$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_p = 1.040$ kJ/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.4$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a)**

**Step 1 — Initial state.**

State 1: $p_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K, $V_1 = 0.3$ m³.

$$m = \frac{p_1V_1}{RT_1} = \frac{100 \times 0.3}{0.2968 \times 300} = \frac{30}{89.04} = 0.3369 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — State 2 (end of isothermal compression, $V_2 = V_1/5 = 0.06$ m³).**

$$T_2 = T_1 = 300 \text{ K}, \quad p_2 = p_1(V_1/V_2) = 100 \times 5 = 500 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — State 3 (end of constant-volume heating, $p_3 = 600$ kPa).**

$$V_3 = V_2 = 0.06 \text{ m}^3, \quad T_3 = T_2 \times \frac{p_3}{p_2} = 300 \times \frac{600}{500} = 360 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Process 1→2 (isothermal).**

For an ideal gas isothermal process, $\Delta U_{12} = 0$.

$$W_{12} = mRT_1 \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = 0.3369 \times 0.2968 \times 300 \times \ln(0.2)$$

$$= 0.3369 \times 89.04 \times (-1.6094) = 30.00 \times (-1.6094) = -48.28 \text{ kJ}$$

$$Q_{12} = W_{12} = -48.28 \text{ kJ} \quad (\text{heat rejected, since } Q_{12} < 0)$$

**Step 5 — Process 2→3 (constant volume).**

$$W_{23} = 0$$

$$Q_{23} = \Delta U_{23} = m c_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.3369 \times 0.743 \times (360 - 300) = 0.3369 \times 0.743 \times 60 = 15.02 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 6 — Totals for the combined process.**

$$\boxed{W_{total} = W_{12} + W_{23} = -48.28 + 0 = -48.28 \text{ kJ}}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = Q_{12} + Q_{23} = -48.28 + 15.02 = -33.26 \text{ kJ}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta U_{total} = m c_v (T_3 - T_1) = 0.3369 \times 0.743 \times (360 - 300) = 15.02 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Verification:** $Q_{total} - W_{total} = -33.26 - (-48.28) = 15.02 = \Delta U_{total}$ ✓

#### **Model Solution for Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Isentropic compression from State 1 to $p_2' = 600$ kPa.**

$$T_2' = T_1 \left(\frac{p_2'}{p_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} = 300 \times \left(\frac{600}{100}\right)^{0.4/1.4} = 300 \times (6)^{0.2857}$$

$$6^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times \ln 6} = e^{0.2857 \times 1.7918} = e^{0.5120} = 1.669$$

$$T_2' = 300 \times 1.669 = 500.7 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Final volume.**

$$V_2' = \frac{mRT_2'}{p_2'} = \frac{0.3369 \times 0.2968 \times 500.7}{600} = \frac{50.05}{600} = 0.0834 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Work of isentropic compression.**

$$W_{isentropic} = \frac{mR(T_1 - T_2')}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{0.3369 \times 0.2968 \times (300 - 500.7)}{0.4} = \frac{0.3369 \times 0.2968 \times (-200.7)}{0.4}$$

$$= \frac{-20.07}{0.4} = -50.17 \text{ kJ}$$

Equivalently, $Q = 0$ and $W = -\Delta U$:

$$W = -m c_v(T_2' - T_1) = -0.3369 \times 0.743 \times (500.7 - 300) = -0.3369 \times 0.743 \times 200.7 = -50.23 \text{ kJ}$$

(Minor difference due to rounding; the polytropic formula is exact for ideal gas.)

**Step 4 — Comparison.**

| Quantity | Two-step (1→2→3) | Single Isentropic (1→2') |
|----------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Final pressure | 600 kPa | 600 kPa |
| Final temperature | 360 K | 500.7 K |
| Final volume | 0.06 m³ | 0.0834 m³ |
| Work | −48.28 kJ | −50.17 kJ |

**Comment:** The isentropic compression requires **more work** (|−50.17| > |−48.28|) and produces a higher final temperature (500.7 K vs 360 K) and a larger final volume. This is because the isentropic path goes to a higher temperature before reaching the same pressure, demanding more compression work. In the two-step process, the isothermal step allows the gas to reject heat during compression (reducing the work input), and the constant-volume heating step raises the pressure without changing volume — a thermodynamically more efficient pathway for the same final pressure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Sign convention error:** Mixing up work-in vs work-out positive conventions. Always state the convention adopted at the start of your solution.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Gas constant $R$ for nitrogen in kJ/(kg·K) requires $T$ in Kelvin, $V$ in m³, $p$ in kPa. Mixing °C with K is the single most common mistake.
> 3. **Mayer's relation misuse:** Using $c_p - c_v = R$ with the universal gas constant $R_u$ instead of the specific gas constant $R$. Remember: $c_p - c_v = R$ (per unit mass), $\bar{c}_p - \bar{c}_v = R_u$ (per unit mole).
> 4. **Polytropic work formula validity:** The formula $W_b = (p_1V_1 - p_2V_2)/(n-1)$ is **not valid for $n = 1$** (isothermal case). Always state the assumption $n \ne 1$ and use the logarithmic formula for isothermal.
> 5. **Internal energy change requires $\Delta T$, not $\Delta p$ or $\Delta V$:** Many students attempt to compute $\Delta U$ from pressure or volume changes — for an ideal gas, only temperature matters.
> 6. **First Law check:** Always verify $Q - W = \Delta U$ at the end of the solution. Examiners award partial credit for this self-check step.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Energy Balance for Closed Systems**

- **Closed system** = fixed mass; only energy (heat and work) crosses the boundary; **no mass flow**.
- **First Law (cycle):** $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$.
- **First Law (process 1→2):** $Q - W = \Delta U = m(u_2 - u_1)$.
- **Including KE and PE:** $Q - W = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$.
- **Sign convention (KTU classical):** *Work done by the system is positive.* $W > 0$ for expansion.
- **Boundary work:** $W_b = \int_1^2 p\,dV$ = area under process curve on $p$–$V$ diagram.
- **Polytropic work ($n \ne 1$):** $W_b = (p_1V_1 - p_2V_2)/(n-1)$.
- **Isothermal work (ideal gas):** $W_b = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1) = mRT \ln(p_1/p_2)$.
- **Adiabatic (isentropic) work (ideal gas):** $W_b = (p_1V_1 - p_2V_2)/(\gamma - 1) = -mc_v(T_2 - T_1)$.
- **Constant-volume heat:** $Q_v = \Delta U = mc_v(T_2 - T_1)$.
- **Constant-pressure heat:** $Q_p = \Delta H = mc_p(T_2 - T_1)$.
- **Enthalpy definition:** $H = U + pV$; for ideal gas $h = u + RT$.
- **Mayer's relation:** $c_p - c_v = R$ (specific); $\bar{c}_p - \bar{c}_v = R_u$ (molar).
- **Specific heat ratio:** $\gamma = c_p / c_v$.
- **Ideal gas internal energy:** $u = u(T)$ only; $\Delta u = c_v \Delta T$.
- **Ideal gas enthalpy:** $h = h(T)$ only; $\Delta h = c_p \Delta T$.
- **Polytropic specific heat:** $c_n = c_v (n - \gamma)/(n - 1)$.
- **$W_b$ is path-dependent** (not a property); $U$ and $H$ are point functions (properties).
- **Enthalpy is useful for constant-pressure** closed-system processes: $Q_p = \Delta H$.
- **For a cyclic process:** $\Delta U = \Delta H = 0$; $Q_{net} = W_{net}$.
- **Special cases of polytropic index:** $n = 0$ (isobaric), $n = 1$ (isothermal), $n = \gamma$ (isentropic), $n = \infty$ (isochoric).
- **Always verify** $Q - W = \Delta U$ as a self-check in every KTU problem.
- **Always convert temperatures to Kelvin** before substituting into any ideal-gas formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enthalpy and Specific Heats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Enthalpy and Specific Heats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Enthalpy — Formal Thermodynamic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Conformance)**
> **Enthalpy ($H$)** is an extensive thermodynamic property defined as the sum of the internal energy ($U$) of a system and the product of its pressure ($P$) and volume ($V$):
> $$H = U + PV$$

Mathematically, for a system of mass $m$:

$$H = U + PV \quad \text{[Joules, J]}$$

For a unit mass (specific enthalpy), the corresponding expression becomes:

$$h = u + Pv \quad \text{[J/kg]}$$

where:
- $u$ = specific internal energy (J/kg)
- $v$ = specific volume (m³/kg)
- $P$ = absolute pressure (Pa or N/m²)
- $h$ = specific enthalpy (J/kg)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** Enthalpy is a *point function* and a *property of state*. It is an *extensive property* (depends on mass) — $h$ is its intensive counterpart. You **cannot** measure enthalpy directly; only enthalpy *changes* ($\Delta H$) are physically meaningful in energy balances.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: Enthalpy as "Total Energy in a Stream"

**Plain-English Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**

Imagine a fluid flowing steadily through a pipe into a turbine. The fluid carries:
1. Its **internal energy** ($U$) — the microscopic kinetic and potential energy of its molecules.
2. The **flow work** ($PV$) — the energy required to *push* the fluid into the system against the prevailing pressure (think of it as the work done by the upstream fluid to make room for itself).

**Enthalpy is simply the total energy the fluid "carries" with it as it crosses a control volume boundary.** Engineers do not have to calculate $U$ and $PV$ separately for open systems; they just use $h$ directly. This is exactly why enthalpy is the *primary* energy variable in compressor, nozzle, turbine, and heat exchanger analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **Concept Anchor:** Every time you see a *flow work* term in an open-system energy balance, it has been *absorbed* into enthalpy. This is the genius of the $H = U + PV$ definition.

### 1.3 Specific Heats — Formal Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Heat** is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a unit mass of a substance by one degree (Kelvin or °C, since the *interval* is identical).

**Two principal specific heats are defined:**

**(a) Specific Heat at Constant Volume ($C_v$)**

$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial q}{\partial T}\right)_v = \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_v \quad \text{[kJ/kg·K]}$$

**Physical Meaning:** The energy required to raise the temperature of 1 kg of a substance by 1 K while keeping its volume constant. For a closed, rigid container, all heat added goes into raising internal energy.

**(b) Specific Heat at Constant Pressure ($C_p$)**

$$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial q}{\partial T}\right)_p = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_p \quad \text{[kJ/kg·K]}$$

**Physical Meaning:** The energy required to raise the temperature of 1 kg by 1 K at constant pressure. In a constant-pressure process, some heat does expansion work; hence $C_p > C_v$ always.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Value (Air at 300 K):**
> For air treated as an ideal gas at room temperature, $C_p = 1.005$ **kJ/kg·K** and $C_v = 0.718$ **kJ/kg·K**. The universal gas constant $R = 0.287$ **kJ/kg·K** for air. These values are *board-exam favorites* in KTU numerical problems.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy: $C_p$ vs $C_v$ — The "Stretching Balloon"

Imagine heating a gas:
- **In a rigid container (constant $V$):** All the heat energy goes into speeding up molecules → temperature rises *efficiently*. You need less heat for a given $\Delta T$.
- **In a balloon open to atmosphere (constant $P$):** As the gas heats, it *expands* and does work pushing the atmosphere back. Some heat energy is "wasted" doing this expansion work, so you need *more* heat for the same $\Delta T$.

**Conclusion:** $C_p > C_v$ — the "extra" energy in $C_p$ is precisely the flow work that performs expansion against the surroundings.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Why Enthalpy is Indispensable in Engineering

The First Law for an **open system (steady flow)** is:

$$q - w = \Delta h + \frac{\Delta V^2}{2} + g\Delta z$$

For a **closed system (non-flow)**:

$$q - w = \Delta u$$

In the open-system form, the $PV$ work (flow work) is *automatically* included in the $h$ term. This dramatically simplifies energy balances across turbines, compressors, boilers, condensers, and nozzles — every component in a power plant or refrigeration cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** In Module 2, you will repeatedly use $\Delta h = C_p \Delta T$ in steady-flow equipment. The trick is recognizing that *no separate $PV$ work calculation is needed* — it is baked into the property.

### 2.2 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Deriving Enthalpy from First Law (for closed system):**

Differentiating $H = U + PV$:

$$dH = dU + dV \cdot P + V \cdot dP$$

Substituting the First Law for a closed reversible system, $dU = dQ_{rev} - PdV$:

$$dH = dQ_{rev} - PdV + PdV + VdP = dQ_{rev} + VdP$$

**At constant pressure** ($dP = 0$):

$$(dH)_P = (dQ_{rev})_P \implies C_p = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_p$$

**Step 2 — Establishing Mayer's Relation:**

The Mayer Relation is the cornerstone connecting $C_p$, $C_v$, and $R$:

$$C_p - C_v = R \quad \text{(for ideal gases)}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio of Specific Heats ($\gamma$):**

$$\gamma = \frac{C_p}{C_v}$$

For air: $\gamma = 1.4$. For monoatomic gases (He, Ar): $\gamma \approx 1.67$. For triatomic gases (CO₂): $\gamma \approx 1.29$.

**Step 4 — Variable Specific Heats:**

For real engineering calculations over large temperature ranges, $C_p$ and $C_v$ are *not* constant. They are expressed as polynomial functions of temperature:

$$\frac{C_p}{R} = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3$$

$$\frac{\bar{C}_p}{R} = a + \frac{b}{2}T_1 + \frac{c}{3}T_1^2 + \frac{d}{4}T_1^3 + \cdots$$

where $a, b, c, d$ are empirical gas constants from standard thermodynamic tables.

**Step 5 — Specific Heats of Mixtures:**

For a mixture of ideal gases, the mixture specific heats are mass-weighted averages:

$$C_{p,\text{mix}} = \sum_{i} m_i C_{p,i} \quad ; \quad C_{p,\text{mix}} = \sum_i y_i \bar{C}_{p,i}$$

where $m_i$ is the mass fraction and $y_i$ is the mole fraction.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Where Enthalpy is Used |
|---|---|
| Steam Power Plants | Rankine cycle analysis — boiler, turbine, condenser enthalpies |
| Gas Turbines | Brayton cycle — compressor work $= C_p(T_2 - T_1)$ |
| Jet Engines | Nozzle exit velocity from enthalpy drop |
| Refrigeration | COP calculations using refrigerant enthalpies |
| Combustion | Adiabatic flame temperature using sensible enthalpy |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When a problem says "air enters a compressor at 300 K and exits at 600 K", the *first* equation you should write is $h_2 - h_1 = C_p(T_2 - T_1)$. Do not start with $Q - W = \Delta U$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 80% of KTU Module 2 questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = U + PV$ | Definition of total enthalpy | J |
| 2 | $h = u + Pv$ | Specific enthalpy | J/kg |
| 3 | $C_v = \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_v$ | Specific heat at constant volume | kJ/kg·K |
| 4 | $C_p = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_p$ | Specific heat at constant pressure | kJ/kg·K |
| 5 | $C_p - C_v = R$ | **Mayer's Relation** (ideal gas) | kJ/kg·K |
| 6 | $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ | Specific heat ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_p = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$ | $C_p$ in terms of $R$ and $\gamma$ | kJ/kg·K |
| 8 | $C_v = \frac{R}{\gamma - 1}$ | $C_v$ in terms of $R$ and $\gamma$ | kJ/kg·K |
| 9 | $R = C_p - C_v$ | Universal gas constant for ideal gas | kJ/kg·K |
| 10 | $dU = m C_v dT$ | Internal energy change (ideal gas) | J |
| 11 | $dH = m C_p dT$ | Enthalpy change (ideal gas) | J |
| 12 | $\Delta H = m C_{p,\text{avg}} \Delta T$ | Sensible enthalpy change | J |
| 13 | $C_{p,\text{mix}} = \sum m_i C_{p,i}$ | Mixture specific heat (mass basis) | kJ/kg·K |
| 14 | $C_{p,m,\text{mix}} = \sum y_i \bar{C}_{p,i}$ | Mixture molar specific heat | kJ/kmol·K |
| 15 | $\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = \bar{R} = 8.314$ | Molar Mayer's relation | kJ/kmol·K |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap in Tables:** When writing absolute values or magnitudes inside these formulas, KTU students frequently type `|x|` — this **breaks the markdown table**. Always use `\vert x \vert` or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX.

### 2.5 Physical Constants for Board Exam (Bold & Memorize)

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | $\bar{R}$ | **8.314** | kJ/kmol·K |
| Gas Constant for Air | $R_{\text{air}}$ | **0.287** | kJ/kg·K |
| $C_p$ for Air (300 K) | $C_{p,\text{air}}$ | **1.005** | kJ/kg·K |
| $C_v$ for Air (300 K) | $C_{v,\text{air}}$ | **0.718** | kJ/kg·K |
| $\gamma$ for Air | $\gamma_{\text{air}}$ | **1.4** | dimensionless |
| Molecular Weight of Air | $M_{\text{air}}$ | **28.97** | kg/kmol |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mayer's Relation ($C_p - C_v = R$)

**Starting Point:** Enthalpy definition for an ideal gas.

$$H = U + PV$$

**Step 1:** For a unit mass, divide by $m$:

$$h = u + Pv$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right) = \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right) + \left[\frac{\partial (Pv)}{\partial T}\right]$$

**Step 3:** For an ideal gas, $Pv = RT$. The product $Pv$ depends *only* on $T$:

$$\left[\frac{\partial (Pv)}{\partial T}\right] = \left[\frac{\partial (RT)}{\partial T}\right] = R$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the definitions of $C_p$ and $C_v$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_p = C_p \quad ; \quad \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_v = C_v$$

**Step 5:** Combine — note that for an ideal gas, $u$ and $h$ depend *only* on $T$ (Joule's Law), so the partial derivative constraint is dropped:

$$C_p = C_v + R$$

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R \quad \text{(Mayer's Relation)}}$$

**Step 6:** Dimensional Check:
- $C_p$: kJ/kg·K
- $C_v$: kJ/kg·K
- $R$: kJ/kg·K ✓
- All terms are energy per unit mass per unit temperature.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Enthalpy as a Function of $T$ and $P$ — Total Differential

**Starting Point:**

$$H = U + PV$$

**Step 1:** Take the total differential:

$$dH = dU + PdV + VdP$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the First Law for a closed system (reversible), $dU = dQ_{rev} - PdV$:

$$dH = dQ_{rev} - PdV + PdV + VdP$$

**Step 3:** The $PdV$ terms cancel:

$$dH = dQ_{rev} + VdP$$

**Step 4:** For a reversible process, $dQ_{rev} = TdS$. Substituting:

$$dH = TdS + VdP$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result — The Gibbs Fundamental Relation:**
> $$dH = TdS + VdP$$
> This is one of the four Maxwell-derived fundamental relations and is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU exams.

**Step 5:** This form shows that $H$ is naturally a function of $S$ and $P$:
$$H = H(S, P)$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: General $C_p$ and $C_v$ Relation (Real Gases)

**Starting Point:** For a real (non-ideal) gas, $C_p \neq C_v + R$. The general relation is:

$$C_p - C_v = T \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$$

**Step 1:** Begin from the entropy differential for a simple compressible system:

$$dS = \frac{C_v dT}{T} + \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$$

**Step 2:** At constant pressure, $dP = 0$:

$$dS = \frac{C_v dT}{T} + \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$$

**Step 3:** By cyclic relation, $\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P \cdot \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_v \cdot \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_T = -1$.

After applying the cyclic identity and Euler chain rule manipulation:

$$C_p - C_v = T \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$$

**Step 4:** For an ideal gas, $Pv = RT$, so:
$$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P} \quad ; \quad \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \frac{R}{v}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute:

$$C_p - C_v = T \cdot \frac{R}{P} \cdot \frac{R}{v} = T \cdot \frac{R}{Pv} \cdot R$$

Since $Pv = RT$:

$$C_p - C_v = T \cdot \frac{R}{RT} \cdot R = R$$

**Result confirmed:** Mayer's relation holds for ideal gases.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to Identify $C_p$ vs $C_v$ in Problems

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Framework for Choosing the Right Specific Heat:**

| Process Condition | Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Constant Volume (rigid tank) | $C_v$ | No boundary work; all $Q$ → $\Delta U$ |
| Constant Pressure (open flow, piston-cylinder) | $C_p$ | Flow work / expansion work is included |
| Closed, non-quasi-static | $C_v$ (for ideal gas, $T$-only) | Internal energy depends on $T$ only |
| Steady flow through device | $C_p$ (via $\Delta h$) | Enthalpy is the conserved quantity |
| Adiabatic (no $Q$) | $C_v$ for $\Delta U$, $C_p$ for $\Delta h$ | Depends on what you're calculating |
| Polytropic ($Pv^n = $ const) | Use relation with work | $\Delta U$ uses $C_v$; $\Delta H$ uses $C_p$ |

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A piston-cylinder device contains 0.5 kg of air at 300 kPa and 27 °C. The air is heated at constant pressure until its volume doubles. Calculate:
(a) The final temperature.
(b) The work done by the air.
(c) The change in internal energy.
(d) The change in enthalpy.

**Given:** $m = 0.5$ kg, $P_1 = 300$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K, $V_2 = 2V_1$.

**Assumptions:** Air as ideal gas with $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

**Solution (a) — Final Temperature:**

At constant pressure, $\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2}$:

$$\frac{V_1}{300} = \frac{2V_1}{T_2} \implies T_2 = 600 \text{ K}$$

**Solution (b) — Work Done:**

$$W = P(V_2 - V_1) = P \cdot V_1 = mRT_1$$

$$W = 0.5 \times 0.287 \times 300 = 43.05 \text{ kJ}$$

**Solution (c) — Change in Internal Energy:**

$$\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1) = 0.5 \times 0.718 \times (600 - 300)$$

$$\Delta U = 0.5 \times 0.718 \times 300 = 107.7 \text{ kJ}$$

**Solution (d) — Change in Enthalpy:**

$$\Delta H = m C_p (T_2 - T_1) = 0.5 \times 1.005 \times 300$$

$$\Delta H = 150.75 \text{ kJ}$$

**Verification using First Law:**
$$Q = \Delta U + W = 107.7 + 43.05 = 150.75 \text{ kJ}$$
This equals $\Delta H$ ✓ (since $Q_P = \Delta H$ at constant pressure).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Enthalpy Property Relation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[System State: T, P, V, U] --> B[Internal Energy U]
    A --> C[Pressure P]
    A --> D[Volume V]
    B --> E[Calculate H = U + PV]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Enthalpy H]
    F --> G[Apply to Open System Steady Flow]
    G --> H[Turbine Work]
    G --> I[Compressor Work]
    G --> J[Nozzle Exit Velocity]
    G --> K[Heat Exchanger Energy Balance]
    E --> L[Calculate dh = Cp dT]

```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Specific Heat Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD

    B -->|CLOSED| C{Is Volume constant?}
    B -->|OPEN| D[Use Cp and dh = Cp dT]
    C -->|YES| E[Use Cv and dU = Cv dT]
    C -->|NO| F{Is Pressure constant?}
    F -->|YES| G[Use Cp and dH = Cp dT]
    F -->|NO| H[Use Polytropic Relation]
    D --> I[Apply Steady Flow Energy Equation]
    E --> J[Apply First Law Q = dU]
    G --> K[Apply First Law Q = dH at const P]
    I --> L[Solve for W or V or m]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    H --> L
    style A fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff

    style G fill:#9f1239,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: $C_p$ vs $C_v$ Functional Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Heat Input Q] --> B{Process Type}
    B -->|Constant V| C[Q goes to Internal Energy U]
    B -->|Constant P| D[Q goes to Enthalpy H]

    D --> F[Q = m Cp dT]
    E --> G[dU = m Cv dT]
    F --> H[dH = m Cp dT]
    G --> I[For Ideal Gas U is a function of T only]
    H --> J[For Ideal Gas H is a function of T only]
    I --> K[Apply Mayer: Cp - Cv = R]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Final Energy Balance: Q - W = dU or dH]
    style A fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff

    style F fill:#831843,color:#ffffff

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term 'enthalpy' and explain why it is considered a thermodynamic property of state.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Enthalpy ($H$) is an extensive thermodynamic property defined as the total heat content of a system, mathematically expressed as:

$$H = U + PV$$

where $U$ is the internal energy, $P$ is the absolute pressure, and $V$ is the volume of the system.

**(1 mark)** It is a *property of state* because it depends only on the state variables ($P, V, U$) of the system, not on the path taken to reach that state. Mathematically, $H$ is an exact differential, meaning $\oint dH = 0$ over a cyclic process. **(2 marks)** This path-independence is the defining criterion for a thermodynamic property, as opposed to heat ($Q$) or work ($W$) which are path functions. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and prove Mayer's relation for an ideal gas.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement:** For an ideal gas, the difference between specific heat at constant pressure ($C_p$) and specific heat at constant volume ($C_v$) is equal to the characteristic gas constant $R$. **(1 mark)**

$$C_p - C_v = R$$

**Proof:** Starting from $h = u + Pv$ and differentiating with respect to $T$:

$$\frac{dh}{dT} = \frac{du}{dT} + \frac{d(Pv)}{dT}$$

For an ideal gas, $Pv = RT$, hence $\frac{d(Pv)}{dT} = R$. **(1 mark)**

Substituting the definitions $C_p = \frac{dh}{dT}$ and $C_v = \frac{du}{dT}$ (which hold for ideal gases since $u$ and $h$ depend only on $T$): **(0.5 marks)**

$$C_p = C_v + R \implies C_p - C_v = R \quad \blacksquare$$ **(0.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**Q3 (a). [7 Marks] Derive an expression for the enthalpy change of an ideal gas during a process from state 1 to state 2, considering both constant and variable specific heats.**

**Model Solution:**

**Case 1: Constant Specific Heats**

For an ideal gas, $h$ is a function of $T$ only (Joule's Law). The differential change in specific enthalpy is:

$$dh = C_p \, dT$$

Integrating from state 1 to state 2:

$$\int_{h_1}^{h_2} dh = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p \, dT$$

For constant $C_p$:

$$h_2 - h_1 = C_p (T_2 - T_1) \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**Case 2: Variable Specific Heats**

When $C_p$ varies with temperature, it is often expressed as a polynomial:

$$C_p(T) = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3$$

The average specific heat over a temperature range is defined as:

$$\bar{C}_{p,1-2} = \frac{1}{T_2 - T_1} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p(T) \, dT$$

The enthalpy change becomes:

$$h_2 - h_1 = \bar{C}_{p,1-2} (T_2 - T_1) \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

Or, using a reference state $T_{\text{ref}}$ and tabulated $h(T)$ values from gas tables:

$$h_2 - h_1 = [h(T_2) - h(T_{\text{ref}})] - [h(T_1) - h(T_{\text{ref}})] \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

This approach is preferred for high-temperature combustion and propulsion problems where variable specific heats significantly affect accuracy.

---

**Q3 (b). [7 Marks] A rigid tank contains 2 kg of an ideal gas at 400 kPa and 300 K. The gas is heated until its temperature rises to 600 K. If $C_v = 0.82$ kJ/kg·K and $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, calculate: (i) Final pressure, (ii) Change in internal energy, (iii) Change in enthalpy, (iv) Heat transferred.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $m = 2$ kg, $P_1 = 400$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 600$ K, $C_v = 0.82$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $V = $ const.

**Step 1: Calculate $C_p$ using Mayer's Relation:**

$$C_p = C_v + R = 0.82 + 0.287 = 1.107 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**(i) Final Pressure (constant volume process):**

$$\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2} \implies P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1} = 400 \times \frac{600}{300}$$

$$\boxed{P_2 = 800 \text{ kPa} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}}$$

**(ii) Change in Internal Energy:**

$$\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1) = 2 \times 0.82 \times (600 - 300) = 2 \times 0.82 \times 300$$

$$\boxed{\Delta U = 492 \text{ kJ} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}}$$

**(iii) Change in Enthalpy:**

$$\Delta H = m C_p (T_2 - T_1) = 2 \times 1.107 \times 300$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H = 664.2 \text{ kJ} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}}$$

**(iv) Heat Transferred:**

For a rigid tank (constant volume), $W = 0$. By the First Law:

$$Q = \Delta U + W = 492 + 0$$

$$\boxed{Q = 492 \text{ kJ} \quad \text{(2.5 marks)}}$$

**Sanity Check:** $\Delta H - \Delta U = 664.2 - 492 = 172.2$ kJ. Also, $mR\Delta T = 2 \times 0.287 \times 300 = 172.2$ kJ ✓ (Mayer's relation verified).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Pattern]**

**Q4 (a). [7 Marks] Explain the difference between $C_p$ and $C_v$ for an ideal gas. Derive the relationship $C_p - C_v = R$ starting from the definition of enthalpy.**

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Difference:**

- $C_v$ is the specific heat capacity at **constant volume** — it is the rate of change of internal energy with temperature at constant $V$.
- $C_p$ is the specific heat capacity at **constant pressure** — it is the rate of change of enthalpy with temperature at constant $P$.
- Physically, $C_p > C_v$ because at constant pressure, some heat supplied is used to perform expansion work against the surroundings, in addition to raising the temperature.

**Derivation of Mayer's Relation:**

Starting from the definition of specific enthalpy:

$$h = u + Pv \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to temperature:

$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right) = \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right) + \left(\frac{\partial (Pv)}{\partial T}\right) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

For an ideal gas, $Pv = RT$, so:

$$\left(\frac{\partial (Pv)}{\partial T}\right) = R \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

By Joule's Law for ideal gases, $u$ and $h$ are functions of temperature only:

$$C_p = \frac{dh}{dT} \quad \text{and} \quad C_v = \frac{du}{dT} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

Substituting:

$$C_p = C_v + R \implies \boxed{C_p - C_v = R} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Note:** This relation is rigorously valid *only* for ideal gases. For real gases, the more general expression $C_p - C_v = T \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$ must be used.

---

**Q4 (b). [7 Marks] A piston-cylinder assembly contains 0.3 m³ of air at 100 kPa and 30 °C. The air is compressed polytropically to 800 kPa following $PV^{1.3} = $ constant. Calculate the work done, change in internal energy, change in enthalpy, and heat transfer. Take $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_1 = 0.3$ m³, $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 303$ K, $P_2 = 800$ kPa, $n = 1.3$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step 1: Mass of Air**

$$m = \frac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1} = \frac{100 \times 0.3}{0.287 \times 303} = \frac{30}{86.961}$$

$$m = 0.345 \text{ kg} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2: Final Temperature**

Using $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$:

$$V_2 = V_1 \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} = 0.3 \left(\frac{100}{800}\right)^{1/1.3}$$

$$V_2 = 0.3 \times (0.125)^{0.7692} = 0.3 \times 0.2154 = 0.0646 \text{ m³}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{P_2 V_2}{mR} = \frac{800 \times 0.0646}{0.345 \times 0.287} = \frac{51.68}{0.099}$$

$$T_2 = 521.6 \text{ K} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 3: Work Done (Polytropic)**

$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n} = \frac{800 \times 0.0646 - 100 \times 0.3}{1 - 1.3}$$

$$W = \frac{51.68 - 30}{-0.3} = \frac{21.68}{-0.3}$$

$$\boxed{W = -72.27 \text{ kJ (work done ON the system)} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}}$$

**Step 4: Change in Internal Energy**

$$\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1) = 0.345 \times 0.718 \times (521.6 - 303)$$

$$\Delta U = 0.345 \times 0.718 \times 218.6 = 54.13 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta U = 54.13 \text{ kJ} \quad \text{(1 mark)}}$$

**Step 5: Change in Enthalpy**

$$\Delta H = m C_p (T_2 - T_1) = 0.345 \times 1.005 \times 218.6$$

$$\Delta H = 75.78 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H = 75.78 \text{ kJ} \quad \text{(1 mark)}}$$

**Step 6: Heat Transfer (First Law)**

$$Q = \Delta U + W = 54.13 + (-72.27)$$

$$\boxed{Q = -18.14 \text{ kJ (heat rejected by the system)} \quad \text{(1 mark)}}$$

**Verification:** $\Delta H - \Delta U = 75.78 - 54.13 = 21.65$ kJ. Also, $mR\Delta T = 0.345 \times 0.287 \times 218.6 = 21.65$ kJ ✓.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error in Work:** Students frequently forget that compression work is *negative* (work is done *on* the system). Always check: if volume decreases, $W < 0$.
> 2. **Mixing $C_p$ and $C_v$:** Using $C_p$ for $\Delta U$ or $C_v$ for $\Delta H$ costs 2-3 marks. Memorize: **U uses $C_v$, H uses $C_p$**.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** If $P$ is in kPa, $V$ in m³, then $PV$ is in kJ. Do not convert to Joules unnecessarily — KTU accepts kJ if all other units match.
> 4. **Missing Mayer's Verification Step:** Examiners give bonus marks for showing $C_p - C_v = R$ verification at the end. Always do this.
> 5. **Ideal Gas Assumption Not Stated:** If you use $Pv = RT$ or $C_p - C_v = R$ without stating "**assuming ideal gas behavior**", you lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Negative $Q$ in Adiabatic/Compression:** A negative $Q$ is *physically valid* (heat is rejected). Do not "correct" it to a positive value.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this for last-minute revision before KTU exams:**

- [x] **Enthalpy Definition:** $H = U + PV$ (extensive), $h = u + Pv$ (intensive, J/kg or kJ/kg).
- [x] **Specific Heat at Constant Volume:** $C_v = (\partial u / \partial T)_v$ — used for closed, constant-V systems.
- [x] **Specific Heat at Constant Pressure:** $C_p = (\partial h / \partial T)_p$ — used for open/flow systems and constant-P processes.
- [x] **Mayer's Relation:** $C_p - C_v = R$ — valid for ideal gases only. **Memorize.**
- [x] **Specific Heat Ratio:** $\gamma = C_p / C_v = 1.4$ for air. Used in isentropic relations.
- [x] **Gibbs Fundamental Relation:** $dH = TdS + VdP$ — board-exam favorite.
- [x] **Enthalpy Change for Ideal Gas:** $\Delta h = C_p \Delta T$ (constant $C_p$) or $\Delta h = \int C_p(T) dT$ (variable).
- [x] **Internal Energy Change:** $\Delta u = C_v \Delta T$ (ideal gas, Joule's Law).
- [x] **Mixture Specific Heats:** $C_{p,\text{mix}} = \sum m_i C_{p,i}$ (mass-weighted).
- [x] **Air Standard Values:** $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$.
- [x] **Constant-Volume Process:** $Q = \Delta U = mC_v \Delta T$, $W = 0$, $\Delta H = mC_p \Delta T$.
- [x] **Constant-Pressure Process:** $Q = \Delta H = mC_p \Delta T$, $W = P\Delta V = mR\Delta T$.
- [x] **Polytropic Process:** $W = (P_2V_2 - P_1V_1)/(1-n)$, use First Law to find $Q$.
- [x] **Always State:** "Assuming ideal gas behavior" before using $Pv = RT$ or Mayer's relation.
- [x] **First Law Recap:** $Q - W = \Delta U$ (closed), $q - w = \Delta h + \Delta ke + \Delta pe$ (open).
- [x] **Sign Convention:** Heat in = positive, Work out = positive (KTU standard).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Module 2 questions on "Enthalpy and Specific Heats" typically combine a *definition/proof* (3 marks) with a *numerical problem* involving air (7-10 marks). Practice the polytropic process and constant-volume heating problems — they are the most repeated.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Work and the Energy of a Flowing Fluid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flow Work and the Energy of a Flowing Fluid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flow Work (Flow Energy / Displacement Work in Flow)**
> Flow work is the work required to push a mass of fluid into or out of a control volume against the pressure exerted at the boundary. It is the energy transfer that occurs because the fluid mass is *moving* across the system boundary, and is mathematically expressed per unit mass as $w_{flow} = P \cdot v$, where $P$ is the absolute pressure and $v$ is the specific volume of the fluid.

In open system analysis (control volume approach), unlike a closed system where only the system boundary moves, the mass itself crosses the boundary. To make this mass cross, the surroundings must do work on the system — this is the **flow work**, denoted $W_{flow}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Energy of a Flowing Fluid (E)**
> A fluid in steady flow possesses three forms of energy: internal energy ($U$), kinetic energy ($\frac{1}{2}mV^2$), and potential energy ($mgz$). When flow work is added to internal energy, the sum $U + PV$ is defined as **Enthalpy ($H$)**. Therefore, the total energy of a flowing fluid per unit mass is:
> $$e = u + \frac{P}{\rho} + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz = h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$$
> where $h = u + Pv$ is the specific enthalpy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are pumping water from a ground-level tank to a water tank on the first floor of a building through a pipe.

- The water is **moving** through the pipe, so it carries **kinetic energy**.
- The water is **higher up** than the pump, so it carries **potential energy** (against gravity).
- The water has some temperature, so it carries **internal energy**.

But here is the subtle point most students miss: To *push* a packet of water *into* the pipe at the inlet, the pump must do work against the pressure of the water already inside the pipe. This push-work is **flow work**. It is invisible in a closed bucket, but absolutely critical in an open pipe.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Takeaway**
> Think of flow work as the "entry tax" the surroundings must pay to insert mass into a pressurized system. In a closed container, no mass crosses the boundary, so this tax is zero. In a flowing pipe, the tax is non-zero and is the key to connecting internal energy with **enthalpy**.

## 1.3 Key Symbols and Standard Metrics

- **Pressure ($P$):** Pa or kPa — absolute pressure only for thermodynamic analysis.
- **Specific volume ($v$):** m³/kg.
- **Specific internal energy ($u$):** kJ/kg.
- **Specific enthalpy ($h$):** kJ/kg.
- **Specific flow work ($Pv$):** kJ/kg.
- **Specific kinetic energy ($V^2/2$):** kJ/kg.
- **Specific potential energy ($gz$):** kJ/kg.
- **Universal gas constant ($R$):** **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** or **0.287 kJ/(kg·K)** for air.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The introduction of flow work comes from the realization that the **First Law of Thermodynamics** is universal, but its form changes depending on whether mass crosses the system boundary.

1. **Step 1 — Identify the system type.**
   - **Closed system (Control Mass):** No mass crosses boundary. Work is only $PdV$ (boundary work).
   - **Open system (Control Volume):** Mass crosses boundary. Work includes $PdV$ **plus** flow work $W_{flow}$.

2. **Step 2 — Recognize the need for flow work.**
   - At the inlet, the upstream fluid must push a parcel of fluid into the control volume. This requires work = $P_1 \cdot V_1$ (where $V_1$ is the volume of that parcel).
   - At the outlet, the fluid inside the control volume pushes the parcel out, doing work = $P_2 \cdot V_2$ on the surroundings.

3. **Step 3 — Express flow work per unit mass.**
   - Dividing by mass $m$: $w_{flow,in} = P_1 v_1$ and $w_{flow,out} = P_2 v_2$.
   - Net flow work per unit mass: $w_{flow,net} = P_1 v_1 - P_2 v_2$ (in − out).

4. **Step 4 — Combine internal energy and flow work into enthalpy.**
   - The energy carried *by* the fluid due to its state is $u + Pv = h$ (enthalpy).
   - This is a **property** of the fluid (state function), not a form of energy transfer.

5. **Step 5 — Construct the total energy of a flowing fluid.**
   - Total specific energy: $e_{total} = u + Pv + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz = h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$.

6. **Step 6 — Apply to the energy balance of a control volume.**
   - This sets the stage for the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for nozzles, turbines, compressors, pumps, and heat exchangers.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Enthalpy

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we define $H = U + PV$**
> In a flow process, the combination $U + PV$ appears naturally in the energy balance because the fluid carries both its molecular internal energy *and* the flow work needed to displace it. Defining enthalpy avoids carrying the $PV$ term separately every time, simplifying all open-system equations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation (per unit mass) | Units | Used For |
|---|--------------|--------------------------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Specific flow work (inlet) | $w_{flow,1} = P_1 v_1$ | kJ/kg | Energy to push mass in |
| 2 | Specific flow work (outlet) | $w_{flow,2} = P_2 v_2$ | kJ/kg | Energy recovered pushing mass out |
| 3 | Net specific flow work | $w_{flow,net} = P_1 v_1 - P_2 v_2$ | kJ/kg | Net flow work in a CV |
| 4 | Specific enthalpy | $h = u + Pv$ | kJ/kg | Combining $u$ and flow work |
| 5 | Enthalpy change | $\Delta h = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | For a flow process |
| 6 | Total specific energy | $e = h + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$ | kJ/kg | Energy carried by flowing fluid |
| 7 | For ideal gas | $h = c_p T$ | kJ/kg | Enthalpy from temperature |
| 8 | Enthalpy – internal energy link | $h - u = Pv = RT$ (ideal gas) | kJ/kg | For ideal gas |
| 9 | Rate of flow work | $\dot{W}_{flow} = \dot{m} Pv$ | kW (kJ/s) | Per second flow work |
| 10 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A V = \dfrac{AV}{v}$ | kg/s | Continuity equation |

> [!TIP]
> For an **incompressible fluid**, $v$ is approximately constant, so $Pv$ is often small compared to $u$. For an **ideal gas**, $Pv = RT$, and the $h = c_p T$ relation is highly useful.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steam power plants:** Boiler feedwater carries enthalpy as $h_{in}$; turbine exhaust leaves at $h_{out}$. The work extracted is the enthalpy drop.
- **Gas turbines and jet engines:** Compressed hot air enters the combustion chamber with high $h$; exhaust jets produce thrust from high kinetic and enthalpy.
- **Refrigeration and HVAC:** Refrigerant enthalpy tables are used at every component (compressor, condenser, expansion valve, evaporator).
- **Hydraulic systems and pipelines:** Flow work is the basis of energy analysis in pumps, where pump work equals the enthalpy rise.
- **Rocket propulsion:** The thrust equation depends directly on the enthalpy and kinetic energy of the exhaust gases.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Flow Work

**Setup:** Consider a fluid element of volume $V$ and mass $m$ at the inlet of a control volume. The pressure at the inlet is $P_1$. To push this element across the boundary into the control volume, the upstream fluid must perform work on it.

**Step 1 — Recognize the geometry.**
The fluid element has a small cross-sectional area $A$ and travels a small distance $dx$. Its volume is:
$$V_1 = A \cdot dx$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the work done.**
The work done by the upstream fluid to push the element through the inlet is force times distance:
$$W_{flow,in} = F \cdot dx = P_1 A \cdot dx = P_1 V_1$$

**Step 3 — Convert to per-unit-mass form.**
Dividing by mass $m$, and noting that $V_1 / m = v_1$ (specific volume), we get:
$$w_{flow,1} = \frac{P_1 V_1}{m} = P_1 v_1$$

**Step 4 — Apply the same logic at the outlet.**
At the outlet, the fluid inside the control volume pushes the element out. The work done *by* the system on the surroundings is:
$$w_{flow,2} = P_2 v_2$$

**Step 5 — Net flow work (per unit mass).**
$$w_{flow,net} = w_{flow,1} - w_{flow,2} = P_1 v_1 - P_2 v_2$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Total Energy of a Flowing Fluid

**Step 1 — Start from the total energy content.**
A flowing fluid carries:
- Internal energy: $u$ (molecular energy)
- Flow work: $Pv$ (energy to push the fluid in)
- Kinetic energy: $\frac{V^2}{2}$ (energy of motion)
- Potential energy: $gz$ (energy due to elevation)

**Step 2 — Combine per unit mass.**
$$e = u + Pv + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the definition of enthalpy $h = u + Pv$.**
$$e = h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$$

**Step 4 — Express in rate form (per second).**
$$\dot{E} = \dot{m}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right) \quad \text{[kW]}$$

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation Showing Why Enthalpy Appears in the First Law for Open Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the *heart* of why $h$ shows up in the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) and frequently appears in KTU problems.

**Step 1 — Write the First Law for a closed system.**
$$Q - W = \Delta U$$

**Step 2 — Adapt to a control volume (open system) over a small time interval $dt$.**
Consider a small element of fluid of mass $dm$ entering the CV. To push it in, the surroundings do work $dW_{flow,in} = P_1 v_1 \, dm$ on the CV.

**Step 3 — Energy entering the CV with the mass.**
- Internal energy: $u_1 \, dm$
- Kinetic energy: $\frac{V_1^2}{2} \, dm$
- Potential energy: $g z_1 \, dm$
- Flow work done on the fluid: $P_1 v_1 \, dm$

Total energy entering: $(u_1 + P_1 v_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1) dm$.

**Step 4 — Define enthalpy.**
$$h_1 = u_1 + P_1 v_1$$

So the energy entering per unit mass becomes:
$$e_{in} = h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1$$

**Step 5 — By the same logic, energy leaving per unit mass is:**
$$e_{out} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$

**Step 6 — Final energy balance for the control volume (per unit mass).**
$$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$$

This is the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** — it is the foundation for analyzing nozzles, turbines, compressors, pumps, and heat exchangers.

> [!NOTE]
> Note how $Pv$ no longer appears separately — it has been *absorbed* into $h$. This is the entire reason enthalpy is the preferred property in open-system thermodynamics.

## 3.4 Derivation of $h = c_p T$ for an Ideal Gas

**Step 1 — Start from $h = u + Pv$.**
For an ideal gas, $Pv = RT$, so:
$$h = u + RT$$

**Step 2 — Use the ideal gas internal energy relation.**
For an ideal gas, $u$ is a function of temperature only:
$$du = c_v \, dT$$

**Step 3 — Integrate to get $u$.**
$$u = c_v T + \text{const.}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back.**
$$h = c_v T + RT = (c_v + R) T$$

**Step 5 — Apply Mayer's relation** $c_p = c_v + R$:
$$h = c_p T$$

Therefore, for an ideal gas, $\Delta h = c_p \Delta T$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Energy Partition of a Flowing Fluid

This diagram shows how the total specific energy of a flowing fluid is partitioned into enthalpy, kinetic, and potential contributions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid in steady flow] --> B[Internal Energy u]
    A --> C[Flow Work Pv]
    A --> D[Kinetic Energy V squared over 2]
    A --> E[Potential Energy gz]
    B --> F[Enthalpy h equals u plus Pv]
    C --> F
    F --> G[Total Specific Energy e]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    G --> H[Energy carried by fluid per unit mass]
    H --> I[Drives SFEE in turbines nozzles pumps]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Role of Flow Work in an Open System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Inlet[Inlet Boundary of CV] -->|Fluid Enters| CV[Control Volume]
    CV -->|Fluid Leaves| Outlet[Outlet Boundary of CV]
    PushIn[Flow Work done on fluid: P1 v1] -.-> Inlet
    PushOut[Flow Work done by fluid: P2 v2] -.-> Outlet
    CV --> KE[Kinetic Energy Change]
    CV --> PE[Potential Energy Change]
    CV --> dU[Internal Energy Change]
    PushIn --> Enthalpy[Contributes to Enthalpy h = u plus Pv]
    dU --> Enthalpy
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the First Law for Open Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Heat Transfer Q] --> EB[Energy Balance of CV]
    W[Shaft Work W] --> EB
    M1[Mass Inlet: m dot times e1] --> EB
    M2[Mass Outlet: m dot times e2] --> EB
    EB --> SFEE[Steady Flow Energy Equation]
    SFEE --> N1[Nozzle Application]
    SFEE --> N2[Turbine Application]
    SFEE --> N3[Compressor Application]
    SFEE --> N4[Pump Application]
    SFEE --> N5[Heat Exchanger Application]
    FW[Flow Work Pv] --> SFEE
    ENTH[Enthalpy h] --> SFEE
    KE[Kinetic V squared over 2] --> SFEE
    PE[Potential gz] --> SFEE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing up closed and open system work:** Many students write $W = P \Delta V$ for an open system. This is wrong. For an open system, the work term in the energy balance is the **shaft work**, and the $PV$ terms appear as **enthalpy**.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Using $P$ in bar and $v$ in m³/kg gives $Pv$ in **kJ/kg only if you convert bar to kPa** (1 bar = 100 kPa). Failing this conversion is the single most common numerical error.
> 3. **Using gauge pressure:** All thermodynamic calculations require **absolute pressure**. Substituting gauge pressure is a guaranteed loss of 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Sign convention confusion:** $Q$ *into* the system is positive, $W$ *out* of the system is positive (KTU convention varies — always write the convention assumed).
> 5. **Kinetic energy unit mismatch:** $\frac{V^2}{2}$ has units of m²/s² = J/kg. Convert to **kJ/kg** by dividing by 1000. Students often forget this and get answers off by a factor of 1000.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define flow work and explain why it appears in the analysis of open systems. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Flow work is the work required to push a mass of fluid across the boundary of a control volume against the pressure at the boundary. Per unit mass, it is expressed as $w_{flow} = P \cdot v$, where $P$ is the absolute pressure and $v$ is the specific volume of the fluid.

In an **open system** (control volume), mass continuously enters and leaves the system. To make a fluid element enter the control volume, the upstream fluid must do work on it equal to $P_1 v_1$. Similarly, when the fluid leaves, the system does work $P_2 v_2$ on the downstream fluid. In contrast, in a **closed system**, no mass crosses the boundary, so this term does not arise. Hence flow work is a feature unique to open system analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> Flow work is also called **flow energy** or **displacement work in flow**.

---

### Question 2. Define enthalpy. Why is it considered a property of the system? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

Enthalpy is a thermodynamic property defined as the sum of internal energy and the flow work of a fluid:
$$H = U + PV$$
On a per unit mass basis: $h = u + Pv$ (kJ/kg).

It is considered a **property** (state function) because $U$, $P$, and $V$ are all point functions — their values depend only on the state of the system, not on the path taken to reach that state. Therefore, the combination $U + PV$ is also a point function, and its differential $dH = dU + dV \, dP$ is exact.

Enthalpy is especially useful in open system analysis because the energy carried by a fluid stream per unit mass is $h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$, simplifying the energy balance considerably.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A. (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the flow work per unit mass of a fluid. Show that the total specific energy of a flowing fluid is $h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$. (7 Marks)
