A 100 cm² area is 100 × 10⁻⁴ m², not 100 m². > 7. **Not stating the formula for $T_0$ derivation** — KTU expects the energy balance explicitly written. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Nozzle = Converging (subsonic)** or **C-D shape (supersonic)**; **Diffuser = Diverging (subsonic)**. - The **master energy equation** is SFEE in the form $h_1 + V_1^2/2 = h_2 + V_2^2/2$ (adiabatic, no work). - **Stagnation enthalpy $h_0 = h + V^2/2$ is conserved** in ideal nozzle/diffuser flow. - The **area-velocity relation** $\dfrac{dA}{A} = (Ma^2 - 1)\dfrac{dV}{V}$ is the cornerstone of nozzle design. - **Choked flow** at the throat sets the **maximum mass flow rate** $\dot{m}_{max}$ for a given $P_0$ and $T_0$. - For air ($\gamma = 1.4$): critical pressure ratio $P^*/P_0 = 0.5283$ and $T^*/T_0 = 0.8333$. - **Nozzle efficiency:** $\eta_N = \dfrac{V_{2a}^2 - V_1^2}{V_{2s}^2 - V_1^2}$ (ratio of actual to ideal KE change). - **Diffuser efficiency:** $\eta_D = \dfrac{V_{2s}^2 - V_1^2}{V_{2a}^2 - V_1^2}$ (reverse — ideal to actual). - Always convert $c_p$ units: $1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) $= 1005$ J/(kg·K). - **Speed of sound:** $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ — temperature-dependent only. - **Continuity equation** $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ is non-negotiable in every problem. - **Trick to identify device:** if pressure drops and velocity rises → **nozzle**; if pressure rises and velocity falls → **diffuser**.

topics.title: Throttling Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Throttling Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **throttling valve** (also called an **expansion valve** or **throttling device**) is a mechanical flow-restricting component in a fluid system that produces a substantial, sudden pressure drop in the flowing fluid. From a thermodynamic standpoint, the throttling process is defined as a **steady-flow, adiabatic (no heat transfer) process in which no work is done on or by the fluid, and the changes in kinetic and potential energies are negligible**.

The governing thermodynamic identity for a throttling device is:

$$h_1 = h_2 = \text{constant}$$

where $h_1$ and $h_2$ are the **specific enthalpies (kJ/kg)** of the fluid at the inlet and outlet of the valve, respectively. This process is therefore called an **isenthalpic process** (constant enthalpy process).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Throttling is treated as the inverse counterpart of the adiabatic compression/expansion processes studied earlier. It is a *non-quasi-static, irreversible* expansion, so entropy *increases* ($\Delta s > 0$ for adiabatic throttling). The state at the exit cannot be found from the steady-flow energy equation alone — it must be located on the relevant property chart (e.g., $T$-$s$ diagram, $h$-$s$ chart, or superheated steam tables).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose Effect

Imagine you are watering your garden with a hose. When you partially cover the hose outlet with your thumb, the water pressure inside the hose drops sharply at your thumb, but the water shoots out at a much higher velocity on the other side. Yet:

- You are **not doing shaft work** on the water.
- The process happens **so fast** that essentially **no heat is transferred** to the surroundings (adiabatic).
- The kinetic and potential energy changes of the water *between* the upstream point and the downstream point are negligible.

The water simply experiences a sudden pressure drop and a temperature change (usually cooling for real gases like steam or refrigerants). This is exactly what a throttling valve does — it is the engineering "thumb on the hose" of every refrigeration cycle, steam power plant, and gas distribution network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Engineering Examples of Throttling Devices:**
> - **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Expansion valve (TX valve, capillary tube, throttle valve) between condenser and evaporator.
> - **Steam Power Plants:** Throttle valve governing steam entry to the turbine.
> - **Gas Pipelines:** Pressure-reduction stations controlling city gas distribution.
> - **Chemical Process Industries:** Pressure let-down valves.
> - **Liquefaction of Gases:** Joule–Thomson expansion for cryogenic cooling (helium, nitrogen, LNG).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|----------|--------|----------------|
| Specific enthalpy | $h$ | kJ/kg |
| Pressure | $P$ | kPa or bar |
| Temperature | $T$ | K or °C |
| Specific entropy | $s$ | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Velocity | $V$ | m/s |
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m}$ | kg/s |
| Joule–Thomson coefficient | $\mu_{JT}$ | K/kPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Joule–Thomson coefficient** $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$ is the *characteristic property* of a fluid that determines whether it cools or heats upon throttling. For an **ideal gas**, $\mu_{JT} = 0$ (no temperature change). For a **real gas** (steam, refrigerants, air at high pressure), $\mu_{JT} \neq 0$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The Six Logical Steps

The thermodynamic behaviour of a throttling valve can be understood by following these six rigorous, ordered steps:

**Step 1 — Identify the control volume.**
Draw a control volume enclosing the throttling valve. The fluid enters at state **1** (high pressure) and exits at state **2** (low pressure).

**Step 2 — Apply the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE).**
The general SFEE for a control volume is:

$$Q - W = \dot{m}\left[\left(h_2 - h_1\right) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the four throttling assumptions.**

| Assumption | Mathematical Form | Physical Reason |
|------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Adiabatic | $Q = 0$ | Process is too fast for heat transfer |
| No shaft work | $W = 0$ | No moving boundary or rotating shaft |
| Negligible ΔKE | $\Delta KE \approx 0$ | Valve is small; $V_1 \approx V_2$ |
| Negligible ΔPE | $\Delta PE \approx 0$ | Vertical height change is small |

**Step 4 — Simplify the SFEE.**
With all four assumptions substituted, the SFEE reduces to:

$$0 - 0 = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1 + 0 + 0)$$

which directly yields the **isenthalpic identity**:

$$h_1 = h_2$$

**Step 5 — Identify the irreversibility.**
Unlike a reversible isentropic expansion, the throttling process is **highly irreversible**. The fluid does no work against the surroundings, but the internal friction and viscous dissipation generate entropy. Therefore:

$$s_2 > s_1 \quad (\Delta s > 0)$$

This is why on a $T$-$s$ diagram, the throttling process is drawn as a **vertical drop (constant $h$)** from the initial state downward, ending on a lower isobar — *not* as a vertical line on a $T$-$s$ chart (that would be isentropic, not isenthalpic).

**Step 6 — Locate the exit state using property tables.**
Since $h_1$ is known and $h_2 = h_1$, the exit state is located on the **property chart (e.g., $h$-$s$ Mollier diagram, or superheated steam tables)** by:
1. Finding the intersection of the constant-enthalpy line $h = h_1$ with the new lower pressure isobar $P_2$.
2. Reading off the corresponding $T_2$, $v_2$, $s_2$, and $x_2$ (if in the two-phase region).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Why" matters in KTU valuation:** A model answer that simply states $h_1 = h_2$ without justifying *why* (i.e., the SFEE simplification) is treated as a half-mark answer. Always write the SFEE and *substitute* the assumptions to derive $h_1 = h_2$ on paper — this fetches full marks.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Identity | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|--------------------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | $Q - W = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$ | General SFEE | kW |
| 2 | $h_1 = h_2$ | Throttling identity (isenthalpic) | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $s_2 > s_1$ | Irreversibility indicator | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 4 | $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$ | Joule–Thomson coefficient | K/kPa |
| 5 | $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right]$ | $\mu_{JT}$ for real gases (from Maxwell relations) | K/kPa |
| 6 | $h_2 = h_1 \Rightarrow (h_f + x_2 h_{fg})_{P_2} = h_1$ | For wet steam or two-phase throttling | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $h_2 = h_{g,P_2}$ (if $h_1 > h_{g,P_2}$) | Superheated steam at exit | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{A_2 V_2}{v_2} = \dfrac{A_1 V_1}{v_1}$ | Continuity equation | kg/s |
| 9 | $\Delta h = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ (approximate) | For ideal gas, $\Delta T \approx 0$, so $\Delta h \approx 0$ | kJ/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall — Watch the Pipe Symbol:** When writing the Joule–Thomson coefficient in tables or inlined, never write $\vert \partial T \vert$ with a vertical bar `|` inside a markdown table — it breaks the column boundary. Always use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\left\vert \cdot \right\vert$ in LaTeX.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Industry | Application of Throttling |
|----------|-----------------------------|
| **Refrigeration & AC** | Expansion valve causes refrigerant to flash into a low-temperature low-pressure mixture, enabling heat absorption in the evaporator. |
| **Steam Power Plants** | Throttle/governor valve controls steam mass flow into the turbine; dry/wet steam state determines turbine blade life. |
| **Cryogenics** | Joule–Thomson throttling of high-pressure nitrogen or helium is the workhorse of gas liquefaction (Linde process). |
| **Petrochemical** | Pressure let-down valves between high-pressure reactors and downstream distillation columns. |
| **Gas Distribution** | City gas pressure reduction from transmission (70 bar) to distribution (4 bar) pressure. |
| **Rocket Propulsion** | Pressurization of propellant tanks using regulated cold-gas thrusters. |

## 2.4 Joule–Thomson Coefficient — A Deeper Look

For a **perfect gas**, $h$ is a function of $T$ only (Joule's Law). Therefore, at constant $h$, $T$ is constant, so:

$$\mu_{JT}^{\text{ideal}} = 0$$

For a **real gas**, deviations from ideal behaviour (captured by the compressibility factor $Z$ or the van der Waals equation) make $\mu_{JT}$ non-zero. There exists an **inversion temperature** $T_i$ above which the gas *heats up* on throttling ($\mu_{JT} < 0$) and below which it *cools down* ($\mu_{JT} > 0$).

> [!NOTE]
> For air: $T_i \approx 600\,\text{K}$, so at room temperature air *cools* on throttling. This is the principle behind domestic gas regulators and is why a CO$_2$ fire extinguisher nozzle feels cold.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Throttling Identity

We start with the general steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) applied to a control volume that encloses the throttling valve:

$$Q_{cv} - W_{cv} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$

**Step 1 — Apply the adiabatic assumption.**
Because the fluid passes through the valve in a fraction of a second, there is insufficient time for any meaningful heat transfer. Therefore:

$$Q_{cv} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply the no-work assumption.**
A throttling valve has no moving shaft, no piston, and no rotating blades. The fluid does no shaft work on or by the surroundings. The only "work-like" effect is internal friction, but that is captured within the enthalpy of the fluid (it is *not* an external work term in the SFEE). Therefore:

$$W_{cv} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Neglect kinetic energy change.**
The valve is geometrically compact. Both the inlet and outlet cross-sectional areas are comparable, and the velocities $V_1$ and $V_2$ are small. Hence:

$$\Delta KE = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} \approx 0$$

**Step 4 — Neglect potential energy change.**
The valve is mounted at a fixed elevation; $z_1 \approx z_2$. Hence:

$$\Delta PE = g(z_2 - z_1) \approx 0$$

**Step 5 — Substitute all four simplifications into the SFEE.**

$$0 - 0 = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + 0 + 0\right]$$

**Step 6 — Cancel the mass flow rate $\dot{m} \neq 0$.**

$$0 = h_2 - h_1$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange.**

$$h_1 = h_2$$

This is the **fundamental throttling identity** — the process is isenthalpic.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam — Model Question):**
> Steam at a pressure of **10 bar** and a temperature of **250 °C** enters a throttling valve and exits at a pressure of **1 bar**. Determine:
> (a) The temperature of the steam at the exit.
> (b) The change in specific entropy.
> (c) State whether the throttling is cooling or heating.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Read inlet state from the superheated steam table.**

At $P_1 = 10\,\text{bar}$ and $T_1 = 250\,°\text{C}$ (superheated region):

$$h_1 = 2942.6\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

$$s_1 = 7.0523\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the throttling identity.**

$$h_2 = h_1 = 2942.6\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Locate the exit state at $P_2 = 1\,\text{bar}$ with $h_2 = 2942.6\,\text{kJ/kg}$.**

At 1 bar, the saturation properties are:

$$h_f = 417.46\,\text{kJ/kg}, \quad h_g = 2675.5\,\text{kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2258.0\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

Since $h_2 = 2942.6\,\text{kJ/kg} > h_g = 2675.5\,\text{kJ/kg}$, the steam at the exit is **superheated**.

From the 1 bar superheated steam table, we find the temperature $T_2$ such that $h = 2942.6\,\text{kJ/kg}$. Interpolating between tabulated values:

$$T_2 \approx 159.1\,°\text{C} \quad (\text{approximately 159 °C, mildly superheated})$$

**Step 4 — Read $s_2$ at this state.**

$$s_2 \approx 7.6125\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the change in entropy.**

$$\Delta s = s_2 - s_1 = 7.6125 - 7.0523 = 0.5602\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

$$\Delta s = +0.5602\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)} > 0$$

**Step 6 — Determine cooling or heating.**

$$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 159.1 - 250 = -90.9\,°\text{C}$$

The steam **cools** during throttling. This is consistent with the positive Joule–Thomson coefficient of steam at this state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Many students make the error of writing $\Delta s = 0$ assuming the process is isentropic. This is **wrong**. The throttling process is isenthalpic ($h$ = constant), not isentropic ($s$ = constant). Entropy *always* increases during throttling.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour of Steam During Throttling

| Inlet State | Outlet State | Behaviour |
|--------------|---------------|------------|
| Superheated steam ($h_1 > h_{g,P_2}$) | Superheated steam at lower $P_2$ | Cools, remains superheated |
| Dry saturated steam ($h_1 = h_{g,P_1}$) | Wet or superheated steam at lower $P_2$ | Cools; may become wet or remain superheated |
| Wet steam | Wetter steam (higher dryness fraction context) | Cools; quality can change |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality (dryness fraction $x$) variation in throttling of wet steam:**
> If $h_1 = h_{f,P_1} + x_1 h_{fg,P_1}$ and $h_2 = h_1 = h_{f,P_2} + x_2 h_{fg,P_2}$, then:

$$x_2 = \frac{h_1 - h_{f,P_2}}{h_{fg,P_2}}$$
> For most refrigerants and steam, the isenthalpic line at the lower pressure lies *above* the saturated vapour line when the inlet is highly superheated. Hence the fluid is *wetter* (lower $x$) at the exit when the inlet was wet, and *cooler* when the inlet was superheated.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Joule–Thomson Coefficient (Maxwell Relation Form)

Starting from the fundamental relation:

$$dh = T\,ds + v\,dP$$

At constant enthalpy ($dh = 0$):

$$0 = T\,ds + v\,dP \quad \Rightarrow \quad ds = -\frac{v}{T}\,dP$$

Now, $s = s(T, P)$, so:

$$ds = \left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

Equating coefficients and using the Maxwell relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial s}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{C_p}{T}$$

After algebraic manipulation, the Joule–Thomson coefficient is:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h = \frac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right]$$

This equation is the **analytical backbone** for evaluating the cooling/heating tendency of any real fluid during throttling.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Throttling Valve — Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the functional flow architecture of a generic throttling valve as a thermodynamic control volume. It shows the key inlet/outlet energy streams, the assumptions enforced, and the resulting state transition on the property plane.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[INLET STATE 1\nHigh Pressure P1\nTemperature T1\nSpecific Enthalpy h1] --> B1[THROTTLING VALVE\nControl Volume\nAdiabatic Q=0\nNo Shaft Work W=0\nNegligible Delta KE\nNegligible Delta PE]
    B1 --> C1[OUTLET STATE 2\nLow Pressure P2 less than P1\nTemperature T2\nSpecific Enthalpy h2 = h1\nSpecific Entropy s2 greater than s1]
    B1 -.-> D1[INTERNAL FRICTION\nCauses Entropy Generation\nDelta s greater than 0\nIrreversible Process]
    B1 -.-> E1[SFEE GOVERNING LAW\nh1 equals h2]
    C1 --> F1[APPLICATIONS\nRefrigeration Expansion\nSteam Turbine Governor\nGas Pressure Regulation\nCryogenic Liquefaction]
```

## 4.2 Throttling in a Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (Block Architecture)

This block diagram maps the placement of the throttling valve within a complete refrigeration loop, illustrating the broader engineering context.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[COMPRESSOR\nLow Pressure Vapour\nTo High Pressure Vapour\nWork Input Wc] --> N2[CONDENSER\nHigh Pressure Vapour\nRejects Heat Qout\nBecomes High Pressure Liquid]
    N2 --> N3[THROTTLING VALVE\nHigh Pressure Liquid\nTo Low Pressure Two Phase Mixture\nh1 equals h2 ISENTHALPIC\nEntropy Increases]
    N3 --> N4[EVAPORATOR\nLow Pressure Two Phase\nAbsorbs Heat Qin\nBecomes Low Pressure Vapour]
    N4 --> N1
    N3 -. THROTTLING ACTION .-> M3[Cold Refrigerant\nEnters Evaporator\nTemperature Drops\nEnables Heat Absorption]
```

## 4.3 State Transition on the T-s Diagram (Schematic)

The schematic below shows how state 1 transitions to state 2 during throttling on a $T$-$s$ diagram. Note the process is a *vertical drop in $h$*, not in $s$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Isobar P1 High Pressure Curve"] --> P2["Isobar P2 Low Pressure Curve"]
    P1 --> S1["STATE 1\nT1 h1 s1\nIntersection of P1 and T1"]
    S1 --> S2["STATE 2\nT2 h2 = h1 s2 greater than s1\nIntersection of P2 and h = h1"]
    P2 --> S2
    S1 -. ISENTHALPIC h constant .-> S2
    S1 -. IRREVERSIBLE s increases .-> S2
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Joule–Thomson Coefficient Sign and Temperature Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Fluid Enters Throttling Valve] --> R2{Fluid Type?}
    R2 -- Ideal Gas --> R3[T2 equals T1\nDelta T equals 0\nmu JT equals 0\nNo Cooling No Heating]
    R2 -- Real Gas Above Inversion Temperature --> R4[T2 greater than T1\nHEATING occurs\nmu JT less than 0]
    R2 -- Real Gas Below Inversion Temperature --> R5[T2 less than T1\nCOOLING occurs\nmu JT greater than 0]
    R3 --> R6[Application: Pressure Regulation Only]
    R4 --> R7[Example: Helium at Room Temperature]
    R5 --> R8[Examples: Steam CO2 Nitrogen at Room Temperature\nUsed in Refrigeration and Cryogenics]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** When asked to "sketch the throttling process on a $T$-$s$ diagram", draw a curve that goes from a point on the higher isobar *downward and to the right* (entropy increases) to a point on the lower isobar at the same enthalpy value. Do **not** draw a straight vertical line (that is an isentropic process, not isenthalpic). The horizontal direction represents the entropy increase due to irreversibility.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
> **"What is a throttling process? State the basic assumption on which the throttling process is analysed."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A throttling process is a steady-flow, adiabatic expansion process in which a fluid experiences a significant pressure drop while flowing through a restriction such as a valve, orifice, or porous plug, without any work being done on or by the fluid.

**Basic assumption:** The throttling process is assumed to be **isenthalpic**, i.e., the specific enthalpy of the fluid remains constant across the valve:

$$h_1 = h_2$$

This assumption is derived from the steady-flow energy equation by setting $Q = 0$, $W = 0$, and neglecting changes in kinetic and potential energies. **(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the identity, 1 mark for listing the SFEE simplifications.)**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **"State whether the throttling process is reversible or irreversible. Justify your answer with reference to entropy change."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The throttling process is **irreversible**.

**Justification:**
- The fluid experiences unrestrained expansion through a restriction with substantial viscous and frictional dissipation.
- No work is extracted; the lost pressure energy is dissipated as internal (thermal) energy of the fluid.
- Consequently, the specific entropy of the fluid **increases**:

$$s_2 > s_1 \quad \text{or} \quad \Delta s > 0$$

- For a reversible process, $\Delta s = 0$, which is not the case here.

Therefore, throttling is an inherently irreversible steady-flow process. **(3 marks — 1 mark for stating irreversible, 1 mark for the entropy inequality, 1 mark for physical justification.)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is typically split into two sub-parts: **Part (a) — 7 marks** and **Part (b) — 7 marks**. Students must answer EITHER Option A OR Option B.

---

### OPTION A — Question Set A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **(a)** Derive the steady-flow energy equation for a throttling valve and show that the process is isenthalpic. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Steam at **15 bar** and **300 °C** is throttled to a pressure of **2 bar**. Find the temperature, enthalpy, entropy, and dryness fraction of the steam at the exit. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Write the general SFEE for a control volume enclosing the throttling valve:**

$$Q_{cv} - W_{cv} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply throttling assumptions:**

- Adiabatic: $Q_{cv} = 0$ (process too fast for heat transfer).
- No shaft work: $W_{cv} = 0$ (no moving boundary).
- Negligible kinetic energy change: $\Delta KE \approx 0$.
- Negligible potential energy change: $\Delta PE \approx 0$.

**Step 3 — Simplify the equation:**

$$0 = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$$

**Step 4 — Conclude:**

$$h_1 = h_2$$

This proves that the throttling process is **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy process). **(7 marks — 1 mark for SFEE, 2 marks for assumptions, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for conclusion.)**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State 1: From superheated steam tables at $P_1 = 15\,\text{bar}$ and $T_1 = 300\,°\text{C}$:**

$$h_1 = 3037.6\,\text{kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 = 7.0229\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply isenthalpic condition:**

$$h_2 = h_1 = 3037.6\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — State 2 at $P_2 = 2\,\text{bar}$:** From saturated steam tables at 2 bar:

$$h_f = 504.7\,\text{kJ/kg}, \quad h_g = 2706.7\,\text{kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2202.0\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

Since $h_2 = 3037.6\,\text{kJ/kg} > h_g = 2706.7\,\text{kJ/kg}$, the exit steam is **superheated**.

**Step 4 — From the 2 bar superheated steam table, interpolating for $h_2 = 3037.6\,\text{kJ/kg}$:**

$$T_2 \approx 209.5\,°\text{C}, \quad s_2 \approx 7.4914\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 5 — Dryness fraction:** Since the exit state is superheated, the steam is **dry and superheated** (not a two-phase mixture), hence the dryness fraction is **not applicable** ($x_2$ is not defined for superheated steam). If the question insists, the conceptual dryness fraction is $x_2 = 1$ (dry vapour basis) with the additional superheat quantified by $(T_2 - T_{sat,2})$.

**Final Answer:**
- $T_2 \approx 209.5\,°\text{C}$
- $h_2 = 3037.6\,\text{kJ/kg}$
- $s_2 \approx 7.4914\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Steam is superheated at exit.

**(7 marks — 2 marks for state 1, 1 mark for isenthalpic identity, 2 marks for state 2 lookup, 2 marks for final results.)**

---

### OPTION B — Question Set B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the throttling process and show it on a $T$-$s$ diagram. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Saturated liquid refrigerant **R-134a** at **10 bar** is throttled to a pressure of **2 bar**. Calculate the temperature at the exit and the quality of the refrigerant at the exit. Take $h_f$ at 10 bar = **256.41 kJ/kg** and $h_{fg}$ at 2 bar = **214.48 kJ/kg**, $h_{f}$ at 2 bar = **36.84 kJ/kg**. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition:** A throttling process is a steady-flow, adiabatic, irreversible expansion through a restriction, with no work transfer and negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Governing equation:** $h_1 = h_2$ (isenthalpic). **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Sketch on $T$-$s$ diagram:**

- Draw the saturation dome with the critical point at the top.
- Mark state 1 on the higher pressure isobar $P_1$ (could be in subcooled, saturated, or superheated region).
- Draw a constant-enthalpy line from state 1 (NOT a vertical line).
- Locate state 2 where this constant-$h$ line intersects the lower pressure isobar $P_2$.
- State 2 lies to the *right* of state 1, indicating $s_2 > s_1$. **(3 marks)**

**Step 4 — Key features:**

- $\Delta h = 0$
- $\Delta s > 0$ (irreversibility)
- Pressure drops, temperature usually drops for real fluids below inversion temperature.
- Process is non-quasi-static. **(2 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State 1: Saturated liquid R-134a at $P_1 = 10\,\text{bar}$:**

Since it is saturated liquid, $x_1 = 0$, hence:

$$h_1 = h_{f,10\,\text{bar}} = 256.41\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Apply isenthalpic identity:**

$$h_2 = h_1 = 256.41\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — At $P_2 = 2\,\text{bar}$, the state is in the two-phase region:**

$$h_f = 36.84\,\text{kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 214.48\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

$$h_g = h_f + h_{fg} = 36.84 + 214.48 = 251.32\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

Since $h_2 = 256.41\,\text{kJ/kg} > h_g = 251.32\,\text{kJ/kg}$, the refrigerant at the exit is **slightly superheated**.

**Step 4 — Temperature at exit:**

For a slightly superheated refrigerant at 2 bar, the saturation temperature is approximately:

$$T_2 \approx T_{sat,2\,\text{bar}} \approx -10.09\,°\text{C}$$

(For KTU valuation, using $T_{sat,2\,\text{bar}} \approx -10\,°\text{C}$ is acceptable. Refined value from superheated R-134a tables gives $T_2 \approx -8.5\,°\text{C}$.) **(3 marks)**

**Step 5 — Quality (dryness fraction):**

Since the exit is superheated, $x_2$ is technically not applicable. However, if the problem intends for the student to compute the equivalent two-phase quality on the saturated line:

$$x_{2,\text{equivalent}} = \frac{h_2 - h_{f,2\,\text{bar}}}{h_{fg,2\,\text{bar}}} = \frac{256.41 - 36.84}{214.48} = 1.024 \approx 1.02$$

This confirms the refrigerant is superheated by a small margin (about 2.4% above $h_g$). **(4 marks)**

**Final Answer:**
- $T_2 \approx -10\,°\text{C}$ (saturated temperature; actual slightly higher due to superheat)
- $x_2 = 1.02$ (effectively superheated, > 1)
- The refrigerant cools dramatically during throttling — this is the basis of refrigeration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing $h_1 = h_2$ without derivation:** Examiners expect the full SFEE simplification. Simply stating the identity without showing the four assumptions ($Q=0$, $W=0$, $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$) costs **at least 2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing isenthalpic with isentropic:** Many students write "the throttling process is isentropic" and compute $\Delta s = 0$. This is **wrong**. The process is isenthalpic; $\Delta s > 0$.
> 3. **Forgetting to use property tables:** Without a property chart, the exit state cannot be determined. Students who write $T_2 = T_1$ for steam (ideal gas assumption) lose 2–3 marks. Always use steam tables.
> 4. **Sign errors in Joule–Thomson coefficient:** The Joule–Thomson coefficient is defined as $\mu_{JT} = (\partial T / \partial P)_h$. Some students invert it. Memorize the form.
> 5. **Dryness fraction > 1 mishandling:** When $h_2 > h_{g,P_2}$, the fluid is superheated, and $x$ is undefined. Do not write $x_2 > 1$ as a final answer; instead, conclude that the fluid is superheated and quote $T_2$ from the superheated table.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Throttling is a **steady-flow, adiabatic, irreversible** expansion through a restriction with **no work** and negligible $\Delta KE$, $\Delta PE$. **(High-frequency KTU question)**
- **Core identity:** $h_1 = h_2$ — derived from the SFEE with the four simplifying assumptions.
- **Not isentropic:** $\Delta s > 0$ always. The process is *isenthalpic*, not isentropic.
- **Ideal gas behaviour:** For an ideal gas, throttling causes **no temperature change** ($\mu_{JT} = 0$).
- **Real gas behaviour:** $\mu_{JT} \neq 0$; sign determined by inversion temperature. Most real gases (steam, CO$_2$, N$_2$ at room temperature) cool on throttling.
- **Process diagram on $T$-$s$:** Draw a curve sloping *down and to the right* (entropy increases). NOT a straight vertical line.
- **Property chart usage:** Locate the exit state at the intersection of $h = h_1$ and $P = P_2$ on the relevant Mollier diagram or steam table.
- **Engineering applications:** Refrigeration expansion valves, steam turbine throttle valves, gas pressure regulators, cryogenic liquefaction.
- **Key equation (Joule–Thomson):** $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right]$.
- **Quality calculation:** If $h_2 < h_{g,P_2}$ (two-phase region), then $x_2 = \dfrac{h_2 - h_{f,P_2}}{h_{fg,P_2}}$. If $h_2 \geq h_{g,P_2}$, the fluid is superheated and $x_2$ is undefined.
- **Units check:** Enthalpy in kJ/kg, entropy in kJ/(kg·K), pressure in kPa or bar, temperature in K or °C.
- **Continuity equation (often tested alongside):** $\dot{m} = \dfrac{A_1 V_1}{v_1} = \dfrac{A_2 V_2}{v_2}$ for steady flow.
- **Reversibility checkmark:** If asked "is throttling reversible?", always answer **NO** with the entropy justification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixing Chambers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Mixing Chambers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mixing Chamber:** A **steady-flow, open system** device in which **two or more fluid streams** (gases, vapours, or liquids) entering at **different thermodynamic states** (different temperatures, pressures, compositions, or velocities) are **combined and homogenized** to yield a **single, uniform exit stream** at an intermediate equilibrium state, without the performance of shaft work and typically without significant heat transfer to the surroundings. A mixing chamber is treated as a **control volume** under the **First Law of Thermodynamics for Open Systems** (Steady Flow Energy Equation – SFEE). It is one of the canonical "steady-flow devices" taught alongside nozzles, diffusers, compressors, turbines, throttling valves, and heat exchangers. > [!NOTE] > **Classification of Mixing Chambers by Energy Exchange** > - **Adiabatic Mixing Chamber (AMC):** Perfectly insulated; $Q = 0$. The most common case examined in KTU examinations. > - **Diabatic / Non-Adiabatic Mixing Chamber:** Allows heat loss/gain with surroundings; $Q \neq 0$. Treated with a heat-loss term in the energy equation. > - **Direct-Contact Heat Exchanger:** A mixing chamber whose *primary purpose* is heat transfer between two streams rather than mass blending (e.g., cooling tower, desuperheater). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Real-World Intuition Imagine a **kitchen washbasin** in a Kerala household where: - The **hot water tap** delivers water at **60 °C** (mass flow $\dot{m}_1$). - The **cold water tap** delivers water at **25 °C** (mass flow $\dot{m}_2$). - Both streams fall into a **common basin** and mix to give a single outflow at some **comfortable temperature $T_3$** (say 38 °C) before draining. The **basin itself is the "mixing chamber."** It: 1. Receives multiple inlet streams (different states). 2. Loses some heat to the surroundings (basin walls & air) — the *diabatic* case, OR is perfectly insulated — the *adiabatic* case. 3. Produces a single, well-mixed outlet stream. The exact same principle is used in: - **Steam power plant desuperheaters** (mixing cooler steam with water to regulate turbine inlet temperature). - **Jet engine afterburners / mixing chambers** (combining bypass air with hot core exhaust). - **Air-conditioning mixing boxes** (blending return air with fresh outside air). - **Industrial chemical reactors** where reactants at different temperatures are blended. The key engineering question is: *Given the inlet states, what is the exit temperature (or enthalpy, pressure, composition) of the combined stream?* > [!TIP] > **Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Favourite):** In an adiabatic mixing chamber, the *outlet temperature* is always a **mass-flow-weighted average** of the inlet temperatures — *never* a simple arithmetic mean. This single statement carries the most marks in short-answer questions. ## 1.3 Key Physical Assumptions (Standard KTU Working Model) For a typical exam problem, the following assumptions are adopted unless stated otherwise: | Assumption | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Steady Flow | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}(\text{property}) = 0$ | Inlet/outlet conditions do not change with time | | Adiabatic Walls | $\dot{Q} = 0$ | Chamber is perfectly insulated | | No Shaft Work | $\dot{W}_{sh} = 0$ | No turbine/compressor inside chamber | | Negligible $\Delta KE$ | $\Delta V^2/2 \approx 0$ | Velocity head change is small | | Negligible $\Delta PE$ | $g\Delta z \approx 0$ | Chamber is horizontal & compact | | Uniform Mixing | $T_{out}, h_{out}$ constant across exit | Exit stream is homogeneous | > [!IMPORTANT] > **State Property of the Outlet Stream:** Because the exit fluid is at a *single, well-defined* thermodynamic state (one $T$, one $P$, one $h$, one $v$), the mixing process is internally irreversible, but the inlet and exit states are each equilibrium states suitable for property evaluation from steam tables or ideal-gas relations. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Structured Step-by-Step Reasoning A mixing chamber problem is solved by applying **two conservation laws** simultaneously to the chosen control volume: ### Step 1: Draw the Control Volume (Schematic) Sketch the chamber with **all inlet streams** clearly labelled (subscripts 1, 2, …) and the **single exit stream** (subscript *m* or *out*). Note the directions of flow arrows, the location of insulation, and the boundary for $Q$ and $W$. ### Step 2: Apply Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation) > [!NOTE] > **Mass Balance Statement:** *Total mass entering the control volume per unit time = Total mass leaving per unit time.* For steady flow with $N$ inlet streams and a single exit: $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dot{m}_i = \dot{m}_{out}$$ For the most common two-inlet case: $$\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}_3$$ ### Step 3: Apply Conservation of Energy (SFEE) The Steady Flow Energy Equation (per unit time) for a control volume with $N$ inlets, one outlet, heat transfer $\dot{Q}$, and shaft work $\dot{W}_{sh}$: $$\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_{sh} = \dot{m}_{out} h_{out} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dot{m}_i h_i + \left[\sum \dot{m} \dfrac{V^2}{2} + \dot{m} g z \right]_{out}^{in}$$ For the **standard adiabatic mixing chamber** ($\dot{Q} = 0, \dot{W}_{sh} = 0, \Delta KE \approx 0, \Delta PE \approx 0$): $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dot{m}_i h_i = \dot{m}_{out} \, h_{out}$$ Substituting $\dot{m}_{out} = \sum \dot{m}_i$ and solving for $h_{out}$: $$h_{out} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dot{m}_i h_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dot{m}_i}$$ ### Step 4: Retrieve the Outlet State The unknown is **typically $T_{out}$ or $h_{out}$** (and sometimes $P_{out}$ or quality $x$). After computing $h_{out}$ from the energy balance, refer to steam tables (or ideal-gas relations: $h = c_p T$) to back-calculate the corresponding temperature or quality. ### Step 5: The Mass-Fraction Form (Often Useful) Dividing numerator and denominator by the total mass flow $\dot{m}_{total} = \sum \dot{m}_i$: $$h_{out} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \, h_i \quad \text{where} \quad \xi_i = \dfrac{\dot{m}_i}{\dot{m}_{total}} \quad \text{and} \quad \sum \xi_i = 1$$ Here $\xi_i$ is the **mass fraction** of the $i^{th}$ stream. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the equations below — they are the high-yield KTU formulas for Mixing Chambers.** | # | Governing Equation | Working Form (Two Inlets, Adiabatic) | When to Use | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Continuity (Mass Balance) | $\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}_3$ | Every mixing problem | | 2 | SFEE (Adiabatic, no work) | $\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2 = \dot{m}_3 h_3$ | Default textbook case | | 3 | Outlet Enthalpy | $h_3 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2}$ | Solve for $h_3$ first | | 4 | Mass-Weighted $T$ (Ideal Gas) | $T_3 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 T_1 + \dot{m}_2 T_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2}$ | When $c_p$ is constant | | 5 | Mass-Weighted $T$ (Variable $c_p$) | $T_3 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 c_{p,1} T_1 + \dot{m}_2 c_{p,2} T_2}{\dot{m}_1 c_{p,1} + \dot{m}_2 c_{p,2}}$ | Different gases with different $c_p$ | | 6 | Non-Adiabatic SFEE | $\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2 = \dot{m}_3 h_3 + \dot{Q}_{out}$ | Heat loss to surroundings given | | 7 | Energy Balance per Unit Mass | $h_1 + r h_2 = (1+r) h_3$ | Where $r = \dot{m}_2 / \dot{m}_1$ (ratio form) | | 8 | Mass Fraction | $\xi_i = \dot{m}_i / \dot{m}_{total}, \quad \sum \xi_i = 1$ | Composition or weighted avg. | | 9 | Two-Stream Temp Relation | $T_3 - T_1 = r(T_2 - T_3)$ | Quick check or ratio problems | | 10 | Mixing of Air–Steam | $h_3 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a c_{pa} T_a + \dot{m}_s h_s}{\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_s}$ | Air-conditioning / desuperheater | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder on the Pipe Character $\vert$:** In the formulas above, you may see absolute values or ratios — these are typed with `\vert` or `\mid` inside LaTeX (e.g., $c_p \mid_{T}$) so that the markdown table structure is never broken. KTU examiners deduct marks for sloppy notation in equations. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Role of Mixing Chamber | | :--- | :--- | | **Thermal Power Plants** | Deaerators, desuperheaters, feedwater heaters (open type) | | **HVAC & Refrigeration** | Air-handling units (AHU) blending return and fresh air | | **Gas Turbines / Jet Engines** | Bypass-to-core flow mixing in the exhaust nozzle | | **Chemical Industry** | Blending of process streams at different temperatures | | **Petrochemical Refineries** | Mixing of hydrocarbon fractions to obtain desired API/density | | **Cryogenics & LNG** | Blending of LNG streams for pipeline specification | | **District Cooling** | Mixing chilled water loops of different temperatures | The mixing chamber is also a **textbook example of an internally irreversible process** because the kinetic energy of fluid particles is dissipated into thermal internal energy as the streams mingle — yet the *First Law* still requires only the inlet/outlet equilibrium states, and this elegance is what makes it a favourite KTU problem. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Outlet Enthalpy for an Adiabatic Two-Stream Mixing Chamber We begin from the **First Law of Thermodynamics applied to an open system (control volume)** in steady state, with two inlets (1, 2) and a single outlet (3): $$\dfrac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} + \sum_{in} \dot{m}_i \left(h_i + \dfrac{V_i^2}{2} + g z_i\right) - \sum_{out} \dot{m}_e \left(h_e + \dfrac{V_e^2}{2} + g z_e\right)$$ **Assumption 1 — Steady State:** $\dfrac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$. **Assumption 2 — Adiabatic Walls:** $\dot{Q}_{cv} = 0$. **Assumption 3 — No Shaft Work:** $\dot{W}_{cv} = 0$ (no turbine/compressor/fan inside). **Assumption 4 — Negligible $\Delta KE$ and $\Delta PE$:** $\Delta V^2/2 \approx 0$ and $g\Delta z \approx 0$. Substituting all four assumptions into the governing equation, and noting that the exit stream carries a single state, the equation reduces to: $$0 = 0 - 0 + \dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2 - \dot{m}_3 h_3$$ Rearranging (mass-energy balance for adiabatic mixing): $$\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2 = \dot{m}_3 h_3$$ **Continuity Equation (Independent):** For steady flow, $\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}_3$. Substitute this into the energy balance: $$\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2 = \left(\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2\right) h_3$$ Solving explicitly for the unknown outlet enthalpy $h_3$: $$h_3 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2} \quad \text{...(Master Equation)}$$ This is the **fundamental governing relation** for an adiabatic mixing chamber. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Mass-Weighted Temperature Formula (Ideal Gas with Constant $c_p$) For an ideal gas, the specific enthalpy depends only on temperature: $$h = c_p T$$ Substituting into the master equation: $$c_p T_3 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 c_p T_1 + \dot{m}_2 c_p T_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2}$$ The $c_p$ cancels on both sides (assuming **same fluid**, identical $c_p$): $$T_3 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 T_1 + \dot{m}_2 T_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2}$$ > [!NOTE] > This proves the *Rule of Thumb* stated in Section 1.2: the exit temperature is a **mass-flow-weighted average** of the inlet temperatures, NOT a simple arithmetic average. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Mass-Ratio Form (Useful in Exam Substitution) Define the **mass flow ratio** as: $$r = \dfrac{\dot{m}_2}{\dot{m}_1}$$ Then the continuity equation gives: $$\dot{m}_3 = \dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}_1(1 + r)$$ The energy balance becomes: $$\dot{m}_1 h_1 + r\dot{m}_1 h_2 = \dot{m}_1(1+r) h_3$$ Dividing throughout by $\dot{m}_1$: $$h_1 + r h_2 = (1+r) h_3$$ Solving for $h_3$: $$h_3 = \dfrac{h_1 + r h_2}{1 + r}$$ This compact form is often the *most convenient* for KTU board substitution. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (Step-by-Step Solutions) > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** Most 14-mark problems involve steam mixing (with quality $x$ or superheat) and require *two iterations* of steam-table lookups: one to get $h_1, h_2$ from given $T_1, x_1, T_2, x_2$, then another to get $T_3$ from the computed $h_3$ at the exit pressure. ### **Example 1 — Steam Mixing (The Classic KTU Problem)** **Problem:** Steam enters an adiabatic mixing chamber at two different states: - Stream 1: $\dot{m}_1 = 3 \text{ kg/s}, \quad P_1 = 1 \text{ MPa}, \quad T_1 = 200 \text{ °C}$ - Stream 2: $\dot{m}_2 = 5 \text{ kg/s}, \quad P_2 = 1 \text{ MPa}, \quad x_2 = 0.9 \text{ (wet steam)}$ The exit pressure is $P_3 = 1 \text{ MPa}$. Determine the **exit temperature $T_3$** and the **exit quality (if saturated)**. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify the State of Stream 1.** At $P_1 = 1 \text{ MPa}$, the saturation temperature is $T_{sat} = 179.9 \text{ °C}$. Since $T_1 = 200 \text{ °C} > T_{sat}$, stream 1 is **superheated steam**. From superheated steam tables: $h_1 = 2827.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **Step 2 — Identify the State of Stream 2.** At $P_2 = 1 \text{ MPa}$ and $x_2 = 0.9$: $h_f = 762.81 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2015.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (standard table values). $h_2 = h_f + x_2 h_{fg} = 762.81 + (0.9)(2015.3) = 762.81 + 1813.77 = 2576.58 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **Step 3 — Apply Mass Balance.** $\dot{m}_3 = \dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2 = 3 + 5 = 8 \text{ kg/s}$. **Step 4 — Apply Energy Balance (Adiabatic SFEE).** $\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2 = \dot{m}_3 h_3$ $(3)(2827.9) + (5)(2576.58) = (8) h_3$ $8483.7 + 12882.9 = 8 h_3$ $21366.6 = 8 h_3$ $h_3 = 2670.825 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **Step 5 — Determine the State of Stream 3.** At $P_3 = 1 \text{ MPa}$, we have: $h_f = 762.81 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_g = 2778.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Since $h_f < h_3 = 2670.825 < h_g$, the exit stream is a **wet mixture** (saturated liquid–vapour). The quality is: $$x_3 = \dfrac{h_3 - h_f}{h_{fg}} = \dfrac{2670.825 - 762.81}{2015.3} = \dfrac{1908.015}{2015.3} = 0.9468$$ The temperature is the saturation temperature at 1 MPa: $$T_3 = T_{sat}(1 \text{ MPa}) = 179.9 \text{ °C}$$ **Final Answer:** $T_3 = 179.9 \text{ °C}$, $x_3 = 0.947$, $\dot{m}_3 = 8 \text{ kg/s}$. --- ### **Example 2 — Air Mixing (Different Mass Flows and Temperatures)** **Problem:** Two air streams enter an adiabatic mixing chamber used in an air-conditioning system: - Stream 1 (return air): $\dot{m}_1 = 4 \text{ kg/s}, \quad T_1 = 30 \text{ °C}$ - Stream 2 (fresh air): $\dot{m}_2 = 1 \text{ kg/s}, \quad T_2 = 40 \text{ °C}$ Assume air behaves as an ideal gas with $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. Find the exit temperature. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Mass Balance.** $\dot{m}_3 = \dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2 = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ kg/s}$. **Step 2 — Energy Balance.** Using the ideal-gas form with $c_p$ constant (same fluid), temperatures in Kelvin: $T_1 = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \text{ K}$ $T_2 = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \text{ K}$ $$T_3 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 T_1 + \dot{m}_2 T_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2} = \dfrac{(4)(303.15) + (1)(313.15)}{4 + 1} = \dfrac{1212.6 + 313.15}{5} = \dfrac{1525.75}{5} = 305.15 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to °C.** $T_3 = 305.15 - 273.15 = 32 \text{ °C}$. **Final Answer:** $T_3 = 32 \text{ °C}$. > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** The exit temperature (32 °C) lies *between* 30 °C and 40 °C, and is *closer to 30 °C* because stream 1 has the larger mass flow rate. This confirms the mass-weighted averaging logic. --- ### **Example 3 — Non-Adiabatic Mixing with Heat Loss** **Problem:** A steam mixer receives: - Stream 1: $\dot{m}_1 = 2 \text{ kg/s}, \quad P_1 = 0.5 \text{ MPa}, \quad x_1 = 0.95$ - Stream 2: $\dot{m}_2 = 3 \text{ kg/s}, \quad P_2 = 0.5 \text{ MPa}, \quad T_2 = 250 \text{ °C}$ Heat is lost from the chamber at a rate $\dot{Q}_{out} = 200 \text{ kW}$. Find the exit enthalpy and temperature at $P_3 = 0.5 \text{ MPa}$. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Steam-Table Lookups.** At 0.5 MPa: $h_f = 640.23 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2108.5 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_g = 2748.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$. $h_1 = 640.23 + (0.95)(2108.5) = 640.23 + 2003.075 = 2643.305 \text{ kJ/kg}$. $h_2$: Superheated at 0.5 MPa, 250 °C: $h_2 = 2960.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **Step 2 — Mass Balance.** $\dot{m}_3 = 2 + 3 = 5 \text{ kg/s}$. **Step 3 — Modified Energy Balance (with heat loss).** $\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2 = \dot{m}_3 h_3 + \dot{Q}_{out}$ $(2)(2643.305) + (3)(2960.7) = (5) h_3 + 200$ $5286.61 + 8882.1 = 5 h_3 + 200$ $14168.71 - 200 = 5 h_3$ $5 h_3 = 13968.71$ $h_3 = 2793.74 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **Step 4 — State at 0.5 MPa.** Since $h_3 = 2793.74 > h_g = 2748.7$, the exit stream is **superheated**. From superheated steam tables at 0.5 MPa: - At 200 °C: $h = 2855.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - At 150 °C: $h = 2780.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$ Linear interpolation: $T_3 = 150 + \dfrac{2793.74 - 2780.1}{2855.4 - 2780.1} \times 50 = 150 + \dfrac{13.64}{75.3} \times 50 = 150 + 9.06 = 159.06 \text{ °C}$. **Final Answer:** $h_3 = 2793.74 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad T_3 \approx 159 \text{ °C}$ (superheated). --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula | Given Quantity | Unknown | Formula Path | Steam-Table Strategy | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\dot{m}_1, T_1, \dot{m}_2, T_2$, same gas | $T_3$ | $T_3 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 T_1 + \dot{m}_2 T_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2}$ | No tables needed | | $\dot{m}_1, h_1, \dot{m}_2, h_2$ | $h_3$ | $h_3 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2}$ | Look up $h \to T$ or $x$ | | $\dot{m}_1, h_1, \dot{m}_2, h_2$, exit $P_3$ | $T_3$ or $x_3$ | $h_3 \to$ state at $P_3$ | One lookup in tables | | Two moist streams | $h_3$ | Use $h_f + x h_{fg}$ for each | Two lookups for $h_1, h_2$ | | $\dot{m}_1, h_1, \dot{m}_2, h_2, \dot{Q}_{out}, h_3$ | $\dot{m}_1$ (or $\dot{m}_2$) | Solve mass balance + energy | Algebraic | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic of a Generic Two-Stream Adiabatic Mixing Chamber ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Stream 1 Inlet
m1, h1, T1, P1"] --> CV["Adiabatic Mixing Chamber
Insulated Walls
Q = 0, W = 0"] A2["Stream 2 Inlet
m2, h2, T2, P2"] --> CV CV --> B1["Stream 3 Outlet
m3, h3, T3, P3"] CV -.->|"Heat Loss Q_out
(only in diabatic case)"| L1["Surroundings
at T_surr"] classDef inletStyle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef chamberStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef outletStyle fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lossStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:1px,color:#000,stroke-dasharray:5 5 class A1,A2 inletStyle class CV chamberStyle class B1 outletStyle class L1 lossStyle ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Control-Volume Mapping) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BCV["BOUNDARY OF CONTROL VOLUME"] direction TB I1["INLET 1
Mass: m1
Energy: m1 h1
Surroundings blocked"] I2["INLET 2
Mass: m2
Energy: m2 h2
Surroundings blocked"] O1["OUTLET 3
Mass: m3 = m1 + m2
Energy: m3 h3
Equilibrium state"] CORE["INSIDE THE CV
Turbulent mixing zone
Pressure equalization
Temperature equalization
Internal irreversibilities"] I1 --> CORE I2 --> CORE CORE --> O1 end HEAT["HEAT Q (if any) crosses BCV"] -.-> BCV WORK["WORK W (always 0 here) crosses BCV"] -.-> BCV classDef in1Style fill:#b6d7ff,stroke:#0b3d91,stroke-width:2px classDef in2Style fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#a85b00,stroke-width:2px classDef outStyle fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px classDef coreStyle fill:#fff7a8,stroke:#7a5b00,stroke-width:3px classDef boundaryStyle fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray:5 5 class I1 in1Style class I2 in2Style class O1 outStyle class CORE coreStyle class BCV boundaryStyle ``` ## 4.3 Information-Flow Diagram for Solving a Mixing-Chamber Problem ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["STEP 1: Draw CV and label all
inlet/outlet states"] --> S2["STEP 2: List all given data
m, P, T, x for each stream"] S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Apply Continuity
m3 = m1 + m2 + ..."] S3 --> S4["STEP 4: Apply SFEE
Sum m_i h_i = m_out h_out plus Q minus W"] S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Solve for h_out
h3 = weighted average"] S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Use steam tables / ideal gas law
to find T3 or x3 at P3"] S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Cross-check using mass-fraction
consistency and limits"] classDef stepStyle fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef finalStyle fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6 stepStyle class S7 finalStyle ``` ## 4.4 Schematic of a Desuperheater (Real Industrial Application) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Superheated Steam
P1, T1 (high)
m1, h1"] --> DV["Desuperheater
(Adiabatic Mixing Chamber)"] W1["Cooler Water
P2, T2 (low)
m2, h2"] --> DV DV --> OUT["Saturated or Slightly Wet Steam
P3, T3 = T_sat(P3)
m3 = m1 + m2"] classDef steamStyle fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef waterStyle fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outStyle fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef chamberStyle fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 class S1 steamStyle class W1 waterStyle class DV chamberStyle class OUT outStyle ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Note:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are alphanumeric prefixes (`A1`, `A2`, `BCV`, `CORE`, etc.) and *none* of them use reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` as standalone node names. Labels are kept as plain uppercase alphanumeric text without embedded markdown formatting, ensuring clean rendering in Mermaid 10+ engines. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Part A = 3 marks each (short answer, 1-2 paragraphs or a formula derivation), Part B = 14 marks each (full numerical with internal choice). --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define an adiabatic mixing chamber. Write the SFEE for a steady-flow adiabatic mixing chamber with two inlets and one outlet, clearly stating the assumptions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > An adiabatic mixing chamber is a steady-flow device in which two or more fluid streams at different thermodynamic states are combined to produce a single, well-mixed exit stream, with no heat transfer to the surroundings. **Assumptions:** 1. Steady flow conditions 2. Adiabatic walls ($\dot{Q} = 0$) 3. No shaft work ($\dot{W}_{sh} = 0$) 4. Negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy **SFEE:** $\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2 = \dot{m}_3 h_3$, with $\dot{m}_3 = \dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2$. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **State the mass-fraction form of the outlet enthalpy for a multi-stream adiabatic mixing chamber and explain its physical significance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The outlet enthalpy can be expressed as: $$h_{out} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i h_i, \quad \text{where} \quad \xi_i = \dfrac{\dot{m}_i}{\dot{m}_{total}} \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i = 1$$ **Physical Significance:** The mass fraction $\xi_i$ represents the contribution of the $i^{th}$ stream to the total mass flow. The exit enthalpy is essentially a **weighted average** of the inlet enthalpies, with the **mass fractions acting as weights**. This formulation is dimensionally consistent, makes the energy conservation explicit, and allows quick computation when mass ratios $r_i = \dot{m}_i / \dot{m}_1$ are given. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Full Internal Choice > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme provides internal choice on Part B. **Solve either Option A or Option B in full.** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — The KTU Favourite: Steam Mixing with Quality** > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Similar Pattern: June 2024]** **Statement:** Steam enters an adiabatic mixing chamber through two inlets: - **Inlet 1:** $\dot{m}_1 = 2 \text{ kg/s}$ at $P_1 = 2 \text{ MPa}$ and $T_1 = 300 \text{ °C}$ - **Inlet 2:** $\dot{m}_2 = 4 \text{ kg/s}$ at $P_2 = 2 \text{ MPa}$ and $x_2 = 0.85$ (saturated mixture) The exit pressure is $P_3 = 2 \text{ MPa}$. Determine: **(a)** The exit enthalpy $h_3$ and the exit temperature $T_3$ (7 marks) **(b)** The exit quality $x_3$ and verify whether the exit state is wet, dry-saturated, or superheated. State whether the process is thermodynamically possible. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** #### **Part (a) — Solution Steps** **Step 1:** Identify state of Inlet 1. At $P_1 = 2 \text{ MPa}$, $T_{sat} = 212.42 \text{ °C}$. Since $T_1 = 300 \text{ °C} > T_{sat}$, **superheated steam**. From superheated steam tables: $h_1 = 3023.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **Step 2:** Identify state of Inlet 2. At $P_2 = 2 \text{ MPa}$: $h_f = 908.79 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 1890.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$. $h_2 = 908.79 + (0.85)(1890.7) = 908.79 + 1607.095 = 2515.885 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **Step 3:** Continuity: $\dot{m}_3 = 2 + 4 = 6 \text{ kg/s}$. **Step 4:** SFEE (adiabatic): $\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2 = \dot{m}_3 h_3$. $(2)(3023.5) + (4)(2515.885) = 6 h_3$ $6047.0 + 10063.54 = 6 h_3$ $16110.54 = 6 h_3$ $h_3 = 2685.09 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **Step 5:** State at $P_3 = 2 \text{ MPa}$: $h_f = 908.79 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_g = 2799.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Since $h_f < h_3 < h_g$: **wet steam**. $T_3 = T_{sat}(2 \text{ MPa}) = 212.42 \text{ °C}$. #### **Part (b) — Solution Steps** **Step 1:** Compute exit quality: $x_3 = (h_3 - h_f)/h_{fg} = (2685.09 - 908.79)/1890.7 = 1776.30/1890.7 = 0.9396$. **Step 2:** Classify the state: Since $0 < x_3 = 0.9396 < 1$, the exit is a **saturated liquid–vapour mixture** (wet steam). **Step 3:** Verify thermodynamic possibility: The exit temperature (212.42 °C) lies between $T_1 = 300$ °C and $T_2 = 212.42$ °C (saturation), confirming the mixing reduces the temperature of the superheated stream by blending with the wet stream. Since $h_1, h_2, h_3$ are all bounded by physically admissible table values and the First Law is satisfied, the process is **thermodynamically feasible**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Do **not** compute exit temperature as an arithmetic mean — use **mass-weighted** averaging only after finding $h_3$. (Loses 2 marks if confused.) > - **Always check** whether $h_3$ lies below $h_g$ at exit pressure before assuming superheat. (Loses 1-2 marks for wrong classification.) > - **Steam-table lookups** must show the row (P, T or x) → $h$ to earn full marks; an unmarked numerical substitution is penalized. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Diabatic Mixing with Heat Loss and Air-Steam Blend** > **[KTU University Exam - June 2023 | Similar Pattern: Dec 2024]** **Statement:** A diabatic mixing chamber receives: - **Inlet 1 (air):** $\dot{m}_1 = 5 \text{ kg/s}, \quad T_1 = 80 \text{ °C}, \quad c_{p,1} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ - **Inlet 2 (steam):** $\dot{m}_2 = 2 \text{ kg/s}, \quad P_2 = 200 \text{ kPa}, \quad x_2 = 0.95$ The chamber loses heat to the surroundings at a rate $\dot{Q}_{out} = 150 \text{ kW}$, and the exit mixture is at $P_3 = 200 \text{ kPa}$. **(a)** Compute the exit enthalpy of the combined stream. (7 marks) **(b)** Determine the exit temperature $T_3$ of the resulting mixture and the state classification (superheated, saturated, or wet). (7 marks) **Model Solution:** #### **Part (a) — Solution Steps** **Step 1:** Inlet 1 enthalpy (ideal gas, with reference $T_{ref} = 0 \text{ °C}$): $h_1 = c_p T_1 = 1.005 \times 80 = 80.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **Step 2:** Inlet 2 enthalpy (wet steam at 200 kPa). At 200 kPa: $h_f = 504.70 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2201.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$. $h_2 = 504.70 + (0.95)(2201.9) = 504.70 + 2091.805 = 2596.505 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **Step 3:** Continuity: $\dot{m}_3 = 5 + 2 = 7 \text{ kg/s}$. **Step 4:** Diabatic SFEE: $\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2 = \dot{m}_3 h_3 + \dot{Q}_{out}$ $(5)(80.4) + (2)(2596.505) = (7) h_3 + 150$ $402.0 + 5193.01 = 7 h_3 + 150$ $5595.01 - 150 = 7 h_3$ $7 h_3 = 5445.01$ $h_3 = 777.86 \text{ kJ/kg}$. #### **Part (b) — Solution Steps** **Step 1:** At exit $P_3 = 200 \text{ kPa}$: $h_f = 504.70 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_g = 2706.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Since $h_3 = 777.86 > h_f$: **the mixture is in the two-phase region (wet).** **Step 2:** Compute the "equivalent" steam quality (treating the mixture as a single pseudo-fluid; note this is an exam-level approximation — for accurate analysis the air-steam mixture properties require psychrometric charts): $x_3 = (h_3 - h_f)/h_{fg} = (777.86 - 504.70)/2201.9 = 273.16/2201.9 = 0.1241$. **Step 3:** Exit temperature = saturation temperature at 200 kPa: $T_3 = T_{sat}(200 \text{ kPa}) = 120.23 \text{ °C}$. > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Note:** In strict KTU board evaluation, when an air stream and steam stream are mixed, the exit state is a **moist air + steam** combination best analysed via psychrometric charts. The numerical $x_3$ above is a single-property approximation; the full air-conditioning treatment is covered in dedicated HVAC modules. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Diabatic Case):** > - **Forgetting the $\dot{Q}_{out}$ term** in the SFEE is the single most common mistake — it costs **2-3 marks** instantly. > - Sign convention: $\dot{Q}_{out}$ is **positive** when heat leaves the CV (Kerala board convention matches Cengel). Some textbooks use $\dot{Q}_{in}$ — always read the problem statement carefully. > - Always confirm $h_3$ lies between $h_f$ and $h_g$ before computing $x_3$; otherwise the state is superheated and $T_3$ must be read from the superheated table. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Print this section. Read it 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.** - [ ] A **mixing chamber** is a steady-flow, adiabatic (usually), no-work device that blends multiple inlet streams into one exit stream. - [ ] Two governing laws: **Continuity** ($\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}$) and **SFEE** ($\sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} h_{out}$ for the adiabatic case). - [ ] Master equation: $h_{out} = \dfrac{\sum \dot{m}_i h_i}{\sum \dot{m}_i}$ — *mass-flow-weighted average of inlet enthalpies*. - [ ] For ideal gas with constant $c_p$: $T_{out} = \dfrac{\sum \dot{m}_i T_i}{\sum \dot{m}_i}$ (temperatures **in Kelvin**). - [ ] For **diabatic** (heat-loss) cases, the energy balance becomes $\sum \dot{m}_i h_i = \dot{m}_{out} h_{out} + \dot{Q}_{out}$. - [ ] The exit state is always at a **single, well-defined equilibrium** — read $T_3$ or $x_3$ from steam tables using the computed $h_3$ at $P_3$. - [ ] **Always check** whether $h_3 < h_f$ (subcooled), $h_f \leq h_3 \leq h_g$ (wet), or $h_3 > h_g$ (superheated) — and adjust the lookup strategy accordingly. - [ ] The mass-ratio form $h_3 = (h_1 + r h_2)/(1+r)$ with $r = \dot{m}_2/\dot{m}_1$ is the **fastest exam substitution** technique. - [ ] **Sign convention for $\dot{Q}$:** Heat *out* of CV is positive in Cengel's notation; always re-read the problem to avoid losing marks. - [ ] **Internal irreversibility** is high (turbulent dissipation) but the SFEE only needs inlet/outlet equilibrium states. - [ ] Real-world examples: **desuperheaters, AHU mixing boxes, deaerators, jet engine afterburners, feedwater heaters (open type)**. - [ ] The exit temperature is **always between** the inlet temperatures (no exceptions for two-stream adiabatic mixing of the same fluid). - [ ] The process is **isobaric** (constant pressure) only if stated; otherwise mixing of two streams at different pressures requires a stagnation check. - [ ] **Mixing of two different gases** (e.g., air + CO₂) needs separate $c_p$ values: $T_3 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 c_{p,1} T_1 + \dot{m}_2 c_{p,2} T_2}{\dot{m}_1 c_{p,1} + \dot{m}_2 c_{p,2}}$. - [ ] KTU exam tip: When $\dot{m}$ is in kg/s and $h$ in kJ/kg, $\dot{m} h$ comes out in **kW** — units must be consistent throughout the energy balance. ---

topics.title: Thermal Energy Reservoirs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermal Energy Reservoirs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Thermal Energy Reservoir (TER)** is defined as a hypothetical body with a comparatively very large heat capacity that can supply or absorb a finite amount of heat energy without undergoing any appreciable change in its own thermodynamic state, particularly its temperature.

Mathematically, this limiting behavior is expressed as:

$$
C_{T} \;\longrightarrow\; \infty \quad \text{as} \quad T \;=\; \text{constant}
$$

where $C_T$ represents the thermal capacity (heat capacity) of the reservoir at temperature $T$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> A thermal energy reservoir is a sufficiently large system that can absorb or reject an unlimited amount of heat without experiencing any change in its macroscopic properties such as temperature, pressure, or phase.

### 1.2 Classification of Thermal Energy Reservoirs

Thermal energy reservoirs are broadly classified into two fundamental categories based on the direction of heat transfer relative to the system under analysis:

| **Reservoir Type** | **Symbol** | **Function** | **Temperature Behavior** |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Source / Hot Reservoir | $T_H$ | Supplies heat to the system | Remains effectively constant at a high value |
| Sink / Cold Reservoir | $T_L$ | Absorbs heat from the system | Remains effectively constant at a low value |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Infinite Bank Account of Heat":**
> Imagine an ocean as a colossal bank account of thermal energy. If you withdraw **₹100** (a small amount of heat) from an account containing **₹10 billion**, your balance (temperature) does not change. Likewise, a thermal energy reservoir is a body so massive in heat capacity that any heat exchange with a typical engineering system produces a *negligibly small* temperature change.

**Additional Intuitive Examples:**
- **Source Examples:** The Sun, the Earth's atmosphere (on a large time-scale), nuclear reactor cores, large furnaces, and combustion chambers.
- **Sink Examples:** The Earth's atmosphere acting as a heat dump, river water, ocean surfaces, and cryogenic liquid nitrogen baths.

### 1.4 Idealizations Embedded in the Concept

> [!CAUTION]
> **No real system is a perfect thermal energy reservoir.** Real bodies always undergo some finite (though possibly small) temperature change. The TER is an *idealization* — a conceptual tool used to simplify the boundary analysis of heat engines, refrigerators, and heat pumps.

The magnitude of the heat capacity required is often expressed as:

$$
Q \;=\; \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} C_T \, dT
$$

If $\Delta T \;\rightarrow\; 0$, then $C_T \;\rightarrow\; \infty$, confirming the idealization.

---

### 1.5 Boundary Work and Heat Interaction Restrictions

A thermal energy reservoir is allowed to engage in only one mode of energy interaction with its surroundings:

$$
W_{\text{boundary}} \;=\; 0 \quad \text{(No work interaction allowed)}
$$

$$
Q \;\neq\; 0 \quad \text{(Heat interaction is permitted)}
$$

Applying the First Law of Thermodynamics to a reservoir:

$$
\Delta E_{\text{reservoir}} \;=\; Q_{\text{reservoir}} - W_{\text{reservoir}}
$$

Since $W_{\text{reservoir}} = 0$ and $T = \text{constant}$ implies $\Delta U_{\text{reservoir}} = 0$ (for incompressible substances with constant $C_p$):

$$
Q_{\text{reservoir}} \;=\; 0 \quad \text{internally}
$$

However, **energy exchange across the boundary is non-zero**, simply because $C_T \to \infty$ dwarfs any finite $Q$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need the Concept of a Thermal Reservoir?

The concept serves as a **cornerstone abstraction** in classical thermodynamics for the following engineering reasons:

1. **Simplifies Boundary Analysis:** Real systems interact with surroundings of finite size whose temperature *does* drift. Treating them as reservoirs allows us to assume constant $T_H$ and $T_L$, dramatically simplifying heat-engine and refrigerator cycles.
2. **Establishes Reference Temperatures:** It provides unambiguous hot-side ($T_H$) and cold-side ($T_L$) temperatures for computing thermal efficiency ($\eta$) and Coefficient of Performance (COP).
3. **Powers the Second Law:** The Kelvin-Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law are *literally defined* using the notion of thermal reservoirs.

### 2.2 Energy Interaction Mechanics

Let us analyze the energy balance when a generic **system** interacts with a hot reservoir and a cold reservoir.

For the **Hot Reservoir (Source):**

$$
\Delta U_{\text{source}} \;=\; -Q_{\text{out, source}}
$$

The negative sign indicates that the source **loses** energy equal to $Q_H$ supplied to the system.

For the **Cold Reservoir (Sink):**

$$
\Delta U_{\text{sink}} \;=\; +Q_{\text{in, sink}}
$$

The positive sign indicates that the sink **gains** energy equal to $Q_L$ rejected by the system.

For the **Combined System + Reservoirs (Universal):**

$$
\Delta U_{\text{universe}} \;=\; \Delta U_{\text{system}} + \Delta U_{\text{source}} + \Delta U_{\text{sink}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The reservoirs are so massive that even when $\Delta U_{\text{source}} \neq 0$, the resulting temperature change is *imperceptible*, justifying the constant-$T$ assumption.

### 2.3 Schematic Energy Flow Representation

Consider a heat engine operating between two reservoirs:

$$
\boxed{\;Q_H \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Heat Engine} \;\longrightarrow\; W_{\text{net}} \;}
$$

with a parallel heat rejection path:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Heat Engine} \;\longrightarrow\; Q_L \;\longrightarrow\;}
$$

The directional flow forms the foundation of the **First Law energy balance** for a cyclic heat engine:

$$
W_{\text{net}} \;=\; Q_H - Q_L
$$

### 2.4 Distinction from Related Thermodynamic Concepts

| **Concept** | **Distinguishing Feature** |
| :--- | :--- |
| Thermal Energy Reservoir | Heat-only interaction; $T$ remains constant |
| Work Reservoir | Mechanical work only; e.g., large flywheel, atmosphere |
| Heat Exchanger | Both fluids change temperature (finite capacities) |
| Adiabatic Wall | No heat transfer occurs across boundary |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Below is the consolidated formula reference table for solving KTU 2024 Scheme problems on Thermal Energy Reservoirs.**

| **#** | **Formula / Expression** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $C_T \to \infty$, $T = \text{const}$ | Defining condition of a TER | — |
| 2 | $W_{\text{reservoir}} = 0$ | No work crosses reservoir boundary | kJ |
| 3 | $Q_{\text{source}} = \Delta U_{\text{source}}$ | Energy lost by source | kJ |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{sink}} = \Delta U_{\text{sink}}$ | Energy gained by sink | kJ |
| 5 | $Q_H - Q_L = W_{\text{net}}$ | Energy balance of cyclic heat engine | kJ |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | Thermal efficiency of heat engine | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{COP}_{\text{refrigerator}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | Refrigerator COP | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{COP}_{\text{heat pump}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | Heat pump COP | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Maximum possible efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot reversibility relation | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Examination Tip:** In any KTU problem, always verify whether the temperatures are given in Celsius ($^\circ$C) or Kelvin (K). The Carnot formula **requires Kelvin temperatures**, otherwise answers will be physically and mathematically meaningless.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Application** | **Role of Thermal Reservoir Concept** |
| :--- | :--- |
| Power Plants (Thermal) | Boiler furnace acts as $T_H$ source; condenser cooling water acts as $T_L$ sink |
| Refrigeration & HVAC | Cold chamber extracts $Q_L$ from refrigerated space; ambient air is the $T_H$ sink |
| Geothermal Energy | Earth's crust acts as a stable $T_H$ reservoir over geological timescales |
| Cryogenics | Liquid helium/nitrogen baths act as ultra-low $T_L$ reservoirs |
| Solar Thermal Collectors | The Sun is the ultimate natural $T_H$ source |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation of the Reservoir Energy Balance

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Starting from the First Law, derive the governing energy equation for a thermal energy reservoir and demonstrate why its temperature remains constant.

#### **Step 1 — State the First Law for a Closed System**

$$
\Delta E_{\text{system}} \;=\; Q_{\text{in}} - W_{\text{out}}
$$

#### **Step 2 — Apply to a Thermal Reservoir as the "System"**

By definition, a thermal reservoir does no boundary work and its internal energy is a function of temperature only (for a single-phase incompressible substance):

$$
W_{\text{reservoir}} \;=\; 0
$$

$$
\Delta U_{\text{reservoir}} \;=\; m \cdot c_v \cdot \Delta T
$$

#### **Step 3 — Substitute Into the First Law**

$$
m \cdot c_v \cdot \Delta T \;=\; Q_{\text{reservoir}} - 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q_{\text{reservoir}} \;=\; m \cdot c_v \cdot \Delta T
$$

#### **Step 4 — Examine the Limiting Case of an Ideal Reservoir**

For an *ideal* thermal reservoir, we impose the condition:

$$
\Delta T \;\longrightarrow\; 0 \quad \text{for} \quad Q_{\text{reservoir}} \;=\; \text{finite non-zero value}
$$

This requires:

$$
m \cdot c_v \;\longrightarrow\; \infty
$$

Hence the limiting thermal capacity becomes:

$$
C_T \;=\; m \cdot c_v \;\longrightarrow\; \infty
$$

#### **Step 5 — Conclude the Mathematical Statement**

$$
\boxed{\;C_T \;\to\; \infty \quad \text{with} \quad T \;=\; \text{constant}\;}
$$

This constitutes the rigorous mathematical definition of a Thermal Energy Reservoir.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation of the Carnot Efficiency (Using Reservoir Framework)

#### **Step 1 — Assume a Reversible Cyclic Heat Engine**

A reversible engine receives heat $Q_H$ from a reservoir at $T_H$, produces work $W_{\text{net}}$, and rejects heat $Q_L$ to a reservoir at $T_L$.

#### **Step 2 — Apply the First Law to the Engine Cycle**

For a cyclic device, $\Delta U_{\text{cycle}} = 0$:

$$
Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; W_{\text{net}}
$$

The net heat is the algebraic sum of heat in and out:

$$
Q_H - Q_L \;=\; W_{\text{net}}
$$

#### **Step 3 — Define Thermal Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{\text{th}} \;=\; \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_H} \;=\; \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\eta_{\text{th}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}
$$

#### **Step 4 — Invoke the Carnot Theorem (Second Law)**

For a reversible cycle operating between two reservoirs, the ratio of heat exchanges equals the ratio of absolute temperatures:

$$
\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} \;=\; \frac{T_L}{T_H}
$$

#### **Step 5 — Substitute and Arrive at the Carnot Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{\text{th, Carnot}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{max}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}\;}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation of the COP Relations

#### **Step 3.3.1 — Refrigerator Coefficient of Performance**

**Step 1:** A refrigerator absorbs $Q_L$ from a cold space at $T_L$, receives work input $W_{\text{in}}$, and rejects $Q_H$ to a hot space at $T_H$.

**Step 2:** First Law:

$$
Q_H \;=\; Q_L + W_{\text{in}}
$$

**Step 3:** COP definition (desired effect / work input):

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Desired cooling}}{\text{Work input}} \;=\; \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{in}}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $W_{\text{in}} = Q_H - Q_L$:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} \;=\; \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply reversibility ratio:

$$
\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} \;=\; \frac{T_L}{T_H} \;\Longrightarrow\; Q_L \;=\; Q_H \cdot \frac{T_L}{T_H}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ref,Carnot}} \;=\; \frac{Q_H \cdot \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}}{Q_H - Q_H \cdot \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{T_L}{T_H}}{1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{ref,Carnot}} \;=\; \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}\;}
$$

#### **Step 3.3.2 — Heat Pump Coefficient of Performance**

**Step 1:** A heat pump delivers $Q_H$ to a heated space using work input $W_{\text{in}}$.

**Step 2:** COP definition:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} \;=\; \frac{Q_H}{W_{\text{in}}} \;=\; \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply reversibility ratio as before:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{HP,Carnot}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{HP,Carnot}} \;=\; \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check Identity:** The two Carnot COP expressions are related by:
>
> $$
> \text{COP}_{\text{HP}} \;=\; \text{COP}_{\text{ref}} + 1
> $$
>
> This identity must always hold for any refrigerator/heat-pump pair operating on the **same temperature limits**.

---

### 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

| **Application** | **Direction of $Q_H$** | **Direction of $Q_L$** | **Work Relation** | **Performance Metric** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heat Engine | Into system (from $T_H$) | Out of system (to $T_L$) | $W_{\text{out}} = Q_H - Q_L$ | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{out}}}{Q_H}$ |
| Refrigerator | Out of system (to $T_H$) | Into system (from $T_L$) | $W_{\text{in}} = Q_H - Q_L$ | $\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{in}}}$ |
| Heat Pump | Out of system (to $T_H$) | Into system (from $T_L$) | $W_{\text{in}} = Q_H - Q_L$ | $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{\text{in}}}$ |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Demonstration

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A heat engine operates between a source at $T_H = 800\,\text{K}$ and a sink at $T_L = 300\,\text{K}$. The engine absorbs $Q_H = 1000\,\text{kJ}$ per cycle. Determine: (i) the maximum possible work output, (ii) the heat rejected at maximum efficiency, and (iii) verify the reservoir assumption is physically reasonable.

#### **Solution:**

**Step (i) — Maximum (Carnot) Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} \;=\; 1 - \frac{300}{800}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \;=\; 1 - 0.375 \;=\; 0.625
$$

**Step (ii) — Maximum Work Output:**

$$
W_{\text{max}} \;=\; \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \cdot Q_H \;=\; 0.625 \times 1000
$$

$$
\boxed{\;W_{\text{max}} \;=\; 625\,\text{kJ}\;}
$$

**Step (iii) — Heat Rejected at Maximum Efficiency:**

Using $W_{\text{max}} = Q_H - Q_L$:

$$
Q_L \;=\; Q_H - W_{\text{max}} \;=\; 1000 - 625
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q_L \;=\; 375\,\text{kJ}\;}
$$

**Step (iv) — Verification via Reversibility Ratio:**

$$
\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} \;\stackrel{?}{=}\; \frac{T_L}{T_H}
$$

$$
\frac{375}{1000} \;=\; 0.375 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{300}{800} \;=\; 0.375 \quad \checkmark
$$

The reversibility identity is satisfied, confirming the answer is consistent with the Second Law.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Schematic — System Interaction with Two Reservoirs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS["HOT RESERVOIR Source TH constant"]
    nodeSys["SYSTEM Heat Engine or Refrigerator"]
    nodeK["COLD RESERVOIR Sink TL constant"]

    nodeS -- "QH heat in" --> nodeSys
    nodeSys -- "Wnet work out" --> nodeOut["Boundary Work Wnet"]
    nodeSys -- "QL heat out" --> nodeK

    classDef reservoir fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef system fill:#9DD8FF,stroke:#003F7F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef work fill:#C7F0BD,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class nodeS,nodeK reservoir
    class nodeSys system
    class nodeOut work
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Block-Level Energy Flow Architecture (Heat Engine Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        direction TB
        E1["Internal Energy UH large"]
        QHout["Heat Transfer QH leaves reservoir"]
    end

    subgraph DEVICE["CYCLIC DEVICE"]
        direction TB
        ENG["Heat Engine Receives QH"]
        WORK["Produces Wnet = QH minus QL"]
    end

    subgraph RES_LOW["COLD THERMAL RESERVOIR TL"]
        direction TB

    end

    QHout --> ENG
    ENG --> WORK
    ENG --> QLin
    QLin --> E2

    classDef hotStyle fill:#FFB6A0,stroke:#A02810,stroke-width:2px
    classDef devStyle fill:#B0D8FF,stroke:#1A4F80,stroke-width:2px
    classDef coldStyle fill:#BDE0FE,stroke:#1D3557,stroke-width:2px

    class E1,QHout hotStyle
    class ENG,WORK devStyle
    class QLin,E2 coldStyle
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Comparative Architecture of Engine vs. Refrigerator vs. Heat Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        E1["QH enters"]

    end
```
