        R1["QL enters"]
        R2["Win enters"]

    end

        H1["QL enters"]

        H3["QH leaves"]
    end

    REF_BLOCK -- "Different COP definition" --> HP_BLOCK

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**Q: Define a thermal energy reservoir. Give two real-world examples each for a high-temperature and a low-temperature reservoir.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **thermal energy reservoir (TER)** is a body with a very large thermal capacity that can absorb or supply a finite amount of heat without experiencing any appreciable change in its temperature. *(1 mark)*

It is characterized mathematically by $C_T \to \infty$ and $T = \text{constant}$. *(1 mark)*

**Examples:**
- **High-Temperature Reservoirs (Sources):** Boiler furnace gases, geothermal reservoirs, the Sun, nuclear reactor cores. *(0.5 marks)*
- **Low-Temperature Reservoirs (Sinks):** Atmospheric air, river/sea water, condenser cooling water, cryogenic liquid baths. *(0.5 marks)*

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: Explain why a thermal energy reservoir cannot perform work but can only exchange heat.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

By definition, a thermal energy reservoir is constrained to undergo **only heat interactions** with its surroundings. *(1 mark)*

This is because a reservoir's state variables (especially temperature) remain constant; any work output would require the reservoir to undergo a change in state (e.g., expansion or compression), violating the constant-$T$ assumption. *(1 mark)*

Mathematically, $W_{\text{reservoir}} = 0$, while $Q_{\text{reservoir}} \neq 0$. Hence, the First Law applied to a reservoir simplifies to $\Delta U_{\text{reservoir}} = Q_{\text{reservoir}}$, with the temperature held effectively constant due to the infinite heat capacity. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **QUESTION A** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]

**(a)** Define a thermal energy reservoir. Explain, with the help of neat sketches, how it interacts with a heat engine. State clearly the assumptions involved in treating a real body as a thermal reservoir. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A heat engine operates between a source at $T_H = 900\,\text{K}$ and a sink at $T_L = 300\,\text{K}$. The engine draws $500\,\text{kJ}$ of heat from the source per cycle. Assuming the engine is reversible, determine: *(i)* the heat rejected to the sink, *(ii)* the work output, and *(iii)* the thermal efficiency. *(7 Marks)*

---

##### **Solution to Question A(a):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** A thermal energy reservoir is a hypothetical body of very large thermal capacity that can supply or absorb a finite amount of heat without undergoing any change in temperature. For a hot reservoir (source), $T_H$ remains constant during heat supply; for a cold reservoir (sink), $T_L$ remains constant during heat absorption.

**Sketch and Interaction Description (3 Marks):** A heat engine receives heat $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir at $T_H$, converts part of it into work $W_{\text{net}}$, and rejects the remaining heat $Q_L$ to the cold reservoir at $T_L$.

**Assumptions (2 Marks):**
1. The reservoir has infinite heat capacity — i.e., $C_T \to \infty$.
2. The reservoir undergoes no work interaction — i.e., $W = 0$.
3. The reservoir's temperature remains spatially uniform and temporally constant.
4. Real bodies such as the atmosphere and oceans approximate reservoirs over practical engineering time-scales.

---

##### **Solution to Question A(b):**

**Given:**

$$
T_H = 900\,\text{K}, \quad T_L = 300\,\text{K}, \quad Q_H = 500\,\text{kJ}, \quad \text{Cycle: Reversible}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply Reversibility Condition (1 Mark):**

For a reversible (Carnot) cycle:

$$
\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} \;=\; \frac{T_L}{T_H}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Heat Rejected (2 Marks):**

$$
Q_L \;=\; Q_H \cdot \frac{T_L}{T_H} \;=\; 500 \times \frac{300}{900}
$$

$$
Q_L \;=\; 500 \times 0.3333 \;=\; 166.67\,\text{kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Work Output (2 Marks):**

$$
W_{\text{net}} \;=\; Q_H - Q_L \;=\; 500 - 166.67
$$

$$
W_{\text{net}} \;=\; 333.33\,\text{kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Thermal Efficiency (2 Marks):**

$$
\eta_{\text{th}} \;=\; \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_H} \;=\; \frac{333.33}{500} \;=\; 0.6667
$$

**Verification via Carnot Formula:**

$$
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} \;=\; 1 - \frac{300}{900} \;=\; 0.6667 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Answer Box:**

$$
\boxed{\;Q_L \;=\; 166.67\,\text{kJ}, \quad W_{\text{net}} \;=\; 333.33\,\text{kJ}, \quad \eta_{\text{th}} \;=\; 66.67\%\;}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Differentiate clearly between a **heat engine**, a **refrigerator**, and a **heat pump** in terms of their interaction with thermal reservoirs. Derive the relationship $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \text{COP}_{\text{ref}} + 1$. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A refrigerator extracts $200\,\text{kJ}$ of heat per cycle from a cold space maintained at $-10\,^\circ\text{C}$ and rejects heat to the surroundings at $35\,^\circ\text{C}$. If the refrigerator operates on a reversed Carnot cycle, determine: *(i)* the COP of the refrigerator, *(ii)* the work input required, and *(iii)* the heat rejected to the surroundings. *(7 Marks)*

---

##### **Solution to Question B(a):**

**Differentiating the Three Devices (4 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Heat Engine** | **Refrigerator** | **Heat Pump** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Objective | Produce work | Produce cooling | Produce heating |
| $Q_H$ Direction | Into engine from $T_H$ | Out of device to $T_H$ | Out of device to $T_H$ |
| $Q_L$ Direction | Out of engine to $T_L$ | Into device from $T_L$ | Into device from $T_L$ |
| Work Direction | Output | Input | Input |
| Performance Metric | $\eta_{\text{th}}$ | $\text{COP}_{\text{ref}}$ | $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}}$ |

**Derivation of $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \text{COP}_{\text{ref}} + 1$ (3 Marks):**

**Step 1:** From the First Law energy balance:

$$
Q_H \;=\; Q_L + W_{\text{in}}
$$

**Step 2:** Express both COPS in terms of $Q_H$, $Q_L$, $W_{\text{in}}$:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} \;=\; \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{in}}}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} \;=\; \frac{Q_H}{W_{\text{in}}}
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} - \text{COP}_{\text{ref}} \;=\; \frac{Q_H}{W_{\text{in}}} - \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{in}}} \;=\; \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{W_{\text{in}}}
$$

**Step 4:** From Step 1, $Q_H - Q_L = W_{\text{in}}$:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} - \text{COP}_{\text{ref}} \;=\; \frac{W_{\text{in}}}{W_{\text{in}}} \;=\; 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} \;=\; \text{COP}_{\text{ref}} + 1\;}
$$

---

##### **Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:**

$$
T_L \;=\; -10\,^\circ\text{C} \;=\; 263\,\text{K}, \quad T_H \;=\; 35\,^\circ\text{C} \;=\; 308\,\text{K}, \quad Q_L \;=\; 200\,\text{kJ}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Error:** Students frequently forget to convert Celsius to Kelvin. The Carnot formulas are dimensionally valid **only for absolute temperatures in Kelvin**. A -10 °C value used directly will produce a negative denominator in COP and an incorrect answer.

**Step 1 — Compute COP of Refrigerator (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ref,Carnot}} \;=\; \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \;=\; \frac{263}{308 - 263}
$$

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ref,Carnot}} \;=\; \frac{263}{45} \;=\; 5.844
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Work Input (2 Marks):**

Using $\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{in}}}$:

$$
W_{\text{in}} \;=\; \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_{\text{ref}}} \;=\; \frac{200}{5.844}
$$

$$
W_{\text{in}} \;=\; 34.22\,\text{kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Heat Rejected to Surroundings (2 Marks):**

Using the First Law:

$$
Q_H \;=\; Q_L + W_{\text{in}} \;=\; 200 + 34.22
$$

$$
Q_H \;=\; 234.22\,\text{kJ}
$$

**Verification (1 Mark):** Using the reversibility ratio:

$$
\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} \;=\; \frac{200}{234.22} \;=\; 0.8540
$$

$$
\frac{T_L}{T_H} \;=\; \frac{263}{308} \;=\; 0.8540 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Answer Box:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} \;=\; 5.84, \quad W_{\text{in}} \;=\; 34.22\,\text{kJ}, \quad Q_H \;=\; 234.22\,\text{kJ}\;}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Mixing Celsius with Kelvin in Carnot formulas leads to a -30% to -50% mark deduction. **Always convert first.**
> 2. **Sign Convention Confusion:** Heat *into* the system is positive; heat *out of* the system is positive in magnitude but must be clearly labeled. Do not write negative $Q_L$.
> 3. **Reservoir Mislabeling:** A common error is reversing $T_H$ and $T_L$. Always remember: $T_H$ is the reservoir the system rejects heat *to* (in a refrigerator).
> 4. **Forgetting Work Interaction:** Writing $Q_H = Q_L$ in a refrigerator problem (i.e., ignoring $W_{\text{in}}$) is a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Misusing the Reversibility Ratio:** The relation $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ is valid **only for reversible cycles**. For irreversible cycles, use the Clausius inequality.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 High-Yield KTU 2024 Revision Summary — Thermal Energy Reservoirs 🔥**

- ✅ **Definition:** A TER is a body with infinite heat capacity ($C_T \to \infty$) that maintains constant temperature during heat exchange.
- ✅ **Two Types:** **Source** (Hot, $T_H$, supplies heat) and **Sink** (Cold, $T_L$, absorbs heat).
- ✅ **No Work Interaction:** $W_{\text{reservoir}} = 0$ — this is non-negotiable for any TER.
- ✅ **First Law for Reservoir:** $\Delta U_{\text{reservoir}} = Q_{\text{reservoir}}$ (since $W = 0$).
- ✅ **Energy Balance for Heat Engine:** $W_{\text{net}} = Q_H - Q_L$ (cyclic device, $\Delta U = 0$).
- ✅ **Thermal Efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$.
- ✅ **Refrigerator COP:** $\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$.
- ✅ **Heat Pump COP:** $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$.
- ✅ **Identity to Memorize:** $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \text{COP}_{\text{ref}} + 1$ — this is a favorite KTU 3-mark question.
- ✅ **Carnot (Reversible) Efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ — **Temperatures MUST be in Kelvin.**
- ✅ **Carnot (Reversible) COPs:** $\text{COP}_{\text{ref,Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ and $\text{COP}_{\text{HP,Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$.
- ✅ **Reversibility Identity:** $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ — applies *only* to reversible cycles.
- ✅ **Real-World Examples:** Atmosphere (sink), furnace (source), oceans (sink), Sun (source), rivers (sink).
- ✅ **Examination Trap:** Confusing Celsius with Kelvin — leads to cascading numerical errors.
- ✅ **Conceptual Hierarchy:** TER (heat only) → Heat Engine/Refrigerator/Heat Pump (work + heat) → Power Plant/AC (real engineering application).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Engines and Thermal Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Engines and Thermal Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Heat Engine?

A **Heat Engine** is a thermodynamic device operating in a cycle that converts a portion of the heat supplied to it from a high-temperature source into useful mechanical work, while rejecting the remaining heat to a low-temperature sink. The working substance (typically a gas or vapour) returns to its initial state after every cycle, ensuring the process can be repeated continuously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A heat engine is a cyclic device whose sole purpose is to produce net work (W_net) by transferring heat from a high-temperature body (source) to a low-temperature body (sink), in accordance with the **Second Law of Thermodynamics – Kelvin-Planck Statement**.

### Formal Constituents of a Heat Engine (KTU Board Vocabulary):
1. **High-Temperature Source (Hot Reservoir):** Supplies heat $Q_H$ (also denoted $Q_{in}$ or $Q_1$) at temperature $T_H$.
2. **Working Substance:** The medium (gas, vapour, or fluid) that undergoes the cycle and performs the energy conversion.
3. **Low-Temperature Sink (Cold Reservoir):** Receives the rejected heat $Q_L$ (also denoted $Q_{out}$ or $Q_2$) at temperature $T_L$.
4. **Net Work Output:** The useful mechanical work $W_{net}$ extracted per cycle.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Water Wheel Driven by a Hot Spring:**
> Imagine a stream of hot water cascading from a high hill (the **hot reservoir**) onto a paddle wheel. The wheel rotates and lifts a small bucket of water up to a lower pond (the **cold reservoir**), while most of the original water mass continues downhill. The rotation of the wheel represents **useful work**, the hot spring represents **heat input**, and the leftover water that flows away represents **waste heat rejection**.
>
> Just as you cannot extract 100% of the water's potential energy (some always escapes downhill), a heat engine can never convert 100% of its input heat into work — this is a **fundamental truth of nature**, formalized by the Second Law.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (with KTU Units)

| Metric | Symbol | SI Unit | KTU Notation |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Heat Supplied | $Q_H$ | J (Joule) or kJ | $Q_{in}$, $Q_1$ |
| Heat Rejected | $Q_L$ | J or kJ | $Q_{out}$, $Q_2$ |
| Net Work | $W_{net}$ | J or kJ | $W$ |
| Source Temperature | $T_H$ | K (Kelvin) | $T_1$ |
| Sink Temperature | $T_L$ | K (Kelvin) | $T_2$ |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | Dimensionless (%) | $\eta$ |
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \mathrm{W/m^2 \cdot K^4}$ | Used only in radiation modules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Cardinal Rule:** All temperatures used in efficiency formulas **must be in Kelvin (K)**, not Celsius (°C). Failure to convert is the single most common error flagged by KTU examiners.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Schematic Energy Flow and the First Law Applied to a Cycle

A heat engine, being a **cyclic device**, returns its working fluid to its initial state at the end of every cycle. Therefore:

$$\oint dU = 0 \quad \text{(per cycle)}$$

Applying the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to one complete cycle (with sign convention: heat added to system is positive, work done by system is positive):

$$Q_{net} - W_{net} = \Delta U_{cycle} = 0$$

Hence, the **Energy Balance Equation** (often called the KTU "Golden Triangle"):

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$$

## 2.2 Thermal Efficiency — The Central Metric

The **Thermal Efficiency** of a heat engine is defined as the ratio of the net work output to the total heat input per cycle:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{Q_{in} - Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

### Interpretation of $\eta_{th}$:
- $\eta_{th} = 1$ (i.e., 100%) would mean **no heat rejection** — this is the **Kelvin-Planck violation** (a *Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind, PMM2*).
- $\eta_{th} = 0$ would mean all heat is rejected and no work is produced.
- **Always** $0 < \eta_{th} < 1$ for any real engine.

## 2.3 The Carnot (Reversible) Heat Engine — Upper Bound

The **Carnot Cycle** operating between a source at $T_H$ and sink at $T_L$ delivers the **maximum possible** thermal efficiency:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

This is the benchmark for all real engines. The second law mandates:

$$\eta_{th,\,real} \le 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

with equality only for a fully reversible (frictionless, quasi-static, no dissipation) cycle.

## 2.4 The Heat Engine Family

| Type of Engine | Description | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **External Combustion** | Combustion occurs outside the working fluid | Steam Power Plant (Rankine), Stirling |
| **Internal Combustion** | Combustion occurs inside the working fluid | Otto, Diesel, Brayton (Gas Turbine) |
| **Reversible Engine** | No entropy generation; reaches Carnot efficiency | Carnot, ideal Stirling |
| **Irreversible (Real) Engine** | Always has friction, heat leak, finite-time processes | All practical engines |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these six relations — they form the spine of Module 2 numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Validity |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ | Energy balance per cycle | All cyclic engines |
| 2 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$ | Definition of efficiency | All heat engines |
| 3 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | Efficiency in terms of heat ratio | All heat engines |
| 4 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Maximum possible efficiency | Reversible cycle only |
| 5 | $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Heat ratio for reversible cycle | Carnot cycle only |
| 6 | $COP_{Ref} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{th} - 1}$ | Refrigerator COP (related) | All refrigerators |
| 7 | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{\eta_{th}}$ | Heat Pump COP (related) | All heat pumps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Conversion (must memorize):**
> $$T(\text{K}) = t(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$$
> For KTU numericals, **273** is the accepted approximation unless otherwise specified.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plant Design:** The thermal efficiency dictates fuel cost and emission rates. Raising $T_H$ (e.g., supercritical steam at 873 K) and lowering $T_L$ (e.g., condenser at 303 K) are the two primary design levers.
- **Automotive Engines:** Otto and Diesel cycles approach ~38% and ~45% peak thermal efficiencies in real operation.
- **Combined Cycle Plants:** Combining Brayton (gas turbine) and Rankine (steam) cycles pushes overall plant efficiency beyond **60%**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Thermal Efficiency from the First Law

**Starting Point:** A heat engine operating in a cycle between a hot reservoir ($Q_H$ at $T_H$) and a cold reservoir ($Q_L$ at $T_L$).

**Step 1 — Apply First Law to the Cycle:**

The net change in internal energy over one complete cycle is zero, because internal energy is a property (point function):

$$\Delta U_{cycle} = \oint dU = 0$$

**Step 2 — Write the First Law in Sign Convention (KTU Board Standard):**

Heat added to the system is taken as positive; work done by the system is taken as positive.

$$Q_{net} - W_{net} = \Delta U_{cycle}$$

**Step 3 — Expand $Q_{net}$ into Input and Output Components:**

$$Q_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = Q_H - Q_L$$

Substituting into Step 2:

$$(Q_H - Q_L) - W_{net} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $W_{net}$:**

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

**Step 5 — Define Thermal Efficiency:**

By definition, the ratio of desired output (work) to required input (heat from source):

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Expression for $W_{net}$:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify:**

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** and is the starting point of every Module 2 numerical.

## 3.2 Derivation of Carnot (Reversible) Efficiency from Entropy Balance

**Step 1 — Reversible Cycle Entropy Statement:**

For a reversible cycle, the Clausius equality holds:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply to the Two Isothermal Reservoirs:**

During heat reception from the source (at $T_H$) and rejection to the sink (at $T_L$):

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$

The negative sign in the second term reflects heat leaving the system.

**Step 3 — Solve for the Heat Ratio:**

$$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Efficiency Expression:**

From Section 3.1, Step 7:

$$\eta_{th,rev} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

Thus:

$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** This derivation is the **proof of the Kelvin-Planck statement** and earns full marks whenever a question asks "Show that the maximum efficiency of a heat engine operating between two reservoirs is $1 - T_L/T_H$."

## 3.3 KTU Numerical Pattern: Analytical Matrix

Below is the **canonical 4-step problem-solving matrix** that the KTU Board Examiner expects you to write out for any Module 2 numerical:

| Step | Action | What to Write on Paper |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Data** | List given values, convert $T$ to Kelvin | $T_H = 800 + 273 = 1073\,\mathrm{K}$, $T_L = 300 + 273 = 573\,\mathrm{K}$ |
| **2. Concept** | Identify the governing formula | $\eta_{th} = 1 - Q_L/Q_H$ or $\eta = 1 - T_L/T_H$ (reversible) |
| **3. Substitute** | Plug values with units | $\eta = 1 - 573/1073$ |
| **4. Result** | State answer in % with correct sign | $\eta = 0.466 = 46.6\%$ |

## 3.4 Sample Numerical — Full KTU Board Style Solution

**Problem:** A heat engine receives **1000 kJ** of heat from a source at **600 K** and rejects heat to a sink at **300 K**. If the engine produces **400 kJ** of work, determine:
(a) The thermal efficiency.
(b) The heat rejected.
(c) Compare with the Carnot efficiency.

### Solution:

**Given:**
$Q_H = 1000 \, \mathrm{kJ}$, $T_H = 600 \, \mathrm{K}$, $T_L = 300 \, \mathrm{K}$, $W_{net} = 400 \, \mathrm{kJ}$.

### Part (a) — Thermal Efficiency:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{400}{1000} = 0.40 = 40\%$$

### Part (b) — Heat Rejected:

Applying energy balance:

$$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1000 - 400 = 600 \, \mathrm{kJ}$$

### Part (c) — Carnot Efficiency:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{600} = 0.50 = 50\%$$

Since $40\% < 50\%$, the **Second Law is satisfied** (real engine < reversible engine).

$$\eta_{real} = 40\% < \eta_{Carnot} = 50\% \;\; \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Heat Engine (Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of energy and matter through a generic heat engine. It satisfies the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards**: alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, and clean uppercase labels.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HOT RESERVOIR T_H] -- Q_H --> B[WORKING SUBSTANCE CYCLIC DEVICE]
    B -- W_NET --> C[USEFUL MECHANICAL WORK SHAFT OUTPUT]
    B -- Q_L --> D[COLD RESERVOIR T_L]
    E[SECOND LAW KELVIN PLANCK] -. CONSTRAINS .-> B
    F[EFFICIENCY FORMULA] -. COMPUTED FROM .-> B
    G["ETA_TH EQUALS W_NET BY Q_H"] -. DEFINES .-> F
    H["CARNOT LIMIT ONE MINUS T_L BY T_H"] -. UPPER BOUND FOR .-> F
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** represent the *physical flow* of energy (heat and work).
- The **dotted arrows** represent *governing constraints* imposed by the Second Law and the efficiency formulas.
- $Q_H$ flows **in**, $Q_L$ flows **out**, and $W_{net}$ is the **delivered useful output**.

## 4.2 Comparative Engine-Type Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[HEAT ENGINES] --> B[EXTERNAL COMBUSTION]
    A --> C[INTERNAL COMBUSTION]
    B --> B1[STIRLING ENGINE]
    B --> B2[RANKINE CYCLE STEAM PLANT]
    C --> C1[OTTO CYCLE PETROL ENGINE]
    C --> C2[DIESEL CYCLE CI ENGINE]
    C --> C3[BRAYTON CYCLE GAS TURBINE]
    A --> D[REVERSIBLE REFERENCE]
    D --> D1[CARNOT ENGINE]
    D --> D2[IDEAL STIRLING]
    E[EFFICIENCY HIERARCHY] --> E1[CARNOT MAX]
    E --> E2[REAL CYCLES LESS THAN CARNOT]
    E --> E3[PMM2 FORBIDDEN EQUALS ONE]
```

## 4.3 P-V and T-S Schematic Description

In the P-V (Pressure-Volume) plane, a heat engine cycle appears as a **closed loop traversed clockwise**. The enclosed area equals the net work done per cycle.

| Diagram Type | Loop Direction | Enclosed Area | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| P-V (Heat Engine) | **Clockwise** | $W_{net}$ (positive) | Produces work |
| P-V (Refrigerator/Heat Pump) | **Counter-clockwise** | $W_{net}$ (input) | Consumes work |
| T-S (Heat Engine) | **Clockwise** | $Q_H - Q_L$ (net heat) | Energy converted to work |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention:** Examiners often draw a small "loop with arrow" and ask students to identify whether the device is a heat engine or refrigerator. **Clockwise on P-V or T-S ⇒ Heat Engine.**

## 4.4 Energy Flow Sankey-Style Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        A[Q_H EQUALS 1000 kJ FROM SOURCE AT T_H]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        B[W_NET EQUALS 400 kJ USEFUL WORK]
        C[Q_L EQUALS 600 kJ WASTE HEAT TO SINK]
    end
    A --> B
    A --> C
    D[SECOND LAW BOUND ETA LESS THAN ONE MINUS T_L BY T_H] -. APPLIES TO .-> A
```

This block-level architecture mirrors a **Sankey diagram**, where input heat bifurcates into useful work and rejected heat, and the second law caps the work fraction at the Carnot limit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define thermal efficiency of a heat engine. Why can it never be 100%?**

**Model Answer:**

The thermal efficiency of a heat engine is the ratio of net work output to heat input per cycle:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$$

It can never reach 100% because of the **Kelvin-Planck statement** of the Second Law: it is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work. A portion of the heat input **must** always be rejected to a lower-temperature sink. Thus $\eta_{th} < 1$ for any cyclic heat engine.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Clausius statement of the Second Law. How does it relate to a refrigerator?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Clausius Statement** of the Second Law: *It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body.*

In a refrigerator, work input is required to drive the cyclic device that extracts heat $Q_L$ from the cold space and rejects $Q_H$ to the hot surroundings. Without work input, the natural flow of heat is from hot to cold — *not* the reverse. Thus the Clausius statement mandates that any refrigerator must have a work input $W_{net} > 0$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module 2 contains a single 14-mark question with internal choice. You must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B. Each option has two sub-parts, typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Option A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

A heat engine operates between a source at $T_H = 900 \, \mathrm{K}$ and a sink at $T_L = 300 \, \mathrm{K}$.

**(a)** Derive the expression for the maximum thermal efficiency of the heat engine operating between the two reservoirs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The engine receives 1500 kJ of heat from the source and produces 600 kJ of work. Determine the heat rejected and check whether the engine satisfies the Second Law. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — State the Kelvin-Planck Statement:**
No cyclic device can convert heat from a single reservoir entirely into work; some heat must be rejected to a lower-temperature sink.

**Step 2 — Write First Law for the Cycle:**

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

**Step 3 — Write Entropy Balance for a Reversible Cycle (Clausius Equality):**

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Apply to the Two Reservoirs:**

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0 \implies \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the Efficiency Expression:**

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 6 — Conclude:**
The maximum (Carnot) efficiency is therefore:

$$\eta_{max} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{900} = 1 - 0.333 = 0.667 = 66.7\%$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $Q_H = 1500 \, \mathrm{kJ}$, $W_{net} = 600 \, \mathrm{kJ}$.

**Step 1 — Heat Rejected from Energy Balance:**

$$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1500 - 600 = 900 \, \mathrm{kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Actual Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{actual} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{600}{1500} = 0.40 = 40\%$$

**Step 3 — Carnot (Maximum) Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{900} = 0.667 = 66.7\%$$

**Step 4 — Compare and Conclude:**

$$\eta_{actual} = 40\% < \eta_{Carnot} = 66.7\% \;\; \checkmark$$

Since the actual efficiency is less than the Carnot (reversible) efficiency, the **Second Law is satisfied**. The engine is feasible.

---

### Option B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** A reversible heat engine operates between a source at $T_H = 800 \, \mathrm{K}$ and a sink at $T_L = 300 \, \mathrm{K}$. If it draws $1200 \, \mathrm{kJ}$ of heat from the source, calculate (i) the efficiency, (ii) the work output, and (iii) the heat rejected. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. For the same temperature limits, find the maximum possible COP of a refrigerator. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Given:** $T_H = 800 \, \mathrm{K}$, $T_L = 300 \, \mathrm{K}$, $Q_H = 1200 \, \mathrm{kJ}$.

**(i) Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 0.625 = 62.5\%$$

**(ii) Work Output:**

$$W_{net} = \eta_{rev} \times Q_H = 0.625 \times 1200 = 750 \, \mathrm{kJ}$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected:**

$$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1200 - 750 = 450 \, \mathrm{kJ}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Definition:**
The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigerator is the ratio of the desired cooling effect (heat removed from the cold space) to the work input required:

$$COP_{Ref} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

**For a Reversible (Carnot) Refrigerator:**

$$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

Substituting:

$$COP_{Ref,\,max} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{300}{800 - 300} = \frac{300}{500} = 0.60$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Errors:**
> 1. **Temperature units in Celsius** (e.g., writing 800°C instead of 1073 K). This error alone can cost **2 to 3 marks** in numericals.
> 2. **Confusing COP of a refrigerator with COP of a heat pump.** Remember: $COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1$ (always higher).
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract** $Q_L$ from $Q_H$ when computing $W_{net}$. Many students write $W = Q_H$ alone, which is the most common error caught by KTU examiners.
> 4. **In the comparison step**, students often compute Carnot efficiency but **fail to write the explicit inequality** $\eta_{real} < \eta_{Carnot}$ — examiners specifically look for this concluding statement.
> 5. **Sign convention errors** in entropy derivation: always write $\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$ with the **negative** sign on the rejected heat term.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Heat Engines & Thermal Efficiency**

- ✅ A heat engine is a **cyclic device** that converts part of $Q_H$ into $W_{net}$ and rejects $Q_L$ to a sink.
- ✅ **First Law (Cycle):** $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$.
- ✅ **Thermal Efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$, always $< 1$.
- ✅ **Carnot (Maximum) Efficiency:** $\eta_{max} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$, valid **only** for reversible cycles.
- ✅ **Second Law (Kelvin-Planck):** No engine can convert *all* heat from one reservoir into work — a sink is mandatory.
- ✅ **Second Law (Clausius):** Heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot without work input.
- ✅ **Heat Ratio for Carnot:** $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$.
- ✅ **Refrigerator COP:** $COP_{Ref} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ (maximum, reversible).
- ✅ **Heat Pump COP:** $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$.
- ✅ **All temperatures in Kelvin** — add 273.15 to Celsius values.
- ✅ **Real engine efficiency is always less than Carnot efficiency** — this is the universal test of Second-Law compliance.
- ✅ **Clockwise loop on P-V or T-S diagram** indicates a heat engine (work-producing).
- ✅ **PMM2** (Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind) is forbidden: $\eta = 1$ violates the Second Law.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refrigerators and Heat Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Refrigerators and Heat Pumps # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of a Refrigerator A **refrigerator** is a cyclic device operating in a thermodynamic cycle that absorbs heat from a low-temperature reservoir (cold space) and rejects it to a high-temperature reservoir (surroundings), with the consumption of work input from an external source. The purpose of a refrigerator is to **maintain the cold body at a temperature lower than that of the surroundings**. $$Q_L = W_{net,in} - Q_H$$ Where: - $Q_L$ = Heat absorbed from the low-temperature reservoir (kJ) - $Q_H$ = Heat rejected to the high-temperature reservoir (kJ) - $W_{net,in}$ = Net work input to the system (kJ) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A refrigerator is a **work-consuming device** that transfers heat from a cold body to a hot body, which is opposite to the natural direction of heat flow (governed by the Second Law of Thermodynamics - Clausius Statement). ## 1.2 Definition of a Heat Pump A **heat pump** is a cyclic device that operates on the **same thermodynamic cycle as a refrigerator** but with a different objective. Its purpose is to **supply heat to a high-temperature reservoir** (e.g., a room being heated in winter) by absorbing heat from a low-temperature source (e.g., outdoor air, ground, or water) and consuming work input. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction:** Both refrigerators and heat pumps use the same physical hardware and operate on the **same cycle**. The difference lies in the **desired output**: > - **Refrigerator** → wants to keep the cold space cold (primary interest: $Q_L$) > - **Heat Pump** → wants to keep the hot space hot (primary interest: $Q_H$) ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Think of a refrigerator and a heat pump as **two siblings sharing the same body** but having different "interests": - **Refrigerator Analogy:** Imagine a water pump that pumps water *uphill*. The work you put in lifts the water. A refrigerator is like a "heat pump" — it pumps heat from a cold place to a hot place. Your home AC absorbs heat from inside the room (cold) and dumps it outside (hot). - **Heat Pump Analogy:** In winter, the same device can run in reverse mode. It absorbs heat from the cold outside air (even if it's $-5^\circ C$, heat is still present) and "pumps" it inside to warm your home. The energy from outside is **free**; you only pay for the work needed to "pump" it. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Example:** A typical window AC unit and a reversible heat pump use the **same compressor, condenser, and evaporator**. In summer, the indoor coil acts as the evaporator (absorbs heat), and in winter, a reversing valve switches roles so the indoor coil becomes the condenser (releases heat). ## 1.4 Coefficient of Performance (COP) — The Key Performance Metric Since refrigerators and heat pumps are not designed to produce work, their efficiency is measured by the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**, defined as the **ratio of desired effect to the work input**. ### Coefficient of Performance of a Refrigerator ($COP_R$): $$COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$$ ### Coefficient of Performance of a Heat Pump ($COP_{HP}$): $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$$ ### Fundamental Relationship: $$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$$ This is a **critical relation** for KTU exams and follows directly from the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to the cyclic device. ## 1.5 Standard Units and Conventions - **Heat transfer ($Q$)**: measured in **kJ** (kilojoules) - **Work input ($W$)**: measured in **kJ** - **COP**: **dimensionless** (no units, since it is a ratio) - **Sign Convention**: Work input to the cycle is **positive**; heat rejected to the high-temperature reservoir is **positive**; heat absorbed from the low-temperature reservoir is **positive** (when using the magnitude form of COP). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Typical COP Values for Real Devices:** > - Household refrigerator: $COP_R \approx 1.5$ to $3.0$ > - Domestic heat pump: $COP_{HP} \approx 3.0$ to $4.0$ > - Industrial refrigeration: $COP_R \approx 2.0$ to $5.0$ > - **Carnot (theoretical maximum) COP**: $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Reverse Carnot Cycle — Theoretical Benchmark The **Reverse Carnot Cycle** is the most efficient refrigeration/heat pump cycle possible. It consists of **four internally reversible processes** operating in the counter-clockwise direction on a P-V and T-S diagram: 1. **Process 1→2: Isentropic Compression** (work input, temperature rises from $T_L$ to $T_H$) 2. **Process 2→3: Isothermal Heat Rejection** (heat $Q_H$ rejected to high-temperature reservoir at $T_H$) 3. **Process 3→4: Isentropic Expansion** (work output, temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$) 4. **Process 4→1: Isothermal Heat Absorption** (heat $Q_L$ absorbed from low-temperature reservoir at $T_L$) > [!NOTE] > **Why Reverse Carnot?** The Carnot cycle is the most efficient heat engine. When run in **reverse**, it becomes the most efficient refrigerator/heat pump. No real cycle can exceed the COP of a reverse Carnot cycle operating between the same temperature limits. ## 2.2 Derivation Logic of the Carnot COP For a **reversible cycle**, the Clausius equality holds: $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$ For the reverse Carnot cycle, the heat transfer occurs only during the two isothermal processes: $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$ $$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ By the **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a cyclic device: $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,in} \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Formula** | **Description** | **Application** | |---|---|---| | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$ | Coefficient of Performance of Refrigerator | General refrigerator analysis | | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$ | Coefficient of Performance of Heat Pump | General heat pump analysis | | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | Heat Pump COP in terms of Refrigerator COP | Quick conversion in problems | | $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot Refrigerator COP (Kelvin scale) | Maximum theoretical COP | | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot Heat Pump COP (Kelvin scale) | Maximum theoretical COP | | $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Heat ratio for reversible cycle | Energy balance check | | $W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L$ | Net work input (First Law) | Energy balance | | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{1}{1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}}$ | Alternative form of Carnot HP COP | Useful for direct computation | | $Q_H = Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}$ | Heat rejected in terms of heat absorbed | Reverse Carnot analysis | | $HSPF = 3.412 \times COP_{HP}$ | Heating Season Performance Factor (English units) | Heat pump rating (BTU/h per Watt) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Temperature Conversion Rule for KTU Exams:** All temperature values in the Carnot COP formulas **MUST be in Kelvin** (K = °C + 273.15). Using Celsius will result in **zero marks**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Domestic Refrigeration:** Food preservation, pharmaceutical storage, vaccine cold chains — all depend on reliable refrigeration cycles. 2. **Air Conditioning:** Maintains thermal comfort in buildings, data centers, and industrial process environments. 3. **Heat Pumps for Heating:** Used in cold-climate countries (Scandinavia, Japan, Northern USA) for energy-efficient home heating. A COP of 4 means 1 kW of electricity produces 4 kW of heat. 4. **Cryogenics & Liquefaction:** Industrial gas liquefaction (LNG, liquid nitrogen, liquid oxygen) uses reverse Brayton and Linde-Hampson cycles — extensions of the same fundamental principles. 5. **Cascaded Refrigeration Systems:** Used in ultra-low temperature applications (e.g., $-80^\circ C$ freezers for biological samples). ## 2.5 The Second Law Limitations The Second Law of Thermodynamics places a **fundamental upper bound** on the COP: > **For any refrigerator operating between $T_L$ and $T_H$:** > $$COP_R \leq \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ > **For any heat pump operating between $T_L$ and $T_H$:** > $$COP_{HP} \leq \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$ Any real cycle will have a COP **strictly less than** the Carnot COP due to **irreversibilities** such as: - Friction in compressor - Pressure drops in pipes and heat exchangers - Non-ideal heat transfer (finite temperature difference) - Heat leaks from hot to cold regions # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Carnot Refrigerator COP from First Principles **Given:** A reverse Carnot cycle operating between a cold reservoir at temperature $T_L$ and a hot reservoir at temperature $T_H$, with heat $Q_L$ absorbed and heat $Q_H$ rejected. **To Prove:** $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ **Step 1:** Apply the **Clausius Equality** for a fully reversible (Carnot) cycle: $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$ **Step 2:** Heat transfer occurs only in the two isothermal processes. On the P-V diagram, isentropic processes (1→2 and 3→4) have $\delta Q = 0$. Thus: $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{-Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$ **Step 3:** Recognize that $Q_H$ is **rejected** to the hot reservoir (so it leaves the system), and $Q_L$ is **absorbed** from the cold reservoir (so it enters the system). The negative sign on $Q_L$ accounts for the direction convention: $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$ **Step 4:** Solve for the ratio of heat transfer: $$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ **Step 5:** Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the cyclic device. For a closed cycle, $\Delta U = 0$, so: $$Q_{net} - W_{net} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L$$ **Step 6:** Substitute into the definition of $COP_R$: $$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$ **Step 7:** Divide numerator and denominator by $Q_L$: $$COP_R = \frac{1}{\frac{Q_H}{Q_L} - 1}$$ **Step 8:** Substitute the ratio from Step 4: $$COP_R = \frac{1}{\frac{T_H}{T_L} - 1} = \frac{1}{\frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}}$$ **Step 9:** Final result: $$COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Pump COP in terms of Refrigerator COP **Given:** A cyclic refrigeration/heat pump device with heat absorbed $Q_L$, heat rejected $Q_H$, and net work input $W_{net,in}$. **To Prove:** $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ **Step 1:** Write the two COP definitions: $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}, \quad COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$$ **Step 2:** Use the First Law energy balance: $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,in}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $Q_H$ into the heat pump COP: $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_L + W_{net,in}}{W_{net,in}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}} + \frac{W_{net,in}}{W_{net,in}}$$ **Step 4:** Recognize the first term as $COP_R$: $$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Work Input for a Given Cooling Load **Given:** A refrigeration system must remove a heat load of $Q_L$ (kJ) at a rate of $\dot{Q}_L$ (kW), with the system operating at a $COP_R$. **To Find:** The required work input rate $\dot{W}_{net,in}$ and the heat rejection rate $\dot{Q}_H$. **Step 1:** From the definition of $COP_R$: $$COP_R = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{W}_{net,in}}$$ **Step 2:** Solve for the work input: $$\dot{W}_{net,in} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{COP_R}$$ **Step 3:** Apply the energy balance to find the heat rejected: $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{net,in}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute the work input expression: $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{COP_R} = \dot{Q}_L \left(1 + \frac{1}{COP_R}\right)$$ **Step 5:** Rearrange to obtain a clean form: $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L \cdot \frac{COP_R + 1}{COP_R}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Refrigerator vs. Heat Pump | **Parameter** | **Refrigerator (Ref.)** | **Heat Pump (HP)** | |---|---|---| | **Primary Objective** | Maintain cold body at low temperature | Supply heat to hot body | | **Desired Output** | $Q_L$ (cooling effect) | $Q_H$ (heating effect) | | **Energy Input** | $W_{net,in}$ (work) | $W_{net,in}$ (work) | | **COP Definition** | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$ | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$ | | **Carnot COP** | $\dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | $\dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | | **Numerical Relation** | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | | **Typical Application** | AC, refrigerators, freezers | Space heating, water heating | | **Quality Criterion** | $COP_R > 1$ is acceptable | $COP_{HP} > 1$ (always > 1) | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A refrigerator operates between a cold space at $-10^\circ C$ and the surroundings at $30^\circ C$. The cooling load is 2 kW. Calculate: 1. The Carnot COP of the refrigerator 2. The required power input 3. The rate of heat rejection to the surroundings 4. The COP of the heat pump operating on the same cycle **Solution:** **Step 1:** Convert temperatures to Kelvin: $$T_L = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15 \text{ K}$$ $$T_H = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the Carnot COP of the refrigerator: $$COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263.15}{303.15 - 263.15}$$ $$COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{263.15}{40} = 6.579$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the required power input: $$\dot{W}_{net,in} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{COP_R} = \frac{2 \text{ kW}}{6.579} = 0.3040 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4:** Calculate the rate of heat rejection: $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{net,in} = 2 + 0.304 = 2.304 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5:** Calculate the heat pump COP on the same cycle: $$COP_{HP,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{303.15}{40} = 7.579$$ **Verification using the relation:** $$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 6.579 + 1 = 7.579 \quad \checkmark$$ **Alternative verification using definition:** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{\dot{Q}_H}{\dot{W}_{net,in}} = \frac{2.304}{0.304} = 7.579 \quad \checkmark$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reverse Carnot Cycle — T-S Diagram The following Mermaid diagram represents the **T-s diagram** of the reverse Carnot cycle, showing the four processes and heat transfer directions. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["STATE 1: Saturated Vapor at T_L"] -->|"Process 1 to 2:
ISENTROPIC COMPRESSION
Work Input W_in"| B["STATE 2: Superheated Vapor at T_H"] B -->|"Process 2 to 3:
ISOTHERMAL HEAT REJECTION
Q_H rejected to HOT reservoir"| C["STATE 3: Saturated Liquid at T_H"] C -->|"Process 3 to 4:
ISENTROPIC EXPANSION
Work Output W_out"| D["STATE 4: Liquid-Vapor Mix at T_L"] D -->|"Process 4 to 1:
ISOTHERMAL HEAT ABSORPTION
Q_L absorbed from COLD reservoir"| A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the T-s Diagram:** The cycle moves **counter-clockwise** on the T-s diagram for a refrigerator/heat pump (opposite to a heat engine, which moves clockwise). The area enclosed by the cycle equals the **net work input**. ## 4.2 Refrigerator Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph COLD["COLD RESERVOIR at T_L"] C1["Heat Source: Cold Space
Refrigerated Cabinet / Cold Room"] end subgraph DEVICE["REFRIGERATION DEVICE"] D1["Cyclic Refrigerator
Reverse Carnot Cycle"] end subgraph HOT["HOT RESERVOIR at T_H"] H1["Heat Sink: Surroundings
Atmospheric Air / Cooling Tower"] end subgraph WORK["WORK INPUT"] W1["Compressor / Power Source
W_net_in"] end C1 -->|"Q_L (Heat Absorbed)"| D1 D1 -->|"Q_H (Heat Rejected)"| H1 W1 -->|"W_net_in (Energy Input)"| D1 style DEVICE fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:3px style COLD fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#003D7A,stroke-width:2px style HOT fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px style WORK fill:#FFFFCC,stroke:#999900,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Heat Pump Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph COLD["LOW-TEMPERATURE SOURCE at T_L"] C1["Heat Source: Outdoor Air / Ground / Water
Even cold air contains thermal energy"] end subgraph DEVICE["HEAT PUMP DEVICE"] D1["Cyclic Heat Pump
Same Reverse Carnot Cycle"] end subgraph HOT["HIGH-TEMPERATURE SINK at T_H"] H1["Heat Destination: Indoor Space / Water Tank
Heated Air or Hot Water"] end subgraph WORK["WORK INPUT"] W1["Compressor / Power Source
W_net_in"] end C1 -->|"Q_L (Heat Absorbed from outdoors)"| D1 D1 -->|"Q_H (Heat Delivered indoors)"| H1 W1 -->|"W_net_in (Energy Input)"| D1 style DEVICE fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:3px style COLD fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#003D7A,stroke-width:2px style HOT fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px style WORK fill:#FFFFCC,stroke:#999900,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Refrigerator vs Heat Pump — Comparison Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph REFRIGERATOR["REFRIGERATOR MODE"] R1["Q_L from Cold Space"] --> R2["Refrigerator Device"] R2 --> R3["Q_H to Surroundings"] W1["W_net_in"] --> R2 end subgraph HEATPUMP["HEAT PUMP MODE"] H1["Q_L from Outdoor Air"] --> H2["Heat Pump Device"] H2 --> H3["Q_H to Indoor Space"] W2["W_net_in"] --> H2 end REFRIGERATOR -.->|"SAME HARDWARE
Different Desired Output"| HEATPUMP ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The same physical hardware (compressor, condenser, evaporator, expansion valve) serves both modes. A **reversing valve** (4-way valve) switches the direction of refrigerant flow to swap the roles of the indoor and outdoor coils. This is the principle behind **reversible split AC units**. ## 4.5 P-V Diagram Representation (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PVCYCLE["P-V DIAGRAM OF REVERSE CARNOT CYCLE"] P1["Point 1: Low P, Low V
Saturated Vapor at T_L"] -->|Isentropic Compression| P2["Point 2: High P, Higher V
Superheated Vapor at T_H"] P2 -->|Isothermal Rejection| P3["Point 3: High P, Lower V
Saturated Liquid at T_H"] P3 -->|Isentropic Expansion| P4["Point 4: Low P, Lowest V
Liquid-Vapor Mix at T_L"] P4 -->|Isothermal Absorption| P1 end ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a Refrigerator. [3 Marks] **Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The **Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator** is defined as the ratio of the desired cooling effect (heat removed from the cold space) to the work input required to achieve it. Mathematically: $$COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$$ Where: - $Q_L$ = rate of heat absorption from the cold reservoir (kW or kJ) - $W_{net,in}$ = net rate of work input to the cycle (kW or kJ) The COP is a **dimensionless quantity** and is always **greater than 1** for a practical refrigerator. A higher COP indicates a more efficient refrigerator. For the **reversible Carnot refrigerator**: $$COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ where $T_L$ and $T_H$ are the absolute temperatures (in Kelvin) of the cold and hot reservoirs, respectively. ### Question 2: Distinguish between a Refrigerator and a Heat Pump. [3 Marks] **Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | **Basis** | **Refrigerator** | **Heat Pump** | |---|---|---| | **Objective** | To maintain a body at a temperature **lower** than the surroundings | To supply heat to a body at a temperature **higher** than the surroundings | | **Desired Output** | Cooling effect $Q_L$ | Heating effect $Q_H$ | | **COP Definition** | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$ | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$ | | **Application** | Food preservation, AC, cold storage | Space heating, water heating | | **Value Range** | Typically $1$ to $7$ | Always $\geq COP_R + 1$ | Both devices operate on the **same thermodynamic cycle** (e.g., vapor compression, reverse Carnot). The key mathematical relationship is: $$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$$ This follows directly from the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a cyclic device. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]` **A refrigerator using R-134a operates between a cold space at $-15^\circ C$ and the surroundings at $35^\circ C$. The actual COP achieved is 3.0. The cooling load on the refrigerator is 5 kW.** #### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the actual power input to the refrigerator and the rate of heat rejection to the surroundings. Compare the actual COP with the Carnot COP and comment on the irreversibilities present. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Convert temperatures to Kelvin** $$T_L = -15 + 273.15 = 258.15 \text{ K}$$ $$T_H = 35 + 273.15 = 308.15 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2: Calculate the Carnot COP (theoretical maximum)** $$COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{258.15}{308.15 - 258.15} = \frac{258.15}{50} = 5.163$$ **Step 3: Calculate the actual power input** Using the actual $COP_R = 3.0$: $$\dot{W}_{net,in} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{COP_{R,actual}} = \frac{5 \text{ kW}}{3.0} = 1.667 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4: Calculate the rate of heat rejection** By the First Law of Thermodynamics: $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{net,in} = 5 + 1.667 = 6.667 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5: Comment on irreversibilities** - The actual COP (3.0) is **less than** the Carnot COP (5.163), confirming the presence of irreversibilities. - The **Carnot COP is the theoretical maximum**; no real refrigerator can exceed it. - Sources of irreversibility include: 1. Friction in the compressor 2. Pressure drops across the condenser, evaporator, and connecting pipes 3. Heat transfer across finite temperature differences 4. Non-ideal expansion (throttling instead of isentropic) 5. Heat leaks from the hot to the cold region - The **second-law efficiency** (or Carnot efficiency ratio) is: $$\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{R,actual}}{COP_{R,Carnot}} = \frac{3.0}{5.163} = 0.581 \text{ or } 58.1\%$$ This indicates that the actual refrigerator achieves **58.1% of the ideal performance**. #### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the same device is used as a heat pump for space heating in winter, with the same actual COP for the refrigerator mode, calculate the COP of the heat pump, the rate of heat supplied to the indoor space, and the electrical energy cost per day (assuming the heat pump runs 10 hours/day and electricity costs ₹8 per kWh). **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate the Heat Pump COP using the fundamental relation** $$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 3.0 + 1 = 4.0$$ **Step 2: Calculate the rate of heat supplied to the indoor space** The work input rate is the same as in part (a): $\dot{W}_{net,in} = 1.667$ kW. $$\dot{Q}_H = COP_{HP} \times \dot{W}_{net,in} = 4.0 \times 1.667 = 6.667 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3: Calculate the total electrical energy consumed per day** $$E_{daily} = \dot{W}_{net,in} \times t = 1.667 \text{ kW} \times 10 \text{ h} = 16.67 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 4: Calculate the daily electricity cost** $$\text{Cost} = E_{daily} \times \text{Rate} = 16.67 \times 8 = \text{₹}133.36$$ **Step 5: Calculate the total heat supplied to the indoor space per day** $$Q_{H,total} = \dot{Q}_H \times t = 6.667 \text{ kW} \times 10 \text{ h} = 66.67 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 6: Comment** For every 1 kWh of electricity, the heat pump delivers **4 kWh of heat** to the indoor space. The remaining 3 kWh is "free" energy extracted from the outdoor environment. This is why heat pumps are highly energy-efficient for space heating. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **A reverse Carnot refrigerator is used to maintain a frozen food storage at $-20^\circ C$. The heat leakage into the cold space is 1.5 kW, and the ambient temperature is $30^\circ C$.** #### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Determine the COP of the refrigerator, the power required to drive the system, and the rate at which heat is rejected to the surroundings. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Convert temperatures to Kelvin** $$T_L = -20 + 273.15 = 253.15 \text{ K}$$ $$T_H = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2: Calculate the Carnot COP** For a reverse Carnot cycle, this is also the actual COP: $$COP_{R} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{253.15}{303.15 - 253.15} = \frac{253.15}{50} = 5.063$$ **Step 3: Calculate the power required** The heat leakage into the cold space is the cooling load that must be removed: $$\dot{Q}_L = 1.5 \text{ kW}$$ $$\dot{W}_{net,in} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{COP_R} = \frac{1.5}{5.063} = 0.2963 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4: Calculate the rate of heat rejection** $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{net,in} = 1.5 + 0.2963 = 1.7963 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5: Verification using the alternative formula** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{\dot{Q}_H}{\dot{W}_{net,in}} = \frac{1.7963}{0.2963} = 6.063$$ $$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 5.063 + 1 = 6.063 \quad \checkmark$$ #### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the same system is used as a heat pump, calculate the COP of the heat pump and the heating capacity. Also, determine the percentage improvement in COP if the cold space temperature is raised to $-10^\circ C$ while keeping the ambient temperature constant. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Heat pump COP** $$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 5.063 + 1 = 6.063$$ **Step 2: Heating capacity** The heating capacity equals the heat rejection rate calculated in part (a): $$\dot{Q}_H = 1.7963 \text{ kW} = 1796.3 \text{ W}$$ **Step 3: Recalculate COP with $T_L = -10^\circ C$** $$T_L^{new} = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15 \text{ K}$$ $$COP_R^{new} = \frac{T_L^{new}}{T_H - T_L^{new}} = \frac{263.15}{303.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{40} = 6.579$$ **Step 4: Calculate the percentage improvement** $$\%\text{ Improvement} = \frac{COP_R^{new} - COP_R^{old}}{COP_R^{old}} \times 100$$ $$\%\text{ Improvement} = \frac{6.579 - 5.063}{5.063} \times 100 = \frac{1.516}{5.063} \times 100 = 29.94\%$$ **Step 5: Comment** Raising the cold space temperature from $-20^\circ C$ to $-10^\circ C$ reduces the temperature difference $T_H - T_L$ from 50 K to 40 K, which **decreases the temperature lift** required by the compressor. This results in a higher COP and greater energy efficiency. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Temperature Unit Error:** Using °C instead of **Kelvin** in the Carnot COP formula. This is a **fatal error** that will fetch **zero marks** for the entire calculation step. Always convert to K first: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. > > 2. **Sign Convention Confusion:** Mixing up $Q_L$ and $Q_H$ in the formulas. Remember: > - $Q_L$ = heat absorbed from cold reservoir (entering the system) > - $Q_H$ = heat rejected to hot reservoir (leaving the system) > - $W_{net,in}$ = work input to the system > > 3. **Confusing $COP_R$ with $COP_{HP}$:** Many students use $COP_R = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ for a refrigerator. This is wrong — that's the **heat pump** formula. > > 4. **Forgetting the Energy Balance:** Always verify that $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,in}$. In numerical problems, the examiner often gives data that allows cross-checking. > > 5. **Not Stating Units:** Even though COP is dimensionless, the **work input and heat transfer rates must have units** (kW, kJ/s, or W). Missing units can cost 0.5 to 1 mark per sub-question. > > 6. **Writing the Formula but Not Substituting:** In KTU valuation, a correct formula with no substitution is worth **partial credit** (typically 1-2 marks out of 7). Always show the substituted numerical values. > > 7. **Ignoring Internal Choice:** In Part B questions, **always attempt both Question A and Question B options** mentally before writing. Choose the one you can answer more confidently. The examiner evaluates only the **first attempted** if both are written. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Refrigerators and Heat Pumps** > > - **Refrigerator:** Cyclic device that **absorbs heat from a cold body** using work input. > - **Heat Pump:** Same hardware as a refrigerator; the **goal is to deliver heat to a hot body**. > - **$COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$** — Always > 1 for practical refrigerators. > - **$COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$** — Always > 1 (and always > $COP_R$). > - **Master Relation:** $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ — derive this from the First Law. > - **Carnot COP (Refrigerator):** $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ > - **Carnot COP (Heat Pump):** $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ > - **Temperatures in Carnot formulas MUST be in Kelvin.** > - **Reverse Carnot Cycle:** Four processes — Isentropic compression → Isothermal heat rejection → Isentropic expansion → Isothermal heat absorption. Runs **counter-clockwise** on T-s and P-V diagrams. > - **Second Law Limit:** $COP_{real} < COP_{Carnot}$ for all real devices. > - **Second-Law Efficiency:** $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}}$ — measures how close a real device is to the ideal. > - **Energy Balance (Cyclic Device):** $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,in}$ > - **Heat Ratio (Reversible):** $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ > - **Real-World Applications:** Domestic refrigerators, window/split ACs, heat pumps for winter heating, cryogenic liquefaction, cold chain logistics, data center cooling, industrial process chilling. > - **Irreversibilities in Real Cycles:** Friction, pressure drops, finite temperature differences, non-isentropic compression/expansion, throttling losses, heat leaks. > - **Typical Real COP Values:** Refrigerator $COP_R$ = 1.5 to 3.0; Heat pump $COP_{HP}$ = 3.0 to 4.0. Values above 5 are exceptional. > - **Definition of Ton of Refrigeration (Extra):** 1 TR = 3.517 kW = heat removal rate to freeze 1 ton of water at 0°C to ice at 0°C in 24 hours. Useful for industrial refrigeration problems. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to compare a real cycle with the Carnot cycle, always compute the **second-law efficiency** $\eta_{II} = COP_{actual}/COP_{Carnot}$. This is a frequent 2-mark follow-up question in KTU Part B problems and demonstrates a deep understanding of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

topics.title: Kelvin-Planck Statement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kelvin-Planck Statement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Kelvin-Planck Statement** of the Second Law of Thermodynamics is formally stated as:

> **"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a thermodynamic cycle and produces no effect other than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."**

In simpler terms, a heat engine operating in a closed cycle cannot convert all the heat it receives from a single thermal reservoir entirely into work. A portion of the heat input must always be rejected to a **lower-temperature sink**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This statement is the cornerstone of the Second Law and forms the theoretical basis for the existence, design, and efficiency limits of all practical heat engines (Carnot, Rankine, Otto, Diesel cycles). It is the "Impossibility Theorem" of 100% thermal-to-mechanical energy conversion.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a water wheel that turns only when water flows from a high reservoir **down** to a low reservoir. The wheel extracts useful work (rotation) from this **flow** (temperature difference).

- If we try to extract work from water sitting in a single, flat pond with no lower level to drain into — the wheel simply will **not** turn.
- Similarly, a heat engine needs a **temperature gradient** (hot source → cold sink) to continuously produce work.
- The Kelvin-Planck statement says you **cannot** build a perfect engine that turns **all** the heat from a single hot pond into mechanical work without a cold pond to dump the leftover energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** "You cannot have a 100% efficient heat engine. Some heat MUST be wasted."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range (Engineering) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat extracted from source | $Q_H$ | **kJ** or **kJ/kg** | 100 – 10,000 kJ |
| Heat rejected to sink | $Q_L$ | **kJ** or **kJ/kg** | 30 – 7,000 kJ |
| Net work output | $W_{net}$ | **kJ** or **kJ/kg** | 10 – 6,000 kJ |
| Source Temperature | $T_H$ | **K (Kelvin)** | 500 K – 2000 K |
| Sink Temperature | $T_L$ | **K (Kelvin)** | 280 K – 350 K |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | **Dimensionless (%)** | 0% < $\eta_{th}$ < 100% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** Temperatures in the Second Law efficiency equations **MUST** be in **Kelvin (K)**, not °C. Failing to convert °C → K is a guaranteed mark deduction in the KTU valuation desk.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Breakdown)

The Kelvin-Planck statement can be understood as a sequence of logical deductions:

1. **Step 1 — The Hypothesis:** Suppose a hypothetical "perfect" heat engine exists that converts heat $Q_H$ from a single reservoir entirely into work $W$, with $Q_L = 0$.
2. **Step 2 — Energy Balance:** From the First Law, for a cyclic device: $W = Q_H - Q_L$. If $Q_L = 0$, then $W = Q_H$ (100% conversion).
3. **Step 3 — The Contradiction:** Such a device would violate real-world observations — friction, irreversibility, and the directional nature of heat flow.
4. **Step 4 — The Conclusion:** It is **impossible**. Therefore, $Q_L > 0$ is mandatory. At least some heat must always be rejected to a sink at a lower temperature.
5. **Step 5 — The Engineering Outcome:** Every practical heat engine must interact with **at least two thermal reservoirs** (source + sink) to produce continuous work.

## 2.2 Why Does the Second Law Exist? (The "Why" & "How")

- **Why?** The First Law only restricts **quantity** of energy (energy cannot be created or destroyed). It does NOT restrict **quality** of energy conversion.
- **How?** The Second Law restricts the **direction** and **quality** of energy transfer. It tells us that *not all heat can be converted to work*.
- **The Bridge:** Without the Kelvin-Planck statement, we would have no theoretical upper limit on engine efficiency — every real engine would be "broken" in the sense of perpetual motion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First Law for Heat Engine Cycle | $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ | Net work from energy balance |
| 2 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | Fraction of heat converted to work |
| 3 | Kelvin-Planck Limit | $\eta_{th} < 1 \quad \text{or} \quad 100\%$ | NO engine can be 100% efficient |
| 4 | Carnot (Reversible) Efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Maximum possible efficiency |
| 5 | Inequality Form (Strict) | $Q_L > 0$ for a single reservoir engine | Formal Kelvin-Planck condition |
| 6 | Clausius Inequality Equivalent | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ | General Second Law form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** Kelvin-Planck forbids a **single-reservoir** engine from having $\eta_{th} = 100\%$. The Clausius statement forbids a **single-reservoir** refrigerator. The two statements are **equivalent** — proving one automatically proves the other.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants (Steam, Gas, Nuclear):** The Kelvin-Planck statement justifies why condensers and cooling towers are mandatory.
- **Internal Combustion Engines:** Defines why exhaust gases carry away ~30-40% of combustion energy.
- **Jet & Rocket Engines:** The expelled high-temperature gases are the mandatory "$Q_L$" equivalent.
- **Solar Thermal Systems:** Explains why solar concentrators alone cannot drive a continuous work-producing cycle without a heat rejection mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From the Kelvin-Planck Statement to Thermal Efficiency Bound

We will mathematically derive the upper bound of thermal efficiency starting from the Kelvin-Planck premise.

### Step 1 — Assume a Cyclic Heat Engine

Consider a heat engine operating in a thermodynamic cycle. It interacts with:
- A **hot reservoir** at $T_H$, from which it draws heat $Q_H$.
- A **cold reservoir** at $T_L$, to which it rejects heat $Q_L$.
- It produces net work $W_{net}$.

### Step 2 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics

For a cyclic process, the change in internal energy is zero ($\Delta U = 0$). Therefore:

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

This is the **First Law energy balance** for any cyclic heat engine. By KTU convention, $Q_H$ and $W_{net}$ are taken as **positive** outputs, and $Q_L$ is a **positive** heat rejection.

### Step 3 — Apply the Kelvin-Planck Statement

The Kelvin-Planck statement asserts that:

$$Q_L > 0$$

This is a **strict inequality** — meaning $Q_L$ can never equal zero for any device that interacts with a single reservoir.

### Step 4 — Derive the Efficiency Limit

The thermal efficiency of the heat engine is defined as:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$$

Substituting the First Law expression for $W_{net}$:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H}$$

Perform the algebraic division:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Inequality $Q_L > 0$

Since both $Q_L$ and $Q_H$ are positive quantities (magnitudes of heat transfer), the ratio $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} > 0$.

Therefore:

$$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} < 1$$

Hence:

$$\eta_{th} < 1 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \eta_{th} < 100\%$$

### Step 6 — Conclusion of the Derivation

The Kelvin-Planck statement, combined with the First Law, mathematically proves that **no heat engine can achieve 100% thermal efficiency**. The maximum theoretical efficiency is reached only for a reversible (Carnot) engine:

$$\eta_{max} = \eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners expect you to explicitly state that $Q_L > 0$ is the **inequality** that drives the proof. Missing this step is a common -2 mark error.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Kelvin-Planck vs. Clausius Statement

| Feature | Kelvin-Planck Statement | Clausius Statement |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject** | Heat Engine | Refrigerator / Heat Pump |
| **Core Forbidding** | 100% heat-to-work conversion | Spontaneous heat flow from cold → hot |
| **Mathematical Form** | $\eta_{th} < 1$ (for single-reservoir) | $COP < \infty$ (for single-reservoir) |
| **Heat Rejection** | $Q_L > 0$ mandatory | $W_{in} > 0$ mandatory |
| **Practical Example** | Steam Power Plant | Household Refrigerator |
| **Equivalence** | If violated, Clausius is violated | If violated, Kelvin-Planck is violated |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Heat Engine Process Architecture (Kelvin-Planck Compliant)

The following diagram illustrates the **mandatory** energy flow pattern of any real heat engine that satisfies the Kelvin-Planck statement.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["HOT RESERVOIR TH"] -->|QH Heat In| B["HEAT ENGINE CYCLE DEVICE"]
    B -->|Wnet Net Work Out| C["WORK OUTPUT SHAFT OR PISTON"]
    B -->|QL Heat Rejected| D["COLD RESERVOIR TL"]
    E["KELVIN PLANCK CONSTRAINT"] -.->|QL must be greater than zero| B
    F["FIRST LAW BALANCE"] -.->|Wnet equals QH minus QL| B
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- **Node A (Hot Reservoir):** Supplies heat $Q_H$ to the engine.
- **Node B (Heat Engine):** The cyclic device converting heat to work.
- **Node C (Work Output):** Useful mechanical or electrical work.
- **Node D (Cold Reservoir):** Mandatory heat rejection target.
- **Dotted Constraint E:** Enforces the Kelvin-Planck rule — $Q_L$ **cannot** be zero.
- **Dotted Constraint F:** Enforces the First Law energy balance.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hypothetical Violation vs. Realistic Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "IMPOSSIBLE PER KELVIN PLANCK"
        P1["Single Reservoir TH"] -->|QH equals Wnet| P2["PERFECT ENGINE 100 percent"]
        P2 -.->|No Sink Needed| X["VIOLATES SECOND LAW"]
    end
    subgraph "ACTUAL REAL ENGINE"
        R1["Hot Reservoir TH"] -->|QH| R3["Real Engine"]
        R3 -->|Wnet less than QH| R4["Work Output"]
        R3 -->|QL greater than zero| R5["Cold Reservoir TL"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Takeaway:** The "perfect engine" branch has **no cold reservoir** — this is precisely what the Kelvin-Planck statement forbids. Every valid engine must include the **Cold Reservoir** node.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Practical Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Boiler Fuel Source"] --> S2["High Temp Heat QH"]
    S2 --> S3["Turbine Work Output"]
    S3 --> S4["Low Pressure Exhaust"]
    S4 --> S5["Condenser QL Rejection"]
    S5 --> S6["Cooling Tower or River"]
    S3 -.->|Net Work| S7["Generator Electrical Output"]
```

This mirrors the real-world **Rankine Cycle**, where the condenser is the physical embodiment of the Kelvin-Planck-imposed $Q_L$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State the Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Why is it impossible to construct a 100% efficient heat engine?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Statement (2 Marks):** "It is impossible to construct a device operating in a thermodynamic cycle that produces no effect other than the absorption of heat from a single reservoir and the production of an equivalent amount of work."

> **Impossibility Reason (1 Mark):** Such a device would have thermal efficiency $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W}{Q_H} = 100\%$, requiring $Q_L = 0$. This violates the Second Law because the engine would not need a cold reservoir, contradicting the observed irreversible nature of heat transfer.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between the Kelvin-Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Kelvin-Planck | Clausius |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | Heat Engines | Refrigerators / Heat Pumps |
| **Forbids** | $Q_L = 0$ (perfect engine) | $W_{in} = 0$ (perfect refrigerator) |
| **Core Idea** | Heat cannot be fully converted to work | Heat cannot flow from cold to hot spontaneously |

> **Conclusion (1 Mark):** Both statements are **equivalent**; the violation of one implies the violation of the other.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** A heat engine operates between a source at **1000 K** and a sink at **300 K**. It receives **1000 kJ** of heat from the source and rejects **400 kJ** to the sink.  
    **(i)** Calculate the net work output.  
    **(ii)** Calculate the thermal efficiency.  
    **(iii)** Comment on whether the engine violates the Kelvin-Planck statement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Prove mathematically that no heat engine operating between two reservoirs can have an efficiency greater than the Carnot efficiency. State the Carnot theorem. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $T_H = 1000$ K, $T_L = 300$ K
- $Q_H = 1000$ kJ, $Q_L = 400$ kJ

**(i) Net Work Output** (1 Mark):

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

$$W_{net} = 1000 - 400 = 600 \text{ kJ}$$

**(ii) Thermal Efficiency** (2 Marks):

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{600}{1000} = 0.60 \text{ or } 60\%$$

**(iii) Kelvin-Planck Compliance Check** (4 Marks):

Step 1 — Compute the Carnot (maximum possible) efficiency:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{1000} = 0.70 \text{ or } 70\%$$

Step 2 — Compare:

$$\eta_{th} = 60\% < \eta_{Carnot} = 70\%$$

Step 3 — Since $Q_L = 400$ kJ $> 0$, heat is being rejected to the sink.

> **Conclusion:** The engine **does NOT violate** the Kelvin-Planck statement. It is feasible because $\eta_{th} < \eta_{Carnot}$ and $Q_L > 0$ is satisfied.

#### Part (b) Solution (7 Marks)

**Carnot Theorem Statement (2 Marks):**

1. The efficiency of a reversible heat engine operating between two given reservoirs is **independent** of the working substance and depends only on the reservoir temperatures.
2. No irreversible heat engine operating between the same two reservoirs can have an efficiency greater than the reversible (Carnot) engine.

**Mathematical Proof (5 Marks):**

Consider two engines operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$:
- **Engine 1:** Reversible (Carnot) with efficiency $\eta_{rev}$
- **Engine 2:** Any other engine (real) with efficiency $\eta_{th}$

Assume for contradiction that $\eta_{th} > \eta_{rev}$.

**Case 1: $\eta_{th} > \eta_{rev}$ and equal $Q_H$:**

$$\frac{W_{th}}{Q_H} > \frac{W_{rev}}{Q_H} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad W_{th} > W_{rev}$$

From the First Law:

$$Q_{L,rev} = Q_H - W_{rev} \quad \text{and} \quad Q_{L,th} = Q_H - W_{th}$$

Since $W_{th} > W_{rev}$:

$$Q_{L,th} < Q_{L,rev}$$

This means the real engine rejects **less** heat to the cold sink than the Carnot engine for the same $Q_H$.

**The Contradiction (2 Marks):**

If we now run the Carnot engine in **reverse** (as a refrigerator) using the work from Engine 2, the net effect would be:
- Heat $Q_{L,rev} - Q_{L,th}$ is transferred from the cold sink to the hot source.
- **No net work** is exchanged with the surroundings.
- The Second Law (Clausius) is violated — heat spontaneously flows from cold to hot.

**Conclusion:** Our assumption $\eta_{th} > \eta_{rev}$ is **false**. Therefore:

$$\eta_{th} \leq \eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

Equality holds only for a reversible (Carnot) engine.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the Kelvin-Planck statement. Why is a heat engine required to reject heat to a low-temperature sink? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A power plant receives **5000 kJ/s** of heat from a furnace at **1500 K** and rejects heat to a river at **300 K**.  
    **(i)** Find the maximum possible thermal efficiency.  
    **(ii)** Find the maximum possible power output.  
    **(iii)** Find the rate of heat rejection to the river. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) Solution (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):** (Refer to the Mermaid diagrams in Section 4.1)

The diagram shows a hot reservoir supplying $Q_H$ to a heat engine, which produces work $W_{net}$ and rejects $Q_L$ to a cold reservoir.

**Explanation (2 Marks):**

The Kelvin-Planck statement asserts that a heat engine interacting with a single thermal reservoir cannot convert all the heat it absorbs into work. Some heat must be rejected.

**Why a Cold Sink is Required (2 Marks):**

A heat engine operates by exploiting a **temperature difference**. The work output is proportional to the heat flow across this gradient. Without a cold sink:
- There is no temperature gradient for continuous operation.
- The system would violate the directional nature of heat flow (Second Law).
