- The engine could not complete a cycle without violating the Kelvin-Planck constraint.

#### Part (b) Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\dot{Q}_H = 5000$ kJ/s
- $T_H = 1500$ K, $T_L = 300$ K

**(i) Maximum Thermal Efficiency (2 Marks):**

The maximum efficiency is the Carnot efficiency:

$$\eta_{max} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{1500} = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80 \text{ or } 80\%$$

**(ii) Maximum Power Output (2 Marks):**

$$\dot{W}_{max} = \eta_{max} \times \dot{Q}_H = 0.80 \times 5000 = 4000 \text{ kJ/s} = 4000 \text{ kW} = 4 \text{ MW}$$

**(iii) Rate of Heat Rejection (3 Marks):**

From the First Law energy balance:

$$\dot{Q}_L = \dot{Q}_H - \dot{W}_{max}$$

$$\dot{Q}_L = 5000 - 4000 = 1000 \text{ kJ/s} = 1000 \text{ kW} = 1 \text{ MW}$$

> **Conclusion:** Even with a perfect (reversible) engine, **20%** of the input heat **must** be rejected to the river. This is the Kelvin-Planck statement in numerical form.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Temperature Unit Error (–1 to –2 marks):** Using $T_L = 27$°C instead of $T_L = 300$ K. Always convert to Kelvin.
> 2. **Sign Convention Confusion (–1 mark):** Forgetting that $Q_L$ is the magnitude of rejected heat (positive in KTU's typical convention).
> 3. **Missing Inequality Statement (–1 mark):** Not explicitly writing $Q_L > 0$ in derivations.
> 4. **Carnot Confusion (–2 marks):** Applying Carnot efficiency to an irreversible engine's *actual* performance — Carnot is the **upper bound**, not the actual efficiency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Kelvin-Planck Statement (Formal):** No cyclic device can convert heat from a single reservoir entirely into work.
- **Core Inequality:** $Q_L > 0$ for any single-reservoir engine.
- **Efficiency Bound:** $\eta_{th} < 100\%$ for all real heat engines.
- **Maximum Efficiency (Carnot):** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ — achieved only by reversible engines.
- **Mandatory Two Reservoirs:** Every practical engine needs a hot source **AND** a cold sink.
- **Equivalence with Clausius:** Kelvin-Planck and Clausius statements are logically equivalent.
- **Unit Rule:** Always use **Kelvin (K)** for $T_H$ and $T_L$ in efficiency formulas.
- **First Law Companion:** $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ is the energy balance that pairs with the Second Law inequality.
- **Real-World Anchors:** Steam power plants, car engines, jet engines, gas turbines — all obey this law.
- **KTU Exam Frequency:** This topic appears in nearly every KTU ESE cycle — expect it as a 3-mark definition or a 7-14 mark application/proof question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Second Law: Clausius Statement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Second Law: Clausius Statement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Clausius Statement (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CLAUSIUS STATEMENT OF THE SECOND LAW OF THERMODYNAMICS**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*
> — **Rudolf Clausius (1850)**

In simpler, KTU-board-friendly language:

> *"Heat cannot flow spontaneously from a colder body to a hotter body without the input of external work."*

This statement explicitly identifies the **natural direction** of heat transfer. Heat naturally flows from **high temperature (hot reservoir)** to **low temperature (cold reservoir)**. The reverse process (cold → hot) requires an external agent such as a **refrigerator** or **heat pump**, which consumes work.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two cups of water:
- **Cup A** at 80 °C (hot)
- **Cup B** at 20 °C (cold)

**What happens naturally?** Heat flows from Cup A → Cup B until both reach 50 °C (thermal equilibrium). This is **natural, spontaneous, and requires no external input**.

**What Clausius forbids?** The reverse — heat flowing from Cup B (20 °C) → Cup A (80 °C) — making Cup A hotter and Cup B colder, **without any external work**. This would violate our everyday experience and is **forbidden by the Second Law**.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Cold Reservoir Temperature | **T_C (K)** |
| Hot Reservoir Temperature | **T_H (K)** |
| Direction of Spontaneous Heat Flow | **High T → Low T** |
| Direction Forbidden by Clausius | **Low T → High T (without work)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** The Clausius statement is a **negative statement** (it says what is *impossible*), similar in structure to the Kelvin-Planck statement. The Clausius and Kelvin-Planck statements are **proved to be equivalent** — violating one implies violating the other.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Absolute Zero:** **0 K = −273.15 °C**
- All temperatures in thermodynamic Second Law analysis **MUST be in Kelvin** to maintain dimensional correctness and physical meaning.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Clausius Statement

The Clausius statement can be decomposed into the following logical elements:

1. **Device Operates in a Cycle:** The system returns to its initial state. No net change occurs within the system itself. This is the hallmark of a **cyclic device** (e.g., refrigerator, heat pump).
2. **Sole Effect on Surroundings:** The *only* result on the surroundings is the heat transfer Q_C from the cold body to the hot body. Nothing else changes (no work extracted, no mass transferred, etc.).
3. **Direction of Heat Transfer:** Heat is moved from a **lower temperature** reservoir to a **higher temperature** reservoir.
4. **Impossibility:** This process **cannot occur spontaneously**; it must violate at least one of the other fundamental principles (typically energy conservation or another Second Law form).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Clausius Statement — Microscopic View

From **statistical thermodynamics**:
- A system with high temperature has higher average molecular kinetic energy.
- Spontaneous heat flow from cold → hot would imply that molecules in the cold body spontaneously organize themselves to transfer energy to already-higher-energy molecules in the hot body.
- This would require a **decrease in entropy** of an isolated system, which is **statistically improbable** (effectively impossible) because there are vastly more microstates corresponding to the equilibrium (heat-dispersed) configuration.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** This statistical argument connects directly to the **Clausius Inequality** and the **Entropy Principle** covered later in the module. The Clausius Statement is essentially the macroscopic declaration of the **entropy non-decrease principle**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clausius Statement (Verbal) | $Q_C \text{ (from low T) } \rightarrow Q_H \text{ (to high T)}$ | Heat flow cold→hot forbidden without work | — |
| Clausius Inequality | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ | For any cyclic process | Dimensionless |
| Equality Condition | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$ | Holds only for **reversible** cycles | Dimensionless |
| Reversible Heat Transfer | $Q = T \cdot \Delta S$ | For internally & externally reversible | J (since T·K × J/K) |
| COP of Refrigerator | $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | Performance metric | Dimensionless |
| COP of Heat Pump | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | Performance metric | Dimensionless |
| Maximum COP (Reversible) | $\text{COP}_{R,max} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot refrigerator limit | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** In any problem, **temperatures T_L and T_H must be in Kelvin (K)**. Using Celsius will produce numerically wrong answers and is the **#1 cause of mark deductions** in board exams.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Clausius Statement directly underpins the design and operation of:

1. **Refrigeration Systems** (Domestic refrigerators, AC units, cold storage) — *requires work input to move heat from cold to hot.*
2. **Heat Pumps** (Building heating systems, water heaters) — *reverse-cycle devices for heating applications.*
3. **Cryogenic Engineering** (LNG liquefaction, medical cryogenics) — *extreme cold-to-hot heat pumping.*
4. **Vapor Compression & Absorption Cycles** — *all depend on the work-heat exchange dictated by the Second Law.*
5. **Distillation Columns & Chemical Process Industry** — *refrigeration and heat-pumping for separation processes.*

> Without the Clausius Statement, **no refrigerator, no air conditioner, and no heat pump could exist** — because the entire industry of "moving heat uphill" depends on the precise impossibility that Clausius declared.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Proof of Equivalence: Clausius Statement ⇔ Kelvin-Planck Statement

This is a **high-yield KTU derivation**. The two statements are **logically equivalent** — a violation of one implies a violation of the other. The proof is by contradiction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Significance:** KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Show that the Clausius and Kelvin-Planck statements of the Second Law are equivalent."* (Usually 7 marks — Part B sub-question).

---

#### **Part A: Violation of Clausius ⇒ Violation of Kelvin-Planck**

**Assumption (for contradiction):** Suppose there exists a **Clausius-violating refrigerator (R)** that transfers heat $Q_C$ from a cold reservoir at $T_C$ to a hot reservoir at $T_H$ **without any work input** ($W = 0$).

Now, couple this refrigerator with a **heat engine (E)** operating between the same two reservoirs, producing work $W$.

**Step 1: Heat engine takes $Q_H'$ from hot reservoir at $T_H$.**

The hot reservoir receives **net heat**:
$$
Q_{H,net} = Q_H - Q_C
$$

**Step 2: Apply energy balance to the combined system (Refrigerator + Heat Engine).**

Since both devices together form a cyclic system returning to its initial state, the net work of the combined device is:
$$
W_{net} = W \quad (\text{from heat engine alone, since } W_R = 0)
$$

**Step 3: The combined system exchanges heat only with the cold reservoir.**

For the combined system:
- **Hot reservoir:** Loses $Q_H'$ (to engine) and gains $Q_C$ (from refrigerator) → net heat transfer to hot reservoir = $Q_H' - Q_C$. To make the *combined* system appear to violate Kelvin-Planck, we need the **net heat exchange with the hot reservoir to be zero** (i.e., a unidirectional device).

To make the violation explicit, we can set $Q_C = Q_H'$ (by choosing the heat engine's heat input equal to the refrigerator's heat output to the hot reservoir).

**Step 4: Resulting scenario.**

The **combined device**:
- Takes $Q_C$ from cold reservoir,
- Produces net work $W$,
- Exchanges **zero net heat** with the hot reservoir.

This is precisely a **violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement** (a cyclic device that converts heat entirely from a single reservoir into work).

**Conclusion:** A violation of the Clausius statement **⇒** violation of Kelvin-Planck statement. ∎

---

#### **Part B: Violation of Kelvin-Planck ⇒ Violation of Clausius**

**Assumption (for contradiction):** Suppose a **Kelvin-Planck-violating heat engine (E)** operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_C$ such that $Q_C = 0$ (no heat rejected to cold reservoir) and produces work $W = Q_H$ from heat taken only from the hot reservoir.

Now, drive a **refrigerator (R)** using this work $W$, which moves heat $Q_L$ from cold reservoir to hot reservoir.

**Step 1: Energy balance of refrigerator:**
$$
Q_H' = Q_L + W = Q_L + Q_H
$$

**Step 2: Net heat exchange with cold reservoir.**

The cold reservoir **loses** $Q_L$ (to the refrigerator) and **gains nothing** (since the engine rejects $Q_C = 0$).

**Step 3: Net work of combined system.**

For the combined system (engine + refrigerator):
$$
W_{net} = W - W = 0
$$
(since the engine's work is consumed by the refrigerator).

**Step 4: Resulting scenario.**

The **combined device**:
- Operates cyclically,
- Produces **zero net work**,
- Transfers heat $Q_L$ from the **cold reservoir** to the **hot reservoir**,
- Has **no other effect on the surroundings**.

This is precisely a **violation of the Clausius statement**.

**Conclusion:** A violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement **⇒** violation of Clausius statement. ∎

---

#### **Combined Conclusion**

$$
\boxed{\text{Violation of Clausius} \iff \text{Violation of Kelvin-Planck}}
$$

Therefore, the two statements are **logically equivalent** and are simply different verbal expressions of the same underlying physical law.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Two Statements

| **Feature** | **Clausius Statement** | **Kelvin-Planck Statement** |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Heat flow direction (cold→hot) | Heat-to-work conversion (100% conversion) |
| **Forbidden Device** | Self-acting refrigerator | Single-reservoir heat engine |
| **Implied Device (if violated)** | Refrigerator with $W = 0$ | Heat engine with $Q_C = 0$ |
| **Real-world Application** | Refrigeration, Heat Pumps | Heat Engines, Power Plants |
| **Equivalent Form** | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ | $\eta_{th} < 1$ for any cyclic engine |
| **Logical Status** | Equivalent to K-P | Equivalent to Clausius |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Clausius Statement: Natural vs. Forbidden Heat Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Reservoir T_H] -- Natural Direction, Allowed --> B[Cold Reservoir T_C]
    B -. Forbidden without work input .-> A
    A2[Hot Reservoir T_H] -- Q_H --> C[Refrigerator Device]
    C -- Q_L extracted --> B2[Cold Reservoir T_L]
    C -- Work input W --> D[Work Source External]
    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A2 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B2 fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Solid arrow (top):** Natural, spontaneous heat flow from hot to cold. Always allowed.
- **Dotted arrow (top):** Cold-to-hot heat flow **without work** — **FORBIDDEN by Clausius**.
- **Bottom flow:** A real refrigerator (green box) extracts heat $Q_L$ from cold reservoir, **requires external work $W$** (pink box), and rejects $Q_H$ to the hot reservoir. This is the **only legal way** to achieve cold-to-hot heat transfer.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Proof of Equivalence (Clausius ⇔ Kelvin-Planck)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Assume Violation of Clausius]) --> R[Imaginary Refrigerator R: Q_C from T_C to T_H with W_R = 0]
    R --> Couple[Couple R with Real Heat Engine E between same reservoirs]
    Couple --> Engine[Engine E: Takes Q_H from T_H, Rejects Q_C to T_C, Produces W]
    Engine --> Net[Net Effect of Combined System]
    Net --> NetQ[Net Heat to Hot Reservoir: Q_H - Q_C]
    Net --> NetW[Net Work Produced: W]
    Net --> Conclusion{Is this a Kelvin-Planck Violation?}
    Conclusion -- Yes --> End([Conclusion: Clausius Violation implies Kelvin-Planck Violation])
    style Start fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Engine fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Net fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Conclusion fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style End fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:**
- The flowchart shows the **logical chain** of the proof by contradiction: assuming a Clausius violation, we construct a combined system, analyze its net effect, and show it produces a Kelvin-Planck violation.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Clausius Violation Impossibility Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORBIDDEN["FORBIDDEN BY CLAUSIUS"]
        direction LR
        C1[Cold Reservoir T_C] -- Q_C, No Work --> H1[Hot Reservoir T_H]
    end

    subgraph ALLOWED["ALLOWED: Real Refrigerator"]
        direction LR
        C2[Cold Reservoir T_L] -- Q_L --> REFR[Refrigerator Unit]
        REFR -- Q_H --> H2[Hot Reservoir T_H]
        WS[External Work Source] -- W_in --> REFR
    end

    style FORBIDDEN fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style ALLOWED fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:3px
    style C1 fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C2 fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H2 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style REFR fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style WS fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Left (red zone):** What the Clausius Statement forbids — cold-to-hot heat transfer with no work.
- **Right (blue zone):** What is allowed in reality — a real refrigerator with work input accomplishes the same goal legally.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Clausius Statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Why is it considered a "negative" statement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):**
> "It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."

**Explanation (2 marks):**

The Clausius Statement is called a **"negative" statement** because it specifies an **impossibility** rather than a definite outcome. Just like the Kelvin-Planck statement, it tells us what *cannot* happen in nature, not what *will* happen. The impossibility is fundamental — it cannot be circumvented by any engineering design, no matter how refined. This negative phrasing is a hallmark of all Second Law statements (e.g., "perpetual motion machines of the second kind, PMM2, are impossible").

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**A refrigerator is said to "violate" the Clausius statement if it can transfer heat from a cold body to a hot body without any work input. Justify this with one practical example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A standard **domestic refrigerator** transfers heat $Q_L$ from the cold food compartment (interior, $\approx 4$ °C) to the warmer kitchen air (exterior, $\approx 30$ °C) (1 mark). It accomplishes this by consuming electrical work via its compressor (1 mark). The work input is what makes the process physically possible. **If the same refrigerator could perform the heat transfer without any work input**, it would be a Clausius-violating device, which is **impossible** per the Second Law (1 mark). This justifies why every real refrigerator *must* have a compressor or work-consuming component.

---

### 📗 **PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) = 7 marks + (b) = 7 marks. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

#### **Part (a):** State the Clausius and Kelvin-Planck statements of the Second Law. Show that a violation of the Clausius statement implies a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement. **(7 marks)**

#### **Part (b):** A reversible refrigerator operates between a cold reservoir at $-10$ °C and a hot reservoir at $40$ °C. The refrigerator has a power input of $0.5$ kW. Determine: (i) The maximum rate of heat removal from the cold space, (ii) The COP of the refrigerator, (iii) The rate of heat rejection to the hot reservoir. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A — Part (a)** (7 marks)

**1. Statements (2 marks):**

- **Clausius Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body.
- **Kelvin-Planck Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work.

**2. Proof by Contradiction (5 marks):**

**Assumption:** Let there exist a **Clausius-violating refrigerator (R)** that transfers $Q_C$ from a cold reservoir at $T_C$ to a hot reservoir at $T_H$ **without any work input** ($W_R = 0$).

Now, couple this refrigerator with a **real reversible heat engine (E)** operating between the same two reservoirs. Let the engine extract $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir, reject $Q_C'$ to the cold reservoir, and produce work $W$.

**Step 1:** Energy balance of the engine:
$$
W = Q_H - Q_C'
$$

**Step 2:** Energy balance of the combined system (R + E). Since both operate in cycles, the combined system also operates in a cycle.

**Step 3:** Net work output of the combined system:
$$
W_{net,out} = W - W_R = W - 0 = W
$$

**Step 4:** Net heat exchange of the combined system with the hot reservoir:
$$
Q_{H,net} = Q_H - Q_C
$$

**Step 5:** Choose the engine such that $Q_C' = Q_C$ (i.e., the engine rejects the same amount of heat to the cold reservoir as the refrigerator extracts from it). Then the cold reservoir experiences **no net heat exchange**.

**Step 6:** The combined system now:
- Exchanges heat **only with the hot reservoir** (net: $Q_{H,net} = Q_H - Q_C$),
- Produces net work $W$,
- Operates in a cycle.

This is a **direct violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement** (a cyclic device converting heat from a single reservoir into work).

**Conclusion:** Violation of Clausius ⇒ Violation of Kelvin-Planck. ∎ (5 marks for the proof structure)

---

#### **Solution to Question A — Part (b)** (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Cold reservoir temperature: $T_L = -10$ °C $= -10 + 273 = 263$ K
- Hot reservoir temperature: $T_H = 40$ °C $= 40 + 273 = 313$ K
- Power input (work): $W = 0.5$ kW

**Step 1: COP of a Reversible (Carnot) Refrigerator (2 marks)**

$$
\text{COP}_{R,rev} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
$$

Substituting:
$$
\text{COP}_{R,rev} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50} = 5.26
$$

**Step 2: Maximum Rate of Heat Removal from Cold Space (2 marks)**

$$
\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{W} \implies Q_L = \text{COP}_R \times W
$$

$$
Q_L = 5.26 \times 0.5 \text{ kW} = 2.63 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3: Rate of Heat Rejection to Hot Reservoir (1 mark)**

By energy balance:
$$
Q_H = Q_L + W
$$

$$
Q_H = 2.63 + 0.5 = 3.13 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $Q_L = 2.63$ kW
- (ii) $\text{COP}_R = 5.26$
- (iii) $Q_H = 3.13$ kW

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

#### **Part (a):** State the Clausius Statement. Explain with a clear sketch how a violation of the Clausius statement leads to a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement. **(7 marks)**

#### **Part (b):** A heat pump is used to maintain a house at $25$ °C when the outside temperature is $5$ °C. The heat loss from the house is estimated at $0.8$ kW. Determine the minimum power required to drive the heat pump and its COP. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B — Part (a)** (7 marks)

**1. Clausius Statement (2 marks):**
> "It is impossible to construct a cyclic device that produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a lower-temperature body to a higher-temperature body."

**2. Explanation with Sketch Description (5 marks):**

Suppose a hypothetical device **R** (Clausius violator) exists that takes heat $Q_C$ from a cold body at $T_C$ and delivers it to a hot body at $T_H$ **without work input** ($W = 0$). This is the *forbidden* scenario.

Now, let a **real heat engine E** operate between the same two reservoirs — taking $Q_H$ from the hot body, rejecting $Q_C'$ to the cold body, and producing work $W$.

If we operate both devices simultaneously (combined system):
- Hot body: **Loses** $Q_H$ (to engine) and **gains** $Q_C$ (from refrigerator) → net = $Q_H - Q_C$.
- Cold body: **Gains** $Q_C'$ (from engine) and **loses** $Q_C$ (to refrigerator) → net = $Q_C' - Q_C$.
- Work output: $W_{net} = W$ (since refrigerator needs no work).

Choose the engine such that $Q_C' = Q_C$ (cold body sees no change).

Then the **combined system** has only the hot body as a heat source and produces work $W$. This is **Kelvin-Planck violation**.

The sketch is a block diagram showing:
- Refrigerator R (left) — Cold body to Hot body, no work.
- Engine E (right) — Hot body to Cold body, work output.
- Combined net effect — single reservoir (hot) to work.

**Conclusion:** Violation of Clausius ⇒ Violation of Kelvin-Planck. ∎

---

#### **Solution to Question B — Part (b)** (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Indoor temperature: $T_H = 25$ °C $= 298$ K
- Outdoor temperature: $T_L = 5$ °C $= 278$ K
- Heat loss from house: $Q_H = 0.8$ kW (this is the heat the pump must supply)

**Step 1: COP of Reversible Heat Pump (3 marks)**

$$
\text{COP}_{HP,rev} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{298}{298 - 278} = \frac{298}{20} = 14.9
$$

**Step 2: Minimum Power Required (2 marks)**

From the definition of COP:
$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{in}} \implies W_{in} = \frac{Q_H}{\text{COP}_{HP}}
$$

$$
W_{in} = \frac{0.8 \text{ kW}}{14.9} = 0.0537 \text{ kW} = 53.7 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3: COP (already computed) (1 mark):**
$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = 14.9
$$

**Step 4: Verification (1 mark):**

Heat delivered to house: $Q_H = W_{in} + Q_L$
$$
Q_L = Q_H - W_{in} = 0.8 - 0.0537 = 0.746 \text{ kW}
$$
Check: $\text{COP}_{HP} = Q_H / W_{in} = 0.8 / 0.0537 \approx 14.9$ ✓

**Final Answers:**
- Minimum Power: $W_{in} = 0.0537$ kW $\approx 53.7$ W
- $\text{COP}_{HP} = 14.9$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Temperature in Celsius instead of Kelvin:** This is the **#1 cause of mark loss**. KTU strictly requires Kelvin in COP calculations. Using $T_H = 25$ and $T_L = 5$ directly gives a wrong answer.
> 2. **Confusing Refrigerator COP and Heat Pump COP:** $\text{COP}_R = T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ vs. $\text{COP}_{HP} = T_H/(T_H - T_L)$. Both have the **same denominator** but **different numerators**. Mixing them up loses 3-4 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw a sketch** in equivalence proofs: KTU examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for the diagram in Part (a) of equivalence questions.
> 4. **Skipping energy balance verification:** Always verify $Q_H = Q_L + W$ at the end. Examiners reward this as a "consistency check."
> 5. **Statement precision:** Writing "heat flows from cold to hot" without specifying "without external work" or "in a cycle" is **incomplete** — costs 1 mark per missing keyword.

---

### 🧠 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Clausius Statement**

- ✅ **Clausius Statement:** Heat cannot flow spontaneously (no work, cyclic) from cold to hot.
- ✅ **Equivalent to Kelvin-Planck:** Violation of one **⇒** violation of the other (proof by contradiction).
- ✅ **Two-condition requirement:** The forbidden device must be (1) cyclic, and (2) produce *no other effect* besides heat transfer.
- ✅ **Real refrigerators need work** — this is the practical manifestation of the Clausius statement.
- ✅ **COP Formulas (in Kelvin!):**
   - Refrigerator: $\text{COP}_R = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$
   - Heat Pump: $\text{COP}_{HP} = T_H / (T_H - T_L)$
- ✅ **Energy balance:** $Q_H = Q_L + W$ (always, for cyclic devices).
- ✅ **Maximum COP = Reversible (Carnot) COP** — used to find *minimum* work input.
- ✅ **Negative statement:** Like all Second Law statements, it specifies impossibility.
- ✅ **Connected to entropy:** Clausius Statement ⇔ Entropy of isolated system never decreases.
- ✅ **PMM2 (Perpetual Motion Machine of 2nd Kind):** Any device that violates the Clausius or Kelvin-Planck statement is a PMM2 and is impossible.
- ✅ **KTU Board Favorite Topics:**
  1. State the statement (1 mark).
  2. Differentiate from Kelvin-Planck (2 marks).
  3. Prove equivalence (5-7 marks).
  4. Solve COP problems on refrigerator/heat pump (7 marks).
- ✅ **Always use Kelvin** in temperature-based formulas.
- ✅ **Always include a labeled diagram** in equivalence proofs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalence of the Two Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equivalence of the Two Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** has two classical formulations, each attributed to different scientists studying different physical impossibilities. These two statements are **logically equivalent**, meaning that a violation of one necessarily implies a violation of the other.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition — The Two Statements:**
>
> **1. Kelvin–Planck Statement (KP Statement):**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*
>
> **2. Clausius Statement (C Statement):**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

Both statements declare an **impossibility** — they are negative statements, which is a hallmark of the Second Law (in contrast to the First Law, which is a positive conservation principle).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a beach with a **hot ocean** and a **cold ice block**.

- **Clausius Statement (Refrigerator Analogy):** Without spending any electricity (no work input), can heat naturally flow from the cold ice into the hot ocean, making the ocean hotter? **No.** Heat always flows from hot to cold spontaneously — never the reverse, without external effort.
- **Kelvin–Planck Statement (Engine Analogy):** Can you build a boat engine that simply takes heat from the hot ocean and converts **100% of it** into work, with no other effect (no cold sink, no exhaust)? **No.** You always need to dump *some* waste heat to a colder reservoir.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Both statements essentially forbid the same underlying phenomenon — the creation of a **"perpetual motion machine of the second kind" (PMM2)**. The equivalence proof mathematically confirms that these two physical prohibitions are two sides of the same coin.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Heat Reservoir Temperature (T):** Measured in **Kelvin (K)**
- **Work (W):** Measured in **Joules (J)** or **kJ**
- **Heat (Q):** Measured in **Joules (J)** or **kJ**
- **Thermal Efficiency (η):** Dimensionless ratio, always $\eta < 1$ for any real cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight — Keywords to Memorize:**
> "Operating in a cycle," "single reservoir," "no other effect," and "impossible." These four phrases are the **valuation triggers** examiners use to award full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — The Equivalence Theorem

The equivalence of the two statements is proven through a **proof by contradiction** in two parts:

**Part 1: Violation of Kelvin–Planck ⟹ Violation of Clausius**

- Assume a **hypothetical engine (E)** violates the KP statement. This means it extracts heat $Q_H$ from a single hot reservoir at $T_H$ and converts it **entirely** into work $W$ (i.e., $\eta = 100\%$).
- Now take a **real refrigerator (R)** operating between the same two reservoirs ($T_H$ and $T_L$).
- Couple the engine and refrigerator such that the work output of the engine drives the refrigerator.
- The **net effect** is: heat $Q_L$ is transferred from the cold reservoir to the hot reservoir with **no external work input**.
- This directly violates the Clausius statement.

**Part 2: Violation of Clausius ⟹ Violation of Kelvin–Planck**

- Assume a **hypothetical refrigerator (R')** violates the Clausius statement. This means it transfers heat $Q_L$ from a cold reservoir at $T_L$ to a hot reservoir at $T_H$ **without any work input**.
- Now take a **real engine (E')** operating between the same two reservoirs, extracting $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir, producing work $W$, and rejecting $Q_L$ to the cold reservoir.
- Couple them such that the heat rejected by the engine equals the heat absorbed by the violating refrigerator.
- The **net effect** is: heat is extracted solely from the hot reservoir and fully converted to work, with no other change.
- This directly violates the Kelvin–Planck statement.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- The **logical backbone** of the proof is that both statements forbid the same universal constant — they rule out spontaneous, free, perpetual energy exploitation without a temperature gradient.
- The use of a **cyclic device** is critical because the First Law requires energy conservation in a cycle, making the second law violation clearly identifiable as the *only* anomaly.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Condition** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Q_H$ | Heat supplied from hot reservoir | Given | kJ |
| $Q_L$ | Heat rejected to cold reservoir | Given | kJ |
| $W_{net}$ | Net work output of cycle | $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ | kJ |
| $\eta_{th}$ | Thermal efficiency of heat engine | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | Dimensionless |
| COP (Refrigerator) | Coefficient of Performance | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | Dimensionless |
| KP Violation | Impossible engine condition | $\eta_{th} = 1$ (i.e., $Q_L = 0$) | — |
| Clausius Violation | Impossible refrigerator condition | $COP_R = \infty$ (i.e., $W_{net} = 0$) | — |
| Carnot Efficiency | Maximum possible efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This equivalence is the foundation of all modern refrigeration cycles, heat pump design, and power plant optimization. If both statements were independent, the entire framework of heat transfer directionality would collapse.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plant Design:** Engineers know that no matter how well-designed a steam or gas turbine cycle is, it can never achieve 100% efficiency. This equivalence is the **mathematical proof** behind the $T_L / T_H$ limit.
- **Refrigeration Industry:** It establishes that **every refrigerator must consume work** to move heat from cold to hot.
- **Heat Pumps:** Defines the absolute theoretical COP limit for any heating/cooling device.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Proof 1: Violation of Kelvin–Planck Statement Leads to Violation of Clausius Statement

**Setup:**
- Let **Engine A** be a hypothetical engine that **violates the Kelvin–Planck statement**.
- Engine A operates between a single hot reservoir at temperature $T_H$.
- It extracts heat $Q_H$ from $T_H$ and converts it **entirely** into work $W$ (i.e., $Q_L = 0$, $\eta = 100\%$).

**Step 1: Energy balance of Engine A (First Law applied to cycle)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_H &= W + Q_L \\
Q_H &= W + 0 \\
\therefore W &= Q_H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Since Engine A violates the KP statement, it has no heat rejection. By energy conservation, all extracted heat becomes work.

**Step 2: Introduce a Real Refrigerator (Refrigerator B)**

- Refrigerator B is a **real, working refrigerator** that obeys the Second Law.
- It operates between the **same two reservoirs**: $T_H$ (hot) and $T_L$ (cold).
- It requires work input $W$ to extract heat $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir and reject $Q_H'$ to the hot reservoir.

**Step 3: Energy balance of Refrigerator B**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_H' &= Q_L + W
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The work input to the refrigerator equals the difference between the heat rejected to the hot reservoir and the heat absorbed from the cold reservoir.

**Step 4: Couple Engine A and Refrigerator B**

- Use the work output $W$ of Engine A to **drive** Refrigerator B.
- We deliberately choose Engine A such that $W_{A} = W_{B} = W$.

**Step 5: Net Effect of the Combined System**

Let us compute the net heat exchange with each reservoir:

- **Hot Reservoir at $T_H$:**
  - Receives $Q_H'$ from Refrigerator B (gain).
  - Loses $Q_H$ to Engine A (loss).
  - Since $Q_H = W$ (from Step 1) and $Q_H' = Q_L + W$ (from Step 3):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net heat at } T_H &= Q_H' - Q_H \\
&= (Q_L + W) - W \\
&= Q_L
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Cold Reservoir at $T_L$:**
  - Loses $Q_L$ to Refrigerator B (loss).
  - Receives nothing.
  - Net heat at $T_L$ = $-Q_L$ (i.e., it loses $Q_L$).

- **Work Reservoir:**
  - Engine A produces $W$.
  - Refrigerator B consumes $W$.
  - Net work = $0$.

**Step 6: Conclusion of Proof 1**

The combined system operates in a cycle (both Engine A and Refrigerator B operate in cycles), and its **sole net effect** is the transfer of heat $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir at $T_L$ to the hot reservoir at $T_H$, with **no work input required**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Violation of KP Statement} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Violation of Clausius Statement}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Proof 2: Violation of Clausius Statement Leads to Violation of Kelvin–Planck Statement

**Setup:**
- Let **Refrigerator X** be a hypothetical refrigerator that **violates the Clausius statement**.
- It transfers heat $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir at $T_L$ to the hot reservoir at $T_H$ **without any work input** ($W = 0$).

**Step 1: Energy balance of Refrigerator X**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_H'' &= Q_L + W \\
Q_H'' &= Q_L + 0 \\
\therefore Q_H'' &= Q_L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Since Refrigerator X violates the Clausius statement, no work is consumed, and by First Law, the heat delivered to the hot reservoir equals the heat extracted from the cold reservoir.

**Step 2: Introduce a Real Heat Engine (Engine Y)**

- Engine Y is a **real, working heat engine** that obeys the Second Law.
- It operates between the same two reservoirs $T_H$ and $T_L$.
- It extracts heat $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir, produces work $W$, and rejects heat $Q_L$ to the cold reservoir.

**Step 3: Energy balance of Engine Y**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= Q_H - Q_L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Couple Refrigerator X and Engine Y**

- We arrange such that the heat rejected by Engine Y to the cold reservoir ($Q_L$) is exactly equal to the heat absorbed by Refrigerator X from the cold reservoir.
- This means the cold reservoir experiences **zero net heat exchange**.

**Step 5: Net Effect of the Combined System**

- **Hot Reservoir at $T_H$:**
  - Receives $Q_H'' = Q_L$ from Refrigerator X (gain).
  - Loses $Q_H$ to Engine Y (loss).

- **Cold Reservoir at $T_L$:**
  - Loses $Q_L$ to Engine Y (loss).
  - Gains $Q_L$ from Refrigerator X (gain).
  - Net heat at $T_L$ = $0$.

- **Work Reservoir:**
  - Engine Y produces work $W$ (gain).
  - Refrigerator X consumes no work (no loss).
  - Net work output = $W$.

**Step 6: Net Heat Exchange with Hot Reservoir**

The heat absorbed by Refrigerator X from the hot side is $Q_L$ (rejected by the engine itself). To find the net heat extracted purely from the hot reservoir:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net heat from } T_H &= Q_H - Q_L \\
&= W \quad \text{(from Step 3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Conclusion of Proof 2**

The combined system operates in a cycle, and its **sole net effect** is the extraction of heat $Q_H - Q_L$ (which equals $W$) from the **single** hot reservoir at $T_H$ and the production of an equivalent amount of work $W$, with **no other effect** on the surroundings.

This is precisely a violation of the Kelvin–Planck statement.

$$
\boxed{\text{Violation of Clausius Statement} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Violation of Kelvin–Planck Statement}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Combined Logical Conclusion

Since both implications hold true in both directions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Violation of KP} &\Longleftrightarrow \text{Violation of Clausius} \\
\therefore \text{KP Statement} &\Longleftrightarrow \text{Clausius Statement}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The two statements are logically equivalent.** If one is true, the other must be true. If one is false, the other must be false. This is a hallmark of the deep structural unity of thermodynamics.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Contrasting the Two Devices

| **Property** | **KP-Violating Engine** | **Clausius-Violating Refrigerator** |
|---|---|---|
| Heat absorbed from $T_H$ | $Q_H$ | $Q_L$ (rejected to $T_H$) |
| Heat rejected to $T_L$ | $0$ | $0$ (absorbed from $T_L$) |
| Work output | $W = Q_H$ | $0$ |
| Efficiency / COP | $\eta = 100\%$ (impossible) | $COP = \infty$ (impossible) |
| Coupling partner | Real refrigerator | Real heat engine |
| Resulting violation | Clausius statement | Kelvin–Planck statement |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Proof 1 (KP Violation ⟹ Clausius Violation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Hot Reservoir TH"] -->|Q_H Heat| nodeB["Violating Engine A KP"]
    nodeB -->|W Work Output| nodeC["Work Coupling Shaft"]
    nodeC -->|W Work Input| nodeD["Real Refrigerator B"]
    nodeD -->|Q_L from Cold| nodeE["Cold Reservoir TL"]
    nodeD -->|Q_H minus Q_L| nodeA
    nodeF["Net Effect"] --> nodeG["Heat Q_L moves from TL to TH with zero work"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["VIOLATION OF CLAUSIUS STATEMENT"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Proof 2 (Clausius Violation ⟹ KP Violation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP["Hot Reservoir TH"] -->|Q_H Heat| nodeQ["Real Engine Y"]
    nodeQ -->|W Work Output| nodeR["External Work Sink"]
    nodeQ -->|Q_L Rejected| nodeS["Cold Reservoir TL"]
    nodeT["Violating Refrigerator X"] -->|Q_L Delivered| nodeP
    nodeS -->|Q_L Absorbed| nodeT
    nodeU["Net Effect"] --> nodeV["Heat W extracted from TH and fully converted to work"]
    nodeV --> nodeW["VIOLATION OF KELVIN PLANCK STATEMENT"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Equivalence Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["KP Statement"] -->|Logical NOT| node2["Hypothetical KP Violator Engine"]
    node2 -->|Coupled with| node3["Real Clausius Obeyer Refrigerator"]
    node3 -->|Produces| node4["Net Heat Transfer Cold to Hot"]
    node4 -->|Defines| node5["Clausius Violation"]
    node6["Clausius Statement"] -->|Logical NOT| node7["Hypothetical Clausius Violator Refrigerator"]
    node7 -->|Coupled with| node8["Real KP Obeyer Engine"]
    node8 -->|Produces| node9["Net Heat to Work from Single Reservoir"]
    node9 -->|Defines| node10["KP Violation"]
    node5 -.Implies.-> node6
    node10 -.Implies.-> node1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The arrows marked "Logical NOT" represent the assumption of a hypothetical device violating the corresponding statement. The coupling with a real device creates a contradiction, completing the proof by contradiction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]:**
> **State the Kelvin–Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Kelvin–Planck statement** states that it is impossible to construct a heat engine that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the absorption of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work.

The **Clausius statement** states that it is impossible to construct a refrigeration device operating in a cycle whose sole effect is the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body.

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]:**
> **What is meant by the equivalence of the two statements of the Second Law?**

**Model Answer:**

The equivalence of the two statements of the Second Law means that the **Kelvin–Planck** and **Clausius** statements are **logically equivalent**. A violation of one statement logically implies a violation of the other. This is proved using a proof by contradiction, where a hypothetical device violating one statement is coupled with a real device obeying the other, producing a combined system that violates the remaining statement.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]:**

> **(a)** State the Kelvin–Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Prove that the violation of the Kelvin–Planck statement leads to the violation of the Clausius statement. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Kelvin–Planck Statement:**
It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a thermodynamic cycle and produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single thermal reservoir and the production of an equivalent amount of work.

**Clausius Statement:**
It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a thermodynamic cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body.

> *Both statements declare impossibilities, and both are negative formulations. A device satisfying the KP statement would be a 100% efficient heat engine, while a device satisfying the Clausius statement would be a self-acting refrigerator requiring no work input. Both are forbidden by nature.*

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Assumption:** Let Engine A be a hypothetical engine that violates the Kelvin–Planck statement. It extracts heat $Q_H$ from a single hot reservoir at $T_H$ and converts it fully into work $W$ (no heat rejection).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_H &= W \quad \text{(since } Q_L = 0\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Construction:** Couple Engine A with a real refrigerator (Refrigerator B) operating between the same two reservoirs $T_H$ and $T_L$, such that the work output $W$ of Engine A drives Refrigerator B.

**Net Heat Exchange:**

- **Hot Reservoir at $T_H$:** Loses $Q_H$ to Engine A, receives $Q_H' = Q_L + W$ from Refrigerator B.
- **Cold Reservoir at $T_L$:** Loses $Q_L$ to Refrigerator B, receives nothing.

**Net Effect:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net heat to hot reservoir} &= (Q_L + W) - Q_H = (Q_L + W) - W = Q_L
\end{aligned}
$$

The combined cyclic system transfers heat $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir to the hot reservoir **without any external work input**. This is a direct violation of the Clausius statement.

$$
\boxed{Q_E \therefore \text{Violation of KP} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Violation of Clausius}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]:**

> **(a)** State and explain the Clausius statement of the Second Law. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Prove that a violation of the Clausius statement leads to a violation of the Kelvin–Planck statement. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The **Clausius statement** of the Second Law of Thermodynamics states:

> *"It is impossible to construct a system operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a lower temperature body to a higher temperature body."*

**Explanation:**
- This statement declares that heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot.
- For heat to be transferred from a cold reservoir to a hot reservoir, **external work must be supplied** (e.g., a refrigerator consumes electrical work).
- A device that violates this statement would be a self-acting refrigerator (perpetual motion machine of the second kind, PMM2) with infinite coefficient of performance.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Assumption:** Let Refrigerator X be a hypothetical refrigerator that violates the Clausius statement. It transfers heat $Q_L$ from cold reservoir at $T_L$ to hot reservoir at $T_H$ without any work input.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_H' &= Q_L + 0 = Q_L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Construction:** Couple Refrigerator X with a real heat engine (Engine Y) operating between the same two reservoirs.

**Net Heat Exchange:**

- **Cold Reservoir at $T_L$:** Loses $Q_L$ to Engine Y, gains $Q_L$ from Refrigerator X. Net change = $0$.
- **Hot Reservoir at $T_H$:** Loses $Q_H$ to Engine Y, gains $Q_L$ from Refrigerator X.
- **Work Output:** Engine Y produces $W = Q_H - Q_L$.

**Net Effect:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net heat extracted from } T_H &= Q_H - Q_L = W
\end{aligned}
$$

The combined cyclic system extracts heat $W$ from a single hot reservoir and converts it entirely into work $W$, with no other effect on the surroundings. This is a direct violation of the Kelvin–Planck statement.

$$
\boxed{\therefore \text{Violation of Clausius} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Violation of Kelvin–Planck}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the word "cycle"** in either statement is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**. KTU examiners explicitly look for "operating in a cycle."
> 2. **Skipping the "no other effect"** clause in your restatement costs another **1 mark**.
> 3. **Direction of heat and work arrows** in coupled diagrams are critical. Reversing the direction in your schematic is a common error that loses **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Failing to compute the net effect** (i.e., not showing the algebraic cancellation leading to the violation) results in an incomplete proof — examiners award only **3 out of 7 marks** in such cases.
> 5. **Coupling condition ($W_A = W_B$ or $Q_L^{rej} = Q_L^{abs}$) must be explicitly stated**; otherwise, the proof appears logically flawed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Equivalence of the Two Statements**

- The **Kelvin–Planck** statement forbids a 100% efficient heat engine ($\eta = 1$).
- The **Clausius** statement forbids a self-acting refrigerator (COP = $\infty$).
- Both statements are **negative statements** declaring impossibilities.
- A **proof by contradiction** in **two parts** establishes equivalence:
  - Part 1: Violating KP engine + Real refrigerator ⟹ Clausius violation.
  - Part 2: Violating Clausius refrigerator + Real engine ⟹ KP violation.
- The **coupling mechanism** uses the work output of the violating device to drive the real device, or matches the heat rejection rate.
- A **cyclic device** is essential — energy must be conserved in the cycle (First Law is always satisfied; Second Law is what is violated).
- The **net effect** of the combined system must be computed algebraically to show the resulting impossibility.
- This equivalence underpins the **Carnot theorem**, the concept of **PMM2**, and the absolute temperature scale.
- **Real-world implication:** No engine can achieve 100% efficiency, and no refrigerator can operate without work input.
- **KTU hot keywords:** "Operating in a cycle," "single reservoir," "no other effect," "impossible," "equivalent amount of work."
- The two statements together form the **logical foundation** of the Second Law and all its corollaries.
- **Exam tip:** Always state the assumption first, then the coupling, then compute the net effect, then state the conclusion. This four-step structure is the KTU board's expected answer format for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perpetual-Motion Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Perpetual-Motion Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Perpetual-Motion Machine (PMM)** is defined by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (under Module 3 – Second Law of Thermodynamics) as a *hypothetical thermodynamic device* that, once set into operation, would either:

1. **Violate the First Law of Thermodynamics** (PMM of the **first kind**, PMM1), **OR**
2. **Violate the Second Law of Thermodynamics** (PMM of the **second kind**, PMM2),

thereby producing useful work or transferring heat indefinitely *without any external energy input or entropy generation*. Such devices are **thermodynamically impossible** under the postulates of classical equilibrium thermodynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Must Memorize):**
> *"A perpetual-motion machine of the first kind (PMM1) is a device that would produce net work while exchanging heat with a single reservoir only, thereby violating the Kelvin–Planck statement. A perpetual-motion machine of the second kind (PMM2) is a device that would transfer heat from a lower-temperature body to a higher-temperature body without any external work input, thereby violating the Clausius statement."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you build a small toy boat and push it across a still pond. According to real-world physics (viscous drag, surface friction, fluid resistance), the boat **slows down and eventually stops**. To keep it moving forever, you must continuously supply energy — paddle harder, or use a small battery-powered motor.

A **Perpetual-Motion Machine** is the "magic boat" that, once pushed, *never stops*. No friction losses, no energy input, no degradation — **perpetual motion**. While intuitively appealing (who wouldn't want free, endless energy?), the laws of thermodynamics **categorically forbid** its existence.

Two flavours of "magic" exist:
- **PMM1** → "Energy from nothing" (like a kettle boiling water with no electricity or flame — just by *wanting* to boil).
- **PMM2** → "Heat climbing uphill" (like an ice cube in warm soup *making the soup colder* without a freezer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standards Used in PMM Analysis:**
> - **Absolute Zero:** **0 K = −273.15 °C** (the thermal limit below which PMMs cannot operate).
> - **Standard Reference Reservoir (KTU Board Convention):** **T₀ = 300 K = 27 °C** (for calculating Carnot / PMM efficiencies).
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** (used only when PMM is misapplied to gas cycles).

---

### 1.3 Why KTU Includes This Topic

The KTU 2024 Scheme places PMMs in **Module 3 (Second Law of Thermodynamics)** because the topic serves as a **conceptual capstone** that ties together:

- The **First Law** (energy conservation → PMM1 impossibility)
- The **Kelvin–Planck Statement** → PMM1 impossibility (heat engine branch)
- The **Clausius Statement** → PMM2 impossibility (refrigerator branch)
- **Equivalence of the Two Statements** (proved via contradiction using PMMs)
- **Carnot Theorem** corollary — no engine can beat Carnot efficiency

KTU frequently tests this in **2-mark / 3-mark short-answer** format (definitions, identification) and occasionally as a **sub-part of a 14-mark Module-3 question** (e.g., "Prove the equivalence of Kelvin–Planck and Clausius statements using PMM1 and PMM2").

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Perpetual-Motion Machines

The two categories are not arbitrary — they map **directly** to the two corollaries of the Second Law:

#### 2.1.1 Perpetual-Motion Machine of the First Kind (PMM1)

**Definition:** A device that produces net work *without consuming any energy* (heat, fuel, electrical, mechanical, or otherwise). It operates in a cycle and delivers continuous work output while returning to its initial state.

**The 'Why' Behind PMM1 Impossibility:**

A heat engine operating between a **single reservoir at T_H** and producing work **W** would, per the **Kelvin–Planck statement**, violate:

> *"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*

**Mechanism of Violation:**
- The PMM1 *absorbs* heat **Q_H** from a single reservoir.
- It *delivers* equivalent work **W = Q_H** (100% thermal efficiency).
- **No heat rejection (Q_L = 0)** to any lower-temperature sink.
- This means η_th = W/Q_H = **1.0 (100%)**, which **contradicts** the Second Law.

**Energy Reconciliation with First Law:**

For a cyclic device, by the First Law:

$$Q_{net} = W_{net}$$

For PMM1:

$$Q_H - Q_L = W$$

If **Q_L = 0** and **W = Q_H**, then the device creates work from *nothing*. Although the First Law formally balances (Q_net = W), the **process by which Q_L is forced to zero is the violation** — it requires entropy of the universe to decrease, which the Second Law forbids.

#### 2.1.2 Perpetual-Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2)

**Definition:** A device that transfers heat from a low-temperature reservoir to a high-temperature reservoir **without any external work input** (no compressor, no electrical power, no mechanical drive).

**The 'Why' Behind PMM2 Impossibility:**

This directly violates the **Clausius statement**:

> *"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

**Mechanism of Violation:**
- Heat **Q_L** flows from cold reservoir at **T_L** to hot reservoir at **T_H**.
- No work input (W = 0).
- By Clausius, this spontaneous flow is forbidden because it would require the **entropy of the universe to decrease**:

$$\Delta S_{universe} = \frac{Q_L}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} < 0 \quad \text{(since } T_H > T_L\text{)}$$

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow for Identifying PMM Violations

**Step 1 — Identify the device's cycle boundary.**
- PMMs must operate in a **cycle** (returning to initial state) to be perpetual.

**Step 2 — Quantify all energy crossings.**
- Heat in (Q_H), heat out (Q_L), work in (W_in), work out (W_out).

**Step 3 — Apply First Law sanity check.**
- If energy is "created" from no source → **PMM1 candidate**.
- Check: does W_net = Q_net hold? If yes on paper but process is impossible → PMM1.

**Step 4 — Apply Second Law (Kelvin–Planck or Clausius).**
- Single reservoir with work output → **violates K-P** → PMM1.
- Cold-to-hot heat transfer without work → **violates Clausius** → PMM2.

**Step 5 — Compute thermal efficiency (η_th).**
- For PMM1: η_th = 1 (100%) — **impossible** (Carnot cap < 1 unless T_L = 0 K, which is unattainable).

**Step 6 — Conclude impossibility via entropy argument.**

$$\Delta S_{universe} \geq 0 \quad \text{(always, for any real process)}$$

PMMs would force **ΔS < 0**, hence forbidden.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Variables / Meaning** | **Boundary / Domain** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$ | W_net = net work output, Q_H = heat input | $0 \leq \eta_{th} < 1$ | dimensionless |
| Carnot Efficiency (Upper Limit) | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | T_L, T_H in absolute temperature (K) | T_L > 0 K; T_H > T_L | dimensionless |
| PMM1 Condition (Impossible) | $\eta_{th} = 1.0 = 100\%$ | Requires Q_L = 0 | **Forbidden** | dimensionless |
| COP of Refrigerator | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$ | Q_L = cooling effect, W_in = compressor work | $COP_R > 0$ | dimensionless |
| COP of Heat Pump | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$ | Q_H = heating delivered | $COP_{HP} > COP_R$ | dimensionless |
| PMM2 Condition (Impossible) | $W_{net,in} = 0$ with $\dot{Q}_{L \rightarrow H} \neq 0$ | Spontaneous cold-to-hot flow | **Forbidden** | kW |
| Clausius Inequality | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ | For any cyclic process | Equality → reversible | dimensionless |
| Entropy Universe | $\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr}$ | Always ≥ 0 (Second Law) | Real processes | kJ/K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to prove PMM impossibility, **always start with the Second Law statement**, then show the contradiction. Starting with "energy cannot be created" gets you **only 1 mark out of 7** — the rest comes from the rigorous Kelvin–Planck / Clausius contradiction.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although PMMs are impossible, **analyzing them is engineering-critical** because:

1. **Refrigeration Design Validation** — Any proposed refrigerator cycle is checked against the **PMM2 impossibility** to ensure W_in > 0.
2. **Heat Engine Performance Benchmarking** — PMM1 sets the **theoretical 100% ceiling**; real engines are compared to **Carnot efficiency** (which is itself less than PMM1).
3. **Patent Rejection Criterion** — Patent offices (USPTO, IPO India) routinely reject PMM claims using these thermodynamic arguments.
4. **Renewable Energy Justification** — Wind, solar, hydro, tidal *all* require energy input (sunlight, gravitational gradients) — they are **not PMMs**; they obey the First and Second Laws.
5. **Cryogenics & Low-Temperature Physics** — PMM2 impossibility explains why reaching **T → 0 K** requires infinite work.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: PMM1 Impossibility via Kelvin–Planck Statement

**Statement to Prove:** A cyclic heat engine interacting with a *single thermal reservoir* cannot convert the absorbed heat entirely into work.

**Given:**
- A cyclic engine E operates between a single reservoir at temperature **T_H**.
- It absorbs heat **Q_H** from the reservoir.
- It rejects heat **Q_L = 0** (claimed, for PMM1).
- It produces net work **W_net**.

**Step 1 — Apply the First Law to the cyclic engine.**

For any cyclic device, the net change in internal energy over one cycle is zero:

$$\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$$

Therefore, by the First Law:

$$Q_{net} = W_{net}$$

**Step 2 — Expand Q_net and W_net.**

$$Q_H - Q_L = W_{net}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the PMM1 assumption (Q_L = 0).**

$$Q_H - 0 = W_{net}$$

$$\Rightarrow W_{net} = Q_H$$

**Step 4 — Compute the thermal efficiency.**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H} = 1.0 = 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the Kelvin–Planck statement.**

The **Kelvin–Planck statement** asserts that it is impossible for any device operating in a cycle to receive heat from a **single reservoir** and produce an **equivalent amount of work** as its only effect.

Our PMM1 does exactly that:
- Single reservoir → ✓
- Equivalent work as only effect → ✓

**Step 6 — Conclude impossibility.**

By direct contradiction with the **Kelvin–Planck statement** (which is an experimentally verified postulate of the Second Law):

$$\boxed{\therefore \text{ PMM1 is thermodynamically impossible. } \eta_{th} < 1 \text{ for all real heat engines.}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: PMM2 Impossibility via Clausius Statement

**Statement to Prove:** Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a colder body to a hotter body without external work.

**Given:**
- A cyclic refrigerator R operates between:
  - Cold reservoir at **T_L**
  - Hot reservoir at **T_H**, with **T_H > T_L**
- It transfers heat **Q_L** from the cold reservoir to the hot reservoir.
- It consumes **no work input** (W_net,in = 0, claimed for PMM2).

**Step 1 — Apply the First Law to the cyclic refrigerator.**

For a refrigerator in a cycle:

$$Q_L - Q_H + W_{net,in} = 0$$

Wait — careful with sign convention. Let's use the standard sign convention:
- Q_L = heat absorbed from cold reservoir (positive into device)
- Q_H = heat rejected to hot reservoir (positive out of device)
- W_in = work input (positive into device)

First Law (cycle):

$$Q_L + W_{in} = Q_H$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the PMM2 assumption (W_in = 0).**

$$Q_L + 0 = Q_H$$

$$\Rightarrow Q_H = Q_L$$

The device simply **pumps** heat **Q_L** from cold to hot with **no energy penalty**.

**Step 3 — Apply the Second Law — Entropy of the universe.**

For a cyclic device, the entropy change of the device over one cycle is zero:

$$\Delta S_{device} = 0$$

Entropy change of the **cold reservoir** (loses heat Q_L at T_L):

$$\Delta S_{cold} = -\frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

Entropy change of the **hot reservoir** (gains heat Q_H = Q_L at T_H):

$$\Delta S_{hot} = +\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = +\frac{Q_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 4 — Total entropy change of the universe.**

$$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{cold} + \Delta S_{hot} + \Delta S_{device}$$

$$\Delta S_{universe} = -\frac{Q_L}{T_L} + \frac{Q_L}{T_H} + 0$$

$$\Delta S_{universe} = Q_L \left( \frac{1}{T_H} - \frac{1}{T_L} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Analyze the sign.**

Since **T_H > T_L**:

$$\frac{1}{T_H} < \frac{1}{T_L}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{T_H} - \frac{1}{T_L} < 0$$

Also, **Q_L > 0** (heat is being transferred). Therefore:

$$\Delta S_{universe} = Q_L \cdot (\text{negative}) < 0$$

**Step 6 — Conclude impossibility.**

The Second Law requires:

$$\Delta S_{universe} \geq 0 \quad \text{(for any real process)}$$

But our PMM2 forces **ΔS_universe < 0**, which is a **direct violation** of the Clausius statement and the Second Law.

$$\boxed{\therefore \text{ PMM2 is thermodynamically impossible. } W_{in} > 0 \text{ is required for all real refrigerators.}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalence of Kelvin–Planck and Clausius Statements (Using PMMs)

**This is a KTU-favourite 14-mark derivation question.** The proof proceeds via contradiction: assume one statement is violated (PMM exists) → show the other is also violated.

**Part A: Violating Clausius (PMM2) → Violating Kelvin–Planck (PMM1)**

**Given:** Assume PMM2 exists — it transfers heat Q from T_L to T_H with no work input.

**Construction:** Couple the PMM2 with a **real (Carnot) refrigerator** operating between the same two reservoirs. The real refrigerator:
- Receives work input W_C from an external source.
- Absorbs Q_L,C from T_L and rejects Q_H,C to T_H.

Choose the magnitude of PMM2's transfer such that the net heat extracted from T_L by the combined system is zero (i.e., PMM2 pumps Q upward, real refrigerator pumps an equal amount Q downward through work).

The combined device:
- Extracts **zero** net heat from T_L.
- Rejects heat **Q_H,C − Q** to T_H.
- Consumes net work input **W_C**.

**Step 1 — Net heat absorbed from cold reservoir.**

$$Q_{L,net} = Q_{L,C} - Q = 0$$

So the cold reservoir experiences **no net change**.

**Step 2 — Net heat rejected to hot reservoir.**

$$Q_{H,net} = Q_{H,C} - Q$$

**Step 3 — Net work input (by First Law, cycle).**

$$W_{C} = Q_{H,C} - Q_{L,C}$$

Since Q_{L,C} = Q:

$$W_C = Q_{H,C} - Q$$

**Step 4 — Observe the combined system's effect.**

The combined device exchanges heat **only** with the **hot reservoir** at T_H (rejects Q_H,C − Q to T_H) and **consumes work W_C**.

If we **redesign** the system such that the work consumed is provided by the heat rejected itself (a self-loop), the device becomes a **single-reservoir engine** extracting heat from T_H and converting it entirely to work — a **PMM1**.

**Part B: Violating Kelvin–Planck (PMM1) → Violating Clausius (PMM2)**

**Given:** Assume PMM1 exists — it extracts heat Q_H from a single reservoir at T_H and produces equivalent work W = Q_H with Q_L = 0.

**Construction:** Use the work output W to drive a **real refrigerator** between T_L and T_H. The refrigerator:
- Uses work W to pump heat Q_L from T_L to T_H.
- Rejects Q_H,real = Q_L + W to the hot reservoir.

**Step 1 — Energy balance of the refrigerator.**

$$Q_{H,real} = Q_L + W$$

**Step 2 — Total heat delivered to the hot reservoir.**

The PMM1 delivers Q_H to T_H. The refrigerator delivers Q_{H,real} to T_H.

$$Q_{H,total} = Q_H + Q_L + W$$

**Step 3 — Net heat extracted from T_L.**

The PMM1 extracts Q_H from T_H and converts it to W. The refrigerator uses W to extract Q_L from T_L:

$$Q_{L,extracted} = Q_L$$

**Step 4 — Net effect on the reservoirs.**

- Hot reservoir at T_H: loses Q_H (to PMM1) and gains Q_{H,real} = Q_L + W.
- Cold reservoir at T_L: loses Q_L (to refrigerator).

If we choose W = Q_H (PMM1's output), and W = Q_H exactly equals the work needed by the refrigerator:

$$Q_{H,real} = Q_L + Q_H$$

So the hot reservoir receives (Q_H + Q_L) and loses Q_H → **net gain = Q_L** at T_H.
The cold reservoir loses **Q_L** at T_L.

**Step 5 — Net result.**

$$\Delta S_{universe} = \frac{Q_L}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} < 0$$

This is a **PMM2** — heat has flowed from cold to hot with **no external work input** (the PMM1 "self-drove" the refrigerator with its own work output, but in the composite picture, only heat from one reservoir was used).

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\therefore \text{ Violation of Clausius } \Leftrightarrow \text{ Violation of Kelvin–Planck. The two statements are equivalent.}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: PMM1 vs PMM2

| **Parameter** | **PMM1 (First Kind)** | **PMM2 (Second Kind)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Law Violated** | Kelvin–Planck statement of Second Law | Clausius statement of Second Law |
| **Common Misconception** | "Violates First Law" (incorrect — energy is balanced on paper) | "Violates First Law" (incorrect) |
| **Operating Mode** | Heat engine (work output) | Refrigerator (heat pump) |
| **Reservoirs Used** | Single reservoir (T_H only) | Two reservoirs (T_L and T_H) |
| **Work Status** | Net work **output** with no fuel consumption | Net work **input = 0**, yet heat is pumped |
| **Efficiency Claim** | η_th = 100% | COP → ∞ (infinite coefficient of performance) |
| **Entropy Effect** | ΔS_univ = −Q_H/T_H < 0 | ΔS_univ = Q_L(1/T_H − 1/T_L) < 0 |
| **Real-World Analogy** | A kettle boiling without heat | Ice cube freezing warm soup |
| **Why It's Impossible** | Cannot reduce entropy of single reservoir without compensation | Cannot transfer heat uphill without work |
| **KTU Exam Weight** | Frequently asked (definitions + proofs) | Frequently asked (COP and equivalence) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: PMM1 (First Kind) Operating Configuration

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeRes1["HOT RESERVOIR T_H"] -->|"Q_H extracted"| nodePMM1["PMM1 CYCLIC DEVICE"]
    nodePMM1 -->|"W_net = Q_H (100% work output)"| nodeWorkOut["USEFUL WORK SHAFT"]
    nodePMM1 -->|"Q_L = 0 (NO REJECTION)"| nodeGhostSink["LOW TEMP SINK DOES NOT EXIST"]
    nodeWorkOut -->|"Perpetual output"| nodeApp["APPLICATION DRIVEN FOREVER"]
    style nodeRes1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style nodePMM1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeWorkOut fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeGhostSink fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray:5 5
    style nodeApp fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The PMM1 is shown drawing heat **Q_H** from a single hot reservoir, converting it 100% to work, with **no low-temperature heat rejection**. The dashed "ghost sink" represents the *absence* of the required second reservoir — the fundamental impossibility.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: PMM2 (Second Kind) Operating Configuration

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeCold["COLD RESERVOIR T_L"] -->|"Q_L pumped upward"| nodePMM2["PMM2 CYCLIC DEVICE"]
    nodePMM2 -->|"Q_H = Q_L delivered uphill"| nodeHot["HOT RESERVOIR T_H"]
    nodePMM2 -->|"W_in = 0 NO DRIVE NEEDED"| nodeGhostWork["WORK INPUT DOES NOT EXIST"]
    nodeCold -->|"Continuously cooled for free"| nodeApp2["APPLICATION PERPETUAL COOLING"]
    style nodeCold fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style nodePMM2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeHot fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeGhostWork fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray:5 5
    style nodeApp2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The PMM2 pumps heat from cold to hot **without any work input** — a "free refrigerator." The dashed "ghost work" represents the *absence* of required compressor work. This violates the Clausius statement.

---

### 4.3 Combined Schematic: Equivalence Proof Setup (PMM2 + Real Refrigerator ⇒ PMM1)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph COMBINED_SYSTEM["COMBINED SYSTEM NO EXTERNAL WORK REQUIRED"]
        nodePMM2Block["PMM2 DEVICE Q FROM T_L TO T_H NO WORK"]
        nodeRealRef["REAL REFRIGERATOR W DRIVEN Q_L FROM T_L TO T_H"]
        nodeCombinedRes["NET HEAT REJECTED TO T_H"]
        nodeWorkCancel["WORK INPUT W CANCELLED BY PMM1 WORK OUTPUT"]
    end
    nodeCold2["COLD RESERVOIR T_L"] -->|"Q_L,C downward"| nodeRealRef
    nodeRealRef -->|"Q_H,C upward"| nodeCombinedRes
    nodeCold2 -->|"Q upward via PMM2"| nodePMM2Block
    nodePMM2Block -->|"Q delivered to T_H"| nodeCombinedRes
    nodeWorkCancel -->|"Composite single reservoir engine"| nodePMM1Emergent["PMM1 EMERGES"]
    style nodeCold2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style nodePMM2Block fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeRealRef fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeCombinedRes fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeWorkCancel fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style nodePMM1Emergent fill:#FF1493,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:3px
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** When a PMM2 is coupled with a real refrigerator (using PMM1's work to drive the refrigerator), the **cold reservoir sees no net heat exchange**. The combined system exchanges heat **only** with the hot reservoir T_H, producing equivalent work — which is the **signature of a PMM1**. Thus, **PMM2 ⇒ PMM1**. The converse (PMM1 ⇒ PMM2) follows symmetrically.

---

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Identifying a Perpetual-Motion Machine

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart["PROPOSED DEVICE CLAIMED PERPETUAL"]
    nodeQ1{"Does it produce work with no energy input?"}
    nodePMM1Conf["CLASSIFIED AS PMM1 FIRST KIND"]
    nodeQ2{"Does it transfer heat cold to hot with no work input?"}
    nodePMM2Conf["CLASSIFIED AS PMM2 SECOND KIND"]
    nodeQ3{"Does it claim infinite COP or 100 percent efficiency?"}
    nodeImpossible["CONFIRMED THERMODYNAMICALLY IMPOSSIBLE"]
    nodePossible["POSSIBLE BUT LIMITED BY CARNOT / REAL CONSTRAINTS"]
    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -->|"YES"| nodePMM1Conf
    nodeQ1 -->|"NO"| nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -->|"YES"| nodePMM2Conf
    nodeQ2 -->|"NO"| nodeQ3
    nodePMM1Conf --> nodeImpossible
    nodePMM2Conf --> nodeImpossible
    nodeQ3 -->|"YES"| nodeImpossible
    nodeQ3 -->|"NO"| nodePossible
    style nodeStart fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeQ1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeQ2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeQ3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style nodePMM1Conf fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style nodePMM2Conf fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeImpossible fill:#8B0000,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:3px
    style nodePossible fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart is the **KTU examiner's mental checklist** when evaluating a student's answer on PMM identification. If the student forgets to ask one of the three diagnostic questions, they lose 2 marks on classification.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**
*Define a perpetual-motion machine of the second kind (PMM2). Explain why it violates the Clausius statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Perpetual-Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2)** is a hypothetical cyclic device that transfers heat from a low-temperature reservoir (T_L) to a high-temperature reservoir (T_H) **without any external work input** (W_net,in = 0).

It violates the **Clausius statement**, which asserts:
> *"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

For such a device, the entropy change of the universe would be:

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \frac{Q_L}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = Q_L \left( \frac{1}{T_H} - \frac{1}{T_L} \right) < 0$$

since **T_H > T_L** and **Q_L > 0**. This violates the Second Law requirement that **ΔS_univ ≥ 0** for all real processes. Hence, PMM2 is **thermodynamically impossible**. **(3 Marks: 1 — definition, 1 — Clausius statement, 1 — entropy contradiction)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
*State the Kelvin–Planck statement. How is its violation related to a perpetual-motion machine of the first kind (PMM1)?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Kelvin–Planck Statement:**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single thermal reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*

A **PMM1** is a hypothetical device that operates in a cycle, absorbs heat **Q_H** from a **single reservoir at T_H**, and converts it entirely into work **W = Q_H** (with **Q_L = 0**). Its thermal efficiency would be:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1.0 = 100\%$$

This is a **direct violation** of the Kelvin–Planck statement, which forbids any cyclic device from achieving 100% efficiency with a single reservoir. Therefore, **PMM1 is impossible**. **(3 Marks: 1 — K-P statement, 1 — PMM1 mechanism, 1 — efficiency contradiction)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 3, Main Question]**
*(a)* State and explain the Kelvin–Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* Prove the equivalence of the two statements using perpetual-motion machines. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Kelvin–Planck Statement:**
It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than:
- Extracting heat from a single thermal reservoir, AND
- Performing an equivalent amount of work.

In other words, a cyclic heat engine **must reject some heat** to a low-temperature sink. It cannot have 100% efficiency.

$$\eta_{th} < 1 \quad \text{always}$$

The only exception would be if the sink temperature were at absolute zero, which is unattainable (Third Law).

**Clausius Statement:**
It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than:
- Transferring heat from a cold body to a hot body.

In other words, heat cannot spontaneously flow from a lower temperature to a higher temperature **without external work input**. All real refrigerators require work input (W_in > 0) to pump heat from T_L to T_H.

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}} \quad \text{is finite, never infinite}$$

**(Marks breakdown: 1 + 2 for K-P, 1 + 2 for Clausius, 1 for summary)**

---

**Part (b) — Proof of Equivalence — 7 Marks**

We prove the equivalence by contradiction in two parts.

**Part B1: Violation of Clausius (PMM2) ⇒ Violation of Kelvin–Planck (PMM1)**

Assume a PMM2 exists: it transfers heat **Q** from T_L to T_H with no work input.
