> **Given:** A Carnot engine operating between $T_H$ (hot reservoir) and $T_C$ (cold reservoir), with $T_H > T_C$.
> **Find:** Expression for thermal efficiency $\eta_{th}$.

### Step 1 — Define Thermal Efficiency
By definition, thermal efficiency of a heat engine is the ratio of net work output to total heat input:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the First Law to the Complete Cycle
For a closed cycle, the working fluid returns to its initial state, hence $\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$. By the First Law:

$$W_{net} = Q_{net} = Q_H - Q_C$$

Substitute this into the efficiency equation:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_H - Q_C}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_C}{Q_H}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Second Law (Reversibility Condition)
For a **reversible** cycle, the Clausius equality must hold:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$

Breaking the cycle into the four processes:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_C}{T_C} + 0 + 0 = 0$$

The two isentropic processes have $\delta Q = 0$, hence drop out. Rearranging:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_C}{T_C} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{Q_C}{Q_H} = \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

### Step 4 — Final Substitution
Plugging the result from Step 3 into the efficiency expression from Step 2:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = \frac{T_H - T_C}{T_H}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}}$
>
> Valid only when $T_H$ and $T_C$ are in **absolute temperature scale (Kelvin)**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: COP of Reversed Carnot Refrigerator (COP_R)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A Reversed Carnot refrigerator absorbing heat $Q_C$ at low temperature $T_C$ and rejecting heat $Q_H$ at high temperature $T_H$, with work input $W_{net}$.
> **Find:** Expression for $COP_R$.

### Step 1 — Define COP for a Refrigerator
The Coefficient of Performance for a refrigerator is the ratio of **desired effect** (heat extracted from cold space) to **work input** required:

$$COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_C}{W_{net}}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the First Law to the Cycle
Energy balance for the reversed cycle (work is input, so $W_{net}$ is supplied to the system):

$$Q_H = Q_C + W_{net} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad W_{net} = Q_H - Q_C$$

Substitute:

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_C}{Q_H - Q_C}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Second Law (Reversibility)
For the Reversed Carnot cycle, the same equality holds (since all processes are still reversible):

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_C}{T_C}$$

Rearranging:

$$Q_H = Q_C \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_C}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_C}{Q_C \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_C} - Q_C} = \frac{Q_C}{Q_C \left(\dfrac{T_H}{T_C} - 1\right)} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_H - T_C}{T_C}}$$

$$\boxed{COP_R = \frac{T_C}{T_H - T_C}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: COP of Reversed Carnot Heat Pump (COP_HP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** Same reversed cycle, but now the *desired effect* is heat delivered to the hot space.
> **Find:** $COP_{HP}$.

### Step 1 — Define COP for a Heat Pump

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$

### Step 2 — Apply First Law
$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_C$, so:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_C} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{Q_C}{Q_H}}$$

### Step 3 — Use Reversibility Condition
$\dfrac{Q_C}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_C}$$

$$\boxed{COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_C}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Universal Relationship COP_HP = COP_R + 1

### Step 1 — Start from the Two COP Definitions

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_C}{W_{net}}, \quad COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$

### Step 2 — Add $COP_R + 1$

$$COP_R + 1 = \frac{Q_C}{W_{net}} + 1 = \frac{Q_C + W_{net}}{W_{net}}$$

By the First Law, $Q_C + W_{net} = Q_H$ (since the work input plus the heat extracted from the cold space equals the total heat dumped to the hot space). Substituting:

$$COP_R + 1 = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = COP_{HP}$$

$$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Reference Values

| Parameter | Hot Reservoir ($T_H$) | Cold Reservoir ($T_C$) | $\eta_{Carnot}$ | $COP_R$ | $COP_{HP}$ |
|:---------:|:---------------------:|:----------------------:|:----------------:|:-------:|:----------:|
| Steam Power Plant | 800 K | 300 K | 0.625 (62.5%) | — | — |
| Automotive Engine | 1500 K | 300 K | 0.800 (80.0%) | — | — |
| Domestic Refrigerator | 300 K (27°C) | 263 K (−10°C) | — | 7.09 | 8.09 |
| Building Heat Pump | 293 K (20°C) | 273 K (0°C) | — | 13.65 | 14.65 |
| LNG Liquefaction | 300 K | 111 K | — | 0.587 | 1.587 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Carnot Cycle — P-V and T-s Process Flow (Mermaid)

The following block diagram shows the **logical sequence of states** and the energy transfers for the Carnot power cycle. This replaces the conventional P-V diagram with a **block-level functional architecture** (Mermaid-compatible).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[State 1: T1 = TH, V1 small] -->|Process 1-2: Isothermal Expansion| B[State 2: T2 = TH, V2 larger]
    B -->|Process 2-3: Isentropic Expansion| C[State 3: T3 = TC, V3 largest]
    C -->|Process 3-4: Isothermal Compression| D[State 4: T4 = TC, V4 smaller]
    D -->|Process 4-1: Isentropic Compression| A
    H[Hot Reservoir at TH] -->|QH absorbed| A
    C -->|QC rejected| CS[Cold Sink at TC]
    A -->|Net Work Output Wnet| W[Net Work Shaft]
```

## 4.2 Reversed Carnot Cycle — Functional Flow (Refrigeration Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[State 1: T1 = TC, V1 small] -->|Process 1-2: Isentropic Compression| B1[State 2: T2 = TH, V2 medium]
    B1 -->|Process 2-3: Isothermal Heat Rejection| C1[State 3: T3 = TH, V3 larger]
    C1 -->|Process 3-4: Isentropic Expansion| D1[State 4: T4 = TC, V4 largest]
    D1 -->|Process 4-1: Isothermal Heat Absorption| A1
    WS[Work Input Wnet] -->|External power supply| A1
    D1 -->|QC absorbed from cold space| CR[Cold Refrigerated Space at TC]
    B1 -->|QH rejected to hot space| HR[Hot Surroundings at TH]
```

## 4.3 Carnot Cycle — T-s Diagram (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Point 1: High s, T = TH"] -->|"Isothermal: s increases, T=TH constant"| P2["Point 2: Even higher s, T = TH"]
    P2 -->|"Isentropic: s constant, T drops TH to TC"| P3["Point 3: Same s as 2, T = TC"]
    P3 -->|"Isothermal: s decreases, T=TC constant"| P4["Point 4: Lower s, T = TC"]
    P4 -->|"Isentropic: s constant, T rises TC to TH"| P1
```

## 4.4 Schematic Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CC["Standard Carnot Cycle"] -->|Time-reversal| RCC["Reversed Carnot Cycle"]
    CC -->|Produces| W_OUT["Net Work Output positive"]
    CC -->|Heat flow| HF1["QH in, QC out"]
    RCC -->|Consumes| W_IN["Net Work Input positive"]
    RCC -->|Heat flow| HF2["QC in, QH out"]
    CC -->|Used as benchmark for| BE["Heat Engines"]
    RCC -->|Used as benchmark for| RP["Refrigerators and Heat Pumps"]
```

## 4.5 Energy-Entropy Flow Block (Unified View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HR[Hot Reservoir TH] -->|QH| WF[Working Fluid]
    WF -->|QC| CS[Cold Sink TC]
    WF -->|"Wnet = QH - QC"| SH[Shaft / External]
    subgraph "T-s Rectangle (enclosed area = Wnet)"
    WF
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**Define the Carnot cycle and state why no real heat engine can exceed its efficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Carnot cycle is a **reversible thermodynamic cycle** consisting of two isothermal and two isentropic processes, operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_C$.
- **(1 mark)** Its efficiency is $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_C/T_H$.
- **(1 mark)** A real engine has **irreversibilities** (friction, finite $\Delta T$ for heat transfer, unrestrained expansion) which generate entropy and dissipate work potential. Hence, by the **Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law**, $\eta_{real} \leq \eta_{Carnot}$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**State the Clausius equality and explain its role in deriving the Carnot efficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Clausius equality: $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$ for any **reversible** cycle.
- **(1 mark)** Applied to Carnot cycle: $\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} - \dfrac{Q_C}{T_C} = 0$ (isentropic processes contribute zero).
- **(1 mark)** This yields $\dfrac{Q_C}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$, which when substituted into $\eta = 1 - Q_C/Q_H$ gives the Carnot efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Total)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **EITHER** the full Question A **OR** the full Question B.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Style]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive an expression for the thermal efficiency of a Carnot cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_H$ and $T_C$. Show the Carnot cycle on a P-V and T-s diagram.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** State the four processes: isothermal expansion at $T_H$ (1→2), isentropic expansion $T_H \to T_C$ (2→3), isothermal compression at $T_C$ (3→4), isentropic compression $T_C \to T_H$ (4→1).
2. **(1 mark)** Write the First Law for the cycle: $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_C$.
3. **(1 mark)** Write efficiency definition: $\eta = W_{net}/Q_H = 1 - Q_C/Q_H$.
4. **(1 mark)** Apply the Clausius equality: $Q_H/T_H = Q_C/T_C$, hence $Q_C/Q_H = T_C/T_H$.
5. **(1 mark)** Final result: $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_C/T_H$.
6. **(1 mark)** Sketch both P-V (two isotherms + two adiabats) and T-s (rectangle) diagrams.
7. **(1 mark)** State the assumption: reversible processes, constant temperature reservoirs.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A Carnot engine receives heat from a source at 1000 K and rejects heat to a sink at 300 K. If the engine produces 100 kW of net power, determine: (i) Thermal efficiency, (ii) Heat supplied per second, (iii) Heat rejected per second.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T_H = 1000$ K, $T_C = 300$ K, $W_{net} = 100$ kW.

**(i) Thermal efficiency (2 marks):**
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{1000} = 0.70 = 70\%$$

**(ii) Heat supplied $Q_H$ (2.5 marks):**
$$Q_H = \frac{W_{net}}{\eta} = \frac{100}{0.70} = 142.857 \text{ kW} \approx 142.86 \text{ kJ/s}$$

**(iii) Heat rejected $Q_C$ (2.5 marks):**
$$Q_C = Q_H - W_{net} = 142.86 - 100 = 42.86 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Common Mark Loss):**
> - Students frequently **forget to convert Celsius to Kelvin** — if given source at 727°C, you MUST convert to 1000 K. Using °C directly gives wrong answers and **zero credit** for that sub-part.
> - The ratio $Q_C/Q_H = T_C/T_H$ is **only valid** for a Carnot cycle, not for arbitrary cycles. Do not generalize.
> - **Always show units** in the final answer (kW, kJ/s). Missing units may cost 0.5 mark per sub-part.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Reversed Carnot cycle with a neat P-V and T-s diagram. Derive the expression for the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator working on this cycle.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** Define the Reversed Carnot cycle as the time-reversed execution of the standard Carnot cycle — net work is **input** to transfer heat from low to high temperature.
2. **(1 mark)** State the four reversed processes: isentropic compression (1→2), isothermal heat rejection at $T_H$ (2→3), isentropic expansion (3→4), isothermal heat absorption at $T_C$ (4→1).
3. **(1 mark)** Sketch the P-V and T-s diagrams (T-s diagram is again a rectangle, traversed **anticlockwise**).
4. **(1 mark)** Define COP for a refrigerator: $COP_R = Q_C/W_{net}$.
5. **(1 mark)** Apply First Law: $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_C$, hence $COP_R = Q_C/(Q_H - Q_C)$.
6. **(1 mark)** Use reversibility: $Q_H/T_H = Q_C/T_C \Rightarrow Q_H = Q_C T_H/T_C$.
7. **(1 mark)** Substitute and simplify to get $COP_R = T_C/(T_H - T_C)$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A Reversed Carnot refrigerator is used to maintain a cold space at $-15°C$ by rejecting heat to surroundings at $25°C$. If the refrigeration load is 5 kW, determine: (i) COP of the refrigerator, (ii) Power input to the compressor, (iii) Heat rejected to surroundings, (iv) COP if the same machine is used as a heat pump.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T_C = -15 + 273 = 258$ K, $T_H = 25 + 273 = 298$ K, $Q_C = 5$ kW.

**(i) COP of Refrigerator (1.5 marks):**
$$COP_R = \frac{T_C}{T_H - T_C} = \frac{258}{298 - 258} = \frac{258}{40} = 6.45$$

**(ii) Power Input (1.5 marks):**
$$W_{net} = \frac{Q_C}{COP_R} = \frac{5}{6.45} = 0.775 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected to Surroundings (2 marks):**
$$Q_H = Q_C + W_{net} = 5 + 0.775 = 5.775 \text{ kW}$$

**(iv) COP as Heat Pump (2 marks):**
$$COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_C} = \frac{298}{40} = 7.45$$

**Verification using universal relation (bonus 0.5 mark):**
$$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 6.45 + 1 = 7.45 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Most common error:** Students compute $T_H - T_C$ using **Celsius values** (e.g., 25 − (−15) = 40°C) but the **ratio requires Kelvin** for the ratio, so this happens to work numerically. However, if the question gives source and sink in mixed units (e.g., 100°C and 300 K), Celsius-Kelvin mixing **will** give wrong answers. **Always convert to Kelvin FIRST.**
> - Students forget the **sign of work** — for a refrigerator, $W_{net}$ is **input** (positive magnitude of input).
> - Don't confuse the desired effect: for **refrigerator** use $Q_C$ in numerator; for **heat pump** use $Q_H$. The same physical machine can be either, but the COP is computed differently.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3:**

- ✅ Carnot cycle = 2 isothermal + 2 isentropic processes, all reversible.
- ✅ Operates between **two thermal reservoirs** at $T_H$ and $T_C$ (absolute scale).
- ✅ Efficiency: $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_C/T_H = 1 - Q_C/Q_H$.
- ✅ Heat ratio identity: $Q_C/Q_H = T_C/T_H$ (Carnot only).
- ✅ Carnot cycle is the **upper bound** for all real heat engines (Second Law corollary).
- ✅ Reversed Carnot cycle = time-reversed execution → net work input.
- ✅ Reversed Carnot sets the **upper bound for COP** of all refrigerators/heat pumps.
- ✅ $COP_R = T_C/(T_H - T_C) = Q_C/W_{net}$.
- ✅ $COP_{HP} = T_H/(T_H - T_C) = Q_H/W_{net}$.
- ✅ Universal identity: $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ (works for **any** cycle, not just Carnot).
- ✅ $COP_R$ is **always greater than 1** for typical refrigeration applications; $COP_{HP}$ is always **at least 1**.
- ✅ Area enclosed in T-s diagram = Net work (or net work input for reversed cycle).
- ✅ Carnot cycle is **rectangular** on T-s diagram — its simplicity is its strength.
- ✅ **Temperatures MUST be in Kelvin (or Rankine)** — never Celsius or Fahrenheit.
- ✅ The Carnot cycle is **idealized**; real engines approximate it but never match it.
- ✅ PMM2 (Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind) is impossible — a single-reservoir engine with 100% efficiency cannot exist.
- ✅ The Clausius equality $\oint \delta Q_{rev}/T = 0$ is the **mathematical backbone** of the Carnot derivation.
- ✅ The **Kelvin-Planck statement** forbids complete conversion of heat to work in a cycle.
- ✅ The **Clausius statement** forbids spontaneous heat flow from cold to hot body.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carnot Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Carnot Principles ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Carnot Principles** (also known as **Carnot's Theorem**) are two foundational propositions formulated by **Sadi Carnot (1824)** that establish the maximum theoretical efficiency attainable by any heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs. In the KTU 2024 Engineering Thermodynamics syllabus, the Carnot Principles are stated as two corollaries under the broader umbrella of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Carnot's Theorem (KTU Definition):** *No heat engine operating between two given thermal reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible (Carnot) heat engine operating between the same two reservoirs, and all reversible heat engines operating between the same two thermal reservoirs have the same efficiency.* The Carnot Principles consist of two distinct propositions: 1. **Proposition I (Efficiency Limit):** The efficiency of any irreversible heat engine operating between two given reservoirs is always **less than** the efficiency of a reversible heat engine operating between the same two reservoirs. 2. **Proposition II (Equivalence of Reversible Engines):** All reversible heat engines operating between the same two thermal reservoirs (at temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$) have **identical efficiencies**, and this efficiency depends **only** on the reservoir temperatures — not on the working substance. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Water Wheel" Analogy for Carnot Principles:** > Imagine a water wheel driven by water falling from a high tank to a low tank. The maximum power you can extract depends **only on the height difference** between the two tanks, not on the wheel's design. A *frictionless* (reversible) wheel extracts the maximum possible energy. Any real wheel with friction (irreversible) extracts less. Furthermore, any *frictionless* wheel — whether made of wood, metal, or plastic — extracts the **exact same maximum energy**. In thermodynamics, the "height difference" is the **temperature difference** between the hot reservoir ($T_H$) and the cold reservoir ($T_L$). The "frictionless wheel" is the **reversible (Carnot) engine**, and "real wheels with friction" are **irreversible real engines** (like petrol/diesel engines, steam turbines, etc.). ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Absolute Zero Reference:** $\mathbf{0\,K = -273.15\,^\circ C}$ (Third Law Reference Point) - **Carnot Efficiency (Maximum Theoretical):** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ - **COP of Reversible Refrigerator:** $COP_{ref} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ - **COP of Reversible Heat Pump:** $COP_{hp} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ > [!TIP] > For KTU 2024 numericals, **temperatures must ALWAYS be in Kelvin (K)** when substituted into Carnot efficiency formulas. Using Celsius will yield incorrect answers and is a common mistake penalized heavily in board exams. ### 1.4 Significance in KTU 2024 Curriculum The Carnot Principles form the **cornerstone bridge** between the **First Law (energy conservation)** and the **Second Law (entropy / directionality)**. They are the precursor to: - **Entropy** (Clausius Inequality) - **Availability / Exergy** (Module 4–5 topics) - **Vapour Compression Cycles** (Module 4) - **Psychrometrics** (Module 5) > [!WARNING] > A frequent student misconception: Carnot efficiency is **NOT** the efficiency of a real engine. It is the **theoretical maximum** that no real (irreversible) engine can ever exceed. Real engines ALWAYS have $\eta_{real} < \eta_{Carnot}$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Detailed Statement of Carnot's Two Propositions #### Proposition I — The Efficiency Limit Principle **Statement:** *It is impossible to construct a heat engine that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a single body (reservoir) so as to produce an equivalent amount of work.* — (Kelvin-Planck Statement, equivalent to Carnot's 1st Proposition) **Operational Logic:** - Consider two heat engines $E_A$ (reversible) and $E_B$ (irreversible) operating between the same hot reservoir at $T_H$ and cold reservoir at $T_L$. - Both engines receive the same heat $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir. - Suppose (hypothetically) that $\eta_B > \eta_A$ (i.e., the irreversible engine is more efficient). - Then $W_B > W_A$, which means $Q_{L,B} < Q_{L,A}$. - By reversing $E_A$ (using $W_B$ to drive it), we get a **net combined effect** of: spontaneous transfer of heat from the cold reservoir to the hot reservoir **without any external work** — violating the **Clausius Statement** of the Second Law. - This contradiction proves: $\boxed{\eta_{irreversible} < \eta_{reversible}}$. > [!NOTE] > **"Why" this works:** The proof relies on the **Second Law contradiction argument**. If an irreversible engine were more efficient, we could couple it with a reversed reversible engine to violate Clausius's statement — which has been empirically validated. Hence the assumption is rejected. #### Proposition II — Equivalence of All Reversible Engines **Statement:** *All reversible heat engines operating between the same two thermal reservoirs have the same efficiency, which depends only on the reservoir temperatures.* **Operational Logic:** - Consider two reversible engines $E_1$ and $E_2$ operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$. - Run $E_1$ forward (producing work $W_1$) and $E_2$ in reverse (consuming $W_2$). - By Proposition I applied bidirectionally (since both are reversible), we must have $\eta_1 \le \eta_2$ and $\eta_2 \le \eta_1$. - The only way both inequalities hold simultaneously is: $\eta_1 = \eta_2$. - Since neither depends on the working substance (air, water, refrigerant, etc.), efficiency must be a **universal function of temperature alone**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** This proposition is the thermodynamic justification for defining the **thermodynamic temperature scale** (Kelvin scale). Carnot's theorem proves that a temperature scale independent of the working fluid can be constructed purely from the ratio of heats exchanged in a reversible cycle. ### 2.2 The Carnot Cycle on P-V and T-S Diagrams The **Carnot cycle** is the practical embodiment of Carnot's Principles. It consists of **four reversible processes**: | Process | Type | Isothermal | Adiabatic | |---|---|---|---| | 1 → 2 | Heat Addition (Isothermal Expansion) | Yes (at $T_H$) | No | | 2 → 3 | Reversible Adiabatic Expansion | No | Yes | | 3 → 4 | Heat Rejection (Isothermal Compression) | Yes (at $T_L$) | No | | 4 → 1 | Reversible Adiabatic Compression | No | Yes | - **Processes 1-2 and 3-4** are isothermal, requiring heat transfer with external reservoirs. - **Processes 2-3 and 4-1** are isentropic (reversible adiabatic), with no heat transfer. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All temperatures in the formulas below MUST be in Kelvin (K).** Conversion: $T(K) = T(^\circ C) + 273.15$. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Maximum (Carnot) efficiency of a heat engine | dimensionless | | 2 | $\eta_{Carnot} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \dfrac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H}$ | General heat engine efficiency definition | dimensionless | | 3 | $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Heat ratio in a reversible (Carnot) cycle | dimensionless | | 4 | $COP_{Refrigerator} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible refrigerator COP | dimensionless | | 5 | $COP_{Heat\,Pump} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible heat pump COP | dimensionless | | 6 | $COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1$ | Relation between heat pump and refrigerator COPs | dimensionless | | 7 | $\dfrac{Q_{L,rev}}{Q_{H,rev}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Universal heat ratio (Proposition II consequence) | dimensionless | | 8 | $W_{net} = Q_H \left(1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}\right)$ | Net work output of Carnot engine | kJ | | 9 | $\Delta S_{cycle} = 0$ | Entropy change over complete Carnot cycle | kJ/K | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Relevance of Carnot Principles | |:---|:---| | **Power Plants** (Steam, Gas, Nuclear) | Carnot efficiency sets the upper bound for cycle efficiency; engineers use it as a benchmark to grade real cycles (Rankine, Brayton, Otto, Diesel). | | **Refrigeration & HVAC** | Sets the maximum possible COP for any refrigerator/AC; the higher the temperature lift $(T_H - T_L)$, the lower the COP. | | **Internal Combustion Engines** | Carnot cycle (with ideal gas) is a theoretical limit; real SI/CI engines have $\eta_{real} \approx 0.25$ to $0.40$ vs. $\eta_{Carnot} \approx 0.60$ to $0.80$. | | **Cryogenics & Liquefaction** | Determining minimum work input for cooling processes. | | **Renewable Energy** (Solar/Thermal) | Solar collectors face inherently low $T_H$ (sun's effective temperature), limiting Carnot efficiency to ~30–40%. | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Carnot Efficiency from First and Second Laws We start by considering a **reversible Carnot cycle** operating between a hot reservoir at temperature $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at temperature $T_L$. **Step 1: Apply the First Law to a complete cycle.** For any cyclic process, the net change in internal energy is zero ($\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$). Therefore: $$W_{net} = Q_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$ where: - $Q_H$ = heat absorbed from the hot reservoir during isothermal expansion - $Q_L$ = heat rejected to the cold reservoir during isothermal compression **Step 2: Apply the Second Law (Clausius Equality) to a reversible cycle.** The Clausius inequality states: $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$$ For a **reversible** cycle, equality holds: $$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$ **Step 3: Break the integral into the four Carnot processes.** Since processes 2-3 and 4-1 are adiabatic (reversible), $\delta Q = 0$ for them. Only the two isothermal processes contribute: $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{-Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$ The negative sign on $Q_L$ arises because heat is *rejected* from the system (negative heat to the system). **Step 4: Rearrange the equation to get the heat ratio.** $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}}$$ **Step 5: Substitute into the efficiency expression.** The thermal efficiency of any heat engine is defined as: $$\eta = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$ Substituting the result from Step 4: $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ **Step 6: Final result and physical interpretation.** $$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}}$$ - The efficiency depends **only** on the absolute temperatures of the two reservoirs. - Efficiency increases as $T_H$ increases or as $T_L$ decreases. - Efficiency approaches **100%** only if $T_L \to 0\,K$ (impossible per the Third Law). - Efficiency equals **0%** if $T_H = T_L$ (no temperature difference → no work). ### 3.2 Derivation of Reversible Refrigerator COP A refrigerator is a **reversed Carnot heat engine**. The Coefficient of Performance (COP) is defined as the desired effect divided by the work input: $$COP_{Ref} = \frac{\text{Desired cooling effect}}{\text{Work input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$ **Step 1:** From the First Law applied to the reversed cycle: $$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$ **Step 2:** From the Clausius equality (Step 3 of previous derivation): $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$ $$Q_H = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $Q_H$ into the work expression: $$W_{net} = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L = Q_L \left(\frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Substitute into the COP definition: $$COP_{Ref} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \left(\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}\right)}$$ $$\boxed{COP_{Ref} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Reversible Heat Pump COP A heat pump's desired effect is the heat delivered to the hot reservoir: $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$ **Step 1:** From Clausius equality: $$Q_L = Q_H \cdot \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ **Step 2:** Work input: $$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L = Q_H - Q_H \cdot \frac{T_L}{T_H} = Q_H \left(\frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute: $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H \left(\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}\right)}$$ $$\boxed{COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}}$$ **Step 4: Relationship between the two COPs.** $$COP_{HP} - COP_{Ref} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} - \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H - T_L} = 1$$ $$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1}$$ ### 3.4 Proof that All Reversible Engines Have Equal Efficiency (Proposition II) **Setup:** Consider two reversible engines $E_A$ and $E_B$ operating between the same reservoirs at $T_H$ and $T_L$. **Step 1:** Run $E_A$ as an engine (producing work $W_A$ from $Q_{H,A}$) and $E_B$ in reverse as a refrigerator (consuming $W_B$ to reject $Q_{H,B}$). **Step 2:** Suppose $\eta_A > \eta_B$. Then for the same $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir: - $W_A > W_B$ (since $W = \eta \cdot Q_H$) - This excess work $W_A - W_B$ is available as net output. - The combined system would then be a self-acting engine producing net work from a single reservoir — **violating Kelvin-Planck statement**. **Step 3:** Therefore, $\eta_A \le \eta_B$. By symmetry (reverse the roles), $\eta_B \le \eta_A$. **Step 4:** Combining both inequalities: $\eta_A = \eta_B$. **Step 5:** Since the working substance is not constrained, the efficiency must depend only on the reservoirs: $$\boxed{\eta_{rev} = f(T_H, T_L) \text{ only — independent of working fluid}}$$ ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A Carnot heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H = 800\,K$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L = 300\,K$. The engine receives $Q_H = 1000\,kJ$ of heat per cycle. Calculate: (a) the thermal efficiency, (b) the net work output, (c) the heat rejected to the cold reservoir. **Solution:** **(a) Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625$$ $$\eta_{Carnot} = 62.5\%$$ **(b) Net Work Output:** $$W_{net} = \eta \cdot Q_H = 0.625 \times 1000 = 625\,kJ$$ **(c) Heat Rejected:** $$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1000 - 625 = 375\,kJ$$ **Verification using the heat ratio formula:** $$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H} = \frac{300}{800} = 0.375 \implies Q_L = 0.375 \times 1000 = 375\,kJ \quad \checkmark$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Carnot Cycle on P-V Diagram (Block-Level Representation) The P-V diagram of a Carnot cycle shows two isotherms (curves) connected by two adiabats (steeper curves). ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["STATE 1: High P, Low V
Saturation Point at T_H"] node2["STATE 2: Low P, High V
End of Isothermal Expansion"] node3["STATE 3: Very Low P, Very High V
End of Adiabatic Expansion at T_L"] node4["STATE 4: Medium P, Low V
End of Isothermal Compression at T_L"] node1 -->|"Process 1-2
ISOTHERMAL EXPANSION
Heat Q_H in"| node2 node2 -->|"Process 2-3
REVERSIBLE ADIABATIC
EXPANSION (W out)"| node3 node3 -->|"Process 3-4
ISOTHERMAL COMPRESSION
Heat Q_L out"| node4 node4 -->|"Process 4-1
REVERSIBLE ADIABATIC
COMPRESSION (W in)"| node1 style node1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node2 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node3 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Carnot Cycle on T-S Diagram (Rectangular Representation) On a T-S diagram, the Carnot cycle is a **rectangle** — this is the simplest and most elegant representation, which KTU examiners often ask students to draw. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph T_H_LINE["T_H Isotherm (Top - Heat Addition)"] nodeS1["State 1: Low Entropy S1"] nodeS2["State 2: High Entropy S2"] end subgraph T_L_LINE["T_L Isotherm (Bottom - Heat Rejection)"] nodeS3["State 3: High Entropy S2"] nodeS4["State 4: Low Entropy S1"] end nodeS1 -->|"1 to 2: Isothermal Heat Addition at T_H
Q_H = T_H x delta S"| nodeS2 nodeS2 -->|"2 to 3: Isentropic Expansion
delta S = 0, T drops from T_H to T_L"| nodeS3 nodeS3 -->|"3 to 4: Isothermal Heat Rejection at T_L
Q_L = T_L x delta S"| nodeS4 nodeS4 -->|"4 to 1: Isentropic Compression
delta S = 0, T rises from T_L to T_H"| nodeS1 style nodeS1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeS2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeS3 fill:#4169E1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeS4 fill:#4169E1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Visual Takeaway:** On the T-S diagram, the Carnot cycle forms a **rectangle**. The area enclosed by the rectangle represents the **net work output**: > $W_{net} = \text{Area} = (T_H - T_L) \times (S_2 - S_1)$. > Heat $Q_H$ is the area of the top rectangle (at $T_H$); $Q_L$ is the area of the bottom rectangle (at $T_L$). ### 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Carnot Heat Engine ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeHotRes["HOT RESERVOIR
T_H (High T)"] nodeEngine["REVERSIBLE CARNOT
HEAT ENGINE
4 Reversible Processes"] nodeColdRes["COLD RESERVOIR
T_L (Low T)"] nodeWork["NET WORK OUTPUT
W_net = Q_H - Q_L"] nodeSurr["ENVIRONMENT
Heat Rejected"] nodeHotRes -->|"Q_H = T_H * delta S"| nodeEngine nodeEngine -->|"Q_L = T_L * delta S"| nodeColdRes nodeEngine -->|"W_net = Q_H - Q_L"| nodeWork nodeColdRes -->|"Unwanted heat"| nodeSurr style nodeHotRes fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF style nodeEngine fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style nodeColdRes fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF style nodeWork fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeSurr fill:#808080,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` ### 4.4 Reversed Carnot Cycle (Refrigerator/Heat Pump) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeColdRes2["COLD SPACE
T_L (Low T)"] nodeRevEngine["REVERSED CARNOT
CYCLE - REFRIGERATOR"] nodeHotRes2["HOT SINK
T_H (High T)"] nodeWorkIn["WORK INPUT
W_in = Q_H - Q_L"] nodeColdRes2 -->|"Q_L extracted
from cold space"| nodeRevEngine nodeWorkIn -->|"External work
drives compressor"| nodeRevEngine nodeRevEngine -->|"Q_H rejected
to hot sink"| nodeHotRes2 style nodeColdRes2 fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF style nodeRevEngine fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF style nodeHotRes2 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF style nodeWorkIn fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.5 Energy Flow Summary Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["CARNOT ENGINE
INPUTS"] --> nodeB["Heat Q_H from T_H"] nodeA --> nodeC["Work Output W_net"] nodeD["CARNOT ENGINE
OUTPUTS"] --> nodeE["Heat Q_L to T_L"] nodeD --> nodeC nodeF["ENERGY BALANCE"] --> nodeG["W_net = Q_H - Q_L"] nodeF --> nodeH["eta = 1 - T_L/T_H"] nodeF --> nodeI["delta S_universe = 0"] style nodeA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px nodeC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px nodeD fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px nodeE fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px nodeF fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nodeG fill:#FFF,stroke:#000 style nodeH fill:#FFF,stroke:#000 style nodeI fill:#FFF,stroke:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **State and explain Carnot's theorem.** **Model Answer (3 marks — point-wise):** Carnot's theorem consists of two propositions: 1. **(1 mark)** **Proposition I:** No heat engine operating between two given thermal reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible (Carnot) heat engine operating between the same two reservoirs. In other words, $\eta_{irreversible} < \eta_{reversible}$. 2. **(1 mark)** **Proposition II:** All reversible heat engines operating between the same two thermal reservoirs have the same efficiency, which depends only on the reservoir temperatures, not on the working substance. 3. **(1 mark)** **Significance:** This theorem is the basis for defining the **Kelvin thermodynamic temperature scale** and establishes the absolute upper limit of efficiency for any heat engine — a critical result of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Differentiate between a heat engine, a refrigerator, and a heat pump using the Carnot cycle.** **Model Answer (3 marks — tabular):** | Parameter | Heat Engine | Refrigerator | Heat Pump | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Direction of Cycle** | Forward Carnot | Reverse Carnot | Reverse Carnot | | **Desired Output** | Work ($W_{net}$) | Cooling ($Q_L$) | Heating ($Q_H$) | | **Performance Metric** | $\eta = \dfrac{W}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | $COP_{ref} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | $COP_{hp} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | | **Application** | Power plants, vehicles | AC, cold storage | Building heating, geysers | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse the **direction of heat flow** in refrigerators vs. heat pumps. A **refrigerator's goal** is to extract $Q_L$ from a cold space (the "useful" output). A **heat pump's goal** is to deliver $Q_H$ to a hot space. Both operate on the **reversed Carnot cycle**, but with different "desired effect" definitions. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** State and prove Carnot's theorem. Show that all reversible heat engines operating between the same temperature limits have the same efficiency. **(b) [7 Marks]** A Carnot refrigerator operates between a cold reservoir at $-10\,^\circ C$ and a hot reservoir at $40\,^\circ C$. If the refrigerator removes $1200\,kJ$ of heat per minute from the cold space, calculate: (i) the COP of the refrigerator, (ii) the power input to the refrigerator in kW, and (iii) the heat rejected to the hot reservoir per minute. --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### **Part (a) — Proof of Carnot's Theorem [7 Marks]** **Statement (1.5 marks):** - **Proposition I:** No heat engine can be more efficient than a reversible engine operating between the same two reservoirs. - **Proposition II:** All reversible heat engines operating between the same two reservoirs have the same efficiency. **Proof of Proposition I (2.5 marks):** Consider two heat engines $E_1$ (reversible) and $E_2$ (irreversible) operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$. Both receive the same heat $Q_H$. Assume (for contradiction) that $\eta_2 > \eta_1$. Then: $$W_2 > W_1 \implies Q_{L,2} < Q_{L,1}$$ Now reverse $E_1$ to act as a refrigerator using work $W_2$ from $E_2$. The net work available to the reversed $E_1$ is: $$W_{net,combined} = W_2 - W_1 > 0$$ The net heat transferred to the cold reservoir is: $$Q_{L,net} = Q_{L,1} - Q_{L,2} > 0$$ This means the combined system **spontaneously transfers heat from the cold reservoir to the hot reservoir without external work** — violating the Clausius statement. Therefore, $\eta_2 \le \eta_1$. **Proof of Proposition II (2 marks):** By symmetry (swap roles of $E_1$ and $E_2$), $\eta_1 \le \eta_2$. Combined with $\eta_2 \le \eta_1$, we get $\eta_1 = \eta_2$. Since neither depends on the working substance, efficiency is purely a function of $T_H$ and $T_L$. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Hence, $\eta_{rev} = f(T_H, T_L)$, and is given by $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L / T_H$. --- #### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]** **Given Data:** - $T_L = -10 + 273 = 263\,K$ - $T_H = 40 + 273 = 313\,K$ - $Q_L = 1200\,kJ/min$ **(i) COP of the refrigerator (2 marks):** $$COP_{Ref} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50} = 5.26$$ **(ii) Power input to the refrigerator (2.5 marks):** $$W_{net} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_{Ref}} = \frac{1200}{5.26} = 228.14\,kJ/min$$ Converting to kW (divide by 60): $$P = \frac{228.14}{60} = 3.802\,kW$$ **(iii) Heat rejected to the hot reservoir (2.5 marks):** By First Law (energy balance): $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net} = 1200 + 228.14 = 1428.14\,kJ/min$$ **Verification using the heat ratio:** $$\frac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \frac{T_H}{T_L} \implies Q_H = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L} = 1200 \times \frac{313}{263} = 1428.14\,kJ/min \quad \checkmark$$ --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat P-V and T-S diagrams, describe the Carnot cycle. Derive the expression for the thermal efficiency of a Carnot engine. **(b) [7 Marks]** A Carnot heat engine receives $500\,kJ$ of heat from a source at $1000\,K$ and rejects heat to a sink at $300\,K$. Calculate: (i) the efficiency of the engine, (ii) the work output per cycle, (iii) the heat rejected to the sink, and (iv) the COP if the engine is reversed and used as a heat pump. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### **Part (a) — Carnot Cycle Description and Efficiency Derivation [7 Marks]** **Diagram Description (3 marks):** On the **P-V diagram**, the Carnot cycle consists of: - **1→2:** Isothermal expansion at $T_H$ (heat $Q_H$ in) - **2→3:** Reversible adiabatic expansion ($T$ drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$) - **3→4:** Isothermal compression at $T_L$ (heat $Q_L$ out) - **4→1:** Reversible adiabatic compression ($T$ rises from $T_L$ to $T_H$) On the **T-S diagram**, the cycle is a **rectangle** with: - Top edge at $T_H$ (length = $S_2 - S_1$, area under = $Q_H$) - Bottom edge at $T_L$ (area under = $Q_L$) - Enclosed area = $W_{net} = (T_H - T_L)(S_2 - S_1)$ **Derivation of Efficiency (4 marks):** Apply the First Law to the cycle: $$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$ Apply the Clausius equality to a reversible cycle: $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L} \implies \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ Therefore: $$\eta = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ --- #### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]** **Given:** $Q_H = 500\,kJ$, $T_H = 1000\,K$, $T_L = 300\,K$. **(i) Efficiency (1.5 marks):** $$\eta = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{1000} = 1 - 0.30 = 0.70 = 70\%$$ **(ii) Work output (1.5 marks):** $$W_{net} = \eta \cdot Q_H = 0.70 \times 500 = 350\,kJ$$ **(iii) Heat rejected (1.5 marks):** $$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 500 - 350 = 150\,kJ$$ **(iv) COP as Heat Pump (2.5 marks):** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{1000}{1000 - 300} = \frac{1000}{700} = 1.4286$$ This means for every 1 kJ of work input, the heat pump delivers 1.4286 kJ of heat to the hot space. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Temperature in Celsius:** The #1 mistake. Always convert to Kelvin. Marks lost: **1–2 per question**. > 2. **Confusing Heat Engine and Refrigerator COP:** Know the difference between $\eta$, $COP_{ref}$, and $COP_{hp}$. > 3. **Forgetting to specify Kelvin:** Even if the answer is correct, the examiner may deduct marks if the unit is missing or in Celsius. > 4. **Writing the Clausius inequality as equality for irreversible cycles:** Remember: $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$ for irreversible, $= 0$ for reversible. > 5. **Missing the "Reversed" qualifier:** The Carnot refrigerator/heat pump operates on the **reversed** Carnot cycle, which must be explicitly stated. > 6. **Incorrect area interpretation on T-S diagram:** Area enclosed = net work, not heat added. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Carnot Principles** - ✅ **Carnot's Theorem has TWO propositions** — efficiency limit + equivalence of reversible engines. - ✅ **Carnot efficiency formula:** $\eta = 1 - T_L / T_H$ (temperatures in **Kelvin**). - ✅ **Heat ratio in reversible cycle:** $Q_L / Q_H = T_L / T_H$. - ✅ **Refrigerator COP (max):** $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$. - ✅ **Heat Pump COP (max):** $T_H / (T_H - T_L)$. - ✅ **Key relation:** $COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1$. - ✅ **Carnot cycle on T-S = rectangle** (easiest to draw and most scoring in exams). - ✅ **Carnot cycle on P-V:** two isotherms + two adiabats, traversed clockwise (engine) or counter-clockwise (refrigerator). - ✅ **All reversible engines have equal efficiency** — depends only on reservoir temperatures, not working fluid. - ✅ **Irreversible engine efficiency < Carnot efficiency** (always). - ✅ **Kelvin-Planck and Clausius statements are equivalent** (Carnot's theorem proves this). - ✅ **Carnot engine is an idealization** — no real engine can achieve Carnot efficiency. - ✅ **Carnot Principles are a direct consequence of the Second Law** — they have NO derivation from the First Law alone. - ✅ **100% efficiency impossible** — would require $T_L = 0\,K$, violating the Third Law. - ✅ **For maximum efficiency:** Maximize $T_H$ and minimize $T_L$. - ✅ **For maximum refrigeration COP:** Minimize the temperature lift $(T_H - T_L)$. > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid:** *"HOT to COLD, work you make; COLD to HOT, work it takes."* — A simple rhyme to remember that heat engines and refrigerators are thermodynamic opposites.

topics.title: Thermodynamic Temperature Scale
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamic Temperature Scale

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Thermodynamic Temperature Scale

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermodynamic Temperature Scale (Kelvin Scale):** A temperature scale that is **independent of the properties of any particular thermometric substance**. It is defined operationally using the efficiency of a reversible heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs, as established by Lord Kelvin on the basis of Carnot's Theorem.

Mathematically, the ratio of thermodynamic temperatures of two reservoirs is defined by the ratio of heat exchanged by any reversible engine operating between them:

$$\frac{T_{H}}{T_{L}} \;=\; \frac{Q_{H}}{Q_{L}}\bigg|_{\text{rev}}$$

The **SI unit of thermodynamic temperature is the Kelvin (K)**, and the **fixed reference point is the triple point of pure water, assigned exactly 273.16 K**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two people, Alice and Bob, both trying to measure how "hot" a room is. Alice uses a mercury thermometer; Bob uses an alcohol thermometer. Because mercury and alcohol expand differently, Alice and Bob might disagree on the exact reading — even though the room is the same.

> A **thermodynamic temperature scale** is like an "absolute, unbiased judge" for hotness. It does not rely on how any particular material (mercury, alcohol, gas) expands. Instead, it asks a very deep question: *"If I run an idealized (reversible) heat engine between this room and a cold outside, what fraction of heat gets converted to work?"* The answer to this question depends **only on the two temperatures** — not on the engine's working fluid.

This means:
- A **large efficiency** ⇒ the hot reservoir is much hotter than the cold one.
- A **small efficiency** ⇒ the two reservoirs are close in temperature.
- If efficiency = 0 ⇒ both reservoirs are at the **same temperature**.

### 1.3 Why the Standard Scales (°C, °F) Are Not Sufficient

| Scale | Reference Points | Substance-Dependent? |
|---|---|---|
| Celsius | Ice point (0 °C), Steam point (100 °C) | Yes (mercury or alcohol) |
| Fahrenheit | Brine point, ice point, body temperature | Yes |
| Ideal Gas | Extrapolation of gas behavior at low pressure | Approximately (limit p → 0) |
| **Thermodynamic (Kelvin)** | **Triple point of water = 273.16 K** | **No — completely independent** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The thermodynamic temperature scale is introduced in Module 3 specifically because it is the *only* scale that emerges naturally from the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** and the **Carnot cycle** — making it the fundamental scale used in all of thermodynamics.

---

### 1.4 Historical Context

- **1824** — Sadi Carnot proposed that **all reversible engines between the same two reservoirs have the same efficiency**, regardless of working fluid.
- **1848** — Lord Kelvin (William Thomson) used this principle to define a universal temperature scale.
- **1854** — Clausius formalized the relationship and coined the term "thermodynamic temperature."
- **1954** — The 10th General Conference on Weights and Measures adopted the **triple point of water = 273.16 K** as the defining fixed point.
- **2019** — The kelvin was redefined in terms of the **Boltzmann constant** $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (exact).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Chain — From Carnot's Theorem to a Universal Scale

The development of the thermodynamic temperature scale follows a precise logical sequence. Each step builds on the previous one.

### Step 1 — Carnot's Theorem (Foundation Stone)

> [!NOTE]
> **Carnot's Theorem:** It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work. **Equivalently:** No heat engine operating between two given reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible heat engine operating between the same two reservoirs.

This has two crucial consequences:
- **All reversible heat engines** have the **same efficiency** when operating between the same two reservoirs.
- This common efficiency depends **only on the temperatures** of the two reservoirs — not on the working substance.

### Step 2 — Definition of the Thermodynamic Temperature Ratio

Let a reversible heat engine operate between a hot reservoir (H) and a cold reservoir (L).
Let it reject an amount of heat $Q_L$ to the cold reservoir while absorbing $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir.

**Define:**

$$\frac{T_{H}}{T_{L}} \;\equiv\; \frac{Q_{H}}{Q_{L}}$$

This is a **definition**, not a derived result. It defines what we mean by "ratio of thermodynamic temperatures."

> The fact that this definition is **consistent** — i.e., does not depend on which reversible engine we use — is guaranteed by Carnot's theorem.

### Step 3 — Fixing a Reference Point

To convert the ratio into an absolute scale, we need one fixed reference. The chosen reference is the **triple point of water**, where solid, liquid, and vapor coexist in equilibrium.

By definition:

$$T_{\text{tp}} \equiv 273.16 \text{ K}$$

For *any* reversible engine operating between the triple point reservoir and another reservoir at temperature $T$:

$$T \;=\; 273.16 \times \frac{Q}{Q_{\text{tp}}}$$

where $Q$ is heat rejected at temperature $T$ and $Q_{\text{tp}}$ is heat rejected at the triple point.

### Step 4 — The Kelvin Unit

One kelvin is defined as $\dfrac{1}{273.16}$ of the thermodynamic temperature of the triple point of water.

### Step 5 — Absolute Zero

From the efficiency expression:

$$\eta_{\text{rev}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{T_{L}}{T_{H}}$$

The efficiency can never exceed 1 (second law). As $T_L \to 0$, $\eta \to 1$. So the **lowest possible temperature is 0 K — absolute zero**, at which all reversible engines would convert *all* input heat into work.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L}\big\vert_{\text{rev}}$ | Definition of thermodynamic temperature ratio | K/K (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{rev}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot (reversible) efficiency | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{rev}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | Efficiency in terms of heat/work | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T_{\text{tp}} = 273.16$ K (exact) | Triple point of pure water | K |
| 5 | $T(K) = t(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$ | Conversion between Celsius and Kelvin | K |
| 6 | $\Delta T(K) = \Delta t(^{\circ}\text{C})$ | Temperature *differences* are identical | K or °C |
| 7 | $T \to 0 \Rightarrow \eta_{\text{rev}} \to 1$ | Absolute zero as limit of 100% efficiency | K |
| 8 | $W_{\text{net}} = Q_H - Q_L$ | First law for cyclic device | kJ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** For thermodynamic temperature calculations, **always use Kelvin**. Mixing °C in $T_L/T_H$ is the single most common source of error in board examinations.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Coefficient of performance $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ depends on thermodynamic temperatures, not Celsius.
- **Power plants:** Theoretical (Carnot) efficiency sets the upper bound for any cycle — Rankine, Brayton, Otto, Diesel all benchmarked against this.
- **Cryogenics:** Liquefaction of gases (N₂, He, H₂) requires approaching 0 K; the thermodynamic scale gives a clear meaning to "how cold."
- **Heat pump design:** COP of a heat pump is $\dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$.
- **International standards:** All thermodynamic property tables (steam tables, refrigerant tables) are tabulated vs. **K or °C** in a way that respects the Kelvin absolute scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Definition of Thermodynamic Temperature Ratio

**Goal:** Show that $\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L}$ for a reversible engine.

### Setup

Consider two reversible heat engines, A and B, both operating between the same hot reservoir at $T_H$ and cold reservoir at $T_L$. Engine A is a Carnot (reversible) engine; engine B is *any* arbitrary reversible engine.

We will prove that **any two reversible engines between the same two reservoirs must exchange heat in the same ratio**.

### Step 1 — Couple the Two Engines

Let engine A absorb $Q_H^{(A)}$ from the hot reservoir and reject $Q_L^{(A)}$ to the cold reservoir.
Let engine B absorb $Q_H^{(B)}$ and reject $Q_L^{(B)}$.

We will arrange the engines so that A drives B in **reverse** (as a refrigerator / heat pump). The combined device is itself a reversible cycle operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$.

### Step 2 — Apply Carnot's Theorem

By Carnot's theorem, since the combined device is reversible, its efficiency must equal the Carnot efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{combined}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

This is independent of what A and B individually do — it is a property of the reservoirs only.

### Step 3 — Net Heat Exchanges

For the combined device:
- Net heat from hot reservoir: $Q_H^{(A)} - Q_H^{(B)}$
- Net heat to cold reservoir: $Q_L^{(A)} - Q_L^{(B)}$
- Net work: $W_{\text{net}} = (Q_H^{(A)} - Q_H^{(B)}) - (Q_L^{(A)} - Q_L^{(B)})$

By the first law applied to the cyclic combined device: $W_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{net}}$. (No change in internal energy over a complete cycle.)

### Step 4 — The Key Insight

> **For the combined reversible device, the only meaningful measure of "hotness" is the ratio of the net heats exchanged, and this ratio must be the same for *any* reversible engine.**

If we now consider engine A as a **reference Carnot engine** and engine B as a **candidate engine**:

$$\frac{Q_H^{(A)}}{Q_L^{(A)}} \;=\; \frac{Q_H^{(B)}}{Q_L^{(B)}} \;=\; \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$

Thus, for **any** reversible engine:

$$\boxed{\frac{Q_H}{Q_L} \;=\; \frac{T_H}{T_L}}$$

This completes the derivation. The ratio is **substance-independent** because it does not refer to any specific working fluid.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reversible Engine Efficiency in Terms of Temperatures

**Starting point:** Definition from Derivation 1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{rev}} &= \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_H} \quad &\text{(definition of efficiency)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H} \quad &\text{(first law, cyclic: } W = Q_H - Q_L\text{)} \\[6pt]
&= 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} \quad &\text{(algebraic simplification)} \\[6pt]
&= 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} \quad &\text{(substitute the defining ratio } Q_L/Q_H = T_L/T_H\text{)} \\[6pt]
\therefore \quad \eta_{\text{rev}} &= 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} \quad &\blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is monumental:** This single equation tells us that the *upper limit* of any heat engine's efficiency is a number that depends only on the two reservoir temperatures. The properties of steam, air, gasoline — they all become irrelevant to the **theoretical maximum**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalence of Thermodynamic and Ideal-Gas Temperature Scales

This is the crucial step that links the **theoretical** (Kelvin) scale to the **experimental** (gas) scale.

### Setup

Take a reversible engine with an **ideal gas** as the working fluid. Run a Carnot cycle:
- Isothermal expansion at $T_H$ (gas absorbs $Q_H$)
- Adiabatic expansion from $T_H$ to $T_L$
- Isothermal compression at $T_L$ (gas rejects $Q_L$)
- Adiabatic compression from $T_L$ back to $T_H$

### Step 1 — Heat in Isothermal Processes

For an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature. Hence $\Delta U = 0$ in an isothermal process, and by the first law:

$$
Q_H = W_{\text{isothermal,exp}} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} p \, dV = nR T_H \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)
$$

$$
Q_L = W_{\text{isothermal,comp}} = nR T_L \ln\!\left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)
$$

(Negative sign absorbed; $Q_L$ is the magnitude of heat rejected.)

### Step 2 — Adiabatic Relations

For the two reversible adiabatic legs:

$$
\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = \frac{T_H}{T_L}, \qquad \left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = \frac{T_H}{T_L}
$$

Dividing these:

$$
\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{V_3}{V_4}
$$

### Step 3 — The Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_H}{Q_L} &= \frac{nR T_H \ln(V_2/V_1)}{nR T_L \ln(V_3/V_4)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{T_H \ln(V_2/V_1)}{T_L \ln(V_3/V_4)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{T_H \ln(V_2/V_1)}{T_L \ln(V_2/V_1)} \quad &\text{(using } V_2/V_1 = V_3/V_4 \text{)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{T_H}{T_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The thermodynamic temperature ratio equals the **ideal-gas temperature ratio**. This means that *if* we use the ideal-gas scale to measure temperature, it is **identical** to the thermodynamic scale. This is why thermometers using low-pressure gases (helium, hydrogen) provide the most accurate practical realization of the thermodynamic scale.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Demonstration

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample:** A reversible heat engine operates between a source at 600 K and a sink at 300 K. It absorbs 1000 kJ from the source. Find (i) efficiency, (ii) work output, (iii) heat rejected.

**Given:** $T_H = 600$ K, $T_L = 300$ K, $Q_H = 1000$ kJ

**(i) Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{rev}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{600} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 = 50\%$$

**(ii) Work Output:**

$$W = \eta \times Q_H = 0.5 \times 1000 = 500 \text{ kJ}$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected (by definition of thermodynamic temperature ratio):**

$$\frac{T_H}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_L} \Rightarrow Q_L = Q_H \times \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1000 \times \frac{300}{600} = 500 \text{ kJ}$$

**Check (First Law):** $W = Q_H - Q_L = 1000 - 500 = 500$ kJ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Using $Q_L = Q_H \times (1 - \eta) = 1000 \times 0.5 = 500$ kJ gives the same number here, but the **thermodynamic ratio method** is the one that *defines* the temperature scale — and it works even for irreversible engines when used carefully.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Logical Architecture of the Thermodynamic Temperature Scale

The following diagram maps the **logical flow** from the Second Law foundations to the final definition of the thermodynamic temperature scale. It is a "block-level functional architecture" rather than a physical device drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CARNOT THEOREM 2nd Law of Thermodynamics"]:::root --> B["All reversible engines between two reservoirs share the same efficiency"]
    B --> C["Efficiency depends ONLY on reservoir temperatures and NOT on the working substance"]
    C --> D["Define Temperature Ratio T_H over T_L as Q_H over Q_L for any reversible engine"]
    D --> E["Triple point of water fixed at 273.16 K as universal reference"]
    E --> F["Kelvin scale is fully defined substance independent absolute scale"]
    F --> G["Reversible efficiency eta_rev equals 1 minus T_L over T_H"]
    G --> H["As T_L approaches 0 K eta_rev approaches 1  ABSOLUTE ZERO"]
    D --> I["IDEAL GAS CARNOT CYCLE proof"]
    I --> J["Q_H over Q_L equals T_H over T_L using ideal gas"]
    J --> K["Thermodynamic scale identical to ideal gas scale at low pressure limit"]

    classDef root fill:#1a3a6e,stroke:#0b1f3d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    class A root
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Top branch (A → B → C → D → E → F → H):** Pure thermodynamic (Second-Law) derivation. Does not invoke any material.
- **Lower branch (D → I → J → K):** Shows the bridge to experimental thermometry via the ideal gas.
- The **central node (D)** — the definition of temperature ratio — is the keystone.

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Reversible Heat Engine as the "Thermometer"

The reversible engine itself acts as a thermometer. The following block diagram illustrates how two reversible devices share the same reservoirs but might be different working fluids, yet yield the same temperature ratio.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOT["HOT RESERVOIR T_H"]
        HR["Heat Q_H supplied"]
    end
    subgraph COLD["COLD RESERVOIR T_L"]
        CR["Heat Q_L rejected"]
    end
    subgraph ENGINE1["REVERSIBLE ENGINE 1 e.g. Carnot with ideal gas"]
        E1["eta_rev equals 1 minus T_L over T_H"]
    end
    subgraph ENGINE2["REVERSIBLE ENGINE 2 e.g. Carnot with steam"]
        E2["eta_rev equals 1 minus T_L over T_H"]
    end

    HOT --> ENGINE1
    ENGINE1 --> COLD
    HOT --> ENGINE2
    ENGINE2 --> COLD

    E1 -. "Same efficiency" .-> E2
    HR -. "Q_H" .-> E1
    E1 -. "Q_L" .-> CR
    HR -. "Q_H" .-> E2
    E2 -. "Q_L" .-> CR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Takeaway:** Whatever the working fluid of the reversible engine (ideal gas, steam, ammonia, …) and whatever its internal details, the ratio $Q_H/Q_L$ it exchanges with the reservoirs is the same. **This ratio is the thermodynamic temperature ratio.** This is the deep reason the scale is "universal."

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define thermodynamic temperature scale. Why is it considered superior to other temperature scales?**

**Model Answer:**

> The thermodynamic temperature scale (also called the Kelvin scale) is a temperature scale defined on the basis of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**, using the **efficiency of a reversible heat engine** operating between two thermal reservoirs. The ratio of the thermodynamic temperatures of two reservoirs is defined as the ratio of the heats exchanged by a reversible engine operating between them:
>
> $$\frac{T_H}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_L}$$
>
> The scale is anchored by assigning the **triple point of pure water** the value $T_{\text{tp}} = 273.16$ K.
>
> **It is superior** because:
> 1. It is **independent of the properties of any thermometric substance** (no reliance on mercury, alcohol, or any specific gas).
> 2. It is the **only scale** that emerges naturally from the fundamental laws of thermodynamics.
> 3. It is **universal** — the same scale is valid on Earth, in space, or in any laboratory.
> 4. It coincides with the **ideal-gas temperature scale** in the limit of zero pressure.
> 5. It is **absolute** — the zero of the scale is the unattainable absolute zero.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Two reversible heat engines operate between the same two reservoirs. One uses air, the other uses steam. Will they have the same efficiency? Justify using Carnot's theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Yes**, both engines will have the **same efficiency**.
>
> **Justification:** By **Carnot's theorem**, all reversible heat engines operating between the same two thermal reservoirs have the same efficiency, regardless of the working substance. Therefore, both the air-based and steam-based reversible engines must satisfy:
>
> $$\eta_{\text{rev}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$
>
> This efficiency depends **only on the reservoir temperatures** $T_H$ and $T_L$, and not on whether the working fluid is air, steam, helium, or any other substance. This very independence is what makes the thermodynamic temperature scale **universal**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the relationship $\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L}$ for a reversible heat engine, and hence obtain the expression for the Carnot (reversible) efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reversible heat engine operates between a source at 800 K and a sink at 300 K. The engine absorbs 2000 kJ of heat from the source. Determine: (i) the efficiency of the engine, (ii) the work output, (iii) the heat rejected to the sink. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Statement of Carnot's Theorem (1 mark):**
> Carnot's theorem states that all reversible heat engines operating between the same two reservoirs have the same efficiency, and no irreversible engine can be more efficient than a reversible one.

**Step 2 — Consider two reversible engines (1 mark):**
> Let reversible engine A (Carnot) and reversible engine B (any) operate between reservoirs at $T_H$ and $T_L$. Coupling them (A drives B in reverse) yields a reversible combined device.

**Step 3 — Apply efficiency equality (1 mark):**
> Because the combined device is reversible and operates between the same two reservoirs, its efficiency must equal the Carnot efficiency:
> $$\eta_{\text{combined}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 4 — Heat ratios must match (2 marks):**
> Working out the net heat exchanges for the combined device and equating with the Carnot efficiency yields the necessary condition:
> $$\frac{Q_H^{(A)}}{Q_L^{(A)}} = \frac{Q_H^{(B)}}{Q_L^{(B)}}$$
> Both ratios equal the temperature ratio.

**Step 5 — Define thermodynamic temperature ratio and derive efficiency (2 marks):**
> By definition, $\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L}$. Substituting:
> $$\eta_{\text{rev}} = \frac{W}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:** $T_H = 800$ K, $T_L = 300$ K, $Q_H = 2000$ kJ

**(i) Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{\text{rev}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 = 62.5\%
$$

**(ii) Work Output (2 marks):**

$$
W_{\text{net}} = \eta \times Q_H = 0.625 \times 2000 = 1250 \text{ kJ}
$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected (3 marks):**

Method 1 (thermodynamic ratio definition):
$$
Q_L = Q_H \times \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 2000 \times \frac{300}{800} = 2000 \times 0.375 = 750 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Verification (first law):**
$$
W = Q_H - Q_L = 2000 - 750 = 1250 \text{ kJ} \checkmark
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain why the ideal-gas temperature scale and the thermodynamic temperature scale are identical. Use a Carnot cycle with an ideal gas as the working fluid to derive the relationship. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reversible refrigerator maintains a space at $-10$ °C by removing heat at a rate of 3 kW from the space and rejecting it to surroundings at 25 °C. Determine: (i) the rate of heat rejected to the surroundings, (ii) the power input to the refrigerator, (iii) the COP of the refrigerator. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Set up the Carnot cycle with an ideal gas (1 mark):**
> Consider a reversible Carnot cycle using an ideal gas: isothermal expansion at $T_H$, adiabatic expansion to $T_L$, isothermal compression at $T_L$, adiabatic compression back to $T_H$.

**Step 2 — Heat absorbed in isothermal expansion (2 marks):**
> For an isothermal process of an ideal gas, $\Delta U = 0$, so $Q_H = W = nR T_H \ln(V_2/V_1)$.

**Step 3 — Heat rejected in isothermal compression (1 mark):**
> Similarly, $Q_L = nR T_L \ln(V_3/V_4)$.

**Step 4 — Use adiabatic relations to simplify (2 marks):**
> For reversible adiabats: $T_H V_2^{\gamma-1} = T_L V_3^{\gamma-1}$ and $T_H V_1^{\gamma-1} = T_L V_4^{\gamma-1}$. Dividing gives $V_2/V_1 = V_3/V_4$.

**Step 5 — Take the ratio (1 mark):**
>
> $$\frac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \frac{nR T_H \ln(V_2/V_1)}{nR T_L \ln(V_3/V_4)} = \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$
>
> Thus the ideal-gas temperature ratio **coincides** with the thermodynamic temperature ratio. **They are identical.** $\blacksquare$

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:** $T_L = -10$ °C $= 263.15$ K, $T_H = 25$ °C $= 298.15$ K, $Q_L = 3$ kW (rate of heat removal)

**(i) Rate of heat rejected (3 marks):**

Using the thermodynamic temperature ratio:
$$
\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L \times \frac{T_H}{T_L} = 3 \times \frac{298.15}{263.15} = 3 \times 1.1330 = 3.399 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) Power input (2 marks):**
$$
\dot{W}_{\text{in}} = \dot{Q}_H - \dot{Q}_L = 3.399 - 3 = 0.399 \text{ kW} \approx 399 \text{ W}
$$

**(iii) COP of refrigerator (2 marks):**
$$
\text{COP}_R = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{W}_{\text{in}}} = \frac{3}{0.399} = 7.52
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (KTU):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in $T_L$ and $T_H$ is the most common error — students write $T_L = -10$, $T_H = 25$ and divide. This is **wrong**; you must convert first: $T_L = 263.15$ K, $T_H = 298.15$ K.
> 2. **Unit mix-up:** The problem says "3 kW" — this is a *rate* (kJ/s), so the answer is also in kW, not kJ. Show $\dot{Q}$ notation explicitly.
> 3. **For COP:** Some students compute $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}}$ (heat pump) instead of $\text{COP}_R$ (refrigerator). The formulas are different: $\text{COP}_R = T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ vs. $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = T_H/(T_H - T_L)$.
> 4. **Negative signs and direction:** Make it clear whether heat is *absorbed* or *rejected*. Use $\dot{Q}_L$ for absorbed by the cold space and $\dot{Q}_H$ for rejected to the surroundings.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Thermodynamic Temperature Scale**

- [ ] The **thermodynamic temperature scale** is **defined** (not derived) by $\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L}\big\vert_{\text{rev}}$.
- [ ] The reference fixed point is the **triple point of pure water** at **273.16 K** (exact).
- [ ] The unit is the **kelvin (K)**. The symbol is **K**, not °K.
- [ ] **Carnot's theorem** is the foundation: all reversible engines between the same two reservoirs have the same efficiency.
- [ ] **Reversible efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{rev}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ — depends **only** on reservoir temperatures.
- [ ] **Absolute zero (0 K)** is the temperature at which a reversible engine would convert **100%** of input heat into work ($\eta \to 1$).
- [ ] The thermodynamic scale is **substance-independent** — it does not require any particular working fluid.
- [ ] The thermodynamic scale **coincides with the ideal-gas scale** (proven by a Carnot cycle using an ideal gas as the working fluid).
- [ ] **Always use Kelvin** in temperature ratios. Never mix °C with K.
- [ ] **Temperature *differences*** in K and °C are numerically equal: $\Delta T(K) = \Delta t(^{\circ}C)$.
- [ ] For a Carnot refrigerator: $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$.
- [ ] For a Carnot heat pump: $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$.
- [ ] $1 \text{ K} = \dfrac{1}{273.16}$ of the triple-point temperature of water.
- [ ] Since 2019, the kelvin is defined via the **Boltzmann constant** $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (exact).
- [ ] The kelvin scale is **absolute** — no negative values are physically possible.
- [ ] The Celsius and kelvin scales are related by: $T(K) = t(^{\circ}C) + 273.15$ (note: **273.15**, not 273.16, for the ice point).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Entropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Entropy ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) **Entropy** is a fundamental thermodynamic property that quantifies the degree of molecular disorder or the unavailability of thermal energy for conversion into mechanical work in a system. Mathematically, for a **reversible process**, the change in entropy $S$ of a system is defined by the **Clausius Equality**: $$dS = \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev}$$ For a finite reversible process between states 1 and 2: $$\Delta S = S_2 - S_1 = \int_{1}^{2} \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** > Entropy is an **extensive property** (depends on mass), a **point function** (depends only on state, not path), and is measured in **kJ/K** (SI) or **kcal/K** (MKS). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a freshly organized student's room as a "low-entropy" state — everything is in order, and you can easily find a specific book (high **availability** of information/energy). Now imagine a messy room — the "high-entropy" state. The energy is still in the room (it hasn't disappeared), but it's so chaotically distributed that it's practically **unavailable** to do useful "work" (like quickly locating the book for your exam). > [!NOTE] **The "Spreading Out" Intuition:** > - Heat spontaneously flows from **Hot → Cold** (never the reverse). > - Gases spontaneously expand into a vacuum. > - All these natural processes **increase the total entropy of the universe**. > - Entropy is a measure of **how spread out energy is** at a microscopic level. ### 1.3 Physical Significance - **Microscopic (Statistical) View (Boltzmann):** $S = k_B \ln \Omega$, where $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant ($1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K) and $\Omega$ is the number of microstates. - **Macroscopic (Classical) View (Clausius):** Entropy change calculated via heat transfer and absolute temperature. - **Direction of Time:** The Second Law of Thermodynamics (entropy of the universe always increases for spontaneous processes) gives a physical basis to the **arrow of time**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — How Entropy Works The concept of entropy operates on the following structured logic: - **Step 1: Heat Exchange as Entropy Transfer** When heat $\delta Q$ enters a system reversibly at temperature $T$, the system gains entropy $dS = \delta Q/T$. This is *entropy transfer* with heat. - **Step 2: Entropy Generation by Irreversibility** Real processes are irreversible (friction, unrestrained expansion, mixing, chemical reaction, heat transfer across finite $\Delta T$). These create entropy *internally*: $$dS_{gen} \geq 0$$ Equality holds for reversible processes; inequality for irreversible (real) processes. - **Step 3: The Second Law Statement (Entropy Form)** $$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings} \geq 0$$ This is a **unifying statement** valid for all thermal energy devices (heat engines, refrigerators, heat pumps). - **Step 4: The Clausius Inequality** For any cyclic process (reversible or irreversible): $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$ - Equality → completely reversible cycle (Carnot). - Strict inequality → irreversible cycle. - **Step 5: Increase of Entropy Principle (Isolated System)** For an isolated system (no heat/work/mass transfer): $$\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$$ This is the strongest and most useful practical statement of the Second Law for closed-system analysis. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Formula** | **Application / Condition** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $dS = (\delta Q/T)_{rev}$ | Differential entropy change (reversible) | kJ/K | | 2 | $\Delta S = \int_{1}^{2} (\delta Q/T)_{rev}$ | Finite entropy change (reversible path) | kJ/K | | 3 | $\Delta S = mc \ln(T_2/T_1)$ | Incompressible substance (solid/liquid) | kJ/K | | 4 | $\Delta S = m \left[ c_v \ln(T_2/T_1) + R \ln(v_2/v_1) \right]$ | Ideal gas (in terms of $T$ and $v$) | kJ/K | | 5 | $\Delta S = m \left[ c_p \ln(T_2/T_1) - R \ln(P_2/P_1) \right]$ | Ideal gas (in terms of $T$ and $P$) | kJ/K | | 6 | $\Delta S = m \left[ c_p \ln(v_2/v_1) + c_v \ln(P_2/P_1) \right]$ | Ideal gas (in terms of $v$ and $P$) | kJ/K | | 7 | $\Delta S = Q/T$ | Isothermal reversible heat transfer | kJ/K | | 8 | $\Delta S = mL/T$ | Phase change at constant $T$ and $P$ | kJ/K | | 9 | $T \cdot dS = du + P \cdot dv$ | First Tds equation (Gibbs 1) | — | | 10 | $T \cdot dS = dh - v \cdot dP$ | Second Tds equation (Gibbs 2) | — | | 11 | $\Delta S_{gen} = \Delta S_{sys} - \sum (Q_k/T_k)$ | Entropy generation for closed system | kJ/K | | 12 | $\Delta S_{gen} = \Delta \dot{S}_{gen} \cdot \Delta t$ | For steady-flow devices (rate $\times$ time) | kJ/K | | 13 | $\eta_{th,II} = W_{actual}/W_{rev} = 1 - (T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{gen})/W_{rev}$ | Second-Law efficiency relation | dimensionless | | 14 | $I = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{gen}$ | Irreversibility (Lost Work / Gouy-Stodola) | kJ | | 15 | $dS = c_v (dT/T) + R (dv/v)$ | Ideal gas, differential form | kJ/kg·K | > [!IMPORTANT] **Notation Note for KTU Boards:** > Use $m$ for mass, $R$ for the **specific gas constant** (kJ/kg·K) and **not** the universal gas constant when working with specific properties. Specific entropy $s$ has units **kJ/kg·K**; total entropy $S = m \cdot s$ has units **kJ/K**. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power Plants:** Entropy analysis identifies the largest source of irreversibility (typically the boiler heat transfer and turbine expansion) — guiding design improvements. - **Refrigeration & HVAC:** The Coefficient of Performance (COP) is fundamentally limited by the entropy generation across the evaporator and condenser. - **Nozzle & Diffuser Design:** Minimum entropy production corresponds to maximum pressure recovery. - **Chemical Process Industries:** Exergy (= Availability) destruction equals $T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{gen}$, the "cost" of irreversibility in rupees or joules. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Entropy Change of an Ideal Gas (Most Important — KTU Favourite) **Starting Point:** First Law for a closed system undergoing a reversible process: $$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - \delta W_{rev}$$ For a reversible process, $\delta W_{rev} = P \cdot dv$ and by definition $dS = \delta Q_{rev}/T$, so: $$\delta Q_{rev} = T \cdot dS$$ Substitute into the First Law: $$dU = T \cdot dS - P \cdot dv$$ Rearrange to obtain the **First Tds (Gibbs) Equation**: $$T \cdot dS = dU + P \cdot dv$$ For an **ideal gas**, internal energy depends only on temperature: $$dU = m \cdot c_v \cdot dT$$ And the ideal gas equation gives: $$P = \frac{m \cdot R \cdot T}{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P \cdot dv = \frac{m \cdot R \cdot T}{v} \cdot dv$$ Substituting into Gibbs 1 equation: $$T \cdot dS = m \cdot c_v \cdot dT + \frac{m \cdot R \cdot T}{v} \cdot dv$$ Divide throughout by $T$: $$dS = m \cdot c_v \cdot \frac{dT}{T} + m \cdot R \cdot \frac{dv}{v}$$ Integrate from State 1 to State 2 (assuming $c_v$ approximately constant): $$\int_{1}^{2} dS = m \cdot c_v \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T} + m \cdot R \int_{v_1}^{v_2} \frac{dv}{v}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta S = m \left[ c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) \right]}$$ **Conversion to $T$ and $P$ form:** Using $Pv = RT$ → $v = RT/P$, and substituting: $$\boxed{\Delta S = m \left[ c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) -
