Equating this reversible heat to $T\,ds$ is the Second Law in differential form. - **Why $T\,ds = dh - v\,dp$?** Because enthalpy is the natural variable for constant-pressure flow processes. Swapping coordinates from $(u, v)$ to $(h, p)$ makes this equation the workhorse for open-system cycle analysis (Rankine, Brayton, etc.). - **Why include $C_v$ and $C_p$?** Because $T, v$ and $T, p$ are *measurable* state coordinates. The $(u, v)$ and $(h, p)$ forms use $u$ and $h$, which require either tabulated lookup or further relations to compute. Using heat capacities allows *direct analytical integration* along constant-volume or constant-pressure paths. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Form | Primary Use Case | Valid For | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T\,ds = du + P\,dv$ | Energy–Volume | Closed system analysis | All pure substances | | 2 | $T\,ds = dh - v\,dp$ | Enthalpy–Pressure | Open system / flow processes | All pure substances | | 3 | $T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$ | Temperature–Volume | Constant-volume processes | All pure substances | | 4 | $T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p dp$ | Temperature–Pressure | Constant-pressure processes | All pure substances | | 5 | $dh = C_p\,dT$ (special case of Eq. 2) | Constant pressure | Ideal gas | Ideal gas only | | 6 | $du = C_v\,dT$ (special case of Eq. 1) | Constant volume | Ideal gas | Ideal gas only | | 7 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \dfrac{R}{v}$ | Thermal pressure coeff. | Ideal gas substitution | Ideal gas | | 8 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p = \dfrac{R}{P}$ | Thermal expansion coeff. | Ideal gas substitution | Ideal gas | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Conditions to Memorize for KTU:** > - All four relations apply to **pure, simple compressible substances**. > - They are valid only for **reversible (quasi-equilibrium) processes**. > - For an **ideal gas**, the partial derivative terms simplify dramatically (rows 7 & 8 above), reducing Equations 3 and 4 to $T\,ds = C_v\,dT + R\,dv/v$ and $T\,ds = C_p\,dT - R\,dp/p$ respectively. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The T-ds relations underpin virtually every quantitative thermodynamic calculation in industry: - **Power Plant Design (Rankine, Brayton Cycles):** Engineers use $T\,ds = dh - v\,dp$ to determine entropy change of working fluids (steam, air) across turbines, compressors, pumps, and boilers. - **Refrigeration & HVAC:** $T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T(\partial v/\partial T)_p dp$ is the starting point for analyzing throttling, isentropic compression, and condenser performance. - **Gas Dynamics & Nozzle Design:** For ideal gases, the reduced forms directly yield the isentropic flow relations $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/k}$ and $v_2/v_1 = (P_1/P_2)^{1/k}$. - **Chemical Process Industries:** $C_p$ and $C_v$ lookups in process simulators ultimately trace back to these relations. - **Cryogenics:** Used to compute entropy generation and minimum work of liquefaction. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Equation 1: $T\,ds = du + P\,dv$ **Starting Point:** The First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system: $$dQ = du + dW$$ **Step 1 — Apply the reversibility constraint:** For a quasi-equilibrium process, the boundary work is: $$dW_{rev} = P\,dv$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Second Law (Clausius Equality for Reversible Processes):** $$dQ_{rev} = T\,ds$$ **Step 3 — Substitute both into the First Law:** $$T\,ds = du + P\,dv \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Equation 2: $T\,ds = dh - v\,dp$ **Starting Point — Definition of Enthalpy:** $$h = u + Pv$$ **Step 1 — Differentiate both sides (product rule on $Pv$):** $$dh = du + P\,dv + v\,dp$$ **Step 2 — Isolate the term $(du + P\,dv)$:** $$du + P\,dv = dh - v\,dp$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the result of Step 2 into Equation 1:** $$T\,ds = dh - v\,dp \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Equation 3: $T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T(\partial P/\partial T)_v\,dv$ **Starting Point — Express $u$ as a function of natural variables $T$ and $v$:** $$u = u(T, v)$$ **Step 1 — Apply the total differential (chain rule for two variables):** $$du = \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_v dT + \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T dv$$ **Step 2 — Identify the first partial derivative as $C_v$:** By thermodynamic definition, the specific heat at constant volume is: $$C_v \equiv \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_v$$ **Step 3 — Identify the second partial derivative via a Maxwell-type relation:** The partial derivative $(\partial u / \partial v)_T$ is not directly measurable. We use the *cyclic (triple product) rule* and known Maxwell relations (derived later from Helmholtz free energy) to show: $$\left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T = T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P$$ > **Note on the Triple Product Rule Application:** > The triple product rule states: $\left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T \left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_u \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial u}\right)_v = -1$. Combined with the definition of $C_v$ and the ideal gas substitution chain, this leads to the result above. The Maxwell-relation proof is reserved for the next topic; here it is taken as established. **Step 4 — Substitute everything back into the expression for $du$:** $$du = C_v\,dT + \left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P\right] dv$$ **Step 5 — Plug this $du$ into Equation 1 ($T\,ds = du + P\,dv$):** $$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + \left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P\right] dv + P\,dv$$ **Step 6 — The $-P\,dv$ and $+P\,dv$ terms cancel exactly:** $$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.4 Derivation of Equation 4: $T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T(\partial v/\partial T)_p\,dp$ **Starting Point — Express $h$ as a function of natural variables $T$ and $p$:** $$h = h(T, p)$$ **Step 1 — Apply the total differential:** $$dh = \left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_p dT + \left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial p}\right)_T dp$$ **Step 2 — Identify the first partial derivative as $C_p$:** By thermodynamic definition: $$C_p \equiv \left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_p$$ **Step 3 — Identify the second partial derivative via a Maxwell-type relation:** Using a result analogous to the one in §3.3, applied to enthalpy via the Gibbs free energy framework: $$\left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial p}\right)_T = v - T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the expression for $dh$:** $$dh = C_p\,dT + \left[v - T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p\right] dp$$ **Step 5 — Plug this $dh$ into Equation 2 ($T\,ds = dh - v\,dp$):** $$T\,ds = C_p\,dT + \left[v - T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p\right] dp - v\,dp$$ **Step 6 — The $+v\,dp$ and $-v\,dp$ terms cancel exactly:** $$T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p dp \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Ideal Gas Simplification (High-Yield for KTU) For an **ideal gas**, $Pv = RT$, so: **Step 1 — Compute the thermal pressure coefficient:** $$\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\dfrac{RT}{v}\right)_v = \dfrac{R}{v}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the thermal expansion coefficient:** $$\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\dfrac{RT}{P}\right)_p = \dfrac{R}{P}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute these into Equations 3 and 4:** $$ \begin{aligned} T\,ds &= C_v\,dT + T \cdot \dfrac{R}{v}\,dv \\ &= C_v\,dT + R\,\dfrac{dv}{v} \quad \text{(for an ideal gas)} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} T\,ds &= C_p\,dT - T \cdot \dfrac{R}{P}\,dp \\ &= C_p\,dT - R\,\dfrac{dp}{p} \quad \text{(for an ideal gas)} \end{aligned} $$ These are the **workhorse forms** used in nearly every ideal-gas cycle problem at KTU. ## 3.6 Worked Example: Entropy Change of an Ideal Gas (Consolidation Problem) **Problem:** Air (ideal gas, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K)) is heated from $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 100$ kPa to $T_2 = 600$ K, $P_2 = 500$ kPa. Find $\Delta s$ using the appropriate T-ds relation. **Solution Strategy:** Since we know both initial and final $(T, P)$, use the *T–P form* (Equation 4 with ideal-gas substitution): $$T\,ds = C_p\,dT - R\,\dfrac{dp}{p}$$ **Integration from State 1 to State 2:** $$\int_1^2 T\,ds = \int_1^2 C_p\,dT - \int_1^2 R\,\dfrac{dp}{p}$$ **Step 1 — Left-hand side simplifies to $T \cdot \Delta s$? No, careful!** Actually, for a property *change*, we write: $$\Delta s = s_2 - s_1 = \int_1^2 C_p\,\dfrac{dT}{T} - R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$ (assuming constant $C_p$). **Step 2 — Plug in numerical values:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta s &= 1.005 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{600}{300}\right) - 0.287 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{500}{100}\right) \\ &= 1.005 \times \ln(2) - 0.287 \times \ln(5) \\ &= 1.005 \times 0.6931 - 0.287 \times 1.6094 \\ &= 0.6966 - 0.4619 \\ &= 0.2347 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** Positive $\Delta s$ confirms that heating and pressurizing an ideal gas increases its specific entropy, consistent with the Second Law. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Logical Flow of T-ds Relation Derivation The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the hierarchical derivation structure of the four T-ds equations, showing how each relation builds upon its predecessors. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["First Law of Thermodynamics
dQ equals du plus dW"] --> B["Reversibility Assumption
dW rev equals P dv"] A --> C["Second Law Equality
dQ rev equals T ds"] B --> D["Equation 1
T ds equals du plus P dv"] C --> D D --> E["Enthalpy Definition
h equals u plus P v"] E --> F["Equation 2
T ds equals dh minus v dp"] D --> G["u as function of T and v
Apply Total Differential"] G --> H["Identify Cv equals partial du by partial T at v"] G --> I["Maxwell Type Identity
partial u by partial v at T"] H --> J["Equation 3
T ds equals Cv dT plus T partial P by partial T at v dv"] I --> J F --> K["h as function of T and p
Apply Total Differential"] K --> L["Identify Cp equals partial h by partial T at p"] K --> M["Maxwell Type Identity
partial h by partial p at T"] L --> N["Equation 4
T ds equals Cp dT minus T partial v by partial T at p dp"] M --> N J --> O["Ideal Gas Assumption
Pv equals R T"] N --> O O --> P["Simplified Forms for Ideal Gas
T ds equals Cv dT plus R dv by v
T ds equals Cp dT minus R dp by p"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Application Map of the Four T-ds Relations This diagram shows *which T-ds relation to use* depending on the available process data — a critical KTU exam-time decision. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Given a Thermodynamic Problem
Find ds or Tds"] --> Q1["Are u and v given
or tabulated?"] Q1 -- Yes --> Use1["Use Equation 1
T ds equals du plus P dv"] Q1 -- No --> Q2["Are h and p given
or tabulated?"] Q2 -- Yes --> Use2["Use Equation 2
T ds equals dh minus v dp"] Q2 -- No --> Q3["Is the process at
constant volume?"] Q3 -- Yes --> Use3["Use Equation 3
T ds equals Cv dT plus T partial P by partial T at v dv"] Q3 -- No --> Use4["Use Equation 4
T ds equals Cp dT minus T partial v by partial T at p dp"] Use1 --> Check["Is the substance an ideal gas?"] Use2 --> Check Use3 --> Check Use4 --> Check Check -- Yes --> Ideal["Apply Pv equals R T substitution
Use simplified forms"] Check -- No --> General["Use property tables or charts
to evaluate partial derivatives"] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Role of the T-ds Relations in the Thermodynamic Framework ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Foundation["Foundational Laws"] FL1["First Law of Thermodynamics"] FL2["Second Law of Thermodynamics"] end subgraph Bridge["Bridge Equations"] BR["T ds Relations
Four Gibbs Equations"] end subgraph Applications["Downstream Applications"] A1["Maxwell Relations"] A2["Entropy Change Calculations"] A3["Property Relations
for Real Substances"] A4["Thermodynamic Cycles
Rankine, Brayton, Otto"] end FL1 --> BR FL2 --> BR BR --> A1 BR --> A2 BR --> A3 BR --> A4 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **Q: State the four T-ds relations and identify the natural variables used in each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The four T-ds relations for a pure, simple compressible substance are: 1. $T\,ds = du + P\,dv$ — natural variables: $(u, v)$ 2. $T\,ds = dh - v\,dp$ — natural variables: $(h, p)$ 3. $T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$ — natural variables: $(T, v)$ 4. $T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p dp$ — natural variables: $(T, p)$ > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Award **1 mark** for each correctly stated relation. A full mark is given only if the partial derivative subscripts $(v)$ and $(p)$ are explicitly shown — students frequently lose marks by omitting them. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **Q: Starting from the First Law of Thermodynamics, derive the relation $T\,ds = du + P\,dv$ for a reversible process.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the First Law differential form for a closed system: $$dQ = du + dW$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** For a quasi-equilibrium (reversible) process, the boundary work is $dW_{rev} = P\,dv$. Substituting: $$dQ_{rev} = du + P\,dv$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** From the Second Law (Clausius equality) for a reversible process, $dQ_{rev} = T\,ds$. Substituting: $$T\,ds = du + P\,dv$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model 2024] **Derive the four T-ds relations for a pure, simple compressible substance undergoing a reversible process. Hence, simplify the relations for an ideal gas.** #### Part (a) — Derivation of the First Two T-ds Relations (7 Marks) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Starting Point:** Combine the First Law ($dQ = du + dW$) with the Second Law for a reversible process ($dQ_{rev} = T\,ds$) and the reversible work expression ($dW_{rev} = P\,dv$): $$T\,ds = du + P\,dv \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks) — Conversion to Enthalpy:** Starting from the definition $h = u + Pv$, differentiate: $$dh = du + P\,dv + v\,dp$$ Therefore $du + P\,dv = dh - v\,dp$. Substituting into Equation (1): $$T\,ds = dh - v\,dp \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Step 3 (3 marks) — Mathematical Justification:** Note that these two relations are *exact differentials*, meaning they are valid for any reversible path between two equilibrium states of the substance. The relations are independent of the process path — only the end states matter. This path-independence is what makes $s$ a *thermodynamic property* and the T-ds relations *exact*. #### Part (b) — Derivation of the Last Two T-ds Relations & Ideal Gas Simplification (7 Marks) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Temperature–Volume Form:** Express $u$ as a function of $(T, v)$: $$du = \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_v dT + \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T dv = C_v\,dT + \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T dv$$ Using the identity $\left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T = T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P$ and substituting into Eq. (1): $$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv \quad \text{...(3)}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks) — Temperature–Pressure Form:** Similarly express $h$ as a function of $(T, p)$: $$dh = C_p\,dT + \left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial p}\right)_T dp$$ Using the identity $\left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial p}\right)_T = v - T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p$ and substituting into Eq. (2): $$T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p dp \quad \text{...(4)}$$ **Step 3 (3 marks) — Ideal Gas Simplification:** For an ideal gas, $Pv = RT$: From the ideal gas law: $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \dfrac{R}{v}$ and $\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p = \dfrac{R}{P}$. Substituting into Equations (3) and (4): $$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + R\,\dfrac{dv}{v} \quad \text{...(3-IG)}$$ $$T\,ds = C_p\,dT - R\,\dfrac{dp}{p} \quad \text{...(4-IG)}$$ These reduced forms are the most commonly used T-ds relations in cycle analysis for air-standard and gas-standard cycles. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Alternative Choice] **Using the T-ds relations, derive expressions for the change in entropy of an ideal gas as a function of (i) temperature and pressure, and (ii) temperature and volume. A rigid tank contains 2 kg of air initially at 300 K and 150 kPa. The tank is heated until the temperature reaches 500 K. Determine the change in entropy of the air. Take $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K).** #### Part (a) — Derivation of Entropy Change Expressions (7 Marks) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Expression in $(T, p)$:** Starting from the ideal-gas form of Eq. (4): $$T\,ds = C_p\,dT - R\,\dfrac{dp}{p}$$ Dividing through by $T$ and integrating from state 1 to state 2 (assuming constant $C_p$): $$s_2 - s_1 = C_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad \text{...(I)}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks) — Expression in $(T, v)$:** Starting from the ideal-gas form of Eq. (3): $$T\,ds = C_v\,dT + R\,\dfrac{dv}{v}$$ Integrating from state 1 to state 2: $$s_2 - s_1 = C_v \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_2}{v_1}\right) \quad \text{...(II)}$$ **Step 3 (3 marks) — Verification of Equivalence:** Using the ideal gas law $P_1 v_1 / T_1 = P_2 v_2 / T_2$: $$s_2 - s_1 = C_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2 P_1}{T_1 P_2}\right) = (C_p - R) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_2}{v_1}\right)$$ Since $C_p - R = C_v$ for an ideal gas, this reduces to Equation (II). Both expressions are consistent. ✓ #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem on a Rigid Tank (7 Marks) **Given Data:** - Mass of air: $m = 2$ kg - Initial temperature: $T_1 = 300$ K - Initial pressure: $P_1 = 150$ kPa - Final temperature: $T_2 = 500$ K - $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Identify Process Type:** A *rigid tank* implies **constant volume** ($v_2 = v_1$). Therefore, the $T$–$v$ form of the entropy change (Eq. II) is the most efficient choice because the $R \ln(v_2/v_1)$ term becomes zero. **Step 2 (2 marks) — Apply the T-v Form:** $$s_2 - s_1 = C_v \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_2}{v_1}\right) = C_v \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + 0$$ $$s_2 - s_1 = 0.718 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{500}{300}\right)$$ **Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute Numerical Value:** $$ \begin{aligned} s_2 - s_1 &= 0.718 \times \ln(1.6667) \\ &= 0.718 \times 0.5108 \\ &= 0.3668 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 (1 mark) — Total Entropy Change for the Mass:** $$\Delta S = m \cdot (s_2 - s_1) = 2 \times 0.3668 = 0.7336 \text{ kJ/K}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to multiply by mass:** Many students correctly compute the *specific* entropy change $s_2 - s_1$ but forget to multiply by $m = 2$ kg to get the *total* entropy change $\Delta S$ in kJ/K. **Lose 1 mark.** > 2. **Using $C_p$ instead of $C_v$** for a constant-volume process is a frequent error. For a rigid tank, use $C_v$, not $C_p$. **Lose 2 marks.** > 3. **Mixing units:** Ensure $C_v$ is in **kJ/(kg·K)** and $R$ is in **kJ/(kg·K)**. Mixing kJ with J is a common dimensional error. **Lose 1 mark.** > 4. **Showing the wrong equation:** Always start your solution by clearly stating *which* T-ds relation you are using and *why*. The KTU board rewards clear, logical steps. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — The T-ds Relations** - ✅ **Four canonical equations** to memorize verbatim: 1. $T\,ds = du + P\,dv$ 2. $T\,ds = dh - v\,dp$ 3. $T\,ds = C_v\,dT + T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$ 4. $T\,ds = C_p\,dT - T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_p dp$ - ✅ **Derivation foundation:** Equations 1 and 2 come from First Law + Second Law. Equations 3 and 4 come from total differential expansion plus Maxwell-type identities. - ✅ **Ideal gas simplifications** are essential for KTU cycle problems: - $T\,ds = C_v\,dT + R\,dv/v$ - $T\,ds = C_p\,dT - R\,dp/p$ - ✅ **Choose the right relation** based on available data: - Know $u, v$ → Eq. 1 - Know $h, p$ → Eq. 2 - Know $T, v$ (especially constant-volume) → Eq. 3 - Know $T, p$ (especially constant-pressure) → Eq. 4 - ✅ **Validity conditions:** Pure substance, simple compressible system, **reversible (quasi-equilibrium)** process only. - ✅ **For an ideal gas at constant volume:** $\Delta s = C_v \ln(T_2/T_1)$. - ✅ **For an ideal gas at constant pressure:** $\Delta s = C_p \ln(T_2/T_1)$. - ✅ **Common exam trap:** Forgetting the **subscripts** on partial derivatives — $(\partial P/\partial T)_v$ is *not* the same as $(\partial P/\partial T)_p$. - ✅ **Dimensional check:** $T\,ds$ has units of kJ/kg (specific energy), confirming that $T\,ds$ is a *specific energy quantity*, not a temperature. - ✅ **Path independence:** The T-ds relations are *exact differentials* — the result depends only on the initial and final states, not on the path. - ✅ **Bridge to next topics:** These relations feed directly into the **Maxwell relations** (next module section) and the **entropy change derivations for incompressible substances and ideal gases**. - ✅ **Units snapshot:** $T$ in K, $s$ in kJ/(kg·K), $u, h$ in kJ/kg, $v$ in m³/kg, $P$ in kPa, $C_v, C_p$ in kJ/(kg·K), $R$ in kJ/(kg·K).

topics.title: Isentropic Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Isentropic Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Isentropic Efficiency** is defined as the ratio of the actual work/energy output (or input) of a thermodynamic device to the work/energy that would be obtained if the process between the same initial and final states were carried out **isentropically** (i.e., a reversible adiabatic process, $\Delta s = 0$).

Mathematically, it is the dimensionless metric that quantifies the **deviation of a real (irreversible) adiabatic process** from an **ideal (reversible) adiabatic process** operating between the same inlet conditions and the same outlet pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize):** *Isentropic efficiency is the ratio of the actual energy delivered (or absorbed) by a device to the energy that would be delivered (or absorbed) if the process were isentropic between the same initial state and the same final pressure.*

### 1.2 Why Isentropic Efficiency? — The Real-World Analogy

**Intuitive Analogy — The Bucket with a Hole:**

Imagine you want to carry water from a higher tank (State 1) to a lower tank (State 2) using a bucket.

- **Ideal Process (Isentropic):** A *perfect, frictionless, spill-proof* bucket that delivers **100%** of the potential energy difference.
- **Actual Process:** A *real* bucket that leaks, splashes, and tilts — only delivers, say, **85%** of the available energy.

The ratio $\frac{\text{Energy Actually Delivered}}{\text{Energy Theoretically Available}} = \eta$ is precisely the **Isentropic Efficiency**.

**In real engineering**, irreversibilities such as friction, turbulence, heat leakage, and internal losses mean that the *actual* work/kinetic energy is always less than the *isentropic* ideal. Isentropic efficiency measures this gap numerically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Isentropic efficiency is **not a property of the fluid**; it is a **performance characteristic of the device** (turbine, compressor, nozzle). Two different turbines handling the same gas can have different isentropic efficiencies.

### 1.3 The Three Canonical Devices

In the KTU 2024 Engineering Thermodynamics syllabus (Module 3), isentropic efficiency is formally introduced for **three primary steady-flow devices**:

| Device | Function | Isentropic Efficiency Symbol |
|--------|----------|------------------------------|
| Turbine | Converts fluid enthalpy → shaft work (produces work) | $\eta_{T}$ |
| Compressor | Consumes work → increases fluid enthalpy | $\eta_{C}$ |
| Nozzle | Converts fluid enthalpy → kinetic energy | $\eta_{N}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick (KTU):** "Turbines **T**ake, Compressors **C**onsume" — work direction flips the numerator and denominator!

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Governing Logic — Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown

The derivation of isentropic efficiency rests on **three foundational principles** that every KTU student must internalize:

1. **First Law (Steady Flow Energy Equation):** $q - w = h_2 - h_1$ for an adiabatic process, so $w = h_1 - h_2$.
2. **Isentropic Assumption:** For the ideal case, the entropy remains constant: $s_{2s} = s_1$.
3. **Same End-Pressure Rule:** The actual and isentropic processes share the **same inlet state (1)** and the **same outlet pressure ($P_2$)**, but **different outlet entropies** ($s_{2a} > s_{2s}$ due to irreversibilities).

**The Logical Flow (Bullet Reasoning):**

- Step 1 → Real processes are **adiabatic but irreversible**, generating entropy internally.
- Step 2 → For a given $P_1$, $T_1$, and $P_2$, the **isentropic outlet state (2s)** lies on a vertical line on the $T\text{-}s$ diagram (constant $s$).
- Step 3 → The **actual outlet state (2a)** lies to the **right** of (2s) on the same $P_2$ isobar because $s_{2a} > s_{2s}$ (Second Law).
- Step 4 → Work or kinetic energy changes are computed from enthalpy differences.
- Step 5 → Efficiency is defined as the ratio of actual energy change to the **maximum possible** (isentropic) energy change.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas — Master These Before Attempting Any KTU Numerical.**

| # | Device | Isentropic Efficiency Formula | Alternate Form | Typical Range |
|---|--------|-------------------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Turbine** ($\eta_T$) | $\eta_{T} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_{2a}}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$ | $\eta_{T} = \dfrac{W_{a}}{W_{s}}$ | 70% – 90% |
| 2 | **Compressor** ($\eta_C$) | $\eta_{C} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$ | $\eta_{C} = \dfrac{W_{s}}{W_{a}}$ | 75% – 85% |
| 3 | **Nozzle** ($\eta_N$) — enthalpy form | $\eta_{N} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_{2a}}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$ | $\eta_{N} = \dfrac{\Delta h_{a}}{\Delta h_{s}}$ | 90% – 98% |
| 4 | **Nozzle** ($\eta_N$) — KE form | $\eta_{N} = \dfrac{V_{2a}^{\,2}}{V_{2s}^{\,2}}$ | $\eta_{N} = \dfrac{KE_{a}}{KE_{s}}$ | 90% – 98% |
| 5 | **Diffuser** ($\eta_D$) — enthalpy form | $\eta_{D} = \dfrac{h_{2a} - h_1}{h_{2s} - h_1}$ | — | 85% – 95% |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA-NUMBERING PITFALL (KTU Exam Trap!):**
> - For a **turbine**, the actual work is **less** than isentropic work, so the *numerator* is the smaller value: $(h_1 - h_{2a}) < (h_1 - h_{2s})$.
> - For a **compressor**, the actual work is **greater** than isentropic work, so the *denominator* is the larger value: $(h_{2a} - h_1) > (h_{2s} - h_1)$.
> - Students often invert compressor efficiency — **Memorize the numerator/denominator position carefully!**

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Isentropic efficiency is **the single most important performance metric** in the design and evaluation of:

- **Gas Turbine Power Plants** — Jet engines, power generation turbines (Brayton cycle).
- **Steam Power Plants** — High-pressure, intermediate, and low-pressure turbine stages (Rankine cycle).
- **Refrigeration & HVAC** — Centrifugal and reciprocating compressors (Vapor-compression cycle).
- **Rocket Propulsion** — Convergent-divergent (de Laval) nozzles, where nozzle efficiency directly determines thrust.
- **Aircraft & Wind Tunnels** — Supersonic diffusers.

> [!NOTE]
> In all these systems, **higher isentropic efficiency = less fuel consumption, less waste heat, lower operating cost**. It is the designer's figure of merit.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Turbine Isentropic Efficiency ($\eta_T$)

**Starting Point — Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for an adiabatic turbine:**

$$
w = h_1 - h_2
$$

**Step 1:** For the **actual (irreversible adiabatic)** process, the actual work output is:

$$
w_a = h_1 - h_{2a}
$$

**Step 2:** For the **ideal (isentropic)** process between the same inlet state (1) and same exit pressure ($P_2$), with $s_{2s} = s_1$:

$$
w_s = h_1 - h_{2s}
$$

**Step 3:** Define the turbine isentropic efficiency as the ratio of actual work to isentropic work:

$$
\eta_{T} \equiv \frac{w_a}{w_s}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the enthalpy expressions:

$$
\eta_{T} = \frac{h_1 - h_{2a}}{h_1 - h_{2s}}
$$

**Step 5 (Logical verification):** Because of irreversibilities, $s_{2a} > s_{2s}$. On the $T\text{-}s$ diagram, point 2a lies to the right of point 2s at the same $P_2$. Therefore $h_{2a} > h_{2s}$, which makes the **numerator smaller** than the denominator. Hence $\eta_T < 1$ — physically consistent. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Compressor Isentropic Efficiency ($\eta_C$)

**Starting Point — SFEE for an adiabatic compressor:**

$$
w_{in} = h_2 - h_1
$$

**Step 1:** Actual work input (greater, due to irreversibilities):

$$
w_{a} = h_{2a} - h_1
$$

**Step 2:** Isentropic (minimum) work input:

$$
w_{s} = h_{2s} - h_1
$$

**Step 3:** Define compressor isentropic efficiency as the ratio of *ideal* (minimum) work to *actual* work:

$$
\eta_{C} \equiv \frac{w_s}{w_a}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$
\eta_{C} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}
$$

**Step 5 (Logical verification):** Irreversibilities increase entropy: $s_{2a} > s_{2s}$, so $h_{2a} > h_{2s}$. Thus the **denominator is larger** than the numerator, giving $\eta_C < 1$. The compressor must consume *more* work than the ideal minimum. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Nozzle Isentropic Efficiency ($\eta_N$)

**Starting Point — SFEE for an adiabatic nozzle (no work, no heat):**

$$
h_1 + \frac{V_1^{\,2}}{2} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^{\,2}}{2}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2} = 2(h_1 - h_2)
$$

**Step 1:** For a nozzle, $V_1 \ll V_2$ (typically $V_1$ is negligible for a high-subsonic inlet). Hence:

$$
V_2^{\,2} \approx 2(h_1 - h_2)
$$

**Step 2:** Actual exit velocity (real process):

$$
V_{2a}^{\,2} = 2(h_1 - h_{2a})
$$

**Step 3:** Isentropic exit velocity (ideal):

$$
V_{2s}^{\,2} = 2(h_1 - h_{2s})
$$

**Step 4:** Define nozzle isentropic efficiency (using KE form):

$$
\eta_{N} \equiv \frac{V_{2a}^{\,2}}{V_{2s}^{\,2}}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting:

$$
\eta_{N} = \frac{2(h_1 - h_{2a})}{2(h_1 - h_{2s})} = \frac{h_1 - h_{2a}}{h_1 - h_{2s}}
$$

**Step 6 (Logical verification):** Both KE and enthalpy forms give identical efficiency values. For a nozzle, $h_{2a} > h_{2s}$, so the numerator is smaller, yielding $\eta_N < 1$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU 3-Mark Style Setup)

**Problem Statement:** Air enters an adiabatic turbine at $P_1 = 1.0\ \text{MPa}$, $T_1 = 500\ \text{K}$ and exits at $P_2 = 0.1\ \text{MPa}$. The turbine isentropic efficiency is $\eta_T = 0.85$. For air, take $c_p = 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$.

**Solution Strategy:**

Begin by computing the isentropic exit temperature for an ideal gas using:

$$
\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}
$$

**Step-by-Step Computation:**

$$
\frac{T_{2s}}{500} = \left(\frac{0.1}{1.0}\right)^{\frac{0.4}{1.4}} = (0.1)^{0.2857}
$$

Evaluating the exponent:

$$
(0.1)^{0.2857} = 0.5179
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{2s} = 500 \times 0.5179 = 258.95\ \text{K}
$$

Now compute the actual exit temperature using the definition of turbine efficiency:

$$
\eta_{T} = \frac{c_p(T_1 - T_{2a})}{c_p(T_1 - T_{2s})} = \frac{T_1 - T_{2a}}{T_1 - T_{2s}}
$$

Solving for $T_{2a}$:

$$
T_1 - T_{2a} = \eta_{T}(T_1 - T_{2s})
$$

$$
T_{2a} = T_1 - \eta_{T}(T_1 - T_{2s})
$$

Substitute values:

$$
T_{2a} = 500 - 0.85 \times (500 - 258.95)
$$

$$
T_{2a} = 500 - 0.85 \times 241.05 = 500 - 204.89 = 295.11\ \text{K}
$$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Isentropic exit temperature | $T_{2s}$ | **258.95 K** |
| Actual exit temperature | $T_{2a}$ | **295.11 K** |
| Specific work (isentropic) | $w_s$ | **241.05 kJ/kg** |
| Specific work (actual) | $w_a$ | **204.89 kJ/kg** |

---

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Isentropic vs Actual Process in a Turbine

The following Mermaid **block-level functional architecture** illustrates how the inlet state (1) branches into two parallel paths — the isentropic ideal path and the real irreversible path — converging at the same outlet pressure ($P_2$) but yielding different outlet states (2s vs 2a).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INLET STATE 1: P1, T1, h1, s1"] --> B["CONSTANT PRESSURE EXIT TARGET: P2"]
    A --> C["ISENTROPIC PATH: ds = 0, Reversible"]
    A --> D["ACTUAL PATH: ds greater than 0, Irreversible"]
    C --> E["IDEAL EXIT STATE 2s: h2s, s1 unchanged"]
    D --> F["ACTUAL EXIT STATE 2a: h2a greater than h2s, s2a greater than s1"]
    E --> G["ISENTROPIC WORK: ws = h1 minus h2s"]
    F --> H["ACTUAL WORK: wa = h1 minus h2a"]
    G --> I["EFFICIENCY CALC: eta_T = wa divided by ws"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["OUTPUT: eta_T less than 1 indicates irreversibility"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Comparison Block — Three Devices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TURBINE ["TURBINE Block"]
        T1["Fluid Inlet: high P, high h"] --> T2["h drops across device"]
        T2 --> T3["Work Output wa = h1 minus h2a"]
        T3 --> T4["eta_T = wa divided by ws"]
    end
    subgraph COMPRESSOR ["COMPRESSOR Block"]
        C1["Fluid Inlet: low P, low h"] --> C2["h rises across device"]
        C2 --> C3["Work Input wa = h2a minus h1"]
        C3 --> C4["eta_C = ws divided by wa"]
    end
    subgraph NOZZLE ["NOZZLE Block"]
        N1["Fluid Inlet: high P, low V"] --> N2["h drops, V rises"]
        N2 --> N3["KE rise actual: V2a squared divided by 2"]
        N3 --> N4["eta_N = V2a squared divided by V2s squared"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Indicator Diagram on T-s Chart (Schematic Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Point 1: P1, T1, s1 (inlet)"] --> P2["Vertical drop on T-s chart: constant s = s1"]
    P2 --> P3["Point 2s: P2, T2s, s1 (isentropic exit)"]
    P1 --> P4["Curved actual path: entropy increases"]
    P4 --> P5["Point 2a: P2, T2a, s2a (actual exit)"]
    P5 --> P6["s2a is GREATER than s1 (irreversibility proof)"]
    P3 --> P7["Area under 1 to 2s = Isentropic Work"]
    P5 --> P8["Area under 1 to 2a = Actual Work"]
    P8 --> P9["eta_T = Area actual divided by Area isentropic"]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

Define isentropic efficiency of a turbine. Why is the actual work output always less than the isentropic work output for an adiabatic turbine?

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Marking Key Pattern):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** The isentropic efficiency of a turbine is defined as the ratio of the actual work output to the work output that would be obtained if the expansion process from the same initial state to the same exit pressure were carried out isentropically (reversibly and adiabatically).

$$
\eta_{T} = \frac{w_{a}}{w_{s}} = \frac{h_1 - h_{2a}}{h_1 - h_{2s}}
$$

**Reasoning (1.5 Marks):** In a real adiabatic turbine, the process is irreversible due to friction, turbulence, and internal fluid losses. These irreversibilities cause an entropy increase, $s_{2a} > s_{2s}$. Since the actual exit state (2a) lies to the right of the isentropic exit state (2s) on the $T\text{-}s$ diagram at the same exit pressure $P_2$, the actual exit enthalpy is higher: $h_{2a} > h_{2s}$. Consequently, the actual enthalpy drop $h_1 - h_{2a}$ is smaller than the isentropic enthalpy drop $h_1 - h_{2s}$, making $w_a < w_s$ and ensuring $\eta_T < 1$.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

Explain why the isentropic efficiency formula for a compressor has the isentropic work in the **numerator** and the actual work in the **denominator**, while the reverse is true for a turbine.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Marking Key Pattern):**

**Core Concept (1.5 Marks):** The fundamental purpose of efficiency is to express the *useful, achieved* quantity as a fraction of the *ideal, maximum (or minimum)* quantity.

**Turbine Logic (0.75 Marks):** In a turbine, the *maximum possible* work is the isentropic work $w_s$, and the *actual* (achieved) work is $w_a < w_s$. Hence efficiency is $\eta_T = w_a / w_s$.

**Compressor Logic (0.75 Marks):** In a compressor, the *minimum required* work is the isentropic work $w_s$, and the *actual* (consumed) work is $w_a > w_s$. Hence efficiency is $\eta_C = w_s / w_a$.

In both cases, the **ideal quantity** forms the *denominator* of the fraction (the reference 100%), ensuring $\eta \leq 1$.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question typically contains **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students must answer **either the full Question A OR the full Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Starting from the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for an adiabatic turbine, derive the expression for the isentropic efficiency of a turbine. State clearly why the actual outlet enthalpy $h_{2a}$ is greater than the isentropic outlet enthalpy $h_{2s}$ at the same exit pressure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Air enters an adiabatic gas turbine at $P_1 = 1.2\ \text{MPa}$, $T_1 = 800\ \text{K}$, and expands to $P_2 = 0.1\ \text{MPa}$. The turbine isentropic efficiency is $\eta_T = 0.88$. Calculate:
  - (i) The isentropic exit temperature $T_{2s}$ and the actual exit temperature $T_{2a}$.
  - (ii) The actual specific work output and the isentropic specific work output.

Take $c_p = 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$ and $\gamma = 1.4$ for air. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — SFEE for Adiabatic Turbine (2 Marks):**

For a steady-flow adiabatic turbine with negligible kinetic and potential energy changes:

$$
w = h_1 - h_2
$$

where $w$ is the specific work output.

**Step 2 — Define the Two Work Quantities (2 Marks):**

For the *actual* irreversible expansion:

$$
w_a = h_1 - h_{2a}
$$

For the *isentropic* (ideal, reversible adiabatic) expansion between the same inlet state and the same exit pressure $P_2$, with $s_{2s} = s_1$:

$$
w_s = h_1 - h_{2s}
$$

**Step 3 — Define Isentropic Efficiency (2 Marks):**

$$
\eta_{T} = \frac{w_a}{w_s} = \frac{h_1 - h_{2a}}{h_1 - h_{2s}}
$$

**Step 4 — Justification of $h_{2a} > h_{2s}$ (1 Mark):**

Real expansion processes are irreversible, generating entropy internally. Hence $s_{2a} > s_{2s}$. On the $T\text{-}s$ diagram at constant $P_2$, an entropy increase corresponds to a temperature increase, and therefore an enthalpy increase: $h_{2a} > h_{2s}$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Part (i) — Find $T_{2s}$ and $T_{2a}$ (4 Marks):**

For isentropic expansion of an ideal gas:

$$
\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}
$$

Compute the pressure ratio exponent:

$$
\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{1.4 - 1}{1.4} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857
$$

Compute the pressure ratio:

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{0.1}{1.2} = 0.08333
$$

Substitute:

$$
T_{2s} = 800 \times (0.08333)^{0.2857} = 800 \times 0.4476 = 358.08\ \text{K}
$$

Now solve for the actual exit temperature using the efficiency formula:

$$
\eta_T = \frac{T_1 - T_{2a}}{T_1 - T_{2s}}
$$

Rearrange:

$$
T_{2a} = T_1 - \eta_T (T_1 - T_{2s})
$$

$$
T_{2a} = 800 - 0.88 \times (800 - 358.08) = 800 - 0.88 \times 441.92
$$

$$
T_{2a} = 800 - 388.89 = 411.11\ \text{K}
$$

**Part (ii) — Find Specific Works (3 Marks):**

Isentropic specific work:

$$
w_s = c_p (T_1 - T_{2s}) = 1.005 \times 441.92 = 444.07\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Actual specific work:

$$
w_a = c_p (T_1 - T_{2a}) = 1.005 \times 388.89 = 390.83\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Verification using efficiency:**

$$
\eta_T = \frac{w_a}{w_s} = \frac{390.83}{444.07} = 0.8801 \approx 0.88\ \checkmark
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the isentropic efficiency of a compressor, and explain why the actual work input is greater than the isentropic work input. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal air compressor handles $2.5\ \text{kg/s}$ of air. The inlet conditions are $P_1 = 100\ \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 300\ \text{K}$. The exit pressure is $P_2 = 500\ \text{kPa}$, and the compressor isentropic efficiency is $\eta_C = 0.80$. Determine:
  - (i) The isentropic exit temperature $T_{2s}$ and the actual exit temperature $T_{2a}$.
  - (ii) The isentropic power input and the actual power input in kW.

Take $c_p = 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, and $R = 0.287\ \text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — SFEE for Adiabatic Compressor (2 Marks):**

$$
w_{in} = h_2 - h_1
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Two Work Quantities (2 Marks):**

Actual work (real, irreversible):

$$
w_a = h_{2a} - h_1
$$

Isentropic work (minimum required):

$$
w_s = h_{2s} - h_1 \quad \text{with} \quad s_{2s} = s_1
$$

**Step 3 — Define Isentropic Efficiency (2 Marks):**

$$
\eta_{C} = \frac{w_s}{w_a} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Justification of $w_a > w_s$ (1 Mark):**

Real compression involves friction and turbulence, increasing entropy. Therefore $h_{2a} > h_{2s}$ at the same exit pressure, requiring more work input than the ideal reversible case.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Part (i) — Find $T_{2s}$ and $T_{2a}$ (4 Marks):**

$$
\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = (5)^{0.2857} = 1.5838
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{2s} = 300 \times 1.5838 = 475.14\ \text{K}
$$

Solve for $T_{2a}$ using the compressor efficiency relation:

$$
\eta_C = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_{2a} - T_1}
$$

Rearrange:

$$
T_{2a} - T_1 = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_C}
$$

$$
T_{2a} = 300 + \frac{475.14 - 300}{0.80} = 300 + \frac{175.14}{0.80} = 300 + 218.93
$$

$$
T_{2a} = 518.93\ \text{K}
$$

**Part (ii) — Power Inputs (3 Marks):**

Isentropic specific work:

$$
w_s = c_p (T_{2s} - T_1) = 1.005 \times 175.14 = 176.02\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Actual specific work:

$$
w_a = c_p (T_{2a} - T_1) = 1.005 \times 218.93 = 220.02\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Isentropic power input:

$$
\dot{W}_s = \dot{m} \cdot w_s = 2.5 \times 176.02 = 440.05\ \text{kW}
$$

Actual power input:

$$
\dot{W}_a = \dot{m} \cdot w_a = 2.5 \times 220.02 = 550.05\ \text{kW}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\eta_C = \frac{440.05}{550.05} = 0.8000 \approx 0.80\ \checkmark
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Real Examiner Feedback):**
>
> 1. **Inverted Compressor Formula:** Writing $\eta_C = (h_{2a} - h_1)/(h_{2s} - h_1)$ instead of the correct form. This gives $\eta_C > 1$, which is *physically impossible* and immediately flagged by examiners. **Penalty: -2 to -3 marks.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Nozzle KE form with Enthalpy form:** Writing $\eta_N = V_{2s}^2 / V_{2a}^2$ (inverted). Remember: **actual KE is less than isentropic KE**, so actual goes in the numerator.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the constant-pressure rule:** Students sometimes compute $T_{2s}$ and $T_{2a}$ at *different* exit pressures. Both must terminate at the **same $P_2$**.
>
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $P$ in Pa with $c_p$ in kJ/kg·K without converting. Always keep $P$ in **kPa** when $c_p$ is in **kJ/kg·K**.
>
> 5. **Skipping the "Justification of $h_{2a} > h_{2s}$" statement** in derivation sub-parts. KTU examiners specifically award **1 mark** for this conceptual link.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Isentropic efficiency = (actual energy change) / (isentropic energy change) for the same inlet state and same exit pressure.
- **Turbine:** $\eta_T = (h_1 - h_{2a}) / (h_1 - h_{2s})$ — **actual** in numerator (work-producing device).
- **Compressor:** $\eta_C = (h_{2s} - h_1) / (h_{2a} - h_1)$ — **isentropic** in numerator (work-consuming device, minimum work is the ideal).
- **Nozzle:** $\eta_N = (h_1 - h_{2a}) / (h_1 - h_{2s}) = V_{2a}^2 / V_{2s}^2$ — KE form and enthalpy form are equivalent.
- **Entropy Rule:** $s_{2a} > s_{2s} = s_1$ in all real processes (Second Law).
- **Enthalpy Rule:** $h_{2a} > h_{2s}$ for turbines and nozzles; the reverse order holds at the *inlet* for compressors.
- **Always** share the **same inlet (1)** and **same exit pressure ($P_2$)** between actual and isentropic processes.
- **Numerical Recipe:** (1) Compute $T_{2s}$ via $T_{2s}/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$, (2) Apply efficiency to back-solve $T_{2a}$, (3) Multiply $\Delta T$ by $c_p$ for specific work.
- **Always verify** the final efficiency value lies in $(0, 1)$ — if it doesn't, you have inverted the formula.
- **For KTU 2024 exams**, expect numerical questions combining turbine + compressor efficiency, or nozzle efficiency with KE exit velocity computation.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **EXERGY (AVAILABILITY) — KTU Official Definition**
> **Exergy** (also termed **Availability** or **Available Energy**) of a system is defined as the **maximum useful work** that can be extracted as the system is brought reversibly from its initial state to the **dead state** (thermodynamic equilibrium with the environment).

The **Dead State** is the unique state where the system is in complete thermal, mechanical, and chemical equilibrium with the environment. At this state, no further useful work can be extracted.

**Reference Environment Conditions (Standard):**
- **T₀ = 298.15 K (25 °C)**
- **p₀ = 101.325 kPa (1 atm)**
- g₀ = 9.81 m/s² (gravitational)
- Composition: standard atmospheric composition (N₂, O₂, CO₂, H₂O, etc.)

> [!NOTE] **KTU Terminology Mapping**
> The terms *Exergy*, *Availability*, and *Available Energy* are **synonymous** in the KTU syllabus. However, distinguish carefully between:
> - **Exergy** = Total maximum useful work extractable
> - **Irreversibility (I)** = Exergy *destroyed* (lost) during a real process

## 1.2 The "Why" — Why Do We Need Exergy?

The **First Law** (Energy Conservation) tells us energy is *never lost*, only transformed. The **Second Law** reveals the *true* limit: only a fraction of energy is *available* to do work. The rest becomes **unavailable energy** (anergy) and degrades as low-grade heat.

> [!NOTE] **Energy Decomposition**
> $$\text{Total Energy} = \text{Exergy (Useful)} + \text{Anergy (Unavailable)}$$

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Water Tank

Imagine a **water tank on a hill**:

| Concept | Analogy | Thermodynamic Meaning |
|---------|---------|----------------------|
| **Water at top of hill** | Reservoir at high temperature/pressure | System away from dead state |
| **Exergy** | Height of water above ground | Useful work potential |
| **Anergy** | Water that has already reached the ground | Energy dumped to environment |
| **Dead state** | Water in a flat pool at sea level | Equilibrium with environment |
| **Irreversibility** | Water spilled on the rocks (lost height) | Exergy destroyed by friction/entropy |

A hot gas at 1000 K has **huge exergy** (lots of "height" above T₀ = 298 K). The same energy at 305 K is mostly **anergy** — the gas barely has any height left to fall from.

## 1.4 Categories of Exergy

1. **Physical Exergy** — due to deviation of T and p from environment.
2. **Kinetic Exergy** — due to bulk motion: $\frac{V^2}{2}$
3. **Potential Exergy** — due to elevation: $gz$
4. **Chemical Exergy** — due to compositional difference from environment.
5. **Internal Exergy** — the physical/chemical part itself.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Exergy

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield Classification**
> Every exergy term in the KTU 2024 syllabus falls under one of these four categories. Memorize the symbols — they appear in every Part B question.

| Type | Symbol | Source |
|------|--------|--------|
| Non-Flow (Closed System) Exergy | **Φ** (Phi) | Stationary systems |
| Flow (Open System) Exergy | **Ψ** (Psi) | Streams entering/leaving devices |
| Kinetic Exergy | $\frac{V^2}{2}$ | Bulk fluid motion |
| Potential Exergy | $gz$ | Elevation above datum |

## 2.2 Derivation Logic (Operational Steps)

For a **closed system** undergoing a reversible process from state 1 to dead state 0:

**Step 1 — Apply First Law to a reversible process (no entropy generation):**
$$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - \delta W_{max}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the only heat interaction occurs with environment at T₀:**
$$\delta Q_{rev} = T_0 \, dS$$

**Step 3 — For a quasi-static process, work includes boundary work:**
$$\delta W_{max} = -p_0 \, dV \quad \text{(at constant external pressure } p_0\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the above to yield maximum useful work** (developed in SECTION 3).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **EXAM-READY FORMULA TABLE — All KTU 2024 Module 3 Numerical Substitutions**

| # | Formula | Name / Application | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Phi = (U - U_0) + p_0(V - V_0) - T_0(S - S_0)$ | **Non-flow exergy (closed system)** | kJ |
| 2 | $\Phi = (u - u_0) + p_0(v - v_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$ | Non-flow exergy (specific, per kg) | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$ | **Flow exergy (open system)** | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $\psi_{flow} = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$ | Flow exergy, neglecting KE & PE | kJ/kg |
| 5 | $\phi = c_v(T - T_0) - T_0\left[c_v \ln\frac{T}{T_0} + R \ln\frac{v}{v_0}\right]$ | Non-flow exergy of **ideal gas** | kJ/kg |
| 6 | $\psi = c_p(T - T_0) - T_0\left[c_p \ln\frac{T}{T_0} - R \ln\frac{p}{p_0}\right]$ | Flow exergy of **ideal gas** | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $I = T_0 \, \Delta s_{gen,total}$ | **Irreversibility = Exergy destroyed** | kJ |
| 8 | $\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{Exergy recovered}}{\text{Exergy supplied}} = 1 - \frac{I}{\text{Exergy supplied}}$ | **Second-Law Efficiency** | — |
| 9 | $\Phi_Q = \left(1 - \frac{T_0}{T}\right) Q$ | **Exergy transfer by heat** (Carnot factor) | kJ |
| 10 | $\Delta\Phi = W_{useful} - I$ | Closed-system exergy balance | kJ |
| 11 | $\sum \dot{m}_e \psi_e - \sum \dot{m}_i \psi_i = \dot{W}_{useful} - \dot{I}$ | Open-system (steady) exergy balance | kW |
| 12 | $\dot{E}_{destroyed} = T_0 \dot{S}_{gen}$ | Rate of exergy destruction | kW |

> [!NOTE] **Critical Sign Convention (KTU Pattern)**
> - $\Phi$ is **positive** when system is **away** from dead state.
> - $\Phi = 0$ at the dead state.
> - $\Delta\Phi$ is **negative** during a natural (irreversible) process → *Decrease of Exergy Principle*.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Exergy |
|-------|----------------------|
| Power Plants | Pinpoint where exergy is destroyed (boiler, turbine, condenser) |
| Refrigeration | Identify minimum work input (theoretical COP limit) |
| HVAC | Optimize compressor performance using η_II |
| Chemical Process | Locate reactor hot spots with exergy loss |
| Cryogenics | Quantify liquefaction work minimums (Linde, Claude cycles) |
| IC Engines | Compare real cycles to ideal Otto/Diesel via second-law efficiency |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Non-Flow Exergy of a Closed System

**Starting premise:** Bring a closed system reversibly from state **1** to dead state **0**, extracting maximum useful work $W_{max}$ (excluding work done on/pushing back the atmosphere).

**Step 1 — First Law for a closed system (reversible, heat only to environment at T₀):**

$$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - \delta W_{rev,total}$$

$$dU = T_0 \, dS - \delta W_{rev,total}$$

**Step 2 — The total reversible work includes two parts:**
- **Useful work** $\delta W_{useful}$ (what we want to extract)
- **Work to push back the atmosphere** $p_0 \, dV$ (this is *not* useful, since it just expands against T₀, p₀)

$$\delta W_{rev,total} = \delta W_{useful} + p_0 \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into First Law equation:**

$$dU = T_0 \, dS - \delta W_{useful} - p_0 \, dV$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the useful work differential:**

$$\delta W_{useful} = -dU + T_0 \, dS - p_0 \, dV$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from state 1 to dead state 0 (reversible path):**

$$W_{useful} = -\int_1^0 dU + T_0 \int_1^0 dS - p_0 \int_1^0 dV$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate each integral explicitly:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\int_1^0 dU &= -(U_0 - U_1) = U_1 - U_0 \\
T_0 \int_1^0 dS &= T_0 (S_0 - S_1) \\
-p_0 \int_1^0 dV &= -p_0 (V_0 - V_1) = p_0(V_1 - V_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Combine and define as non-flow exergy Φ:**

$$W_{max} = (U_1 - U_0) + p_0(V_1 - V_0) - T_0(S_1 - S_0)$$

$$\boxed{\Phi = (U - U_0) + p_0(V - V_0) - T_0(S - S_0)}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to per-unit-mass form (divide by mass m):**

$$\boxed{\phi = (u - u_0) + p_0(v - v_0) - T_0(s - s_0)}$$

This is **Formula #2** in the cheat sheet and is the most-tested KTU expression for closed systems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Flow Exergy of an Open System (Steady-Flow Device)

**Starting premise:** For a control volume in **steady flow**, the First Law per unit mass is:

$$q - w_s = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$$

**Step 1 — Apply Second Law (reversible operation, no entropy generation):**

$$ds = \frac{\delta q_{rev}}{T_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta q_{rev} = T_0 \, ds$$

**Step 2 — Maximum shaft work is therefore:**

$$w_{s,max} = -\Delta h - \frac{\Delta V^2}{2} - g \Delta z + T_0 \Delta s$$

**Step 3 — Reverse the sign (work output is positive when extracted from system):**

$$w_{s,max} = -\Delta h + T_0 \Delta s - \frac{\Delta V^2}{2} - g \Delta z$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate from state (T, p, V, z) to dead state (T₀, p₀, 0, 0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\Delta h &= -(h - h_0) \\
T_0 \Delta s &= T_0(s - s_0) \\
-\frac{\Delta V^2}{2} &= 0 - \frac{V^2}{2} = -\frac{V^2}{2} \\
-g \Delta z &= -(gz - 0) = -gz
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine all four terms to get the flow exergy:**

$$\boxed{\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz}$$

This is **Formula #3** in the cheat sheet — the single most-tested open-system exergy expression in KTU 2024 ESE.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Exergy of an Ideal Gas (Non-Flow)

Starting from:
$$\phi = (u - u_0) + p_0(v - v_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$$

**Step 1 — Substitute ideal-gas relations:**
- Internal energy: $u - u_0 = c_v(T - T_0)$
- Entropy: $s - s_0 = c_v \ln\frac{T}{T_0} + R \ln\frac{v}{v_0}$

**Step 2 — Insert into exergy equation:**

$$\phi = c_v(T - T_0) + p_0(v - v_0) - T_0\left[c_v \ln\frac{T}{T_0} + R \ln\frac{v}{v_0}\right]$$

This is **Formula #5**. For isothermal processes (T = T₀), the first and log terms vanish, leaving:
$$\phi_{isothermal} = p_0(v - v_0) - T_0 R \ln\frac{v}{v_0}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Exergy of an Ideal Gas (Flow Exergy)

From Formula #3 with KE/PE neglected:
$$\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$$

**Step 1 — Ideal-gas relations:**
- Enthalpy: $h - h_0 = c_p(T - T_0)$
- Entropy: $s - s_0 = c_p \ln\frac{T}{T_0} - R \ln\frac{p}{p_0}$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\boxed{\psi = c_p(T - T_0) - T_0\left[c_p \ln\frac{T}{T_0} - R \ln\frac{p}{p_0}\right]}$$

This is **Formula #6** — most commonly tested with air-standard cycles (Otto, Diesel, Brayton).

---

## 3.5 Derivation V — Irreversibility from Entropy Generation

For any real (irreversible) process, the **Clausius Inequality** states:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$$

This leads to the **entropy balance** for a closed system:

$$S_2 - S_1 = \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} + S_{gen}$$

Rearranging for entropy generation:
$$S_{gen} = (S_2 - S_1) - \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T_b}$$

**Step 1 — Multiply both sides by T₀ (environment temperature, a constant):**

$$T_0 S_{gen} = T_0(S_2 - S_1) - T_0 \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T_b}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the exergy balance identity. After algebraic rearrangement (using $W_{useful} = \Phi_1 - \Phi_2 - T_0 S_{gen}$), we arrive at:**

$$\boxed{I = T_0 \, S_{gen}}$$

This is **Formula #7** — the **Gouy-Stodola Theorem**. It is the foundation of every exergy destruction calculation in KTU 2024.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Gouy-Stodola Theorem (KTU 2024 High-Yield)**
> The lost work (irreversibility) equals the environment temperature times the total entropy generation of the universe for the process.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE] **Standard 14-Mark Numerical Pattern (What KTU Examiners Expect)**

| Step | Action | Tool/Formula |
|------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | Identify system type (closed/open) | Choose Φ or ψ |
| 2 | List environment conditions | T₀, p₀ given |
| 3 | Compute Δu, Δh, Δs for working fluid | Ideal-gas or table lookup |
| 4 | Substitute into Formula #2 or #3 | Numerical plug-in |
| 5 | If reversible work → Φ = W_max directly | Definition |
| 6 | If actual work given → $I = W_{rev} - W_{actual}$ | Gouy-Stodola |
| 7 | Compute η_II using Formula #8 | Second-law efficiency |
| 8 | State units explicitly (kJ or kJ/kg) | Required in answer |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Define exergy. How is it different from energy?**

**Model Answer:**

Exergy is the maximum useful work that can be extracted from a system as it reversibly reaches the dead state (equilibrium with the environment). **Difference from energy:**

| Aspect | Energy | Exergy |
|--------|--------|--------|
| Conservation | Conserved (1st Law) | Not conserved — destroyed by irreversibility |
| Basis | First Law | First + Second Law combined |
| Value | Quantity only | Quantity *and* quality (availability) |
| At dead state | May be non-zero | **Always zero** |
| Direction of natural change | Constant | Always **decreases** |

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2. What is the dead state? Why is exergy zero at the dead state?**

**Model Answer:**

The **dead state** is the unique thermodynamic state at which a system is in complete **thermal, mechanical, and chemical equilibrium** with the environment (T = T₀, p = p₀, same composition, no velocity/elevation).

Exergy is zero at the dead state because:
- No temperature difference → no thermal work possible.
- No pressure difference → no expansion work possible.
- No chemical potential difference → no reaction work possible.
- No velocity/elevation → no KE/PE work possible.
- Therefore, **W_max = 0** at the dead state by definition.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Numerical Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### 🅰️ **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**A piston-cylinder device contains 0.5 kg of air at 1500 K and 1000 kPa. The air expands reversibly to a final state of 300 K and 100 kPa. The environment is at T₀ = 300 K, p₀ = 100 kPa. Determine:**

**(a)** The maximum work (non-flow exergy) produced during the process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The irreversibility (exergy destroyed) and second-law efficiency, given that the actual work output is 320 kJ. **(7 marks)**

**Given:** Air as ideal gas, $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Recognize: closed system (piston-cylinder), use Formula #2 (non-flow exergy):**

$$\Phi = (U_1 - U_0) + p_0(V_1 - V_0) - T_0(S_1 - S_0)$$

**Step 2 — Calculate change in internal energy:**

$$\Delta U = m \, c_v (T_1 - T_0) = 0.5 \times 0.718 \times (1500 - 300) = 430.8 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate volumes using ideal gas law $v = RT/p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= \frac{R T_1}{p_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 1500}{1000} = 0.4305 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} \\
v_2 &= \frac{R T_2}{p_2} = \frac{0.287 \times 300}{100} = 0.861 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate entropy change (Formula for ideal gas):**

$$\Delta s = c_v \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} + R \ln\frac{v_2}{v_1}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s &= 0.718 \ln\frac{300}{1500} + 0.287 \ln\frac{0.861}{0.4305} \\
&= 0.718 \times (-1.6094) + 0.287 \times (0.6931) \\
&= -1.1556 + 0.1989 = -0.9567 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute all values into exergy formula:**

$$p_0(v_2 - v_0) - T_0 \Delta s :$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_0(v_2 - v_0) &= 100 \times (0.861 - 0.861) = 0 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad (\text{since } T_2 = T_0, v_2 = v_0) \\
T_0 \Delta s &= 300 \times (-0.9567) = -287.01 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= c_v(T_1 - T_0) - T_0 \Delta s \\
&= 0.718 \times 1200 - 300 \times (-0.9567) \\
&= 861.6 + 287.01 = 1148.61 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Total non-flow exergy:**

$$\Phi_1 = m \times \phi_1 = 0.5 \times 1148.61 = \mathbf{574.3 \text{ kJ}}$$

> **Since the process is reversible, W_max = Φ = 574.3 kJ ✓**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Reversible work = 574.3 kJ (from part a).**

**Step 2 — Apply Gouy-Stodola to find irreversibility:**

$$I = W_{rev} - W_{actual} = 574.3 - 320 = \mathbf{254.3 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Step 3 — Second-law efficiency:**

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{W_{actual}}{W_{rev}} = \frac{320}{574.3} = \mathbf{0.557 \text{ or } 55.7\%}$$

---

### 🅱️ **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**A steady-flow heat exchanger receives steam at 3 MPa and 400 °C and discharges it at 0.1 MPa and 200 °C. The mass flow rate is 2 kg/s. The environment is at T₀ = 25 °C, p₀ = 100 kPa. Determine:**

**(a)** The exergy at the inlet and outlet per unit mass (neglect KE and PE). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The rate of exergy destruction and the second-law efficiency, given that the actual heat loss to surroundings is 50 kW. **(7 marks)**

**Use steam tables.**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Inlet: Superheated steam at 3 MPa, 400 °C (from steam tables):**

- $h_1 = 3230.9$ kJ/kg
- $s_1 = 6.9212$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 2 — Outlet: Superheated steam at 0.1 MPa, 200 °C:**

- $h_2 = 2875.3$ kJ/kg
- $s_2 = 7.8343$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 3 — Dead state: Saturated liquid at T₀ = 25 °C, p₀ = 100 kPa:**

- $h_0 \approx 104.89$ kJ/kg
- $s_0 \approx 0.3674$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 4 — Apply Formula #4 (flow exergy neglecting KE/PE):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi_1 &= (h_1 - h_0) - T_0(s_1 - s_0) \\
&= (3230.9 - 104.89) - 298.15 \times (6.9212 - 0.3674) \\
&= 3126.01 - 298.15 \times 6.5538 \\
&= 3126.01 - 1954.05 = \mathbf{1171.96 \text{ kJ/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi_2 &= (h_2 - h_0) - T_0(s_2 - s_0) \\
&= (2875.3 - 104.89) - 298.15 \times (7.8343 - 0.3674) \\
&= 2770.41 - 298.15 \times 7.4669 \\
&= 2770.41 - 2226.24 = \mathbf{544.17 \text{ kJ/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Decrease in exergy = exergy "lost" in flow:**

$$\Delta \psi = \psi_1 - \psi_2 = 1171.96 - 544.17 = 627.79 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Rate of exergy destruction:**

$$\dot{I} = \dot{m} \times \Delta \psi = 2 \times 627.79 = \mathbf{1255.6 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 3 — Exergy supplied (input stream):**

$$\dot{X}_{in} = \dot{m} \times \psi_1 = 2 \times 1171.96 = 2343.9 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Exergy recovered (output stream):**

$$\dot{X}_{out} = \dot{m} \times \psi_2 = 2 \times 544.17 = 1088.3 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Second-law efficiency:**

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{\dot{X}_{out}}{\dot{X}_{in}} = \frac{1088.3}{2343.9} = \mathbf{0.464 \text{ or } 46.4\%}$$

**Verification using Formula #8:** $1 - I/X_{in} = 1 - 1255.6/2343.9 = 0.464$ ✓

---

> [!WARNING] **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing Φ and ψ:** Students use closed-system exergy formula in open-system problems and lose 4–5 marks instantly. **Memorize**: piston-cylinder/tank = Φ; nozzle/turbine/compressor/pipe = ψ.
> 2. **Forgetting the dead-state reference:** Every numerical answer must explicitly state T₀, p₀, h₀, s₀ used. Missing this loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Sign errors in Gouy-Stodola:** $I = W_{rev} - W_{actual}$ (work output) or $I = W_{actual} - W_{rev}$ (work input) — signs matter! 3 marks lost if reversed.
> 4. **Using °C instead of K in entropy formula:** Always convert temperatures to **Kelvin** before applying $c_p \ln(T/T_0)$.
> 5. **Not simplifying $(v_0 - v_0) = 0$** when final state equals dead state. 1 mark lost.
> 6. **Forgetting units:** Answer must have units **kJ** (closed) or **kJ/kg** (specific) or **kW** (rate). KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing units.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT] **Essential Concepts to Memorize (High KTU Frequency)**

- ✅ Exergy = **Maximum useful work** extractable to dead state.
- ✅ Dead state conditions: T = T₀, p = p₀, same composition, V = 0, z = 0.
- ✅ **Non-flow exergy (closed):** $\Phi = (U - U_0) + p_0(V - V_0) - T_0(S - S_0)$
- ✅ **Flow exergy (open):** $\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$
- ✅ **Decrease of exergy principle:** Real processes always have $\Phi_2 < \Phi_1$.
- ✅ **Irreversibility (Gouy-Stodola):** $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen} = W_{rev} - W_{actual}$
- ✅ **Exergy transfer by heat:** $\Phi_Q = \left(1 - \frac{T_0}{T}\right) Q$ (Carnot factor).
- ✅ **Second-law efficiency:** $\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{Exergy recovered}}{\text{Exergy supplied}}$
- ✅ Reference environment: **T₀ = 298.15 K, p₀ = 101.325 kPa** (unless stated otherwise).
- ✅ **Anergy** = Total energy − Exergy (dumped to environment).
- ✅ **Ideal gas flow exergy:** $\psi = c_p(T - T_0) - T_0\left[c_p \ln\frac{T}{T_0} - R \ln\frac{p}{p_0}\right]$
- ✅ **Ideal gas non-flow exergy:** $\phi = c_v(T - T_0) - T_0\left[c_v \ln\frac{T}{T_0} + R \ln\frac{v}{v_0}\right]$
- ✅ Exergy is **path-independent** (a property); irreversibility is **path-dependent**.
- ✅ KTU 2024 typical weightage: 1–2 questions × 7 marks each in ESE; often combined with Second Law and Entropy modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reversible Work and Irreversibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reversible Work and Irreversibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Reversible Work (W_rev)
**Reversible work** is defined as the **maximum useful work** (or minimum work required) that can be delivered (or received) by a system as it undergoes a process between two specified states, with the system remaining in thermodynamic equilibrium with its surroundings at every infinitesimal step.

Mathematically, it is the work produced by a **quasi-static, frictionless process** that has no dissipative effects (unrestrained expansion, mixing, chemical reaction, heat transfer through finite temperature difference, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Reversible work is the work done during a reversible process where the system passes through a continuous series of equilibrium states."*

### 1.2 Irreversibility (I)
**Irreversibility** is the portion of useful work that is **destroyed** or **lost** due to the presence of irreversibilities (friction, unrestrained expansion, finite ΔT heat transfer, etc.) in a real process.

It quantifies the difference between the **maximum possible (reversible) work** and the **actual work** obtained.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Spilling Cup**
> Imagine pouring water gently from a cup into a glass (reversible — you can pour it back perfectly with zero effort). Now, imagine dropping the cup on the floor and letting the water splash everywhere (irreversible — the work done is lost as heat/sound, and the water cannot return to the cup by itself). 
> 
> The "reversible work" is the gentle pour — the *ideal* energy transfer. The "irreversibility" is the wasted energy from the drop — it is the gap between what *could* have been achieved and what *actually* happened.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Reversible work** is the **EXERGY** (Available Energy) of the system for a closed system.
> - **Irreversibility** is proportional to **Entropy Generation**: $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$
> - **Standard reference environment** in KTU problems: $T_0 = 298\,\text{K}$ (25°C), $P_0 = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$, unless otherwise stated.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conditions for a Reversible Process
A process is **reversible** ONLY if ALL of the following conditions are met simultaneously:

- The process is **quasi-static** (system passes through a continuous sequence of equilibrium states).
- There is **no friction** (mechanical, fluid, or otherwise).
- **Heat transfer** occurs across an **infinitesimally small temperature difference** ($\Delta T \to 0$).
- **No unrestrained expansion** (e.g., gas into vacuum).
- **No mixing** of dissimilar substances without work extraction.
- **No chemical reaction** or phase change that is non-quasi-equilibrium.

### 2.2 Why is Reversible Work the Maximum?
- For a **work-producing** device (e.g., expansion): friction and unrestrained expansion cause the working fluid to dissipate work into internal energy/sound. Removing these gives the **maximum work**.
- For a **work-consuming** device (e.g., compression): friction and unrestrained effects require *more* work than the reversible ideal. Reversible is therefore the **minimum work**.

### 2.3 Sources of Irreversibility (KTU List)
1. **Friction** (mechanical, fluid, viscous)
2. **Unrestrained expansion** (free expansion into vacuum)
3. **Heat transfer** through a finite temperature difference
4. **Mixing** of different substances at different states
5. **Chemical reactions** with finite driving force
6. **Hysteresis** and electrical resistance

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Sign Convention** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reversible Work (Closed system) | $W_{rev} = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$ | Work out: positive | kJ |
| Irreversible Work (Closed system) | $W_{irrev} = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext} \, dV$ | $P_{ext} \neq P$ inside | kJ |
| Irreversibility (Work-producing) | $I = W_{rev} - W_{actual}$ | Always $\geq 0$ | kJ |
| Irreversibility (Work-consuming) | $I = W_{actual} - W_{rev}$ | Always $\geq 0$ | kJ |
| Gouy–Stodola Theorem | $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ | $T_0$ = dead state temp | kJ |
| Available Energy (Exergy) | $\phi = W_{rev}$ | Maximum useful work | kJ |
| Unavailable Energy | $\phi_{un} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ | Energy degraded | kJ |
| Second-Law Efficiency | $\eta_{II} = \frac{W_{actual}}{W_{rev}}$ | Ratio $\leq 1$ | — |
| Isothermal Rev Work (Ideal Gas) | $W_{rev} = mRT \ln\frac{V_2}{V_1}$ | Quasi-static | kJ |
| Polytropic Rev Work (Ideal Gas) | $W_{rev} = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1}$ | $n \neq 1$ | kJ |
| Isentropic Rev Work | $W_{rev} = \frac{mR(T_1 - T_2)}{k-1}$ | $k = c_p/c_v$ | kJ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** Whenever a problem gives a real/irreversible process alongside a reversible path between the same end states, the **irreversibility** is the difference: $I = W_{rev} - W_{actual}$ (for turbines, engines) or $I = W_{actual} - W_{rev}$ (for compressors, pumps).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Steam Power Plants:** Reversible work = Carnot-equivalent ideal turbine work. Real turbine losses = irreversibility.
- **Refrigeration:** Reversible work = Carnot compressor work. Actual compressor work $\geq W_{rev}$.
- **Internal Combustion Engines:** Indicates combustion-driven entropy generation (lost work).
- **Chemical Plants:** Identifies which unit operation (e.g., throttle valve, mixing tank) wastes the most exergy — critical for **exergy analysis** and energy auditing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Reversible Work in a Closed System

**Starting Point:** The boundary work for a quasi-static process is given by the pressure of the system acting on the boundary.

$$dW = P \, dA \cdot ds = P \, dV$$

**Step 1:** For a closed system undergoing a quasi-static (internally reversible) process:

$$W_{rev} = \int_{1}^{2} P \, dV$$

**Step 2:** By the thermodynamic sign convention (work done BY system is positive), the work output is:

$$W_{rev} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

But KTU standard convention is **work done ON the system is negative**, hence:

$$W_{rev} = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

**Step 3:** For an **ideal gas undergoing isothermal reversible expansion** ($PV = mRT$):

$$W_{rev} = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{mRT}{V} \, dV = -mRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluating the integral:

$$W_{rev} = -mRT \left[ \ln V \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = -mRT \left( \ln V_2 - \ln V_1 \right)$$

**Step 5:** Final boxed result:

$$W_{rev} = mRT \ln\frac{V_1}{V_2} = mRT \ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Gouy–Stodola Theorem ($I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$)

**Step 1:** Consider a closed system delivering work $W_{actual}$ in an environment at $T_0, P_0$. Apply the **First Law**:

$$Q - W_{actual} = \Delta U$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Second Law** for the system + surroundings (combined isolated universe):

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \geq 0$$

**Step 3:** The surroundings receive heat $-Q$ at temperature $T_0$:

$$\Delta S_{surr} = \frac{-Q}{T_0} = \frac{W_{actual} - \Delta U}{T_0}$$

**Step 4:** Total entropy change of universe:

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \frac{W_{actual} - \Delta U}{T_0}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by $T_0$:

$$T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{univ} = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{sys} + W_{actual} - \Delta U$$

**Step 6:** The **reversible work** is the work that would be obtained if all processes were reversible (i.e., $\Delta S_{univ} = 0$):

$$W_{rev} = \Delta U - T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{sys}$$

**Step 7:** Subtract actual from reversible:

$$W_{rev} - W_{actual} = -T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{sys} - T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{surr}$$

**Step 8:** Recognizing $\Delta S_{univ} = S_{gen}$:

$$I = W_{rev} - W_{actual} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$

This is the **Gouy–Stodola Theorem**, the foundation of exergy-based irreversibility analysis.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Irreversibility of an Unrestrained Expansion (Free Expansion)

**Setup:** A gas in a rigid, insulated tank expands into a vacuum (no work done, no heat transfer). 

**Step 1:** Energy balance — no heat, no work:

$$\Delta U = Q - W = 0 - 0 = 0$$

So $T_1 = T_2$ for an ideal gas.

**Step 2:** Compute reversible work between the same end states using isothermal path:

$$W_{rev} = mRT \ln\frac{V_1}{V_2}$$

Since $V_2 > V_1$, $W_{rev} > 0$ (system *could* have done work).

**Step 3:** Actual work:

$$W_{actual} = 0 \quad (\text{expansion into vacuum})$$

**Step 4:** Irreversibility:

$$I = W_{rev} - W_{actual} = mRT \ln\frac{V_2}{V_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Even though the gas did *zero* work and no heat was lost, **work potential has been destroyed** by the unrestrained expansion. This lost work is exactly $mRT\ln(V_2/V_1)$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Reversible Polytropic Work

**Step 1:** For a polytropic process: $PV^n = \text{constant} = C$

Thus $P = C \cdot V^{-n}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute into the reversible work integral:

$$W_{rev} = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} C \cdot V^{-n} \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral:

$$W_{rev} = -C \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = -\frac{C}{1-n} \left( V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back $C = P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$:

$$W_{rev} = -\frac{P_1 V_1^n}{1-n} \left( V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Simplify using $P_1 V_1^n \cdot V_1^{1-n} = P_1 V_1$ and similarly for state 2:

$$W_{rev} = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is **invalid for $n = 1$** (isothermal). Use the logarithmic form for isothermal cases.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Reversible vs. Irreversible

| **Parameter** | **Reversible Process** | **Irreversible Process** |
|---|---|---|
| Equilibrium path | Continuous equilibrium states | System is out of equilibrium |
| Driving force | Infinitesimal (e.g., $dP$, $dT$) | Finite (e.g., $\Delta P$, $\Delta T$) |
| Friction | Zero | Non-zero |
| Heat transfer $\Delta T$ | Zero | Finite |
| Direction | Reversible (path-independent) | Spontaneous in one direction |
| Work output (expansion) | **Maximum** | Less than maximum |
| Work input (compression) | **Minimum** | More than minimum |
| Entropy generation $S_{gen}$ | $S_{gen} = 0$ | $S_{gen} > 0$ |
| Irreversibility $I$ | $I = 0$ | $I = T_0 S_{gen} > 0$ |
| Second-Law Efficiency | $\eta_{II} = 1$ (100%) | $\eta_{II} < 1$ |
| Existence in reality | Idealization only | All real processes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Energy Degradation via Irreversibility

This diagram maps how useful work potential degrades through an irreversible process in a closed system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Energy Input Q] --> B[System Boundary]
    B --> C[Reversible Work Wrev]
    B --> D[Actual Work Wactual]
    C --> E[Maximum Useful Work Available]
    D --> F[Useful Work Delivered]
    E --> G[Irreversibility I = Wrev - Wactual]
    F --> G
    G --> H[T0 x Sgen]
    H --> I[Unavailable Energy to Environment]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#00802b,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#00802b,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Comparison Flow: Reversible vs. Irreversible Expansion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    State1A[State 1: P1 V1 T1] -->|Quasi-static frictionless| PathR[Reversible Path]
    PathR --> State2A[State 2: P2 V2 T2]
    PathR --> Wrev1[Wrev MAX = Integral P dV]
    
    State1A -->|Sudden unrestrained| PathI[Irreversible Path]
    PathI --> State2A
    PathI --> Wact1[Wactual LESS than Wrev]
    
    Wrev1 --> Diff[Irreversibility I = Wrev - Wactual]
    Wact1 --> Diff
    Diff --> Heat[Energy Lost as Internal Energy or Heat]
    
    style State1A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style State2A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style PathR fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#00802b,stroke-width:2px
    style PathI fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style Wrev1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#00802b,stroke-width:2px
    style Wact1 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style Diff fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px
    style Heat fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture: Sources of Irreversibility in a Thermal System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Thermal or Mechanical Input] --> M1{Mechanical Component}
```
