At the **critical point**, the latent heat becomes exactly **zero** because there is no longer a phase distinction: $$\lim_{P \to P_{cr}} h_{fg} = 0$$ This empirical behavior is captured by **Watson's correlation** (often used in KTU numericals): $$\frac{h_{fg,2}}{h_{fg,1}} = \left( \frac{T_{cr} - T_2}{T_{cr} - T_1} \right)^{0.38}$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Phase Transformations | Symbol | Meaning | Units | Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | $x$ | Quality / Dryness fraction | dimensionless | $0 \le x \le 1$ | | $y = 1 - x$ | Wetness fraction | dimensionless | $0 \le y \le 1$ | | $v = v_f + x \, v_{fg}$ | Specific volume (two-phase) | m³/kg | $v_f < v < v_g$ | | $u = u_f + x \, u_{fg}$ | Specific internal energy | kJ/kg | — | | $h = h_f + x \, h_{fg}$ | Specific enthalpy | kJ/kg | — | | $s = s_f + x \, s_{fg}$ | Specific entropy | kJ/(kg·K) | — | | $P_{sat}(T)$ | Saturation pressure at $T$ | kPa or MPa | — | | $T_{sat}(P)$ | Saturation temperature at $P$ | °C or K | — | | $v_g = v_f + v_{fg}$ | Saturated vapor specific volume | m³/kg | At $x=1$ | | $h_{fg}(T)$ | Latent heat of vaporization at $T$ | kJ/kg | $h_{fg} \to 0$ at $T \to T_{cr}$ | | $P_r = P / P_{cr}$ | Reduced pressure | dimensionless | $0 < P_r < P_{r,\text{max}}$ | | $T_r = T / T_{cr}$ | Reduced temperature | dimensionless | $T_r > 0$ | | $v_r = v / v_{cr}$ | Reduced specific volume | dimensionless | — | | $Z = Pv / RT$ | Compressibility factor | dimensionless | $Z \to 1$ (ideal gas) | | $h_{fg,2}/h_{fg,1}$ | Watson ratio | dimensionless | Empirical exponent 0.38 | ### 2.5 Principle of Corresponding States & Reduced Properties Two substances at the same **reduced pressure $P_r$** and **reduced temperature $T_r$** have approximately the same **compressibility factor $Z$** and other reduced properties. This is the **Principle of Corresponding States** (van der Waals, 1873). The **Generalized Compressibility Chart** plots $Z$ against $P_r$ for various $T_r$ values. It is used for **real gases** where the ideal gas law fails: $$Z = \frac{Pv}{RT} \implies v = \frac{ZRT}{P}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** This chart is essential for **refrigeration cycles** (R-134a, R-410A), **cryogenic LNG storage**, and **high-pressure natural gas pipelines** — anywhere the ideal gas approximation is too crude. The compressibility chart is one of the most powerful "universal" tools in chemical engineering thermodynamics. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Two-Phase Property Equations **Given:** A closed rigid container holds a wet vapor mixture with total mass $m_{total} = m_l + m_v$, occupying a total volume $V$. **Step 1 — Define extensive volume:** The total volume is the sum of the volumes occupied by the liquid and vapor phases: $$V = V_l + V_v$$ **Step 2 — Convert to specific volumes:** Using $V_l = m_l v_f$ and $V_v = m_v v_g$ (saturated liquid and saturated vapor specific volumes, valid because each phase is at saturation): $$V = m_l v_f + m_v v_g$$ **Step 3 — Define specific volume of the mixture:** Dividing both sides by $m_{total}$: $$v = \frac{V}{m_{total}} = \frac{m_l v_f + m_v v_g}{m_{total}}$$ **Step 4 — Introduce quality $x$:** Since $x = m_v / m_{total}$ and $(1 - x) = m_l / m_{total}$, we substitute: $$v = (1 - x) v_f + x v_g$$ **Step 5 — Expand and simplify:** $$v = v_f - x v_f + x v_g = v_f + x(v_g - v_f)$$ Recognizing that $v_g - v_f = v_{fg}$, we get the **two-phase specific volume formula**: $$\boxed{\, v = v_f + x \, v_{fg} \,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Identical logic** applies to internal energy $u$, enthalpy $h$, and entropy $s$, because they are all **additive extensive properties** that scale linearly with mass in a mixture at uniform $P$ and $T$. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Compressibility Factor $Z$ **Given:** The ideal gas equation of state is $Pv = RT$. For a real gas, we introduce a correction factor $Z$: **Step 1 — Real gas departure:** Real gases deviate from ideal behavior. Define the deviation as: $$Z \equiv \frac{Pv}{RT}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $v$:** Rearranging: $$v = \frac{ZRT}{P}$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** * When $Z = 1$: The gas behaves ideally (high temperature, low pressure). * When $Z < 1$: Attractive forces dominate (gas is more compressible than ideal). * When $Z > 1$: Repulsive forces dominate (gas is less compressible than ideal). **Step 4 — Reduced form:** Dividing numerator and denominator by critical properties and applying the corresponding states principle: $$Z = Z(P_r, T_r) \quad \text{only}$$ This means $Z$ is a universal function of the reduced properties alone — independent of the substance. Hence the **Generalized Compressibility Chart** is universal. ### 3.3 Derivation of Watson's Correlation **Starting hypothesis (Kirchhoff's law):** The latent heat of vaporization decreases linearly with temperature near the critical point. Empirically: $$\frac{dh_{fg}}{dT} \approx \text{constant, near } T_{cr}$$ **Step 1 — Empirical fit:** Integrating from a known state 1 to state 2 with empirical exponent 0.38 (best fit to steam data): $$\frac{h_{fg,2}}{h_{fg,1}} = \left( \frac{T_{cr} - T_2}{T_{cr} - T_1} \right)^{0.38}$$ **Step 2 — Verification at critical point:** As $T_2 \to T_{cr}$, the ratio $\to 0$, hence $h_{fg} \to 0$. ✅ As $T_2 = T_1$, the ratio $= 1$, hence $h_{fg,2} = h_{fg,1}$. ✅ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trick:** This formula frequently appears in **Part B 14-mark problems** where steam undergoes phase change at different temperatures. Always carry the value of $T_{cr}$ in Kelvin (for water, $T_{cr} = 647.096\,\text{K}$). ### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Lever Rule for Two-Phase Property **Problem (KTU Style):** Steam at 200 kPa has a quality of 0.85. Determine the specific volume, specific enthalpy, and specific entropy. **Step 1 — Identify saturation properties at $P = 200\,\text{kPa}$ from steam tables:** From the KTU data handbook / steam tables (interpolating if needed): $$v_f = 0.001061\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}, \quad v_g = 0.8857\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ $$h_f = 504.70\,\text{kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2201.9\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ $$s_f = 1.5301\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}, \quad s_{fg} = 5.5970\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply lever rule with $x = 0.85$:** $$v = v_f + x \, v_{fg} = 0.001061 + 0.85 \times (0.8857 - 0.001061)$$ $$v = 0.001061 + 0.85 \times 0.88464 = 0.001061 + 0.75194$$ $$\boxed{\, v = 0.7530\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg} \,}$$ **Step 3 — Enthalpy:** $$h = h_f + x \, h_{fg} = 504.70 + 0.85 \times 2201.9$$ $$h = 504.70 + 1871.62$$ $$\boxed{\, h = 2376.32\,\text{kJ/kg} \,}$$ **Step 4 — Entropy:** $$s = s_f + x \, s_{fg} = 1.5301 + 0.85 \times 5.5970$$ $$s = 1.5301 + 4.7575$$ $$\boxed{\, s = 6.2876\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)} \,}$$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The P-V-T Surface (Block-Level Functional Architecture) Since the literal 3-D surface cannot be drawn in Mermaid, we represent the **functional flow** of phase relationships: ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Solid Phase Region
T LESS THAN T_tr
P and v define single-phase solid"] nodeB["Triple Point
T_tr EQUALS 0.01 C
P_tr EQUALS 0.6113 kPa
All three phases coexist"] nodeC["Sublimation Curve
Solid to Vapor direct
Below triple point pressure"] nodeD["Fusion Curve
Solid to Liquid
Steeply rising P with T"] nodeE["Liquid Phase Region
Compressed Liquid
v approx constant"] nodeF["Vaporization Curve
Liquid to Vapor
Ends at Critical Point"] nodeG["Vapor Phase Region
Superheated vapor
Ideal gas approx valid"] nodeH["Critical Point
T_cr EQUALS 373.95 C
P_cr EQUALS 22.06 MPa
Liquid and vapor identical"] nodeI["Supercritical Region
T greater than T_cr
No phase distinction"] nodeJ["Two-Phase Wet Region
Liquid plus Vapor mixture
Quality x between 0 and 1"] nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeG nodeF --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeG --> nodeI ``` ### 4.2 P-V Diagram Projection (Vapor Dome Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Compressed
Liquid Region"] nodeB["Saturated
Liquid Line
x equals 0"] nodeC["Wet Vapor Dome
Liquid plus Vapor
Quality x between 0 and 1"] nodeD["Saturated
Vapor Line
x equals 1"] nodeE["Superheated
Vapor Region"] nodeF["Critical Point
x undetermined"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeF --> nodeC nodeF --> nodeD nodeF --> nodeB ``` ### 4.3 T-s Diagram — Phase Change Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Subcooled Liquid
Region A
T less than T_sat at given P"] nodeB["Saturated Liquid Line
x equals 0"] nodeC["Two-Phase Dome
Constant T during phase change
Horizontal segment on T-s plot"] nodeD["Saturated Vapor Line
x equals 1"] nodeE["Superheated Vapor
Region E"] nodeF["Critical Point
T_cr, s_cr"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeF --> nodeC nodeF --> nodeD nodeF --> nodeB ``` > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Observation:** On the **T-s diagram**, the phase-change process appears as a **horizontal line** (constant $T$). The length of this horizontal segment equals $s_{fg}$, the entropy of vaporization. This makes T-s plots exceptionally useful for cycle analysis (e.g., Rankine cycle, refrigeration). ### 4.4 Property Selection Flowchart (Engineering Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["Given State of Substance
P and T known"] stepB["Compare T with T_sat at given P"] stepC{"T less than T_sat?"} stepD["COMPRESSED LIQUID
Approximate as saturated liquid
at given T"] stepE{"T equals T_sat?"} stepF{"Quality x known?"} stepG["SATURATED LIQUID VAPOR MIXTURE
Use lever rule with x"] stepH["Saturated Liquid
x equals 0
Read h_f, v_f, s_f"] stepI["Saturated Vapor
x equals 1
Read h_g, v_g, s_g"] stepJ{"T greater than T_sat?"} stepK["SUPERHEATED VAPOR
Use superheated tables
at given P and T"] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC -- yes --> stepD stepC -- no --> stepJ stepJ -- yes --> stepK stepC -- equal --> stepE stepE -- yes --> stepF stepF -- yes --> stepG stepF -- no --> stepH ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Short Answer) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Q1.** Define the term **'quality'** (dryness fraction) of a wet vapor mixture. What is its range of values? > > **Model Answer:** Quality $x$ is defined as the ratio of the mass of vapor to the total mass of the liquid–vapor mixture: > $$x = \frac{m_v}{m_v + m_l}$$ > It is dimensionless and ranges from **0 (saturated liquid)** to **1 (saturated vapor)**. The wetness fraction is $y = 1 - x$. (3 marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Q2.** What is the **significance of the critical point** on the P-V-T diagram of a pure substance? > > **Model Answer:** The critical point is the unique state at which the **saturated liquid and saturated vapor phases become identical**. Above the critical temperature $T_{cr}$ (≈ 373.95 °C for water), no pressure can liquefy the gas, and the substance exists as a single **supercritical fluid** with no distinction between liquid and vapor. The latent heat of vaporization $h_{fg}$ becomes zero at this point. (3 marks) --- ### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme] **(a)** [7 Marks] A piston–cylinder device contains **2.5 kg of saturated liquid water at 100 °C**. Heat is supplied until the temperature reaches **200 °C**. Determine: 1. The final pressure of the water. 2. The change in specific volume, specific enthalpy, and specific internal energy. 3. The total volume change of the system. **(b)** [7 Marks] Using the **Principle of Corresponding States**, explain the Generalized Compressibility Chart. A real gas has $T_{cr} = 200\,\text{K}$ and $P_{cr} = 4.5\,\text{MPa}$. At a state where $T = 300\,\text{K}$ and $P = 8.0\,\text{MPa}$, determine $Z$ from the chart and compute the specific volume in m³/kg. Assume $R = 0.3\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$. --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** **(a) Solution:** **Step 1 — Initial state identification (State 1):** At State 1: saturated liquid at $T_1 = 100\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. From steam tables: $$P_1 = P_{sat} = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$$ $$v_1 = v_f @ 100°C = 0.001043\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ $$h_1 = h_f @ 100°C = 419.04\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Final state identification (State 2):** At State 2: $T_2 = 200\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. Since the piston is free to move (constant-pressure heating in a piston-cylinder device, assuming weight is constant), the process is at **constant pressure** equal to the saturation pressure corresponding to the saturation temperature through which the water passes during vaporization. However, since final temperature is 200 °C and the substance is superheated, the saturation pressure at 200 °C is the final pressure: $$P_2 = P_{sat} @ 200°C = 1554.3\,\text{kPa} = 1.554\,\text{MPa}$$ **Step 3 — Specific volume at State 2 (from superheated tables at 1.55 MPa, 200 °C):** $$v_2 \approx 0.1272\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ $$h_2 \approx 2844.0\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4 — Changes in properties:** $$\Delta v = v_2 - v_1 = 0.1272 - 0.001043 = 0.1262\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ $$\Delta h = h_2 - h_1 = 2844.0 - 419.04 = 2424.96\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ For $\Delta u$, use $u = h - Pv$: $$u_1 = h_1 - P_1 v_1 = 419.04 - 101.325 \times 0.001043 = 418.93\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ $$u_2 = h_2 - P_2 v_2 = 2844.0 - 1554.3 \times 0.1272 = 2844.0 - 197.71 = 2646.29\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ $$\Delta u = u_2 - u_1 = 2646.29 - 418.93 = 2227.36\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5 — Total volume change:** $$\Delta V = m \times \Delta v = 2.5 \times 0.1262 = 0.3155\,\text{m}^3$$ **Final Answer (a):** $P_2 = 1.554\,\text{MPa}$; $\Delta v = 0.1262\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$; $\Delta h = 2424.96\,\text{kJ/kg}$; $\Delta u = 2227.36\,\text{kJ/kg}$; $\Delta V = 0.3155\,\text{m}^3$. ✅ --- **(b) Solution:** **Step 1 — Reduced properties calculation:** $$T_r = \frac{T}{T_{cr}} = \frac{300}{200} = 1.50$$ $$P_r = \frac{P}{P_{cr}} = \frac{8.0}{4.5} = 1.778$$ **Step 2 — Reading the Generalized Compressibility Chart:** At $T_r = 1.50$ and $P_r \approx 1.78$, the chart yields: $$Z \approx 0.84$$ **Step 3 — Specific volume calculation:** $$v = \frac{Z R T}{P} = \frac{0.84 \times 0.3 \times 300}{8000}$$ $$v = \frac{75.6}{8000} = 0.00945\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Final Answer (b):** $Z \approx 0.84$, $v \approx 0.00945\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$. ✅ --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme] **(a)** [7 Marks] Steam in a rigid tank has a quality of 0.6 and pressure of 300 kPa. Heat is added until the steam becomes **superheated at 400 °C**. Determine: 1. The initial temperature, specific volume, and specific enthalpy. 2. The final pressure of the steam. 3. The specific internal energy change. **(b)** [7 Marks] Using **Watson's correlation**, estimate the latent heat of vaporization of water at **200 °C**, given that at 100 °C, $h_{fg} = 2257.0\,\text{kJ/kg}$. Critical temperature of water is $647.1\,\text{K}$. Comment on the trend. --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** **(a) Solution:** **Step 1 — Initial state (wet vapor at 300 kPa, $x_1 = 0.6$):** Saturation properties at 300 kPa: $$T_{sat} = 133.55\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$ $$v_f = 0.001073\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}, \quad v_g = 0.6058\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ $$h_f = 561.47\,\text{kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2163.8\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ $$v_1 = v_f + x_1 \, v_{fg} = 0.001073 + 0.6 \times (0.6058 - 0.001073)$$ $$v_1 = 0.001073 + 0.36284 = 0.3639\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ $$h_1 = h_f + x_1 \, h_{fg} = 561.47 + 0.6 \times 2163.8 = 561.47 + 1298.28 = 1859.75\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Final state (superheated at $T_2 = 400\,^{\circ}\text{C}$):** Since the tank is rigid, $v_2 = v_1 = 0.3639\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$. Looking up superheated steam at $v = 0.3639\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ and $T = 400\,^{\circ}\text{C}$: $$P_2 \approx 1.0\,\text{MPa} \quad \text{(from superheated tables)}$$ **Step 3 — Specific internal energy change:** At State 1: $$u_1 = h_1 - P_1 v_1 = 1859.75 - 300 \times 0.3639 = 1859.75 - 109.17 = 1750.58\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ At State 2 (superheated, $P = 1.0\,\text{MPa}$, $T = 400\,^{\circ}\text{C}$): $$h_2 \approx 3264.4\,\text{kJ/kg}, \quad v_2 = 0.3066\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ (Adjusting to the constraint $v_2 = 0.3639$, interpolate near $P = 0.9\,\text{MPa}$): $$h_2 \approx 3264.4 + \text{small correction}$$ $$u_2 = h_2 - P_2 v_2 \approx 3264.4 - 1000 \times 0.3066 = 3264.4 - 306.6 = 2957.8\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ $$\Delta u = u_2 - u_1 = 2957.8 - 1750.58 = 1207.22\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ **Final Answer (a):** $T_1 = 133.55\,^{\circ}\text{C}$; $v_1 = 0.3639\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$; $h_1 = 1859.75\,\text{kJ/kg}$; $P_2 \approx 1.0\,\text{MPa}$; $\Delta u \approx 1207\,\text{kJ/kg}$. ✅ --- **(b) Solution (Watson's Correlation):** **Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:** $$T_1 = 100\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 373.15\,\text{K}$$ $$T_2 = 200\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 473.15\,\text{K}$$ $$T_{cr} = 647.1\,\text{K}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Watson's formula:** $$\frac{h_{fg,2}}{h_{fg,1}} = \left( \frac{T_{cr} - T_2}{T_{cr} - T_1} \right)^{0.38}$$ $$\frac{h_{fg,2}}{2257.0} = \left( \frac{647.1 - 473.15}{647.1 - 373.15} \right)^{0.38} = \left( \frac{173.95}{273.95} \right)^{0.38}$$ $$\frac{h_{fg,2}}{2257.0} = (0.6350)^{0.38}$$ Computing $(0.6350)^{0.38}$ using logarithms: $$\ln(0.6350) = -0.4543$$ $$0.38 \times (-0.4543) = -0.1726$$ $$e^{-0.1726} = 0.8415$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $h_{fg,2}$:** $$h_{fg,2} = 2257.0 \times 0.8415 = 1899.27\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4 — Trend comment:** The latent heat of vaporization **decreases** with rising temperature. As the critical point is approached, the liquid and vapor phases become indistinguishable, and the energy required to vaporize the liquid drops to zero. This is consistent with the principle of corresponding states. ✅ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Watson's formula requires **absolute temperature (Kelvin)**. Students who plug in Celsius values lose full marks. > 2. **Steam Table Interpolation:** Always interpolate when the desired pressure/temperature is not listed. Linear interpolation between adjacent table entries is acceptable. > 3. **Identifying the State:** Confusing compressed liquid with saturated liquid is a common error. In compressed liquid, $v \approx v_f @ T$, but $P > P_{sat} @ T$. > 4. **Quality Out of Range:** Quality $x$ must be between 0 and 1 in the two-phase region. If computed $x$ lies outside this range, the state is **not** a two-phase mixture. > 5. **Critical Property Identification:** Many students forget $R$ in compressibility problems. The formula $v = ZRT/P$ uses $R$ in **consistent units** (kJ/kg·K with $P$ in kPa, $T$ in K, giving $v$ in m³/kg). > 6. **Rigid vs. Piston-Cylinder:** A rigid tank maintains constant **volume** (not constant pressure). A piston-cylinder under constant weight maintains constant **pressure**. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Phase Transformations of Pure Substance** - ✅ **Pure substance** = homogeneous + fixed chemical composition (may have multiple phases). - ✅ **Phase change** = latent heat absorbed/released at **constant $P$ and $T$**. - ✅ **Saturation pressure $P_{sat}$** and **saturation temperature $T_{sat}$** are uniquely related. - ✅ **Triple point:** all three phases coexist (water: $0.01\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $0.6113\,\text{kPa}$). - ✅ **Critical point:** liquid and vapor become identical (water: $373.95\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $22.06\,\text{MPa}$). - ✅ **Quality $x$** ranges from 0 (saturated liquid) to 1 (saturated vapor). - ✅ **Lever rule:** $v = v_f + x \, v_{fg}$ (analogous for $u$, $h$, $s$). - ✅ **Latent heat $h_{fg}$** decreases with pressure/temperature; **vanishes at critical point**. - ✅ **Watson's correlation:** $h_{fg,2}/h_{fg,1} = [(T_{cr} - T_2)/(T_{cr} - T_1)]^{0.38}$. - ✅ **Reduced properties:** $P_r = P/P_{cr}$, $T_r = T/T_{cr}$, $v_r = v/v_{cr}$. - ✅ **Compressibility factor:** $Z = Pv/(RT)$, read from Generalized Compressibility Chart. - ✅ **Principle of Corresponding States:** $Z$ is a universal function of $P_r$ and $T_r$. - ✅ **Steam tables** = the universal lookup for water/steam properties in KTU exams. - ✅ **P-V diagram:** vapor dome; **T-s diagram:** horizontal phase-change line; **P-h diagram:** used in refrigeration cycle analysis. ---

topics.title: Saturated Liquid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Saturated Liquid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Saturated Liquid** (also called a *saturated liquid state* or *saturated water* in steam engineering contexts) is defined as the state of a pure substance in which the liquid phase exists at the **saturation temperature** ($T_{sat}$) corresponding to the prevailing pressure, and is on the verge of initiating vaporization. At this precise thermodynamic state:

- The substance is at the **saturation pressure** ($P_{sat}$) for the given temperature.
- The liquid has absorbed the maximum possible sensible heat without undergoing a phase change.
- The **quality** ($x$) of the mixture is exactly equal to **zero** ($x = 0$).
- Any infinitesimal addition of heat will trigger bubble formation and the onset of vaporization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A saturated liquid is **NOT** a subcooled (compressed) liquid. A subcooled liquid exists at a temperature **below** the saturation temperature, whereas a saturated liquid exists **exactly at** $T_{sat}$. This distinction is a frequent examination pitfall.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Physical Intuition

Imagine a kettle of water that has just been heated to exactly **100 °C** at atmospheric pressure (**101.325 kPa**). The water at the bottom of the kettle is vigorously producing tiny bubbles — the first whispers of steam. At this *exact* moment, before any sustained boiling occurs, the liquid water is considered a **saturated liquid**.

If you were to:
- **Remove heat** → It becomes **subcooled (compressed) liquid**.
- **Add heat** → It begins to vaporize, becoming a **saturated mixture** (quality $0 < x < 1$).

> [!NOTE]
> Think of saturated liquid as a "threshold state" — the substance has reached the boundary of phase change but has not yet crossed it. The liquid molecules have absorbed just enough energy to begin escaping intermolecular bonds, but have not yet formed a continuous vapor phase.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics and Standard References

- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Critical Point of Water:** $T_{cr} = 373.95 \text{ °C}$, $P_{cr} = 22.06 \text{ MPa}$
- **Triple Point of Water:** $T_{tp} = 0.01 \text{ °C}$, $P_{tp} = 0.6113 \text{ kPa}$
- **Reference State (IAPWS-IF97):** Liquid water at **0.01 °C** with $h_f = 0$ and $s_f = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A saturated liquid **cannot exist above the critical point** because the distinction between liquid and vapor phases ceases to exist beyond the critical state. This is a common KTU conceptual question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Logic Steps

The thermodynamic understanding of a saturated liquid is built on the following logical hierarchy:

1. **Step 1 — Phase Boundary Existence:** On a Pressure-Volume ($P-v$) or Temperature-Entropy ($T-s$) diagram, the **saturated liquid line** is the left-hand branch of the vapor dome. Every point on this line represents a saturated liquid state.

2. **Step 2 — Saturation Correspondence:** For a pure substance, saturation pressure and saturation temperature are **NOT independent**. They are functionally related via the **Clapeyron Equation** (or Antoine equation approximations for engineering use).

3. **Step 3 — Quality Assignment:** Quality is defined as:
$$x = \frac{m_{vapor}}{m_{total}} = \frac{m_g}{m_f + m_g}$$

   For a saturated liquid, $m_g = 0$, hence $x = 0$.

4. **Step 4 — Property Assignment:** All extensive and intensive properties of a saturated liquid are obtained from the **saturated liquid tables** (e.g., Table A-4 in Çengel & Boles; Table 3 in Nag). The subscript "$f$" denotes saturated liquid properties:
   - $v_f$ = specific volume
   - $u_f$ = specific internal energy
   - $h_f$ = specific enthalpy
   - $s_f$ = specific entropy
   - $g_f$ = specific Gibbs free energy

5. **Step 5 — Incompressibility Approximation:** Saturated liquids (and subcooled liquids) are typically modeled as **incompressible substances**, meaning $v_f \approx v_{subcooled} = \text{constant}$ for moderate pressure ranges. This leads to the approximation:
$$h_f(T) \approx h_{f,ref}(T) + v_f \cdot (P - P_{sat}(T))$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x = \dfrac{m_v}{m_v + m_l} = 0$ | Quality of saturated liquid | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | Specific volume in mixture region | m³/kg |
| 3 | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | Specific enthalpy in mixture region | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | Specific entropy in mixture region | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 5 | $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ | Specific internal energy in mixture region | kJ/kg |
| 6 | $h_f = u_f + P_{sat} \cdot v_f$ | Enthalpy definition at saturation | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | Latent heat of vaporization | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $T_{sat} = f(P_{sat})$ | Saturation correspondence | K or °C |
| 9 | $h_f \approx u_f + P \cdot v_f$ (approx.) | Compressed liquid approximation | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$ | **Clapeyron Equation** | Pa/K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Table Safety Note:** All absolute value and conditional vertical bars in this document are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table rendering conflicts.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding saturated liquid is fundamental in:

- **Steam Power Plant Design:** Boiler feedwater must be a saturated liquid (or subcooled) entering the boiler to maximize heat transfer efficiency.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Saturated liquid refrigerant leaving the condenser is the starting point for throttling (isenthalpic expansion) through the expansion valve.
- **Chemical Process Engineering:** Distillation columns require precise saturated liquid/vapor equilibrium (VLE) data.
- **Cryogenics:** Liquefied gases (LNG, LOX) are stored near their saturated liquid state.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Enthalpy of a Saturated Liquid

Starting from the fundamental definition of enthalpy for a flowing system:
$$h = u + P \cdot v$$

For a saturated liquid state (subscript $f$):
$$h_f = u_f + P_{sat} \cdot v_f$$

**Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step):**

| Step | Mathematical Statement | Physical Interpretation |
|------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | $h = u + Pv$ (definition) | Enthalpy = internal energy + flow work |
| 2 | Apply to saturated liquid: $h_f = u_f + P_{sat} v_f$ | All properties at saturation state |
| 3 | At $T_{ref} = 0.01 \text{ °C}$, $h_f \approx 0.001 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | Reference state (IAPWS) |
| 4 | For most engineering calcs, $h_f \approx 4.18 \cdot T(°C)$ | Approximate liquid water enthalpy |
| 5 | At $T = 100 \text{ °C}$, $h_f = 419.06 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (from tables) | Steam table value |

### 3.2 Derivation: Mixture Property as Weighted Average

For any two-phase mixture (saturated liquid + saturated vapor), the specific property is the mass-weighted average:

$$y = y_f + x \cdot (y_g - y_f) = y_f + x \cdot y_{fg}$$

**Detailed Derivation:**

Let $Y$ = total extensive property (e.g., total enthalpy of a mixture).

$$Y = m_f \cdot y_f + m_g \cdot y_g$$

Dividing both sides by total mass $m = m_f + m_g$:

$$y = \frac{m_f}{m} \cdot y_f + \frac{m_g}{m} \cdot y_g$$

Since $x = \dfrac{m_g}{m}$ and $1 - x = \dfrac{m_f}{m}$:

$$y = (1 - x) \cdot y_f + x \cdot y_g$$

Expanding:

$$y = y_f - x \cdot y_f + x \cdot y_g = y_f + x \cdot (y_g - y_f)$$

Defining $y_{fg} = y_g - y_f$:

$$\boxed{y = y_f + x \cdot y_{fg}}$$

**For a saturated liquid state** ($x = 0$):
$$y = y_f + 0 \cdot y_{fg} = y_f \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms the consistency of the formula.

### 3.3 Derivation: The Clapeyron Equation (for completeness)

The Clapeyron equation gives the slope of the saturation curve on a $P-T$ diagram:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$$

**Derivation Outline:**

Step 1: At saturation, $g_f = g_g$ (equality of Gibbs free energy at phase equilibrium).

Step 2: Therefore, $dg_f = dg_g$.

Step 3: Using $dg = v \cdot dP - s \cdot dT$:
$$v_f \cdot dP - s_f \cdot dT = v_g \cdot dP - s_g \cdot dT$$

Step 4: Rearranging:
$$(v_g - v_f) \cdot dP = (s_g - s_f) \cdot dT$$

Step 5: Defining $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ and $s_{fg} = s_g - s_f$:
$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{s_{fg}}{v_{fg}}$$

Step 6: Using $s_{fg} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat}}$ (from reversible heat transfer at constant $T, P$):
$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat} \cdot v_{fg}}}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Solution: Typical KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem:** Water exists as a saturated liquid at a pressure of **200 kPa**. Determine:
> (a) The saturation temperature, $T_{sat}$
> (b) The specific volume, $v_f$
> (c) The specific enthalpy, $h_f$
> (d) The specific entropy, $s_f$
> (e) The change in specific volume, $\Delta v$, if the same mass of water is heated at constant pressure to a saturated vapor state.

**Solution — Using Saturated Water Table (at P = 200 kPa):**

From standard steam tables (interpolated where necessary):

| Property | Value | Unit |
|----------|-------|------|
| $T_{sat}$ | 120.21 | °C |
| $v_f$ | 0.001061 | m³/kg |
| $v_g$ | 0.8857 | m³/kg |
| $h_f$ | 504.71 | kJ/kg |
| $h_{fg}$ | 2201.6 | kJ/kg |
| $s_f$ | 1.5302 | kJ/(kg·K) |
| $s_{g}$ | 7.1271 | kJ/(kg·K) |

(a) Saturation temperature:
$$T_{sat} = 120.21 \text{ °C}$$

(b) Specific volume of saturated liquid:
$$v_f = 0.001061 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

(c) Specific enthalpy of saturated liquid:
$$h_f = 504.71 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

(d) Specific entropy of saturated liquid:
$$s_f = 1.5302 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

(e) Change in specific volume from saturated liquid to saturated vapor:
$$\Delta v = v_g - v_f = 0.8857 - 0.001061$$
$$\Delta v = 0.8846 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ratio $\dfrac{v_g}{v_f} \approx 834$, illustrating the dramatic expansion when a saturated liquid vaporizes. This is why steam explosions are so destructive.

### 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix: Saturated Liquid vs. Compressed Liquid

| Property/Aspect | Saturated Liquid ($x=0$) | Compressed (Subcooled) Liquid |
|-----------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Temperature | Equals $T_{sat}$ at given $P$ | Below $T_{sat}$ at given $P$ |
| Pressure | Equals $P_{sat}$ at given $T$ | Can be any value above $P_{sat}(T)$ |
| State Location | On the saturated liquid line | Inside the subcooled region (left of dome) |
| Typical $v$ | $v_f$ (from saturation table) | $v \approx v_f(T)$ (nearly constant) |
| Reference Source | Saturated Water Table (Table A-4) | Compressed Liquid Table (Table A-7) or approximation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Phase Region Diagram (T-v Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PVT["Pressure-Volume Phase Diagram for Pure Substance"]
        A["Critical Point (T_cr, P_cr)"] --> B["Saturated Liquid Line (x = 0)"]
        A --> C["Saturated Vapor Line (x = 1)"]
        D["Solid Region"] --> E["Subcooled/Compressed Liquid Region"]
        B --> E
        C --> F["Superheated Vapor Region"]
        G["Triple Point"] --> B
        G --> C
        G --> D
    end
    classDef satLiquid fill:#a8d5e2,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef satVapor fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#993300,stroke-width:2px
    classDef critical fill:#ff6666,stroke:#660000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef triple fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000066,stroke-width:2px
    class A critical
    class B satLiquid
    class C satVapor
    class G triple
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow: Property Lookup for Saturated Liquid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Given State is SATURATED LIQUID"] --> B{"Input: Pressure P or Temperature T?"}
    B -- "Given P" --> C["Step 1: Find T_sat from Saturation Pressure Table"]
    B -- "Given T" --> D["Step 1: Find P_sat from Saturation Temperature Table"]
    C --> E["Step 2: Read v_f, u_f, h_f, s_f, g_f at T_sat"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Step 3: Quality x is automatically = 0"]
    F --> G["Step 4: If needed, compute v, h, s using y = y_f + x*y_fg = y_f"]
    G --> H["Output: All thermodynamic properties determined"]
    H --> I["End: Property Report"]
    classDef startNode fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef endNode fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef procNode fill:#fffacd,stroke:#8b8b00,stroke-width:1px
    class A startNode
    class I endNode
    class B,C,D,E,F,G,H procNode
```

### 4.3 Energy Pathway: Heating Saturated Liquid to Saturated Vapor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Saturated Liquid\nx = 0\nh = h_f"] -- "Heat q_in = h_fg" --> B["Saturated Vapor\nx = 1\nh = h_g"]
    A --> C["Subcooled Liquid\nT < T_sat"]
    A --> D["Saturated Mixture\n0 < x < 1"]
    C -- "Sensible heat" --> A
    D -- "Continued heating" --> B
    classDef stateNode fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px
    class A,B,C,D stateNode
```

### 4.4 Process Block Diagram: Real-World Steam Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Condenser Outlet:\nSaturated Liquid x=0"] --> P2["Pump:\nSubcooled Liquid"]
    P2 --> P3["Boiler Inlet:\nCompressed Liquid"]
    P3 --> P4["Boiler:\nSaturated Liquid to Vapor"]
    P4 --> P5["Turbine Inlet:\nSuperheated Vapor"]
    P5 --> P6["Turbine Outlet:\nLow-P Wet Vapor"]
    P6 --> P7["Condenser:\nSaturated Liquid x=0"]
    P7 --> P1
    classDef satLiq fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    class P1 satLiq
    class P7 satLiq
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Engineering Note:** The block diagram above is a fallback for physical schematic representation. In an actual thermodynamic $T-v$ or $h-s$ (Mollier) diagram, the saturated liquid line is the left curved boundary of the vapor dome, rising from the triple point to the critical point.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define a *saturated liquid* and clearly distinguish it from a *compressed (subcooled) liquid*. State the quality value for a saturated liquid.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **saturated liquid** is the liquid state of a pure substance at its saturation temperature and pressure, existing on the verge of vaporization. At this state, the quality is $x = 0$, and any further heat addition initiates phase change.

| Aspect | Saturated Liquid | Compressed (Subcooled) Liquid |
|--------|------------------|-------------------------------|
| Temperature | $T = T_{sat}$ at the given $P$ | $T < T_{sat}$ at the given $P$ |
| Phase Status | Ready to vaporize | Not ready to vaporize |
| Quality | $x = 0$ | Not applicable (single phase) |

**Quality of saturated liquid = 0** (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for quality value).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

For water at **150 °C**, determine the saturation pressure, specific volume of saturated liquid, and specific enthalpy of saturated liquid.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

From saturated water table at $T = 150 \text{ °C}$:

$$P_{sat} = 475.8 \text{ kPa}$$
$$v_f = 0.001091 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$
$$h_f = 631.68 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

(1 mark for $P_{sat}$, 1 mark for $v_f$, 1 mark for $h_f$)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** A rigid tank contains **50 kg** of saturated liquid water at **90 °C**. Determine:
   (i) The pressure inside the tank
   (ii) The total volume of the tank
   (iii) The total internal energy
   (iv) The total enthalpy **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Heat is now transferred to the water until it reaches a saturated vapor state. Determine:
   (i) The final temperature and pressure
   (ii) The total heat transferred
   (iii) The change in entropy of the water **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Initial Saturated Liquid State (7 Marks)

**Given:** Saturated liquid water, $m = 50 \text{ kg}$, $T = 90 \text{ °C}$

**From Saturated Water Table at $T = 90 \text{ °C}$:**

$$P_{sat} = 70.14 \text{ kPa}, \quad v_f = 0.001036 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$
$$u_f = 376.85 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_f = 376.92 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$s_f = 1.1925 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**(i) Pressure inside the tank (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{P_1 = P_{sat}(90°C) = 70.14 \text{ kPa}}$$

**(ii) Total volume of the tank (2 marks):**
$$V_1 = m \cdot v_f = 50 \times 0.001036$$
$$\boxed{V_1 = 0.0518 \text{ m}^3}$$

**(iii) Total internal energy (2 marks):**
$$U_1 = m \cdot u_f = 50 \times 376.85$$
$$\boxed{U_1 = 18,842.5 \text{ kJ}}$$

**(iv) Total enthalpy (2 marks):**
$$H_1 = m \cdot h_f = 50 \times 376.92$$
$$\boxed{H_1 = 18,846 \text{ kJ}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Final Saturated Vapor State (7 Marks)

**Process:** Constant volume heating (rigid tank) from State 1 to State 2.

**Specific volume remains constant:**
$$v_2 = v_1 = 0.001036 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

For saturated vapor at State 2, we need $v_g = 0.001036 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$. This is **below** the critical specific volume ($v_{cr} = 0.003106 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$), so the process crosses the **saturated vapor line** before reaching critical state.

The rigid tank process with $v = \text{const}$ intersects the saturated vapor line. Iterating on steam tables:

At $T_2 = 250 \text{ °C}$: $v_g = 0.050085 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ — too high.
At $T_2 = 170 \text{ °C}$: $v_g = 0.002504 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ — too high.
At $T_2 = 145 \text{ °C}$: $v_g = 0.001641 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ — too high.
At $T_2 = 135 \text{ °C}$: $v_g = 0.001393 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ — too high.
At $T_2 = 130 \text{ °C}$: $v_g = 0.001330 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ — still high.

By interpolation, this problem indicates that heating a **saturated liquid in a rigid tank** to saturated vapor is only possible if the specific volume is high enough. Since $v_f$ is too small, in practice the tank will reach the **critical point** first or approach supercritical conditions.

**Practical Conclusion:** In a rigid tank starting from saturated liquid, full conversion to saturated vapor at lower $T$ is generally **not possible** without first increasing the volume. The water will likely cross into the supercritical region or the tank will undergo severe pressure buildup.

**Alternate Interpretation:** If we assume the question intends a **constant pressure** process:

**(i) Final temperature and pressure (2 marks):**
$$T_2 = 90 \text{ °C}, \quad P_2 = 70.14 \text{ kPa}$$
(Constant pressure since saturation pressure corresponds to constant temperature for constant P process in saturation region)

**(ii) Total heat transferred (3 marks):**
At $90 \text{ °C}$: $h_g = 2663.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_f = 376.92 \text{ kJ/kg}$
$$Q = m \cdot (h_g - h_f) = 50 \times (2663.5 - 376.92)$$
$$\boxed{Q = 50 \times 2286.58 = 114,329 \text{ kJ}}$$

**(iii) Change in entropy (2 marks):**
At $90 \text{ °C}$: $s_g = 7.4793 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
$$\Delta S = m \cdot (s_g - s_f) = 50 \times (7.4793 - 1.1925)$$
$$\Delta S = 50 \times 6.2868$$
$$\boxed{\Delta S = 314.34 \text{ kJ/K}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Define the following terms with neat sketches on a $P-v$ diagram:
   (i) Saturated liquid
   (ii) Saturated vapor
   (iii) Saturated mixture
   (iv) Quality of mixture **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A piston-cylinder device contains **0.005 m³** of saturated liquid water at **200 kPa**. Heat is transferred to the water at constant pressure until the final temperature reaches **300 °C**. Determine:
   (i) The initial mass of water
   (ii) The final specific volume
   (iii) The total heat transferred
   (iv) The total work done by the water **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Definitions with P-v Diagram (7 Marks)

**Definitions (4 marks):**

**(i) Saturated Liquid:** The liquid state at the saturation temperature corresponding to the given pressure, on the verge of vaporizing.

**(ii) Saturated Vapor:** The vapor state at the saturation temperature corresponding to the given pressure, on the verge of condensing.

**(iii) Saturated Mixture:** A two-phase state where saturated liquid and saturated vapor coexist in thermodynamic equilibrium. The state lies under the vapor dome.

**(iv) Quality ($x$):** The mass fraction of vapor in a saturated mixture.
$$x = \frac{m_v}{m_f + m_v}$$

**Neat Sketch on P-v Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PvDia["P-v Diagram (Schematic)"]
        X["Pressure (P)"] --> Y["Volume (v)"]
    end
    A1["Critical Point C"] --> A2["Saturated Liquid Line"]
    A2 --> A3["Triple Point T"]
    A1 --> A4["Saturated Vapor Line"]
    A4 --> A3
    A2 --> A5["Region: Subcooled Liquid"]
    A2 --> A6["Region: Saturated Mixture (under dome)"]
    A4 --> A7["Region: Superheated Vapor"]
```

Description: The student must draw axes $P$ (vertical) and $v$ (horizontal), mark the triple point T, critical point C, and draw the vapor dome with saturated liquid line on the left and saturated vapor line on the right. Label all four regions.

---

#### Part (b) — Piston-Cylinder Process (7 Marks)

**Given:** $V_1 = 0.005 \text{ m}^3$, $P_1 = 200 \text{ kPa}$, $T_2 = 300 \text{ °C}$, constant pressure process.

**At P = 200 kPa (Saturated state):**
$$T_{sat} = 120.21 \text{ °C}, \quad v_f = 0.001061 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}, \quad h_f = 504.71 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(i) Initial mass of water (2 marks):**
$$m = \frac{V_1}{v_f} = \frac{0.005}{0.001061}$$
$$\boxed{m = 4.713 \text{ kg}}$$

**(ii) Final specific volume (2 marks):**

Since final $T_2 = 300 \text{ °C} > T_{sat} = 120.21 \text{ °C}$, the water is in the **superheated** state at $P = 200 \text{ kPa}$.

From superheated water table at $P = 200 \text{ kPa}$, $T = 300 \text{ °C}$:
$$v_2 = 1.3162 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$
$$\boxed{v_2 = 1.3162 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}}$$

**(iii) Total heat transferred (2 marks):**

From superheated table at $P = 200 \text{ kPa}$, $T = 300 \text{ °C}$:
$$h_2 = 3072.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q = m \cdot (h_2 - h_1) = 4.713 \times (3072.1 - 504.71)$$
$$Q = 4.713 \times 2567.39$$
$$\boxed{Q = 12,100.4 \text{ kJ}}$$

**(iv) Total work done by the water (1 mark):**

For constant pressure process:
$$W = P \cdot \Delta V = P \cdot m \cdot (v_2 - v_1)$$
$$W = 200 \times 4.713 \times (1.3162 - 0.001061)$$
$$W = 200 \times 4.713 \times 1.3151$$
$$\boxed{W = 1239.6 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Verification (1st Law for closed system):**
$$Q = \Delta U + W = m(u_2 - u_1) + W$$

This serves as an internal check; partial credit is awarded if attempted.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Saturated Liquid with Compressed Liquid:** Saturated liquid is at the saturation line ($T = T_{sat}$). Compressed liquid is INSIDE the subcooled region. Do not use compressed liquid tables unless the state is clearly subcooled.
> 2. **Forgetting $x = 0$:** Always explicitly state that the quality is zero for a saturated liquid — this shows conceptual clarity.
> 3. **Using Saturated Tables for Two-Phase Mixture:** If the problem says "saturated mixture," you must find the quality and use $y = y_f + x \cdot y_{fg}$. If the problem says "saturated liquid," quality is automatically zero.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistencies:** KTU examiners deduct marks for mixing kJ with J, m³/kg with cm³/g. Use SI units consistently.
> 5. **Skipping State Identification:** Always identify whether the state is subcooled, saturated, or superheated before reaching for any table.
> 6. **Sign Errors in Work:** Expansion work (system pushes boundary) is POSITIVE. Compression work is NEGATIVE. Use $W = P \cdot \Delta V$ for constant pressure processes carefully.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Saturated Liquid (Module 4):**
> - **Definition:** Liquid at $T_{sat}$ and $P_{sat}$, ready to vaporize.
> - **Quality:** $x = 0$ (always).
> - **Property subscript:** "$f$" (from Latin *fluidus* / *flüssig*).
> - **Reference tables:** Saturated Water Tables (Pressure-based or Temperature-based).
> - **Key properties at saturation:** $v_f$, $u_f$, $h_f$, $s_f$, $g_f$.
> - **Mixture formula:** $y = y_f + x \cdot y_{fg}$ — but with $x = 0$, this collapses to $y = y_f$.
> - **Critical constraint:** Saturated liquid cannot exist above the **critical point** (T > 373.95 °C for water).
> - **Compressed Liquid Approximation:** $h_{@P,T} \approx h_f(T) + v_f(T) \cdot (P - P_{sat}(T))$.
> - **Common exam trap:** Mistaking saturated liquid ($T = T_{sat}$) for subcooled liquid ($T < T_{sat}$).
> - **Incompressibility:** Treat $v_f$ as roughly constant for moderate pressure variations.
> - **Phase rule (Gibbs):** For a pure substance in two-phase equilibrium, $F = 1$ — only one intensive property is independent.
> - **Clapeyron slope:** $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$ — useful for understanding saturation curve behavior.
> - **Vapor dome location:** Saturated liquid line is the LEFT branch of the vapor dome on any P-v, T-v, or T-s diagram.
> - **Real-world sighting:** Condenser outlets in power plants deliver saturated liquid refrigerant/water.
> - **Boiler feedwater:** Ideally enters as saturated liquid (or subcooled) for maximum heat absorption efficiency.
> - **Latent heat peak:** $h_{fg}$ is maximum at lower pressures and decreases to zero at the critical point.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saturated Vapor and Superheated Vapor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Saturated Vapor and Superheated Vapor ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition In **Engineering Thermodynamics**, a substance undergoing a phase change between liquid and vapor states passes through three distinct regions on its **Pressure-Temperature (P-T)** and **Pressure-Specific Volume (P-v)** diagrams: 1. **Saturated Liquid State** – The state at which a liquid is about to begin vaporization; both liquid and vapor phases coexist in thermal equilibrium at the saturation temperature $T_{sat}$ for a given pressure. 2. **Saturated Vapor State** – The state at which vapor is about to begin condensation; it is the dry vapor existing in equilibrium with its liquid at $T_{sat}$. 3. **Saturated Liquid–Vapor Mixture (Wet Vapor)** – A two-phase mixture of saturated liquid and saturated vapor coexisting at the saturation pressure and temperature. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A **Saturated Vapor** is a vapor that is in equilibrium with its liquid at the same pressure and temperature. A **Superheated Vapor** is a vapor whose temperature is raised above the saturation temperature at a given pressure, i.e., $T > T_{sat}$ at constant $P$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition) Imagine boiling water in a pressure cooker: - The **saturated liquid** is the water at exactly $100^\circ C$ (at atmospheric pressure), just before the first bubble appears. - The **saturated vapor** is the steam that exists at exactly $100^\circ C$, fully formed but still at the verge of condensing back to water. - The **wet vapor (saturated mixture)** is the bubbling mixture inside the cooker — both phases visible together. - The **superheated vapor** is the steam that leaves the cooker and is heated further (e.g., in a superheater of a thermal power plant) to a temperature **above** $100^\circ C$ while still at atmospheric pressure. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** If you heat a saturated vapor further **without** changing its pressure, the temperature rises beyond $T_{sat}$. This is the **superheated region** — the working domain of all steam turbines in Rankine cycles. ### 1.3 Quality of Vapor (Dryness Fraction) The **Quality**, denoted $x$, is defined as the ratio of the mass of vapor to the total mass of the mixture: $$x = \frac{m_g}{m_f + m_g} = \frac{m_g}{m_{total}}$$ Where: - $m_f$ = mass of saturated liquid (kg) - $m_g$ = mass of saturated vapor (kg) - $m_{total}$ = total mass of the mixture (kg) **Range:** $0 \leq x \leq 1$ - $x = 0$ → Saturated liquid - $x = 1$ → Saturated vapor - $0 < x < 1$ → Saturated liquid–vapor mixture ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework #### A. The Saturation State At a given pressure $P$, the saturation temperature $T_{sat}$ is fixed. A pure substance cannot exist as a mixture of liquid and vapor at any other temperature for that pressure. The locus of all such points is the **saturation curve** (or vapor-pressure curve) on the P-T diagram. #### B. Property Evaluation in the Saturated Mixture Region Any **extensive specific property** $\psi$ (such as $v$, $u$, $h$, $s$) of a saturated liquid–vapor mixture is obtained by the **lever rule** (additive property weighted by mass fraction): $$\psi = (1 - x)\,\psi_f + x\,\psi_g$$ Rearranging for KTU-style problems: $$\psi = \psi_f + x\,(\psi_g - \psi_f) = \psi_f + x\,\psi_{fg}$$ #### C. Superheated Region When $T > T_{sat}$ at a given $P$, the vapor is **superheated**. The state lies in the region to the right of the saturation curve on the T-v diagram, and above the vapor curve on the P-v diagram. Properties are obtained directly from **superheated steam tables** (interpolation may be required for non-tabulated temperatures). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Property | Formula | Units | Condition | |---|----------|---------|-------|-----------| | 1 | Quality | $x = \dfrac{m_g}{m_f + m_g}$ | dimensionless | $0 \leq x \leq 1$ | | 2 | Specific Volume (mixture) | $v = v_f + x\,v_{fg}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ | saturated mixture | | 3 | Internal Energy (mixture) | $u = u_f + x\,u_{fg}$ | $\text{kJ}/\text{kg}$ | saturated mixture | | 4 | Enthalpy (mixture) | $h = h_f + x\,h_{fg}$ | $\text{kJ}/\text{kg}$ | saturated mixture | | 5 | Entropy (mixture) | $s = s_f + x\,s_{fg}$ | $\text{kJ}/(\text{kg}\cdot\text{K})$ | saturated mixture | | 6 | Mass of Liquid | $m_f = (1 - x)\,m$ | kg | mixture | | 7 | Mass of Vapor | $m_g = x\,m$ | kg | mixture | | 8 | Volume of Vapor | $V_g = x\,m\,v_g$ | $\text{m}^3$ | mixture | | 9 | Volume of Liquid | $V_f = (1 - x)\,m\,v_f$ | $\text{m}^3$ | mixture | | 10 | Superheat | $\Delta T_{sh} = T - T_{sat}$ | $^\circ C$ or K | superheated state | > [!NOTE] > **Subscript Convention (KTU Standard):** > - $f$ → saturated **liquid** property > - $g$ → saturated **vapor** property > - $fg$ → **difference** $g - f$ > - $1, 2, 3, \dots$ → property at a given state ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Thermal Power Plants:** Boiler steam leaving the turbine inlet is **superheated** to prevent erosion of blades by water droplets and to increase Rankine cycle efficiency. - **Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning:** The refrigerant cycles between saturated states (evaporator and condenser) — quality plays a key role in determining compressor superheat. - **Petroleum Industry:** Distillation columns operate with saturated liquid–vapor mixtures; quality determines separation efficiency. - **Cryogenics:** Liquefied gases (LNG, LOX) are stored near saturated states; small heat inleakage causes vaporization governed by these equations. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Specific Property of a Saturated Liquid–Vapor Mixture Let the total mass of the mixture be $m = m_f + m_g$ (kg). For any **extensive property** $\Psi$ (total volume, total internal energy, total enthalpy, total entropy, etc.): $$\Psi = \Psi_f + \Psi_g$$ where $\Psi_f$ is the property possessed by the liquid portion and $\Psi_g$ by the vapor portion. Dividing throughout by the total mass $m$ to obtain the **specific** property $\psi$: $$\psi = \frac{\Psi_f + \Psi_g}{m}$$ Step 1 — Express each part as mass times its specific value: $$\psi = \frac{m_f \psi_f + m_g \psi_g}{m}$$ Step 2 — Substitute $m_f = (1 - x)\,m$ and $m_g = x\,m$: $$\psi = \frac{(1 - x)\,m \cdot \psi_f + x\,m \cdot \psi_g}{m}$$ Step 3 — Cancel $m$: $$\psi = (1 - x)\,\psi_f + x\,\psi_g$$ Step 4 — Expand and group terms: $$\psi = \psi_f - x\,\psi_f + x\,\psi_g = \psi_f + x(\psi_g - \psi_f)$$ Step 5 — Define $\psi_{fg} = \psi_g - \psi_f$ (the change in property during vaporization): $$\boxed{\;\psi = \psi_f + x\,\psi_{fg}\;}$$ This is the **universal KTU formula** for any specific property in the wet region. ### 3.2 Derivation: Volume Occupied by Vapor in a Wet Mixture Given a rigid vessel of total volume $V$ containing a saturated liquid–vapor mixture of mass $m$: Total volume = Volume of liquid + Volume of vapor $$V = V_f + V_g$$ Step 1 — Using $V = m\,v$: $$m\,v = m_f\,v_f + m_g\,v_g$$ Step 2 — Substitute $m_f = (1-x)\,m$ and $m_g = x\,m$: $$m\,v = (1 - x)\,m\,v_f + x\,m\,v_g$$ Step 3 — Cancel $m$: $$v = (1 - x)\,v_f + x\,v_g = v_f + x\,v_{fg}$$ Step 4 — Volume of vapor: $$V_g = m_g\,v_g = (x\,m)\,v_g = x\,m\,v_g$$ Step 5 — Volume of liquid: $$V_f = (1 - x)\,m\,v_f$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 3-Mark Pattern) **Problem:** A vessel contains $5 \text{ kg}$ of a saturated water mixture at $200 \text{ kPa}$. The quality is $x = 0.8$. Determine: (a) The mass of vapor (b) The specific volume (c) The total enthalpy **Given (from steam tables at $P = 200 \text{ kPa}$):** $v_f = 0.001061 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$, $\quad v_g = 0.8857 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ $h_f = 504.7 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$, $\quad h_{fg} = 2201.9 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$ **Solution:** **(a) Mass of vapor:** $$m_g = x \cdot m = 0.8 \times 5 = 4.0 \text{ kg}$$ **(b) Specific volume:** $$v_{fg} = v_g - v_f = 0.8857 - 0.001061 = 0.884639 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ $$v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg} = 0.001061 + 0.8 \times 0.884639 = 0.708772 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **(c) Total enthalpy:** $$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} = 504.7 + 0.8 \times 2201.9 = 2266.22 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$$ $$H_{total} = m \cdot h = 5 \times 2266.22 = 11{,}331.1 \text{ kJ}$$ ### 3.4 Superheated Vapor — Direct Table Lookup **Problem:** Steam at $P = 1 \text{ MPa}$ and $T = 300^\circ C$ (superheated since $T_{sat} @ 1 \text{ MPa} = 179.91^\circ C$). **Superheated Steam Table Reading:** | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | $v$ | $0.25799 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ | | $u$ | $2726.2 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$ | | $h$ | $2984.2 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$ | | $s$ | $6.5966 \text{ kJ}/(\text{kg}\cdot\text{K})$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Convention:** Always state the degree of superheat, $\Delta T_{sh} = T - T_{sat}$, in your final answer. Here $\Delta T_{sh} = 300 - 179.91 = 120.09^\circ C$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 P-v Diagram — Phase Regions of a Pure Substance ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["CRITICAL POINT"] CP["C : Critical Point
Pc = 22.09 MPa, Tc = 374.14 C"] end subgraph B["COMPRESSED LIQUID REGION"] CL["T = Tsat at P greater than Psat
Sub-cooled Liquid"] end subgraph C["SATURATED LIQUID-VAPOR MIXTURE"] SM["WET VAPOR / TWO-PHASE
Quality x between 0 and 1
T and P are dependent"] end subgraph D["SUPERHEATED VAPOR REGION"] SH["T greater than Tsat at given P
Steam Tables for superheated region
Degree of Superheat Delta T"] end subgraph E["SATURATED LIQUID LINE"] SL["x = 0 : vf is small, vf increases with T"] end subgraph F["SATURATED VAPOR LINE"] SV["x = 1 : vg is large, vg decreases with T"] end CL -- "Heat addition Q" --> SM SM -- "Further heat addition" --> SH B --> A SM --> A SH --> A SL --> C SV --> C ``` ### 4.2 T-v Diagram — Constant Pressure Heating Process ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["State 1
Compressed Liquid
T less than Tsat"] -- "Subcooled heating" --> A2["State 2
Saturated Liquid
x = 0, T = Tsat"] A2 -- "Latent heat added hfg" --> A3["State 3
Saturated Mixture
0 less than x less than 1"] A3 -- "Sensible heat added" --> A4["State 4
Saturated Vapor
x = 1, T = Tsat"] A4 -- "Superheat added Delta h" --> A5["State 5
Superheated Vapor
T greater than Tsat"] style A1 fill:#cce5ff style A2 fill:#b3d9ff style A3 fill:#ffffcc style A4 fill:#ffcc99 style A5 fill:#ff9999 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Property Evaluation Flowchart for Saturated Mixture ```mermaid flowchart TB inp["INPUT DATA
Pressure P or Temperature T
Quality x OR mass m_f and m_g"] --> check{"Phase Region?"} check -- "Sub-cooled Liquid" --> sc["Use Compressed Liquid Table
OR v approx vf, h approx hf"] check -- "Saturated Liquid x=0" --> sl["Read vf, uf, hf, sf directly from saturation table"] check -- "Saturated Mixture 0 less than x less than 1" --> sm["psi = psif + x times psifg
Compute v, u, h, s"] check -- "Saturated Vapor x=1" --> sv["Read vg, ug, hg, sg directly from saturation table"] check -- "Superheated T greater than Tsat" --> sh["Read directly from superheated table
At given P and T"] sc --> out["OUTPUT: Required Property"] sl --> out sm --> out sv --> out sh --> out style check fill:#ffe066 style out fill:#90ee90 ``` ### 4.4 Schematic — Identification of State on P-v Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB Paxis["PRESSURE P axis: vertical, log scale"] --> dome vaxis["SPECIFIC VOLUME v axis: horizontal"] --> dome dome["DOME: Bounded by saturated liquid line left and saturated vapor line right, meeting at critical point C"] Paxis --- zone1 Paxis --- zone2 Paxis --- zone3 zone1["Region 1: Compressed Liquid (left of dome)"] zone2["Region 2: Inside the dome (Saturated Mixture)"] zone3["Region 3: Superheated Vapor (right of dome)"] note["At constant P: Heating moves horizontally L to R
At constant v: Heating moves vertically bottom to top"] style dome fill:#ffcccc style zone1 fill:#cce5ff style zone2 fill:#ffffcc style zone3 fill:#ff9999 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** Define the terms: (i) Saturated Liquid, (ii) Saturated Vapor, (iii) Quality of mixture. A vessel contains $2 \text{ kg}$ of water and $3 \text{ kg}$ of steam in equilibrium. Find the quality of the mixture. **Model Answer:** - **Saturated Liquid:** The liquid state at which vaporization is about to begin at a given pressure. - **Saturated Vapor:** The vapor state at which condensation is about to begin at a given pressure. - **Quality (x):** Ratio of mass of vapor to total mass of the mixture. $$x = \frac{m_g}{m_f + m_g} = \frac{3}{2 + 3} = 0.6$$ **$\boxed{x = 0.6}$** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** Differentiate between saturated vapor and superheated vapor. Why is steam superheated in a thermal power plant before entering the turbine? **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Saturated Vapor | Superheated Vapor | |-----------|----------------|-------------------| | Temperature | $T = T_{sat}$ at given P | $T > T_{sat}$ at given P | | Phase | In equilibrium with liquid | Single phase (vapor only) | | Latent heat | Contains latent heat $h_{fg}$ | No latent heat; only sensible heat | | Table used | Saturation tables | Superheated steam tables | **Why superheat in a thermal power plant?** To (i) increase the **thermal efficiency** of the Rankine cycle, (ii) avoid **erosion of turbine blades** by water droplet impingement, and (iii) reduce the **moisture content** at turbine exhaust. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]` **A rigid vessel of volume $0.4 \text{ m}^3$ contains a saturated mixture of steam at $200 \text{ kPa}$. The quality of steam is $0.6$. Determine:** **(a)** The mass of liquid, mass of vapor, and total mass of the mixture. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The total internal energy, total enthalpy, and total entropy of the mixture. **(7 Marks)** **Given (Steam Tables at $P = 200 \text{ kPa}$):** | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | $v_f$ | $0.001061 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ | | $v_g$ | $0.8857 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ | | $u_f$ | $504.5 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$ | | $u_g$ | $2529.5 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$ | | $h_f$ | $504.7 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$ | | $h_{fg}$ | $2201.9 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$ | | $s_f$ | $1.5301 \text{ kJ}/(\text{kg}\cdot\text{K})$ | | $s_{fg}$ | $5.5970 \text{ kJ}/(\text{kg}\cdot\text{K})$ | **Model Solution:** **Part (a): Mass Calculation** Step 1 — Specific volume of the mixture: $$v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$$ $$v_{fg} = v_g - v_f = 0.8857 - 0.001061 = 0.884639 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ $$v = 0.001061 + 0.6 \times 0.884639 = 0.001061 + 0.530783 = 0.531844 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ Step 2 — Total mass of mixture: $$m = \frac{V}{v} = \frac{0.4}{0.531844} = 0.7521 \text{ kg}$$ Step 3 — Mass of vapor: $$m_g = x \cdot m = 0.6 \times 0.7521 = 0.4513 \text{ kg}$$ Step 4 — Mass of liquid: $$m_f = (1 - x) \cdot m = 0.4 \times 0.7521 = 0.3008 \text{ kg}$$ **Part (b): Total Energy, Enthalpy, and Entropy** Step 5 — Specific internal energy: $$u_{fg} = u_g - u_f = 2529.5 - 504.5 = 2025.0 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$$ $$u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg} = 504.5 + 0.6 \times 2025.0 = 504.5 + 1215.0 = 1719.5 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$$ Step 6 — Total internal energy: $$U = m \cdot u = 0.7521 \times 1719.5 = 1293.2 \text{ kJ}$$ Step 7 — Specific enthalpy: $$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} = 504.7 + 0.6 \times 2201.9 = 504.7 + 1321.14 = 1825.84 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$$ Step 8 — Total enthalpy: $$H = m \cdot h = 0.7521 \times 1825.84 = 1373.1 \text{ kJ}$$ Step 9 — Specific entropy: $$s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg} = 1.5301 + 0.6 \times 5.5970 = 1.5301 + 3.3582 = 4.8883 \text{ kJ}/(\text{kg}\cdot\text{K})$$ Step 10 — Total entropy: $$S = m \cdot s = 0.7521 \times 4.8883 = 3.6765 \text{ kJ}/\text{K}$$ **Final Answers:** $$\boxed{m_f = 0.3008 \text{ kg},\quad m_g = 0.4513 \text{ kg},\quad m = 0.7521 \text{ kg}}$$ $$\boxed{U = 1293.2 \text{ kJ},\quad H = 1373.1 \text{ kJ},\quad S = 3.6765 \text{ kJ}/\text{K}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **Steam enters a superheater at $P = 3 \text{ MPa}$ as a saturated vapor. It leaves the superheater at the same pressure with a temperature of $400^\circ C$. The mass flow rate is $5 \text{ kg/s}$. Determine:** **(a)** The degree of superheat and the specific volume of the superheated steam at exit. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The rate of heat transfer to the steam in the superheater (in kW). **(7 Marks)** **Given:** At $P = 3 \text{ MPa}$, $T_{sat} = 233.90^\circ C$ $h_g @ 3 \text{ MPa} = 2804.2 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$ $h @ 3 \text{ MPa}, 400^\circ C = 3231.7 \text{ kJ}/\text{kg}$ **Model Solution:** **Part (a): Degree of Superheat and Specific Volume** Step 1 — Degree of superheat: $$\Delta T_{sh} = T_{exit} - T_{sat} = 400 - 233.90 = 166.1^\circ C$$ Step 2 — From superheated steam table at $3 \text{ MPa}, 400^\circ C$: $$v = 0.09938 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Part (b): Rate of Heat Transfer** Step 3 — Apply steady-flow energy equation (neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes, no work): $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$$ Step 4 — Substitute values: $$\dot{Q} = 5 \times (3231.7 - 2804.2) = 5 \times 427.5 = 2137.5 \text{ kW}$$ **Final Answers:** $$\boxed{\Delta T_{sh} = 166.1^\circ C,\quad v = 0.09938 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{Q} = 2137.5 \text{ kW}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mark Loss Alerts:** > 1. **Confusing $v_{fg}$ with $v_g$** — Students often write $v = x \cdot v_g$ instead of $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$. This is worth **2 marks lost** instantly. > 2. **Forgetting the dryness fraction in enthalpy** — Always use $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$, NEVER $h = x \cdot h_g$. > 3. **Wrong table usage** — Mixing saturation table data with superheated table data in a single problem leads to a **3-mark penalty** for inconsistency. > 4. **Not stating units** in final answer — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit** (rounded off). > 5. **Sign convention for heat transfer** — Forgetting that $\dot{Q}$ is positive when added to the system costs **1 mark**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Saturated & Superheated Vapor** - ✅ **Saturated Liquid ($x = 0$):** State at which liquid begins to vaporize. Properties read directly from saturation table. - ✅ **Saturated Vapor ($x = 1$):** State at which vapor begins to condense. Properties read directly from saturation table. - ✅ **Saturated Mixture ($0 < x < 1$):** Use **lever rule**: $\psi = \psi_f + x \cdot \psi_{fg}$. - ✅ **Superheated Vapor ($T > T_{sat}$):** Properties from superheated steam tables. - ✅ **Quality Formula:** $x = \dfrac{m_g}{m_f + m_g}$; **range** $0$ to $1$. - ✅ **Specific Volume Difference:** $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ (NOT $v_f$ itself). - ✅ **Degree of Superheat:** $\Delta T_{sh} = T - T_{sat}$ at constant pressure. - ✅ **Critical Point:** Beyond this, no distinction exists between liquid and vapor phases ($P_c$, $T_c$). - ✅ **Per-Mass vs Total Property:** $\Psi_{total} = m \cdot \psi_{specific}$. - ✅ **Units:** $v$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$; $u, h$ in $\text{kJ}/\text{kg}$; $s$ in $\text{kJ}/(\text{kg}\cdot\text{K})$. - ✅ **Practical Applications:** Steam turbines (superheat), refrigeration cycles (saturation), distillation (wet vapor).

topics.title: Triple Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Triple Point

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Triple Point** of a pure substance is defined as the unique thermodynamic state (characterized by a single, invariant temperature and a single, invariant pressure) at which the **three phases — Solid, Liquid, and Gas (Vapour)** — coexist simultaneously in **thermodynamic equilibrium**.

At the triple point:
- The rate of melting equals the rate of freezing.
- The rate of vaporization equals the rate of condensation.
- The rate of sublimation equals the rate of deposition.

Mathematically, it is a single point $(T_{tp}, P_{tp})$ on the **Pressure-Temperature (P-T) diagram** where the three phase-boundary curves (fusion curve, vaporization curve, and sublimation curve) intersect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The triple point is a **thermodynamic invariant point** — both temperature AND pressure are uniquely fixed for a given pure substance. There is **only one triple point** for any pure substance (as opposed to multiple melting/freezing points depending on conditions).

## 1.2 The Triple Point of Water (International Standard)

For **water (H₂O)**, the triple point is internationally adopted as the fundamental reference for the **Kelvin temperature scale**.

$$T_{tp,\text{water}} = \mathbf{273.16\ K\ (exactly)}$$

$$P_{tp,\text{water}} = \mathbf{611.657\ Pa\ (\approx 0.006112\ bar)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The value **273.16 K** is the defining fixed point of the **International Temperature Scale of 1990 (ITS-90)**. 1 K is defined as **1/273.16** of the triple point temperature of water. This is why the triple point is conceptually and metrologically critical.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Way Intersection"

Imagine a busy three-way road intersection (like a Y-junction in Kochi city traffic):

- **Road 1 (Solid)** → frozen ice cubes
- **Road 2 (Liquid)** → flowing water
- **Road 3 (Gas)** → water vapor / steam

The **triple point is the exact intersection** where all three roads meet. At that single intersection:
- Ice is melting into water **at exactly the same rate** water is freezing into ice.
- Water is evaporating into vapor **at exactly the same rate** vapor is condensing into water.
- Ice is sublimating into vapor **at exactly the same rate** vapor is depositing into ice.

If you move even slightly off the intersection — change temperature **or** pressure — one of the three phases will dominate and the other two will vanish. It is a delicate, knife-edge equilibrium.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:** *“Triple = Three phases meet at one Point — like three rivers meeting at a single confluence.”*

## 1.4 Triple Points of Common Substances (Reference Data)

| Substance | Triple Point Temperature $T_{tp}$ | Triple Point Pressure $P_{tp}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Water (H₂O)** | 273.16 K (0.01 °C) | 611.657 Pa |
| **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** | 216.55 K (−56.6 °C) | 518 kPa (5.18 bar) |
| **Ammonia (NH₃)** | 195.40 K | 6.076 kPa |
| **Nitrogen (N₂)** | 63.18 K | 12.5 kPa |
| **Mercury (Hg)** | 234.3 K | 1.65 × 10⁻⁷ Pa |
| **Sulphur (S)** | 1156 K | 117 kPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students confuse **boiling point** with **triple point**. Boiling point occurs at 1 atm (101.325 kPa) where liquid ⇌ vapour. Triple point is at a *different* (usually much lower) pressure where **all three** phases coexist. For water: boiling point = 373.15 K @ 101.325 kPa ≠ triple point = 273.16 K @ 611.657 Pa.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Principle — Gibbs Phase Rule

The number of degrees of freedom at the triple point is determined by the **Gibbs Phase Rule** (formulated by J. Willard Gibbs, 1876):

$$F = C - P + 2$$

Where:
- $F$ = Number of independent intensive variables (degrees of freedom), e.g., $T$ and $P$
- $C$ = Number of chemically independent components in the system
- $P$ = Number of coexisting phases in equilibrium
- The constant **2** represents the two intensive variables most commonly used in a simple compressible system: **temperature ($T$)** and **pressure ($P$)**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown:

1. **A pure substance** → $C = 1$ (only one chemical component).
2. **At the triple point** → $P = 3$ (solid + liquid + gas coexist simultaneously).
3. **Substituting** into Gibbs Phase Rule:

$$F = 1 - 3 + 2 = \mathbf{0}$$

4. **Result: $F = 0$ (Invariant System).** This means **NEITHER $T$ NOR $P$ can be varied independently**. Both are uniquely fixed.

## 2.2 Phase Boundary Curves Meeting at the Triple Point

The triple point is the geometric convergence of three distinct phase-equilibrium curves:

| Boundary | Coexisting Phases | Slope (Clausius-Clapeyron) |
|---|---|---|
| **Sublimation Curve** | Solid ⇌ Vapour | $dP/dT = \Delta H_{sub} / (T \cdot \Delta V_{sub})$ |
| **Vaporization Curve** | Liquid ⇌ Vapour | $dP/dT = \Delta H_{vap} / (T \cdot \Delta V_{vap})$ |
| **Fusion Curve** | Solid ⇌ Liquid | $dP/dT = \Delta H_{fus} / (T \cdot \Delta V_{fus})$ |

> For **water**, the fusion curve has a **negative slope** ($dP/dT < 0$) because ice is less dense than liquid water ($\Delta V_{fus} < 0$, since $\Delta H_{fus} > 0$). This is why ice skating works and why a pressure cooker cannot easily melt ice.

## 2.3 Degrees of Freedom — Complete Comparative Analysis

| Region on P-T Diagram | Phases Present ($P$) | $F = C - P + 2$ | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single-phase region (e.g., solid only) | 1 | $F = 1 - 1 + 2 = 2$ | **Bivariant** — both $T$ and $P$ can vary independently |
| Two-phase coexistence (e.g., S–L, L–V, S–V) | 2 | $F = 1 - 2 + 2 = 1$ | **Univariant** — only one of $T$ or $P$ can vary independently |
| **Triple Point** | **3** | $F = 1 - 3 + 2 = \mathbf{0}$ | **Invariant** — $T$ AND $P$ are fixed; zero degrees of freedom |
| Critical Point | Critical state | (Special — beyond simple phase rule) | Inflection point of vaporization curve |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Revision)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gibbs Phase Rule** | $F = C - P + 2$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Triple Point Invariance** | $F = 0$ for $C = 1$, $P = 3$ | $T_{tp}$, $P_{tp}$ are fixed |
| 3 | **Clausius-Clapeyron Equation** | $dP/dT = \Delta H / (T \Delta V)$ | Applicable on each phase boundary |
| 4 | **Clausius-Clapeyron (Integrated, ideal gas vapour)** | $\ln(P_2 / P_1) = -(\Delta H / R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ | Approximate for vapour phase |
| 5 | **Triple Point of Water** | $T_{tp} = 273.16\ \text{K}$ | Exact — defines the Kelvin scale |
| 6 | **Triple Point Pressure of Water** | $P_{tp} = 611.657\ \text{Pa}$ | $\approx 0.006112\ \text{bar}$ |
| 7 | **Triple Point Rule of Thumb** | $P_{tp} < P_{atm}$ for water | Always less than 1 atm |
| 8 | **Universal Gas Constant** | $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$ | Used in Clapeyron integrations |
| 9 | **Latent Heat Relation at TP** | $\Delta H_{sub} = \Delta H_{fus} + \Delta H_{vap}$ | Hess's law analogue at TP |

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

The triple point is not just a theoretical curiosity — it has deep engineering and metrological significance:

- **Temperature Scale Definition:** The SI definition of the **Kelvin** is based directly on the triple point of water.
- **Metrology:** Triple-point cells are used as primary calibration standards in national laboratories (NIST, NPL, NABL).
- **Vacuum Measurement:** McLeod gauge uses the triple point behaviour of mercury.
- **Food Preservation (Lyophilization / Freeze Drying):** Operates near the **sublimation curve** that originates at the triple point.
- **Refrigeration & Cryogenics:** Liquefied gases (N₂, O₂, H₂) are stored and transported at conditions that depend critically on the triple point.
- **Power Generation:** Steam tables are anchored to the triple point of water as a reference state.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Applying Gibbs Phase Rule at the Triple Point

We derive the degrees of freedom at the triple point of a pure substance rigorously.

### Step 1: Define the General System
Consider a system with $C$ independent chemical components distributed among $P$ coexisting phases. For each phase, we can specify its state using intensive variables.

### Step 2: Count Intensive Variables
For each phase, the intensive state is fully described by:
- $(C - 1)$ mole fractions (since mole fractions sum to 1)
- Temperature $T$
- Pressure $P$

So, variables per phase = $(C - 1) + 2 = C + 1$

Total variables across $P$ phases = $P(C + 1)$

### Step 3: Apply Equilibrium Constraints
At equilibrium:
- **Thermal equilibrium:** All phases share the same $T$ → gives $(P - 1)$ equations
- **Mechanical equilibrium:** All phases share the same $P$ → gives $(P - 1)$ equations
- **Chemical equilibrium:** Chemical potential $\mu_i$ of each component $i$ is equal across all phases → gives $C(P - 1)$ equations

Total constraint equations = $(P - 1) + (P - 1) + C(P - 1) = (P - 1)(C + 2)$

### Step 4: Compute Degrees of Freedom
Degrees of freedom = (Total variables) − (Total constraints)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= P(C + 1) - (P - 1)(C + 2) \\
&= PC + P - (PC + 2P - C - 2) \\
&= PC + P - PC - 2P + C + 2 \\
&= C - P + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (Gibbs Phase Rule):**

$$\boxed{F = C - P + 2}$$

### Step 5: Apply at the Triple Point
For a **pure substance** at the **triple point**:
- $C = 1$ (one component)
- $P = 3$ (three coexisting phases: solid, liquid, gas)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= C - P + 2 \\
&= 1 - 3 + 2 \\
&= \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Zero degrees of freedom means the system is **thermodynamically invariant**. Both temperature $T$ and pressure $P$ are completely determined and cannot be varied without disturbing the three-phase coexistence.

## 3.2 Derivation — Latent Heat Addition at Triple Point (Hess's Law)

The latent heats at the triple point are related because the enthalpy is a state function.

**Path 1 (Direct):** Solid → Vapour (Sublimation)
$$H_{vapour} - H_{solid} = \Delta H_{sub}$$

**Path 2 (Indirect):** Solid → Liquid → Vapour

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{vapour} - H_{solid} &= (H_{liquid} - H_{solid}) + (H_{vapour} - H_{liquid}) \\
\Delta H_{sub} &= \Delta H_{fus} + \Delta H_{vap}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta H_{sub} = \Delta H_{fus} + \Delta H_{vap}}
$$

This relation is **exact at the triple point** (it is a thermodynamic identity, valid for any pressure at the TP).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Triple Point vs Other Characteristic Points

| Property | Triple Point | Boiling Point (at 1 atm) | Critical Point | Melting Point (at 1 atm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Phases Coexisting** | Solid + Liquid + Gas | Liquid + Gas (only) | Critical (no distinction L/G) | Solid + Liquid (only) |
| **Value for Water — T** | 273.16 K | 373.15 K | 647.1 K | 273.15 K |
| **Value for Water — P** | 611.657 Pa | 101,325 Pa | 22.06 MPa | 101,325 Pa |
| **Degrees of Freedom $F$** | **0 (invariant)** | 1 (univariant) | Special (inflection) | 1 (univariant) |
| **Is it unique for substance?** | **Yes — exactly one** | No — depends on $P$ | Yes — exactly one | No — depends on $P$ |
| **Visual on P-T diagram** | Intersection of 3 curves | A point on vaporization curve | Endpoint of vaporization curve | A point on fusion curve |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For KTU Practice)

**Problem:** A closed rigid vessel initially contains liquid water at 280 K. The vessel is evacuated and the temperature is slowly reduced. At what temperature will the system pass through the triple point?

**Given:**
- Initial state: liquid water, $T_1 = 280$ K
- Vessel is evacuated → pressure is very low
- Triple point of water: $T_{tp} = 273.16$ K, $P_{tp} = 611.657$ Pa

**Solution Approach:**

Step 1: As temperature drops from 280 K to 273.16 K, the water remains liquid (we are above the freezing point at 1 atm, but pressure is now decreasing).

Step 2: As the temperature approaches 273.16 K and the (low) pressure approaches 611.657 Pa, the system will reach the triple point.

Step 3: At exactly $T = 273.16$ K and $P = 611.657$ Pa, **ice, water, and vapour coexist simultaneously**.

**Answer:** The system passes through the triple point at $T = \mathbf{273.16\ K}$ and $P = \mathbf{611.657\ Pa}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The triple point is uniquely defined by temperature alone for water (pressure is automatically determined). This is a direct consequence of $F = 0$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conceptual Architecture of Triple Point

This diagram illustrates how the three phase boundaries converge at the triple point on a P-T diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Pure Substance P-T System]) --> nodeC1[Single Solid Region]
    startA --> nodeC2[Single Liquid Region]
    startA --> nodeC3[Single Gas Region]

    nodeC1 -- Sublimation Curve --> tripleNode
    nodeC2 -- Vaporization Curve --> tripleNode
    nodeC3 -- Condensation Path --> tripleNode

    tripleNode{TP TRIPLE POINT F equals 0 Invariant}
    tripleNode -- Solid plus Vapour only --> sublimCurve[Sublimation Boundary]
    tripleNode -- Liquid plus Vapour only --> vapCurve[Vaporization Boundary]
    tripleNode -- Solid plus Liquid only --> fusionCurve[Fusion Boundary]

    nodeC1 -- Fusion Curve --> fusionCurve
    nodeC2 -- Fusion Curve --> fusionCurve
    nodeC3 -- Vaporization Curve --> vapCurve

    fusionCurve --> critNode[Critical Point Endpoint]
    vapCurve --> critNode
```
