```mermaid
    critNode --> supRegion[Supercritical Region No Phase Boundary]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Flow of Phase Equilibrium at Triple Point

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph phase1 [Solid Phase]
        dir1[Solid H2O Ice]
    end

    subgraph phase2 [Liquid Phase]
        dir2[Liquid H2O Water]
    end

    subgraph phase3 [Vapour Phase]
        dir3[Vapour H2O Steam]
    end

    phase1 -- Melting equal Freezing Rate --> phase2
    phase2 -- Vaporization equal Condensation Rate --> phase3
    phase3 -- Deposition equal Sublimation Rate --> phase1

    tripleNode2[(Triple Point T 273.16 K P 611.657 Pa F 0)]
    phase1 --> tripleNode2
    phase2 --> tripleNode2
    phase3 --> tripleNode2

    tripleNode2 --> resultA[Dynamic Equilibrium Maintained]
    tripleNode2 --> resultB[No Degree of Freedom for T or P]
    tripleNode2 --> resultC[Defines the Kelvin SI Unit]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual P-T Diagram Schematic (ASCII Representation)

```
PRESSURE (P)
   │
   │   ╔═══════════════════ SOLID ═══════════════════╗
   │   ║                                              ║
   │   ║   SUBLIMATION            FUSION              ║
   │   ║   CURVE                  CURVE               ║
   │   ║      \                   /                   ║
   │   ║       \                 /  (negative slope   ║
   │   ║        \               /    for water)      ║
   │   ║         \             /                     ║
   │   ║          \           /                       ║
   │   ║           \         /                        ║
   P_tp ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ •←─────/─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─  ║
   │   ║             \     /                         ║
   │   ║              \   /   VAPORIZATION CURVE      ║
   │   ║               \ /                           ║
   │   ║                X  ← CRITICAL POINT           ║
   │   ╚══════════════════════════════════════════════╝
   │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────► TEMPERATURE (T)
                    T_tp                  T_critical
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **single dot** at $(T_{tp}, P_{tp})$ is where **three curves meet**.
- Moving up/right along any one curve → only **two** phases coexist.
- Moving into any of the three regions → only **one** phase is stable.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Drawing Exam:** Always draw the triple point as a clear intersection point, label all three curves, and indicate the phase regions. Examiners award marks for a **neat, well-labelled** P-T diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the term "Triple Point" of a pure substance. State the triple point temperature and pressure of water.**

**Model Answer:**

The **triple point** of a pure substance is the unique thermodynamic state at which the solid, liquid, and vapour phases coexist in **thermodynamic equilibrium**.

For **water**:
- $T_{tp} = \mathbf{273.16\ K}$ (i.e., $0.01\ ^\circ\text{C}$)
- $P_{tp} = \mathbf{611.657\ Pa}$ (i.e., $\approx 0.006112\ \text{bar}$)

This temperature defines the **Kelvin scale** internationally. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for values)*

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**State Gibbs Phase Rule. What is the degree of freedom at the triple point of a pure substance?**

**Model Answer:**

**Gibbs Phase Rule:** $F = C - P + 2$, where $F$ = degrees of freedom, $C$ = number of components, $P$ = number of phases.

For a **pure substance** at the **triple point**:
- $C = 1$ (one component)
- $P = 3$ (three phases)
- $F = 1 - 3 + 2 = \mathbf{0}$ (zero degrees of freedom — invariant system)

This means both $T$ and $P$ are uniquely fixed at the triple point. *(3 marks: 1 for rule, 2 for application)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **forget to state the value of $C$ and $P$ explicitly** before substituting into Gibbs Phase Rule → loses 2 marks.
> - Students confuse **degrees of freedom with intensive variables** — $F$ counts **independently variable** intensive properties, not all of them.
> - On P-T diagram questions, students often **fail to label the three phase regions** and the **three boundary curves** → loses 3–4 marks.
> - Always write the **full numerical value with units** for triple point of water; do not write "273 K" alone.

---

### Question B1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

#### **Option A:**

**(a) Explain the concept of Triple Point with a neat labelled P-T diagram. Show all three phase regions and three phase-boundary curves. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The triple point is a unique thermodynamic state of a pure substance at which the solid, liquid, and vapour phases coexist simultaneously in equilibrium. On a **Pressure-Temperature (P-T) diagram**, it is represented as a single point $(T_{tp}, P_{tp})$ where three phase-boundary curves intersect.

**The three curves are:**

1. **Sublimation curve** — separates solid from vapour (below triple point pressure)
2. **Vaporization curve** — separates liquid from vapour (above triple point pressure, up to critical point)
3. **Fusion curve** — separates solid from liquid (above triple point pressure)

**The three regions** are: solid region (high P, low T), liquid region (moderate P and T), and vapour region (low P).

**For water:** $T_{tp} = 273.16$ K and $P_{tp} = 611.657$ Pa.

The fusion curve for water has a **negative slope** because ice is less dense than liquid water.

*(7 marks: 2 for explanation, 3 for diagram, 1 for water values, 1 for fusion curve note)*

**(b) Using Gibbs Phase Rule, show that the triple point of a pure substance has zero degrees of freedom. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** State Gibbs Phase Rule:
$$F = C - P + 2$$

**Step 2:** Identify values at the triple point:
- Number of components $C = 1$ (pure substance)
- Number of coexisting phases $P = 3$ (solid + liquid + vapour)

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= C - P + 2 \\
&= 1 - 3 + 2 \\
&= \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation:
- $F = 0$ means the system is **thermodynamically invariant**.
- Both temperature $T$ and pressure $P$ are uniquely fixed for a given pure substance.
- Neither $T$ nor $P$ can be varied independently without destroying the three-phase coexistence.

**Step 5:** Significance:
This invariance is why the triple point of water ($T = 273.16$ K) is chosen as the **defining reference point** of the International Temperature Scale (ITS-90).

*(7 marks: 1 for rule, 1 for identifying C and P, 1 for substitution, 2 for result, 2 for interpretation)*

---

#### **Option B:**

**(a) Derive Gibbs Phase Rule for a non-reactive multi-component, multi-phase system. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Intensive Variables:**
For each of the $P$ phases, the intensive state is specified by:
- Temperature $T$ (same for all phases due to thermal equilibrium)
- Pressure $P$ (same for all phases due to mechanical equilibrium)
- $(C - 1)$ independent mole fractions (since mole fractions sum to 1)

Total variables per phase: $(C - 1) + 2 = C + 1$
Total variables for $P$ phases: $P(C + 1)$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium Constraint Equations:**

- **Thermal equilibrium:** $(P - 1)$ equations (all phases at same $T$)
- **Mechanical equilibrium:** $(P - 1)$ equations (all phases at same $P$)
- **Chemical equilibrium:** Chemical potential $\mu_i^{(1)} = \mu_i^{(2)} = \dots = \mu_i^{(P)}$ gives $C(P - 1)$ equations

Total constraints: $(P - 1) + (P - 1) + C(P - 1) = (P - 1)(C + 2)$

**Step 3 — Degrees of Freedom:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \text{Total Variables} - \text{Total Constraints} \\
&= P(C + 1) - (P - 1)(C + 2) \\
&= PC + P - PC - 2P + C + 2 \\
&= C - P + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{F = C - P + 2}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for variables, 2 for constraints, 2 for algebra, 1 for final result)*

**(b) Compare Triple Point and Critical Point of water in terms of temperature, pressure, and the phases involved. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Tabular Comparison:**

| Property | Triple Point of Water | Critical Point of Water |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 273.16 K | 647.1 K |
| **Pressure** | 611.657 Pa | 22.06 MPa |
| **Phases Coexisting** | Solid + Liquid + Vapour (three phases) | Critical state — no phase distinction |
| **Degrees of Freedom ($F$)** | 0 (invariant) | 0 (special invariant — CP is unique) |
| **Uniqueness for substance** | Exactly one triple point | Exactly one critical point |
| **Location on P-T diagram** | Intersection of 3 phase-boundary curves | Termination point of vaporization curve |
| **Above this point** | Cannot exist — triple point is minimum | Supercritical fluid region |

**Discussion:**

- The **triple point** marks the lowest pressure at which the liquid phase can exist for water.
- The **critical point** marks the highest temperature and pressure at which a distinct liquid-vapour interface can exist.
- Between the triple point and critical point lies the **vaporization curve** (liquid ⇌ vapour equilibrium).
- For water, the critical point pressure (22.06 MPa) is approximately **36,000 times** larger than the triple point pressure.

*(7 marks: 4 for table, 3 for discussion)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Quick-Revision Checklist for "Triple Point":**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Triple point = unique state where Solid + Liquid + Vapour coexist in equilibrium.
- ⭐ **Water Triple Point:** $T = 273.16$ K (exact) and $P = 611.657$ Pa.
- ⭐ **Gibbs Phase Rule:** $F = C - P + 2$. At TP: $F = 1 - 3 + 2 = 0$ → invariant system.
- ⭐ **Invariance Means:** Neither $T$ nor $P$ can be varied independently at the triple point.
- ⭐ **Three Phase-Boundary Curves:** Sublimation (S–V), Vaporization (L–V), Fusion (S–L) — all meet at the triple point.
- ⭐ **Latent Heat Relation at TP:** $\Delta H_{sub} = \Delta H_{fus} + \Delta H_{vap}$ (exact, from Hess's law).
- ⭐ **Fusion Curve for Water:** Has a **negative slope** (because $\rho_{ice} < \rho_{water}$).
- ⭐ **SI Definition of Kelvin:** $1\ \text{K} = (1/273.16)$ of the triple point temperature of water.
- ⭐ **Triple Point vs Boiling Point:** Boiling point is at 1 atm (only L–V coexist); triple point is at a unique lower pressure (S–L–V coexist).
- ⭐ **Triple Point vs Critical Point:** Both are unique invariant points for a pure substance; triple point has 3 phases coexisting, critical point has only one indistinguishable phase.
- ⭐ **Engineering Applications:** ITS-90 temperature scale, triple-point water cells for calibration, freeze-drying, cryogenic storage, McLeod vacuum gauges.
- ⭐ **Exam Trick:** Always explicitly state "$C = 1$" and "$P = 3$" before substituting into Gibbs Phase Rule to earn full marks.
- ⭐ **Avoid:** Confusing triple point with freezing point; confusing degrees of freedom with number of variables; omitting the negative slope of water's fusion curve.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Triple Point**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties During Change of Phase
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Properties During Change of Phase

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Engineering Thermodynamics, a **phase change** (or change of phase) refers to the physical transformation of a substance from one state of matter to another — typically involving transitions between solid, liquid, and gaseous phases. During such a transition, the substance absorbs or rejects energy at essentially **constant pressure and constant temperature** (for a pure substance), and the process is characterised by the coexistence of two phases in thermodynamic equilibrium.

The **KTU 2024 Scheme** explicitly emphasises the following sub-topics under Module 4:
- **P-V-T surfaces** for pure substances
- **P-V and P-T diagrams** showing saturation dome
- **Dryness fraction (Quality)** of a liquid-vapour mixture
- **Property tables** — Saturated water tables, Superheated steam tables
- **Steam calculation methods** using tables
- **Mollier diagram (h-s chart)** for steam

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must master the use of saturated and superheated steam tables, the concept of quality, and the graphical interpretation of phase change on P-V, T-S, and h-s diagrams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive View

Imagine a sealed pressure cooker on a stove:
- **Before heating:** The water inside is a pure liquid (compressed or saturated liquid state).
- **As heat is added:** The temperature of water rises until it reaches the **saturation temperature** corresponding to the cooker's pressure (e.g., 100 °C at 1.01325 bar).
- **During boiling:** Even though you keep supplying heat, the **temperature does not rise**. All the heat goes into breaking the molecular bonds — this is the **latent heat** (hidden heat) of vaporisation. Bubbles of vapour form *inside* the liquid — this is the **saturated liquid + vapour mixture region**.
- **After all liquid vaporises:** The temperature begins to rise again. The vapour is now in the **superheated** region.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** During phase change, *temperature stays constant* but *internal energy and volume increase dramatically* because energy is being used to overcome intermolecular forces rather than to increase kinetic energy.

## 1.3 Phases of a Pure Substance & Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Saturated Liquid | f | Liquid on the verge of vaporisation |
| Saturated Vapour | g | Vapour on the verge of condensation |
| Compressed (Subcooled) Liquid | — | Liquid at temperature below saturation |
| Superheated Vapour | — | Vapour at temperature above saturation |
| Quality (Dryness Fraction) | $x$ | Mass fraction of vapour in mixture |
| Latent Heat | $h_{fg}$ | Enthalpy of vaporisation at given pressure |

The **critical point** marks the end of the saturation curve — beyond it, the distinction between liquid and vapour disappears (e.g., for water: $T_{cr}$ = **374.14 °C**, $P_{cr}$ = **220.64 bar**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Phase Change Process

### Step 1 — The P-V-T Surface
A pure substance exists over a three-dimensional surface in $P$–$V$–$T$ space. Two-dimensional projections of this surface yield the **P-V diagram** and the **T-S diagram**.

### Step 2 — The Saturation Dome
On a P-V diagram:
- **Saturated liquid line** (left side) — all points where liquid just begins to vaporise.
- **Saturated vapour line** (right side) — all points where vapour just begins to condense.
- **Inside the dome:** Two-phase (liquid + vapour) mixture.
- **Critical point (C):** The dome's apex, where $h_f = h_g$ and $v_f = v_g$.

### Step 3 — Constant-Pressure Heating (Sub-Atmospheric, Atmospheric, Super-Pressurised)
- $P < P_{cr}$: Horizontal line crosses the dome (phase change occurs).
- $P > P_{cr}$: Horizontal line stays in supercritical region — **no distinct phase change** (used in supercritical power cycles like the **Rankine cycle with supercritical boilers** at ~250 bar).

### Step 4 — Quality (Dryness Fraction)
For a liquid-vapour mixture at a given $P$ or $T$:

$$x = \frac{m_{vapour}}{m_{vapour} + m_{liquid}} = \frac{m_g}{m_g + m_f}$$

Where:
- $x = 0$ → Saturated liquid
- $x = 1$ → Saturated vapour
- $0 < x < 1$ → Wet vapour mixture

### Step 5 — Properties of the Wet Mixture
Using the **lever rule**, any intensive property $y$ of the mixture:

$$y = y_f + x \cdot y_{fg}$$

Where $y_{fg} = y_g - y_f$ is the property change during phase change.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of \vert to render absolute values in equations.

| # | Property | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quality from specific volume | $x = \dfrac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f}$ | dimensionless | Lever rule |
| 2 | Specific volume of mixture | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | m³/kg | $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ |
| 3 | Internal energy of mixture | $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | |
| 4 | Enthalpy of mixture | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | Most-used in steam problems |
| 5 | Entropy of mixture | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | kJ/(kg·K) | |
| 6 | Latent heat relationship | $h_{fg} = u_{fg} + P \cdot v_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | From $h = u + Pv$ |
| 7 | Clapeyron equation | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$ | Pa/K | Slope of saturation curve |
| 8 | Clausius-Clapeyron (ideal gas) | $\ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | dimensionless | Approximate |
| 9 | Saturated steam relation | $h_g = h_f + h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | Steam tables |
| 10 | Specific enthalpy from $u$, $v$ | $h = u + P \cdot v$ | kJ/kg | Fundamental |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Phase-change thermodynamics underpins:
- **Steam power plants (Rankine cycle)** — boilers, turbines, condensers.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC** — evaporators and condensers operate on the same principle with refrigerants.
- **Cryogenics** — LNG storage, liquid nitrogen production.
- **Chemical process industries** — distillation columns, evaporators.
- **Heat exchangers** — phase-change materials (PCMs) for thermal energy storage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Property of a Two-Phase Mixture (Lever Rule)

### Starting Premise
Consider a closed system of total mass $m$ at a given pressure, consisting of saturated liquid of mass $m_f$ and saturated vapour of mass $m_g$, where $m = m_f + m_g$.

### Total Volume Constraint
The total volume of the mixture is the sum of the volumes of each phase:

$$V = V_f + V_g$$

Dividing both sides by total mass $m$:

$$\frac{V}{m} = \frac{V_f}{m} + \frac{V_g}{m}$$

But $V/m = v$ (specific volume of mixture), $V_f/m_f = v_f$ (specific volume of saturated liquid), and $V_g/m_g = v_g$ (specific volume of saturated vapour). So:

$$v \cdot m = v_f \cdot m_f + v_g \cdot m_g$$

### Substitute Mass Constraint
Using $m_f = m - m_g$:

$$v \cdot m = v_f (m - m_g) + v_g \cdot m_g$$

Expanding:

$$v \cdot m = v_f \cdot m - v_f \cdot m_g + v_g \cdot m_g$$

Rearranging:

$$v \cdot m - v_f \cdot m = m_g (v_g - v_f)$$

$$m(v - v_f) = m_g (v_g - v_f)$$

### Introduce Quality
Define quality $x = m_g / m$, so $m_g = x \cdot m$. Substituting:

$$m(v - v_f) = x \cdot m (v_g - v_f)$$

Cancelling $m$:

$$\boxed{\; x = \frac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f} \;}$$

And therefore the specific volume of the mixture:

$$\boxed{\; v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg} \;}$$

### Generalisation to Any Property
By the **lever-rule principle** in thermodynamics, this linear interpolation extends to *any* extensive property $Y$ (and hence its intensive counterpart $y = Y/m$):

$$\boxed{\; y = (1-x)\,y_f + x\,y_g = y_f + x\,y_{fg} \;}$$

where $y_{fg} = y_g - y_f$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Clapeyron Equation

### Starting Premise
During phase change at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$, the specific Gibbs free energies of the two phases are equal:

$$g_f = g_g$$

Since $g = h - Ts$, and both phases change with $T$ and $P$:

$$dg_f = dg_g$$

Using $dg = v\,dP - s\,dT$:

$$v_f \, dP - s_f \, dT = v_g \, dP - s_g \, dT$$

### Rearrange
$$(v_g - v_f)\,dP = (s_g - s_f)\,dT$$

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{s_g - s_f}{v_g - v_f} = \frac{s_{fg}}{v_{fg}}$$

### Latent Heat Relation
From the second TdS equation for phase change (reversible isothermal): $T\,ds_{fg} = h_{fg}$, so $s_{fg} = h_{fg}/T$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{\; \frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}} \;}$$

This is the **Clapeyron equation**, governing the slope of the saturation curve on the P-T diagram.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Finding Steam Properties

### Problem Statement
**[KTU University Exam Pattern]**
Determine the specific volume, internal energy, enthalpy, and entropy of wet steam at **8 bar** with a quality of **x = 0.85**.

### Given
- $P = 8$ bar = 800 kPa
- $x = 0.85$

### Saturated Steam Table Values at 8 bar

| Property | Saturated Liquid (f) | Saturated Vapour (g) |
|---|---|---|
| $v$ (m³/kg) | 0.001115 | 0.2403 |
| $u$ (kJ/kg) | 720.87 | 2569.8 |
| $h$ (kJ/kg) | 721.76 | 2761.0 |
| $s$ (kJ/(kg·K)) | 2.0457 | 6.8150 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step A — Latent (fg) values:**

$$v_{fg} = 0.2403 - 0.001115 = 0.239185 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$u_{fg} = 2569.8 - 720.87 = 1848.93 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$h_{fg} = 2761.0 - 721.76 = 2039.24 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$s_{fg} = 6.8150 - 2.0457 = 4.7693 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step B — Apply the lever rule:**

Specific Volume:

$$v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg} = 0.001115 + 0.85 \times 0.239185$$

$$v = 0.001115 + 0.203307 = 0.204422 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

Internal Energy:

$$u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg} = 720.87 + 0.85 \times 1848.93$$

$$u = 720.87 + 1571.59 = 2292.46 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

Enthalpy:

$$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} = 721.76 + 0.85 \times 2039.24$$

$$h = 721.76 + 1733.35 = 2455.11 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

Entropy:

$$s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg} = 2.0457 + 0.85 \times 4.7693$$

$$s = 2.0457 + 4.0539 = 6.0996 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

### Verification using $h = u + Pv$

$$u + P \cdot v = 2292.46 + 800 \times 0.204422 = 2292.46 + 163.54 = 2456.00 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Minor rounding discrepancy (0.89 kJ/kg) is acceptable in KTU valuation — caused by rounding in $v_{fg}$.

### **Final Answer**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| $v$ | **0.2044 m³/kg** |
| $u$ | **2292.46 kJ/kg** |
| $h$ | **2455.11 kJ/kg** |
| $s$ | **6.0996 kJ/(kg·K)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 P-V Diagram with Phase Regions (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Compressed Liquid Region"] -->|Heat Addition| B["Saturated Liquid Line"]
    B -->|Latent Heat h_fg| C["Two Phase Mixture Region Wet Steam"]
    C -->|Quality x rises 0 to 1| D["Saturated Vapour Line"]
    D -->|Superheat Added| E["Superheated Vapour Region"]
    E -->|Critical Point C| F["Supercritical Region No Distinct Phase"]
    F -->|Cooling| A
    C -->|Constant Pressure Process| G["Horizontal Line Inside Dome"]
    G -->|Isobar at P less than P_cr| H["Sensible Heat plus Latent Heat"]
```

## 4.2 T-S Diagram with Phase-Change Constant-Pressure Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph T_S_Process["Constant Pressure Heating of Water on T-S Diagram"]
        T1["Point 1 Compressed Liquid"] -->|Sensible Heat Heating of Subcooled Liquid| T2["Point 2 Saturated Liquid"]
        T2 -->|Latent Heat h_fg at Constant T| T3["Point 3 Inside Dome Wet Mixture"]
        T3 -->|Constant T rising x from 0 to 1| T4["Point 4 Saturated Vapour"]
        T4 -->|Superheating| T5["Point 5 Superheated Steam"]
        T5 -->|Increasing Pressure Curve Shifts Right| T6["Higher Pressure Isobar"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram of State Determination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Data"]
        IP["Given P or T and Quality x or Specific Volume v"]
    end
    subgraph LOOKUP["Steam Table Lookup"]
        L1["Find saturation table at given P or T"]
        L2["Read properties y_f and y_g"]
        L3["Compute y_fg = y_g minus y_f"]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE["Apply Lever Rule"]
        C1["y = y_f + x times y_fg"]
        C2["Verify using h = u + P times v"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output State Properties"]
        OP["v, u, h, s of the mixture"]
    end
    IP --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> C1 --> C2 --> OP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Note:** The Mollier (h-s) chart is essentially a graphical implementation of the "Compute" block — the line of constant pressure inside the wet region is a straight line from $h_f$ at $x=0$ to $h_g$ at $x=1$, allowing direct readout of properties and visualisation of throttling and expansion processes.

## 4.4 Phase Diagram Process Map (P-T Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PT1["Triple Point T triple 0.01 C, P triple 0.006112 bar"] -->|Solid Vapour sublimation Curve| PT2["Critical Point C"]
    PT2 -->|Fusion Curve separates Solid and Liquid| PT3["Normal Boiling Point at 1 atm"]
    PT3 -->|Vaporisation Curve ends at Critical Point| PT2
    PT1 -->|Vaporisation line of Water| PT2
    PT1 -->|Sublimation line of Ice| PT4["Lower Pressure Region"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Q1. Define 'quality' of steam. How is it expressed mathematically for a wet steam mixture?**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 4 | CO2 | Bloom: Remember]**

**Model Answer:**
Quality (or **dryness fraction**) of steam is defined as the ratio of the mass of dry saturated vapour to the total mass of the liquid–vapour mixture. It is denoted by the symbol $x$ and is a dimensionless quantity ranging from 0 (saturated liquid) to 1 (saturated vapour).

$$x = \frac{m_g}{m_f + m_g} = \frac{m_g}{m_{total}}$$

In terms of specific volumes (lever rule):

$$x = \frac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f}$$

**Marking Key (3 marks):**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Formula 1: 1 mark
- Formula 2 (lever rule): 1 mark

---

### **Q2. State the Clapeyron equation and explain its physical significance.**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 4 | CO2 | Bloom: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**
The Clapeyron equation gives the slope of the saturation curve (phase-equilibrium curve) on a P-T diagram for any pure substance undergoing phase change:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$$

**Significance:**
- It quantifies how the saturation pressure changes with temperature during a phase change.
- The slope $dP/dT$ is always **positive** for liquid–vapour transitions (since $h_{fg}, T, v_{fg}$ are all positive).
- At the **critical point**, $v_f \to v_g$, hence $v_{fg} \to 0$ and $dP/dT \to \infty$ (saturation curve becomes vertical on the P-T plot).

**Marking Key (3 marks):**
- Equation: 2 marks
- Significance: 1 mark

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | Module 4 | CO2, CO3 | Bloom: Apply / Analyse]**

**(a)** A rigid vessel of volume **0.05 m³** contains a liquid-vapour mixture of water at **5 bar** with a quality of **0.6**. Determine:
- (i) Mass of liquid and mass of vapour present. (4 marks)
- (ii) Total internal energy and total enthalpy of the mixture. (3 marks)

**(b)** Steam at **10 bar** has an enthalpy of **2700 kJ/kg**. Determine its temperature, quality (if wet), and specific volume using steam tables. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Given:** $V = 0.05 \; \text{m}^3$, $P = 5 \; \text{bar} = 500 \; \text{kPa}$, $x = 0.6$.

**Saturated Properties at 5 bar:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| $v_f$ | 0.001093 m³/kg |
| $v_g$ | 0.3749 m³/kg |
| $u_f$ | 639.54 kJ/kg |
| $u_g$ | 2561.0 kJ/kg |
| $h_f$ | 640.09 kJ/kg |
| $h_g$ | 2748.7 kJ/kg |

**Step 1 — Specific volume of mixture:**

$$v = v_f + x(v_g - v_f) = 0.001093 + 0.6(0.3749 - 0.001093)$$

$$v = 0.001093 + 0.6 \times 0.373807 = 0.001093 + 0.224284 = 0.225377 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Total mass:**

$$m = \frac{V}{v} = \frac{0.05}{0.225377} = 0.22186 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of vapour and liquid:**

$$m_g = x \cdot m = 0.6 \times 0.22186 = 0.13312 \; \text{kg}$$

$$m_f = m - m_g = 0.22186 - 0.13312 = 0.08874 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Internal energy of mixture:**

$$u = u_f + x(u_g - u_f) = 639.54 + 0.6(2561.0 - 639.54)$$

$$u = 639.54 + 0.6 \times 1921.46 = 639.54 + 1152.88 = 1792.42 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$U = m \cdot u = 0.22186 \times 1792.42 = 397.66 \; \text{kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Enthalpy of mixture:**

$$h = h_f + x(h_g - h_f) = 640.09 + 0.6(2748.7 - 640.09)$$

$$h = 640.09 + 0.6 \times 2108.61 = 640.09 + 1265.17 = 1905.26 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$H = m \cdot h = 0.22186 \times 1905.26 = 422.69 \; \text{kJ}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given:** $P = 10 \; \text{bar}$, $h = 2700 \; \text{kJ/kg}$.

**Saturated Properties at 10 bar:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| $T_{sat}$ | 179.9 °C |
| $h_f$ | 762.81 kJ/kg |
| $h_g$ | 2778.1 kJ/kg |
| $v_f$ | 0.001127 m³/kg |
| $v_g$ | 0.1944 m³/kg |
| $s_f$ | 2.1387 kJ/(kg·K) |
| $s_g$ | 6.5865 kJ/(kg·K) |

**Step 1 — Check if wet steam:**

Since $h_f = 762.81 < h = 2700 < h_g = 2778.1$, the steam is **wet**.

**Step 2 — Find quality:**

$$x = \frac{h - h_f}{h_g - h_f} = \frac{2700 - 762.81}{2778.1 - 762.81} = \frac{1937.19}{2015.29} = 0.9613$$

**Step 3 — Find temperature:**

For a wet mixture, $T = T_{sat}$ at the given pressure = **179.9 °C**.

**Step 4 — Specific volume:**

$$v = v_f + x(v_g - v_f) = 0.001127 + 0.9613(0.1944 - 0.001127)$$

$$v = 0.001127 + 0.9613 \times 0.193273 = 0.001127 + 0.185824 = 0.18695 \; \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 | Module 4 | CO2, CO3 | Bloom: Apply / Analyse]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **P-V, T-S, and h-s (Mollier) diagrams** for a pure substance undergoing phase change. Clearly mark the **critical point, saturated liquid line, saturated vapour line, two-phase region, and constant-pressure lines**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Dry saturated steam at **15 bar** is throttled to **1 bar**. Using the **Mollier diagram**, determine the temperature and quality of the resulting steam. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Sketch Description

**P-V Diagram:**
- Y-axis: Pressure $P$ (log scale preferred)
- X-axis: Specific volume $v$
- **Saturation dome** with critical point $C$ at the apex.
- **Inside dome**: Two-phase (wet) region — horizontal constant-pressure lines pass through this region.
- **Saturated liquid line**: Left boundary of dome.
- **Saturated vapour line**: Right boundary of dome.
- **Above critical point**: Single supercritical fluid region (no distinction).
- **Triple point** marked at low $P$ and $T$ where all three phases coexist.

**T-S Diagram:**
- Y-axis: Temperature $T$
- X-axis: Entropy $s$
- **Critical point C** at the top of the dome.
- **Inside dome**: Horizontal lines (constant $T$ = constant $P$ during phase change).
- **Saturated liquid line**: Left boundary.
- **Saturated vapour line**: Right boundary.
- **Outside dome**: Compressed liquid (left), superheated vapour (right).

**Mollier (h-s) Diagram:**
- Y-axis: Enthalpy $h$
- X-axis: Entropy $s$
- Only the wet region and superheated region are usually shown.
- **Constant pressure lines** in wet region are **straight** with slope $T_{sat}$.
- **Constant temperature lines** in superheated region slope steeply upward.

*(For KTU, neat labelled sketches carry 4 marks and 3 marks is for explanation of regions.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Throttling Process

**Given:** $P_1 = 15 \; \text{bar}$, Dry saturated steam ($x_1 = 1$); $P_2 = 1 \; \text{bar}$.

**Steam Table Values:**

At 15 bar: $h_1 = h_g = 2792.2 \; \text{kJ/kg}$, $s_1 = s_g = 6.6796 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$.

At 1 bar: $h_f = 417.46$, $h_{fg} = 2258.0$, $h_g = 2675.5$; $s_f = 1.3026$, $s_{fg} = 6.0568$, $s_g = 7.3594$.

**Step 1 — Apply Throttling Energy Equation:**

For a throttling (isenthalpic) process: $h_1 = h_2$.

$$h_2 = 2792.2 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Check state at 1 bar:**

Since $h_f = 417.46 < h_2 = 2792.2 > h_g = 2675.5$ → **Superheated steam**.

But the Mollier chart approach (per KTU's stated method) treats slight superheat by extending the constant-pressure line into superheated region.

**Step 3 — Quality (using Mollier chart convention):**

Reading the Mollier chart along the constant-enthalpy line $h = 2792.2$ from 15 bar down to 1 bar. The intersection falls slightly into the **superheated region**.

However, if KTU expects the student to assume $h_2 \approx h_{g@1bar} = 2675.5$:

$$x_2 = \frac{h_2 - h_{f,2}}{h_{fg,2}} = \frac{2792.2 - 417.46}{2258.0} = \frac{2374.74}{2258.0} = 1.0517$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $x_2 > 1$, the steam is slightly **superheated**. (The "quality" is mathematically > 1 only when formal wet-region formula is naively applied; physically the steam is superheated.)

**Step 4 — Temperature (from superheated steam tables at 1 bar, h = 2792.2 kJ/kg):**

Interpolation between:
- At 1 bar, 200 °C: $h = 2875.3$ kJ/kg
- At 1 bar, 150 °C: $h = 2776.4$ kJ/kg

By linear interpolation:

$$T = 150 + \frac{2792.2 - 2776.4}{2875.3 - 2776.4} \times 50 = 150 + \frac{15.8}{98.9} \times 50 \approx 150 + 7.99 \approx 158 \; °C$$

**Final Answer for Q.B(b):** Temperature ≈ **158 °C**, Quality indicates **superheated** steam.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert bar to kPa:** 1 bar = 100 kPa. Using $P$ in bar in $h = u + Pv$ without conversion is a **favourite mark-deduction trap** (loses 0.5–1 mark per question).
> 2. **State confusion:** Always verify if $h$ lies between $h_f$ and $h_g$ at the given pressure before assuming "wet steam" and applying the lever rule.
> 3. **Property mixing:** Never use $v_f$ from one pressure with $v_g$ from another. Both must come from the **same saturation table row**.
> 4. **Triple-point vs critical-point:** Don't confuse them — triple point is where **all three phases** coexist (0.01 °C, 0.006112 bar for water).
> 5. **Units in Clapeyron:** $h_{fg}$ must be in J/kg and $v_{fg}$ in m³/kg when $T$ is in Kelvin to give $dP/dT$ in Pa/K.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Phase change** occurs at constant $T$ and $P$ for a pure substance, with both phases coexisting in equilibrium.
- The **P-V diagram** shows the **saturation dome** with critical point at the apex; horizontal lines inside the dome represent constant-pressure phase-change processes.
- The **T-S diagram** also has a dome, but with horizontal lines inside (constant $T$).
- The **Mollier (h-s) chart** is the engineer's favourite — constant-pressure lines are **straight** in the wet region.
- **Quality** $x$ ranges from 0 (saturated liquid) to 1 (saturated vapour); for $x > 1$ the steam is **superheated**.
- **Lever rule** is the master key — apply to any property $y$: $y = y_f + x \cdot y_{fg}$.
- **Clapeyron equation** $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$ gives the slope of the saturation curve.
- **Critical point** of water: $T_{cr} = 374.14 \; °C$, $P_{cr} = 220.64 \; \text{bar}$.
- **Triple point** of water: $T_{tp} = 0.01 \; °C$, $P_{tp} = 0.006112 \; \text{bar}$.
- Always use **consistent units**: bar ↔ kPa conversion (1 bar = 100 kPa).
- **Mollier chart** is best for **throttling, expansion, and mixing** problems (isenthalpic processes).
- Steam tables provide **only** discrete points — interpolation may be needed in superheated region (linear interpolation is acceptable at KTU level).
- **Latent heat $h_{fg}$** decreases with pressure and vanishes at the critical point.
- The two-phase region disappears above the critical point — supercritical fluid is neither liquid nor gas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: P-v and P-T Diagram of Pure Substance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** P-v and P-T Diagram of Pure Substance ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pure Substance (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > A **pure substance** is defined as a homogeneous chemical element or compound, having a fixed chemical composition and uniform physical structure, that may exist in **more than one phase** simultaneously without any change in its chemical identity. A pure substance does **not** have to be a single chemical element or a single chemical compound; it can be a *mixture* of various phases of the same chemical component (e.g., a liquid–vapor mixture of water), provided the chemical composition is the same in all phases. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 4, the study of pure substances is the gateway to understanding **phase-change processes**, **refrigeration cycles**, **steam power plants (Rankine cycle)**, and **gas mixtures**. Every thermodynamic analysis in power engineering and refrigeration hinges on mastering P-v and P-T diagrams. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine a sealed pressure cooker containing only **water and steam** (no salt, no impurities). Chemically, it is *H₂O* everywhere — liquid in the bottom, vapor at the top. Even though two phases coexist, it is still a *pure substance* because every molecule is the same compound. Think of a *kettle boiling water* on a stove: - Below **100 °C** (at 1 atm): all **subcooled (compressed) liquid**. - At exactly **100 °C** (at 1 atm): liquid and vapor coexist — the **saturation state**. - Above **100 °C** (still at 1 atm): if you keep adding heat, you get **superheated vapor**. The P-v and P-T diagrams are the *maps* of this journey from solid → liquid → vapor, recording exactly **where** the phase boundaries lie. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Critical Pressure of Water:** $P_{cr} = 22.06 \text{ MPa}$ (≈ **220.64 bar**) > - **Critical Temperature of Water:** $T_{cr} = 373.95 \text{ °C} = 647.1 \text{ K}$ > - **Critical Specific Volume of Water:** $v_{cr} = 0.003106 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ > - **Triple Point of Water:** $P_{tp} = 0.006112 \text{ bar}$, $T_{tp} = 0.01 \text{ °C} = 273.16 \text{ K}$ > - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)** > - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$ ### 1.4 The Three Phases of a Pure Substance A pure substance can exist in three principal phases: | Phase | Symbol | Characteristic | |---|---|---| | **Solid** | *S* | Molecules tightly bound; low specific volume; high density | | **Liquid** | *f* (fluid) | Molecules loosely bound; moderate specific volume | | **Vapor/Gas** | *g* | Molecules far apart; very high specific volume; low density | A substance can also exist as a **supercritical fluid** (above the critical point), where the distinction between liquid and vapor disappears. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Phase-Change Process — The Underlying Physics When a pure substance is heated at **constant pressure**, the following sequence occurs: 1. **Subcooled/Compressed Liquid Region** — Temperature rises; substance is *not* on the verge of vaporizing. 2. **Saturated Liquid State** — Temperature reaches the **saturation temperature** ($T_{sat}$); first bubble of vapor forms. 3. **Saturated Liquid–Vapor Mixture (Wet Region)** — Heat addition causes phase change at *constant* $T$ and $P$; both phases coexist. 4. **Saturated Vapor State** — Last drop of liquid vaporizes; the substance is now dry saturated vapor. 5. **Superheated Vapor Region** — Further heating raises $T$ above $T_{sat}$ at the same pressure. > [!NOTE] > **Key Observation:** During the phase change (step 3), $P$ and $T$ remain constant while the specific volume $v$ increases drastically (especially for water: $v_g/v_f \approx 1600$ at 100 °C). ### 2.2 Saturation Properties — Terminology Master List | Term | Symbol | Definition | |---|---|---| | **Saturation Temperature** | $T_{sat}$ | Temperature at which a pure substance changes phase at a given pressure | | **Saturation Pressure** | $P_{sat}$ | Pressure at which phase change occurs at a given temperature | | **Saturated Liquid** | — | Liquid about to begin vaporization | | **Saturated Vapor** | — | Vapor about to begin condensation | | **Quality (Dryness Fraction)** | $x$ | Mass fraction of vapor in a liquid–vapor mixture | | **Subcooled/Compressed Liquid** | — | Liquid at temperature below $T_{sat}$ for the given pressure | | **Superheated Vapor** | — | Vapor at temperature above $T_{sat}$ for the given pressure | | **Critical Point** | $C$ | Point at which saturated liquid and saturated vapor states become identical | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (P-v & P-T Diagrams) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** for Module 4. Master it thoroughly before attempting any KTU numerical. | # | Property / Relation | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Quality (Dryness Fraction) | $x = \dfrac{m_v}{m_v + m_l} = \dfrac{m_v}{m_{total}}$ | — | $0 \le x \le 1$ | | 2 | Average Specific Volume in Wet Region | $v = v_f + x \cdot (v_g - v_f) = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | m³/kg | Linear interpolation along the tie-line | | 3 | Average Internal Energy | $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | | | 4 | Average Enthalpy | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | | | 5 | Average Entropy | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | kJ/(kg·K) | | | 6 | Latent Heat of Vaporization | $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | kJ/kg | Decreases as $P$ rises; vanishes at critical point | | 7 | Latent Heat of Fusion | $h_{if} = h_f - h_i$ | kJ/kg | Where $i$ = saturated ice/solid | | 8 | Clausius–Clapeyron Equation (P-T slope) | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$ | Pa/K | Governs the **vaporization curve** on P-T diagram | | 9 | Latent Heat of Sublimation | $h_{ig} = h_{ig}$ (solid → vapor directly) | kJ/kg | $h_{ig} = h_{if} + h_{fg}$ | | 10 | Reduced Properties (compressibility) | $P_R = P/P_{cr},\ T_R = T/T_{cr}$ | — | Used for principle of corresponding states | | 11 | Saturated Steam Tables | Read $v_f, v_g, u_f, u_g, h_f, h_g, s_f, s_g$ at $T_{sat}$ or $P_{sat}$ | varied | Primary problem-solving tool | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Warning:** When writing $v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ in your KTU answer book, **never** use the vertical pipe character $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value within table rows. Use the **{ }** braces as above to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. ### 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Steam Power Plants (Rankine Cycle):** The boiler, turbine, condenser, and pump operate entirely in the **wet region** and **superheated region** of the P-v diagram. The entire cycle is plotted on P-v coordinates. > - **Refrigeration (Vapor-Compression Cycle):** The refrigerant undergoes phase change at the evaporator and condenser — quality $x$ is the key variable on the low-pressure side. > - **Gas Turbines (Brayton Cycle):** The working fluid stays in the **superheated gas region**, far from saturation; P-v analysis uses ideal-gas relations. > - **LNG/Cryogenics:** Operations occur near the critical point, where properties are extremely sensitive to P and T. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quality ($x$) in Terms of Specific Volumes **Starting Concept:** When a pure substance is in the **two-phase (wet) region**, let: - $m_l$ = mass of saturated liquid - $m_v$ = mass of saturated vapor - $m = m_l + m_v$ = total mass - $V_l = m_l \cdot v_f$ = volume of liquid - $V_v = m_v \cdot v_g$ = volume of vapor - $V = V_l + V_v$ = total volume **Step 1 — Express the total volume:** The total volume is the sum of liquid volume and vapor volume. $$ V = V_l + V_v $$ **Step 2 — Substitute volumes in terms of specific volumes:** Since $V_l = m_l v_f$ and $V_v = m_v v_g$: $$ V = m_l v_f + m_v v_g $$ **Step 3 — Divide both sides by total mass $m$:** Dividing by $m$ to obtain average specific volume $v = V/m$: $$ v = \frac{m_l}{m} v_f + \frac{m_v}{m} v_g $$ **Step 4 — Introduce the dryness fraction:** By definition, $x = m_v / m$, so the liquid fraction is $(1 - x) = m_l / m$. Substituting: $$ v = (1 - x) v_f + x v_g $$ **Step 5 — Expand and simplify:** Expand the right-hand side: $$ v = v_f - x v_f + x v_g $$ Group the $x$ terms: $$ v = v_f + x (v_g - v_f) $$ **Step 6 — Define $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$:** This is the *difference* in specific volumes between saturated vapor and saturated liquid. It represents the volume change per unit mass during complete vaporization. $$ \boxed{\,v = v_f + x \, v_{fg}\,} $$ **Step 7 — Solve for $x$:** Rearrange to isolate $x$: $$ x (v_g - v_f) = v - v_f $$ $$ \boxed{\,x = \frac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f} = \frac{v - v_f}{v_{fg}}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **most important formula** for KTU Module 4 numerical problems. The numerator $v - v_f$ represents how far the actual specific volume lies above the saturated liquid line; the denominator $v_{fg}$ represents the *full width* of the dome at the given pressure. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Clausius–Clapeyron Equation (Slope of P-T Vaporization Curve) The Clausius–Clapeyron equation describes **how the saturation pressure varies with saturation temperature** along the phase boundary on the P-T diagram. **Step 1 — Use the Second Law Reversibility Condition:** For a reversible phase-change process at constant $T$ and $P$, the change in Gibbs free energy is zero: $$ dG = V \, dP - S \, dT = 0 $$ **Step 2 — Apply to both phases (liquid and vapor):** For saturated liquid: $dG_f = V_f \, dP - S_f \, dT = 0$ For saturated vapor: $dG_g = V_g \, dP - S_g \, dT = 0$ Equating the two (both must equal the same $dG$ of the system): $$ V_f \, dP - S_f \, dT = V_g \, dP - S_g \, dT $$ **Step 3 — Group $dP$ and $dT$ terms:** Rearranging: $$ (V_f - V_g) \, dP = (S_f - S_g) \, dT $$ **Step 4 — Convert volume terms to specific volumes:** Multiplying both sides by $1/m$ (using specific quantities): $$ (v_f - v_g) \, dP = (s_f - s_g) \, dT $$ **Step 5 — Solve for $dP/dT$:** $$ \frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{s_f - s_g}{v_f - v_g} = \frac{s_g - s_f}{v_g - v_f} $$ **Step 6 — Substitute $s_{fg} = s_g - s_f$ and $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$:** $$ \frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{s_{fg}}{v_{fg}} $$ **Step 7 — Use the latent heat relation:** From the second law for a reversible constant-pressure, constant-temperature heat addition: $T \, ds = dh - v \, dP$, with $dP = 0$: $$ T \, ds_{fg} = dh_{fg} = h_{fg} $$ Therefore: $$ s_{fg} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T} $$ **Step 8 — Final form of the Clausius–Clapeyron equation:** $$ \boxed{\,\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \, v_{fg}}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The slope of the vaporization curve on the P-T diagram is *steep* (it always rises). As $T \to T_{cr}$, $h_{fg} \to 0$ and the slope flattens, until the curve terminates at the critical point. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Latent Heat of Sublimation (Additivity of Phase Transitions) If a solid can pass directly to vapor (sublimation), the latent heats are **additive** because enthalpy is a state function. **Step 1 — Enthalpy path for solid → liquid → vapor:** $$ h_{fg} = h_g - h_f \quad \text{and} \quad h_{if} = h_f - h_i $$ **Step 2 — Enthalpy path for solid → vapor directly:** $$ h_{ig} = h_g - h_i $$ **Step 3 — Combine the paths:** $$ h_g - h_i = (h_g - h_f) + (h_f - h_i) = h_{fg} + h_{if} $$ $$ \boxed{\,h_{ig} = h_{fg} + h_{if}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This additivity is what allows the P-T diagram's three curves (sublimation, vaporization, fusion) to **meet** at the **triple point** with a unique $P$ and $T$. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Region-by-Region Property Behaviour | Region on P-v Diagram | Phase(s) Present | $T$ Behavior | $v$ Behavior | Quality $x$ | Ideal-Gas Valid? | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Compressed Liquid** (left of dome) | Liquid only | $T < T_{sat}$ | $v \approx v_f$ small | $x = 0$ (no vapor) | No | | **Saturated Liquid Line** (left dome) | Liquid, 1st bubble forming | $T = T_{sat}$ | $v = v_f$ | $x = 0$ | No | | **Wet Region** (inside dome) | Liquid + Vapor mixture | $T = T_{sat}$ (constant) | $v_f \le v \le v_g$ | $0 \le x \le 1$ | No | | **Saturated Vapor Line** (right dome) | Vapor, last drop condensing | $T = T_{sat}$ | $v = v_g$ | $x = 1$ | No | | **Superheated Vapor** (right of dome) | Vapor only | $T > T_{sat}$ | $v > v_g$ large | $x = 1$ (by definition) | Approximate | | **Supercritical Fluid** (above $P_{cr}$, right of dome) | Single phase | $T > T_{cr}$ | $v > v_{cr}$ | N/A | No | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 P-v Diagram of a Pure Substance — Block Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid diagram is a *functional map* of the P-v diagram, identifying the **regions, boundaries, and key points** as interconnected blocks. The pressure axis is conceptualized as the vertical column and specific volume as the horizontal axis. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["COMP SUB COOLED LIQUID REGION
T less than T sat
v approximately v f
high density low v"] B["SAT LIQUID LINE
left boundary of dome
v equals v f
x equals zero
first bubble of vapor forms"] C["WET REGION TWO PHASE
inside the dome
liquid and vapor coexist
T and P constant
v f less v less v g
quality x between 0 and 1"] D["SAT VAPOR LINE
right boundary of dome
v equals v g
x equals 1
last drop of liquid vaporizes"] E["SUPERHEATED VAPOR REGION
T greater than T sat
v greater than v g
low density large v"] F["SUPERCRITICAL FLUID REGION
above critical pressure
T greater than T critical
single phase no distinction"] G["CRITICAL POINT C
top of dome
P equals 22.06 MPa for water
T equals 373.95 C
v equals 0.003106 m cubed per kg
saturated liquid and vapor merge"] H["TRIPLE POINT T
bottom of dome area
P equals 0.006112 bar
T equals 0.01 C
all three phases coexist"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E C --> G D --> G G --> F E --> F B --> H D --> H C --> H ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the P-v Diagram:** > - Vertical axis → Pressure $P$ (increasing upward). > - Horizontal axis → Specific Volume $v$ (increasing rightward). > - The **dome** is called the **vapor dome**, **two-phase region**, or **saturation dome**. > - Process lines (isobaric, isothermal, isentropic, etc.) are drawn on this map. > - A **horizontal line** inside the dome is called a **constant-pressure (isobaric) tie line** — its length spans from $v_f$ to $v_g$. ### 4.2 P-T Diagram of a Pure Substance — Block Functional Architecture Flow The P-T diagram plots pressure against temperature, with **three distinct phase-boundary curves** meeting at the triple point. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["SOLID REGION
high pressure low T
molecules tightly bound
ice for water"] B["LIQUID REGION
moderate P and T
above fusion line
right of fusion line"] C["VAPOR REGION
low P high T
below vaporization line
gas phase"] D["FUSION CURVE
solid liquid boundary
nearly vertical line
slight negative slope for water
ends at triple point"] E["VAPORIZATION CURVE
liquid vapor boundary
positive slope
ends at critical point C"] F["SUBLIMATION CURVE
solid vapor boundary
positive slope
below triple point"] G["TRIPLE POINT T
P equals 0.006112 bar
T equals 0.01 C
three phases coexist
unique for each substance"] H["CRITICAL POINT C
P equals 22.06 MPa
T equals 373.95 C
end of vaporization curve
liquid vapor distinction vanishes"] D --> G F --> G E --> G D --> A D --> B E --> B E --> C F --> A F --> C E --> H B --> H C --> H ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the P-T Diagram:** > - The **fusion curve** has a *positive slope* for most substances, but a *slightly negative slope* for water (ice floats — a unique anomaly). > - The **vaporization curve** ends at the **critical point**; beyond it, no distinction between liquid and vapor exists. > - The **sublimation curve** exists only below the triple-point pressure. > - The Clausius–Clapeyron equation $dP/dT = h_{fg}/(T v_{fg})$ governs the slope of the vaporization curve. ### 4.3 Comparison Matrix — P-v vs. P-T Diagram | Feature | P-v Diagram | P-T Diagram | |---|---|---| | **Axes** | $P$ (vertical) vs. $v$ (horizontal) | $P$ (vertical) vs. $T$ (horizontal) | | **Shape of Phase Boundary** | Dome / bell-shaped ("saturation dome") | Three separate curves meeting at triple point | | **Critical Point** | Top of dome (peak) | End of vaporization curve | | **Triple Point** | Not shown explicitly (line of states) | Single unique point where all three curves meet | | **Used Most For** | Process analysis (Rankine, refrigeration cycles) | Phase stability analysis, chemical equilibrium | | **Constant-T Process** | Vertical line (going up or down in pressure) | Horizontal line (along isotherm) | | **Constant-P Process** | Horizontal line (constant-pressure tie line) | Horizontal line (isobar crosses phase regions) | | **Constant-$v$ Process** | Vertical line | Not a straight line; curves in P-T space | --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]** > **Q1.** Define a *pure substance* in thermodynamics. Give **two** examples in which a pure substance exists in two or more phases. > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A pure substance is a homogeneous chemical element or compound that has a **fixed chemical composition** in all its phases. It may exist in more than one phase simultaneously without any change in its chemical identity. > Examples: (i) Water + steam mixture — both phases are H₂O. (ii) Liquid nitrogen coexisting with its vapor inside a cryogenic storage tank. > *(Award full 3 marks for: definition 1 mark, examples 1 mark, key idea 1 mark.)* > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]** > **Q2.** What is the **critical point** of a pure substance? Why does the latent heat of vaporization vanish at the critical point? > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The critical point is the state at which the saturated liquid and saturated vapor phases become **indistinguishable** — they merge into a single phase. Above the critical point, no amount of pressure can liquefy the substance. > At the critical point, $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f = 0$ because $h_g = h_f$; the specific enthalpies of saturated liquid and saturated vapor become identical, so the latent heat of vaporization vanishes. > *(1 mark critical point definition, 1 mark property identification, 1 mark reason for $h_{fg} = 0$.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A — [14 Marks]** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A]** > **(a)** Sketch the **P-v diagram** of a pure substance. Label the following on it: *saturated liquid line, saturated vapor line, critical point, triple point, compressed liquid region, wet region, superheated vapor region.* Briefly explain the significance of the critical point. **[7 marks]** > > **(b)** A closed rigid vessel of volume **0.05 m³** contains a mixture of liquid water and steam at a pressure of **0.5 MPa**. The quality of the mixture is **0.6**. Determine: > (i) The mass of liquid and the mass of vapor in the vessel. > (ii) The total internal energy of the mixture. > **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — Sketch and Explanation [7 marks]** **Sketch (3 marks):** Draw the standard P-v saturation dome with the following labelled features (P vertical, v horizontal): ``` P ↑ │ Superheated│ Vapor │╲ Region │ ╲ │ ╲ Critical │___●___ C Point │ / \ │/ \ Wet │ wet \ Region │region\ (Liquid+ │ \ Vapor) │ \ ════════════════════════ T = T_sat Saturated │ \ Saturated Liquid │ \ Vapor Line │ \ Line (left │ \ (right dome) │ \ dome) │ Compressed │ Triple Point (T_p, P_tp) ≈ Liquid │ at lower-left of dome Region │ ───────────┴────────────────────→ v ``` **Significance of Critical Point (4 marks):** - At the critical point, the saturated liquid and saturated vapor become a **single indistinguishable phase** — the distinction between liquid and vapor disappears. - Beyond the critical pressure, no amount of compression alone can liquefy the gas; this is the boundary of the **supercritical fluid region**. - Properties $h_f = h_g$, $v_f = v_g$, $u_f = u_g$, $s_f = s_g$ at the critical point. So the latent heat $h_{fg} = 0$. - It marks the **upper terminus** of the vaporization curve on the P-T diagram. --- **Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]** **Given:** - Vessel volume: $V = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$ - Pressure: $P = 0.5 \text{ MPa} = 500 \text{ kPa}$ - Quality: $x = 0.6$ **Step 1 — Look up saturation properties at P = 0.5 MPa from steam tables (1 mark):** From standard steam tables at $P = 0.5 \text{ MPa}$: - $v_f = 0.001093 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ - $v_g = 0.3749 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ - $u_f = 639.68 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - $u_g = 2561.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - $u_{fg} = u_g - u_f = 1921.52 \text{ kJ/kg}$ **Step 2 — Total mass of mixture (1 mark):** Average specific volume: $$ v = v_f + x \, v_{fg} = 0.001093 + 0.6 \times (0.3749 - 0.001093) $$ $$ v = 0.001093 + 0.6 \times 0.373807 = 0.001093 + 0.224284 = 0.225377 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} $$ Total mass: $$ m = \frac{V}{v} = \frac{0.05}{0.225377} = 0.2218 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 3 — Mass of liquid and mass of vapor (1.5 marks):** Mass of vapor: $$ m_v = x \cdot m = 0.6 \times 0.2218 = 0.1331 \text{ kg} $$ Mass of liquid: $$ m_l = (1 - x) \cdot m = 0.4 \times 0.2218 = 0.0887 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 4 — Internal energy of mixture per unit mass (1 mark):** $$ u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg} = 639.68 + 0.6 \times 1921.52 $$ $$ u = 639.68 + 1152.91 = 1792.59 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 5 — Total internal energy of mixture (1.5 marks):** $$ U = m \cdot u = 0.2218 \times 1792.59 = 397.6 \text{ kJ} $$ **Final Answers:** - $m_l = 0.0887 \text{ kg}$ - $m_v = 0.1331 \text{ kg}$ - $U = 397.6 \text{ kJ}$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Most common mistake:** Confusing $v_g$ and $v_f$ during interpolation — students often write $v = v_f + x(v_f - v_g)$ with a wrong sign. The correct expression is $v = v_f + x(v_g - v_f)$. > 2. **Unit trap:** $P$ must be in **MPa** or **kPa consistently** when reading from the steam table; do not mix bar and MPa without conversion. > 3. **Quality limit:** If $x$ is found to be outside $[0, 1]$, the state is *not* in the wet region — re-check your interpolation. KTU evaluators deduct **2 marks** instantly for an out-of-range $x$. --- #### **Question B — [14 Marks]** (Module 4 Internal Choice Alternative) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B]** > **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **P-T diagram** of a pure substance. Identify the **sublimation, vaporization, and fusion curves**, and explain the significance of the **triple point** and the **critical point**. **[7 marks]** > > **(b)** A piston–cylinder device contains **2 kg** of saturated liquid water at **100 °C**. The water is heated at constant pressure until it becomes a **saturated vapor**. Determine: > (i) The initial and final specific volumes. > (ii) The total change in enthalpy ($\Delta H$). > **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — P-T Diagram and Significance [7 marks]** **Sketch (3 marks):** ``` P ↑ │ Critical │ Point C │ ● │ ╱│ │ ╱ │ FUSION ─────────────│───● │ CURVE │ │ │ (Solid-Liquid) │ │ │ VAPORIZATION CURVE │ │ │ (Liquid-Vapor) │ │ │ │ Triple │ │ Point │ │ ●───────┘ │ ╱ │╱ SUBLIMATION CURVE │ (Solid-Vapor) │ └─────────────────→ T ``` **Three curves and their physical meaning (3 marks):** 1. **Sublimation curve:** Boundary between **solid** and **vapor** phases (e.g., dry ice → CO₂ gas). Valid only *below* the triple-point pressure. 2. **Fusion (melting) curve:** Boundary between **solid** and **liquid** phases. For most substances, slope is positive; for water, it has a *slightly negative* slope (anomaly — explains why ice floats). 3. **Vaporization curve:** Boundary between **liquid** and **vapor** phases. Terminus is the critical point. **Significance of the triple point (0.5 marks):** The triple point is the **only** state at which solid, liquid, and vapor coexist in equilibrium. It is unique for every pure substance. For water, it is fixed at $T_{tp} = 0.01 \text{ °C}$ and $P_{tp} = 0.006112 \text{ bar}$. **Significance of the critical point (0.5 marks):** The critical point is the **terminus** of the vaporization curve — beyond it, no clear distinction between liquid and vapor exists. The substance enters the **supercritical fluid** regime, used in supercritical CO₂ extraction, supercritical steam power cycles, etc. --- **Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]** **Given:** - Mass: $m = 2 \text{ kg}$ - Initial state: Saturated liquid at $T = 100 \text{ °C}$ - Final state: Saturated vapor at $T = 100 \text{ °C}$ (constant-pressure process) - Pressure remains at $P = P_{sat}(100 \text{ °C}) = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$ **Step 1 — Look up saturation properties at T = 100 °C from steam tables (1 mark):** At $T = 100 \text{ °C}$: - $v_f = 0.001043 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ - $v_g = 1.6729 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ - $h_f = 419.04 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - $h_g = 2676.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f = 2257.06 \text{ kJ/kg}$ **Step 2 — Initial and final specific volumes (2 marks):** Initial state (saturated liquid): $$ v_1 = v_f = 0.001043 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} $$ Final state (saturated vapor): $$ v_2 = v_g = 1.6729 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} $$ **Step 3 — Initial and final specific enthalpies (1 mark):** $$ h_1 = h_f = 419.04 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ h_2 = h_g = 2676.1 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 4 — Enthalpy change per kg (1 mark):** $$ \Delta h = h_2 - h_1 = h_{fg} = 2676.1 - 419.04 = 2257.06 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 5 — Total enthalpy change (1 mark):** $$ \Delta H = m \cdot \Delta h = 2 \times 2257.06 = 4514.12 \text{ kJ} $$ **Step 6 — Verify with internal energy check (bonus 1 mark):** This $\Delta H$ also equals the heat added at constant pressure: $Q = m \cdot h_{fg} = 2 \times 2257.06 = 4514.12 \text{ kJ}$ — confirming the first law for a constant-pressure closed system: $Q = \Delta H$. **Final Answers:** - $v_1 = 0.001043 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$, $v_2 = 1.6729 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ - $\Delta H = 4514.12 \text{ kJ}$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Critical trap:** When the state is at *saturated liquid* and final state is *saturated vapor*, the quality changes from $x_1 = 0$ to $x_2 = 1$. Many students erroneously compute a "non-zero" initial quality and a "non-unity" final quality, losing **1.5 marks** for misinterpreting the boundary states. > 2. **Look-up mistake:** Forgetting to read properties at the *correct pressure* or *temperature* (not mixing $100 \text{ °C}$ and $101.325 \text{ kPa}$'s row with the $100 \text{ kPa}$ row) is another 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Constant-pressure check:** The question implies a *constant-pressure* process (piston–cylinder with no friction, no spring, no weight changes). The heat supplied is exactly $\Delta H$ — a key exam-friendly shortcut. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — P-v and P-T Diagram of Pure Substance** - [ ] **Pure substance** = fixed chemical composition, can exist in multiple phases (e.g., water + steam). - [ ] **Three phases:** Solid, Liquid, Vapor (+ Supercritical fluid above critical point). - [ ] **P-v diagram:** Dome-shaped two-phase region. Inside dome = wet region; outside dome = single phase. - [ ] **Saturated liquid line** (left dome) = $x = 0$, $v = v_f$. **Saturated vapor line** (right dome) = $x = 1$, $v = v_g$. - [ ] **Compressed liquid** = left of dome, $T < T_{sat}$. **Superheated vapor** = right of dome, $T > T_{sat}$. - [ ] **Quality formula:** $x = (v - v_f)/(v_g - v_f)$. Always check that $0 \le x \le 1$. - [ ] **Property relations in wet region:** $v = v_f + x v_{fg}$; same form for $u$, $h$, $s$ (all linear in $x$). - [ ] **Latent heat of vaporization:** $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$. Vanishes at critical point. - [ ] **Critical point of water:** $P_{cr} = 22.06 \text{ MPa}$, $T_{cr} = 647.1 \text{ K}$. Above this → supercritical fluid. - [ ] **Triple point of water:** $P_{tp} = 0.006112 \text{ bar}$, $T_{tp} = 273.16 \text{ K}$. All three phases coexist. - [ ] **P-T diagram:** Three curves — *sublimation* (S↔V), *vaporization* (L↔V), *fusion* (S↔L). All meet at triple point. - [ ] **Vaporization curve** terminates at the **critical point**; **sublimation curve** terminates at the **triple point**. - [ ] **Clausius–Clapeyron:** $dP/dT = h_{fg}/(T v_{fg})$ — slope of the P-T vaporization curve. - [ ] **Water anomaly:** Fusion curve has a slightly *negative* slope on P-T diagram (ice is less dense than water). - [ ] **Latent heat additivity:** $h_{ig} = h_{if} + h_{fg}$ (sublimation = fusion + vaporization). - [ ] **During phase change:** $T$ and $P$ are constant, but $v$, $u$, $h$, $s$ all increase. - [ ] **Always use steam tables** (or refrigerant tables) for accurate numerical values — never approximate with ideal gas in the wet region. - [ ] **Ideal-gas assumption is invalid** in the wet region and near saturation; valid only for superheated vapor far from saturation. ---

topics.title: Mollier Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mollier Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Mollier Chart** (also called a **Mollier Diagram** or **Enthalpy–Entropy Diagram**) is a thermodynamic graphical representation in which the **enthalpy (h)** of a working substance is plotted as the ordinate (vertical axis) and the **entropy (s)** as the abscissa (horizontal axis). The chart was developed by the German scientist **Richard Mollier (1863–1933)** in **1904**, originally to study steam engines.

For substances other than water/steam, analogous enthalpy-based charts are used and are also loosely called Mollier charts:
- **Refrigerants (e.g., R-12, R-134a, R-22, NH₃):** Pressure–Enthalpy (p–h) diagram
- **Moist air (Psychrometrics):** Enthalpy–Humidity Ratio (h–ω) chart
- **Gases at high temperature:** Enthalpy–Temperature (h–T) diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** For the B.Tech syllabus, the Mollier Chart primarily refers to the **h–s (enthalpy–entropy) diagram for steam/water**, used extensively to analyze Rankine cycles, throttling, expansion, and isentropic processes. The p–h chart for refrigerants is a closely related sub-topic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a long road trip on a map:
- The **map's vertical coordinate** tells you "how much energy (fuel/altitude) you have at any point."
- The **map's horizontal coordinate** tells you "the disorder/uncertainty (traffic complexity) of the road at that point."

A Mollier chart is exactly such a map for a working substance:
- **Vertical axis (h)** → "How much useful energy (enthalpy) does the steam possess?"
- **Horizontal axis (s)** → "How disordered/randomised is the molecular motion (entropy)?"

The chart lets you **trace the entire thermodynamic journey** of steam — heating, boiling, expansion in a turbine, condensation — by following a path, and read off the energy (enthalpy) at every stop along the way. The chart visually answers the question: *"If I let this steam expand isentropically (no entropy change), how much energy can I extract?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Mollier chose h vs. s (not p vs. v):**
> 1. In any **steady-flow process**, the heat transfer equals the change in enthalpy: $q = \Delta h$ (when KE and PE are negligible). Thus the **vertical distance moved on the chart directly gives the heat transferred**.
> 2. In a **reversible adiabatic (isentropic)** process, $\Delta s = 0$, which means the process appears as a **straight vertical line on the chart**. This is the ideal turbine/compressor trajectory.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Standard Values)

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit (SI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Enthalpy | $h$ | kJ/kg | Vertical axis |
| Entropy | $s$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Horizontal axis |
| Pressure | $p$ | bar or kPa | Plotted as constant-pressure lines |
| Temperature | $T$ | °C or K | Plotted as constant-temperature lines |
| Specific volume | $v$ | m³/kg | Plotted as constant-volume lines |
| Quality (dryness fraction) | $x$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | $0$ = saturated liquid, $1$ = saturated vapour |

> [!TIP]
> The Mollier chart combines the **P-v, T-s, and p-h diagrams** into a single graphical tool, making it the **most powerful** single chart for analysing steam and refrigeration cycles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the h–s Diagram for Steam

The Mollier (h–s) diagram for water/steam consists of three distinct regions, separated by the **saturated liquid line** (left curve) and the **saturated vapour line** (right curve), which meet at the **critical point**.

### Region 1: Subcooled (Compressed) Liquid Region
- Lies to the **left** of the saturated liquid line.
- Water at temperatures **below** the saturation temperature for the given pressure.
- All property lines (constant $p$, $T$, $v$) are very steep and nearly coincide with the saturated liquid line.
- The enthalpy here is nearly equal to that of saturated liquid at the same temperature: $h \approx h_f @ T$.

### Region 2: Wet Region (Two-Phase / Mixture Region)
- Lies **between** the saturated liquid line and the saturated vapour line.
- Contains a mixture of saturated liquid and saturated vapour.
- **Quality (dryness fraction)** $x$ is the key parameter:
$$x = \frac{\text{mass of vapour}}{\text{total mass}} = \frac{m_g}{m_f + m_g}, \quad 0 \le x \le 1$$
- **Constant-pressure lines** and **constant-temperature lines** coincide inside the wet region (both are horizontal, since during phase change at constant pressure, temperature is also constant).
- **Tie lines** (horizontal) are drawn at regular intervals of $x$ (typically 0.1, 0.2, ... 0.9) connecting the two saturation curves.

### Region 3: Superheated Vapour Region
- Lies to the **right** of the saturated vapour line.
- Steam at temperatures **above** the saturation temperature.
- Constant-pressure lines diverge and rise gently to the right.
- Constant-temperature lines (isotherms) lie above the constant-pressure lines in this region (since $T$ increases upward more steeply).

## 2.2 Key Property Relations on the Mollier Chart

For any two-phase (wet) state:
$$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} \quad \text{or} \quad h = h_g - (1-x) \cdot h_{fg}$$
$$s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg} \quad \text{or} \quad s = s_g - (1-x) \cdot s_{fg}$$
$$u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}, \quad v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$$

where subscript $f$ = saturated liquid, $g$ = saturated vapour, and $fg$ = difference (latent).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> On the Mollier chart, the **constant-quality lines** in the wet region are **straight lines** (not curves) connecting $h_f$ to $h_g$ on the two saturation curves. This is a very KTU-favoured exam point.

## 2.3 The Critical Point

The **critical point** is where the saturated liquid and saturated vapour lines meet. For water:
- $T_{cr} = 374.15 \,°\text{C}$
- $p_{cr} = 221.2 \, \text{bar} = 22.12 \, \text{MPa}$
- $h_{cr} = 2087.5 \, \text{kJ/kg}$
- $s_{cr} = 4.412 \, \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$

Above the critical point, the distinction between liquid and vapour vanishes; this is the **supercritical region**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Relation | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h = u + pv$ | Definition of enthalpy | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $ds = \dfrac{dq_{rev}}{T}$ | Entropy definition | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 3 | $dh = T\,ds + v\,dp$ | Combined First & Second Law (Tds) | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $h = h_f + x\,h_{fg}$ | Wet steam enthalpy | kJ/kg |
| 5 | $s = s_f + x\,s_{fg}$ | Wet steam entropy | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 6 | $h_g = h_f + h_{fg}$ | Latent heat of vaporisation | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $w_{turbine} = h_1 - h_2$ | Steady-flow work (turbine) | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $w_{pump} = h_2 - h_1$ | Steady-flow work (pump) | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $q_{evap} = h_g - h_f = h_{fg}$ | Latent heat (constant pressure) | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $q_{boiler} = h_1 - h_4$ (Rankine) | Total heat added in boiler | kJ/kg |
| 11 | $\eta_{Rankine} = \dfrac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_1-h_2)-(h_4-h_3)}{h_1-h_4}$ | Rankine cycle efficiency | dimensionless |
| 12 | $x = \dfrac{s - s_f}{s_{fg}}$ | Quality from entropy | – |
| 13 | $\Delta h_{throttling} = 0$ | Throttling: $h_1 = h_2$ | kJ/kg |
| 14 | $w_{reversible} = -\int v\,dp$ | Flow work, reversible | kJ/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants used in KTU problems for steam:**
> - $R_{steam} = 0.4615 \, \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - $C_p$ (superheated) $\approx 2.1 \, \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - $h_w$ (saturated water at 0 °C) $\approx 0$ (reference)

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why Mollier Charts Matter

1. **Steam Power Plants (Rankine, Reheat, Regenerative cycles):** The chart makes it trivial to read turbine exit enthalpy after isentropic expansion, pump exit enthalpy, and boiler inlet enthalpy — the building blocks of cycle efficiency calculation.
2. **Refrigeration (p–h chart):** COP, refrigeration effect, and compressor work are read off directly as vertical distances.
3. **Throttling / Flash calculations:** For calorimeter problems, $h_1 = h_2$ is a horizontal line on the chart.
4. **Nozzle and Diffuser design:** Isentropic expansion paths are vertical lines.
5. **Psychrometric processes:** Sensible heating, cooling, dehumidification all appear as characteristic straight-line segments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why Constant-Pressure Lines Coincide with Constant-Temperature Lines in the Wet Region

**Start with the combined First and Second Law (Tds equation):**
$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dp$$

**At constant pressure, $dp = 0$:**
$$T\,ds = dh$$

**In the wet region, the temperature is fixed at $T_{sat}$ during phase change** (since pressure is fixed and the substance is in two-phase equilibrium, $T = T_{sat}(p)$). Thus, $T$ is also constant.

**Conclusion:**
$$\frac{dh}{ds} = T = T_{sat} = \text{constant}$$

This means the slope $\frac{dh}{ds}$ of a constant-pressure line equals the saturation temperature. On the Mollier chart, this line is therefore a **straight line with positive slope $T_{sat}$**. Since $T_{sat}$ varies only slightly with pressure compared to the absolute scale, the constant-pressure and constant-temperature lines **coincide** in the wet region.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam line:** "In the wet region of the h–s diagram, constant-pressure lines and constant-temperature lines are the same, lying along horizontal paths (or near-horizontal with slope = T_sat)."

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quality ($x$) from Coordinates on the h–s Chart

**Given:** State point in the wet region. Entropy at the point is $s$. Saturated liquid entropy at the same temperature is $s_f$, and saturated vapour entropy is $s_g$.

**Total entropy of the wet mixture:**
$$s = s_f + x(s_g - s_f) = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$$

**Solving for $x$:**
$$x = \frac{s - s_f}{s_g - s_f} = \frac{s - s_f}{s_{fg}}$$

**Similarly for enthalpy:**
$$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} \implies x = \frac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both expressions for $x$ must give the same answer for the same state — this is a useful self-check in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Enthalpy at a State Given $(T, p)$ — Using Mollier Chart

A common KTU problem type: *"Find the enthalpy of steam at 15 bar and 300 °C."*

**Step 1 — Identify the region:**
$$T_{sat} @ 15 \text{ bar} = 198.3 \,°\text{C}$$
Since $T_{given} = 300 \,°\text{C} > T_{sat}$, the state is in the **superheated region**.

**Step 2 — Locate on chart:**
- Move horizontally along $s$-axis until you find the **constant-pressure line at 15 bar**.
- Then move along the isobar until you reach the **constant-temperature line at 300 °C** (in the superheated region, the isotherm lies above the isobar — they intersect at a single point).

**Step 3 — Read off $h$:**
$$h \approx 3037.6 \, \text{kJ/kg (from superheated steam tables)}$$

**Step 4 — Verify using superheated steam table formula:**
For superheated steam, $h$ can be approximated as:
$$h \approx h_g @ p + C_p^{steam} \cdot (T - T_{sat})$$

$$h_{g} @ 15 \text{ bar} \approx 2792.2 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$h \approx 2792.2 + 2.1 \cdot (300 - 198.3)$$

$$h \approx 2792.2 + 2.1 \cdot 101.7 = 2792.2 + 213.6 = 3005.8 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

The small discrepancy vs. tabular value (3037.6) is due to the $C_p$ approximation; table values are exact.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Isentropic Turbine Exit Quality

**Setup:** Steam enters a turbine at state 1 ($p_1$, $T_1$ superheated). It expands **isentropically** to pressure $p_2$. Find the quality $x_2$ and the work output.

**Step 1 — Identify state 1:** Superheated, so $h_1$ is read directly from the chart at the intersection of the $p_1$ isobar and $T_1$ isotherm.

**Step 2 — Identify state 2:** Since expansion is **isentropic**, $s_2 = s_1$.

**Step 3 — Check if state 2 is wet or superheated:**
- Compare $s_2 = s_1$ with $s_g @ p_2$.
- If $s_2 < s_g @ p_2$ → **wet region** → use quality formula.
- If $s_2 > s_g @ p_2$ → **superheated** → read $h_2$ from superheated tables.

**Step 4 (wet case) — Compute $x_2$:**
$$x_2 = \frac{s_1 - s_f @ p_2}{s_{fg} @ p_2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $h_2$:**
$$h_2 = h_f @ p_2 + x_2 \cdot h_{fg} @ p_2$$

**Step 6 — Compute turbine work:**
$$w_{turbine} = h_1 - h_2 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$

**Worked numerical example (KTU-pattern):**
- $p_1 = 30 \text{ bar}, T_1 = 400 \,°\text{C}$ (superheated)
- $p_2 = 0.1 \text{ bar}$ (condenser)
- Assume isentropic expansion.

From steam tables:
- $h_1 = 3230.9 \, \text{kJ/kg}$, $s_1 = 6.9212 \, \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- At $p_2 = 0.1 \text{ bar}$: $s_f = 0.6493$, $s_g = 8.1502$, $s_{fg} = 7.5009$, $h_f = 191.8$, $h_{fg} = 2392.8$ kJ/kg.

Since $s_2 = s_1 = 6.9212 < s_g = 8.1502$, state 2 is wet:
$$x_2 = \frac{6.9212 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = \frac{6.2719}{7.5009} = 0.8362$$

$$h_2 = 191.8 + 0.8362 \cdot 2392.8 = 191.8 + 2000.9 = 2192.7 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$w_{turbine} = 3230.9 - 2192.7 = 1038.2 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Typical Process Trajectories on the h–s Chart

| Process | Equation | Path on h–s Chart | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Isentropic expansion/compression | $s = \text{const}$ | Vertical straight line | Down (turbine) / Up (compressor) |
| Isobaric heating/cooling | $p = \text{const}$ | Curve (Tds eqn) | Right (heating) / Left (cooling) |
| Isothermal (ideal gas, rev.) | $T = \text{const}$ | Curve $dh = T\,ds$ | Slope = T |
| Throttling | $h = \text{const}$ | **Horizontal straight line** | Right (pressure drop) |
| Constant volume | $v = \text{const}$ | Curve (steeper than isobar) | – |
| Phase change (boiling/cond.) | $p, T$ both const. | **Horizontal line** in wet region | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **throttling process is a horizontal line** on the Mollier chart because enthalpy is conserved. This is the basis of the **throttling calorimeter** for measuring steam quality.

## 3.6 Derivation 5: Work Done and Heat Transfer from Mollier Chart

For a **steady-flow, open system** (neglecting KE, PE):

**First Law:**
$$q - w = h_2 - h_1$$

**Second Law (reversible):**
$$q_{rev} = \int T\,ds \quad \text{or} \quad q = \int T\,ds \text{ (area under process curve on T–s)}$$

**From Mollier chart specifically:**
- $w = -(h_2 - h_1)$ (turbine) → **vertical drop = work output**
- $q = h_2 - h_1$ (constant pressure) → **vertical rise = heat added**

For a complete Rankine cycle traced on the h–s diagram, the area enclosed equals the **net work output per kg** (and equals net heat input by the First Law).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of the h–s (Mollier) Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional regions and line types** of the Mollier chart — a schematic that KTU examiners appreciate for visual clarity.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start(["Start: h-s Chart for Steam"]) --> regions["Chart has 3 main regions"]
    regions --> R1["Region 1: Subcooled Liquid - left of saturated liquid line"]
```
