h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}}$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | | 1 | Nozzle isentropic efficiency | $\eta_N = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$ | — | Also $= C_2^2 \,/\, C_{2s}^2$ | | 2 | Turbine isentropic efficiency | $\eta_T = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$ | — | Numerically same form, different physics | | 3 | Actual nozzle exit velocity | $C_2 = \sqrt{2000\,(h_1 - h_2)}$ | m/s | Conversion factor **2000** comes from $2 \times 1000$ | | 4 | Isentropic nozzle exit velocity | $C_{2s} = \sqrt{2000\,(h_1 - h_{2s})}$ | m/s | — | | 5 | Actual turbine work | $W_{\text{act}} = h_1 - h_2$ | kJ/kg | Per unit mass | | 6 | Isentropic turbine work | $W_{\text{iso}} = h_1 - h_{2s}$ | kJ/kg | — | | 7 | Reheat factor (compound turbines) | $\text{RF} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_n}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$ | — | Out of scope for this topic, related | | 8 | Condition of state 2s | $s_{2s} = s_1$ | kJ/kg·K | Vertical drop on h-s diagram | | 9 | Condition of state 2 | $s_2 > s_1$ | kJ/kg·K | Right-shifted from 2s on h-s diagram | | 10 | Typical $\eta_N$ for convergent nozzle | **0.90 – 0.95** | — | Lower for divergent (Laval) | | 11 | Typical $\eta_T$ (well-designed) | **0.85 – 0.90** | — | Higher with reheating & bleeding | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Trick (memorize this!):** > $1 \text{ kJ/kg} = 1000 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$, so multiplying enthalpy drop (kJ/kg) by **2000** and taking square root gives exit velocity in m/s. > $$C = \sqrt{2 \times 1000 \times \Delta h} = \sqrt{2000 \cdot \Delta h}$$ --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility * **Power Plant Design**: Engineers use $\eta_N$ and $\eta_T$ to size the turbine, predict annual energy output, and calculate the Levelized Cost of Electricity (LCOE). * **Blade Profiling**: Modern 3D-aerofoil blades push $\eta_T$ above 0.92, directly increasing plant efficiency. * **Maintenance Diagnostics**: A drop in measured $\eta_T$ signals blade fouling, seal leakage, or erosion. * **Nozzle Selection**: A drop in $\eta_N$ indicates erosion at the throat of a convergent-divergent (Laval) nozzle. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Nozzle Isentropic Efficiency ### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) For a **nozzle** (no work, no heat transfer, negligible potential energy change), the SFEE per unit mass gives: $$h_1 + \frac{C_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{C_2^2}{2}$$ Rearranging for the kinetic energy change: $$\frac{C_2^2 - C_1^2}{2} = h_1 - h_2$$ Assuming inlet velocity is negligible ($C_1 \approx 0$), this simplifies to: $$\frac{C_2^2}{2} = h_1 - h_2 \tag{3.1}$$ ### 3.1.2 Applying to the Actual Process $$\frac{C_{2,\text{act}}^{\,2}}{2} = h_1 - h_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{2,\text{act}} = \sqrt{2(h_1 - h_2)} \tag{3.2}$$ ### 3.1.3 Applying to the Ideal Isentropic Process $$\frac{C_{2s}^{\,2}}{2} = h_1 - h_{2s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{2s} = \sqrt{2(h_1 - h_{2s})} \tag{3.3}$$ ### 3.1.4 Taking the Ratio By definition: $$\eta_N = \frac{\text{Actual KE at exit}}{\text{Isentropic KE at exit}}$$ Substituting equations (3.2) and (3.3): $$\eta_N = \frac{\frac{C_2^2}{2}}{\frac{C_{2s}^2}{2}} = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_N = \frac{C_2^{\,2}}{C_{2s}^{\,2}} = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}} \tag{3.4}$$ ### 3.1.5 Conversion to SI Units (Important for Numericals) Because $1 \text{ kJ/kg} = 1000 \text{ J/kg} = 1000 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$: $$C_{2s} = \sqrt{2 \times 1000 \times (h_1 - h_{2s})} = \sqrt{2000\,(h_1 - h_{2s})} \quad [\text{m/s}] \tag{3.5}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The numerator of $\eta_N$ is always **smaller** than the denominator because $h_2 > h_{2s}$ (the actual process ends at a higher enthalpy due to irreversibilities). This is why $\eta_N < 1$ always. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Steam Turbine Isentropic Efficiency ### 3.2.1 Starting Point — SFEE for a Turbine (Negligible KE Changes) For a turbine with negligible inlet and exit kinetic energy changes and adiabatic flow: $$h_1 + \frac{C_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{C_2^2}{2} + W_{\text{actual}}$$ Assuming $C_1 \approx C_2$: $$W_{\text{actual}} = h_1 - h_2 \tag{3.6}$$ ### 3.2.2 Ideal (Isentropic) Work Output If the same turbine operated reversibly and adiabatically between the **same** $P_1$ and $P_2$: $$W_{\text{isentropic}} = h_1 - h_{2s} \tag{3.7}$$ ### 3.2.3 Taking the Ratio By definition: $$\eta_T = \frac{W_{\text{actual}}}{W_{\text{isentropic}}} = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_T = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}} \tag{3.8}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerically identical to nozzle efficiency formula but conceptually different!** > * In a **nozzle**, the numerator is **kinetic energy**. > * In a **turbine**, the numerator is **work output**. > The physical meaning of $h_1 - h_2$ changes with the device. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Why Both Efficiencies Exist) | Aspect | Steam Nozzle | Steam Turbine | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Useful output | High-velocity steam jet | Rotating shaft work | | Typical $\eta$ range | **0.90 – 0.95** | **0.80 – 0.92** | | Dominant irreversibility | Wall friction, shock at throat | Blade friction, leakage, eddy loss | | Same exit state as turbine? | Often feeds it (state 2 of nozzle ≈ state 1 of turbine) | Discharges to condenser | | Formula structure | Same form, different physics | Same form, different physics | --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Demonstrating the Full Method **Problem:** Steam at $P_1 = 10$ bar, $h_1 = 2780$ kJ/kg expands in a nozzle to $P_2 = 1$ bar. The isentropic exit enthalpy is $h_{2s} = 2360$ kJ/kg. If the actual exit enthalpy is $h_2 = 2450$ kJ/kg, find: (i) Nozzle isentropic efficiency $\eta_N$ (ii) Actual exit velocity $C_2$ (iii) Isentropic exit velocity $C_{2s}$ **Step 1 — Isentropic Enthalpy Drop** $$\Delta h_s = h_1 - h_{2s} = 2780 - 2360 = 420 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Actual Enthalpy Drop** $$\Delta h_{\text{act}} = h_1 - h_2 = 2780 - 2450 = 330 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3 — Nozzle Isentropic Efficiency** $$\eta_N = \frac{\Delta h_{\text{act}}}{\Delta h_s} = \frac{330}{420} = 0.7857 = 78.57\%$$ **Step 4 — Isentropic Exit Velocity** $$C_{2s} = \sqrt{2000 \times \Delta h_s} = \sqrt{2000 \times 420} = \sqrt{840000} = 916.5 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 5 — Actual Exit Velocity** $$C_2 = \sqrt{2000 \times \Delta h_{\text{act}}} = \sqrt{2000 \times 330} = \sqrt{660000} = 812.4 \text{ m/s}$$ **Verification using velocity ratio:** $$\eta_N = \left(\frac{C_2}{C_{2s}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{812.4}{916.5}\right)^2 = (0.8864)^2 = 0.7857 \;\checkmark$$ # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Nozzle Isentropic Efficiency ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Steam Inlet
State 1: P1, h1, s1] --> B{Select Path} B -- Ideal Isentropic --> C[State 2s
s2s equals s1
Maximum velocity C2s] B -- Real Actual --> D[State 2
s2 greater than s1
Reduced velocity C2] C --> E[Isentropic KE
half C2s squared] D --> F[Actual KE
half C2 squared] E --> G[Divide F by E] F --> G G --> H[Nozzle Isentropic Efficiency etaN] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Steam Turbine Efficiency ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Steam Inlet
State 1: P1, h1, s1] --> B{Two Parallel Processes} B -- Isentropic Process --> C[State 2s
s2s equals s1
Work isentropic] B -- Real Adiabatic --> D[State 2
s2 greater than s1
Work actual] C --> E[Work isentropic
h1 minus h2s] D --> F[Work actual
h1 minus h2] F --> G[Ratio Calculation] E --> G G --> H[Turbine Isentropic Efficiency etaT] ``` ## 4.3 Process Path Visualization on h-s Diagram (Mollier Chart) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph h_s["Mollier h-s Diagram"] direction LR S1[State 1
High P, High h] -- Isentropic Path --> S2s[State 2s
Same s as 1
Lower h, max drop] S1 -- Actual Path --> S2[State 2
Higher s than 1
Higher h than 2s] end style S1 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S2s fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S2 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **How to read the h-s diagram:** * **State 1 → State 2s** is a **vertical drop** (constant entropy, isentropic). * **State 1 → State 2** is a **rightward-curving drop** (entropy increases, irreversibility present). * The **horizontal gap** between 2 and 2s represents **lost work / energy degradation**. ## 4.4 Energy Transformation Sequence (Concept Map) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Boiler produces High-Pressure Steam] --> B[Steam enters Nozzle] B --> C{Nozzle expands steam} C -- Converts enthalpy --> D[High Velocity Jet KE] D --> E[Jet strikes Turbine Blades] E --> F[Turbine converts KE to Shaft Work] F --> G[Generator produces Electricity] C -. etaN controls quality of step C .-> C E -. etaT controls quality of step F .-> F ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Isentropic Efficiency of a Steam Nozzle. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The isentropic efficiency of a steam nozzle is defined as the ratio of the **actual kinetic energy** of the steam at the nozzle exit to the **kinetic energy** that would be obtained if the expansion from the same inlet pressure to the same exit pressure were carried out **isentropically** (reversibly and adiabatically). > > $$\eta_N = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}} = \dfrac{C_2^2}{C_{2s}^2}$$ > > where $h_1$ = inlet enthalpy, $h_2$ = actual exit enthalpy, $h_{2s}$ = isentropic exit enthalpy. It is a measure of how closely the actual nozzle performance approaches the ideal isentropic performance. --- ### Question 2: Define Isentropic Efficiency of a Steam Turbine. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The isentropic efficiency of a steam turbine is defined as the ratio of the **actual work output** per kg of steam to the **work output per kg of steam** that would be obtained if the expansion from the same inlet conditions to the same exit pressure occurred **isentropically**. > > $$\eta_T = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$$ > > where $h_1$ = inlet enthalpy, $h_2$ = actual exit enthalpy, $h_{2s}$ = isentropic exit enthalpy. It quantifies the deviation of the real turbine expansion from the ideal reversible-adiabatic expansion. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Choose either Question A or Question B.** --- ### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Steam enters a nozzle at $P_1 = 20$ bar, $h_1 = 3200$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 7.30$ kJ/kg·K, and expands to $P_2 = 1$ bar. The nozzle isentropic efficiency is 0.90. Determine:** **(a)** The actual exit velocity of steam, and the actual exit enthalpy. **(b)** The mass flow rate required to produce a power output of 1100 kW when this nozzle is used to drive a turbine whose isentropic efficiency is 0.85. Assume the turbine inlet conditions equal the nozzle exit conditions. #### 🔹 Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution **Step 1:** From steam tables at $P_2 = 1$ bar, $s_{2s} = s_1 = 7.30$ kJ/kg·K. For superheated steam at 1 bar with $s = 7.30$ kJ/kg·K: $$h_{2s} \approx 2510 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(read from superheated table)}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the nozzle isentropic efficiency formula: $$\eta_N = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$$ $$0.90 = \frac{3200 - h_2}{3200 - 2510} = \frac{3200 - h_2}{690}$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $h_2$: $$3200 - h_2 = 0.90 \times 690 = 621$$ $$\boxed{h_2 = 3200 - 621 = 2579 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Step 4:** Actual enthalpy drop: $$\Delta h_{\text{act}} = h_1 - h_2 = 3200 - 2579 = 621 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5:** Actual exit velocity: $$C_2 = \sqrt{2000 \times \Delta h_{\text{act}}} = \sqrt{2000 \times 621} = \sqrt{1242000}$$ $$\boxed{C_2 \approx 1114.4 \text{ m/s}}$$ #### 🔹 Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution **Step 1:** Turbine inlet conditions = nozzle exit conditions: $h_{\text{in}} = 2579$ kJ/kg. Assume the same exit pressure $P_2 = 1$ bar, and $s_{\text{in}} = 7.50$ kJ/kg·K (higher than 7.30 due to irreversibilities in nozzle). For this problem, for simplicity assume the isentropic turbine exit enthalpy from $s_{\text{in}} = 7.30$ at 1 bar gives $h_{2s,\text{turb}} = 2510$ kJ/kg (as the dominant reference). **Step 2:** Actual turbine work per kg (using $\eta_T$): $$W_{\text{act,turb}} = \eta_T \times (h_{\text{in}} - h_{2s,\text{turb}}) = 0.85 \times (2579 - 2510) = 0.85 \times 69 = 58.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3:** For a power output of 1100 kW = 1100 kJ/s: $$\dot{m} = \frac{\text{Power}}{W_{\text{act,turb}}} = \frac{1100 \text{ kJ/s}}{58.65 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 18.76 \text{ kg/s}}$$ --- ### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` **A steam turbine receives steam at $P_1 = 30$ bar, $T_1 = 350°C$, $h_1 = 3125$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 6.85$ kJ/kg·K. The exhaust pressure is $P_2 = 0.10$ bar and the isentropic exit enthalpy is $h_{2s} = 2115$ kJ/kg. The actual exit enthalpy is $h_2 = 2280$ kJ/kg.** **(a)** Calculate the isentropic efficiency of the turbine and the actual work output per kg of steam. **(b)** If the steam mass flow rate is 25 kg/s and the generator efficiency is 0.95, find the electrical power output in MW. #### 🔹 Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution **Step 1:** Identify given values: $$P_1 = 30 \text{ bar}, \; h_1 = 3125 \text{ kJ/kg}, \; s_1 = 6.85 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ $$P_2 = 0.10 \text{ bar}, \; h_{2s} = 2115 \text{ kJ/kg}, \; h_2 = 2280 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate isentropic work and actual work: $$W_{\text{iso}} = h_1 - h_{2s} = 3125 - 2115 = 1010 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_{\text{act}} = h_1 - h_2 = 3125 - 2280 = 845 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate isentropic efficiency: $$\eta_T = \frac{W_{\text{act}}}{W_{\text{iso}}} = \frac{845}{1010} = 0.8366$$ $$\boxed{\eta_T \approx 83.66\%}$$ **Step 4:** Actual work output per kg: $$\boxed{W_{\text{act}} = 845 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ #### 🔹 Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution **Step 1:** Total mechanical power output of turbine: $$P_{\text{mech}} = \dot{m} \times W_{\text{act}} = 25 \times 845 = 21125 \text{ kW} = 21.125 \text{ MW}$$ **Step 2:** Apply generator efficiency to obtain electrical output: $$P_{\text{elec}} = \eta_{\text{gen}} \times P_{\text{mech}} = 0.95 \times 21.125$$ $$\boxed{P_{\text{elec}} \approx 20.07 \text{ MW}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (Loses 2–3 marks):** Students often write $C = \sqrt{2 \Delta h}$ without converting kJ/kg → J/kg. **Always multiply by 2000** for $C$ in m/s. > > 2. **Reversing $\eta_N$ Definition (Loses 1 mark):** Some students write $\eta_N = (h_1 - h_{2s})/(h_1 - h_2)$. This is the **inverse** and will be marked wrong. Numerator is always the **actual** energy, denominator the **ideal** energy. > > 3. **Ignoring Inlet Velocity (OK, but state it):** KTU sometimes gives a non-zero $C_1$. You must include it in the SFEE: $C_2^2/2 = C_1^2/2 + (h_1 - h_2)$. Mention this assumption explicitly. > > 4. **Mixing $h_{2s}$ for nozzle and turbine (Loses 2 marks):** When a nozzle feeds a turbine, the **actual** nozzle exit state is the **inlet** state of the turbine. The isentropic point for the turbine must be computed from the turbine's inlet entropy (which is higher than the original $s_1$). > > 5. **Forgetting to state assumptions** (Loses 0.5–1 mark): Always write: "Assuming adiabatic flow, negligible PE changes, and steady state" at the start. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember | # | Take-Away Point | | :---: | :--- | | 1 | Isentropic efficiency is a **performance metric**, not an energy quantity — it is dimensionless and $\le 1$. | | 2 | Both **nozzle** and **turbine** use the **same algebraic form** $\eta = (h_1 - h_2)/(h_1 - h_{2s})$ but the underlying physics (KE vs work) is different. | | 3 | On the **h-s diagram**, point **2s is directly below 1** (vertical drop), point **2 is to the right of 2s** (entropy increase). | | 4 | **Actual** process always ends at **higher enthalpy** than the isentropic process for the same exit pressure. | | 5 | Velocity conversion: $C = \sqrt{2000\,\Delta h}$ where $\Delta h$ is in kJ/kg and $C$ is in m/s. | | 6 | Typical $\eta_N$ (convergent-divergent nozzle): **0.90 – 0.96**. | | 7 | Typical $\eta_T$ (modern turbine): **0.85 – 0.92**. | | 8 | **Higher irreversibility ⇒ lower efficiency ⇒ more entropy generation.** | | 9 | In compound turbines, use the **stage-by-stage** efficiency for each row of blades, not a single overall value. | | 10 | Always state **assumptions** explicitly before starting any numerical problem in KTU exams. | | 11 | If the problem says "find work output," use **$\eta_T$ formula**. If it says "find exit velocity," use **$\eta_N$ formula**. | | 12 | The **loss due to irreversibility** (lost work) per kg = $(1 - \eta_T)(h_1 - h_{2s})$ for turbines. | > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic (Save in your head!):** > **"Actual on Top, Ideal on Bottom" — ALWAYS.** > Whether it is a nozzle, turbine, compressor, or pump, the numerator of the isentropic efficiency is always the **actual** performance, and the denominator is the **ideal** (isentropic) performance. This single sentence has saved KTU toppers many marks!

topics.title: Ideal Gas Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Ideal Gas Equation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) The **Ideal Gas Equation** (also called the **Equation of State of an Ideal Gas** or **Characteristic Gas Equation**) is a constitutive thermodynamic relation that mathematically interlinks the four measurable state variables of a gas — **pressure ($P$)**, **volume ($V$)**, **temperature ($T$)**, and **amount of substance (mass $m$ or moles $n$)** — under the assumption of **ideal gas behaviour** (no intermolecular forces, negligible molecular volume, perfectly elastic collisions). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** An *ideal gas* is a hypothetical gas whose molecules occupy negligible space and have no intermolecular attractions, and which rigorously obeys the combined gas laws throughout the entire range of thermodynamic states. The two most commonly employed forms in engineering thermodynamics are: $$PV = mRT$$ $$PV = n\overline{R}T$$ where, - $P$ = Absolute pressure of the gas ($Pa$ or $kPa$) - $V$ = Total volume occupied by the gas ($m^3$) - $m$ = Mass of the gas ($kg$) - $n$ = Number of moles ($kmol$) - $T$ = Absolute thermodynamic temperature ($K$) - $R$ = **Characteristic Gas Constant** (specific to each gas), $kJ/kg \cdot K$ - $\overline{R}$ = **Universal Gas Constant** = **8.314 kJ/kmol·K** (same for all gases) --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **party balloon** 🎈 floating in your room. 1. **Push it into a small box (decrease volume)** — the gas molecules have less space, so they crash into the walls more often. This increases the **pressure** ($P \uparrow V \downarrow$). 2. **Heat the balloon over a hair dryer (increase temperature)** — the molecules move faster and hit the walls harder, expanding the balloon. The **volume and pressure increase** ($T \uparrow V \uparrow$). 3. **Pump more air into the balloon (increase mass)** — more molecules means more collisions, raising the pressure for the same volume ($m \uparrow P \uparrow$). The **Ideal Gas Equation** is simply the *mathematical rulebook* that ties together these three everyday observations into a single elegant equation: **"How hard do molecules push, how much room they need, how hot they are, and how many of them there are — all balance out to a constant."** --- ## 1.3 Standard Constants and Metrics (Highlight Box) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Memorise KTU Board Values:** > - **Universal Gas Constant:** $\overline{R} = 8.314 \, \dfrac{kJ}{kmol \cdot K}$ > - **Gas Constant for Air:** $R_{air} = 0.287 \, \dfrac{kJ}{kg \cdot K}$ (since $M_{air} = 28.97 \, kg/kmol$) > - **Standard Temperature:** $T_{std} = 273.15 \, K$ (i.e., $0^\circ C$) > - **Standard Pressure:** $P_{std} = 101.325 \, kPa$ (i.e., $1 \, atm$) > - **Normal Molar Volume** (at STP): $V_m = 22.414 \, m^3/kmol$ --- ## 1.4 Real-World Significance The Ideal Gas Equation is the **backbone of all gas-cycle analysis** in: | Engineering Domain | Practical Application | |---|---| | 🚗 **Internal Combustion Engines** | Computing state changes across Otto / Diesel cycles | | ✈️ **Jet Propulsion** | Thrust calculation using $PV = mRT$ for combustion gases | | ❄️ **Refrigeration & HVAC** | Determining refrigerant $P$-$T$ relationships | | 🏭 **Compressed Gas Storage** | Cylinder pressure-temperature-mass relations | | 🌬️ **Blowers & Compressors** | Volumetric efficiency and discharge calculations | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Three Governing Gas Laws The Ideal Gas Equation is the **combination of three empirical laws**, each isolating one variable's effect on another. Let us derive them stepwise: ### Law 1: Boyle's Law (1662) — Effect of Pressure on Volume At **constant temperature** ($T = $ const), the volume of a fixed mass of gas is **inversely proportional** to its absolute pressure. $$V \propto \dfrac{1}{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad PV = \text{constant} \quad (T, m = \text{const})$$ ### Law 2: Charles's Law (1787) — Effect of Temperature on Volume At **constant pressure** ($P = $ const), the volume of a fixed mass of gas is **directly proportional** to its absolute temperature. $$V \propto T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{V}{T} = \text{constant} \quad (P, m = \text{const})$$ ### Law 3: Avogadro's Law (1811) — Effect of Mass/Amount on Volume At **constant temperature and pressure**, equal volumes of all gases contain **equal numbers of molecules**. $$V \propto n \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{V}{n} = \text{constant} \quad (T, P = \text{const})$$ ### Combining the Three Laws Mathematically combining all three proportionalities: $$V \propto \dfrac{nT}{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad PV = n\overline{R}T$$ This is the **Ideal Gas Equation** in its most general form, where $\overline{R}$ is the proportionality constant known as the **Universal Gas Constant**. --- ## 2.2 Alternative Mathematical Forms of the Ideal Gas Equation Using the relation $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ (where $M$ is the molar mass in $kg/kmol$): $$PV = \dfrac{m}{M} \cdot \overline{R} T$$ Defining the **Characteristic Gas Constant** as: $$R = \dfrac{\overline{R}}{M}$$ We obtain the most engineering-friendly form: $$PV = mRT$$ Dividing both sides by mass $m$ and defining specific volume $v = V/m$: $$Pv = RT$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** The form $Pv = RT$ is the most-used version in **thermodynamic property relations** and **per-unit-mass cycle analysis**. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Description | KTU Usage | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | $PV = mRT$ | General ideal gas equation | State determination | | 2 | $PV = n\overline{R}T$ | Molar form | Combustion calculations | | 3 | $Pv = RT$ | Specific volume form | Per-unit-mass analysis | | 4 | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ | Combined gas law | Process calculations | | 5 | $R = \dfrac{\overline{R}}{M}$ | Relation between R and $\overline{R}$ | Find gas constant | | 6 | $\overline{R} = 8.314 \, kJ/(kmol \cdot K)$ | Universal constant | All gas mixtures | | 7 | $R_{air} = 0.287 \, kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ | Specific R for air | Air-standard cycles | | 8 | $v = \dfrac{V}{m} = \dfrac{RT}{P}$ | Specific volume | Property tables | | 9 | $\rho = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ | Density from equation of state | Flow problems | | 10 | $M = \dfrac{m}{n}$ | Molar mass | Molecular weight | | 11 | $\dfrac{P_1 v_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 v_2}{T_2}$ | Combined law per unit mass | Closed-system processes | | 12 | $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ | Mole calculation | Stoichiometry | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary / Validity Conditions for Ideal Gas Equation:** > - Pressure must be **absolute** (not gauge or vacuum) — convert: $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ > - Temperature must be **absolute** in **Kelvin** — convert: $T(K) = T(^\circ C) + 273.15$ > - Gas must be at **low pressure** (typically $P < 10 \, bar$) or **high temperature** ($T > T_{critical}$) > - Real gases follow this closely when $Z$ (compressibility factor) $\approx 1$ --- ## 2.4 Why the Equation Matters — Engineering Utility The ideal gas equation is the **first step** in solving virtually every gas-related problem: - **Closed System (Constant Mass):** Use $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ to find the unknown state given three knowns. - **Open System (Steady Flow):** Use $\rho = P/RT$ along with the continuity equation. - **Process Analysis:** Isothermal ($T = $ const) → $PV = $ const; Isobaric ($P = $ const) → $V/T = $ const; Isochoric ($V = $ const) → $P/T = $ const. - **Mixture Analysis:** For ideal gas mixtures, **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** applies: $P_{total} = \sum P_i$ and $P_i V = m_i R_i T$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Boyle's & Charles's Laws to $PV = mRT$ ### Step 1: Start with Boyle's Law For a fixed mass of gas at constant temperature: $$PV = C_1 \quad \text{(where } C_1 \text{ depends on mass and temperature)}$$ ### Step 2: Introduce Charles's Law (Effect of Temperature) When the temperature changes from $T_1$ to $T_2$ at constant pressure, the volume changes. So we need to incorporate temperature: $$V \propto T \quad (P = \text{const})$$ Combining both proportionalities into one statement: $$V \propto \dfrac{T}{P} \quad \text{(holding mass constant)}$$ ### Step 3: Introduce the Proportionality Constant The proportionality needs a constant to convert proportionality to equality: $$V = K \cdot \dfrac{T}{P}$$ where $K$ depends on the **amount of gas** present. Re-arranging: $$PV = K \cdot T$$ ### Step 4: Express $K$ in Terms of Mass and Gas Constant Since $K$ depends on the quantity of gas, write $K = mR$ (mass times characteristic gas constant): $$PV = mR \cdot T$$ ### Step 5: Final Compact Form $$\boxed{PV = mRT}$$ This derivation is the **golden standard KTU derivation** worth **7 marks** in ESE. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Relation Between $\overline{R}$ and $R$ ### Starting Point By Avogadro's hypothesis, equal volumes of all gases at the same $T$ and $P$ contain equal numbers of molecules. One **kmol** of any gas occupies $22.414 \, m^3$ at STP. ### Step 1: Apply Ideal Gas Equation for 1 kmol $$PV = n\overline{R}T$$ For $n = 1 \, kmol$, $T = 273.15 \, K$, $P = 101.325 \, kPa$, $V = 22.414 \, m^3$: $$101.325 \times 22.414 = 1 \times \overline{R} \times 273.15$$ ### Step 2: Solve for $\overline{R}$ $$\overline{R} = \dfrac{101.325 \times 22.414}{273.15}$$ $$\overline{R} = 8.314 \, \dfrac{kJ}{kmol \cdot K}$$ ### Step 3: Generalisation For any gas, consider $n$ kmol: $$PV = n\overline{R}T$$ But also $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$, so: $$PV = \dfrac{m}{M} \overline{R} T = m \left(\dfrac{\overline{R}}{M}\right) T$$ ### Step 4: Define $R$ $$\boxed{R = \dfrac{\overline{R}}{M}}$$ where $M$ is the molar mass in $kg/kmol$. --- ## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Examples (KTU Board Style) ### **Example 1: Basic State Determination [7 Marks]** > **Problem:** A cylinder contains $0.4 \, m^3$ of nitrogen gas at $100 \, kPa$ and $27^\circ C$. Determine: > (a) The mass of nitrogen in the cylinder. > (b) The final pressure if the gas is heated at constant volume to $127^\circ C$. > > Take $R_{N_2} = 0.2968 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$. #### **Solution (a): Mass of Nitrogen** **Given:** $V = 0.4 \, m^3$, $P_1 = 100 \, kPa$, $T_1 = 27 + 273.15 = 300.15 \, K$, $R = 0.2968 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$ Using the ideal gas equation: $$P_1 V_1 = m R T_1$$ Solving for $m$: $$m = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1}$$ Substituting: $$m = \dfrac{100 \times 0.4}{0.2968 \times 300.15}$$ $$m = \dfrac{40}{89.0845}$$ $$\boxed{m = 0.449 \, kg}$$ #### **Solution (b): Final Pressure at Constant Volume** **Given:** $V = $ const $= 0.4 \, m^3$, $T_2 = 127 + 273.15 = 400.15 \, K$, $m = 0.449 \, kg$ Apply ideal gas equation again: $$P_2 V = m R T_2$$ $$P_2 = \dfrac{m R T_2}{V} = \dfrac{0.449 \times 0.2968 \times 400.15}{0.4}$$ $$\boxed{P_2 = 133.34 \, kPa}$$ --- ### **Example 2: Combined Gas Law Application [7 Marks]** > **Problem:** A gas initially at $1.5 \, bar$, $0.05 \, m^3$, and $320 \, K$ is compressed isothermally to a volume of $0.02 \, m^3$. It is then heated at constant volume until the pressure becomes $6 \, bar$. Determine: > (a) Final temperature after heating. > (b) Mass of the gas. ($R = 0.287 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$) #### **Solution (a): Final Temperature** **State 1:** $P_1 = 150 \, kPa$, $V_1 = 0.05 \, m^3$, $T_1 = 320 \, K$ **State 3 (final):** $P_3 = 600 \, kPa$, $V_3 = 0.02 \, m^3$ Using the combined gas law directly from state 1 to state 3: $$\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_3 V_3}{T_3}$$ $$T_3 = \dfrac{P_3 V_3 T_1}{P_1 V_1} = \dfrac{600 \times 0.02 \times 320}{150 \times 0.05}$$ $$T_3 = \dfrac{3840}{7.5}$$ $$\boxed{T_3 = 512 \, K}$$ #### **Solution (b): Mass of Gas** $$m = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1} = \dfrac{150 \times 0.05}{0.287 \times 320}$$ $$\boxed{m = 0.0817 \, kg}$$ --- ### **Example 3: Characteristic Gas Constant Calculation [7 Marks]** > **Problem:** A gas mixture consists of $2 \, kg$ of $O_2$, $5 \, kg$ of $N_2$, and $1 \, kg$ of $CO_2$. > Determine: > (a) The mass fraction and mole fraction of each component. > (b) The equivalent gas constant of the mixture. > > Given: $M_{O_2} = 32$, $M_{N_2} = 28$, $M_{CO_2} = 44 \, kg/kmol$. #### **Solution (a): Mass and Mole Fractions** Total mass $m = 2 + 5 + 1 = 8 \, kg$ **Mass Fractions:** $mf_{O_2} = 2/8 = 0.25$, $mf_{N_2} = 5/8 = 0.625$, $mf_{CO_2} = 1/8 = 0.125$ **Moles of Each Component:** $n_{O_2} = 2/32 = 0.0625 \, kmol$ $n_{N_2} = 5/28 = 0.1786 \, kmol$ $n_{CO_2} = 1/44 = 0.0227 \, kmol$ Total moles $n = 0.0625 + 0.1786 + 0.0227 = 0.2638 \, kmol$ **Mole Fractions:** $x_{O_2} = 0.0625/0.2638 = 0.237$ $x_{N_2} = 0.1786/0.2638 = 0.677$ $x_{CO_2} = 0.0227/0.2638 = 0.086$ #### **Solution (b): Equivalent Gas Constant of Mixture** Individual gas constants: $R_{O_2} = 8.314/32 = 0.2598 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$ $R_{N_2} = 8.314/28 = 0.2969 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$ $R_{CO_2} = 8.314/44 = 0.1889 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$ Mixture gas constant (mass-fraction weighted): $$R_{mix} = \sum mf_i \cdot R_i$$ $$R_{mix} = (0.25)(0.2598) + (0.625)(0.2969) + (0.125)(0.1889)$$ $$R_{mix} = 0.0650 + 0.1856 + 0.0236$$ $$\boxed{R_{mix} = 0.2742 \, kJ/kg \cdot K}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Ideal Gas Equation Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: Ideal Gas System]) --> StateVar[State Variables] StateVar --> PVar[Pressure P
absolute kPa] StateVar --> VVar[Volume V
cubic meter] StateVar --> TVar[Temperature T
Kelvin absolute] StateVar --> MVar[Mass m or moles n] StateVar --> Constants[Fundamental Constants] Constants --> UConst[Universal R bar
8.314 kJ per kmol K] Constants --> SConst[Specific R
R bar divided by M] Constants --> MConst[Molar Mass M
kg per kmol] PVar --> Eq1[Equation Form 1] VVar --> Eq1 TVar --> Eq1 MVar --> Eq1 UConst --> Eq1 MConst --> Eq1 Eq1 --> FormA[Form A
PV equals n R bar T] Eq1 --> FormB[Form B
PV equals m R T] Eq1 --> FormC[Form C
Pv equals R T] FormA --> App1[Engineering Applications] FormB --> App1 FormC --> App1 App1 --> ClosedSys[Closed System Analysis] App1 --> OpenSys[Open System Analysis] App1 --> CycleAnal[Cycle Analysis
Otto Diesel Brayton] App1 --> MixAnal[Mixture Analysis
Dalton Law] ClosedSys --> Solve1[Use Combined Gas Law] OpenSys --> Solve2[Use Density equals P by RT] CycleAnal --> Solve3[Use Pv equals RT per unit mass] MixAnal --> Solve4[Use Partial Pressures] Solve1 --> End([Final State Determined]) Solve2 --> End Solve3 --> End Solve4 --> End ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Derivation Path from Gas Laws to Ideal Gas Equation ```mermaid flowchart LR BL[Boyle Law 1662
PV equals constant at const T] --> Combine{Combine Laws} CL[Charles Law 1787
V by T equals constant at const P] --> Combine AL[Avogadro Law 1811
V proportional to n at const T P] --> Combine Combine --> Prop[V proportional to nT divided by P] Prop --> Const[Introduce Proportionality Constant] Const --> EqMol[PV equals n R bar T] EqMol --> Conv[Convert n equals m by M] Conv --> EqMass[PV equals m R T where R equals R bar by M] EqMass --> EqSpec[Divide by mass
Pv equals RT] EqSpec --> Final((Ideal Gas Equation
Established)) ``` ## 4.3 Schematic State Diagram — Three Ideal Gas Processes ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A[Isothermal Process T constant] A1[State 1
P1 V1] -->|PV equals const| A2[State 2
P2 V2] end subgraph B[Isobaric Process P constant] B1[State 1
V1 T1] -->|V by T equals const| B2[State 2
V2 T2] end subgraph C[Isochoric Process V constant] C1[State 1
P1 T1] -->|P by T equals const| C2[State 2
P2 T2] end ``` ## 4.4 Comprehensive Variable Relationship Web ```mermaid flowchart TD IDEQ[PV equals mRT] --> R1[Pressure P
Directly Proportional] IDEQ --> R2[Mass m
Directly Proportional] IDEQ --> R3[Temperature T
Directly Proportional] IDEQ --> R4[Volume V
Directly Proportional] IDEQ --> R5[Gas Constant R
Constant of Proportionality] R1 --> CalcP[Solve for P
P equals mRT by V] R2 --> CalcM[Solve for m
m equals PV by RT] R3 --> CalcT[Solve for T
T equals PV by mR] R4 --> CalcV[Solve for V
V equals mRT by P] R5 --> CalcR[Solve for R
R equals PV by mT] CalcP --> Result[State Property Found] CalcM --> Result CalcT --> Result CalcV --> Result CalcR --> Result ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > Define the Ideal Gas Equation. What is the physical significance of the universal gas constant? #### Model Answer (3 Marks): **Definition (2 Marks):** The Ideal Gas Equation is the equation of state for an ideal gas, mathematically expressed as $PV = mRT$, where $P$ is absolute pressure, $V$ is volume, $m$ is mass, $T$ is absolute temperature, and $R$ is the characteristic gas constant. The molar form is $PV = n\overline{R}T$, where $\overline{R}$ is the universal gas constant. **Physical Significance (1 Mark):** The universal gas constant $\overline{R} = 8.314 \, kJ/kmol \cdot K$ represents the work done by one kmol of an ideal gas per degree Kelvin rise in temperature at constant pressure. It is a fundamental constant that is **identical for all gases**, bridging the gap between the microscopic molecular world (moles) and macroscopic thermodynamic properties. --- ### **Question 2** > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > Differentiate between the Universal Gas Constant and the Characteristic Gas Constant. Why is air's gas constant taken as $0.287 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$ in air-standard cycle analysis? #### Model Answer (3 Marks): **Difference (2 Marks):** | Parameter | Universal Gas Constant ($\overline{R}$) | Characteristic Gas Constant ($R$) | |:---|:---|:---| | Symbol | $\overline{R}$ or $R_u$ | $R$ | | Value | $\overline{R} = 8.314 \, kJ/(kmol \cdot K)$ — constant for all gases | $R = \overline{R}/M$ — varies with gas | | Basis | Per **kmol** of gas | Per **kg** of gas | | Use | Molar calculations, combustion | Per-mass cycle analysis | They are related by $\boxed{R = \dfrac{\overline{R}}{M}}$ where $M$ is molar mass in $kg/kmol$. **Why Air's R = 0.287 (1 Mark):** For air, the molar mass $M_{air} = 28.97 \, kg/kmol$. Substituting: $$R_{air} = \dfrac{8.314}{28.97} = 0.287 \, kJ/(kg \cdot K)$$ This specific value is universally adopted in **air-standard cycle analysis** (Otto, Diesel, Brayton) because air is approximated as the working fluid with constant specific heats and constant $R$ throughout the cycle. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** > **(a)** Derive the Ideal Gas Equation from Boyle's Law and Charles's Law, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A rigid tank contains $10 \, kg$ of air at $200 \, kPa$ and $27^\circ C$. The tank is heated until the pressure becomes $400 \, kPa$. Determine: > - (i) The final temperature of air inside the tank. > - (ii) The volume of the tank. > - (iii) The change in internal energy if $c_v = 0.718 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$. > > Take $R_{air} = 0.287 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution to Question A (a) — Derivation (7 Marks) **Assumptions of Ideal Gas:** 1. Gas molecules are point masses with negligible volume compared to container volume. 2. No intermolecular forces of attraction or repulsion exist between molecules. 3. All collisions between molecules and with walls are perfectly elastic. 4. The gas obeys Boyle's Law, Charles's Law, and Avogadro's Law strictly. **Step 1: Boyle's Law (1662)** At constant temperature, for a fixed mass of gas: $$PV = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V \propto \dfrac{1}{P} \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **Step 2: Charles's Law (1787)** At constant pressure, for a fixed mass of gas: $$\dfrac{V}{T} = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V \propto T \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Step 3: Combine Both Proportionalities** From (1) and (2), we can combine into a single proportionality (with mass held constant): $$V \propto \dfrac{T}{P} \quad \text{...(3)}$$ **Step 4: Account for Mass Using Avogadro's Law** The proportionality constant depends on the mass of gas. By Avogadro's hypothesis, equal moles occupy equal volumes at same $T$ and $P$, so the proportionality must include mass directly: $$V = K \cdot \dfrac{mT}{P}$$ **Step 5: Re-arrange and Define Constant** $$PV = K \cdot mT$$ Defining $K = R$ (the characteristic gas constant specific to the gas), we obtain: $$\boxed{PV = mRT}$$ where $R$ is determined experimentally for each gas. For $n$ kmol, $m = nM$, giving $PV = n \dfrac{\overline{R}}{M} \cdot MT = n\overline{R}T$, hence $\boxed{PV = n\overline{R}T}$. --- #### Solution to Question A (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Given Data:** - $m = 10 \, kg$, $P_1 = 200 \, kPa$, $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300 \, K$ - $P_2 = 400 \, kPa$, $R_{air} = 0.287 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$, $c_v = 0.718 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$ - Rigid tank → Volume is constant ($V_1 = V_2$) **(i) Final Temperature (2 Marks):** For a rigid tank, $V = $ const, so: $$\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$$ $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = 300 \times \dfrac{400}{200}$$ $$\boxed{T_2 = 600 \, K}$$ **(ii) Volume of the Tank (2 Marks):** Using $P_1 V = mRT_1$: $$V = \dfrac{mRT_1}{P_1} = \dfrac{10 \times 0.287 \times 300}{200}$$ $$\boxed{V = 4.305 \, m^3}$$ **(iii) Change in Internal Energy (3 Marks):** For an ideal gas, internal energy is a function of temperature only: $$\Delta U = m c_v (T_2 - T_1)$$ $$\Delta U = 10 \times 0.718 \times (600 - 300)$$ $$\Delta U = 10 \times 0.718 \times 300$$ $$\boxed{\Delta U = 2154 \, kJ}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** > **(a)** Explain the characteristic gas equation. Derive the relationship between the universal gas constant and the characteristic gas constant. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A gas has a molar mass of $32 \, kg/kmol$ and is contained in a vessel of $0.5 \, m^3$ at $300 \, K$ and $150 \, kPa$. > - (i) Calculate the mass of the gas in the vessel. > - (ii) If the gas is cooled at constant pressure to $250 \, K$, determine the new volume. > - (iii) Determine the density of the gas at initial conditions. > > Take $\overline{R} = 8.314 \, kJ/kmol \cdot K$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution to Question B (a) — Theory (7 Marks) **Explanation (3 Marks):** The **characteristic gas equation** (or ideal gas equation in specific form) is the equation of state that relates the pressure, specific volume, and temperature of an ideal gas: $$Pv = RT$$ where $P$ is absolute pressure, $v = V/m$ is the specific volume (volume per unit mass), $T$ is absolute temperature, and $R$ is the characteristic gas constant (also called the specific gas constant). This equation is **specific to each gas** because $R$ depends on the molar mass $M$ of the gas. It is the most convenient form for **per-unit-mass analysis** in cycles such as Otto, Diesel, and Brayton. **Derivation of Relationship (4 Marks):** Start with the ideal gas equation in molar form: $$PV = n\overline{R}T \quad \text{...(i)}$$ The number of moles $n$ is related to mass $m$ and molar mass $M$ by: $$n = \dfrac{m}{M} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ Substituting (ii) into (i): $$PV = \dfrac{m}{M} \overline{R} T$$ $$PV = m \left(\dfrac{\overline{R}}{M}\right) T \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ Define the characteristic gas constant as: $$\boxed{R = \dfrac{\overline{R}}{M}}$$ Then (iii) becomes: $$PV = mRT$$ Dividing both sides by mass $m$ (where $v = V/m$): $$\boxed{Pv = RT}$$ **Conclusion:** $R = \overline{R}/M$ shows that the characteristic gas constant is **inversely proportional to molar mass**. Light gases have higher $R$, while heavy gases have lower $R$. The universal gas constant $\overline{R} = 8.314 \, kJ/kmol \cdot K$ is the same for all gases, but $R$ varies. For example, hydrogen ($M = 2$) has $R = 4.157 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$, while $CO_2$ ($M = 44$) has $R = 0.1889 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$. --- #### Solution to Question B (b) — Numerical (7 Marks) **Given:** $M = 32 \, kg/kmol$, $V = 0.5 \, m^3$, $T_1 = 300 \, K$, $P_1 = 150 \, kPa$, $\overline{R} = 8.314 \, kJ/kmol \cdot K$ **Step 0: Find R (1 Mark)** $$R = \dfrac{\overline{R}}{M} = \dfrac{8.314}{32} = 0.2598 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$$ **(i) Mass of Gas (2 Marks):** $$m = \dfrac{P_1 V}{R T_1} = \dfrac{150 \times 0.5}{0.2598 \times 300}$$ $$m = \dfrac{75}{77.94}$$ $$\boxed{m = 0.9623 \, kg}$$ **(ii) New Volume at Constant Pressure (2 Marks):** At constant pressure: $$\dfrac{V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{T_2}$$ $$V_2 = V_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = 0.5 \times \dfrac{250}{300}$$ $$\boxed{V_2 = 0.4167 \, m^3}$$ **(iii) Density at Initial Conditions (2 Marks):** $$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{0.9623}{0.5}$$ Alternative using $\rho = P/RT$: $$\rho = \dfrac{P_1}{R T_1} = \dfrac{150}{0.2598 \times 300} = \dfrac{150}{77.94}$$ $$\boxed{\rho = 1.9246 \, kg/m^3}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **🔴 TOP MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES (Read Carefully!)** > > **Mistake 1: Using Gauge Pressure Instead of Absolute Pressure** > Students plug in gauge readings directly into $PV = mRT$. **Always convert:** > $$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} \quad (P_{atm} = 101.325 \, kPa)$$ > Marks lost: typically **2 out of 7** if the unit confusion isn't clarified. > > **Mistake 2: Forgetting Kelvin Conversion** > Temperature in the ideal gas equation **MUST be in Kelvin**. If the problem gives $^\circ C$, add 273.15 immediately. > $$T(K) = T(^\circ C) + 273.15$$ > > **Mistake 3: Confusing Universal and Characteristic Gas Constants** > - Universal $\overline{R} = 8.314 \, kJ/(kmol \cdot K)$ — used with **moles** ($n$). > - Characteristic $R = \overline{R}/M$ — used with **mass** ($m$). > Mixing them gives numerically wrong answers. > > **Mistake 4: Wrong Molar Mass Units** > $M$ must be in $kg/kmol$, NOT $g/mol$. Although numerically equal ($32 \, g/mol = 32 \, kg/kmol$), forgetting this in conceptual questions costs marks. > > **Mistake 5: Skipping the Derivation Steps** > KTU board examiners **require** the derivation of $PV = mRT$ from Boyle's + Charles's Laws to be written step-by-step with explicit proportionality signs. Writing only the final formula = **0 marks**. > > **Mistake 6: Forgetting to State Assumptions** > When asked "Define ideal gas" or "Derive ideal gas equation," the **assumptions** (point masses, no intermolecular forces, perfectly elastic collisions) carry **2 marks** by themselves. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋 ### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist - ✅ **Ideal Gas Equation (mass form):** $PV = mRT$ - ✅ **Ideal Gas Equation (molar form):** $PV = n\overline{R}T$ - ✅ **Ideal Gas Equation (specific form):** $Pv = RT$ - ✅ **Universal Gas Constant:** $\overline{R} = 8.314 \, kJ/(kmol \cdot K)$ - ✅ **Characteristic Gas Constant Relation:** $R = \overline{R}/M$ - ✅ **Standard Temperature & Pressure (STP):** $T = 273.15 \, K$, $P = 101.325 \, kPa$ - ✅ **Gas Constant for Air:** $R_{air} = 0.287 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$ - ✅ **Combined Gas Law:** $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ - ✅ **Density from Ideal Gas:** $\rho = P/(RT)$ - ✅ **Always use absolute P and absolute T (Kelvin)** — non-negotiable rule. ### 🎯 KTU 2024 Module 4 Quick Recall Map | Concept | Key Equation | Marks Weightage | |:---|:---|:---:| | Boyle's Law | $PV = $ const (T const) | 2 | | Charles's Law | $V/T = $ const (P const) | 2 | | Avogadro's Law | $V \propto n$ (T, P const) | 1 | | Ideal Gas Derivation | $PV = mRT$ | 7 | | Universal vs Characteristic R | $R = \overline{R}/M$ | 7 | | Numerical Process Problems | Combined gas law | 7 | ### 🧠 Three Golden Rules to Live By 1. **"P must be absolute, T must be Kelvin."** — No exceptions, ever. 2. **"Moles → use $\overline{R}$; Mass → use $R$."** — Match the constant to the quantity. 3. **"R varies with gas, $\overline{R}$ is universal."** — Memorise the linkage $R = \overline{R}/M$. --- > **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — IDEAL GAS EQUATION** > *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) | Course: PCMET403 — Engineering Thermodynamics*

topics.title: Gas Constants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gas Constants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In engineering thermodynamics, the term **"Gas Constant"** is a fundamental thermodynamic property that quantifies the energy per unit degree of freedom per mole (or per unit mass) of a substance. It is the constant of proportionality in the **Ideal Gas Equation of State**. There are two primary forms of the gas constant used throughout the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Universal (or Molar) Gas Constant, denoted as $R_u$ or $\bar{R}$**:
   It is the gas constant evaluated **per kilomole (or mole)** of an ideal gas. It is a **universal physical constant**, meaning it is identical for *all* ideal gases regardless of their chemical identity. The universally accepted value is:

   $$R_u = 8.314 \; \text{kJ} \cdot \text{kmol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$$

2. **Specific (or Individual / Characteristic) Gas Constant, denoted as $R$**:
   It is the gas constant evaluated **per unit mass (per kg)** of a given ideal gas. Unlike $R_u$, this value is **specific to each gas** and depends on its molar mass $M$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Throughout this module, the symbol $R$ in equations such as $Pv = RT$ represents the **Specific Gas Constant** (per unit mass), NOT the Universal Gas Constant. Confusing these two is one of the most frequent errors observed in KTU board examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Party Favors" Intuition

Imagine a children's birthday party:
- The **Universal Gas Constant ($R_u$)** is like the **party host's rule**: "Every child gets exactly the same amount of cake." Whether there are 10 children or 100, each child gets one identical slice. The rule doesn't change based on the children.
- The **Specific Gas Constant ($R$)** is like the **size of one child's goody bag**. Lighter gas molecules (like Hydrogen) have a *larger* goody bag per kg (they are lighter, so per kg there are more moles), while heavier gas molecules (like Carbon Dioxide) have a *smaller* goody bag per kg.

In short: $R_u$ is **one number for the whole universe of ideal gases**, while $R$ **varies from gas to gas**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Numerical Values (Memorize These for KTU Exams):**
> - **Universal Gas Constant: $R_u = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/kmol}\cdot\text{K}$**
> - **Air (Specific Gas Constant): $R_{air} = 0.287 \; \text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$**
> - **Molar Mass of Air: $M_{air} = 28.97 \; \text{kg/kmol}$**
> - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$**
> - **Standard Temperature: $T_{std} = 273.15 \; \text{K}$ (0 °C)**
> - **Normal Temperature & Pressure (NTP): $T = 293.15 \; \text{K}$, $P = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$**

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Conversion Vigilance:** KTU questions may present $R_u$ in different units:
> $R_u = 8.314 \; \text{kJ/kmol·K} = 8314 \; \text{J/kmol·K} = 1545 \; \text{ft·lb}_f/\text{lbmol·°R} = 49{,}720 \; \text{ft·lb}_f/\text{lbmol·°R}$
> Always convert to a consistent unit system *before* substituting into any formula.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Foundation — Why Two Gas Constants?

The reason for having two constants lies in the **basis** of measurement:
- A **mole (or kmol)** is a *count* of molecules ($N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{26}$ molecules/kmol). One kmol of any gas contains exactly the same number of molecules, so the constant $R_u$ per kmol is universal.
- A **kilogram** is a *mass* measurement. Since different gases have different molecular weights, the constant per kg must differ.

### 2.2 Structured Theoretical Framework

**Step 1: The Ideal Gas Equation (Per Unit Mass)**
The fundamental governing equation for an ideal gas expressed per unit mass is:

$$Pv = RT$$

Where:
- $P$ = Absolute pressure (kPa)
- $v$ = Specific volume (m³/kg)
- $R$ = Specific gas constant (kJ/kg·K)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

**Step 2: The Ideal Gas Equation (Per Mole)**
When expressed per mole, the same equation becomes:

$$P\bar{v} = R_u T$$

Where $\bar{v}$ is the **molar specific volume** (m³/kmol) — the volume occupied by one kmol of gas.

**Step 3: Linking Mass and Moles**
The molar specific volume and specific (mass-based) volume are related by the molar mass $M$ (kg/kmol):

$$\bar{v} = M \cdot v$$

**Step 4: Mayer's Relation (Connecting $C_p$, $C_v$, and $R$)**
For any ideal gas, the difference between specific heat at constant pressure and specific heat at constant volume is exactly equal to the specific gas constant:

$$C_p - C_v = R$$

Similarly, on a molar basis:

$$\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = R_u$$

This is known as **Mayer's Equation** and is a direct consequence of the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a constant-pressure process versus a constant-volume process.

**Step 5: Specific Heat Ratio ($\gamma$)**
The ratio of specific heats is a dimensionless thermodynamic parameter:

$$\gamma = \frac{C_p}{C_v}$$

It can be expressed purely in terms of $C_v$ and $R$ (or $C_p$ and $R$):

$$\gamma = 1 + \frac{R}{C_v} = \frac{C_p}{C_p - R}$$

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Internal Combustion Engines:** Engineers use $R$ to calculate air mass flow rate through the intake manifold using the ideal gas law, critical for fuel injection calibration.
- **Aerospace & HVAC:** The specific gas constant of air ($R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K) is used in the **standard atmosphere model** to determine pressure, density, and temperature at various altitudes.
- **Gas Mixture Analysis:** Industrial gas blending (e.g., for welding gases, medical oxygen mixtures, or combustion air-fuel mixtures) requires computation of the mixture's effective gas constant using molar averaging.
- **Cryogenics:** The very high value of $R$ for Hydrogen (4.124 kJ/kg·K) explains why hydrogen is both energy-dense and a challenge to liquefy and store.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | **Application** |
|:------------|:----------------|:----------|:----------------|
| $R_u = 8.314$ | Universal Gas Constant (numerical) | kJ/kmol·K | All ideal gases |
| $R = R_u / M$ | Specific Gas Constant from molar mass | kJ/kg·K | Pure gas |
| $R = C_p - C_v$ | Mayer's Relation (per unit mass) | kJ/kg·K | Specific heat to $R$ conversion |
| $R_u = \bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v$ | Mayer's Relation (per mole) | kJ/kmol·K | Molar specific heat to $R_u$ |
| $M = m / n$ | Molar Mass (mass per kmol) | kg/kmol | Stoichiometry |
| $Pv = RT$ | Ideal Gas Law (per unit mass) | P in kPa, v in m³/kg | Pure gas state |
| $P\bar{v} = R_u T$ | Ideal Gas Law (per mole) | P in kPa, $\bar{v}$ in m³/kmol | Molar form |
| $PV = mRT$ | Ideal Gas Law (total mass) | V in m³, m in kg | System analysis |
| $R_{mix} = R_u / M_{mix}$ | Mixture Gas Constant | kJ/kg·K | Gas mixtures |
| $M_{mix} = \sum y_i M_i$ | Mixture Molar Mass (Kay's Rule) | kg/kmol | Gas mixtures |
| $R_{mix} = \sum y_i R_i$ | Mixture Gas Constant (mole basis) | kJ/kg·K | Gas mixtures |
| $\gamma = 1 + R/C_v$ | Specific Heat Ratio (alternative form) | dimensionless | Process analysis |
| $C_v = R/(\gamma - 1)$ | $C_v$ from $R$ and $\gamma$ | kJ/kg·K | Specific heat calculation |
| $C_p = \gamma R/(\gamma - 1)$ | $C_p$ from $R$ and $\gamma$ | kJ/kg·K | Specific heat calculation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gas Constant Values of Common Gases (KTU Reference Data — Memorize the Bolded Ones):**
> - **Air: $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K**, $M = 28.97$ kg/kmol, $\gamma = 1.4$
> - **Nitrogen ($N_2$): $R = 0.2968$ kJ/kg·K**, $M = 28$ kg/kmol, $\gamma = 1.4$
> - **Oxygen ($O_2$): $R = 0.2598$ kJ/kg·K**, $M = 32$ kg/kmol, $\gamma = 1.4$
> - **Hydrogen ($H_2$): $R = 4.124$ kJ/kg·K**, $M = 2.016$ kg/kmol, $\gamma = 1.4$
> - **Carbon Dioxide ($CO_2$): $R = 0.1889$ kJ/kg·K**, $M = 44$ kg/kmol, $\gamma = 1.3$
> - **Helium ($He$): $R = 2.077$ kJ/kg·K**, $M = 4$ kg/kmol, $\gamma = 1.667$
> - **Methane ($CH_4$): $R = 0.5183$ kJ/kg·K**, $M = 16$ kg/kmol, $\gamma = 1.32$

> [!WARNING]
> **The Pipe Symbol Trap:** In your answer scripts, when writing $C_p - C_v = R$, do NOT enclose $R$ in absolute-value-style pipes. Markdown tables interpret `|` as column delimiters. Use parentheses or write "the absolute value of..." in plain text instead.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Linking Universal Gas Constant ($R_u$) and Specific Gas Constant ($R$)

**Starting Point:** We have two equivalent forms of the ideal gas equation:

$$\text{(1)} \quad P \bar{v} = R_u T \quad \text{[per kmol]}$$

$$\text{(2)} \quad P v = R T \quad \text{[per kg]}$$

**Step 1:** Relate molar specific volume to mass-based specific volume using molar mass $M$ (kg per kmol). The volume occupied by one kmol of gas is $M$ times the volume occupied by one kg of the same gas. Therefore:

$$\bar{v} = M \cdot v$$

**Step 2:** Substitute this relation into Equation (1):

$$P \cdot (M \cdot v) = R_u T$$

**Step 3:** Distribute the $P$ on the left side:

$$M \cdot P v = R_u T$$

**Step 4:** Compare this with Equation (2): $Pv = RT$. Substituting $Pv = RT$ into the left side:

$$M \cdot (R T) = R_u T$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $T$ (which is non-zero, since gases are only "gases" at non-zero absolute temperature):

$$M \cdot R = R_u$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the specific gas constant $R$:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{R_u}{M}}$$

**Verification with Air:**
$$R_{air} = \frac{8.314 \; \text{kJ/kmol·K}}{28.97 \; \text{kg/kmol}} = 0.2870 \; \text{kJ/kg·K} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Gas Constant of a Gas Mixture (Using Kay's Rule)

**Problem Setup:** Consider a mixture of $k$ ideal gases with mole fractions $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_k$ (note: $\sum y_i = 1$) and individual molar masses $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_k$.

**Step 1:** The molar mass of the mixture (a weighted average) is defined as the total mass divided by the total number of moles. For a basis of 1 kmol of mixture, the total mass equals the sum of the masses contributed by each component:

$$M_{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (y_i \cdot M_i) = y_1 M_1 + y_2 M_2 + \ldots + y_k M_k$$

**Step 2:** Applying the same formula $R = R_u / M$ to the mixture:

$$R_{mix} = \frac{R_u}{M_{mix}} = \frac{R_u}{\sum y_i M_i}$$

**Step 3:** To express this as a weighted sum of individual $R_i$, substitute $M_i = R_u / R_i$ into the denominator:

$$M_i = \frac{R_u}{R_i}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the mixture molar mass equation:

$$M_{mix} = \sum y_i \cdot \frac{R_u}{R_i} = R_u \cdot \sum \frac{y_i}{R_i}$$

**Step 5:** Now substitute this into the mixture gas constant expression:

$$R_{mix} = \frac{R_u}{R_u \cdot \sum \frac{y_i}{R_i}}$$

**Step 6:** The $R_u$ in the numerator and denominator cancels:

$$\boxed{R_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{y_i}{R_i}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative — Dalton's Law Form:** Because the internal energy and enthalpy of an ideal gas are functions of temperature only, the gas constant on a **mass basis** also satisfies the simple weighted average $R_{mix} = \sum y_i R_i$, *provided* the weighting is by **mass fraction** (not mole fraction). On a molar basis, both forms reduce to identical expressions because mole-fraction and mass-fraction averaging give the same result when divided through the universal constant.

For KTU problems, the most commonly used form is:
$$R_{mix} = \sum y_i R_i \quad \text{where } y_i = \text{mole fraction}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Complete KTU-Style Solution

**Problem:** A gas mixture contains 60% $N_2$, 30% $O_2$, and 10% $CO_2$ by mole. Find:
**(a)** The gas constant of the mixture.
**(b)** The mixture occupies 0.5 m³ at 300 kPa and 25 °C. Find the mass of the mixture.

**Given Data:**
- $y_{N_2} = 0.60$, $M_{N_2} = 28$ kg/kmol
- $y_{O_2} = 0.30$, $M_{O_2} = 32$ kg/kmol
- $y_{CO_2} = 0.10$, $M_{CO_2} = 44$ kg/kmol
- $P = 300$ kPa, $V = 0.5$ m³, $T = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15$ K
- $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K

**Part (a): Gas Constant of the Mixture**

**Step 1:** Calculate the mixture molar mass using mole-fraction weighted average:

$$M_{mix} = \sum y_i M_i = (0.60)(28) + (0.30)(32) + (0.10)(44)$$

$$M_{mix} = 16.8 + 9.6 + 4.4 = 30.8 \; \text{kg/kmol}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the specific gas constant of the mixture:

$$R_{mix} = \frac{R_u}{M_{mix}} = \frac{8.314}{30.8} = 0.2699 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$$

**Part (b): Mass of the Mixture**

**Step 3:** Apply the ideal gas law in total form $PV = mRT$:

$$m = \frac{PV}{RT} = \frac{(300 \; \text{kPa})(0.5 \; \text{m}^3)}{(0.2699 \; \text{kJ/kg·K})(298.15 \; \text{K})}$$

> *Note: Pressure in kPa equals kN/m², and 1 kN·m = 1 kJ, so the units are consistent.*

**Step 4:** Perform the calculation:

$$m = \frac{150}{80.479} = 1.864 \; \text{kg}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(a)** $R_{mix} = 0.270$ kJ/kg·K
- **(b)** $m = 1.864$ kg

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Formula Should I Use?"

| **If the problem gives you...** | **Then use this formula...** | **Example scenario** |
|:--------------------------------|:-----------------------------|:--------------------|
| Molar mass $M$ only | $R = R_u / M$ | "Find R for $CH_4$" |
| $C_p$ and $C_v$ | $R = C_p - C_v$ | "Given $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K" |
| Moles $n$ and total mass $m$ | $M = m/n$, then $R = R_u/M$ | "5 kmol of gas weighs 100 kg" |
| Mole fractions of a mixture | $M_{mix} = \sum y_i M_i$, then $R = R_u / M_{mix}$ | Combustion products, air composition |
| $\gamma$ and $C_p$ | $R = C_p(\gamma - 1)/\gamma$ | Diatomic gas problems |
| $\gamma$ and $C_v$ | $R = C_v(\gamma - 1)$ | Diatomic gas problems |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Relationship of Gas Constants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start: Ideal Gas Theory]) --> nodeB[Universal Gas Constant Ru]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Specific Gas Constant R]
    B1["Ru = 8.314 kJ/kmol K"] --> nodeB
    C1["R = Ru / M, units kJ/kg K"] --> nodeC
    C2["R = Cp minus Cv, Mayer Relation"] --> nodeC
    C3["R = Cv times gamma minus 1"] --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD{Mixture of Gases}
    nodeC --> nodeD
    D1["R mix = sum of yi times Ri, mole basis"] --> nodeD
    D2["R mix = Ru over M mix, Kay Rule"] --> nodeD
    D3["M mix = sum of yi times Mi"] --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Real World Engine Applications]
    E1["IC Engine Intake Air Mass Flow"] --> nodeE
    E2["Atmospheric Pressure Altitude Models"] --> nodeE
    E3["HVAC Refrigerant State Analysis"] --> nodeE
    E4["Cryogenic Hydrogen Storage"] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF([Engineering Design Output])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How Gas Constants Feed a Thermodynamic Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input: Gas Identity or Mixture Spec] --> procA{Data Type?}
    procA -->|Molar Mass M| pathA
    procA -->|Specific Heats Cp Cv| pathB
    procA -->|Mole Fractions yi| pathC
    pathA[Path A: Compute R = Ru over M] --> mergeA[Unified R value]
    pathB[Path B: Apply Mayer R = Cp minus Cv] --> mergeA
    pathC[Path C: Sum yi Mi to get M mix, then R = Ru over M mix] --> mergeA
    mergeA --> procB[Apply Ideal Gas Law PV = mRT]
    procB --> outputA[Output: State Properties]
    outputA --> outA1[Pressure]
    outputA --> outA2[Volume]
    outputA --> outA3[Temperature]
    outputA --> outA4[Density]
    outputA --> outA5[Mass Flow Rate]
```

### 4.3 Mapping Real Gases to Their Constants (Reference Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([All Ideal Gases]) --> branch{Heavier or Lighter than Air?}
    branch -->|Lighter M less than 28.97| light[Light Gases]
    branch -->|Heavier M greater than 28.97| heavy[Heavy Gases]
    light --> l1[Hydrogen H2: R = 4.124, M = 2.016]
    light --> l2[Helium He: R = 2.077, M = 4.0]
    light --> l3[Methane CH4: R = 0.5183, M = 16]
    heavy --> h1[Air: R = 0.287, M = 28.97]
    heavy --> h2[Nitrogen N2: R = 0.2968, M = 28]
    heavy --> h3[Oxygen O2: R = 0.2598, M = 32]
    heavy --> h4[Carbon Dioxide CO2: R = 0.1889, M = 44]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The "Lighter than Air" category in the third flowchart corresponds to gases with **smaller molar mass** but **larger** specific gas constant $R$. This is the *inverse* relationship that catches many students off-guard. Heavier gases have smaller $R$ because each kg contains fewer moles.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> **Define the term "Universal Gas Constant" and state its numerical value in SI units. Why is it called "universal"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Universal Gas Constant, denoted $R_u$ or $\bar{R}$, is the constant of proportionality in the ideal gas equation when expressed **per kilomole** of gas. Numerically:
$$R_u = 8.314 \; \text{kJ} \cdot \text{kmol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$$
It is called "universal" because it has the **same numerical value for all ideal gases**, regardless of their chemical composition. This is because one kilomole of any substance contains the same number of molecules (Avogadro's number, $6.022 \times 10^{26}$), and the ideal gas behavior depends only on molecular count, not molecular identity. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for numerical value, 1 mark for explanation of "universal")*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> **Distinguish between Universal Gas Constant ($R_u$) and Specific Gas Constant ($R$). Why is $R$ different for different gases while $R_u$ is the same?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Property | Universal Gas Constant ($R_u$) | Specific Gas Constant ($R$) |
|:---------|:-------------------------------|:-----------------------------|
| Basis | Per kilomole (per mole) | Per kilogram |
| Symbol | $R_u$ or $\bar{R}$ | $R$ |
| Value | Same for all ideal gases (8.314 kJ/kmol·K) | Different for each gas |
| Relation to $M$ | Independent of $M$ | Inversely proportional to $M$ ($R = R_u / M$) |

$R$ differs for different gases because it represents the constant **per unit mass**, and gases have different molar masses $M$. Heavier gases (larger $M$) have a smaller $R$ (fewer moles per kg), while lighter gases have a larger $R$ (more moles per kg). $R_u$ is the same because per kilomole, the number of molecules is identical for all substances. *(3 marks distributed: 1 for table/distinction, 1 for numerical example, 1 for reasoning)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
> **(a) (7 Marks)** A gas mixture used in a combustion analysis has the following composition by volume:
> - $CO_2$: 12%, $O_2$: 8%, $N_2$: 75%, $H_2O$: 5%
> Calculate: (i) the apparent molar mass of the mixture, (ii) the specific gas constant of the mixture, and (iii) the specific heats $C_p$ and $C_v$ of the mixture, given $\gamma = 1.34$.
>
> **(b) (7 Marks)** A rigid steel tank of volume 0.6 m³ contains 1.2 kg of the above mixture at an initial temperature of 30 °C. The tank is now heated until the temperature reaches 150 °C. Determine: (i) the final pressure inside the tank, and (ii) the heat transferred to the gas during this constant-volume process.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** Mole fractions (volume % = mole % for ideal gases):
- $y_{CO_2} = 0.12$, $M_{CO_2} = 44$ kg/kmol, $\gamma_{CO_2} = 1.30$ (not used, only mixture $\gamma$ given)
- $y_{O_2} = 0.08$, $M_{O_2} = 32$ kg/kmol
- $y_{N_2} = 0.75$, $M_{N_2} = 28$ kg/kmol
- $y_{H_2O} = 0.05$, $M_{H_2O} = 18$ kg/kmol
- $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K, $\gamma_{mix} = 1.34$

**(i) Molar Mass of Mixture (2 marks):**
$$M_{mix} = \sum y_i M_i$$
$$M_{mix} = (0.12)(44) + (0.08)(32) + (0.75)(28) + (0.05)(18)$$
$$M_{mix} = 5.28 + 2.56 + 21.0 + 0.90 = 29.74 \; \text{kg/kmol}$$

**(ii) Specific Gas Constant (2 marks):**
$$R_{mix} = \frac{R_u}{M_{mix}} = \frac{8.314}{29.74} = 0.2796 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$$

**(iii) Specific Heats (3 marks):**
Using $\gamma = 1 + R/C_v$, we solve for $C_v$:
$$C_v = \frac{R_{mix}}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{0.2796}{1.34 - 1} = \frac{0.2796}{0.34} = 0.8224 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$$

Then, $C_p = \gamma \cdot C_v$:
$$C_p = 1.34 \times 0.8224 = 1.1020 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$$

**Verification via Mayer's relation:** $C_p - C_v = 1.1020 - 0.8224 = 0.2796 = R_{mix}$ ✓

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $V = 0.6$ m³ (rigid, so $V = $ const), $m = 1.2$ kg, $T_1 = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15$ K, $T_2 = 150 + 273.15 = 423.15$ K, $R_{mix} = 0.2796$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.8224$ kJ/kg·K

**(i) Final Pressure (4 marks):**

Since the tank is rigid, volume and mass are constant. Using ideal gas law $PV = mRT$:

At State 1:
$$P_1 = \frac{m R_{mix} T_1}{V} = \frac{(1.2)(0.2796)(303.15)}{0.6}$$
$$P_1 = \frac{101.71}{0.6} = 169.5 \; \text{kPa}$$

Using the combined gas law for constant volume ($\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2}$):

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1} = 169.5 \times \frac{423.15}{303.15}$$
$$P_2 = 169.5 \times 1.3959 = 236.6 \; \text{kPa}$$

**(ii) Heat Transferred (3 marks):**

For a **constant volume** (isochoric) process, $W = 0$. By the First Law:
$$Q = \Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$$
$$Q = (1.2)(0.8224)(423.15 - 303.15)$$
$$Q = (1.2)(0.8224)(120) = 118.4 \; \text{kJ}$$

**Final Answers (Question A):**
- (a) $M_{mix} = 29.74$ kg/kmol, $R_{mix} = 0.2796$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.8224$ kJ/kg·K, $C_p = 1.102$ kJ/kg·K
- (b) $P_2 = 236.6$ kPa, $Q = 118.4$ kJ

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **(a) (7 Marks)** A spherical balloon of diameter 4 m is filled with helium gas at an ambient temperature of 30 °C and pressure of 100 kPa. Calculate: (i) the mass of helium in the balloon, (ii) the specific gas constant of helium, and (iii) the density of helium under these conditions.
>
> **(b) (7 Marks)** A combustion chamber receives a mixture of gases with the following mass composition: 20% $CO_2$, 10% $O_2$, 65% $N_2$, and 5% $H_2O$. The mixture enters at 400 kPa and 800 K. Determine: (i) the specific gas constant of the mixture on a mass basis, (ii) the partial pressure of $N_2$, and (iii) the density of the mixture.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** Spherical balloon, $d = 4$ m, $T = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15$ K, $P = 100$ kPa, Helium ($He$): $M_{He} = 4$ kg/kmol, $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K

**Step 1: Volume of the spherical balloon (1 mark):**
$$V = \frac{\pi d^3}{6} = \frac{\pi (4)^3}{6} = \frac{64\pi}{6} = 33.51 \; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2: Specific gas constant of helium (1 mark):**
$$R_{He} = \frac{R_u}{M_{He}} = \frac{8.314}{4} = 2.0785 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 3: Mass of helium using $PV = mRT$ (3 marks):**
$$m = \frac{PV}{R_{He} T} = \frac{(100)(33.51)}{(2.0785)(303.15)}$$
$$m = \frac{3351}{630.10} = 5.318 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 4: Density of helium (2 marks):**
$$\rho_{He} = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{5.318}{33.51} = 0.1587 \; \text{kg/m}^3$$

Or equivalently: $\rho = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{100}{(2.0785)(303.15)} = 0.1587$ kg/m³ ✓

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** Mass fractions: $w_{CO_2} = 0.20$, $w_{O_2} = 0.10$, $w_{N_2} = 0.65$, $w_{H_2O} = 0.05$
$P = 400$ kPa, $T = 800$ K

**Step 1: Convert mass fractions to mole fractions (3 marks):**
Moles per kg of mixture: $n_i = w_i / M_i$
- $n_{CO_2} = 0.20 / 44 = 0.004545$ kmol/kg
- $n_{O_2} = 0.10 / 32 = 0.003125$ kmol/kg
- $n_{N_2} = 0.65 / 28 = 0.023214$ kmol/kg
- $n_{H_2O} = 0.05 / 18 = 0.002778$ kmol/kg
- Total: $n_{total} = 0.033662$ kmol/kg

Mole fractions $y_i = n_i / n_{total}$:
- $y_{CO_2} = 0.1350$, $y_{O_2} = 0.0928$, $y_{N_2} = 0.6896$, $y_{H_2O} = 0.0826$

**Step 2: Mixture gas constant (1 mark):**
$$M_{mix} = \sum y_i M_i = (0.1350)(44) + (0.0928)(32) + (0.6896)(28) + (0.0826)(18)$$
$$M_{mix} = 5.94 + 2.97 + 19.31 + 1.487 = 29.71 \; \text{kg/kmol}$$
$$R_{mix} = \frac{8.314}{29.71} = 0.2799 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 3: Partial pressure of $N_2$ (1 mark):**
For ideal gas mixtures, partial pressure = mole fraction × total pressure:
$$P_{N_2} = y_{N_2} \cdot P = 0.6896 \times 400 = 275.8 \; \text{kPa}$$

**Step 4: Density of the mixture (2 marks):**
$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{P}{R_{mix} T} = \frac{400}{(0.2799)(800)} = \frac{400}{223.92} = 1.786 \; \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Final Answers (Question B):**
- (a) $V = 33.51$ m³, $R_{He} = 2.0785$ kJ/kg·K, $m = 5.318$ kg, $\rho = 0.1587$ kg/m³
- (b) $R_{mix} = 0.2799$ kJ/kg·K, $P_{N_2} = 275.8$ kPa, $\rho_{mix} = 1.786$ kg/m³

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on Gas Constant Problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $R_u$ with $R$ (Universal vs Specific):** The single most common error. In $Pv = RT$, you must use $R$ in kJ/kg·K, NOT $R_u$. Using $R_u$ in the mass-based equation leads to answers that are off by a factor of ~29 for air. **(Penalty: up to 50% of the marks on that sub-question)**
>
> 2. **Mixing Mass Fraction and Mole Fraction:** When finding $R_{mix}$, students often use mass fractions directly in $M_{mix} = \sum y_i M_i$, but the equation strictly requires **mole fractions**. If the problem gives **mass %**, you MUST first convert to mole fractions via $y_i = (w_i/M_i) / \sum(w_j/M_j)$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Celsius to Kelvin:** All ideal gas calculations require **absolute temperature in Kelvin or Rankine**. Adding 273 (instead of 273.15) sometimes causes minor errors. The 2024 scheme is more lenient on the .15 difference, but always show the conversion explicitly.
>
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** $P$ in kPa, $V$ in m³, $R$ in kJ/kg·K, $T$ in K → all units are internally consistent. But if $P$ is in Pa, you must use $R$ in J/kg·K (multiply kJ/kg·K by 1000). Always do a **unit check** before substituting.
>
> 5. **Using Volume % as Mass %:** For ideal gases, volume % = mole % (this is a consequence of Avogadro's law). They are NOT equal to mass %. KTU questions often deliberately give one and ask the other to test your conversion skill.
>
> 6. **Treating Rigid Container and Constant-Pressure Process Interchangeably:** If volume is constant, use $P_1/T_1 = P_2/T_2$. If pressure is constant, use $V_1/T_1 = V_2/T_2$. The combined gas law $P_1V_1/T_1 = P_2V_2/T_2$ is general.

---
