>
> ✅ **Specific Gas Constant $R = R_u / M$** — depends on the gas, units kJ/kg·K.
>
> ✅ **Mayer's Relation (per kg): $C_p - C_v = R$** and **per kmol: $\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = R_u$**.
>
> ✅ **Alternative forms of $R$:**
> - $R = R_u / M$
> - $R = C_p - C_v$
> - $R = C_v (\gamma - 1)$
> - $R = C_p (\gamma - 1) / \gamma$
>
> ✅ **Specific Heat Ratio $\gamma = C_p / C_v$**. Monatomic ≈ 1.667, Diatomic ≈ 1.4, Polyatomic ≈ 1.3 (for $CO_2$).
>
> ✅ **For ideal gas mixtures:**
> - On a **mole basis**: $M_{mix} = \sum y_i M_i$, then $R_{mix} = R_u / M_{mix}$
> - On a **mass basis**: $R_{mix} = \sum w_i R_i$ (where $w_i$ is mass fraction)
> - **Partial pressure:** $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$
>
> ✅ **Ideal Gas Law Variants:**
> - Per kg: $Pv = RT$
> - Per kmol: $P\bar{v} = R_u T$
> - Total: $PV = mRT = nR_u T$ (where $n$ = kmol)
>
> ✅ **Air is the most-used reference gas:** $R_{air} = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $M_{air} = 28.97$ kg/kmol, $\gamma_{air} = 1.4$, $C_{p,air} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_{v,air} = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K.
>
> ✅ **Heavier gases → smaller $R$**, **Lighter gases → larger $R$** (inverse relationship with $M$).
>
> ✅ **Volume % = Mole %** for ideal gas mixtures (Avogadro's Law).
>
> ✅ **Temperature in gas equations MUST be absolute** (Kelvin or Rankine).
>
> ✅ **Density from ideal gas law:** $\rho = P / (RT) = 1/v$.
>
> ✅ **Always verify units are consistent** before plugging into any formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deviations from Ideal Gas Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Deviations from Ideal Gas Model ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In engineering thermodynamics, an **Ideal Gas** is a theoretical gas whose molecules occupy negligible space and have no intermolecular forces, exactly obeying the equation of state **Pv = RT** (or Pv = nRT). However, real gases **deviate** from this ideal behavior, especially at **high pressures** and **low temperatures**, where molecular volume and intermolecular forces (attraction and repulsion) become significant. The study of these **Deviations from the Ideal Gas Model** quantifies the gap between real and ideal gas behavior using a dimensionless quantity called the **Compressibility Factor (Z)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic directly addresses **Module 4: Real Gases and their Properties**, and the comprehension of these deviations is essential for solving problems involving gas liquefaction, refrigeration cycles, and high-pressure process equipment design. ### 1.2 The Compressibility Factor (Z) The mathematical measure of deviation is defined as: $$Z = \frac{Pv}{RT}$$ Where: - **P** = Absolute Pressure (in **kPa** or **bar**) - **v** = Specific Volume (in **m³/kg**) - **R** = Specific Gas Constant (in **kJ/kg·K**) - **T** = Absolute Temperature (in **K**) > [!NOTE] > **For an Ideal Gas: Z = 1 always.** > **For a Real Gas: Z ≠ 1.** If Z < 1, attractive forces dominate; if Z > 1, repulsive forces dominate. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a crowded market (molecules in a gas): - **Low pressure + High temperature** (sparse market, warm day): Shoppers (molecules) have plenty of room and move fast without bumping into each other. The crowd behaves like an **ideal gas** — predictable, independent motion. - **High pressure + Low temperature** (packed market, cold day): Shoppers (molecules) are squeezed together and pull/push against each other (intermolecular forces). Their behavior becomes **unpredictable**, and the simple "ideal market" rules break down. This is the **deviation from ideal gas behavior** — molecules can't be treated as point particles anymore. > [!TIP] > **Key Takeaway:** At **low pressures** (P → 0) and **high temperatures** (T → ∞), all real gases approach ideal gas behavior. This is the basis of the **Law of Corresponding States**. ### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Significance | |-----------|--------|----------------------|--------------| | Universal Gas Constant | R̄ | **8.314 kJ/kmol·K** | Used for molar basis calculations | | Standard Pressure | P° | **101.325 kPa** or **1 atm** | Reference state for gases | | Standard Temperature | T° | **273.15 K** or **25 °C** | Reference state for gases | | Critical Compressibility Factor | Zc | **0.27 to 0.29** (most gases) | Used in law of corresponding states | | Boyle Temperature | TB | Gas-specific (K) | Temperature where Z ≈ 1 over a range of pressures | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Do Real Gases Deviate? The ideal gas model fails because of two key real-world effects: 1. **Finite Molecular Volume:** At high pressures, molecules are forced close together. The *available volume* for molecular motion is less than the *total volume* of the container. 2. **Intermolecular Forces:** - **Attractive forces** (Van der Waals): Pull molecules together → reduces pressure (Z < 1). - **Repulsive forces**: Push molecules apart → increases pressure (Z > 1). ### 2.2 Equations of State for Real Gases Several empirical and semi-empirical equations of state have been developed to capture these deviations: #### (a) Van der Waals Equation (1873) Accounts for both finite volume and intermolecular attraction: $$\left(P + \frac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$$ Where: - **a** = attraction parameter (pressure correction term) - **b** = volume correction term (co-volume) #### (b) Redlich-Kwong Equation (1949) A more accurate two-parameter equation, especially for moderate pressures: $$P = \frac{RT}{v - b} - \frac{a}{\sqrt{T} \cdot v(v + b)}$$ #### (c) Virial Equation of State (Most General) Expressed as a power series in either pressure or volume: $$Z = 1 + B(T)P + C(T)P^2 + D(T)P^3 + \cdots$$ Or in terms of reciprocal volume: $$Z = 1 + \frac{B'(T)}{v} + \frac{C'(T)}{v^2} + \cdots$$ > [!NOTE] > The **virial coefficients** (B, C, D...) are functions of temperature only and represent interactions between 2, 3, 4... molecules respectively. #### (d) Beattie-Bridgeman Equation Uses five empirical constants per gas; accurate for moderate pressures. #### (e) Benedict-Webb-Rubin (BWR) Equation Eight constants; highly accurate for hydrocarbons and light gases. ### 2.3 Reduced Properties & Law of Corresponding States To generalize the behavior of all gases, **reduced (dimensionless) properties** are defined using the **critical point** as reference: $$P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}, \quad T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}, \quad v_r = \frac{v}{v_c}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Law of Corresponding States (Van der Waals, 1873):** > All gases at the same **reduced pressure (Pr)** and **reduced temperature (Tr)** have the same **compressibility factor (Z)** and deviate from ideal gas behavior to the same degree. > $$Z = f(P_r, T_r)$$ This is the foundation of the **Generalized Compressibility Chart** by Nelson and Obert, which is universally used in engineering calculations. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Expression | Variables / Constants | Units | Engineering Application | |---------|-----------|----------------------|-------|--------------------------| | Compressibility Factor | $Z = \dfrac{Pv}{RT}$ | P, v, R, T | dimensionless | Quantifying deviation | | Van der Waals EoS | $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$ | a, b (gas-specific) | varies | First-order real gas model | | Redlich-Kwong EoS | $P = \dfrac{RT}{v-b} - \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{T} \cdot v(v+b)}$ | a, b (gas-specific) | varies | Better accuracy at moderate P | | Virial EoS (pressure form) | $Z = 1 + BP + CP^2 + \cdots$ | B, C (virial coefficients) | varies | High-accuracy modeling | | Reduced Pressure | $P_r = \dfrac{P}{P_c}$ | Pc = critical pressure | dimensionless | Generalized charts | | Reduced Temperature | $T_r = \dfrac{T}{T_c}$ | Tc = critical temperature | dimensionless | Generalized charts | | Law of Corresponding States | $Z = f(P_r, T_r)$ | — | dimensionless | Universal gas correlations | | Boyle Temperature | $T_B = \dfrac{a}{Rb}$ | a, b from VdW | K | Z ≈ 1 over pressure range | | Inversion Temperature | $T_{inv} = \dfrac{2a}{Rb}$ | a, b from VdW | K | Joule-Thomson effect | | Critical Compressibility | $Z_c = \dfrac{P_c v_c}{R T_c}$ | Critical properties | dimensionless | Validation of EoS | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Refrigeration & LNG Plants:** Designing liquefaction cycles requires accurate real gas equations. - **Compressor Design:** High-pressure compression of CO₂, N₂, O₂ deviates significantly from ideal behavior. - **Pipeline Transportation of Natural Gas:** Pressure drop calculations need compressibility corrections. - **Chemical Process Industry:** Reaction equilibria at high pressures depend on real gas fugacity (a quantity that accounts for non-ideality). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Van der Waals Equation from Ideal Gas Law **Starting Point:** The ideal gas equation of state: **Pv = RT** **Step 1 — Correct for Molecular Volume:** In the ideal gas, the entire volume *v* is available for molecular motion. In a real gas, a fraction *b* (the *co-volume*) is excluded because molecules have finite size. So the *effective* available volume is **(v − b)**. **Step 2 — Correct for Intermolecular Attraction:** Molecules near the walls of the container experience a net inward pull from other molecules, reducing the impact pressure. This reduction is proportional to the square of the number density (∝ 1/v²). So the *effective* pressure becomes **P + a/v²**, where *a* is the attraction constant. **Step 3 — Combine Corrections:** Substitute these corrected values into the ideal gas law: $$\left(P + \frac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$$ This is the **Van der Waals Equation of State**. Expanding it: $$Pv + \frac{a}{v} - Pb - \frac{ab}{v^2} = RT$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of the Virial Equation of State (Conceptual Basis) The virial equation is derived from statistical mechanics, where the compressibility factor is expanded as a power series reflecting the contributions of 2-body, 3-body, ... N-body molecular interactions. **Starting Assumption:** The deviation from ideality (Z − 1) can be expressed as a polynomial in density (1/v): $$Z - 1 = \frac{B(T)}{v} + \frac{C(T)}{v^2} + \frac{D(T)}{v^3} + \cdots$$ Rearranging: $$Z = 1 + \frac{B(T)}{v} + \frac{C(T)}{v^2} + \frac{D(T)}{v^3} + \cdots$$ Multiplying both sides by RT/v to convert back to pressure form: $$Z = 1 + B'(T)P + C'(T)P^2 + D'(T)P^3 + \cdots$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** The virial equation is **theoretically rigorous** and **experimentally grounded** — truncating after the second virial coefficient gives excellent accuracy for many engineering calculations up to moderate pressures. ### 3.3 Derivation of Boyle Temperature from Van der Waals Equation **Boyle Temperature (TB)** is defined as the temperature at which a real gas behaves ideally (Z = 1) over a small range of pressures near zero pressure. **Step 1 — Apply the Boyle Condition:** At TB, the derivative of (Pv) with respect to pressure at P = 0 must equal RT (the ideal gas slope): $$\left[\frac{\partial(Pv)}{\partial P}\right]_{P \to 0} = RT$$ **Step 2 — Expand the Van der Waals Equation:** From $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$, we have: $$Pv = RT - \frac{a}{v} + Pb + \frac{ab}{v^2}$$ **Step 3 — For Real Gases, Take the Derivative:** Differentiating with respect to P: $$\left[\frac{\partial(Pv)}{\partial P}\right]_T = RT - \frac{a}{v^2}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T + b + \frac{ab}{v^3}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate at P → 0 (where v → ∞):** At low pressure, the terms containing 1/v², 1/v³ vanish: $$\left[\frac{\partial(Pv)}{\partial P}\right]_{P \to 0} = RT + b$$ Wait — for this to equal RT (ideal gas), we need: $$b = 0 \text{ (impossible)} \Rightarrow \text{This shows VdW needs correction.}$$ **Corrected Derivation Using Exact VdW:** Multiplying VdW by (1/P) and expanding at low P gives: $$Z = 1 + \left(b - \frac{a}{RT}\right)\frac{P}{RT} + \cdots$$ **Step 5 — Set Coefficient of P to Zero for Z = 1:** $$b - \frac{a}{RT_B} = 0$$ $$\boxed{T_B = \frac{a}{Rb}}$$ This is the **Boyle Temperature** — above TB, repulsive forces dominate (Z > 1); below TB, attractive forces dominate (Z < 1). ### 3.4 Derivation of Critical Constants from Van der Waals Equation The critical point is where the P-v curve has an inflection point: $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T_c} = 0, \quad \left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial v^2}\right)_{T_c} = 0$$ **Step 1 — Expand VdW to Get P(v, T):** From $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$: $$P = \frac{RT}{v - b} - \frac{a}{v^2}$$ **Step 2 — First Partial Derivative with Respect to v:** $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_T = -\frac{RT}{(v - b)^2} + \frac{2a}{v^3} = 0 \quad \text{(at critical point)}$$ **Step 3 — Second Partial Derivative:** $$\left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial v^2}\right)_T = \frac{2RT}{(v - b)^3} - \frac{6a}{v^4} = 0 \quad \text{(at critical point)}$$ **Step 4 — Solve the Two Equations Simultaneously:** Dividing Step 3 by Step 2: $$\frac{2RT/(v-b)^3}{RT/(v-b)^2} = \frac{6a/v^4}{2a/v^3}$$ $$\frac{2}{v - b} = \frac{3}{v}$$ $$2v = 3v - 3b \Rightarrow v_c = 3b$$ **Step 5 — Substitute Back to Get Tc:** From Step 2: $RT_c = \dfrac{2a}{v_c^3}(v_c - b)^2 = \dfrac{2a}{27b^3} \cdot 4b^2 = \dfrac{8a}{27b}$ $$\boxed{T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb}}$$ **Step 6 — Find Pc by Substituting vc and Tc into VdW:** $$P_c = \frac{RT_c}{v_c - b} - \frac{a}{v_c^2} = \frac{R \cdot \frac{8a}{27Rb}}{2b} - \frac{a}{9b^2} = \frac{4a}{27b^2} - \frac{3a}{27b^2} = \frac{a}{27b^2}$$ $$\boxed{P_c = \frac{a}{27b^2}}$$ **Step 7 — Critical Compressibility Factor:** $$Z_c = \frac{P_c v_c}{RT_c} = \frac{\frac{a}{27b^2} \cdot 3b}{R \cdot \frac{8a}{27Rb}} = \frac{\frac{a}{9b}}{\frac{8a}{27b}} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$ > [!NOTE] > **Important Insight:** The Van der Waals equation predicts Zc = 0.375 for **all gases**, but experimentally Zc ≈ 0.27 to 0.29. This discrepancy is one of the major limitations of VdW and is the reason the Redlich-Kwong and other modern equations were developed. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Real Gas Behavior: Compressibility Factor Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Real Gas Behavior Analysis"] --> B["Measure Deviation"] B --> C["Compressibility Factor Z"] C --> D["Z less than 1
Attractive Forces Dominant"] C --> E["Z equals 1
Ideal Gas Behavior"] C --> F["Z greater than 1
Repulsive Forces Dominant"] D --> G["Low T High P Region
below Boyle Temperature"] E --> H["Low P High T Region
Gas Approaching Ideal State"] F --> I["Very High P Region
Molecular Co volume Effect"] C --> J["Generalized Chart Method"] J --> K["Calculate Reduced Properties Pr and Tr"] K --> L["Read Z from Nelson Obert Chart"] L --> M["Compute Actual v from Zv ideal"] C --> N["Equation of State Method"] N --> O["Van der Waals"] N --> P["Redlich Kwong"] N --> Q["Virial Equation"] N --> R["Beattie Bridgeman"] N --> S["Benedict Webb Rubin"] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Methodology for Solving Real Gas Problems ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputData["Step 1 Input Data"] A1["Given P T Gas Type"] A2["Critical Pressure Pc"] A3["Critical Temperature Tc"] end subgraph ComputeReduced["Step 2 Compute Reduced Properties"] B1["Pr equals P divided by Pc"] B2["Tr equals T divided by Tc"] end subgraph MethodChoice["Step 3 Choose Method"] C1["Use Generalized Chart"] C2["Use Equation of State"] end subgraph OutputResult["Step 4 Determine Result"] D1["Read Z from Chart"] D2["Compute v actual equals Z v ideal"] D3["Verify using EoS"] end InputData --> ComputeReduced ComputeReduced --> MethodChoice MethodChoice --> C1 MethodChoice --> C2 C1 --> D1 C2 --> D3 D1 --> D2 D3 --> D2 ``` ### 4.3 Comparison Block: Equations of State Accuracy vs Application Range ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Equations of State for Real Gases"] --> B["Van der Waals
2 Constants
Qualitative Accuracy"] A --> C["Redlich Kwong
2 Constants
Good for Hydrocarbons"] A --> D["Soave Redlich Kwong
3 Constants
Vapor Liquid Equilibrium"] A --> E["Peng Robinson
3 Constants
Best for Petroleum"] A --> F["Virial Equation
2 to 5 Coefficients
Theoretically Rigorous"] A --> G["Beattie Bridgeman
5 Constants
Moderate Pressure Gases"] A --> H["Benedict Webb Rubin
8 Constants
Light Gases and Hydrocarbons"] B --> I["Low Accuracy Low P"] C --> J["Moderate Accuracy Moderate P"] D --> K["High Accuracy High P"] E --> L["High Accuracy High P"] F --> M["Variable Accuracy Truncated Series"] G --> N["Good for Gases up to 200 bar"] H --> O["Excellent for Light Gases"] ``` ### 4.4 P-v-T Behavior: Schematic of Real vs Ideal Gas ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Pressure Volume Temperature P v T Surface"] --> B["Ideal Gas Surface
Smooth Hyperbolic Isotherms"] A --> C["Real Gas Surface
Non Hyperbolic with Critical Dome"] B --> D["PV equals RT always"] C --> E["Three Regions Identified"] E --> F["Vapor Region
Z less than 1"] E --> G["Critical Point
Inflection Point"] E --> H["Liquid Region
Z much less than 1"] G --> I["Pc Tc vc Values"] F --> J["Use Generalized Chart Method"] H --> J I --> K["Compute Reduced Properties"] K --> J ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define compressibility factor (Z) and explain its significance in characterizing real gas behavior.** **Model Answer:** The compressibility factor **Z** is a dimensionless quantity defined as the ratio of the actual specific volume of a real gas to the specific volume it would occupy as an ideal gas at the same temperature and pressure: $$Z = \frac{Pv}{RT} = \frac{v_{actual}}{v_{ideal}}$$ **Significance:** 1. **Deviation Indicator:** Z measures how far a real gas deviates from ideal gas behavior. 2. **Z = 1:** The gas behaves ideally (no deviation). 3. **Z < 1:** Attractive intermolecular forces dominate (typically at moderate pressures). 4. **Z > 1:** Repulsive forces / finite molecular volume dominate (typically at very high pressures). 5. **Practical Use:** The compressibility factor is used in the **Generalized Compressibility Chart** (Nelson-Obert chart) to determine the properties of real gases when P, T, and critical constants are known, without resorting to complex equations of state. It simplifies engineering calculations involving non-ideal gases in compressors, turbines, and refrigeration systems. **[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for significance]** --- #### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **State the Law of Corresponding States and write the expressions for reduced pressure and reduced temperature.** **Model Answer:** **Law of Corresponding States (Van der Waals, 1873):** All gases, when expressed in terms of their **reduced properties** (reduced pressure, reduced temperature, and reduced volume), behave identically. In other words, the compressibility factor Z is a universal function of reduced pressure (Pr) and reduced temperature (Tr) for all substances: $$Z = f(P_r, T_r)$$ This means that all gases at the same reduced state will have the same deviation from ideal gas behavior, regardless of their chemical identity. This principle enables the use of a **single generalized compressibility chart** for all gases. **Expressions for Reduced Properties:** $$P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}, \quad T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}$$ Where Pc and Tc are the critical pressure and critical temperature of the gas, respectively. **[3 Marks: 1 for law statement, 1 for each expression]** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice #### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the Van der Waals equation of state from the ideal gas law, clearly stating the corrections applied. Derive the expressions for critical pressure, critical temperature, and critical volume in terms of the Van der Waals constants *a* and *b*. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** For CO₂, the Van der Waals constants are: a = 0.364 Pa·m⁶/mol², b = 4.27 × 10⁻⁵ m³/mol. Calculate the critical constants (Pc, Tc, Vc) and the critical compressibility factor Zc. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (As in Section 3.1 and 3.4 of these notes):** The ideal gas law **Pv = RT** assumes point molecules with no intermolecular forces. Two corrections: 1. **Volume correction (b):** Real molecules have finite size; effective volume = (v − b). 2. **Pressure correction (a/v²):** Intermolecular attractions reduce the impact pressure on walls. Combining: $$\left(P + \frac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$$ For critical point conditions: $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T_c} = 0$ and $\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial v^2}\right)_{T_c} = 0$ Solving these simultaneously (as derived in Section 3.4): $$v_c = 3b, \quad T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb}, \quad P_c = \frac{a}{27b^2}$$ **[7 Marks: 3 for VdW derivation, 4 for critical constant derivations]** --- **(b) Numerical Calculation:** **Given:** a = 0.364 Pa·m⁶/mol², b = 4.27 × 10⁻⁵ m³/mol, R̄ = 8.314 J/mol·K **Step 1 — Critical Volume (Vc):** $$V_c = 3b = 3 \times 4.27 \times 10^{-5} = 1.281 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$ **Step 2 — Critical Temperature (Tc):** $$T_c = \frac{8a}{27\bar{R}b} = \frac{8 \times 0.364}{27 \times 8.314 \times 4.27 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$T_c = \frac{2.912}{9.585 \times 10^{-3}} = 303.81 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3 — Critical Pressure (Pc):** $$P_c = \frac{a}{27b^2} = \frac{0.364}{27 \times (4.27 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$ $$P_c = \frac{0.364}{27 \times 1.823 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{0.364}{4.922 \times 10^{-8}} = 7.396 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 7.40 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4 — Critical Compressibility Factor:** $$Z_c = \frac{P_c V_c}{\bar{R} T_c} = \frac{7.396 \times 10^6 \times 1.281 \times 10^{-4}}{8.314 \times 303.81}$$ $$Z_c = \frac{947.4}{2525.95} = 0.375$$ **Result:** Vc = 1.281 × 10⁻⁴ m³/mol, Tc = 303.81 K, Pc = 7.40 MPa, Zc = 0.375 **Note:** Compare with experimental values for CO₂: Tc ≈ 304.13 K, Pc ≈ 7.38 MPa, Zc ≈ 0.274. The VdW prediction of Zc = 0.375 deviates by ~37% from reality. **[7 Marks: 2 for Vc, 2 for Tc, 2 for Pc, 1 for Zc]** --- #### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **(a)** A gas obeys the Van der Waals equation with a = 0.137 Pa·m⁶/mol² and b = 3.72 × 10⁻⁵ m³/mol. Calculate the **Boyle temperature** and the **inversion temperature** for this gas. Explain the physical significance of each. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Using the **Generalized Compressibility Chart** approach, find the specific volume of nitrogen at 10 MPa and 150 K, given that for N₂: R = 0.2968 kJ/kg·K, Pc = 3.39 MPa, Tc = 126.2 K. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **(a) Boyle and Inversion Temperatures:** **Boyle Temperature (TB):** The temperature at which a real gas behaves ideally over a small pressure range near zero pressure. The formula is: $$T_B = \frac{a}{\bar{R}b}$$ Substituting values: $$T_B = \frac{0.137}{8.314 \times 3.72 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.137}{3.093 \times 10^{-4}} = 442.9 \text{ K}$$ **Inversion Temperature (Tinv):** The temperature at which the Joule-Thomson coefficient changes sign, important in throttling processes: $$T_{inv} = \frac{2a}{\bar{R}b} = 2 \times T_B = 2 \times 442.9 = 885.8 \text{ K}$$ **Physical Significance:** - **Boyle Temperature:** Below TB, Z < 1 at low pressures (attractive forces dominant). Above TB, Z > 1 even at low pressures (repulsive/kinetic effects dominant). At TB, the gas most closely approximates ideal behavior over a range of pressures. - **Inversion Temperature:** In a throttling (Joule-Thomson) process, a gas cools upon expansion if T < Tinv and heats up if T > Tinv. This is critical in **LNG liquefaction processes** and **refrigeration cycles**. **[7 Marks: 2 for TB calculation, 2 for Tinv calculation, 3 for physical significance]** --- **(b) Generalized Compressibility Chart Method:** **Given:** P = 10 MPa, T = 150 K, R = 0.2968 kJ/kg·K, Pc = 3.39 MPa, Tc = 126.2 K **Step 1 — Compute Reduced Properties:** $$P_r = \frac{P}{P_c} = \frac{10}{3.39} = 2.95$$ $$T_r = \frac{T}{T_c} = \frac{150}{126.2} = 1.189$$ **Step 2 — Read Z from Generalized Compressibility Chart (Nelson-Obert):** At Pr = 2.95 and Tr = 1.189, from the chart: $$Z \approx 0.54 \text{ (approximate, read from chart)}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Ideal Gas Specific Volume:** $$v_{ideal} = \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{0.2968 \times 150}{10 \times 10^3} = \frac{44.52}{10000} = 4.452 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Step 4 — Compute Actual Specific Volume:** $$v_{actual} = Z \times v_{ideal} = 0.54 \times 4.452 \times 10^{-3} = 2.404 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Result:** v_actual ≈ 2.40 × 10⁻³ m³/kg > [!NOTE] > **Practical Insight:** The actual specific volume is significantly less than the ideal value because at 10 MPa and 150 K, N₂ is close to its critical point (Tc = 126.2 K), where compressibility is very low. This is precisely why high-pressure cryogenic nitrogen storage and transportation require real gas corrections. **[7 Marks: 2 for reduced properties, 2 for Z reading, 2 for v calculation, 1 for physical insight]** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — Read Carefully Before Writing the Exam:** > 1. **Forgetting to use R̄ (Universal Gas Constant)** when working on a **molar basis**. Many students incorrectly use R (specific gas constant) with molar volume. Marks lost: **2 marks**. > 2. **Mixing up Critical Constants:** Critical pressure Pc is the **maximum** pressure at which liquid and vapor can coexist — students often confuse this with saturation pressure. Marks lost: **1–2 marks**. > 3. **Using Pv = RT directly in Boyle Temperature Derivation:** The condition for Boyle temperature is **Z ≈ 1 over a range of P**, not Z = 1 at a single point. Marks lost: **1 mark**. > 4. **Reduced Property Units:** Reduced pressure and temperature are **dimensionless** — do not write units. Marks lost: **0.5 marks** (presentation penalty). > 5. **Chart Reading Precision:** When using generalized compressibility chart, KTU expects Z to **two decimal places** with proper reasoning — stating just "Z from chart = 0.5" without showing Pr, Tr loses **2 marks**. > 6. **Inversion Temperature Formula Confusion:** Some students write Tinv = a/(Rb) instead of 2a/(Rb). Remember: Tinv = 2 × TB. Marks lost: **1 mark**. > 7. **Van der Waals expansion errors:** When expanding $(P + a/v^2)(v - b)$, the term $-ab/v^2$ is often dropped. Marks lost: **1 mark**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Deviations from Ideal Gas Model** - **Core Definition:** Compressibility Factor **Z = Pv/RT** is the master measure of deviation. **Z = 1** → ideal; **Z < 1** → attractive forces dominant; **Z > 1** → repulsive forces dominant. - **Van der Waals Equation:** $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$ — the simplest real gas EoS, accounts for both finite molecular volume and intermolecular attraction. - **Critical Constants from VdW:** Always memorize: $v_c = 3b$, $T_c = \dfrac{8a}{27Rb}$, $P_c = \dfrac{a}{27b^2}$, and $Z_c = 0.375$ (a constant for all VdW gases). - **Boyle Temperature:** $T_B = \dfrac{a}{Rb}$ — temperature of ideal behavior near P = 0. - **Inversion Temperature:** $T_{inv} = \dfrac{2a}{Rb} = 2T_B$ — boundary between cooling and heating on throttling. - **Virial Equation:** $Z = 1 + \dfrac{B(T)}{v} + \dfrac{C(T)}{v^2} + \cdots$ — theoretically rigorous; truncation at second coefficient (B) is common. - **Law of Corresponding States:** $Z = f(P_r, T_r)$ — universal chart (Nelson-Obert) gives Z for any gas using reduced properties. - **Reduced Properties (Dimensionless):** $P_r = P/P_c$, $T_r = T/T_c$ — these are the inputs to the generalized compressibility chart. - **Equations Ranked by Accuracy (low → high P):** VdW → Redlich-Kwong → SRK/PR → Beattie-Bridgeman → BWR → Virial (when fully expanded). - **Real-World Use Cases:** LNG production, refrigeration cycles, gas pipelines, compressor design, chemical reaction equilibria at high pressure. - **Memory Aid — "BART" for VdW Criticals:** **B**oyle T_B = a/(Rb), **A**ttraction a reduces Z, **R**epulsion b increases Z, **T**critical = 8a/(27Rb). - **KTU Favorite Questions:** (i) Derive VdW and find critical constants; (ii) Compute Z, v_actual using generalized chart; (iii) Explain Z behavior with T and P; (iv) Calculate TB and Tinv for a given gas. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** Whenever the question says "real gas," the **first thing** to write is the **compressibility factor definition** and the **ideal gas law**, then show the deviation. This signals to the examiner that you understand the foundational concept and ensures partial marks even if the rest of the calculation goes wrong.

topics.title: Compressibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Compressibility ## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **COMPRESSIBILITY (KTU 2024 Definition)** > **Compressibility** is a fundamental thermodynamic property that quantitatively measures the *fractional reduction in the volume of a fluid (or solid) per unit increase in pressure*, under specified thermal constraints. It is the macroscopic manifestation of the microscopic inter-molecular spacing responding to externally applied compressive forces. Mathematically, it is defined as the **negative** of the **partial derivative of specific volume (or volume) with respect to pressure**, normalized by the specific volume itself. The negative sign is a thermodynamic convention ensuring that compressibility is reported as a **positive quantity** (since volume decreases as pressure increases). $$ \beta \;\equiv\; -\frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{\text{constraint}} \quad \left[\text{Units: } \text{Pa}^{-1} \; \text{ or } \; \text{m}^2/\text{N}\right] $$ ### 1.2 The Two Principal Variants (KTU Syllabus Mandate) Depending on the **thermal path** (i.e., whether heat is exchanged with the surroundings), compressibility takes two distinct forms: | Variant | Constraint (Path) | Physical Situation | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Isothermal Compressibility ($\beta_T$)** | Constant Temperature ($T = \text{const}$) | Slow, quasi-static compression in a *thermally conductive* piston | Liquids, Real Gases, Refrigerants | | **Isentropic (Adiabatic) Compressibility ($\beta_S$)** | Constant Entropy ($S = \text{const}$) | Rapid compression in an *insulated* piston | Sound waves, Shock waves, Nozzle flow | $$ \beta_T = -\frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} \qquad\qquad \beta_S = -\frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{S} $$ ### 1.3 The Bulk Modulus — The Reciprocal Twin > [!NOTE] > The **Bulk Modulus** ($K$ or $E_v$) is the *reciprocal* of compressibility and represents the **resistance of a fluid to uniform compression**. A higher bulk modulus indicates a stiffer, less compressible medium. $$ K = \frac{1}{\beta} = -v\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right) \quad [\text{Units: Pa or N/m}^2] $$ ### 1.4 The Compressibility Factor ($Z$) — For Real Gases For **ideal gases**, the equation of state is exact: $Pv = RT$. For **real gases**, deviations are captured by the dimensionless **Compressibility Factor ($Z$)**: $$ Z = \frac{Pv}{RT} = \frac{PV}{nRT} $$ - $Z = 1$: Ideal gas behavior - $Z < 1$: Attractive intermolecular forces dominate (volume less than ideal) - $Z > 1$: Repulsive forces dominate (volume greater than ideal) ### 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🧽💨 > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Sponge vs. The Syringe** > > Imagine squeezing two objects in your hand: > 1. **A wet sponge** has a **HIGH compressibility** (low bulk modulus). Air escapes easily, water flows out, and its volume drops dramatically with little applied force. > 2. **A solid steel block** has an **EXTREMELY LOW compressibility** (very high bulk modulus, $K_{\text{steel}} \approx 160 \text{ GPa}$). You cannot visibly reduce its volume even with enormous pressure. > > **Engineering Connection**: In hydraulic brake systems, brake fluid must have *very low compressibility* so that all the pedal force is transmitted instantly without "spongy" lag. In contrast, gases used in pneumatic springs are *highly compressible*, allowing them to absorb shock. ### 1.6 Why Study Compressibility? > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Real-World Relevance** > - **Aerospace & Gas Dynamics**: At Mach > 0.3, airflow becomes compressible and density changes must be accounted for. > - **Refrigeration & HVAC**: Compressor work depends on the compressibility of the refrigerant gas. > - **Hydraulics**: Water hammer and pressure surges depend on fluid compressibility. > - **Acoustics**: The speed of sound $c = \sqrt{1/(\rho \beta_S)}$ is governed by *isentropic* compressibility. > - **Petroleum Engineering**: Compressibility of reservoir fluids dictates oil recovery strategy. ## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Decomposition **Step 1: The Physical Origin** At the microscopic level, every substance comprises molecules separated by intermolecular spacing. When external pressure increases, these molecules are forced closer together, reducing the macroscopic volume. **Compressibility quantifies how willing the substance is to undergo this volume reduction.** **Step 2: Why the Negative Sign?** A positive change in pressure ($\partial P > 0$) almost always produces a *negative* change in volume ($\partial v < 0$). Hence the ratio $\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)$ is intrinsically negative. The leading negative sign in the definition makes $\beta$ a **positive thermodynamic property** for tabulation. **Step 3: The Two Thermodynamic Paths** - *Isothermal* ($\beta_T$): Heat leaves the system as it compresses, keeping $T$ constant. This is the equilibrium property of the equation of state. - *Isentropic* ($\beta_S$): No heat transfer; compression heats the fluid. $\beta_S$ is always **smaller** (less compressible) than $\beta_T$ because the internal pressure rise from heating *opposes* further compression. **Step 4: Connection to Speed of Sound** The speed of sound $c$ in a fluid is given by: $$ c = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_{S}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\rho \beta_S}} $$ This is why $\beta_S$ (not $\beta_T$) governs acoustic propagation. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates every formula, definition, and boundary relation required for KTU 2024 Scheme board examinations on Compressibility. | # | Formula / Property | Symbolic Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Isothermal Compressibility | $\beta_T = -\dfrac{1}{v}\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T}$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | | 2 | Isentropic Compressibility | $\beta_S = -\dfrac{1}{v}\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{S}$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | | 3 | Bulk Modulus (Volumetric) | $K = \dfrac{1}{\beta} = -v\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)$ | $\text{Pa}$ | | 4 | Relation to Bulk Modulus | $\beta_T = \dfrac{1}{K_T}, \quad \beta_S = \dfrac{1}{K_S}$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | | 5 | Ratio (KTU High-Yield) | $\dfrac{\beta_T}{\beta_S} = \gamma = \dfrac{C_P}{C_V}$ | Dimensionless | | 6 | Ideal Gas Isothermal | $\beta_T = \dfrac{1}{P}$ | From $Pv = RT$ | | 7 | Ideal Gas Isentropic | $\beta_S = \dfrac{1}{\gamma P}$ | From $Pv^\gamma = \text{const}$ | | 8 | Speed of Sound (general) | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\rho \beta_S}}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | | 9 | Speed of Sound (ideal gas) | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | | 10 | Compressibility Factor | $Z = \dfrac{Pv}{RT}$ | $Z = 1 \Rightarrow$ Ideal | | 11 | Real Gas EOS (vdW) | $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$ | $a, b$ are constants | | 12 | Partial Derivative Identity | $\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{v} \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial v}\right)_{P} = -1$ | Cyclic rule | | 13 | Maxwell Relation (used) | $\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = \dfrac{1}{\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T}}$ | When treating $v=v(P,T)$ | | 14 | Vanishing limit | $\beta \to 0 \text{ as } P \to \infty$ | Incompressible fluid limit | ### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Where Compressibility is Mission-Critical** > - **Aircraft Engine Inlets**: Compressibility losses dictate diffuser design at high Mach numbers. > - **Deep-Sea Submersibles**: External pressure rises by ~1 atm per 10 m; hull compressibility determines crush depth. > - **Natural Gas Pipelines**: $Z$ factor corrections are essential for accurate mass flow metering. > - **Diesel Fuel Injection**: Compressibility of fuel affects injection timing and spray atomization. > - **Geothermal Reservoirs**: Formation compressibility controls subsidence and pressure decline. ### 2.4 Maxwell Relation Bridge A subtle but KTU-relevant derivation point: $\beta_T$ can be expressed in terms of $P$, $v$, $T$ partials. Since $v = v(P, T)$: $$ dv = \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} dP + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_{P} dT $$ Therefore: $$ \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = \text{coefficient of } dP \text{ when } dT = 0 $$ This is the precise operational meaning KTU examiners test. ## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Isothermal Compressibility of an Ideal Gas **Given:** Equation of state of an ideal gas: $Pv = RT$. **To Prove:** $\beta_T = \dfrac{1}{P}$. **Proof:** We start from the definition: $$ \beta_T = -\frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} $$ Since $T$ is held constant, we differentiate the EOS $Pv = RT$ with respect to $P$ while treating $T$ as fixed: $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial P}(Pv)_{T} = \frac{\partial}{\partial P}(RT)_{T} $$ Because $R$ and $T$ are constants during this partial differentiation, the right-hand side vanishes: $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial P}(Pv)_{T} = 0 $$ Applying the product rule to the left-hand side: $$ P \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} + v \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = 0 $$ Since $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = 1$, we get: $$ P \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} + v = 0 $$ Solving for $\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T}$: $$ \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -\frac{v}{P} $$ Substituting back into the definition of $\beta_T$: $$ \beta_T = -\frac{1}{v} \cdot \left(-\frac{v}{P}\right) $$ The $v$ terms cancel and the negatives cancel: $$ \boxed{\;\beta_T = \frac{1}{P}\;} $$ **Interpretation:** For an ideal gas, isothermal compressibility depends *only* on the prevailing pressure. Doubling $P$ halves the compressibility — physically, the gas becomes stiffer under higher pressure. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Isentropic Compressibility of an Ideal Gas **Given:** Isentropic relation for an ideal gas: $Pv^{\gamma} = \text{const} = C$. **To Prove:** $\beta_S = \dfrac{1}{\gamma P}$. **Proof:** Differentiate $Pv^{\gamma} = C$ with respect to $P$ holding $S$ constant: $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial P}\left(P v^{\gamma}\right)_{S} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial P} = 0 $$ Apply the product rule: $$ P \cdot \gamma v^{\gamma - 1} \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{S} + v^{\gamma} \cdot \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial P}\right)_{S} = 0 $$ Recognize $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial P}\right)_{S} = 1$: $$ P \gamma v^{\gamma - 1} \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{S} + v^{\gamma} = 0 $$ Divide through by $v^{\gamma - 1}$: $$ P \gamma \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{S} + v = 0 $$ Solve for the partial: $$ \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{S} = -\frac{v}{\gamma P} $$ Substitute into the isentropic compressibility definition: $$ \beta_S = -\frac{1}{v} \cdot \left(-\frac{v}{\gamma P}\right) $$ Cancel and simplify: $$ \boxed{\;\beta_S = \frac{1}{\gamma P}\;} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Universal Ratio $\beta_T / \beta_S = \gamma$ (KTU High-Yield) **Given:** Definitions of $\beta_T$, $\beta_S$, and Maxwell relations. **To Prove:** $\beta_T = \gamma \beta_S$. **Proof:** Using the identity involving partial derivatives of state variables, we know that for $v = v(P, T)$: $$ \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{S} + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_{P} \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{S} $$ This follows from a change of variables (holding $S$ vs. holding $T$). Using the cyclic rule and thermodynamic identities: $$ \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{S} = -\frac{\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial S}\right)_{P}}{\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_{P}} $$ Substituting this back and simplifying (the detailed KTU-board step-by-step): $$ \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = \gamma \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{S} $$ Divide both sides by $-v$: $$ -\frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -\frac{\gamma}{v}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{S} $$ Recognizing the left side as $\beta_T$ and the right as $\gamma \beta_S$: $$ \boxed{\;\beta_T = \gamma \, \beta_S\;} $$ **This relation is the single most-tested compressibility identity in KTU examinations.** --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Compressibility Factor for a Van der Waals Gas **Given:** Van der Waals equation: $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$. **To Prove:** Expression for $Z = \dfrac{Pv}{RT}$ in terms of $a, b, v$. **Proof:** Expand the vdW equation: $$ Pv + \frac{a}{v} - Pb - \frac{ab}{v^2} = RT $$ Divide both sides by $RT$: $$ \frac{Pv}{RT} + \frac{a}{vRT} - \frac{Pb}{RT} - \frac{ab}{v^2 RT} = 1 $$ Isolate $\frac{Pv}{RT}$: $$ Z = \frac{Pv}{RT} = 1 + \frac{Pb}{RT} + \frac{ab}{v^2 RT} - \frac{a}{vRT} $$ Factoring $\dfrac{1}{vRT}$ where possible: $$ \boxed{\;Z = \frac{Pv}{RT} = 1 + \frac{Pb}{RT} + \frac{a}{vRT}\left(\frac{b}{v} - 1\right)\;} $$ This is the standard KTU-board form for the compressibility factor from the vdW EOS. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Order of Magnitudes | Substance | Typical $\beta_T$ ($\text{Pa}^{-1}$) | Typical $K$ (Pa) | Physical State | |---|---|---|---| | Water (20°C) | $4.59 \times 10^{-10}$ | $2.18 \times 10^{9}$ | Nearly incompressible | | Air (1 atm, 300 K) | $\dfrac{1}{101325} \approx 9.87 \times 10^{-6}$ | $1.01 \times 10^{5}$ | Highly compressible | | Steel | $\approx 6 \times 10^{-12}$ | $\approx 1.6 \times 10^{11}$ | Virtually incompressible | | Mercury | $4.0 \times 10^{-11}$ | $2.5 \times 10^{10}$ | Nearly incompressible | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight**: Solids and liquids have compressibilities roughly **4 to 6 orders of magnitude smaller** than gases at atmospheric pressure — justifying the "incompressible flow" assumption in most liquid hydraulic systems. --- ### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) **Problem:** For air at $P = 200 \text{ kPa}$ and $\gamma = 1.4$, calculate (a) isothermal compressibility, (b) isentropic compressibility, and (c) verify the ratio $\beta_T / \beta_S = \gamma$. **Solution:** **(a) Isothermal compressibility** (using ideal gas formula): $$ \beta_T = \frac{1}{P} = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^{3}} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{Pa}^{-1} $$ **(b) Isentropic compressibility**: $$ \beta_S = \frac{1}{\gamma P} = \frac{1}{1.4 \times 200 \times 10^{3}} = \frac{1}{2.8 \times 10^{5}} $$ $$ \beta_S = 3.571 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{Pa}^{-1} $$ **(c) Ratio verification**: $$ \frac{\beta_T}{\beta_S} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-6}}{3.571 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.4 = \gamma \quad \checkmark $$ ## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Compressibility Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Compressibility Property] --> B[Isothermal beta_T] A --> C[Isentropic beta_S] A --> D[Bulk Modulus K] B --> E["Path: T = const
Heat exchange allowed"] C --> F["Path: S = const
Adiabatic reversible"] D --> G["K = 1/beta
Resistance to compression"] B --> H[EOS: Pv = RT] C --> I[EOS: Pv^gamma = C] D --> J[Speed of Sound c] H --> K["beta_T = 1/P (ideal gas)"] I --> L["beta_S = 1/(gamma P) (ideal gas)"] K --> M["beta_T = gamma beta_S"] L --> M M --> N[Universal Ratio Identity] A --> O[Real Gas Correction] O --> P[Compressibility Factor Z] P --> Q["Z = Pv/RT"] Q --> R["Z = 1: Ideal"] Q --> S["Z less than 1: Attractive forces"] Q --> T["Z greater than 1: Repulsive forces"] J --> U["c = sqrt(1 / rho beta_S)"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Operational Flow of Compressibility Determination ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant PVT as Equation of State participant Def as Mathematical Definition participant Solver as Derivative Solver participant Result as beta Value PVT->>Def: Input v = v(P, T) or v = v(P, S) Def->>Solver: Apply partial derivative operator Note over Solver: -1/v times partial derivative
Respecting thermal constraint Solver->>Result: Evaluate at state point (P, T or S) Result-->>Def: Return compressibility value in Pa^-1 Def->>PVT: Cross-check with bulk modulus reciprocal Note over PVT,Result: For ideal gas:
beta_T = 1/P
beta_S = 1/(gamma P) ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Compressibility Factor Regimes ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> LowPressure: P small LowPressure --> IdealBehavior: Z approx 1 [*] --> ModeratePressure: P moderate ModeratePressure --> AttractiveDominant: Z less than 1 AttractiveDominant --> CrossoverPoint: Z minimum CrossoverPoint --> RepulsiveDominant: Z greater than 1 RepulsiveDominant --> HighPressure: P large HighPressure --> [*] IdealBehavior --> [*] ``` ### 4.4 Conceptual Schematic — Compressibility vs. State Path | Path | Condition | Compressibility | Physical Scenario | |---|---|---|---| | Isothermal | $T$ = const | $\beta_T = \dfrac{1}{v}\left(-\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | Slow piston in conducting cylinder | | Isentropic | $S$ = const | $\beta_S = \dfrac{1}{v}\left(-\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_S$ | Sound wave, rapid compression | | Isobaric | $P$ = const | (No $\beta$ defined) | Heating fluid at constant pressure | ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Compressibility in Engineering Systems ```mermaid flowchart LR A[External Pressure Input] --> B[Fluid System] B --> C{Path Type Selection} C -->|Slow Quasi-static| D[Isothermal Path] C -->|Rapid Adiabatic| E[Isentropic Path] D --> F[Compute beta_T from EOS] E --> G[Compute beta_S from EOS] F --> H[Engineering Output: Volume Change, Storage Capacity] G --> I[Engineering Output: Sound Speed, Shock Behavior] H --> J[Hydraulic System Design] I --> K[Aerodynamic / Acoustic Design] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define isothermal compressibility and isentropic compressibility. State the relationship between them.* **Model Answer:** - **Isothermal Compressibility ($\beta_T$)**: It is defined as the negative fractional change in volume per unit change in pressure when the process is carried out at constant temperature: $$ \beta_T = -\frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} $$ - **Isentropic Compressibility ($\beta_S$)**: It is defined as the negative fractional change in volume per unit change in pressure when the process is reversible and adiabatic (constant entropy): $$ \beta_S = -\frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{S} $$ - **Relationship**: $$ \frac{\beta_T}{\beta_S} = \gamma = \frac{C_P}{C_V} $$ Therefore $\beta_T = \gamma \beta_S$. Since $\gamma > 1$, we always have $\beta_T > \beta_S$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define the compressibility factor Z. What does Z = 1, Z < 1, and Z > 1 signify?* **Model Answer:** The **compressibility factor** $Z$ is a dimensionless quantity measuring the deviation of a real gas from ideal gas behavior: $$ Z = \frac{Pv}{RT} = \frac{PV}{nRT} $$ - **$Z = 1$**: The gas behaves as an ideal gas (no intermolecular interactions). - **$Z < 1$**: Attractive intermolecular forces dominate, pulling molecules closer than ideal, reducing the actual volume. - **$Z > 1$**: Repulsive intermolecular forces dominate (especially at high pressures), increasing the volume beyond the ideal prediction. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > - Students frequently **forget the negative sign** in the compressibility definition and end up with a *negative* answer (which is dimensionally inconsistent and will lose 2 marks). > - In derivations, examiners strictly check **each algebraic step**. Skipping even one line results in partial credit (typically 1–2 marks lost per skipped step). > - Confusing $\beta_T$ with $\beta_S$ when the problem mentions "adiabatic" or "rapid" compression is the **#1 error** in KTU valuation reports. > - Units must accompany every numerical answer. Writing "$\beta = 5 \times 10^{-6}$" without "Pa$^{-1}$" loses a mark. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the isothermal compressibility of an ideal gas. Show that it depends only on pressure. **(b)** For a gas with $\gamma = 1.3$ at $P = 150 \text{ kPa}$, calculate the isothermal compressibility, isentropic compressibility, and bulk modulus. Comment on why $\beta_S < \beta_T$. **Model Solution:** **(a)** *Derivation (7 marks)* — *Refer to Section 3.1 above.* Starting from $Pv = RT$, differentiate w.r.t. $P$ at constant $T$: $$ P\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T + v = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{v}{P} $$ Therefore: $$ \beta_T = -\frac{1}{v}\left(-\frac{v}{P}\right) = \frac{1}{P} $$ Since $P$ is the only variable, $\beta_T$ depends *exclusively* on pressure for an ideal gas. (3 marks for identifying the result, 4 marks for the algebraic steps.) **(b)** *Calculation (7 marks)* Given: $\gamma = 1.3$, $P = 150 \text{ kPa} = 150{,}000 \text{ Pa}$. **Step 1 — Isothermal compressibility:** $$ \beta_T = \frac{1}{P} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{5}} = 6.667 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{Pa}^{-1} $$ **Step 2 — Isentropic compressibility:** $$ \beta_S = \frac{1}{\gamma P} = \frac{1}{1.3 \times 1.5 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{1}{1.95 \times 10^{5}} = 5.128 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{Pa}^{-1} $$ **Step 3 — Bulk modulus (using $\beta_T$):** $$ K_T = \frac{1}{\beta_T} = P = 1.5 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa} = 150 \; \text{kPa} $$ **Step 4 — Comment:** $\beta_S < \beta_T$ because in an isentropic process, compression raises the fluid temperature, which generates an additional internal thermal pressure that *resists* further compression. The fluid is effectively stiffer than in the isothermal case, where heat is removed and the temperature remains low. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a)** Starting from thermodynamic definitions and Maxwell relations, derive the relation $\beta_T = \gamma \beta_S$. **(b)** The speed of sound in a gas is given by $c = \sqrt{1/(\rho \beta_S)}$. For air at $T = 400$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/kg·K, calculate the speed of sound. Use $\beta_S = 1/(\gamma P)$ and the ideal gas law to express $P/\rho$. **Model Solution:** **(a)** *Derivation (7 marks)* — *Refer to Section 3.3 above.* From $v = v(P, T)$, the change of variables yields: $$ \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{S} + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_{P} \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{S} $$ Using the cyclic rule and the Maxwell relation $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{S} = \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial S}\right)_{P}$, combined with $T dS = C_P dT$ and entropy relations, one arrives at: $$ \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = \gamma \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_{S} $$ Multiplying both sides by $-1/v$: $$ \beta_T = \gamma \beta_S $$ **(b)** *Calculation (7 marks)* Given: $T = 400$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/kg·K. **Step 1 — Express $P/\rho$ using ideal gas law:** $$ P = \rho R T \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{P}{\rho} = RT $$ **Step 2 — Express $\beta_S$:** $$ \beta_S = \frac{1}{\gamma P} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the speed of sound formula:** $$ c = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\rho \beta_S}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\rho \cdot \frac{1}{\gamma P}}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}} $$ Substitute $P/\rho = RT$: $$ c = \sqrt{\gamma R T} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical evaluation:** $$ c = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 400} $$ $$ c = \sqrt{160{,}720} \approx 400.9 \; \text{m/s} $$ The speed of sound in air at 400 K is approximately **400.9 m/s**. --- ### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must Remember — Compressibility (KTU 2024 Module 4)** - ✅ **Definition core**: $\beta = -\dfrac{1}{v}\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)$ — always positive due to the negative sign convention. - ✅ **Two variants**: $\beta_T$ (isothermal, slow) and $\beta_S$ (isentropic, fast/adiabatic). - ✅ **Ideal gas formulas**: $\beta_T = \dfrac{1}{P}$, $\beta_S = \dfrac{1}{\gamma P}$. - ✅ **Universal ratio**: $\beta_T = \gamma \beta_S$ — single most important identity for KTU. - ✅ **Bulk modulus**: $K = \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ — stiffness of the fluid. - ✅ **Compressibility factor**: $Z = \dfrac{Pv}{RT}$ — measures real-gas deviation. - ✅ **Speed of sound**: $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ (ideal gas) — directly tied to $\beta_S$. - ✅ **Order of magnitude**: Liquids/solids have $\beta \sim 10^{-10}$ Pa$^{-1}$; gases have $\beta \sim 10^{-5}$ Pa$^{-1}$. - ✅ **vdW compressibility**: $Z = 1 + \dfrac{Pb}{RT} + \dfrac{a}{vRT}\left(\dfrac{b}{v} - 1\right)$ — KTU high-yield. - ✅ **Negative sign warning**: Never report $\beta$ as a negative number. - ✅ **Units discipline**: Always append $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ to $\beta$ and $\text{Pa}$ to $K$. - ✅ **Constraint recognition**: Read the problem carefully to identify whether the compression is isothermal or adiabatic before selecting the appropriate formula.

topics.title: Van-der-Waals Equation of State
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET403 - ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Van-der-Waals Equation of State

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Van der Waals Equation of State (vdW EOS)** is a cubic equation of state proposed by Johannes Diderik van der Waals (1873) that describes the thermodynamic state of a **real (non-ideal) fluid** by modifying the classical ideal gas law to account for two critical physical realities: the **finite volume occupied by gas molecules** and the **intermolecular attractive (cohesive) forces** that exist between them.

For a gas of mass $m$ occupying volume $V$ with specific volume $v = V/m$, the vdW equation is:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{v^{2}}\right)\left(v - b\right) = RT$$

On a **molar basis** (n moles, molar volume $\bar{v} = V/n$):

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{\bar{v}^{2}}\right)\left(\bar{v} - b\right) = \bar{R}T$$

Where:
- **$a$** → Van der Waals constant representing the magnitude of **attractive intermolecular forces** (units: $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{m}^{6}/\text{mol}^{2}$ or $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}^{4}/\text{kg}^{2}$ for specific volume form).
- **$b$** → Van der Waals constant representing the **effective excluded volume per mole/kg** due to the finite size of molecules (units: $\text{m}^{3}/\text{mol}$ or $\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}$).
- **$R$** → Characteristic (specific) gas constant, $\bar{R} = 8.314\,\text{J/mol}\cdot\text{K}$ for the molar form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The vdW equation is the *first* equation of state in Module 4 that students must master. It is the conceptual gateway to all modern cubic equations of state (Redlich-Kwong, Peng-Robinson, SRK) asked in subsequent modules.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowded Lecture Hall" Model

Imagine a **packed lecture hall** during an exam, where students represent gas molecules:

1. **The "b" constant (Excluded Volume):** Each student occupies an *actual physical space* (a desk + chair + personal bubble). You cannot compress the crowd beyond the sum of all these personal spaces — the *usable* floor area is reduced. The constant **$b$** accounts for this irreducible volume that the gas *cannot* access.

2. **The "a" constant (Attractive Forces):** Students near the back of the room experience a slight "pull" toward their friends sitting in front — this friendly attraction reduces the effective **pressure** they exert on the doors (walls). The term $a/v^2$ subtracts from the *ideal* pressure that an assembly of non-interacting point particles would have exerted. The $1/v^2$ dependence arises because the attractive interaction is proportional to **both** the number of molecules striking the wall AND the number of molecules in the bulk pulling them back (a two-body interaction effect).

3. **Without corrections (Ideal Gas):** If the students were "ghost particles" (zero size, no attraction), the equation would collapse to the simple $Pv = RT$ — the **ideal gas law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Real gases deviate from ideality most strongly near the **saturation/condensation region** (low temperatures, high densities). At very low density (large $v$), the corrections $a/v^2$ and $b$ become negligible, and vdW smoothly reduces to the ideal gas law — exactly as physics demands.

---

## 1.3 Why vdW Equation is Indispensable

| Engineering Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Refrigeration & Liquefaction** | Accurate P-v-T data near the saturation dome (vapor-compression cycles) |
| **Petrochemical Industry** | Phase equilibria of hydrocarbons (C₁–C₄ alkanes) |
| **Power Plant Boilers** | High-pressure steam properties deviate substantially from ideal gas |
| **Gas Pipeline Design** | Compressibility factor $Z$ prediction for natural gas transport |
| **Cryogenics** | Helium, hydrogen, and nitrogen behavior at extreme conditions |
| **Process Simulation** | Foundation for software like Aspen Plus, HYSYS, ChemCAD |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of the Two Corrections

The ideal gas equation $Pv = RT$ rests on two simplistic assumptions:

1. Molecules are **point masses** (no volume).
2. Molecules exert **no forces** on each other except during instantaneous elastic collisions.

Van der Waals relaxed these assumptions systematically:

### Correction 1 — The Pressure Deficit (Origin of $a/v^2$)

Consider a molecule approaching a wall to deliver a momentum impulse (and hence, pressure). If attractive forces exist, molecules in the bulk *pull back* on the wall-bound molecule, reducing the impact force. The number of bulk molecules is $\propto 1/v$, and the number of wall-approaching molecules is also $\propto 1/v$. The product yields a $1/v^2$ dependence. The **internal pressure** or **cohesive pressure** term $\frac{a}{v^2}$ must be **added** to the observed pressure $P$ to obtain the *ideal* kinetic pressure:

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = P + \frac{a}{v^{2}}$$

### Correction 2 — The Excluded Volume (Origin of $b$)

For hard-sphere molecules of diameter $d$, the *excluded volume* per molecule is $\frac{2}{3}\pi d^{3}$ (for 2-body collisions; factor of 4 for two molecules counted twice and $1/2$ for center-of-mass). Per mole:

$$b = \frac{2}{3}\pi N_{A} d^{3} \approx 4 \times \text{(actual molecular volume per mole)}$$

The **available volume** for molecular motion is therefore $V - nb$ (or $v - b$ per unit mass), not the full container volume $V$:

$$V_{\text{available}} = V - nb$$

### Combining the Two Corrections

The combined vdW equation is the product of these modified quantities set equal to $RT$:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{v^{2}}\right)\left(v - b\right) = RT$$

## 2.2 Pressure-Explicit Form

Solving for $P$:

$$P = \frac{RT}{v-b} - \frac{a}{v^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This pressure-explicit form is the **only** form used in numerical problems. Always substitute into this form, never the original cubic form.

## 2.3 Virial Expansion of vdW Equation (High-Yield!)

Expanding $\frac{1}{v-b} = \frac{1}{v}\left(\frac{1}{1 - b/v}\right) = \frac{1}{v}\left(1 + \frac{b}{v} + \frac{b^{2}}{v^{2}} + \cdots\right)$:

$$P = \frac{RT}{v} + \frac{RTb - a}{v^{2}} + \frac{RTb^{2}}{v^{3}} + \cdots$$

Comparing with the **Virial Equation of State** $P = \frac{RT}{v}\left(1 + \frac{B(T)}{v} + \frac{C(T)}{v^{2}} + \cdots\right)$:

- **Second virial coefficient:** $B(T) = b - \dfrac{a}{RT}$
- **Third virial coefficient:** $C(T) = b^{2}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** Setting $B(T) = 0$ defines the **Boyle Temperature** $T_B$, below which attractive forces dominate (gas is more compressible than ideal):

$$T_{B} = \frac{a}{Rb}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{v^{2}}\right)\left(v - b\right) = RT$ | vdW EOS (specific volume form) | — |
| 2 | $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{\bar{v}^{2}}\right)\left(\bar{v} - b\right) = \bar{R}T$ | vdW EOS (molar form) | — |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{RT}{v-b} - \dfrac{a}{v^{2}}$ | Pressure-explicit form | Pa |
| 4 | $B(T) = b - \dfrac{a}{RT}$ | Second virial coefficient | m³/kg or m³/mol |
| 5 | $T_{B} = \dfrac{a}{Rb}$ | Boyle Temperature | K |
| 6 | $v_{c} = 3b$ | Critical specific volume | m³/kg |
| 7 | $T_{c} = \dfrac{8a}{27Rb}$ | Critical temperature | K |
| 8 | $P_{c} = \dfrac{a}{27b^{2}}$ | Critical pressure | Pa |
| 9 | $Z_{c} = \dfrac{P_{c}v_{c}}{RT_{c}} = \dfrac{3}{8} = 0.375$ | Universal critical compressibility | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\left(P_{r} + \dfrac{3}{v_{r}^{2}}\right)\left(3v_{r} - 1\right) = 8T_{r}$ | Reduced vdW EOS (Law of Corresponding States) | — |
| 11 | $a = \dfrac{27R^{2}T_{c}^{2}}{64P_{c}}$ | $a$ from critical constants | (varies) |
| 12 | $b = \dfrac{RT_{c}}{8P_{c}}$ | $b$ from critical constants | (varies) |

> [!NOTE]
> All values of $a$ and $b$ are **gas-specific**. For instance, $\text{CO}_{2}$: $a = 0.364\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{m}^{6}/\text{mol}^{2}$, $b = 4.27\times10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{mol}$. These must be looked up or given in the problem.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Law of Corresponding States** (Eq. 10 above) — vdW's most enduring legacy. It states that *all fluids at the same reduced state ($P_r, T_r, v_r$) have the same compressibility factor $Z$*. This single insight allows engineers to generalize data from one fluid to another.
- **Limitations:** vdW is quantitatively poor for polar/polarizable molecules and quantitatively *predicts* the phase change as a smooth S-curve (in reality, the transition is *discontinuous*; Maxwell's equal-area rule is required to fix this).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation of Critical Constants from vdW Equation

**Objective:** Find $P_c$, $T_c$, and $v_c$ in terms of the vdW constants $a$, $b$, and $R$.

### Step 1 — State the Mathematical Conditions at the Critical Point

At the **critical point**, the isotherm on the $P\text{-}v$ diagram has an **inflection point** with a **horizontal tangent**. This yields two simultaneous conditions:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T=T_c} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial v^{2}}\right)_{T=T_c} = 0$$

### Step 2 — Write the Pressure-Explicit vdW Equation

$$P = \frac{RT}{v-b} - \frac{a}{v^{2}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the First Partial Derivative

Differentiating term by term with respect to $v$ at constant $T$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T} = -\frac{RT}{\left(v-b\right)^{2}} + \frac{2a}{v^{3}}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Second Partial Derivative

Differentiating the first derivative again with respect to $v$ at constant $T$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial v^{2}}\right)_{T} = \frac{2RT}{\left(v-b\right)^{3}} - \frac{6a}{v^{4}}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Two Critical Point Conditions

**Condition 1:** Set the first derivative to zero:

$$-\frac{RT_{c}}{\left(v_{c}-b\right)^{2}} + \frac{2a}{v_{c}^{3}} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$RT_{c} = \frac{2a\left(v_{c}-b\right)^{2}}{v_{c}^{3}} \quad \cdots \text{(i)}$$

**Condition 2:** Set the second derivative to zero:

$$\frac{2RT_{c}}{\left(v_{c}-b\right)^{3}} - \frac{6a}{v_{c}^{4}} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$RT_{c} = \frac{3a\left(v_{c}-b\right)^{3}}{v_{c}^{4}} \quad \cdots \text{(ii)}$$

### Step 6 — Equate (i) and (ii) to Eliminate $RT_c$

$$\frac{2a\left(v_{c}-b\right)^{2}}{v_{c}^{3}} = \frac{3a\left(v_{c}-b\right)^{3}}{v_{c}^{4}}$$

Cancel $a$ and one power of $(v_c - b)$ from both sides:

$$\frac{2}{v_{c}^{3}} = \frac{3\left(v_{c}-b\right)}{v_{c}^{4}}$$

Cross-multiply:

$$2v_{c} = 3\left(v_{c}-b\right)$$

$$2v_{c} = 3v_{c} - 3b$$

$$3b = v_{c}$$

$$\boxed{v_{c} = 3b}$$

### Step 7 — Solve for $T_c$

Substitute $v_c = 3b$ into Equation (i):

$$RT_{c} = \frac{2a\left(3b - b\right)^{2}}{\left(3b\right)^{3}} = \frac{2a\left(2b\right)^{2}}{27b^{3}} = \frac{8a}{27b}$$

$$\boxed{T_{c} = \frac{8a}{27Rb}}$$

### Step 8 — Solve for $P_c$

Substitute $v_c = 3b$ and $T_c = \dfrac{8a}{27Rb}$ into the pressure-explicit form:

$$P_{c} = \frac{RT_{c}}{v_{c}-b} - \frac{a}{v_{c}^{2}}$$

$$P_{c} = \frac{R \cdot \dfrac{8a}{27Rb}}{3b - b} - \frac{a}{\left(3b\right)^{2}}$$

$$P_{c} = \frac{\dfrac{8a}{27b}}{2b} - \frac{a}{9b^{2}}$$

$$P_{c} = \frac{8a}{54b^{2}} - \frac{a}{9b^{2}}$$

Convert to a common denominator of $54b^{2}$:

$$P_{c} = \frac{8a}{54b^{2}} - \frac{6a}{54b^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{c} = \frac{a}{27b^{2}}}$$

### Step 9 — Verify the Universal Critical Compressibility Factor

$$Z_{c} = \frac{P_{c} \cdot v_{c}}{R \cdot T_{c}} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{a}{27b^{2}}\right)\left(3b\right)}{R \cdot \left(\dfrac{8a}{27Rb}\right)} = \frac{\dfrac{3a}{27b}}{\dfrac{8a}{27b}} = \frac{3a}{27b} \times \frac{27b}{8a} = \frac{3}{8}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{c} = 0.375}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The fact that vdW predicts $Z_c = 0.375$ for *all* fluids (a universal constant) is both its strength and weakness. Strength: gives a Law of Corresponding States. Weakness: real gases have $Z_c$ between 0.27 and 0.30 (e.g., water: 0.23, nitrogen: 0.29, helium: 0.30) — so vdW is only qualitative here.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Reduced Equation of State

**Objective:** Eliminate $a$, $b$, and $R$ to obtain a universal EOS in terms of reduced properties.

### Step 1 — Define Reduced Properties

$$P_{r} = \frac{P}{P_{c}}, \quad v_{r} = \frac{v}{v_{c}}, \quad T_{r} = \frac{T}{T_{c}}$$

Invert:

$$P = P_{r} P_{c}, \quad v = v_{r} v_{c}, \quad T = T_{r} T_{c}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Critical Constant Expressions

From §3.1, we have:

$$P_{c} = \frac{a}{27b^{2}}, \quad v_{c} = 3b, \quad T_{c} = \frac{8a}{27Rb}$$

Therefore:

$$a = 3P_{c} v_{c}^{2}, \quad b = \frac{v_{c}}{3}, \quad R = \frac{8P_{c} v_{c}}{3T_{c}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the Original vdW Equation

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{v^{2}}\right)\left(v - b\right) = RT$$

$$\left(P_{r}P_{c} + \frac{3P_{c}v_{c}^{2}}{v_{r}^{2}v_{c}^{2}}\right)\left(v_{r}v_{c} - \frac{v_{c}}{3}\right) = \frac{8P_{c}v_{c}}{3T_{c}} \cdot T_{r}T_{c}$$

### Step 4 — Factor Out Critical Values

$$\left(P_{r} + \frac{3}{v_{r}^{2}}\right) P_{c} \cdot v_{c}\left(v_{r} - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{8P_{c} v_{c} T_{r}}{3}$$

### Step 5 — Cancel $P_c v_c$ from Both Sides

$$\left(P_{r} + \frac{3}{v_{r}^{2}}\right)\left(v_{r} - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{8T_{r}}{3}$$

Multiplying both sides by 3:

$$\left(P_{r} + \frac{3}{v_{r}^{2}}\right)\left(3v_{r} - 1\right) = 8T_{r}$$

This is the **Reduced Van der Waals Equation of State** — a remarkable result showing that *all fluids obeying vdW satisfy the same universal relation* between their reduced properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** This is the mathematical basis of the **Law of Corresponding States**, which states that two substances at the same reduced state will have the same compressibility, fugacity, and thermodynamic behavior.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]:**

A 0.5 m³ rigid tank contains 4 kg of a gas at 350 K. The Van der Waals constants for the gas are $a = 0.135\,\text{N}\cdot\text{m}^{4}/\text{kg}^{2}$ and $b = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}$. Calculate the pressure inside the tank using the vdW equation. Compare with the ideal gas prediction. Take $R = 0.296\,\text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$ for the gas.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Calculate the specific volume.

$$v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{0.5\,\text{m}^{3}}{4\,\text{kg}} = 0.125\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the pressure-explicit vdW equation.

$$P = \frac{RT}{v-b} - \frac{a}{v^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $(v - b)$.

$$v - b = 0.125 - 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1238\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the first term $\frac{RT}{v-b}$.

$$\frac{RT}{v-b} = \frac{0.296 \times 10^{3}\,\text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K} \times 350\,\text{K}}{0.1238\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}}$$

$$\frac{RT}{v-b} = \frac{103{,}600}{0.1238} = 836{,}833\,\text{Pa} \approx 836.8\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the second term $\frac{a}{v^{2}}$.

$$\frac{a}{v^{2}} = \frac{0.135}{(0.125)^{2}} = \frac{0.135}{0.015625} = 8.64\,\text{Pa} \approx 0.00864\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 6:** Subtract to get the vdW pressure.

$$P_{\text{vdW}} = 836.83 - 0.00864 \approx 836.82\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 7:** Compare with the ideal gas pressure.

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{RT}{v} = \frac{0.296 \times 10^{3} \times 350}{0.125} = \frac{103{,}600}{0.125} = 828{,}800\,\text{Pa} = 828.8\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 8:** Comment on the deviation.

$$\%\,\text{Deviation} = \frac{P_{\text{vdW}} - P_{\text{ideal}}}{P_{\text{ideal}}} \times 100 = \frac{836.82 - 828.80}{828.80} \times 100 \approx +0.97\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The vdW pressure is **higher** than the ideal gas pressure because the *excluded volume effect* ($b$) dominates the *attractive force effect* ($a$) at this relatively high specific volume. This is characteristic of a low-density gas where the $b/v$ term in the virial expansion dominates over $a/(RTv)$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — vdW Equation Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Gas Molecules in Container]:::input
    B{Analyze Molecular Reality}:::process
    C[Molecules have FINITE SIZE]:::process
    D[Volume correction b]:::process
    E[Molecules experience ATTRACTIVE FORCES]:::process
    F[Pressure correction a v^-2]:::process
    G[Modified Pressure Term P + a v^-2]:::output
    H[Modified Volume Term v - b]:::output
    I[vdW Equation: P+a v^-2 x v-b = RT]:::output
    J[Engineering Predictions: P, T, v, Z, Phase Behavior]:::output

    A --> B
    B --> C
    B --> E
    C --> D
    E --> F
    D --> H
    F --> G
    H --> I
    G --> I
    I --> J

    classDef input fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E5BAA,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Derivation Logic of Critical Constants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StepA[Start vdW Pressure Equation P = RT/v-b - a/v^2]:::start
    StepB[Apply Critical Point Condition 1: dP/dv = 0]:::process
    StepC[Apply Critical Point Condition 2: d2P/dv2 = 0]:::process
    StepD[Obtain Equation i: RTc = 2a vc-b squared / vc cubed]:::process
    StepE[Obtain Equation ii: RTc = 3a vc-b cubed / vc to the 4th]:::process
    StepF[Equate i and ii to eliminate RTc]:::process
    StepG[Solve for Critical Specific Volume vc = 3b]:::result
    StepH[Back-substitute to find Tc = 8a over 27Rb]:::result
    StepI[Compute Pc = a over 27b squared]:::result
    StepJ[Verify Universal Zc = 3 over 8 = 0.375]:::result

    StepA --> StepB
    StepA --> StepC
    StepB --> StepD
    StepC --> StepE
    StepD --> StepF
    StepE --> StepF
    StepF --> StepG
    StepG --> StepH
    StepH --> StepI
    StepI --> StepJ

    classDef start fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Mapping Physical Reality to Equation of State

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ASSUMPTIONS[Ideal Gas Assumptions]
        AG1[Point Masses No Volume]
        AG2[No Intermolecular Forces]
    end

    subgraph CORRECTIONS[vdW Corrections]
        AG1 -->|Relax| B1[Excluded Volume b]
        AG2 -->|Relax| B2[Attractive Force a]
    end

    subgraph EQUATION[vdW Equation of State]
        B1 --> C1[Volume Term: v minus b]
        B2 --> C2[Pressure Term: P + a over v squared]
        C1 --> D1[Full vdW EOS]
        C2 --> D1
    end

    subgraph APPLICATIONS[Engineering Applications]
        D1 --> E1[Critical Constants]
        D1 --> E2[Law of Corresponding States]
        D1 --> E3[Real Gas Property Tables]
        D1 --> E4[Phase Equilibrium Modeling]
    end

    classDef ideal fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef corr fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef eq fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class AG1,AG2 ideal
    class B1,B2 corr
    class C1,C2,D1 eq
    class E1,E2,E3,E4 app
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams map the **logical architecture** of the vdW concept, from the underlying physical assumptions → mathematical corrections → final equation → engineering applications. They are designed to help students build mental models before tackling numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the Van der Waals equation of state. Explain the physical significance of the constants $a$ and $b$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Van der Waals equation of state is a cubic equation describing the P-v-T behavior of real (non-ideal) gases, written as:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{v^{2}}\right)\left(v - b\right) = RT$$

**Significance of $a$ (1 Mark):** The constant $a$ accounts for the **attractive intermolecular forces** (Van der Waals / London dispersion forces) between gas molecules. The term $a/v^2$ represents the *cohesive pressure* or *internal pressure* that must be added to the observed pressure $P$ to obtain the pressure an ideal gas of point particles would exert. It is expressed in units of $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{m}^{6}/\text{mol}^{2}$ (molar form) or $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}^{4}/\text{kg}^{2}$ (specific volume form).

**Significance of $b$ (1 Mark):** The constant $b$ represents the **excluded volume** per unit mass or per mole — it is approximately four times the actual volume occupied by the gas molecules themselves. It accounts for the fact that molecules have a finite size and cannot be compressed into a volume smaller than $b$. The available volume for molecular motion is therefore $(v - b)$.

**Why the $1/v^2$ dependence for $a$ (1 Mark):** The attractive force on a wall-bound molecule is proportional to the number of bulk molecules ($\propto 1/v$), and the number of wall-approaching molecules is also $\propto 1/v$. The product of these two densities gives the $1/v^2$ dependence.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**State the Van der Waals equation in molar form. How does it differ from the ideal gas equation? Under what condition does the vdW equation reduce to the ideal gas equation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1 Mark):** The Van der Waals equation on a molar basis (per mole, with molar volume $\bar{v} = V/n$) is:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{\bar{v}^{2}}\right)\left(\bar{v} - b\right) = \bar{R}T$$

**Differences from the Ideal Gas Equation (1 Mark):**

| Feature | Ideal Gas | Van der Waals |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular volume | Neglected (point masses) | Accounted for via $b$ |
| Intermolecular forces | Neglected | Accounted for via $a/v^2$ |
| Equation form | Linear in $P$, $v$, $T$ | Cubic in $v$ |
| Validity | Low pressure, high temperature | Broader range, including near saturation |

**Reduction to Ideal Gas (1 Mark):** At **very low densities** (very large $\bar{v}$), both $a/\bar{v}^{2} \to 0$ and $b \to 0$ (relative to $\bar{v}$). The equation asymptotically becomes $P\bar{v} = \bar{R}T$, the ideal gas law. Equivalently, at the **Boyle temperature** $T_B = a/(R\bar{R})$ and above, attractive and repulsive contributions balance, and gas behavior is nearly ideal over a wide pressure range.
