## 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Starting from the Van der Waals equation of state, derive expressions for the **critical pressure $P_c$, critical temperature $T_c$, and critical specific volume $v_c$** in terms of the constants $a$, $b$, and $R$. Also compute the universal critical compressibility factor $Z_c$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A gas has Van der Waals constants $a = 0.554\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{m}^{6}/\text{mol}^{2}$ and $b = 6.4 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{mol}$. Calculate: **(i)** the critical constants $P_c$, $T_c$, and $V_c$ (per mole); **(ii)** the Boyle temperature $T_B$. Take $\bar{R} = 8.314\,\text{J/mol}\cdot\text{K}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

The critical point is defined by two simultaneous conditions on the isotherm in P-v space:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T_c} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial v^{2}}\right)_{T_c} = 0$$

The pressure-explicit vdW form is:

$$P = \frac{RT}{v-b} - \frac{a}{v^{2}}$$

**First derivative:**

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T} = -\frac{RT}{(v-b)^{2}} + \frac{2a}{v^{3}} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad RT = \frac{2a(v-b)^{2}}{v^{3}} \quad \cdots (1)$$

**Second derivative:**

$$\left(\frac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial v^{2}}\right)_{T} = \frac{2RT}{(v-b)^{3}} - \frac{6a}{v^{4}} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad RT = \frac{3a(v-b)^{3}}{v^{4}} \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Equating (1) and (2):**

$$\frac{2a(v-b)^{2}}{v^{3}} = \frac{3a(v-b)^{3}}{v^{4}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{v} = \frac{3(v-b)}{v^{2}} \cdot v \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2v = 3(v-b) \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_c = 3b$$

**Back-substituting into (1):**

$$RT_c = \frac{2a(2b)^{2}}{(3b)^{3}} = \frac{8a}{27b} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb}$$

**Back-substituting into the pressure equation:**

$$P_c = \frac{RT_c}{v_c - b} - \frac{a}{v_c^{2}} = \frac{8a/(27b)}{2b} - \frac{a}{9b^{2}} = \frac{4a}{27b^{2}} - \frac{3a}{27b^{2}} = \frac{a}{27b^{2}}$$

**Compressibility factor:**

$$Z_c = \frac{P_c v_c}{RT_c} = \frac{\left(\frac{a}{27b^{2}}\right)(3b)}{R \cdot \frac{8a}{27Rb}} = \frac{3/27}{8/27} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Part (i) — Critical Constants:**

Substitute the given values into the derived formulas.

**Critical temperature:**

$$T_c = \frac{8a}{27R b} = \frac{8 \times 0.554}{27 \times 8.314 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$T_c = \frac{4.432}{0.01436} = 308.6\,\text{K}$$

**Critical pressure:**

$$P_c = \frac{a}{27b^{2}} = \frac{0.554}{27 \times (6.4 \times 10^{-5})^{2}}$$

$$P_c = \frac{0.554}{27 \times 4.096 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{0.554}{1.106 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$P_c = 5.01 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} = 5.01\,\text{MPa}$$

**Critical molar volume:**

$$\bar{V}_c = 3b = 3 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} = 1.92 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{mol} = 192\,\text{cm}^{3}/\text{mol}$$

**Part (ii) — Boyle Temperature:**

$$T_B = \frac{a}{Rb} = \frac{0.554}{8.314 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.554}{5.321 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$T_B = 1041.2\,\text{K} \quad \blacksquare$$

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the **Reduced Van der Waals Equation of State** in the form $\left(P_r + \dfrac{3}{v_r^{2}}\right)\left(3v_r - 1\right) = 8T_r$. Clearly state the Law of Corresponding States. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rigid container of 0.08 m³ holds 5 kg of a gas at 400 K. The Van der Waals constants are $a = 0.196\,\text{N}\cdot\text{m}^{4}/\text{kg}^{2}$ and $b = 9.0 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}$. The specific gas constant is $R = 287\,\text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$. Determine: **(i)** the pressure using the vdW equation; **(ii)** the pressure using the ideal gas equation; **(iii)** the percent deviation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Starting Point:** The vdW equation is:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{v^{2}}\right)\left(v - b\right) = RT \quad \cdots (*)$$

**Step 1 — Define Reduced Properties:**

$$P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}, \quad v_r = \frac{v}{v_c}, \quad T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}$$

**Step 2 — Express $a$, $b$, $R$ in terms of critical constants:**

From §3.1: $v_c = 3b \Rightarrow b = \dfrac{v_c}{3}$

From §3.1: $T_c = \dfrac{8a}{27Rb} \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{27R b T_c}{8} = \dfrac{27R T_c (v_c/3)}{8} = \dfrac{9R T_c v_c}{8}$

But we also have $P_c = \dfrac{a}{27b^{2}}$. Combining:

$$a = 3P_c v_c^{2}, \quad R = \frac{8P_c v_c}{3T_c}, \quad b = \frac{v_c}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into (*):**

$$\left(P_r P_c + \frac{3P_c v_c^{2}}{v_r^{2} v_c^{2}}\right)\left(v_r v_c - \frac{v_c}{3}\right) = \frac{8P_c v_c}{3T_c} \cdot T_r T_c$$

**Step 4 — Factor common terms:**

$$P_c v_c \left(P_r + \frac{3}{v_r^{2}}\right)\left(v_r - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{8P_c v_c T_r}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $P_c v_c$ and multiply by 3:**

$$\left(P_r + \frac{3}{v_r^{2}}\right)\left(3v_r - 1\right) = 8T_r \quad \blacksquare$$

**Law of Corresponding States (Statement):** *All fluids, when compared at the same reduced pressure $P_r$ and reduced temperature $T_r$, will have the same reduced volume $v_r$, and hence the same compressibility factor $Z$ and the same deviation from ideal gas behavior.* In other words, the thermodynamic behavior of all substances obeying the vdW EOS is governed by a single universal function of reduced properties alone.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Part (i) — vdW Pressure:**

Specific volume:

$$v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{0.08}{5} = 0.016\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}$$

Volume correction:

$$v - b = 0.016 - 9.0 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0151\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}$$

Attractive pressure correction:

$$\frac{a}{v^{2}} = \frac{0.196}{(0.016)^{2}} = \frac{0.196}{2.56 \times 10^{-4}} = 765.625\,\text{Pa}$$

Applying the pressure-explicit vdW form:

$$P_{\text{vdW}} = \frac{RT}{v-b} - \frac{a}{v^{2}}$$

$$P_{\text{vdW}} = \frac{287 \times 400}{0.0151} - 765.625$$

$$P_{\text{vdW}} = 7{,}602{,}649.0 - 765.625 = 7{,}601{,}883.4\,\text{Pa} \approx 7.602\,\text{MPa}$$

**Part (ii) — Ideal Gas Pressure:**

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{RT}{v} = \frac{287 \times 400}{0.016} = 7{,}175{,}000\,\text{Pa} = 7.175\,\text{MPa}$$

**Part (iii) — Percent Deviation:**

$$\%\,\text{Deviation} = \frac{P_{\text{vdW}} - P_{\text{ideal}}}{P_{\text{ideal}}} \times 100 = \frac{7.602 - 7.175}{7.175} \times 100 = +5.95\%$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{vdW}} \approx 7.602\,\text{MPa}, \quad P_{\text{ideal}} \approx 7.175\,\text{MPa}, \quad \text{Deviation} \approx +5.95\%} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap (2-3 marks lost):** The constants $a$ and $b$ are given *per mole* in some textbooks and *per unit mass* in others. Always verify whether the form is $R$ (specific gas constant) or $\bar{R}$ (universal gas constant) before substituting. Mixing them yields numerical errors.
>
> 2. **Excluded Volume Sign (1-2 marks lost):** Students often write $P(v+b)$ instead of $P(v-b)$. Remember: the available volume is **less** than the container volume, so the sign is **minus**.
>
> 3. **Critical Point Derivative Forgetting (3-4 marks lost):** Many students forget that the critical point requires **TWO** conditions, not one (the inflection point: both first AND second derivative vanish). Setting only $\partial P/\partial v = 0$ is insufficient.
>
> 4. **Reduced Equation Not Derived (1-2 marks lost):** When asked for the reduced EOS, students often write the answer directly. KTU examiners expect the *derivation*, especially the cancellation of $P_c v_c$ from both sides.
>
> 5. **$Z_c$ as a Universal Constant:** Always mention that vdW predicts $Z_c = 0.375$ for ALL fluids. This is its key prediction and a frequent one-mark question.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

✅ **Core Equation:** $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{v^{2}}\right)\left(v - b\right) = RT$ — Memorize in BOTH specific-volume and molar forms.

✅ **Two Corrections Always:**
- **Pressure correction** $+a/v^2$ → accounts for **attractive forces** (added to $P$)
- **Volume correction** $-b$ → accounts for **finite molecular size** (subtracted from $v$)

✅ **Pressure-Explicit Form (Always Use for Numerical Problems):**
$$P = \frac{RT}{v-b} - \frac{a}{v^{2}}$$

✅ **Critical Constants (Trinity):**
- $v_c = 3b$
- $T_c = \dfrac{8a}{27Rb}$
- $P_c = \dfrac{a}{27b^{2}}$

✅ **Universal Critical Compressibility:** $Z_c = \dfrac{3}{8} = 0.375$ — A defining hallmark of vdW EOS.

✅ **Boyle Temperature:** $T_B = \dfrac{a}{Rb}$ — At this temperature, $B(T) = 0$ and gas is "ideal-like" over a wide range.

✅ **Virial Connection:** vdW's second virial coefficient is $B(T) = b - \dfrac{a}{RT}$.

✅ **Law of Corresponding States:** Reduced EOS is universal: $\left(P_r + \dfrac{3}{v_r^{2}}\right)\left(3v_r - 1\right) = 8T_r$.

✅ **Real Gas Behavior Indicators:**
- High density / low temperature → deviations large → use vdW or better EOS
- Low density / high temperature → use ideal gas law

✅ **Limitations to State in Exams:**
- Quantitatively poor (e.g., predicts $Z_c = 0.375$ vs. real $0.27$–$0.30$)
- S-curve below critical point requires **Maxwell construction** for phase change
- Poor for polar molecules and high-density liquids
- Does not account for quantum effects (important for H₂, He at cryogenic conditions)

✅ **Engineering Successors:** Redlich-Kwong (1949), Soave-Redlich-Kwong (1972), Peng-Robinson (1976) — all are *modifications* of the vdW structure that students will study in higher semesters.

✅ **KTU Numerical Recipe:** Given $V$, $m$, $T$, $a$, $b$, $R$ → Find $v = V/m$ → Use pressure-explicit form. NEVER solve the cubic by hand in KTU exams unless specifically asked.
